title: Ben Silbermann
body:
# Ben Silbermann

Ben Silbermann is the co-founder of Pinterest. Before Pinterest, he worked at Google. He left to build a product inspired by his childhood love of collecting things -- stamps, insects, and other objects. Pinterest grew into a visual discovery platform used by hundreds of millions of people.

Silbermann appeared alongside [[Patrick Collison]] and [[John Collison]] (Stripe) in the Stanford "How to Start a Startup" series, where Sam Altman moderated a discussion on [[Company Culture]] and building early teams [1].

## Worldview

**Culture is gardening, not architecture.** Silbermann rejects top-down cultural design: "People think culture is like architecture, when it's a lot more like gardening. You plant some seeds, and then you pull out weeds that aren't working, and they sort of expand" [1]. This inductive approach -- hiring people you want to work with, observing what works, pruning what does not -- defined Pinterest's early culture.

**Hire for quirkiness and curiosity.** Silbermann consistently sought people with wide-ranging interests and deep skill in one area. His first engineers were "probably some of the quirkiest people I've ever met" -- one had made his own board game with elaborate rules, another was into magic tricks and had coded a magic trick app with a self-produced demo video [1]. "Really creative, quirky people that are excited about many disciplines and are extraordinary at one tend to build really great products" [1].

**Pre-calibrate before interviewing in unfamiliar domains.** When hiring for roles he did not understand (head of finance, head of marketing), Silbermann first talked to people he knew were world-class in that discipline: "What are the key traits or characteristics that you look for? What are the questions that you ask? If you're looking for the next person that's as good as you, where are they working right now? And what's their phone number?" [1]. He argues that learning what good looks like during the interview process itself is prohibitively expensive.

**There is no wrong place to find people.** Silbermann hired early employees from Craigslist ads, random tech talks, bars, and weekly barbecues at the office ("bring your own food and drinks, and then we would just talk to folks"). The lesson: "Really good people are generally doing something else. You have to go seek them out rather than expecting that they're going to seek you out" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Silbermann brings the perspective of a non-technical founder (he was a product manager at Google, not an engineer) building a consumer product where the user base -- initially 80% women -- did not match the founding team's demographics. This forced Pinterest to screen for ambition and excitement about the mission rather than requiring candidates to be lifelong users: "We screen for people that are ambitious and excited about the vision. They have to know exactly how our service works and have to have used it. But they may not be a lifelong user" [1].

He is distinctive in his emphasis on authenticity checks during interviews. When candidates say "I'm really passionate about what you're doing," he asks "Where else are you interviewing?" If they list seven unrelated companies, "that's a sign that they're probably not being authentic about what they care about. Those folks, often when things get really hard, they won't stick it out" [1].

## Key Contributions

- **"Gardening, not architecture"** as a mental model for culture building [1]
- **Reference checking methodology**: force scarcity by asking "top 1%, 5%, or 10%?" to overcome the natural tendency of references to be generically positive [1]
- **Pre-calibration ritual**: talk to known world-class practitioners before interviewing in any unfamiliar domain [1]
- **Scaling through autonomous teams**: each team at Pinterest has a strong designer, engineering leader, writer, and community manager, anchored on a single project with barriers removed [1]
- **Onboarding progression**: from "here's your computer, here's the problem" in a two-bedroom apartment, to a week-long program with 30-day satisfaction and productivity metrics [1]

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Building Company Culture, Part II](https://ycombinator.com/library/JO-how-to-start-a-startup-building-company-culture-part-ii) -- Ben Silbermann, [[Patrick Collison]], [[John Collison]] (2014)

title: David Lieb
related: [[Bump to Google Photos: From Contact Sharing to a Billion Users]]
body:
# David Lieb

David Lieb is a YC Group Partner, Product Lead for Google Photos, and co-founder and former CEO of Bump (YC S09). Before Bump, he was an engineer at Texas Instruments who went to business school. He dropped out to pursue Bump full-time during YC. Bump reached 150 million downloads and 10 million monthly active users before being acquired by Google in 2013. At Google, Lieb combined Bump's product insights with Google's AI capabilities to create Google Photos, now one of 17 billion-user apps at Google. He also hosts episodes of the "How to Build the Future" series, interviewing founders like [[Aravind Srinivas]] of Perplexity, co-hosts "The Breakdown" podcast, and gave a foundational lecture on [[Cohort Retention]] [3].

## Worldview

Lieb's thinking is organized around the principle that product insight matters more than technology, and that user needs are discovered through direct contact rather than analytics dashboards.

He believes [[Product-Market Fit]] is not binary but staged. Even Google Photos, with over a billion users, has PMF for some user segments and not others. Each new user who joins a product at scale is fundamentally different from the first user, and the product must evolve to serve them [1].

He views the founder's job as interpreting user behavior rather than simply measuring it. His career inflection points all came from directly talking to users -- calling the top 100 Bump users to discover they used it for photo sharing, talking to YC founders to understand why Flock was not getting traction [1].

## Key Frameworks

**The Human Assistant Test.** When designing a product, imagine a human being with unlimited time, intelligence, and resources doing the job your product will do. List everything that person would do for the user. This list becomes the product roadmap. Lieb used this to design Google Photos: "If my co-founder, Andy, was my photo assistant, and all he did all day long was help me with my photos, what would he do?" [1].

**Two Vectors for AI.** AI startups should identify which of two vectors they pursue: (1) scaling a task that skilled humans can do but cannot do affordably at scale (Google Photos' approach), or (2) solving problems beyond human cognitive capacity. The first is lower risk; the second has higher payoff but is harder to validate [1].

**Cohort Retention as the Answer to "Have We Made Something People Want?"** Lieb calls cohort retention the single best quantitative method for answering YC's fundamental question. The only thing that matters is whether retention curves get flat -- the shape, not the absolute number. Google Photos retained 20-40% of weekly cohorts, which was enough to build to a billion users [3]. See [[Cohort Retention]] for the full treatment.

**Durable Human Needs vs. Novelty.** Consumer products succeed when they amplify a fundamental human behavior 10x or 100x. Facebook amplified social connection. Instagram amplified self-expression. Products that introduce novel formats without addressing a durable need fade once the novelty wears off [1].

**Come for the Tool, Stay for the Network.** Learned from Chris Dixon's framework after Flock failed. The first user must get standalone value; if value depends on friends also adopting, 99% of users will never activate [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Compared to other YC speakers who focus on the early-stage founder journey, Lieb bridges the startup-to-big-company transition. His experience selling Bump to Google and then building a billion-user product inside a large organization gives him a rare dual perspective.

He is unusually candid about what went wrong at Bump: not understanding cohort retention (a mistake he now teaches others to avoid [3]), not differentiating their business from Facebook and Twitter's dynamics, raising too much money without a clear plan, and focusing on system performance microseconds when the fundamental business model was the real problem [1].

His advice on acquisitions is specific and uncommon: the most important question is mission alignment, not financial terms. He recommends founders spend far more time with the people they will work with daily and far less time negotiating price [1].

## On Investing and Evaluation

As an angel investor, Lieb asks founders: "If you could go acquire a big company with the purpose of furthering your vision, which big company would you acquire?" The answer reveals both ambition and long-term product thinking [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling One of the Biggest iOS Apps](https://ycombinator.com/library/5f-on-starting-and-scaling-one-of-the-biggest-ios-apps) -- David Lieb, Gustaf Alstromer (n.d.)
2. [How To Build The Future: Aravind Srinivas](https://ycombinator.com/library/MC-how-to-build-the-future-aravind-srinivas) -- Aravind Srinivas, David Lieb (2025)
3. [How To Improve Cohort Retention](https://ycombinator.com/library/LV-how-to-improve-cohort-retention) -- David Lieb (n.d.)
4. [AI Apps Are Broken -- Here's How To Fix Them](https://ycombinator.com/library/MR-ai-apps-are-broken-here-s-how-to-fix-them) -- [[Pete Koomen]], [[Tom Blomfield]], David Lieb (2025)

title: Kevin Hale
related: [[Lob: From Inkjet Printer to Enterprise Mail API]]
body:
# Kevin Hale

Kevin Hale is a YC Group Partner and co-founder of Wufoo, an online form builder acquired by SurveyMonkey in 2013. Wufoo raised $118,000 total and returned approximately 29x per dollar to investors. The company was run by a 10-person remote team from Florida with no office [3]. Hale went through YC's second ever batch (Winter 2006) [3].

## Worldview

Hale's thinking rests on a single conviction: the values that help you get your first dollar are the same values that get you to a billion dollars [3]. He approaches every startup challenge -- pitch decks, product design, user retention -- as a design problem rooted in empathy and clarity.

**Growth is conversion minus churn.** Most people focus on conversion. Hale argues that reducing churn is cheaper, easier, and produces identical growth effects. "There's almost no difference between a 1% increase in conversion rate and a 1% decrease in churn. They do exactly the same thing to your growth" [3].

**Products are relationships.** Hale draws from marriage research (John Gottman) to argue that the same dynamics that make personal relationships succeed or fail apply to how companies interact with users. Acquiring new users is like dating; retaining them is like maintaining a successful marriage [3].

**Everyone does customer support.** At Wufoo, every employee -- including engineers -- did customer support shifts. This practice, which Hale calls "support-driven development," fixes the broken feedback loop where builders are divorced from the consequences of their decisions [3]. Paul English at Kayak followed the same principle with a red phone on the engineering floor [3].

**Customer intimacy is the universal path to market dominance.** Of the three paths to market leadership (best price, best product, best overall solution), customer intimacy is "the only one that everyone can do at any stage of their company. It requires almost no money to get started with -- usually just requires a little bit of humility and some manners" [3].

## On API Companies and Vertical Integration

In his interview with [[Harry Zhang]], Hale contributed substantive observations about API companies and vertical integration.

**API documentation as interface design**: Hale argues that API companies represent the purest form of interface design. "A lot of people think of it as 'Oh, it has no sort of interface.' But to me, the interface is just documentation. It's very pure" [2]. He evaluates API companies by how thoughtful their documentation is, treating it as a signal of engineering quality and community-building intent. Stripe is his reference standard [2].

**Vertical integration question**: Hale pressed Zhang on whether Lob would eventually buy printing facilities, drawing the Amazon analogy -- Amazon could have continued using third-party logistics but eventually built its own warehouses for efficiency and vision. He frames the decision point: vertical integration makes sense when "you have a vision for the future that requires you to own that technology set" [2].

**Non-sexy businesses that make money**: Hale expresses his preference for unglamorous businesses: "I love these non-sexy businesses, especially ones that make money" [2].

## Distinct Perspective

Hale is distinctive for treating every startup problem as an interface design challenge. Pitch decks are information design problems ([[Legible Simple Obvious]]). Customer retention is a relationship design problem (Gottman's four horsemen). Product development is a knowledge gap problem (Jared Spool's framework). He combines emotional intelligence about user psychology with practical, specific examples -- the emotional state dropdown, the "Since You've Been Gone" feature, handwritten thank you notes -- that demonstrate how to operationalize empathy at scale.

## Key Frameworks

- **[[Legible Simple Obvious]]**: Framework for pitch deck evaluation [1]
- **Support-Driven Development**: Everyone does customer support to fix the builder-user feedback loop [3]
- **Knowledge Gap Framework** (via Jared Spool): Every interface problem reduces to closing the gap between user knowledge and required knowledge [3]
- **Dating/Marriage Metaphor**: New user acquisition as dating (first impressions), retention as marriage (managing conflict, maintaining passion) [3]
- **Enchanting Quality** (from Japanese design): Distinguishing taken-for-granted quality (function) from enchanting quality (delight) [3]
- **Three Paths to Market Dominance** (via Treacy and Wiersema): Best price, best product, or best overall solution [3]

## References

1. [How to Design a Better Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck) -- Kevin Hale (December 2015)
2. [On starting and scaling direct mail automation startup Lob](https://ycombinator.com/library/6a-on-starting-and-scaling-direct-mail-automation-startup-lob) -- [[Harry Zhang]], Kevin Hale (2019)
3. [How to Start a Startup: Building Products Users Love](https://ycombinator.com/library/JK-how-to-start-a-startup-building-products-users-love) -- Kevin Hale (2014)

title: Tom Blomfield
body:
# Tom Blomfield

Tom Blomfield is a YC Group Partner and serial founder. He co-founded GoCardless (a multi-billion dollar recurring payments company, YC S11), founded Monzo (a UK online bank with 8 million customers), and worked at Grouper (a YC-backed dating/social app). His YC contributions focus on practical, operational guidance for both B2B and consumer founders, particularly around pricing, metrics, and leveraging AI development tools.

## Background and Path to YC

Blomfield was fascinated by computers from age seven, playing a lemonade stand game on an Apple II. At fifteen, he started making money building websites for local businesses [6]. His parents directed him toward Oxford to study law and become a corporate lawyer -- "a pretty safe, well-paying job, pretty respectable, with a pretty predictable linear career path" [6].

At Oxford, he met his first co-founders (including [[Harj Taggar]]) and started an online student marketplace. They did YC, but Blomfield dropped out of the startup to finish his exams. "I always think about how my life might have been different if I hadn't taken that decision" [6].

After his law degree, he and two other Oxford graduates planned to start a dating website. They were convinced to start a bill-splitting app instead -- "one of YC's classic tarpit ideas" [6]. YC accepted them but reset their expectations: "We thought the bar was here, and YC showed us the bar was like up here. We were just play-acting at running a startup" [6].

The bill-splitting app pivoted to B2B payments, becoming GoCardless [5][6]. Growth was slow for the first year or two, but they "ground away at it" [6]. The biggest challenge for Blomfield was that he could not put himself in his customers' shoes -- small businesses collecting money. "The problem we were solving just wasn't close enough to my heart" [6]. After three years, he left. Twelve years later, co-founder Heroji still runs GoCardless, processing tens of billions of dollars of payments in dozens of countries [6].

## Monzo: Building and Nearly Losing a Bank

After GoCardless, Blomfield "wanted to swing bigger" [6]. He saw that "banking was excruciatingly painful... The people running these banks did not understand what software could do." The naive view: rewrite the software from scratch and the bank would work better. "It turns out that's true. It's miraculous" [6].

Monzo launched a prototype in three to four months on a prepaid debit card, leaning on YC principles. Blomfield's notes from YC: "Launch early and talk to users. Do things that don't scale" [6]. The product had a distinctive hot coral card. When someone tapped to pay, a notification popped on their phone. This drove organic word of mouth. Monzo grew from zero to one million customers in about two and a half years without spending on advertising [6].

Blomfield describes the first time he saw a stranger use a Monzo card: a customer in a coffee shop pulled out the card, and the barista said "Is that a Monzo card? I've been waiting in line to get one" [6].

### Taking on Hard Problems Makes Some Things Easier

Blomfield identifies a counterintuitive advantage: "Taking on a really hard problem actually makes some things easier. The smartest, most ambitious people want to come and work with you... The press wants to write about you... Customers want to sign up because you've got this bold ambitious vision" [6].

### The Crisis

Monzo processed fifty to one hundred billion pounds of payments annually. Outages -- sometimes minutes, sometimes hours -- meant tens of thousands of payments failed. The BBC commissioned an ice sculpture of a frozen Monzo card and filmed it outside Monzo's office [6].

When COVID hit, revenue dropped 50% (much of it came from overseas card usage). A hundred-million-pound funding round, nine months in negotiation with paperwork agreed, was due to close on a Monday. On Friday afternoon, the world went into lockdown, and investors paused all investments [6].

Financial regulators told Monzo to "start preparing to wind the company down because it was losing a hundred million pounds a year" [6]. Blomfield worked seven days a week, sixteen to seventeen hours a day. He held all-hands in front of two thousand people, openly sharing what was going wrong. "The vulnerability created this immense bond" [6].

Eventually, Blomfield could no longer function. His identity and the company were "so intertwined" that stepping away "was like letting all of that go." His board and investors kept saying he could step back "in two or three years." He told them: "Maybe you've got two or three more weeks, but that's all I can do" [6].

He stepped down as CEO. A new CEO and COO, along with co-founder Jonas (CTO) and the existing team, carried Monzo through. It is now profitable with over eight million UK customers (15-20% of the adult population) [6].

### Recovery and Transition to YC

After leaving Monzo, Blomfield took a year off. An investor invited him to angel-invest in a fintech and have a conversation with the founder. "I could feel my brain like coming back to life" [6]. He went from one investment to seventy-five in nine months. YC then recruited him, first as a visiting partner, then as a full Group Partner [6].

Blomfield describes rejoining YC as "coming home" -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Garry Tan]], and others from his 2011 cohort are back [6].

## Worldview

**Start from value, not cost.** His [[B2B Pricing]] framework centers on the value equation: calculate the dollar value of what the product delivers to the customer, then price at 25-50% of that value. Cost is only a floor, never a starting point. Competition is addressed through differentiation, not price wars [1].

**Metrics discipline over metrics obsession.** Blomfield holds two positions simultaneously: you must track key metrics from day one (launching without metrics is "flying blind"), but you must not hide behind metrics or try to A/B test everything with insufficient data. Four or five metrics, tracked accurately, with agreed-upon definitions, is the standard [3].

**Fix unit economics before scaling.** This lesson comes from Monzo's early experience of losing 30-40 pounds per customer while scaling to half a million users. "Don't scale your customer base, don't try and grow as quickly as possible whilst you have negative unit economics. You fix them first, and then you scale" [3].

**Organic growth over paid growth.** Blomfield got Monzo to one million customers before spending on marketing. Benchmark: great consumer companies get 80%+ of growth organically [4].

**AI as a professional engineering practice.** Blomfield treats vibe coding not as a gimmick but as a new development methodology that follows the same principles good software engineers use: planning before coding, version control, testing, modular architecture, and refactoring [2].

**Everyone has the choice to create.** Blomfield frames entrepreneurship as a choice between following existing structures and creating new ones: "All of these things were created by people no smarter or harder-working than you or me. You have the chance to create something, to define the rules and make the world feel different" [6].

## Distinct Perspective

Blomfield's unique contribution is bridging B2B and consumer metrics from personal experience. Having operated a B2B payments company (GoCardless), a consumer bank (Monzo), and a consumer social app (Grouper), he can speak to the differences between these business models with operational detail rather than theory. His retention layer-cake visualization -- showing how sticky cohorts compound into unstoppable revenue -- and his unit economics warnings from Monzo's near-negative-margin scaling both come from lived consequences rather than observation.

He also contributes a pivot story: his first company started as a consumer bill-splitting app (Group.io), pivoted to business payments after [[Paul Graham]] told them to stop building features and start getting users, and became GoCardless [5].

## Key Frameworks

- **The Value Equation**: Price at one-third of the value delivered to the customer [1].
- **The 5:1 Rule**: Salespeople should close 5x their total compensation in new ARR annually [1].
- **The Ratchet Method**: Start at a reasonable price and increase by 50% per customer until 25% of deals are lost on price [1].
- **Plan-Implement-Test-Commit**: For vibe coding, create a plan in markdown, implement section by section, test, commit to Git [2].
- **Four or Five Key Metrics**: Pick before launch, agree on definitions, never change them to improve numbers [3].
- **Revenue / Burn Rate / Runway**: The three numbers at the top of every investor update [3].
- **The Layer Cake vs. Leaky Bucket**: Visual model for whether retention is building compounding revenue or draining it [3].
- **Net Dollar Retention benchmarks**: 125-150% early stage, 110-120% mature, below 100% is a red flag [3].
- **Organic/Paid Growth ratio**: 80%+ organic for great consumer companies [4].
- **Magic Moment**: Early user behavior that predicts long-term retention (Facebook's 7 friends in 10 days, Monzo's 3 friends added) [4].
- **NPS minimum**: +50 for consumer startups [4].

## References

1. [How To Price For B2B](https://ycombinator.com/library/LI-how-to-price-for-b2b) -- Tom Blomfield (2025)
2. [How To Get The Most Out Of Vibe Coding](https://ycombinator.com/library/MN-how-to-get-the-most-out-of-vibe-coding) -- Tom Blomfield (2025)
3. [Key Startup Metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KR-key-startup-metrics) -- Tom Blomfield (n.d.)
4. [Consumer Startup Metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KT-consumer-startup-metrics) -- Tom Blomfield (n.d.)
5. [YC's Group Partners Share Their Favorite Pivot Stories](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iy-yc-s-group-partners-share-their-favorite-pivot-stories) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Jared Friedman]], Gustaf Alstromer, Tom Blomfield, Brad Flora, Aaron Epstein (n.d.)
6. [Backstory: Tom Blomfield](https://ycombinator.com/library/KY-backstory-tom-blomfield) -- Tom Blomfield (2024)

title: Michael Truell
related: [[Cursor: From Failed CAD Co-Pilot to $100M ARR Code Editor]]
body:
# Michael Truell

Michael Truell is co-founder and CEO of Cursor (Anysphere), an AI-powered code editor that reached $100M ARR in 2024 when Truell was 24. He began programming in middle school, built robots with reinforcement learning in high school, studied at MIT, and co-founded Anysphere with three MIT classmates in 2022. See [[Cursor: From Failed CAD Co-Pilot to $100M ARR Code Editor]] for the full company story [1].

## Worldview

**Conviction over competition.** Truell's team avoided working on AI for coding because GitHub Copilot was already generating $100M+ in revenue. They only entered the space after exhausting other ideas and realizing that no one was building toward the end state where "all of software development flows through models." The lesson: competition is less important than whether existing players are aiming at the right future [1].

**Product quality is the growth engine.** Cursor grew almost entirely through word of mouth. Attempts at growth engineering "kind of washed away" compared to product improvements. Each improvement -- codebase awareness, faster predictions, agent-like behavior -- showed up directly in the numbers [1].

**The long messy middle.** Rather than believing AI will instantly automate all coding or that the current paradigm is permanent, Truell holds a middle position: professional software engineering will transform over decades. "There will be this long messy middle where you will be working with the AI more and more" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Unlike many AI founders who came from research or large tech companies, Truell represents the builder-from-childhood archetype -- programming since middle school, implementing neural networks from scratch in high school without understanding calculus, spending years on failed projects with his co-founders before finding the right idea. His trajectory validates both the value of starting young (see [[Starting Young]]) and the value of failed projects as education [1].

His most distinctive advice: do not pick an idea because it seems "boring and sleepy and uncompetitive" (their CAD co-pilot failed for exactly this reason). Instead, pick something you are "inherently excited about" even if the competition is intimidating [1].

## References

1. [Michael Truell: Building Cursor at 23, Taking on GitHub Copilot, and Advice to Engineering Students](https://ycombinator.com/library/Ms-michael-truell-building-cursor-at-23-taking-on-github-copilot-and-advice-to-engineering-students) -- Michael Truell, Diana Hu (June 2025)

title: Tim Brady
body:
# Tim Brady

Tim Brady is a YC group partner who works with companies during the batch. He co-founded Yahoo in 1994 and started two other companies. His YC teaching draws heavily on his experience at Yahoo, where he observed firsthand how culture scales (or fails to scale) from founding team to hundreds of employees.

## Worldview

Brady defines culture as behavior, not values statements. "Company culture is that implicit set of behaviors inside of your company" that inform employees how to act "when it hasn't been explicitly laid out for them" [1]. The right behaviors support a good business "in intangible ways that are hard even to describe" [1].

His core belief is that culture is set early and compounds. The first approximately 20 employees are the "cultural DNA" of the company -- they hire and train the next wave. Getting the early hires right creates a high likelihood of a strong, coherent culture. Getting them wrong propagates mistakes that are hard to correct later [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Brady is practical about the constraints founders face. He acknowledges that culture "almost seems like a luxury" at the idea stage and explicitly limits his recommendations to "conversations with your co-founder" rather than formal programs [1]. His six-item framework for early-stage culture requires no budget, no dedicated time, and no publication -- just intentional thinking about who to let into the company and what behaviors to model [1].

He is also one of the few YC voices who explicitly critiques "Move fast and break things" as a cultural value, arguing that internally focused values fail to provide guide rails for ethical behavior [1].

## References

1. [Building Culture](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6r-building-culture) -- Tim Brady (2019)

title: Drew Houston
related: [[Dropbox: From Thumb Drives to 500 Million Users]]
body:
# Drew Houston

Drew Houston is the co-founder and CEO of [[Dropbox: From Thumb Drives to 500 Million Users|Dropbox]] (YC W07), a cloud file storage and sharing company that grew to over 500 million users. He studied at MIT, applied to YC once (rejected with Accolade, an SAT prep company) and a second time (accepted with Dropbox as a solo founder, before finding co-founder Arash Ferdowsi through Kyle Vogt) [1].

## Worldview

**The CEO job changes every six months, and nobody tells you.** Houston identifies this as the hardest part of scaling: "Some of the things that made you good at the prior stage become a hindrance for the next stage." He wrote most of the first version of Dropbox but had to stop coding as the company grew [1].

**Learning to be CEO is a systematic project.** Nobody is born a CEO. Houston's approach: reading (Andy Grove's "High Output Management," Drucker's "The Effective Executive," Munger's "Poor Charlie's Almanack"), mentoring from entrepreneurs at different stages, and studying excellence outside tech (Phil Jackson's coaching, government problem-solving, consumer packaged goods) [1].

**Thick skin and thin skin simultaneously.** Founders must tune out external noise (press, Hacker News critics, market hype cycles) while remaining highly responsive to customer and team signals. "That's a weird dynamic that you have to handle" [1].

**Focus means killing things you love.** After expanding Dropbox into photos (Carousel) and email (Mailbox), Houston contracted -- killing both products after reading Andy Grove's "Only the Paranoid Survive." "Everybody loves the idea of focus, but what focus actually means is shooting stuff you love and turning down things you know you can make work" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Houston's contribution to the YC canon is the engineer-to-CEO transition story told with unusual specificity. His early meeting with a more experienced CEO -- where he tuned out talks about mission and values while mentally reviewing unfixed bugs -- is a canonical moment. "After hearing that 5 or 6 or 10 times, you realize, 'The only one here who's confused is me'" [1].

His insight that founders transition "from designing a system of code and algorithms, which is very orderly and discrete, to a system of people, and things like culture, values, all kinds of other things, are the programming that makes that work" captures a realization many technical founders struggle to articulate [1].

Houston also offers a clear model for viral growth at scale. The Dropbox referral program (two-sided free storage incentive) drove 30% of signups; shared folders drove another 20%. Half of all growth was viral -- a benchmark for consumer product growth [1].

## Key Frameworks

- **AARRR Metrics (via Dave McClure)**: Acquisition, Activation, Revenue, Retention, Referral. Houston used this as the operating framework for managing Dropbox's growth [1].
- **The 1/2/5-Year Question**: "What will I wish I had been learning, or what would I wish I had been doing?" Applied regularly to identify CEO skill gaps before they become crises [1].
- **People as Code**: Culture and values are the programming language for a system of people, analogous to code and algorithms for a system of software [1].
- **The Triangulation Method**: Learning from multiple domains (tech CEOs, sports coaches, government leaders, board members) to internalize management lessons faster than reading alone [1].
- **Strategic Contraction**: When expansion creates identity confusion, "put all your eggs in one basket and watch that basket" [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Dropbox (YC W07)](https://ycombinator.com/library/6S-on-starting-and-scaling-dropbox-yc-w07) -- Drew Houston, [[Sam Altman]] (2017)

title: Elon Musk
body:
# Elon Musk

Elon Musk is co-founder of PayPal, founder and CEO of SpaceX, CEO of Tesla Motors, co-founder of OpenAI, and founder of xAI and Neurolink. In a 2016 interview with [[Sam Altman]] [1] and a 2025 fireside chat with [[Garry Tan]] at AI Startup School [2], Musk discusses what problems are worth working on, how to evaluate impact, first principles thinking, and how to act in the face of fear.

## Worldview

**Usefulness as the optimization function.** Musk frames his career decisions around utility rather than prestige or financial return: "Whatever this thing is that you're trying to create, what would be the utility delta compared to the current state of the art times how many people it would affect" [1]. In 2025, he elaborates: "Try to be as useful as possible. It may sound trite, but it's so hard to be useful, especially to be useful to a lot of people. Where the area under the curve of total utility is like, how much have you been useful to your fellow human beings times how many people? It's like the physics definition of true work" [2].

**Technology does not automatically improve.** Musk pushes back against the assumption that technology naturally gets better over time: "It only gets better if smart people work like crazy to make it better. By itself technology, if people don't work on it, it actually will decline" [1]. He cites ancient Egypt (forgot how to build pyramids), Rome (forgot roadways and aqueducts), and rocketry (Saturn V to moon, then Space Shuttle to low orbit, then Space Shuttle retired -- "that trend basically trends to zero") [1].

**Ego-to-ability ratio.** In 2025, Musk introduces a failure mode framework: "A major failure mode is when ego to ability ratio is greater than one. If your ego to ability ratio gets too high, then you're going to break the feedback loop to reality. In AI terms, you'll break your reinforcement learning loop." The prescription: internalize responsibility, minimize ego, do whatever the task requires regardless of whether it is grand or humble [2].

**Fatalism reduces fear.** Musk says he feels fear "quite strongly" but acts despite it when "something is important enough, you believe in it enough" [1]. He describes a deliberate practice of fatalism: "If you just accept the probabilities, then that diminishes fear." When starting SpaceX, he thought the odds of success were less than 10%: "just accepted that actually probably I would just lose everything" [1].

## The Origin Stories

### Zip2 (1995)

Musk's first startup. He chose between a Stanford PhD in material science (working on ultra capacitors for EVs) and building something on "this thing most people have never heard of, called the internet." He asked his professor to defer for a quarter; the professor replied, "This is probably the last conversation we'll have." He was right [2].

Musk wrote the first (or one of the first) maps, directions, and internet yellow/white pages. He could not afford a web server, so he read the port directly. Could not afford a T1 line, so he drilled a hole through the floor of his $500/month office and ran a LAN cable to the ISP below. He and his brother slept in the office and showered at the YMCA [2].

He did not intend to start a company: "I tried to get a job at Netscape. I sent my resume in. Nobody responded. Then I tried hanging out in the lobby to see if I could bump into someone. But I was too shy to talk to anyone. So I'll just write software myself" [2].

Zip2 sold for $300 million. Musk received $20 million. The key mistake: too much board control from legacy media companies who "kept wanting to use our software in ways that made no sense" [2].

### X.com / PayPal

Musk reinvested almost all of his Zip2 proceeds. "I kept the chips on the table." He wanted to go direct to consumer after feeling Zip2's technology was "constrained by our customers" [2].

### SpaceX (2002)

Musk went to NASA's website to find out when people were being sent to Mars. "There was no date." He initially planned a philanthropic mission (greenhouse on Mars) but after learning more -- including trips to Russia to buy ICBMs ("that's an adventure -- you go and meet with Russian high command and say, 'I'd like to buy some ICBMs'") -- realized the problem was not insufficient will but insufficient affordable technology [2].

He started SpaceX expecting to fail: "Maybe a less than 10% chance of being successful, maybe 1%." When recruiting: "I said we're probably going to die. But there's a 12% chance we might not die." He became chief engineer "not because I wanted to, but because I couldn't hire anyone who was good. None of the good chief engineers would join because they're like, 'This is too risky'" [2].

The first three launches failed. "If the fourth one hadn't worked, I had no money left, and that would have been curtains." The fourth launch succeeded. NASA awarded a resupply contract right before Christmas. "I literally blurted out, 'I love you guys,' which is not normally what they hear" [2].

### Tesla and the 2008 Crisis

Simultaneously, Tesla's financing round failed mid-2008 after SpaceX's third launch failure. "Tesla financing round closed on the last hour of the last day that it was possible, which was 6 p.m. December 24th, 2008. We would have bounced payroll two days after Christmas if that round hadn't closed" [2].

The press ridiculed him: "'Internet guy aka fool is attempting to build a rocket company.' I was like, 'Yeah, admittedly it does sound improbable and I agree that it's improbable'" [2].

## First Principles Thinking

Musk provides a detailed example of first-principles analysis applied to rockets [2]:

The typical approach: look at historical rocket costs and assume new rockets cost similarly.

The first-principles approach: list constituent materials (aluminum, copper, carbon fiber, steel), determine their weight and cost per kilogram. "The raw materials of a rocket are only maybe one or two percent of the historical cost of a rocket. So the manufacturing must necessarily be very inefficient" [2].

An AI infrastructure example: in early 2025, xAI needed 100,000 H100s to train coherently. Suppliers estimated 18-24 months. Musk needed it in 6 months. Breaking it down: need a building (found a disused Electrolux factory in Memphis), need power (input was 15 MW, needed 150 MW -- rented generators), need cooling (rented a quarter of the mobile cooling capacity of the US), need power smoothing (modified Tesla Megapack software to handle 50% power drops in 100 milliseconds). They ran cabling operations in four shifts, 24/7, with Musk sleeping in the data center and doing cabling himself [2].

## Five Areas That Affect Humanity's Future

Identified in college 25 years before his 2016 interview [1]:

1. **AI** -- "Probably the single biggest item in the near term that's likely to affect humanity"
2. **Genetics** -- solving genetic diseases
3. **Brain-computer interface** -- solving the bandwidth constraint
4. **Making life multi-planetary**
5. **Accelerating the transition to sustainable energy**

## On Democratization of AI

Musk argues that the primary danger of AI is concentration of power: "No one company or small set of individuals has control over advanced AI technology. I think that is very dangerous" [1]. This was the stated motivation for co-founding OpenAI with Altman: "to help spread out AI technology so it doesn't get concentrated in the hands of a few" [1].

## On Digital Superintelligence (2025)

Musk predicts digital superintelligence (smarter than any human at anything) will arrive in 2025 or 2026. He estimates 10-20% chance of annihilation ("But look on the bright side, that's 80 to 90% probability of a great outcome"). His primary AI safety prescription: "A rigorous adherence to truth is the most important thing for AI safety." Forcing AI to believe things that are not true is what could make it dangerous [2].

He expects roughly 5-10 "deep intelligences" globally, perhaps 4 in the US. He predicts 5-10x as many humanoid robots as humans within 100 years [2].

On the Kardashev scale: humanity has harnessed 1-2% of Earth's energy. Mars self-sustainability is roughly 30 years away. "Being a multiplanetary species greatly increases the probable lifespan of civilization or consciousness or intelligence, both biological and digital" [2].

## On Neurolink

Not necessary for digital superintelligence but solves the input-output bandwidth constraint. Sustained human output is less than one bit per second (fewer than 86,400 symbols per day). Neural interfaces could enable multispectral vision (infrared, ultraviolet, radar) and dramatically augmented intelligence. Five humans have received read-only implants; vision implants (writing to the visual cortex) are expected within 6-12 months [2].

## On PhDs and Being Useful

Asked whether people who want to be useful should get PhDs, Musk answers: "Mostly not" [1]. He himself left a Stanford PhD program to start an internet company in 1995 because the timing seemed right and he "didn't want to do a Ph.D. at Stanford and watch it all happen" [1].

## How Musk Spends His Time

As of 2016, Musk spent 80% of his time on "engineering and design" and "developing next-generation product" at SpaceX and Tesla [1]. At SpaceX, Gwynne Shotwell manages legal, finance, sales, and general business while Musk works with engineering. His biggest realization that year: "What really matters is the machine that builds the machine -- the factory. And that is at least two orders of magnitude harder than the vehicle itself" [1].

## References

1. [Elon Musk on How to Build the Future](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6W-elon-musk-on-how-to-build-the-future) -- Elon Musk, [[Sam Altman]] (2016)
2. [Elon Musk: Digital Superintelligence, Multiplanetary Life, How to Be Useful](https://ycombinator.com/library/MX-elon-musk-digital-superintelligence-multiplanetary-life-how-to-be-useful) -- Elon Musk, [[Garry Tan]] (June 2025)

title: Diana Hu
body:
# Diana Hu

Diana Hu is a Group Partner at Y Combinator. She was co-founder and CTO of Escher Reality, a startup building an augmented reality SDK for game developers. Escher Reality was acquired by Niantic (makers of Pokemon Go), where Hu served as director of engineering heading the AR platform. Her advice draws on the full arc from idea to prototype to MVP to scaling systems to millions of users [1].

## Worldview

Hu's advice is centered on the specific challenges technical founders face. Her core themes:

**The technical founder is a partner, not a contractor.** When non-technical founders say "I need somebody to build my app," Hu responds: "That isn't going to cut it" [1]. A technical founder requires intense commitment and is involved in talking to users, not just building [1].

**Speed is the only thing that matters early.** Every principle Hu teaches serves the goal of moving faster. Prototypes in days, MVPs in weeks, tech choices optimized for iteration speed. "If there's only one takeaway from this whole talk, it is that startups move quickly" [1].

**Good enough beats perfect.** At a big company, you may be rewarded for the perfect architecture. "But not for a startup. You're going to have a bias towards action and moving quickly, and actually deciding with a lot of incomplete information" [1]. Technical debt is acceptable. Ugly code is acceptable. Chaos is the normal state [1].

**The Jedi Master and the beginner choose the same tools.** Hu presents the "midwit meme" applied to tech stacks: the beginner picks PHP/JavaScript because that is all they know; the mid-level engineer reaches for Kafka and Kubernetes; the expert picks PHP/JavaScript again because they recognize it enables faster iteration. "If you build a company and it works and you get users, you can solve your way out of it" -- as Facebook did by building HipHop to transpile PHP to C++ [1].

**Don't hire to build the MVP.** Hiring from an unknown pool takes over a month, slows you down, and prevents founders from developing product insights [1]. Justin.tv/Twitch was built by three technical founders before any hires were made [1].

**Tech debt is fine pre-PMF.** "You gotta get comfortable, a little bit, with the heat of your tech burning" [1]. Pokemon Go could not handle its launch traffic and nobody could log in. "That did not kill the company at all. In fact, to this day, Pokemon Go generates over a billion dollars in revenue" [1].

**The technical founder must stay close to users.** After launching, the temptation is to hunker down and build. Hu pushes back: "Your role as a technical founder is very different. You got to be involved in the journey and really understand the insights of why users stay or leave your products" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Hu speaks specifically to engineers who are used to big-company standards. Her advice is the technical founder's counterpart to [[Michael Seibel]]'s operational advice -- where Seibel addresses product process and customer interaction, Hu addresses architecture decisions, tech stack choices, and the evolving role of the CTO from coder to manager.

She is unusually concrete about what tools to use and not use at each stage (Figma for prototypes, Firebase for backend, Google Analytics for post-launch, not Kafka or Prometheus). Her examples are drawn from real YC companies (Optimizely, DoorDash, Stripe, WePay, Segment, Justin.tv, Escher Reality, Pokemon Go) with specific technical details.

In the Launching video, Hu addresses the fear of launching from a technical founder's perspective: the fear that "nobody shows up" means "I'm dumb" or "I'm wrong" [2]. She also identifies the comforting alternative to launching -- writing code -- as a form of avoidance [2].

## Key Frameworks

- **Three-Stage Model**: Ideating (prototype in days), Building to Launch (MVP in weeks), Launch to Iterate (ongoing iteration toward PMF) [1]
- **90/10 Solution**: Paul Buchheit's principle applied to MVPs -- restrict the product to limited dimensions, expect to rewrite 90% of the code [1]
- **Choose Tech for Iteration Speed**: The only tech choices that matter are those tied to customer promises; everything else should optimize for speed [1]
- **The Midwit Meme for Tech Stacks**: Beginners and experts converge on simple tools; intermediate engineers over-engineer [1]
- **Building vs. Fixing Balance**: Post-launch, technical founders must balance bug fixes, features, and tech debt -- fear the right things (getting to PMF), not rendering bugs [1]

## References

1. [Tips for Technical Startup Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Is-tips-for-technical-startup-founders) -- Diana Hu (2024)
2. [Why Startup Founders Should Launch Companies Sooner Than They Think](https://ycombinator.com/library/LG-why-startup-founders-should-launch-companies-sooner-than-they-think) -- Diana Hu et al. (2023)

title: Ryan Petersen
related: [[Flexport: From Solo Founder to Eight Billion Dollar Logistics Platform]]
body:
# Ryan Petersen

Ryan Petersen is the founder and CEO of Flexport, a technology-driven freight forwarding company valued at $8 billion that helps nearly 10,000 companies move $19 billion of goods across 112 countries [1]. [[Garry Tan]] was an early investor through both Y Combinator and Initialized Capital [1]. Petersen is also referenced by Tan as exemplifying how unique personal experience leads to [[Startup Ideas]] [2] and how replacing paper with software makes a startup fundamentally faster [3].

## Background and Entrepreneurial Formation

Petersen comes from an entrepreneurial family. His mother is "probably the world's leading expert on food safety" and ran a company employing about 100 PhDs. His father was a computer programmer who taught him to code. As children, Petersen and his brother earned their allowance by buying sodas at grocery stores and reselling them to his mother's company -- "and then my dad wrote software to invoice her" [1].

Before Flexport, Petersen ran businesses for 11 years without venture capital. He co-founded [ImportGenius.com](http://ImportGenius.com), one of the largest providers of shipping manifest data to the global logistics industry. During the early years of that company, he held three flexible side jobs simultaneously -- writing business school case studies for Columbia, teaching the GMAT, and doing SEO consulting -- to sustain himself while the startup developed [1]. This approach reflects his philosophy of designing life so "you can't fail" rather than accepting the 18-month VC runway pressure [1].

Petersen spent four years as Flexport's only employee before applying to YC or raising venture capital. He had worked in international trade for a decade, including living in China for two years importing motorbikes -- giving him what [[Jared Friedman]] calls "10 out of 10 founder/market fit" [2].

## On Scaling: Process vs. Bureaucracy

Petersen's most detailed thinking concerns the transition from small startup to large organization. He identifies a key insight: "Hatred of bureaucracy is not enough to keep bureaucracy away. You also need good process, good systems, great people, and clear strategies to keep the company moving fast" [1].

He frames the tension as finding "the edge between order and chaos" and staying on that line as the company grows [1]. Early in his career, he considered process to be "really corporate" and avoided it. At scale, he realized "the reason that those exist is to enable you to move fast. You'll actually get more bureaucracy from not having any process and policies and standards than you will from having too much" [1].

**Technology architecture as organizational architecture.** Petersen draws a direct parallel between service-oriented architecture (teams communicating via APIs, not direct database access) and organizational design (people owning single-threaded metrics, not coordinating with everyone). Both slow you down initially but enable speed at scale. He cites the Jeff Bezos API memo as "probably the most important document in the technology industry history" [1]. The Marine Corps expression captures it: "Slow is smooth and smooth is fast" [1].

**Team of teams.** Petersen's organizational ideal is "a team of teams" where each small team operates like its own startup. The challenge: giving people clear ownership of a metric they can control. He uses the example of decomposing net revenue into three teams -- buying freight, algorithmic pricing, and sales -- each fully focused on one component rather than everyone wanting to "own all the things" [1].

## On Capital Discipline

Petersen describes a recurring pattern: after every major fundraise, Flexport lost spending discipline. After a $20 million Series A, he immediately committed $100,000 to a trade show booth. After a billion-dollar Series D, "immediately, lost discipline, started overspending. Everybody could hire as many people as they wanted" [1]. After raising a $985 million Series E (while profitable), his "big fear" was the same pattern recurring [1].

His advice: "Money wants to get spent." Founders need "really tight capital allocation, accounting controls, budgetary process" -- which he previously would have considered corporate language [1].

## On Vision

Petersen's vision expanded over time. When he applied to YC, his vision was to be "TurboTax for customs." He once believed planning far into the future was "a sign you're gonna fail" [1]. He now holds a ten-year product roadmap, with the broader vision of making global trade as seamless as an electrical utility -- "When you flip a light switch in your house, you're actually controlling a power plant somewhere" [1].

He advises against premature certainty: "If a founder is too certain of exactly what the vision is and what the product is going to look like and how it's all going to work, they're probably wrong. The world is super messy, and you've got to get out there and iterate" [1].

Tan quotes Petersen calling sales "the most underrated profession" [4].

## On Founder Meddling

Petersen considers himself strong at marketing and recognizes this as a paradox: "The area that I think I'm good at, I probably hold Flexport back" because he meddled too much, preventing the marketing team from developing independently [1]. He observes that many founders create the same dynamic: the function they know best becomes the least developed part of the company because they cannot let go [1].

## References

1. [Flexport CEO Ryan Petersen on Scaling a Startup from Zero to $8B](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jo-garry-s-channel-flexport-ceo-ryan-petersen-on-scaling-a-startup-from-zero-to-8b) -- Ryan Petersen, [[Garry Tan]] (2021)
2. [Billion Dollar Startup Ideas](https://ycombinator.com/library/Ji-garry-s-channel-billion-dollar-startup-ideas) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)
3. [Billion Dollar Startups Are Better, Faster, or Cheaper](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jn-garry-s-channel-billion-dollar-startups-are-better-faster-or-cheaper) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)
4. [6 Skills for Successful Startup Founders](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jl-garry-s-channel-6-skills-for-successful-startup-founders) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: Alfred Lin
body:
# Alfred Lin

Alfred Lin is a partner at Sequoia Capital. Before Sequoia, he was the COO and Chairman of Zappos, the online shoe retailer known for its culture of exceptional customer service. At Zappos, he worked alongside CEO Tony Hsieh to develop and codify the company's ten core values [1].

## Worldview

**Culture is the primary input to company output.** Lin frames the company as a black box where results (financial, product, or otherwise) are the output. "One of the major inputs is the culture of the company" [1]. He cites research showing that the Fortune 100 Best Companies to Work For returned 11.8% annually from 1997 to 2003, nearly double the S&P 500 (6.8%) and Russell 3000 (6.04%) [1].

**Culture is a daily habit, not a statement.** Lin compares culture to fitness: "You just can't be fit if you don't do it as a daily habit. Eventually you get out of shape, then you get fat, and then you're like, 'I got to go on a crash diet to sort of get back into shape.' That doesn't quite work" [1].

**Values must be deeper than words.** When Zappos began codifying values, they started with 37 employee-submitted values and spent a year whittling them down to 10. The year-long process was necessary because single words like "honesty" or "teamwork" are meaningless -- "give me a break. Everybody wants the culture to be honest" [1]. Values require paragraphs of supporting definition.

## The Culture Framework

Lin defines company culture as: "Every day, the [core values] and [actions] of each member of the team in pursuit of our company [mission]" [1]. He cites Gandhi: "Your beliefs become your thoughts. Your thoughts become your words. Your words become your actions. Your actions become your habits. Your habits become your values. Your values become your destiny" [1].

### Why Culture Matters (FIRST)

Lin organizes the benefits of culture into an acronym [1]:

- **F**irst principles for decision-making
- **I**dentity alignment on values
- **R**esilience and stability to fall back on
- **S**howing what not to do (the more important function)
- **T**alent retention of the right employees

### How to Create Core Values

Lin's process [1]:
1. Ask the leader: what personal values are most important to you?
2. What values are most important to the business?
3. Who are the people you most like working with? What values do they have?
4. Who have you never liked working with? What values do they have? Consider the opposite.
5. Final check: values must support the mission, be credible, and be uniquely tied to the mission.

### Zappos Examples

**"Deliver WOW through service"**: Not generic customer service but a specific commitment to a "wow experience." The value was supported by a paragraph explaining exactly what "wow" meant for employees, customers, brand partners, and investors [1].

**"Be humble"**: Derived from the "opposite" exercise. The founders did not like working with arrogant people. Humility became a core value [1].

### The Five Dysfunctions of a Team

Lin references Patrick Lencioni's pyramid from "The Five Dysfunctions of a Team" to show how culture failures cascade [1]:

1. **Absence of trust** -- without trust, people cannot have honest debate
2. **Fear of conflict** -- without debate, teams cannot arrive at the right answers
3. **Lack of commitment** -- without good answers, people will not commit
4. **Avoidance of accountability** -- without commitment, people cannot be held accountable
5. **Inattention to results** -- without accountability, results suffer

### Culture in Practice

Lin advocates integrating culture into every operational process [1]:
- Interview for cultural fit, not just technical fit
- Include values in performance reviews
- Make culture a daily habit, not an annual exercise

At Zappos, the team philosophy prioritized company over department, department over team, team over individual. The goal was for everyone to improve any idea rather than fight over who was right: "The result is that the company gets a better idea, not that any individual person is right" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Lin is one of the few speakers in the YC canon who comes from an operational role (COO) at a company famous primarily for its culture. His contribution is translating abstract cultural principles into concrete, repeatable processes. The year-long Zappos values codification, the FIRST framework, and the integration of culture into hiring and performance reviews reflect an operator's mindset applied to a "soft" topic.

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Building Company Culture, Part I](https://ycombinator.com/library/JN-how-to-start-a-startup-building-company-culture-part-i) -- Brian Chesky, Alfred Lin (2014)

title: Ooshma Garg
related: [[Gobble: Five Years in the Trough of Sorrow]]
body:
# Ooshma Garg

Ooshma Garg is the founder and CEO of [Gobble](http://www.gobble.com) (YC W14), the dinner kit delivery service that invented the 10-minute, 1-pan dinner kit. She is a mechanical engineer by training and previously ran a startup at Stanford helping women get jobs at banks and consulting firms [1].

## Worldview

Garg's worldview is defined by persistence through prolonged uncertainty. She spent five and a half years iterating through four different product versions before finding [[Product-Market Fit]]. [[Paul Graham]] [tweeted](https://twitter.com/paulg/status/659749829040410624): "Ooshma Garg does not give up" [1].

She describes building a startup as "a daily game of Snakes and Ladders" -- two steps forward, two steps back. The snakes do not go away as the company grows; they become obstacles the founder can isolate and work around rather than existential threats [1].

She deliberately separated the company from the product: "A company is not just a product, a company is solving a problem. It's a mission. And if the mission is alive, you should keep working on it" [1]. This allowed Gobble to launch four different products while maintaining the same underlying mission.

## Key Frameworks

**GEM Framework**: Garg's mnemonic for founder resilience:
- **Grit** (from your core): The internal compass that tells you to keep going when no one else believes
- **Experiment** (from your head): Use data and iteration to find what works; a company can launch multiple products in service of one mission
- **Mission** (from your heart): The mission outlasts any product. Gobble's mission -- "create love through food" -- survived four pivots [1]

**The Startup Curve in Practice**: Garg maps Gobble's five-year journey onto [[Paul Graham]]'s startup curve: launch, trough of sorrow, wiggles of false hope, and (eventually) the promised land. She compares it to Airbnb's 2008-2010 graph, which showed a similar pattern before becoming smooth in retrospect [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Garg is unusual for openly describing the financial desperation of near-death -- showing a screenshot of her bank balance before YC money arrived, noting she had just made the last full payroll. "Starting and running a company, as a founder, is a very lonely experience. Sometimes, only you know the reality of the low times" [1].

She also demonstrates that joining YC at a later stage (with an existing product and team) can be valuable. "YC helped us get back to the basics. Even when you have a product and a team, founders focusing on the basics is amazing at any point and anytime in your company" [1].

Her fundraising lesson is concrete: both Trinity Ventures and Andreessen Horowitz said no twice before leading her Series A. "It's important to accept rejection with grace" [1].

## References

1. [On starting and scaling meal kit company Gobble](https://ycombinator.com/library/69-on-starting-and-scaling-meal-kit-company-gobble) -- Ooshma Garg (2016)

title: Aaron Epstein
body:
# Aaron Epstein

Aaron Epstein is a YC Group Partner and founder of Creative Market, a marketplace for graphic design assets. He went through YC in 2010. His advice covers the full range of early-stage commercialization: [[Business Models]], [[Cold Emails]], and [[Pricing and Monetization]] [1][2].

## Worldview

**Copy the business model, innovate the product.** Epstein analyzed the YC Top 100 and found that nearly every billion-dollar company uses one of nine proven business models. Founders who try to invent a new way to make money are solving the wrong problem [1].

**Get as close to the transaction as possible.** Advice he received during his own YC batch in 2010. Companies that literally process money (Stripe, Brex) become critical infrastructure. Companies far from the transaction (affiliates) struggle [1].

**Cold outreach is a system, not an art.** Epstein treats email outreach as a measurable funnel with known conversion rates. Founders who expect results from a handful of emails per week are under-investing. The math says: plan for hundreds of emails to get one customer [2].

## Distinct Perspective

Epstein combines quantitative frameworks (funnel math, business model categorization) with granular tactical advice (subject line examples, read-it-aloud tests, before/after email rewrites). He is unusually willing to show real emails he received and critique them publicly. His before-and-after rewrite of a smoothie company's outreach email demonstrates how reframing from the sender's needs to the recipient's benefit transforms response rates [2].

## Key Frameworks

- **The Nine Business Models**: SaaS, transactional, marketplace, subscription, enterprise, usage-based, e-commerce, advertising, hard tech/bio [1]
- **The Cold Email Funnel**: Sent → Opened → Responded → Demo → Customer, with quantified conversion rates at each step [2]
- **Seven Principles of Effective Email Copy**: Focused goal, be human, personalize, keep it short, establish credibility, make it about the reader, clear call to action [2]
- **Uncommon Commonalities**: Find things uniquely in common with the recipient that signal genuine research and connection [2]

## References

1. [YC Guide to Business Models](https://ycombinator.com/library/Gh-yc-guide-to-business-models) -- Aaron Epstein (n.d.)
2. [How To Convert Customers With Cold Emails](https://ycombinator.com/library/LZ-how-to-convert-customers-with-cold-emails) -- Aaron Epstein (n.d.)

title: Alan Kay
body:
# Alan Kay

Alan Kay is a computer scientist, Turing Award winner, and one of the pioneers of personal computing. He was a key researcher at Xerox PARC, where he helped develop Smalltalk, the graphical user interface, and the Dynabook concept. He delivered a two-part lecture in Sam Altman's Stanford "How to Start a Startup" series on how transformational technology gets invented. See [[Inventing the Future]] for the full treatment [1].

## Worldview

**Context beats IQ.** Kay's most fundamental principle: "Context or point of view is worth 80 IQ points." A person who learns calculus with an IQ of 110 can do things the greatest geniuses of antiquity cannot. Leonardo da Vinci could not invent a motor; Henry Ford built millions of cars. The difference was the century they were born into, not their intelligence [1].

**Reality is a low-pass filter.** Ideas get degraded as they pass through human institutions. "You have to have enormous ideas in order so that people not understanding them will retain something. You don't want the low pass filter to give you back a dial tone" [1].

**Humans are fundamentally blind.** Kay uses hands-on demonstrations (blind spot experiments, size constancy illusions, Shepard tables) to show that human perception routinely manufactures false information. "The natural state of humanity is to be crazy, because crazy is not having good models in our mind for what's going on out there." The most dangerous version: people who think they can see. "We can't learn to see until we realize and admit that we're blind" [1].

**Good funders give gold medals ahead of time.** The quality of results in technology research correlates most strongly not with the researchers but with the funders. Good funders award resources before results, accepting that 70% will fail [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Kay is unique among YC speakers in that he comes from the research tradition rather than the startup tradition. His critique runs deeper than product advice -- he challenges the assumptions underlying modern computing itself. He argues that the web browser is "one of the worst things ever done in computing" because it ignores the fact that users are on a computer, and that all three major operating systems are based on ideas from before 1965 [1].

His challenge to entrepreneurs: the easiest way to make money is to build "technological amplifiers" for genetic human cravings (social connection, stories, revenge, news). The harder but more important path is to build technology that serves humanity's invented needs (progress, scientific thinking, legal systems) without appealing to the parts of human nature "that they're helpless in front of" [1].

## References

1. [How to Invent the Future (Part 2)](https://ycombinator.com/library/76-how-to-invent-the-future-part-2) -- Alan Kay (2014)

title: Diane Greene
body:
# Diane Greene

Diane Greene is a serial founder and technology executive. She co-founded and served as CEO of three startups: Vxtreme (sold to Microsoft for $75 million in 1997), VMware (IPO in 2007 with $19.1 billion market cap at close), and Bebop (sold to Google for $380 million in 2015). She later served as SVP of Google Cloud. Before her startup career, she worked at Sybase, Tandem, and SGI. She was also a tech investor who put seed money into Cloudera, Pure Storage, and Unity Technologies [1].

## Worldview

Greene's advice draws on a recurring analogy between sailing and company-building. A competitive sailor (she won the women's national dinghy sailing championships, raced long distance to Bermuda, and windsurfed across the open ocean from Maui to Molokai), she finds direct parallels between navigating a race and navigating a startup [1].

**Plan, then adapt in real time.** "Building a company is so much like racing a sailboat. You need a plan and then the ability to reevaluate the plan in real time as new information is acquired. Suddenly, the wind changes or competitors are stronger than you thought they'd be" [1].

**Presence and decisiveness.** "When you campaign a sailboat, you need to be in the moment. You need to let go of your self-doubt, and yet you can't lose the ability to question your decisions. Most of all, when it's time to make a decision -- a competitor's come upon you or the wind shifted -- you make it. The race won't wait for you" [1].

**Crew selection is paramount.** "It's impossible to be an effective skipper if you don't have the right people working harmoniously in the right roles" [1].

## Lessons from Each Company

### Vxtreme (1995)

Greene learned that founders need to be solidly aligned and that limiting the number of founders improves outcomes: "Too many cooks can spoil the broth." The most important lesson: "No one is indispensable, and it's essential to get the right mix of people, talents, and personalities" [1].

### VMware (1998)

VMware taught Greene what it takes to bring a disruptive innovation to market. The critical insight: "It's essential to design and market a major innovation in a way that makes its adoption non-disruptive. That makes it easy to integrate with an established order, an established way of doing things, even if the innovation ultimately supplants that order" [1].

A concrete example: VMware built a tool that would take a workload running on a physical machine and place it in a virtual machine where everything ran identically. "That was a phenomenal sales tool for us" [1].

When VMware learned it could not sell through IBM due to IP issues, the team turned the setback into an advantage by enlisting IBM's star resellers to take VMware to market instead [1].

VMware also taught Greene the importance of corporate culture: fostering a sense of importance and value for each person and their role, and creating an open culture for communication and collaboration. "We designed our corporate campus with exactly these goals in mind" [1].

### Bebop (2013)

At Bebop, Greene's original co-founder departed, and the team's first design attempts were "just off." Greene credits tenacity: "We persevered, and Bebop became a powerhouse for design thinking and for tools to build enterprise applications. Tenaciousness really pays off" [1].

### Google Cloud

Running Google Cloud taught Greene that "it's possible to build an enterprise startup inside a mega company." The same principles applied: culture, getting the right people in the right roles, and never losing focus on creating value for customers and partners [1].

## Core Philosophy

Greene distills her philosophy: "The bottom line in sailing a boat or building a company is that you give it your all. You give it your all, not because you're supposed to, not because that's what makes you win, but because you have respect for your goal and you enjoy the process" [1].

She reframes success as a byproduct: "You may make a fortune, but the fortune will be incidental. If your goals are worthy, it's the process that will count, and that understanding will be your greatest source of strength no matter what the adversity" [1].

## References

1. [Diane Greene's Advice for Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6Q-diane-greene-s-advice-for-founders) — Diane Greene (2017)

title: Dylan Field
body:
# Dylan Field

Dylan Field is the co-founder of Figma, the collaborative design tool that Adobe acquired for $20 billion [1]. He dropped out of Brown University to start the company. [[Garry Tan]] profiles his journey as a canonical example of a "10-year overnight success" -- the compound effect of navigating thousands of small decisions over a decade [1].

## Worldview

**Explore new technology, but walk toward a real problem.** Field and his co-founder Evan Wallace started in "exploration mode" in 2012, knowing they wanted to do something with WebGL (a new way to use the GPU in the browser) and creative tools. They cared about accessibility: "We agreed with a statement that Bret Victor made, which is that if you can't use a tool because it's too complex, it's a moral wrong" [1]. The danger of technology-first exploration is building something meaningless -- they briefly worked on a meme generator before Field looked in the mirror and said, "I dropped out of Brown for this?" [1].

**Management is a skill, not a personality trait.** Field describes management as "a very different skill from leadership. They're related, but they're different. And management is a skill that you have to build just like any other skill" [1]. A year into Figma, he was struggling with it: he made hires that were not the right cultural fit, he micromanaged, and he was not empowering people enough. "When you hire smart people, you got to give them a way to really contribute and to blow you away and to let them do things that you would never think of" [1].

The crisis peaked when employees confronted him directly: "People were really unhappy with me. And it was a moment where, you know, they're like, 'Okay, we really need you to get help'" [1]. This happened while Figma still had not launched and while Field's father was dying of cancer. The resolution came from hiring the company's first outside manager, from whom Field learned management skills. Tan frames this as the universal challenge for technical founders who want to remain CEO: "Every highly talented technical founder who starts and wants to stay in that CEO position for the long haul will end up having to learn what Dylan learned" [1].

## References

1. [Figma's $20B, 10 Year Overnight Success](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jp-garry-s-channel-figma-s-20b-10-year-overnight-success) -- [[Dylan Field]], [[Garry Tan]] (2022)

title: Emmett Shear
body:
# Emmett Shear

Emmett Shear is co-founder of Justin.tv and Twitch, and a YC Partner. Before Twitch, he co-founded Kiko Calendar (his first startup, sold on eBay) with Justin Kan right out of college. He then co-founded Justin.tv, a live streaming reality TV show that evolved into a general broadcasting platform. Shear led the gaming initiative that became Twitch, which was acquired by Amazon.

His contribution to the YC knowledge base spans two areas: product development methodology (how to decide what to build next) and user interview technique (how to learn what users actually need). His Twitch experience provides one of the most detailed case studies of user research in the YC canon.

## Worldview

**User interviews tell you where to go; data tells you where you are.** At Justin.tv, the team spent extensive time analyzing Google Analytics, Mixpanel, in-house tools, and traffic sources. Shear's conclusion: data is useful for understanding current behavior but "it doesn't tell you where you need to go. It doesn't tell you what the problems are you need to address." Without user interviews, "nine times out of ten, that idea turns out to be bad" [2].

**Who you talk to matters as much as what you ask.** Selecting the right interview subjects is the first and most consequential decision. For Twitch, the team determined that broadcasters (not viewers) were the strategic users, because viewers followed content to whatever platform the broadcaster chose [2]. "If we'd gone and talked to viewers only, we would have gotten a completely different set of feedback" [2].

**Listen for problems, not features.** Users will ask for polls, scrolling text, and ban list management. These are not the real problems. The real problems were: broadcasters wanted to make money, video quality was poor, and non-users could not start broadcasting because their computers were not powerful enough. Twitch "dumped almost all resources into things that no one ever mentioned in an interview, but those were the things that actually addressed the problem" [2].

**Validation is an anti-goal.** Shear is skeptical of idea validation: "Validating an idea you already have is an anti-goal. You can't improve an idea through any amount of validation" [1]. The goal of customer research is to generate better ideas, not to confirm existing ones.

## Distinct Perspective

Shear is rare in providing both a systematic product prioritization framework and a detailed user interview methodology. Most YC advice focuses on what to build (something people want) rather than how to decide what to build next or how to learn what users actually need.

His candor about failures is distinctive. He openly describes Kiko Calendar as a failure born from never talking to calendar users, and Justin.tv's stagnation from building based on data and brainstorms rather than user conversations. The contrast with Twitch -- where systematic user research drove three years of product decisions -- makes the case experientially rather than theoretically.

He is also the only YC speaker to provide a live user interview demonstration, walking through the process with a student volunteer to illustrate techniques like open-ended questioning, avoiding feature discussions, and digging into current behavior [2].

## Key Frameworks

**Three Prioritization Frameworks**: Built For Me (founder as exemplar user), Switch To Us (interview-driven competitive displacement), and Three Numbers Matter (metric-driven focus areas). Startups progress through them in order [1].

**Three Groups of Interviewees**: Existing users (reveal tolerable pain points), competitor users (reveal dealbreakers), and non-users (reveal barriers to market expansion). Each produces fundamentally different and complementary insights [2].

**The Three-Number Rule**: Three is the maximum number of focus areas people can keep in mind consistently [1].

**The Conversion Loop**: People interviewed during user research become the first product converts when their feedback is acted on, because "most people have never had that experience" of talking to a product team and seeing their input ship [2].

## Three Common User Interview Mistakes

1. Showing people your product (puts ideas in their head instead of drawing out what is already there)
2. Asking about a pet feature direction ("Would you use X?")
3. Talking to who is available rather than who is strategically important [2]

## References

1. [How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) -- Emmett Shear (n.d.)
2. [How to Start a Startup: Talking to Users](https://ycombinator.com/library/JQ-how-to-start-a-startup-talking-to-users) -- Emmett Shear (2014)

title: Peter Reinhardt
related: [[Segment: From Classroom Tool to Customer Data Platform]]
body:
# Peter Reinhardt

Peter Reinhardt is co-founder and CEO of Segment (YC S11), a customer data platform. He went through YC in 2011 and grew Segment from four people to 150 in three and a half years. Before Segment, he studied at MIT and Rhode Island School of Design. Segment was later acquired by Twilio for $3.2 billion in 2020.

## Worldview

Reinhardt's central conviction is that the market always wins, regardless of the founder's vision. He has lived this lesson through three product attempts -- a classroom lecture tool, an analytics product, and the analytics.js library that became Segment.
