The first two started from a vision of the future and failed. The third started from 580 lines of code solving a real problem and succeeded [1].

His framework rests on several interconnected beliefs:

**Founders systematically overvalue vision and undervalue market demand.** Reinhardt directly challenges Alan Kay's advice to imagine the future and pull it back to the present: "I'd actually wager that not a single successful company has actually been founded by doing that" [1]. Finding [[Product-Market Fit]] is "a very inductive process. You're going back and forth with customers, and you eventually find something that works" [1].

**False hope is more dangerous than failure.** The emotional grind of the PMF search creates a bias toward seeing mirages. Reinhardt lost ten pounds in three weeks and was hospitalized twice for panic attacks during Segment's search. The specific danger is that "glimmers of hope" -- vague interest from potential customers, a stray website chat, edge-case feature requests -- are mistaken for genuine demand [1].

**PMF is unmistakable.** Reinhardt's most quoted formulation: "Product market fit feels like stepping on a land mine" (credited to the Dropbox founders). "If you are at all questioning whether you have product market fit or not, you don't" [1]. When Segment found PMF with analytics.js, every metric went haywire simultaneously -- Hacker News upvotes, GitHub stars, unsolicited LinkedIn messages demanding beta access, pull requests within 48 hours.

**Kill bad ideas fast, not slow.** The bigger problem is not generating ideas but eliminating bad ones quickly. Reinhardt admires Codecademy's founders for trying 12 ideas in seven weeks during YC, then launching the winning idea four days before Demo Day [1].

**The foothold matters more than the TAM.** Once a startup finds one problem it can solve, customers bring adjacent problems. "It's actually almost trivial to find the next thing and the next thing" [1]. Segment's 580-line library became a platform with six-figure annual contracts.

## Key Frameworks

**The PMF Continuum.** Reinhardt describes the path to a category-leading company as: find any foothold (PMF), reach $1M revenue ("impossible"), reach $10M ("improbable"), reach $100M ("inevitable"), then build a platform [1].

**Idle Interest vs. Genuine Demand.** Reinhardt distinguishes sharply between polite interest ("I totally understand what the value is" = "doesn't care") and genuine demand ("Wait, sorry, you can do what?" followed by immediate action) [1].

**The Training Set Analogy.** Without positive examples of PMF, founders lack a reference point. Reinhardt frames this as a machine learning problem: negative training examples (failed products) provide little signal, but one positive example (a product that works) dramatically improves pattern recognition [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Reinhardt is one of the few speakers in the YC canon who describes PMF failure in clinical, emotionally honest detail -- including hospitalization, weight loss, and the specific cognitive traps (edge-case feature requests, polite customer meetings, stray website chats) that led him to believe he had found PMF when he had not. His contribution is less about what to do (the standard advice: talk to customers, iterate) and more about what false PMF looks and feels like, making it easier for other founders to recognize the difference.

## Board Management

In a separate context, Reinhardt is cited for his practice of writing 3-5 page memos to the board covering highlights, lowlights, and time allocation analysis, including his own strengths and weaknesses as CEO [2].

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Finding Product-Market Fit](https://ycombinator.com/library/86-how-to-start-a-startup-finding-product-market-fit) -- Peter Reinhardt (2017)
2. [How to Create and Manage a Board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board) -- [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)

title: Patrick Collison
body:
# Patrick Collison

Patrick Collison is the co-founder and CEO of Stripe, a payments infrastructure company. He and his brother [[John Collison]] started Stripe while both were in college. The company grew from two co-founders to over 170 employees by the time of this lecture and has since scaled to thousands. The Collisons are Irish and immigrated to the United States.

## Worldview

**Culture solves the bandwidth problem.** Collison views culture as the mechanism that maintains consistency as founders can participate in an exponentially shrinking fraction of company decisions. "Culture is kind of how you -- what the strands are that you want to have, the invariances that you want to maintain, as you can get specifically involved in fewer and fewer decisions over time" [1].

**Transparency as a first principle.** Stripe made radical internal transparency a core cultural value from its earliest days. Every outgoing email was BCC'd to everyone else unless the sender opted out. Collison's reasoning: if everyone shares the mission and has access to information about the current state of the company, "that gets you a huge amount of the way there in terms of working productively together. And it kind of forgives a lot of the other things that tend to break as you grow" [1]. As the company scaled past 170 people, Stripe invested in tooling (mailing list architecture, Gmail filter generation, Hackpad for document transparency, weekly all-hands decks) and cultural norms (when it is appropriate to jump into discussions, how to avoid drive-by criticism that discourages sharing) to maintain the practice [1].

**Think like a value investor when hiring.** The Collisons explicitly framed early hiring as looking for undervalued human capital: "You're looking for the human capital that's significantly undervalued by the market." Stripe's first designer was 18, in high school in Sweden. Their CTO was in college. "You can relax one constraint -- relax the fact that it's apparent that they're talented" [1].

**Time horizons grow with the company.** After one month of operation, a founder plans one month ahead. After four years, four years ahead. This has direct implications for hiring: early on, people must be immediately productive. Later, investing in people who will be ready in a year or two becomes necessary and beneficial [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Collison provides one of the clearest articulations of how to scale culture while acknowledging its inherent limitations. He describes a rapidly growing company as "a very unnatural thing" and frames the question not as "how do we do this perfectly?" but "what's the least bad way of managing that period of growth?" [1]. His candor about imperfection is distinctive: "You probably can't do much better than sort of adequate" [1].

His emphasis on the first ten hires as a 100-person decision is a multiplicative framing: "You're not just hiring those first ten people. You're actually kind of hiring a hundred people because you should think of each one of those people as bringing along sort of another ten people with them" [1].

## Key Contributions

- **Four dimensions of culture**: who you hire and what they value, what you do every day and why, what you communicate, what you celebrate (and punish) [1]
- **Work trials over interviews**: For the first ten hires, work with candidates for about a week before extending an offer. "It's pretty hard to fake it for a week" [1]
- **Three traits of great early hires**: genuine and straightforward, enjoy completing things (not just starting), care intensely about quality [1]
- **The "best among friends" test**: "Is this person probably the best out of all of their friends at what they do?" [1]
- **Growing leaders internally**: Several of Stripe's first ten people moved into leadership roles. "People don't necessarily come out of the womb being good at managing. Being able to develop that in people is a lot of work, but it's damaging if the company can't develop that skill" [1]
- **Startups are not as brutal as the mythology**: "Startups, especially in the earlier days, tend to involve somewhat longer working hours. But there's kind of this tendency to exaggerate it. It's the startup version of fishing" [1]

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Building Company Culture, Part II](https://ycombinator.com/library/JO-how-to-start-a-startup-building-company-culture-part-ii) -- [[Ben Silbermann]], Patrick Collison, [[John Collison]] (2014)

title: Aaron King
body:
# Aaron King

Aaron King is the founder and CEO of [[Snapdocs: Navigating Market Cycles|Snapdocs]] (YC W14), the leading digital closing platform for the mortgage industry, valued at $1.5B. He built the company with minimal outside funding through multiple market cycles.

## Worldview

King's philosophy centers on discipline forged by constraints. He argues that capital efficiencycapital efficiency]] is not just a necessity for bootstrapped companies but a virtue that should persist even after raising large rounds. Capital constraints forced good behaviors — ruthless prioritization, deep customer involvement, short feedback loops — that served Snapdocs well even after raising more funding [1].

He views market cycles as natural and unavoidable rather than threatening. His approach is to selectively ignore some market changes while reacting quickly to others, filtered through alignment with resources, vision, and a north star metric (market share growth) [1].

King is candid about the cost of his approach: the company built many things not engineered for scale or flexibility. But he frames this as a worthwhile trade because "we can afford to reengineer those unscalable solutions because we built something people want" [1].

## Key Frameworks

### Selective Ignoring
Not every market change requires a response. When well-resourced and growing, ignore downturns and stay focused on market share. When the market demands trendy technology that underlying factors will prevent from scaling, ignore it or invest minimally — even over the protests of the sales team [1].

### Concentric Circles for Decision Communication
When rapid directional changes are needed, work outward: start with 3-4 people for the hardest conversations, expand to senior leadership to arm them for communication, then hold a company-wide meeting. This process took about two weeks during Snapdocs' COVID pivot [1].

### Effectiveness vs. Efficiency Toggle
Companies should ask: "Is what I did yesterday the thing that's going to get me to where I need to be tomorrow?" At times, prioritize effectiveness (expand aggressively). At others, return to efficiency (ruthless prioritization). The toggle is driven by market conditions [1].

### Intellectual Honesty About Growth
Resist taking credit during up cycles. Message to the team: the industry works in cycles, do not overly congratulate yourselves now, and do not be too hard on yourselves during inevitable downturns [1].

## Distinct Perspective

King's perspective is shaped by building a company in the mortgage industry — a sector defined by dramatic cyclicality driven by interest rates and economic conditions. Most startup advice assumes continuous growth; King's experience provides a rare lens on how to build through contraction and expansion. His advice on hiring (be fearless about recruiting talented people into risky startups) and on transparency (share cash balance, growth rate, burn, and biggest challenges with the entire team) reflect someone who learned by doing rather than theorizing.

## References

1. [Snapdocs' Aaron King on Navigating Market Cycles](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Gu-snapdocs-aaron-king-on-navigating-market-cycles) — Aaron King, Lindsay Amos (2022)

title: Vinod Khosla
body:
# Vinod Khosla

Vinod Khosla is the founder of Khosla Ventures and the founding CEO of Sun Microsystems. Before Sun, he co-founded Daisy Systems, which went public in the 1980s and raised $100 million. At Sun, the first 15-20 employees included Eric Schmidt (later CEO of Google), Carol Bartz (later CEO of Autodesk and Yahoo), Bill Joy, and Andy Bechtolsheim -- each of whom went on to build or lead billion-dollar companies. Khosla's interview with [[Sam Altman]] in the "How to Build the Future" series is a concentrated argument that company-building is almost entirely about people selection.

## Worldview

Khosla's central thesis is that "a company becomes the people it hires, not the plan it makes" [1]. A business plan is "completely irrelevant, other than to judge how somebody's thought about a problem. Not what they're going to do" [1]. The people you assemble determine not just execution but the quality of future decisions, because those people form the "kitchen cabinet" -- the group you turn to when facing hard, ambiguous, uncertain decisions [1].

This conviction drives everything else in his philosophy: how to pick investors, how to allocate equity, what to look for in hires, and how to think about ambition.

## The $0 Million vs. $0 Billion Mindset

Khosla's framework for founding ambition: a company at zero revenue with a $0 million mindset will hire tactically, make short-term deals, and optimize for near-term survival. The same company at zero revenue with a $0 billion mindset will build the team and infrastructure needed for a decades-long journey. "When you set out on a journey, your mindset determines who you bring onboard, how you approach it, what you set up, what deals you do, which investors you've got" [1].

He uses the Mt. Everest analogy: you must be "obstinate about your vision" (reaching the summit) but "flexible about tactics" (the route to base camp, camp one, camp two). The danger is setting up base camp in the wrong place -- generating revenue and stability that does not position you for the actual summit [1].

## Gene Pool Engineering: Equity as a Weapon

Khosla argues that insufficient equity allocation is the single most important failure mode in the first six months of a company [1]. His positions are specific:

**At Sun**, the first three or four founders kept less than half the common stock -- roughly 25-27% -- with an equal or larger chunk reserved for future hires and about 40% for investors after the Series A [1].

**His son's company** (Curai, an AI healthcare company): Khosla advised his son to keep 15% for himself instead of 45%, recruit a co-founder at 15%, and leave the rest for hiring great engineers. In AI, where the best engineers make million-dollar salaries at Google and Uber, you have to offer 3-5% each. "If you don't do that, you're including only the people who couldn't start their own company, who won't help you evolve your plan" [1].

**The magnet principle**: Some hires are worth their equity even if they did zero work, because they attract other exceptional people. Bill Joy at Sun was "an incredible magnet." Khosla does not think Eric Schmidt would have joined him at 25 years old without the draw of working alongside Joy and Bechtolsheim [1].

[[Sam Altman]] reinforces this: "Being super generous with early employee equity in getting founder quality people as the first 10 employees. All the evidence is on the side of doing this, and yet almost no one does, so there's a huge edge if you're willing to do it" [1].

Khosla calls insufficient option pools his "single biggest beef with YC" [1]. He acknowledges it is counterintuitive: both founders and investors want to minimize the pool and maximize their own percentage. But "they would like to own a higher percentage, but the pie is much smaller" [1].

## Whose Advice to Trust on What Topic

Khosla told Joe Kraus (founder of Excite): "The single hardest decision you'll make is whose advice to trust on what topic" [1]. This extends to investors, board members, and executives.

The qualification to advise an entrepreneur is having built a large company yourself. "Just because you got an MBA and joined a venture firm doesn't mean you're qualified to advise an entrepreneur" [1]. Most investors fail this test. Khosla's assessment: "90% of investors add no value. 70% of investors add negative value to a company" [1]. They give contradictory advice ("improve quality," then "ship faster," then "spend less money") without understanding the trade-offs, because they have never faced gut-wrenching decisions under ambiguity [1].

He describes the near-bankruptcy experience at Sun: monitors from Philips were breaking every 30 days, and for a month or two the earliest he went home was 3 AM and the latest he was back was 7 AM. "Unless you've felt that gut-wrenching decision, you can't advise an entrepreneur" [1].

## Hiring for Rate of Learning, Not Experience

The most important quality in a hire is rate of learning, not domain experience. "Experience doesn't matter, the rate of learning matters. First principle's thinking matters. Pick for the best athlete, not the person who's the most established wide receiver who knows how to run one pattern" [1].

Khosla's evaluation method: give candidates a brand-new problem they have no experience with and observe how they think through it from first principles. He also uses the question: "If I gave you $10 million today, what three startups would you consider, and what are the reasons as an investor you wouldn't want to invest in those?" [1].

For startup founders, the strongest signal is rate of change during a defined period. Altman confirms this is his "number one" indicator: "How fast did they evolve their plan, change their plan?" [1].

When hiring senior executives, the functional competence question ("can you do marketing?") is secondary to the cross-functional question: "What are the questions he will ask? How will he make the CFO better or the VP of engineering better through the questions they ask?" [1].

A specific example: Scott McNealy started at Sun as VP of Manufacturing. Khosla made the gut call to move him to VP of Sales based on behavioral signals, not background. "You have to make those gut calls. It's a very non-intuitive gut call" [1].

## On Investors

"An investor is an employee who you can't fire, and that's how you should think about it" [1]. Khosla advises founders to optimize for investors who care about their vision and understand their technical approach, not those who want 3-4x returns and early liquidity. To evaluate: talk to founders who had hiccups on ambitious paths and ask how the investor behaved when things went wrong [1].

Altman adds that the worst board behavior is adding stress during the moments that are already the most stressful -- sending press clippings of competitors, catastrophizing about death. The best investors are "calming you down, supporting you" during hard risk decisions [1].

## Ambition and Unreasonable People

Khosla has never had a founder pitch an idea he considered too ambitious. His response to ambitious ideas: "That's ambitious. That's awesome. Build the team for it. Now, what is step one, two and three?" [1]. Vision and tactics must coexist: "The only recipe I've ever seen work for making really impactful companies is both the giant vision and a good step one, two, and three," Altman confirms [1].

Khosla's position on management: "If you're reasonable, you won't do unreasonable things. It's just by definition." Great managers will take a great idea and turn it into "a good one, and execute a decent idea at lower risk." For ambitious companies, you need a "think tank of unreasonable people" at the top, with reasonable people layered below to optimize within the macro ideas [1].

## Reinventing Societal Infrastructure

At 60, Khosla wrote a 50-page document called "Reinventing Societal Infrastructure with Technology." The exercise: identify which parts of non-governmental US GDP could be reinvented with technology. He expected to find a small fraction. Instead, "there's no part of non-governmental US GDP that one can't innovate on in ways that are not five, 10% improvements, but are 100 to 1,000% improvements" [1].

The goal: give seven billion people the lifestyle of the top 700 million (energy-rich, healthcare-rich, education-rich, transportation-rich) without destroying the planet. He reports being able to envision a path for every major sector. "Only thing I'm short is entrepreneurs" [1].

Examples span food (Impossible Foods -- "I said yes in our very first meeting. It was just a vision too big to not attempt"), rockets (Rocket Lab), construction (3D printed buildings with 80% less material), and everything computation enables [1].

## References

1. [Vinod Khosla on How to Build the Future](https://ycombinator.com/library/6N-vinod-khosla-on-how-to-build-the-future) -- Vinod Khosla, [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)

title: Stanley Tang
body:
# Stanley Tang

Stanley Tang is a co-founder of DoorDash (YC S13). He graduated from Stanford in 2014 with a degree in Computer Science, building DoorDash while still a student. He and co-founder Andy Fang conceived the idea after interviewing small business owners in Palo Alto and discovering that delivery was a massive unsolved pain point [1].

## Worldview

Tang approaches startup building as experimentation. His three lessons from starting DoorDash [1]:

1. **Test your hypothesis.** Treat startup ideas like experiments, not commitments.
2. **Launch fast.** DoorDash's first version -- paloaltodelivery.com -- launched in under an hour with PDF menus and a personal cell phone number.
3. **Do things that don't scale.** Unscalable tactics are a competitive advantage in the earliest stage.

## The DoorDash Origin Story

Tang was passionate about building technology for small business owners. While interviewing Chloe, the owner of a macaron store in Palo Alto, she revealed "pages and pages of delivery orders" she had to turn down because she had no delivery infrastructure [1]. Over the following weeks, Tang and his co-founders talked to 150-200 small business owners. When they raised the idea of delivery, merchants "kept agreeing" that it was a huge unsolved problem [1].

The founders' concern: if delivery is so obvious, why has nobody solved it? They hypothesized that previous attempts may have failed because consumer demand did not exist. To test this cheaply, they built a landing page in an afternoon with PDF menus from Palo Alto restaurants and a phone number at the bottom -- their personal cell phones [1].

They were not expecting anything. Driving home, they received their first call: someone wanted Thai food. "We're like, we're not doing anything right now. Might as well swing by, pick up some Pad Thai, and deliver it" [1]. The customer turned out to be the author of a book called "Weed the People." The next day, two calls. Then five, then seven, then ten [1].

The validation signal: people were willing to endure a terrible user experience -- looking up PDF menus, calling a phone number -- to get delivery. "When people are willing to put up with all this, we knew we found the need" [1].

## Doing Things That Don't Scale at DoorDash

The co-founders were the delivery drivers, customer support, and marketers. They charged customers through Square, tracked orders in Google Docs, and monitored driver locations with Apple's Find My Friends [1]. At one point, Square shut down their account on suspicion of money laundering due to the rapid pace of small transactions. Co-founder Tony Xu, who had previously worked at Square, resolved it by emailing former colleagues [1].

Tang personally emailed every new customer each night: "How was your first delivery? How did you hear about this?" He personalized each email -- "If I see someone order chicken skewers from Orange Hummus, I would say, 'I love Orange Hummus. How were the chicken skewers?'" [1].

The unscalable work doubled as domain education: driving taught them how delivery logistics worked, dispatching taught them what the driver assignment algorithm should look like, and customer support provided real-time feedback [1].

## The Ice Cream Test

During YC, the co-founders received a text that they needed drivers on the road due to a demand spike. They had been about to try a new ice cream store. They debated for "maybe ten seconds" before choosing to deliver. "That became our motivation for scaling: if we can scale, then you could go get ice cream next time" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Tang is distinctive for demonstrating extreme speed-to-launch -- under an hour from concept to live product. His approach is empirical: build the cheapest possible test, observe real behavior, and only invest in technology after validating demand. His framing of startup ideas as experiments rather than commitments aligns with [[Dalton Caldwell]]'s framework on [[Creating Luck]] through rapid iteration.

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Getting Started, Getting Press, and Doing Things That Don't Scale](https://ycombinator.com/library/JL-how-to-start-a-startup-getting-started-getting-press-and-doing-things-that-don-t-scale) -- Stanley Tang, Walker Williams, Justin Kan (2014)

title: Sarah Nahm
related: [[Lever: Building Recruiting Software from the Inside Out]]
body:
# Sarah Nahm

Sarah Nahm is the co-founder and CEO of [[Lever: Building Recruiting Software from the Inside Out|Lever]] (YC S12), a recruiting software company. She studied design at Stanford, worked at Google (as a speech writer for Marissa Mayer and on the Chrome team under Sundar Pichai), and transitioned from individual contributor designer to CEO on the eve of Lever's Series A in October 2014 [1].

## Worldview

Nahm's philosophy centers on three ideas:

**People before product, co-founders before ideas.** Nahm did not leave Google to start a company. She left to grow, and the co-founding team "were almost trying out working with each other for a while" before committing. When asked what matters most for getting started, she ranks: first, believing in the people you are working with; second, believing in a change happening in the world; third (distant), believing you can bring value that speaks to that change [1].

**Hiring is the strategy, not a task.** "There's a period at which you can be the best founder on the planet and you will be holding your own company back if you haven't figured out how to truly prioritize hiring, how to truly invest in it, and how to do it damn well." Her framing: "A founder's number one job is recruiting" [1].

**Culture before diversity.** An inclusive culture must precede diverse hiring. "What's the point of hiring all this quote diverse talent if when they get there they're not ready to succeed?" The biggest roadblock is founders thinking they need to have demographic diversity before they can work on cultural inclusion [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Nahm brings a designer's lens to company-building. She reframed her loss of product time as a CEO by recognizing "I still work on a product, the product is this company. I still have to do user research. It's just that my users are my employees and my customers" [1].

Her specific contributions to hiring practice -- motivation fit as the first interview stage, impact descriptions replacing job descriptions, proactive sourcing (81% of Lever's engineering team), nurture pipelines with two-to-three-year lead times -- are operational and concrete, grounded in Lever's own data (35% faster hire times for nurtured candidates) [1].

Holly Liu, in the interview, observes that Nahm's insight about "designers should be founders" reflects a broader theme: the skill set of understanding user needs, generating strategy around them, and building systems is deeply applicable to company-building [1].

## Key Frameworks

- **Motivation Fit**: First stage of hiring process. Ask where people are taking their career, what decisions have been meaningful, what they want more of. Listen for ambiguity comfort and specific self-awareness [1].
- **Impact Descriptions**: Replace job descriptions with 1/3/6/12-month outcome descriptions. Attracts top talent and diverse candidates by not broadcasting assumptions about who should apply [1].
- **Career Trajectory Interview**: Walk through a candidate's career from college onward, looking for patterns of success and failure. Modified from top grading methodology [1].
- **The Nurture Pipeline**: Invest in relationships years before a hire. 81% proactive sourcing rate, 35% faster time-to-hire for nurtured candidates [1].
- **Cultural Surface Area**: Culture extends to dish duty, holiday schedules, how differences are discussed. Founders must decide what is and is not part of the culture, and that boundary will shift over time [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Recruiting Software Startup Lever](https://ycombinator.com/library/6I-on-starting-and-scaling-recruiting-software-startup-lever) -- Sarah Nahm, Holly Liu (2019)

title: Vidit Aatrey
body:
# Vidit Aatrey

Vidit Aatrey is the co-founder and CEO of [[Meesho: Social Commerce in India|Meesho]] (YC S16), one of India's largest social commerce platforms. He graduated from IIT Delhi (2008-2012) and worked at MobiHerd before co-founding Meesho with Sanjeev Barnwal in 2015. The company had 700 employees by 2019 and half a million monthly active social store owners [1].

## Worldview

Aatrey's thinking centers on three convictions:

**Stay close to users, especially when they are not you.** Aatrey builds for an audience he is not part of -- women, mostly housewives, in tier 2-3 Indian cities. His response is systematic proximity: 100-200 top users on his personal WhatsApp, monthly "We Hear You" video town halls for users, management call center rotations, and inviting users to company celebrations. "The day you start taking assumptions, start thinking on behalf of your users, you start doing mistakes" [1]. See [[Customer Relationships]] and [[Talking to Users]].

**You can build for others; it is just harder.** Aatrey initially believed the common wisdom that founders must solve their own problems. After Meesho's discovery process, he reversed this view: "The best thing to do is build a product that solves your own problem. The second best thing to do is build a product that solves a problem for someone who can't solve their own problem" [1].

**Do not listen to VCs about what to build.** Aatrey's biggest early mistake was spending four months chasing VC-defined milestones. "After that, I've never gone to them asking what should I do, because I know they don't know" [1]. See [[Investor Advice Pitfalls]].

## Distinct Perspective

Aatrey offers a rare view of building a technology company for emerging-market users who are fundamentally different from the founder. His insights on India's trust deficit, unbranded commerce, and social selling through community networks are specific to contexts where institutional trust is low and personal relationships drive economic activity. He is also distinctive in framing the value of his product not primarily in economic terms but in identity and empowerment -- the professional recognition his users gain matters more to them than the $150-200/month income [1].

His co-founder relationship with Sanjeev Barnwal is a case study in itself. They committed to start a company with no idea, on a phone call, based on a decade of friendship. Aatrey credits this trust for enabling two pivots that he believes would have been impossible with a less trusted co-founder [1].

## Key Frameworks

- **Growth-Quality Cycle**: Alternate six-month periods between growth and quality improvement. Do not try to optimize quality during growth spurts [1].
- **The Second Best Thing**: If you cannot solve your own problem, solve the problem of someone who cannot solve it themselves [1].
- **User Town Halls ("We Hear You")**: Monthly CEO-produced videos for users reporting what the company did, plans to do, and what is not working [1].
- **Founder as Customer Support**: CEO personally adds early users on WhatsApp and provides direct support [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Indian Shopping Site Meesho](https://ycombinator.com/library/6M-on-starting-and-scaling-indian-shopping-site-meesho) -- Vidit Aatrey, Adora Cheung (2019)

title: Pete Koomen
body:
# Pete Koomen

Pete Koomen is a Group Partner at Y Combinator. He was previously co-founder of Optimizely, which helps thousands of companies run A/B tests to measure which design changes improve user behavior [1]. His background gives him a distinctive data-driven lens on conversion optimization, and his startup Optimizely was itself a YC company (Winter '10). He has also built internal AI tools for YC's finance and legal teams and wrote an influential essay arguing that most AI apps are "horseless carriages" -- powerful technology trapped in outdated interfaces [3].

## Worldview

Koomen's advice is empirical and experiment-driven. His core themes:

**Small changes can have big impact.** From his Optimizely experience: "I saw firsthand how small changes can sometimes have a big impact on how your users behave" [1]. This carries through his advice on landing page design, CTA placement, and signup flows.

**Every page must answer two questions.** Koomen evaluates websites by asking: (1) "Does this convince me that I want to sign up?" and (2) "Does the signup process talk me out of it?" [1]. The first question tests value communication; the second tests friction.

**Map and shorten the conversion funnel.** The aha moment -- the point where users experience the core value -- should be as few clicks as possible from landing. Koomen describes working with founders on whiteboards: "The aha moment is at like step seven. And they realize they don't actually need steps two, four, five. And they cut them out. And all of a sudden, the aha moment becomes like step two" [1].

**Post-launch iteration is analytical, not emotional.** In the Launching discussion, Koomen advises founders whose product gets no traction to treat it as a diagnostic problem: "Week by week, you're like, 'Okay, I'm going to tweak one variable'" -- messaging copy, target audience, company size [2]. It is "more art than science" but the frame is analytical: "Something isn't working. Which one of your assumptions was wrong?" [2].

**AI should offload work, not create it.** Koomen's central argument on AI product design: the right question is not "How do I insert AI into my tool?" but "How would I design this tool from scratch to offload as much repetitive work from the user as possible?" [3].

**System prompts should be user-editable.** Developers treat system prompts like code -- hidden, one-size-fits-all, owned by the developer. Koomen argues they should be visible to and editable by users, since they are written in natural language and represent the user's logic, not the developer's. "Because this is all just English, we no longer have to treat these things like black boxes" [3].

**Developers should build tools, not prompts.** If users program agents through natural language system prompts, the developer's job shifts to building the tools (actions, integrations) that agents need to accomplish work in the world [3].

## Distinct Perspective

Koomen is the most conversion-specific voice in the YC canon. Where [[Kevin Hale]] provides the diagnostic framework (seven questions for every page, the knowledge gap), Koomen provides live examples of what goes wrong: competing CTAs, hidden aha moments, carousels that auto-scroll past the user, and forms that introduce unnecessary friction.

His Optimizely background makes him uniquely credible on A/B testing and experimentation. He is also one of the earliest case studies of the [[MVP Planning|prototype stage]]: the Optimizely MVP was built in days as a JavaScript file on S3 that only the founders could operate, shown to marketers to validate demand [2].

On AI, Koomen brings the AB testing mindset to AI product design. His essay includes working demos where readers can edit system prompts and see results in real time. His practical experience building AI tools at YC -- sitting next to finance and legal team members, watching their workflows, writing system prompts together -- gives his advice concrete grounding. "Not everyone has a Pete next to them, but you could have an AI system prompt writer" that translates feedback into prompt edits [3].

## Key Frameworks

- **Two-Question Page Evaluation**: (1) Does this convince me to sign up? (2) Does the signup process talk me out of it? [1]
- **Aha Moment Mapping**: Count the steps from landing to core value experience, then eliminate unnecessary steps [1]
- **Single-Variable Iteration**: When nothing works post-launch, change one variable per week and measure [2]
- **Horseless Carriage Test**: Is the AI app just inserting AI into an old interface, or is it redesigning from the ground up to offload work? [3]
- **Editable System Prompts**: Let users program agents in natural language; developers build tools, not prompts [3]

## References

1. [How to Convert More Visitors into Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KD-how-to-convert-more-visitors-into-customers) -- Pete Koomen, Aaron Epstein (2024)
2. [Why Startup Founders Should Launch Companies Sooner Than They Think](https://ycombinator.com/library/LG-why-startup-founders-should-launch-companies-sooner-than-they-think) -- Pete Koomen et al. (2023)
3. [AI Apps Are Broken -- Here's How To Fix Them](https://ycombinator.com/library/MR-ai-apps-are-broken-here-s-how-to-fix-them) -- Pete Koomen, [[Tom Blomfield]], [[David Lieb]] (2025)

title: Parker Conrad
body:
# Parker Conrad

Parker Conrad is the co-founder and CEO of [[Rippling: Building the Same Company Twice|Rippling]], a $13.5 billion compound software business for HR, finance, and IT. He previously co-founded SigFig (a consumer finance company that went through seven years of pivots) and Zenefits (which reached $65M ARR before Conrad was forced out). His career arc -- seven years of consumer failure, meteoric B2B success, public ouster, and a comeback building the same company better -- gives him an unusual perspective on persistence, product-market fit, and when to give up [1].

## Worldview

**PMF is binary and unmistakable.** Conrad draws the sharpest possible contrast between his SigFig and Zenefits experiences. At SigFig: four ideas out of five fail completely; one mostly fails with a glimmer of hope. At Zenefits: all five ideas work wildly well, plus the ideas you thought would not work also work. "That's how you know if something's working. And if you have that, then you want to keep going. If you don't have that, you probably want to start over somewhere else" [1]. See [[Product-Market Fit]].

**Give up if it is not working.** "There's a lot of advice out there to kind of stick with it, keep going, don't give up. And I think it's terrible advice. I think if it's not working, give up immediately." Conrad spent seven years on SigFig before reaching this conclusion [1].

**Don't fundraise; be unfundable to ignore.** "You need to make your business so good it sort of doesn't matter whether you fit into whatever mold or construct the VCs are looking for." Conrad's approach: wait until investors are sending signed term sheets with blank valuations before engaging in fundraising [1].

**Consumer is random; B2B is deliberate.** "A lot of consumer businesses end up being kind of random... It's one of the reasons I hate consumer businesses." Conrad believes many consumer-turned-B2B founders share this discovery [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Conrad is one of the few founders who built the same company twice. This gives him unusual certainty: "I had a lot of insights about the market and what was needed and very little self-doubt this time around" [1]. His framing of Rippling's founding -- "There's a hundred billion dollar opportunity just sitting right there on the floor, and nobody can see it except for us" -- reflects conviction born of operational experience rather than market analysis [1].

His compound startup thesis challenges the conventional YC advice to "do one thing and do it extremely well." Conrad argues that narrow point solutions hit a ceiling and that the deeper problems in organizations require interoperable application suites. He acknowledges this is "almost heretical" in American startup culture and may result in only three or four companies succeeding at it [1].

On AI, Conrad is a contrarian: the important thing about LLMs is not that they can write but that they can read. Their ability to ingest organizational data and surface anomalies -- where the rep's forecast disagrees with the AI's assessment, where a new hire's 90-day data predicts trouble -- is the transformative application for enterprise software [1].

## Key Frameworks

- **The Blank Valuation Test**: Do not fundraise until investors are sending signed term sheets with the valuation left blank [1].
- **PMF by Contrast**: If you are asking "Is this PMF?", it is not. At PMF, the market sucks the product into existence [1].
- **Compound Software**: Build multiple interoperable applications with shared platform capabilities. Higher barrier, but much stronger moat than point solutions [1].
- **AI as Reader, Not Writer**: LLMs' context windows expand executive visibility. The second opinion (AI vs. human) on deals, new hires, and organizational anomalies is more valuable than AI-generated content [1].
- **Founder Mode with Limits**: Go to ground when something is broken. Do not do it when things are working. "You can't do it everywhere" [1].

## References

1. [How To Build The Future: Parker Conrad](https://ycombinator.com/library/M2-how-to-build-the-future-parker-conrad) -- Parker Conrad, [[Garry Tan]] (2025)

title: Harj Taggar
body:
# Harj Taggar

Harj Taggar is a YC partner and cofounder of Triplebyte (YC S15), a hiring marketplace that helps engineers find work at fast-growing companies. His advice on hiring comes from both sides of the market: helping companies close engineers and observing what candidates experience across hundreds of hiring processes. He also contributed foundational lectures on finding co-founders and on whether to start a startup [4][5].

## Worldview

Taggar views engineering hiring as a sales and optimization problem, not an HR process. His consistent theme is that companies dramatically underinvest in the candidate experience and then blame candidates for not being interested [1].

**Personal networks first, always.** Taggar ranks hiring channels and puts personal networks at the top by a large margin, especially for the first three hires [2].

**Speed as competitive advantage.** For startups competing against larger companies, speed is one of the biggest advantages [1].

**Think like an investor when joining.** In [[Choosing a Startup to Join]], Taggar advises job seekers to evaluate startups the same way investors do -- looking at growth trajectory, founder quality (relative to starting point, not credentials), and depth of user passion rather than market size [3].

**Resilience is the core founder trait.** After 15 years of starting and investing in startups, Taggar identifies resilience as the quality most predictive of founder success. He has abandoned earlier proxies: confidence is unreliable ("someone could appear very confident initially, but not be resilient when their startup hit roadblocks"), credentials are unreliable, and motivations are unreliable [4]. He cites Benchling's founder Sajith as a case study in hidden resilience: quietly spoken, struggled at Demo Day, made no revenue for over a year, and now runs a $6 billion company [4].

**Stress response is the #1 co-founder criterion.** Taggar identifies how someone handles stress as the single most important quality in a co-founder, above complementary skills, shared interests, or pedigree. "Even if they have the perfect set of complementary skills, if they don't handle stress well, it's very unlikely your relationship is going to last" [5].

## Distinct Perspective

Taggar's advice is notable for its specificity and its ranking of channels. He explicitly ranks hiring channels by effectiveness and argues that founders should focus exclusively on personal networks for the first three hires [2].

His advice on joining startups is distinctive for warning against evaluating market size -- "the most common mistake investors make when they're trying to pick a startup" [3]. Instead, he recommends asking to talk to users and gauging the depth of their passion for the product, citing Airbnb as the classic example of a small but fanatical user base [3].

His co-founder advice emphasizes the always-be-scanning approach: work on projects with potential co-founders long before planning a startup, because "the best time to find a co-founder is when you're not actually looking for a co-founder." Phil Libin (Evernote founder) took this to an extreme: he would only make friends with people who could someday be co-founders [5].

On whether to start a startup, Taggar provides a practical worst-case framework: figure out the specific downside, decide if you can handle it, and factor in the learning value. "I don't think enough people factor in how valuable this learning is when they're doing their worst case scenario analysis" [4]. He also treats the search for ideas and co-founders as a single process: "It's hard to think up good ideas yourself... Having someone to talk through that initial idea or impulse with and debate and discuss is important" [4].

## Key Frameworks

- **Hiring channel ranking**: Personal networks > hiring marketplaces > inbound generation > cold outreach > recruiters > meetups > agencies [2]
- **Two-step offer presentation**: Excitement call same day, details call next day [1]
- **Three-factor startup evaluation**: Founders (relative impressiveness), user passion (depth over breadth), growth (trajectory over absolute numbers) [3]
- **Paul Buchheit Test**: "If you're happy working where you are and you don't have a strong desire to do anything else, you're probably going to get paid less and have to work more if you leave for a startup" [3]
- **Resilience Over Confidence**: Quiet, persistent founders often outperform visibly confident ones [4]
- **Worst-Case Analysis**: Make the decision concrete by identifying the specific downside and deciding if it is tolerable [4]
- **Ideas and Co-Founders as One Search**: Don't separate the two; the best co-founder emerges from conversations about ideas [4]
- **Co-founder Branching Algorithm**: Ask your top candidates; if they say no, ask who they would co-found with, get introductions, repeat [5]

## References

1. [Convincing Engineers to Join Your Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4G-convincing-engineers-to-join-your-team) -- Harj Taggar (July 2018)
2. [How to Hire Your First Engineer](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4H-how-to-hire-your-first-engineer) -- Harj Taggar (July 2018)
3. [How to Choose a Startup to Join](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9t-how-to-choose-a-startup-to-join) -- Harj Taggar (n.d.)
4. [Should You Start a Startup?](https://ycombinator.com/library/GV-should-you-start-a-startup) -- Harj Taggar (2022)
5. [How to Find the Right Co-Founder](https://ycombinator.com/library/8h-how-to-find-the-right-co-founder) -- Harj Taggar (2020)

title: Tony Xu
body:
# Tony Xu

Tony Xu is the co-founder and CEO of DoorDash (YC S13), the largest food delivery platform in the United States with mid-60% market share and over seven million drivers. Before DoorDash, he worked at eBay under CEO John Donahoe, who recommended he attend Stanford GSB. He met his co-founders (Andy Fang, Stanley Tang, Evan Moore) at Stanford.

## Worldview

Xu's thinking is organized around a single principle: customer obsession over competitor focus. This is not abstract -- it is operationalized through the specific practice of doing the work yourself, continuously and indefinitely. All four DoorDash co-founders did every delivery for the first period. Xu personally did deliveries for two to three years. Every person at DoorDash, including Xu, still does deliveries every year, twelve years after founding [1].

He believes that doing the work produces insight that no analytics dashboard can replicate. His geographic strategy (suburbs over cities), his understanding of driver demographics, and his ability to weather fundraising rejection all trace back to direct operational experience [1].

## Key Frameworks

**The Three-Question Market Validation.** Before scaling, Xu needed positive answers to three questions: (1) Will consumers pay? (2) Will merchants pay? (3) Will drivers show up? Each required a different method of validation -- consumer retention without marketing spend, door-to-door merchant sales, and a controlled driver switching experiment [1].

**Customer Obsession, Not Competitor Focus.** This became DoorDash's core company value, originating from the Stanford football incident where the founders refunded all orders (costing 40% of their near-zero bank balance) and baked cookies delivered at 5 AM. The principle: do things the right way for customers even if the company might not survive [1].

**Market Creation vs. Market Capture.** Xu distinguishes between companies that capture an existing market (GrubHub partnering with the 20,000 restaurants that already offered delivery) and companies that create a new market (enabling the other 980,000 restaurants to offer delivery by building a logistics network). DoorDash was in the business of creating a market, which investors initially could not evaluate with standard frameworks [1].

**Crisis Simplifies Decision-Making.** In both DoorDash's near-death in 2013 and during COVID-19 in 2020, Xu found that crisis made priorities obvious and execution simpler. "Sometimes in crisis, I actually find that it's a lot easier operating a company because it's very clear what to do" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Xu is notable for the extreme patience required by his journey. DoorDash almost died after demo day, received hundreds of investor rejections across three consecutive years (2016-2018), took a down round on the Series C, and still emerged as the category leader by 2019. His framework for understanding investor rejection: timing matters more than metrics. "There are moments where it's just before that moment" -- when data exists but is not yet overwhelming enough for growth investors to act [1].

His suburban strategy was contrarian at a time when all competitors targeted urban density. The insight came from doing deliveries, not from spreadsheet analysis: suburbs had families with larger baskets, easier parking, simpler deliveries to single-family homes, and higher customer need. "The first instinct was just listening to what the customers had told us" [1].

On COVID-19 decision-making, Xu cut commissions in half (costing $100+ million) and ran a national TV campaign advertising competitors, while DoorDash was unprofitable and preparing for an IPO. He justified this by appealing to mission duration: the company exists to grow the GDP of every city over decades, not to optimize for a single year's financials [1].

## On Becoming an Expert

Xu's advice to his 18-year-old self: "Especially when you're on the cusp of something new -- take AI -- there's no better way to be the expert than just to do the work. And you might be surprised at how quickly you get to become the expert" [1]. He had no logistics or delivery background but became an authority through years of hands-on experience.

## References

1. [How To Build The Future: Tony Xu](https://ycombinator.com/library/MJ-how-to-build-the-future-tony-xu) -- Tony Xu, [[Garry Tan]] (2025)

title: Paul Graham
body:
# Paul Graham

Paul Graham is a programmer, essayist, and co-founder of Y Combinator (with Jessica Livingston, Robert Morris, and Trevor Blackwell). Before YC, he co-founded Viaweb in 1995 with Robert Morris, which was acquired by Yahoo in 1998 and became Yahoo Store. His essays on startups, published primarily at [paulgraham.com](http://www.paulgraham.com), constitute one of the most influential bodies of startup advice in existence. He is the single most prolific source in this wiki, with 14 essays spanning 2005 to 2020.

## Worldview

Graham's worldview on startups rests on a small number of principles he returns to across all his essays:

**Make something people want.** This is the Y Combinator motto, originally chosen as the text for the Summer Founders Program T-shirt [7]. Graham treats it as the root cause of nearly all startup success and failure: "In a sense there's just one mistake that kills startups: not making something users want. If you make something users want, you'll probably be fine, whatever else you do or don't do" [2]. Every other piece of advice flows from this. "The most reliable way to become a billionaire is to start a company that grows fast, and the way to grow fast is to make what users want" [14].

**Determination matters more than intelligence.** "The most important quality in a startup founder is determination. Not intelligence -- determination" [3]. Graham found this somewhat depressing but arrived at it empirically. VCs invest in professors; they should invest in students. Microsoft, Yahoo, and Google were all founded by dropouts [3].

**Startups are counterintuitive.** Nearly every natural instinct about startups is wrong [5]. The correct approach often feels wrong: release before you are ready, do things that don't scale, don't worry about the business model, ignore competitors. "Starting a startup is a task where you can't always trust your instincts" [5].

**The organic method for ideas.** The way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of them but to notice problems -- "Live in the future, then build what's missing" [1]. Deliberately generated ideas are dangerous because they sound plausible but lack real demand [1].

**Users over everything else.** Users are the most powerful force acting on a startup. "If they take you up, no competitor can keep you down" [3]. The three internal threats that kill startups -- disputes, inertia, and ignoring users -- are all more dangerous than any external competitor [3].

**Truth over performance.** Graham consistently argues that the way to persuade is not through salesmanship but through genuine understanding. In fundraising, the way to seem formidable is to stick to the truth [13]. In YC interviews, the best preparation is to deeply understand your users rather than rehearsing a pitch [14]. The throughline: founders who truly understand something will communicate it convincingly without tricks.

**Power law thinking.** Startup outcomes follow an extremely steep power law. Roughly 15 startups per year become the big successes, and investors treat this as binary [13]. This shapes everything about how fundraising works: investors are looking for any chance, however small, of being one of those 15.

**Startups are designed to grow fast.** A startup is not merely a newly founded company or one that works on technology -- it is a company designed to grow fast. Everything else associated with startups -- venture funding, exits, technology focus -- follows from this single property [12].

**Authenticity of motivation.** Graham distinguishes between founders driven by genuine interest in the problem and those driven by money or status. The former build billion-dollar companies; the latter take the first acquisition offer or find easier ways to seem cool [14]. When YC detects exploitative founders, it rejects them -- not on moral grounds alone, but because bad people make bad founders [14].

## Distinct Perspective

What makes Graham's lens distinctive:

**Anti-credentialism.** Graham consistently argues that formal business knowledge, MBA degrees, and "work experience" are unnecessary and potentially harmful. "You don't need to know anything about business to start a startup" [4]. Getting a job may actually make you less able to start a startup by "turning you into a tame animal" [4]. He openly mocks the concept of enterprise content management solutions and the vocabulary of business school [3].

**The negative approach.** Rather than prescribing what to do, Graham often works by elimination -- listing reasons not to do something and showing they are bogus [4], or listing 18 specific mistakes to avoid [2]. His essay "Why to Not Not Start a Startup" systematically dismantles 16 common excuses.

**Concrete over abstract.** Graham's essays are full of specific companies, dollar figures, and named examples. Artix and Viaweb. Stripe's Collison installation. PayPal nearly switching to Windows. Wufoo sending hand-written thank-you notes. The Apple II being cheap because Woz was poor. These specifics carry his arguments more than any general principle.

**Historical perspective.** Graham frequently places startups in deep historical context. Getting a job is only a hundred years old as the default way to make a living [4]. The shift to startups may be comparable to the shift from farming to manufacturing. Medieval serfs would have found it insanely risky to move to a city of three thousand strangers [4].

**The theoretical voice.** Graham is the most theoretical voice in the YC canon. Where others offer tactical advice, Graham provides the conceptual architecture. His "Startup = Growth" essay is one of the most referenced pieces in the YC library because it provides the definition that everything else builds on. His writing style is distinctive for its use of mathematical reasoning and analogies to other domains (scientific research, evolution, optimization) [12].

## Key Frameworks

Graham is responsible for several frameworks that have become standard vocabulary in the startup world:

- **[[Schlep Filter]]** / Schlep Blindness: The unconscious mental filter that prevents you from seeing good ideas because they involve tedious work. Stripe is the canonical example [1].

- **[[Default Alive or Default Dead]]**: The binary diagnostic for startups past their first 8-9 months. Will you reach profitability on the money you have? Half the founders Graham asks do not know [8].

- **[[Equity Equation]]**: The 1/(1-n) formula for whether any trade of company equity is worth it [9].

- **The Well Model**: Good startup ideas look like wells (narrow but deep), not broad shallow holes. Facebook and Microsoft both started as wells [1].

- **Sitcom Startup Ideas**: Ideas generated by trying to think of startup ideas, which sound plausible but nobody actually needs. "A social network for pet owners" [1].

- **The Still Life Effect**: Smart people's tendency to commit to the first idea they have, then rationalize the time invested [7].

- **Relentlessly Resourceful**: The two-word description of the ideal startup founder -- not merely determined, but adaptive. "'Make something people want' is the destination, but 'Be relentlessly resourceful' is how you get there" [6].

- **The Contained Fire**: Starting with a deliberately narrow market to get it really hot before expanding [10].

- **Collison Installation**: The technique of immediately setting users up rather than sending them a link. Named after the Stripe founders [10].

- **Pulling a Meraki**: For hardware startups, assembling the product yourself initially instead of waiting for factory production [10].

- **The Fatal Pinch**: Default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it [8].

- **Playing House**: Young founders who go through the outward motions of a startup (raise money, rent office, hire people) while neglecting the one essential thing: making something people want [5].

- **Growth as Compass**: Use a target weekly growth rate to make every decision. "Anything that gets you the growth you need is ipso facto right" [12]. This converts the bewildering problem of starting a startup into an optimization problem.

- **The S-Curve**: Successful startups follow three phases -- slow/no growth while figuring things out, rapid growth as product-market fit is found, and eventual slowdown from internal limits and market saturation [12].

- **YC Growth Benchmarks**: 5-7% per week is good during YC; 10% is exceptional; 1% signals the startup has not figured out what it is doing [12].

- **The Formidability Test**: Formidable means "justifiably confident" -- someone who seems like they will get what they want regardless of obstacles. Most investors decide within minutes whether a founder is a winner or a loser. The way to pass this test without faking it is to genuinely understand why your startup is worth investing in [13].

- **The Larval Market**: A small but growable market. Apple's market in 1976 is the perfect example -- not many people wanted a computer, but the market was growing. The ideal is founders who are "living in the future" at the leading edge of change [14].

- **The "Why Now?" Question**: Every company that gets really big is riding an external wave. The critical question is why this idea only recently became possible [13].

- **Expected Value of Startups**: The high failure rate is not a bug. If a successful startup can make a founder $100 million, even at 1% probability the expected value is $1 million. For the right people, the probability may be 20-50% [12].

## Evolution of Thinking

Graham's position on certain topics shifted over the decade and a half covered by these essays:

**On when to start.** In 2005-2007, Graham was actively pushing the age of founders lower, trying to find the lower bound. YC began as a summer program partly to let college students participate [7]. By 2014, he explicitly told Stanford students "Do not start a startup in college" and argued that most 20-year-olds should be searching breadth-first, not depth-first [5]. The shift reflects experience: too many young founders were "playing house" rather than solving real problems.

**On cofounders from college.** Remained consistent: school is the best place to find cofounders, and the window closes quickly after graduation [11] [4].

**On the importance of ideas.** Shifted from "we fund people, not ideas" (going so far as accepting applications with no idea in 2007 [4]) to a richer view that the organic method of idea generation -- living in the future and building what is missing -- is actively the best strategy, not just a fallback [1] [5].

**On fundraising.** His 2013 essay on convincing investors emphasizes truth and domain expertise over salesmanship [13]. By 2020, he reiterates this even more strongly: the best preparation for a YC interview is talking to users, not rehearsing slides [14].

## Personal Experience

Graham draws heavily on his own experiences as a founder. His startup Viaweb (originally called Webgen, then renamed after losing the name) made software for building online stores. Before Viaweb, he and Robert Morris started a company called Artix to put art galleries on the Web, which failed because galleries did not want to be online [7]. The Artix experience taught him the lesson that would become YC's motto: make something people want [7].

He references his own poverty as a Viaweb founder: he and Morris were 29 and 30 but lived like 23-year-olds with roughly zero assets [11]. Viaweb's top service tier was $300/month, an order of magnitude below the norm, because that seemed like a lot of money to them [11]. At Viaweb, when merchants refused to use their software, Graham and Morris built stores for them -- selling luggage, pens, and men's shirts. He describes feeling "pretty lame" but recognizes in retrospect it was exactly the right thing [10].

He was also, before Viaweb, "trying to be a starving artist" in New York, which is how he came up with the doomed gallery idea [7].

## Recurring Examples

Certain companies and people appear across many of Graham's essays as touchstones:

- **Stripe** -- The Collison installation, the schlep filter, manual merchant accounts behind the scenes [1] [10]
- **Airbnb** -- Going door to door in New York, waiting 4 months to hire, the rolly bags at YC dinners [8] [10]
- **Facebook** -- The contained fire (Harvard only), Mark Zuckerberg as someone who succeeded despite being a "complete noob at startups" [1] [5] [10]
- **Microsoft** -- The well model (Altair Basic), Traf-o-data as the Artix-equivalent failure [1] [7]
- **Apple / Wozniak** -- The TV monitor, cheap because Woz was poor, "all the best things I did came from not having done it before" [1] [11]
- **Google** -- Larry and Sergey initially tried to sell the algorithm, made search work then monetized, investors created grief [2] [3]
- **Viaweb** -- Graham's own startup, used as a recurring case study throughout [3] [7] [10] [11]
- **Sam Altman** -- Cited as the most successful young founder YC had funded (at 19), seeming "like he had a 40-year-old inside him" [4] [11]
- **Wufoo** -- Released the form-builder before the database, hand-written thank-you notes [3] [10]
- **PayPal** -- Nearly switched to Windows; Max Levchin showed it scaled only 1% as well [2]
- **Pebble** -- Assembled first several hundred watches themselves, learned the value of good screws [10]
- **Dropbox** -- Rejected by Boston VCs, illustration of how investors miscalculate market size [13]

# Paul Graham

## The "Before the Startup" Framework (2014)

In his Stanford "How to Start a Startup" lecture, Graham presents six counterintuitive truths about startups [15]:

1. **Startups are counterintuitive.** Follow your instincts and they will lead you astray. The exception: trust your instincts about people.
2. **Expertise in startups is not what you need.** You need expertise in your users. Mark Zuckerberg succeeded "despite being a complete noob at startups" [15].
3. **Gaming the system stops working.** There is no boss to trick. Users are like sharks -- "meat or no meat" [15].
4. **Startups are all-consuming.** "It will take over your life to a degree that you cannot imagine." Successful founders universally report: "It never gets any easier" [15].
5. **You can't tell if you're good enough.** After nine years of predicting YC founders' success, Graham's accuracy was limited. "The swaggering recruits are no more likely to turn out to be really tough than the quiet ones" [15].
6. **Don't try to think of startup ideas.** Turn your mind into the type that has startup ideas unconsciously. "The very best ideas almost have to start as side projects" [15].

Graham distills all advice for aspiring founders to two words: "Just learn" [15]. The optimal college path is "the classic version of college as education for its own sake" -- not entrepreneurship programs. "The component of entrepreneurship that really matters is domain expertise" [15].

### "Playing House"

Graham and [[Sam Altman]] coined this term for founders who imitate the external forms of a startup without the substance [15]. The pattern: come up with a plausible idea, raise money at a nice valuation, rent a nice office, hire friends, then "gradually realize how completely fucked they are" because they neglected "the one thing that's actually essential which is to make something people want" [15].

The root cause: lifetime training in gaming artificial systems. "Think about what it takes to get into college. Extracurricular activities, check." Startup founders look for the startup equivalent of extracurricular activities [15].

### On Monoculture in Hiring

Asked whether hiring people you like creates dangerous monoculture, Graham dismisses the concern: "The advantages of hiring people that you know and like are far greater than the small disadvantages of having some monoculture. All the most successful startups, someone just hires all their pals out of college" [15].

## References

1. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- Paul Graham (November 2012)
2. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- Paul Graham (October 2006)
3. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- Paul Graham (April 2006)
4. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) -- Paul Graham (March 2007)
5. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- Paul Graham (October 2014)
6. [Be Relentlessly Resourceful](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/94-be-relentlessly-resourceful) -- Paul Graham (March 2009)
7. [Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) -- Paul Graham (April 2005)
8. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- Paul Graham (October 2015)
9. [The Equity Equation](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8u-the-equity-equation) -- Paul Graham (July 2007)
10. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) -- Paul Graham (July 2013)
11. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- Paul Graham (October 2006)
12. [Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) -- Paul Graham (September 2012)
13. [How to Convince Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors) -- Paul Graham (August 2013)
14. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) -- Paul Graham (December 2020)
15. [How to Start a Startup: Before the Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/JI-how-to-start-a-startup-before-the-startup) -- Paul Graham (2014)

title: Austen Allred
related: [[Lambda School (BloomTech): Reinventing CS Education with Income Share Agreements]]
body:
# Austen Allred

Austen Allred is the CEO and co-founder of [Lambda School](https://lambdaschool.com/) (YC S17), later renamed Bloom Institute of Technology. He is not a college graduate. Before Lambda School, he founded a previous startup where a Series A fell through on December 23rd with documents already signed, leaving him broke in rural Utah with a newborn daughter in the hospital. He rebuilt by writing a growth book that generated $120,000 on Kickstarter from a list of a few thousand subscribers [1].

## Worldview

Allred views human capital as the most mispriced and unoptimized asset class in the world. His core argument: every commodity -- pork bellies, stocks, real estate -- has transparent pricing and efficient markets, but human potential has no equivalent. A person in rural Utah making $30,000 may be as capable as someone making $150,000 in San Francisco. The gap is access to opportunity, not ability [1].

He believes traditional education is structurally broken because incentives are misaligned. The University of Phoenix gets paid regardless of student outcomes. Lambda School gets paid only when students get hired, aligning the school's incentives with the student's success [1].

On product quality: Allred argues that product quality is systematically undervalued because it cannot be quantified. "Revenue is a very, very trailing indicator of product." He points to Airbnb, Stripe, Dropbox, and Tesla as companies that won through accumulated product superiority despite competitors with more resources [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Allred is unusual among YC founders for building a company that is both a technology business and an educational institution. He approaches education with the rigor of a fintech company -- underwriting student risk, building predictive models, and treating the ISA as a financial instrument. His phrase "nobody knows how to underwrite student risk" frames education as an unsolved fintech problem [1].

He is also notable for arguing that online education is superior to in-person when built from first principles, rather than being a degraded version of classroom instruction [1].

## Key Frameworks

**The ISA as Financial Innovation**: Deferred payment eliminates the biggest barrier to education (financial risk) while aligning school and student incentives [1].

**Product Quality as Compounding Advantage**: Product quality is hard to measure, which means analysts and competitors systematically underestimate it. Companies that accumulate "a whole lot of subtlety built up over time" develop durable, unmeasurable moats [1].

**Remote as a Core Innovation**: Building a great remote organization requires treating remote as one of the company's core innovations, not an add-on. It consumes innovation budget but unlocks talent advantages [1].

## References

1. [On starting and scaling Bloom Institute of Technology](https://ycombinator.com/library/5N-on-starting-and-scaling-bloom-institute-of-technology) -- Austen Allred (2019)

title: Kirsty Nathoo
body:
# Kirsty Nathoo

Kirsty Nathoo is the Managing Director of Finance at Y Combinator. She has worked with virtually every company that has gone through YC, from incorporation to fundraising to hiring to exits. She is a finance professional, not a lawyer, but her depth of experience across thousands of companies gives her unusual breadth on the mechanical and legal foundations of startup formation.

## Worldview

**Simple, standard, default.** Nathoo's overarching philosophy is that startup mechanics should not be an area of innovation. Delaware C Corp, standard vesting schedules, SAFEs for early fundraising, Clerky or Valley lawyers for paperwork. Deviating from defaults creates problems that surface at the worst possible moment -- during funding rounds or acquisitions -- and cost enormous time and money to fix [1].

**Think forward, not backward.** On [[Equity Split]], Nathoo's position is clear: co-founders should split equity near-equally because all the work is ahead of them. "If you've worked on it for three months, you've probably got five, ten, fifteen years of this company ahead of you. So think about forwards, not backwards" [1]. Equity disputes are the number one reason YC sees co-founders break up [1].

**Paperwork kills companies silently.** Nathoo tells multiple cautionary tales of companies where improperly filed documents -- a Connecticut LLC conversion done wrong, share purchases never properly papered, missing 83(b) elections -- created crises costing six months and half a million dollars or more. The 83(b) election is the single most critical piece of paperwork because it cannot be fixed retroactively [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Nathoo occupies a unique position in the YC knowledge base. While most YC speakers address strategy, product, and growth, Nathoo covers the unsexy but foundational mechanics that underpin everything else. Her talks are notable for their specificity: she walks through cap table math, SAFE conversion calculations, the difference between contractors and employees, and the exact tax implications of failing to file an 83(b) election [1].

Her horror stories -- the Connecticut conversion, the unpapered shares, the founder who took company money to Vegas -- serve as vivid cautionary tales that make abstract legal concepts concrete and memorable.

## Key Contributions

- **When to incorporate**: Not too soon (still brainstorming), not too late (creating significant IP or charging customers) [1]
- **Why Delaware**: Most developed corporate law, investor expectations, maximum flexibility, best privacy protections [1]
- **Vesting explainer**: Four-year vesting with one-year cliff, with clear reasoning for why it protects remaining founders, investors, and sets precedent for employees [1]
- **83(b) election**: The one unfixable mistake in incorporation. Must be filed with the IRS within 30 days [1]
- **SAFE mechanics**: Detailed walkthrough of how SAFEs convert to equity at a discount relative to the priced round [1]
- **Employee equity rule of thumb**: ~10% for the first 10 employees, on a sliding scale, with vesting on all grants [1]
- **Communication standard**: Founders must understand equity themselves before explaining it to employees. Always communicate both number of shares and percentage of company [1]

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Startup Mechanics](https://ycombinator.com/library/JR-how-to-start-a-startup-startup-mechanics) -- Kirsty Nathoo (2014)

title: Brad Flora
body:
# Brad Flora

Brad Flora is a YC Group Partner and founder of two YC-backed companies: Color Lovers (a creative design community with over one million members) and Creative Market (a marketplace for graphic design assets). He also founded Perfect Audience, a retargeting advertising platform that he sold after Facebook launched a competing product built on the same API. Flora appears in 11+ sources in this wiki, primarily as one of three recurring panelists in the "Inside the Group Partner Lounge" series alongside [[Harj Taggar]] and [[Michael Seibel]].

## Worldview

Flora's advice is grounded in his experience building companies across consumer communities, marketplaces, and advertising -- businesses where product-market fit was not immediately obvious and where pivot decisions and go-to-market strategy were the critical variables.

**KPIs as a forcing function.** Flora's most consistent theme is that founders need a single primary KPI and should track it obsessively. His own pivot story illustrates the point: Color Lovers spent a year and a half trying different monetization approaches without a clear KPI. "The reason it took so long was because we didn't have a main KPI that we were focused on." Once they pivoted to Creative Market, the KPI became obvious -- are people buying? -- and Flora became "obsessed with it, refreshing it every 15 minutes" [1]. He advises that if a founder does not know what their main KPI is, they probably need to pivot [1].

**Perfectionism kills speed.** Across the Partner Lounge episodes, Flora consistently identifies perfectionism as the root cause of delayed launches, delayed pricing, and delayed pivots. On launching, he describes the "Oscar ceremony fantasy" -- founders imagine a grand reveal and polish endlessly. The reality: "Often no one cares. You launch, there's no one there" [3]. On pricing, he notes founders act as if the first price is permanent, when in practice "every week you can change your pricing" [6].

**Competitors are just as broken as you.** Flora draws on his experience watching competitors from the outside vs. learning the truth later. "Years later you talked to someone that worked at that company and they say, 'Oh, man, what a mess'" [2]. This personal experience makes him particularly effective at coaching founders through competitive anxiety.

**Structural platform risk is the real danger.** Unlike general competitive worry, Flora takes platform dependency seriously. When Facebook launched Custom Audiences, competing directly with Perfect Audience's product on its own API, Flora recognized the structural impossibility and sold the company. He draws a clear line: worry about competitors who own the platform you depend on; do not worry about competitors who are just other startups [2].

**Sales avoidance is the hidden killer.** Flora identifies the "Civilization fallacy" -- founders who want to treat their startup like a real-time strategy game where they click buttons and watch numbers go up, avoiding the human work of sales. "A lot of times when startups want to sell just to other startups, it's because they are afraid to do the sales" [7].

## Distinct Perspective

Flora's advice is distinctly practical and grounded in his own failures. Where [[Michael Seibel]] draws on Justin.tv/Twitch and [[Harj Taggar]] draws on Triplebyte, Flora draws on Color Lovers, Creative Market, and Perfect Audience -- companies that are less famous but provide clearer illustrations of common founder mistakes.
