His willingness to describe his own wandering ("I like to say that we were wandering in the wilderness") and his own competitive anxiety gives his advice an accessible, peer-to-peer quality.

He is also one of the few YC partners who speaks explicitly about the advantages of being a first-time founder. His observation that second-time founders optimize for how impressive their idea sounds at "dinner parties" -- avoiding ideas like Airbed and Breakfast or Coinbase that sound dumb -- is one of the more memorable framings in the wiki [8].

On the AI wave, Flora compares it to his direct experience of the mobile platform shift: he arrived at YC in 2007, the same week Steve Jobs announced the iPhone. "The apps did not come until the following summer, and the permissions needed did not arrive until 2009. But the following five years produced most of the mobile companies people use today" [11].

## Key Frameworks

- **KPI Clarity Test**: If you do not immediately know your main KPI, you probably need to pivot [1].
- **The Oscar Ceremony Fallacy**: Founders imagine launch as a grand event. In reality, nobody cares, and you launch over and over [3].
- **The Civilization Fallacy**: Founders who want to sit at their computer clicking buttons instead of doing sales are avoiding the human work that builds real businesses [7].
- **Grandfather Pricing**: You can always keep existing users on their current plan and raise prices for new users. Early pricing decisions do not bind you [6].
- **The Dinner Party Problem**: Second-time founders optimize for how impressive their idea sounds socially, avoiding unconventional ideas that might be great [8].
- **Platform Risk vs. Competitive Noise**: The only competitors worth worrying about are those who own the platform you depend on [2].

## References

1. [YC's Group Partners Share Their Favorite Pivot Stories](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iy-yc-s-group-partners-share-their-favorite-pivot-stories) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Jared Friedman]], Gustaf Alstromer, [[Tom Blomfield]], Brad Flora, Aaron Epstein (n.d.)
2. [How to Compete with Amazon and Google](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iz-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-how-to-compete-with-amazon-and-google) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora (2021)
3. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: When to Launch Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J0-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-when-to-launch-your-startup) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora (2021)
4. [Investors Said No, Now What?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J1-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-investors-said-no-now-what) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora (2021)
5. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: Don't Make These Hiring Mistakes](https://ycombinator.com/library/J2-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-don-t-make-these-hiring-mistakes) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora (2022)
6. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: Turning Users Into Paid Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J3-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-turning-users-into-paid-customers) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora (2021)
7. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: The Ideal Customer](https://ycombinator.com/library/J5-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-the-ideal-customer) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora (2021)
8. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: Advantages of a First-Time Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J6-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-advantages-of-a-first-time-founder) -- [[Michael Seibel]], [[Harj Taggar]], Brad Flora (n.d.)
9. [YC's Group Partners Share Productivity Tips](https://ycombinator.com/library/K5-yc-s-group-partners-share-productivity-tips-and-advice-on-actually-getting-things-done) -- YC Group Partners (2022)
10. [How Do Billion Dollar Startups Start?](https://ycombinator.com/library/Li-how-do-billion-dollar-startups-start) -- [[Jared Friedman]], Diana Hu, [[Michael Seibel]], Brad Flora, Aaron Epstein (2024)
11. [How To Use AI In Your Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/M3-how-to-use-ai-in-your-startup) -- Brad Flora, Pete Koomen, Gustaf Alstromer (2025)

title: Harry Zhang
related: [[Lob: From Inkjet Printer to Enterprise Mail API]]
body:
# Harry Zhang

Harry Zhang is the co-founder and CEO of [Lob](https://lob.com/) (YC S13), a company that provides APIs for enterprises to programmatically send physical mail. Before founding Lob, he worked at Microsoft where he built a direct mail program that became one of the most effective marketing channels for his team [1].

## Worldview

Zhang approaches startups with a sales-driven, customer-obsessed methodology. His philosophy: "We want customers to pay us for something before we actually go and do it" [1]. He treats recruiting, enterprise sales, and product development with the same framework -- understand the individual's motivations and tailor the message accordingly.

He is pragmatic about the unsexy nature of his business. "Mail is not exactly the most sexy thing in the world" [1]. But he sees this as an advantage: the market is enormous, the incumbents are slow, and an API layer can give small companies access to the same capabilities that previously required enterprise-scale operations [1].

## Key Frameworks

**The Platform Fee Model**: When enterprise customers compare your product apples-to-apples against commoditized alternatives, restructure pricing to separate the technology value from the commodity. Lob charges a $25-30K platform fee for API access plus competitive per-piece mail pricing. This shifts the buying conversation from "why is your mail more expensive?" to "is our technology worth $25K?" [1].

**The Lighthouse Customer**: Enterprise sales is a chicken-and-egg problem. You need one big customer to get others. Getting the first one combines luck, hard work, and domain understanding. Once you succeed with one, take "that exact same story to a similar customer" [1].

**Sell Ahead, But Align**: It is acceptable to sell capabilities you do not yet have, but you must be transparent about timelines, get commitments in writing, and treat the customer as a partner in building what they need. The mugs API taught Zhang the cost of building without written commitment; the Booking.com deal taught him the value of honest negotiation [1].

**Founder Role Evolution**: A founder's role changes constantly -- from product builder to sales leader to marketing driver. This is not a problem; it is a signal that the company has matured enough in one area to hire specialists. But the founder must be comfortable with every function: "If you as the founder can't sell the product, nobody else is going to be able to either" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Zhang is distinctive for running a B2B infrastructure company that bridges the physical and digital worlds. While most API companies are purely software, Lob must coordinate with hundreds of physical print partners across multiple countries. His decision not to vertically integrate (not buying printers) reflects a deliberate strategic choice to keep the business asset-light and flexible [1].

He is also notable for implementing RSUs instead of stock options at a private company -- an unusual choice designed to remove financial risk from employees and ensure they do not stay for the wrong reasons [1].

## References

1. [On starting and scaling direct mail automation startup Lob](https://ycombinator.com/library/6a-on-starting-and-scaling-direct-mail-automation-startup-lob) -- Harry Zhang, Kevin Hale (2019)

title: Garry Tan
related: [[Coinbase: From Bitcoin at Five Dollars to a Hundred Billion Dollar Company]]
[[Flexport: From Solo Founder to Eight Billion Dollar Logistics Platform]]
[[True Link Financial: Profitability Through Underserved Markets]]
body:
# Garry Tan

Garry Tan is President and CEO of Y Combinator. He was previously a YC partner for five years (working with 700+ companies) and founded Initialized Capital, a venture fund. Before that, he cofounded Posterous (2008), a blogging platform that experienced rapid growth before stalling. He hosts the rebooted "How to Build the Future" series, interviewing founders including [[Sam Altman]], [[Tony Xu]], [[Parker Conrad]], [[Bob McGrew]], and [[Elon Musk]].

Tan's personal channel features a mix of solo videos on startup principles and long-form interviews with founders he has invested in, including [[Brian Armstrong]] (Coinbase), [[Ryan Petersen]] (Flexport), [[Dylan Field]] (Figma), and Kai Stinchcombe (True Link Financial). He was the first investor in Coinbase, turning a $300,000 check into over $2.4 billion [13].

## Worldview

Tan's advice centers on several interlocking themes:

**Radical honesty about reality.** Tan's experience at Posterous -- where two years of 20-30% monthly growth suddenly went flat and he could not accept it -- left a deep mark on his thinking [2]. He now teaches that founders must confront hard truths, even when their entire worldview depends on denying them. He frames this as "the alchemy: going from the dreamland of an individual to creating the reality with a group of your smartest friends" [2]. See [[Confronting Reality]].

**Clarity and simplicity in communication.** Tan's YC application advice reduces to three rules: show rather than tell, use plain English instead of buzzwords, and structure communication as an inverted pyramid (conclusion first, proof second) [1]. He defines a great pitch as "very simple, understandable statements put side by side that, when you zoom out, you realize that is a really big idea" [1]. See [[Applying to YC]].

**Wealth over money.** Tan draws on [[Paul Graham]] and philosopher Alan Watts to distinguish between money (an integer in a database) and wealth (the ability to create value). "If you're focused on the money, you're gonna get stuck as a cog in the machine. You'll end up focused on that next promotion or the trappings of upward mobility" [12]. The path to wealth runs through acquiring rare skills, creating leverage through products that work while you sleep, and building "wealth machines" -- businesses that generate both money and the capacity to generate more money [12]. See [[Wealth vs. Money]].

**The builder ethos.** Across multiple videos, Tan returns to the idea that the most important credential is the ability to build. He funded Coinbase because Brian Armstrong was "a builder" who had coded the entire first version himself [13]. He describes his own career pivot from Microsoft as the moment he stopped consuming and started creating [12]. In his interview with Armstrong, he observes that "the common thread is being a builder, being very focused on... entering a flow state and then having this outcome" [17].

**DIY and authenticity over perfection.** In his video on movements, Tan draws a parallel between punk rock and startups: "Humans have a visceral reaction to experiencing authenticity, even at the cost of perfection. Everyone is so focused on being perfect these days, and to that I say, fuck perfection. Do it yourself" [19]. He points to Armstrong building Coinbase with Twitter Bootstrap, and the Ramones playing three-chord songs at CBGB, as examples of the same principle [19].

## Personal Story: The $200 Million Mistake

In 2003, fresh out of college at 23, Tan was working at Microsoft earning $72,000/year. His friends were starting a company with Peter Thiel. Thiel wrote Tan a personal check for his full annual salary and told him to quit: "Cash this check. Quit your job. This is a zero-risk opportunity for you" [14]. Tan said no -- "Thank you very much, Mr. Thiel, but I might get promoted to level sixty next year" [14]. The company was Palantir, now worth $20 billion+. The missed equity was worth at least $200 million [14].

Tan did eventually join as employee number ten and built one of the major product teams. But the story is a core teaching moment: the opportunity cost of staying at a big company when a high-conviction early-stage opportunity is available [14]. See [[Leaving Big Tech]].

He also turned down a Google job interview in 2004, noting that "it was obviously useful, but it wasn't clear that it was going to make money" [14]. He uses both experiences to frame the asymmetry of startup economics: Google's pure profit per employee is $1.6 million per year after all costs, but engineers creating that value capture only a fraction of it. "The only way you can really access that is by owning equity" [14].

## On the Coinbase Investment

Tan was one of the first checks in Coinbase's seed round, investing $300,000 that became worth over $2.4 billion at IPO [13]. Three factors drove his yes when nearly everyone else said no:

1. **He was primed for the problem.** He had tried to buy Bitcoin on Mt. Gox and experienced the terrible UX firsthand [13].
2. **He was primed for the idea.** Working at Peter Thiel's hedge fund Clarium Capital in 2005, he read "The Dollar Crisis" by Richard Duncan, which primed him for the possibility of fiat currency failure [13]. As Louis Pasteur said, "Chance only favors the prepared mind" [13].
3. **Brian was a builder.** Armstrong had been head of anti-fraud at Airbnb, essentially "being part of the special forces" against hackers daily, and could build and secure the first version of Coinbase himself [13].

Tan describes his investing philosophy in his Armstrong interview: "What gets me up in the morning is believing before people believe, and then helping people believe themselves" [17].

## On the AI Platform Shift

In his interview with [[Sam Altman]], Tan draws a direct comparison between the current AI moment and the mobile platform shift. He observes that "the world is still sleeping on all of this to such an astonishing degree," which is why he is excited for startups. The platform shift is always built by "the people who are young with no prior knowledge" [3].

He points out that even Facebook nearly missed mobile, having to acquire Instagram, attempt to buy Snapchat, and acquire WhatsApp to compensate. Dominant companies of one era routinely fail to lead the next [3].

## Startup Website Design Principles

In a collaboration with [[Aaron Epstein]], Tan reviews five AI startup websites and distills several principles [11]:

- **What is this? Is it for me?** These are the first two questions every visitor asks. Clever taglines that sacrifice clarity for cleverness fail: "You don't want to be clever if clever equals confusing" [11].
- **Single call to action.** Multiple CTAs of equal weight confuse users. Be opinionated about what you want the user to do [11].
- **Feat of strength.** AI startups should show a demo that impresses -- "show the product and a technological feat of strength" rather than describing it abstractly [11].
- **Don't block the experience.** "Try it now" buttons that lead to login walls destroy trust. Let users experience value before asking for commitment [11].
- **Social proof placement.** Weave logos and customer quotes throughout the page rather than dumping them in one section [11].

See [[Startup Website Design]].

## Six Founder Skills

Tan identifies six skill areas that increase a founder's probability of success [18]:

1. **Engineering** -- the "ultimate moat." PageRank protects billions of dollars. Non-technical founders should learn enough to be dangerous [18].
2. **Product** -- knowing what to build and for whom. A continuous process of finding and refining [[Product-Market Fit]], not a one-time event [18].
3. **Design** -- both interaction design (how it works) and visual design (how it looks). "Having taste" matters more than execution ability [18].
4. **Sales and marketing** -- Ryan Petersen of Flexport calls sales "the most underrated profession." Every business is a meme; capturing attention has never mattered more [18].
5. **Finance** -- not critical at the outset, but essential post-PMF. Matt McGinnis from Rippling: "Once I understood that my company was worth the net present value of all future cash flows, I started making better decisions" [18].
6. **Leadership and management** -- leading from empathy creates more connected teams, but accountability is the harder skill. Tan recommends "How to Win Friends and Influence People" [18].

A single person cannot master all six. "Any given single human being could probably only get truly great at one or two of these skills, but you need a team of people who are great at all of these in order to build a startup or business that really matters" [18]. See [[Six Founder Skills]].

## Better, Faster, or Cheaper

Tan argues that being first to market is overrated -- Facebook was the 20th social network, WordPress preceded Posterous by five years [20]. What matters is being better, faster, or cheaper than alternatives:

- **Better**: Google (technical excellence in search), Dropbox (better than USB drives), Discord (low CPU for gamers), Coinbase (better than Mt. Gox) [20].
- **Faster**: Flexport (replacing paper with software speeds freight by days), Uber (faster to find a car), Facebook (remained fast while Friendster used unoptimized MySQL) [20].
- **Cheaper**: Shopify ($29/month vs. running Magento), Lyft (citizen ride-sharing vs. Uber's initial black cars), Instacart (asset-light grocery delivery), Amazon ("their margin is our opportunity") [20].

See [[Better, Faster, or Cheaper]].

## First Principles Thinking

Tan frames the three prerequisites for [[First Principles Thinking]] as curiosity, empathy, and serenity [23]:

1. **Curiosity** -- using techniques like the Five Whys (Toyota Production System) to understand root causes rather than accepting surface explanations [23].
2. **Empathy** -- getting direct experience with users. Airbnb's founders hosted strangers themselves and discovered "the arc of a friendship gets contracted from a year to a day" [23].
3. **Serenity** -- accepting things you cannot change. DoorDash retreated to suburbs when Uber had 50x their marketing budget, found profitability there, then used those profits to win cities [23].

## On Building Movements

Tan argues that every billion-dollar startup begins as a movement -- "a team and a direction and a thing that helps millions and possibly billions of people" [19]. Drawing on Derek Sivers's TED talk about the "first follower," Tan observes that the leader needs guts, but "the first follower is what transforms a lone nut into a leader" [19]. New followers emulate followers, not the leader [19].

He traces the personal computer movement from the Homebrew Computer Club through Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak showing off at hobbyist meetings, to computers on every desk. Every major startup followed the same pattern: starting as a fringe movement among enthusiasts [19]. See [[Building Movements]].

## On Peer Groups

Tan's most significant personal insight from YC is the power of founder peer groups. He initially thought YC's value was having a respected figure (Paul Graham) who believed in you. The deeper value was the peer group: "The thing that I didn't understand until much later was it was the peer group of other people doing that." He advises young people to find their peer group as early as possible [3].

## Standing Behind Founders When It Is Unpopular

Tan describes backing [[Parker Conrad]] and Rippling "at a time when it was extremely unpopular" [5]. He had seen Conrad's work at YC firsthand and knew that Zenefits' enterprise value would not have existed without Conrad. The press narrative was "exactly the opposite of what you and I lived and experienced" [5].

## On the CEO Decision

Tan draws on his experience at Posterous to argue against co-CEO structures. In 2010, he and his co-founder told Andreessen Horowitz that they were both CEO. "The meeting might as well have ended at that moment" [6]. See [[CEO Role and the Co-CEO Mistake]].

## The Learn/Earn Framework

Tan proposes a career evaluation framework: at any point in your career, ask whether you are learning, earning, or both [7]. If neither, quit. See [[Learn, Earn, or Quit]].

## The Three "Why Nows"

Tan identifies three forces that create windows for startup opportunity: new technology, behavior change, and regulation [8]. See [[Why Now]].

## Key Frameworks

- **The Inverted Pyramid**: Conclusion first, proof second [1].
- **The Dreamland-Reality Bridge**: Hold contradictory beliefs -- visionary future and honest present [2].
- **The Idea Maze**: Know the full history of attempts in your space [2].
- **No Adults in the Room**: No one has all the answers [3].
- **The Co-CEO Trap**: Shared leadership is lethal for startups [6].
- **Learn, Earn, or Quit**: Two-axis career evaluation [7].
- **Three "Why Nows"**: Technology, behavior, regulation [8].
- **The Wealth Machine**: A business that generates both money and the capacity to generate more [12].
- **Above vs. Below the API**: Creators of systems vs. workers directed by them [12].
- **Better, Faster, or Cheaper**: The three axes for competitive differentiation [20].
- **Six Founder Skills**: Engineering, product, design, sales/marketing, finance, leadership [18].
- **Curiosity, Empathy, Serenity**: The three prerequisites for first principles thinking [23].

## Distinct Perspective

Compared to other YC partners, Tan draws heavily on philosophy, psychology, and storytelling. His advice cites Miyamoto Musashi, Karl Popper, Ray Dalio, Daniel Kahneman, Carl Jung, Alan Watts, Reinhold Niebuhr, Naval Ravikant, Venkatesh Rao, and references films, music (punk rock, Nirvana, the Ramones), and sports (Dennis Rodman's rebounding technique). This gives his advice a more personal, narrative quality while still arriving at concrete recommendations.

Tan is unusually candid about his own failures. The Posterous case study in [[Confronting Reality]] is a first-person account of founder denial [2]. The Palantir story is a $200 million lesson in opportunity cost [14]. The Microsoft years are framed as a consumptive cycle he had to break [12].

As an interviewer, Tan is notable for connecting founders' stories to broader principles. In his conversation with Tony Xu, he identified that DoorDash's suburban strategy appeared to be data-driven but was actually driven by customer obsession [4]. He draws the Coinbase investment story as a lesson in contrarianism and prepared minds [13]. He compares Flexport to an electrical utility -- a mundane metaphor that captures the ambition of making global trade as seamless as flipping a light switch [21].

## References

1. [Garry Tan's Tips for Applying to YC](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J7-garry-tan-s-tips-for-applying-to-yc) -- Garry Tan (n.d.)
2. [The Main Function: How to Go from Startup Dream to Reality](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/JG-the-main-function-how-to-go-from-startup-dream-to-reality) -- Garry Tan (n.d.)
3. [How To Build The Future: Sam Altman](https://ycombinator.com/library/Lp-how-to-build-the-future-sam-altman) -- [[Sam Altman]], Garry Tan (2024)
4. [How To Build The Future: Tony Xu](https://ycombinator.com/library/MJ-how-to-build-the-future-tony-xu) -- [[Tony Xu]], Garry Tan (2025)
5. [How To Build The Future: Parker Conrad](https://ycombinator.com/library/M2-how-to-build-the-future-parker-conrad) -- [[Parker Conrad]], Garry Tan (2025)
6. [Should You Be the CEO?](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jq-garry-s-channel-should-you-be-the-ceo) -- Garry Tan (2021)
7. [LEARN, EARN or QUIT: My Job/Career Advice](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jg-garry-s-channel-learn-earn-or-quit-my-job-career-advice) -- Garry Tan (2021)
8. [Why Now? The Key to Million Dollar Startup Ideas](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jt-garry-s-channel-why-now-the-key-to-million-dollar-startup-ideas) -- Garry Tan (2022)
9. [How To Build The AGI Future: Bob McGrew](https://ycombinator.com/library/M6-how-to-build-the-agi-future-bob-mcgrew) -- [[Bob McGrew]], Garry Tan (2025)
10. [Elon Musk: Digital Superintelligence, Multiplanetary Life, How to Be Useful](https://ycombinator.com/library/MX-elon-musk-digital-superintelligence-multiplanetary-life-how-to-be-useful) -- [[Elon Musk]], Garry Tan (June 2025)
11. [Critiquing AI Startup Websites with YC President Garry Tan](https://ycombinator.com/library/Iv-critiquing-ai-startup-websites-with-yc-president-garry-tan) -- [[Aaron Epstein]], Garry Tan (n.d.)
12. [Stop Chasing Money -- Chase Wealth](https://ycombinator.com/library/Je-garry-s-channel-stop-chasing-money-chase-wealth) -- Garry Tan (n.d.)
13. [How I Turned $300k into $2 Billion](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jf-garry-s-channel-how-i-turned-300k-into-2-billion) -- Garry Tan (2021)
14. [My $200 Million Startup Mistake](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jh-garry-s-channel-my-200-million-startup-mistake) -- Garry Tan (n.d.)
15. [Billion Dollar Startup Ideas](https://ycombinator.com/library/Ji-garry-s-channel-billion-dollar-startup-ideas) -- Garry Tan (n.d.)
16. [YOU Can Beat Google the Way Amazon Does. Here's How.](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jj-garry-s-channel-you-can-beat-google-the-way-amazon-does-here-s-how) -- Garry Tan (n.d.)
17. [Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong on Cryptocurrency and the Future of Decentralization](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jk-garry-s-channel-coinbase-ceo-brian-armstrong-on-cryptocurrency-and-the-future-of-decentralization) -- [[Brian Armstrong]], Garry Tan (2021)
18. [6 Skills for Successful Startup Founders](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jl-garry-s-channel-6-skills-for-successful-startup-founders) -- Garry Tan (n.d.)

title: Surbhi Sarna
related: [[nVision Medical: From Ovarian Cyst to $275M Acquisition]]
body:
# Surbhi Sarna

Surbhi Sarna is a Group Partner at Y Combinator, where she is YC's first healthcare and biotech partner. Before YC, she founded nVision Medical (also called Envision Medical), which developed the first and only FDA-cleared device for collecting cells from the fallopian tube for cancer evaluation. Boston Scientific acquired the company for $275 million [1]. See [[nVision Medical: From Ovarian Cyst to $275M Acquisition]].

## Worldview

**Founders do not need advanced degrees to build healthcare companies.** Sarna started nVision at 24 with a bachelor's degree, no MBA, PhD, or MD. She was rejected by at least 50 investors. Her evaluation framework as a YC partner: "As long as you really know your stuff, I am not going to look at whether or not you have an MD or PhD and make my decision exclusively on that. I want to know that there's a big problem that needs solving and you are the founder to do it" [1].

**Personal experience creates founder conviction that others cannot replicate.** Sarna's ovarian cyst at age 13, and the diagnostic limitations her family faced, drove her to write in her college personal statement that she would one day start a company in women's health. That conviction sustained her through 50 fundraising rejections and years of device development [1].

**Ignore bias; find the believers.** Sarna acknowledges that fundraising is harder for women and people of color. Investors called women's health "bikini medicine" and said it was not a big enough market. Her advice: "These days I sincerely believe that there are enough people who believe in your potential that you can just ignore the folks who are being biased for whatever reason. Don't let it impact your confidence" [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Sarna's story bridges healthcare founder experience and YC advising. Her path from patient (at 13) to engineer (at Abbott Vascular) to founder (at 24) to $275M exit to YC Group Partner illustrates how personal experience with a problem creates the conviction needed to survive the long, capital-intensive journey of medical device development.

Her customer discovery story -- pretending to have a stomach ache to get a gynecologist appointment, then pitching a catheter concept during a pelvic exam -- is one of the more memorable [[Talking to Users]] examples in the YC canon. It worked: the physician introduced her to other doctors at UC Davis and Stanford [1].

She is also distinctive in explicitly addressing the fundraising experience of women founders and founders of color, including specific investor quotes that reveal bias in healthcare investing [1].

## Key Frameworks

- **Use Your Existing Network**: You do not need a Rolodex. Start with people you know and ask for introductions. Persistence and time get you to the right people [1].
- **Problem Conviction Over Credentials**: The most important qualifier is that "no one believes in the solution as much as you do," not degrees or pedigree [1].
- **Ignore the Biased, Find the Believers**: Do not let rejection from biased investors erode confidence. There are enough believers to build the company [1].

## References

1. [Backstory: Surbhi Sarna](https://ycombinator.com/library/K2-backstory-surbhi-sarna) -- Surbhi Sarna (2024)

title: Bob McGrew
body:
# Bob McGrew

Bob McGrew is the former Chief Research Officer at OpenAI. Before OpenAI, he spent years at Palantir, where he gained management experience and learned about forward-deployed engineering. He joined OpenAI in 2016 not to work at a research lab but because "it was a place full of very smart people with big ambitions" where he could become an expert in deep learning [1].

## Worldview

**Start with the best model, work backwards.** McGrew's advice to AI startup founders is specific: "Start with the very best model you can. Your startup is only going to be successful if it exploits something about AI that realistically is going to be on the frontier." Once it works, distill to cheaper models. "The most important thing in a startup is actually your time. You don't want to be taking three years to get to market" [1].

**Scale is the central problem.** McGrew identifies scale as the common thread across OpenAI's breakthroughs. Getting from zero to a working model is a separate challenge from scaling it, but once something works, scaling is "probably the practical problem in any sort of model building" -- a systems problem, a data problem, and an algorithmic problem simultaneously [1].

**Software needs forward-deployed engineers.** Drawing from Palantir, McGrew argues that the gap between AI capability and AI adoption exists because nobody is doing the integration work. "We just need more software engineers who are like that forward-deployed engineer to link up the intelligence." Palantir was initially criticized for needing forward-deployed engineers; a decade later, it is a highly valuable company and everyone is building that function [1].

## On OpenAI's Culture

McGrew contrasts OpenAI's culture with DeepMind (top-down plan from Demis) and Google Brain (pure academia, let researchers do whatever they want). OpenAI found a middle ground: "There was no big centralized plan. But at the same time, people didn't just let a thousand flowers bloom. Instead, we had opinions about what needed to be done" -- particularly around scale as the path to improvement [1].

A cultural decision: early papers credited "cite as OpenAI" rather than individual authors, avoiding the academic obsession with credit assignment. "The ability to accept that people want credit, but to channel it into internal reputation rather than paper position, was a really important cultural piece" [1].

## On the Path to AGI

McGrew outlines the progression: GPT-1 (Alec Radford's insight that predicting the next token would produce coherent text), combined with the scale learnings from Dota 2 and the robot hand project, led to GPT-2, GPT-3, and GPT-4. The current frontier: reasoning and test-time compute (O1, O3 models), which McGrew sees as cracking the final piece needed for a clear path to scaling toward AGI [1].

He frames AI progress using the Moore's Law analogy: "One big exponential curve, but it's actually the sum of a bunch of little S-curves." Pre-training data is one S-curve that is approaching its ceiling. Reasoning is the new S-curve [1].

## Two Jobs of the Future

McGrew predicts two dominant roles in an AI-heavy future: "genius" (the lone innovator leveraging AI to amplify individual capability) and "manager" (the CEO of a firm that is mostly AI). "Those are two things that would be really fun jobs, honestly" [1].

On why he teaches his eight-year-old son to code despite AI's coding ability: "Even once the computer can do the programming for you, there's still something to having had your hands in it yourself and knowing what's possible and what's not possible." He cites [[Paul Graham]]'s concept of "the resistance of the medium" [1].

## References

1. [How To Build The AGI Future: Bob McGrew](https://ycombinator.com/library/M6-how-to-build-the-agi-future-bob-mcgrew) -- Bob McGrew, [[Garry Tan]] (2025)

title: Walker Williams
body:
# Walker Williams

Walker Williams is the co-founder and CEO of Teespring (YC W13), an e-commerce platform that allows entrepreneurs to launch products and apparel brands without risk, cost, or compromise. At the time of his 2014 talk, Teespring had grown to approximately 180 employees and was shipping tens of thousands of products daily, generating hundreds of millions of dollars in annual revenue [1].

## Worldview

Williams views doing things that don't scale as a startup's most fundamental competitive advantage -- not a phase to outgrow but a practice to maintain as long as humanly possible. "The moment you give it up, you're giving your competitors that are smaller -- that can still do these things -- that advantage over you" [1].

**There is no silver bullet for user acquisition.** Williams states that every CEO he has spoken with confirms this. "Those first users were impossibly hard to get" for nearly every successful company, regardless of how smooth the growth curve looks from the outside [1].

**Detractors are exponentially destructive.** "One detractor who's had a terrible experience on your platform is enough to reverse the progress of ten champions" [1]. This asymmetry means that making things right after mistakes is not optional -- it is existential.

**Optimize for speed over scalability.** Code quality and clean architecture are secondary to finding [[Product-Market Fit]]. "Necessity is the mother of invention" -- solve scaling problems when they become real, not before [1].

## The Teespring Origin

Williams ran into a personal pain point at Brown University: he wanted to create a memorial t-shirt for a dive bar that was shutting down. No existing solution met his needs. Teespring launched to solve that problem [1].

The early business was "ridiculously unsustainable": days of meetings per customer, free design work, days of revisions, launching the product on behalf of the customer, managing social media -- all to sell 50 shirts and generate $1,000 in revenue. "Anybody looking in would have said, 'You guys need to give up'" [1].

Williams's two biggest customer segments evolved over time: entrepreneurs building brands and businesses (over 1,000 people making their full-time living on Teespring) and influencers (YouTube stars, Reddit communities, bloggers) adding merchandise as a revenue stream [1].

## Three Phases of Unscalable Work

### 1. Finding First Users

Founders must spend personal time bringing in users themselves. "It could mean sending 100 emails a day, getting on the phone and just calling as many people as you can, going through a network" [1]. Williams compares it to pushing a boulder up a hill: the steepest incline is at the beginning, but it gets easier and eventually the boulder rolls on its own [1].

Williams advises against giving away the product for free. Free users treat products differently from paying users, and free distribution creates a false sense of security about conversion potential [1].

### 2. Turning Users into Champions

A champion is a user who talks about and advocates for the product [1]. The easiest way to create champions is to delight users with an experience they will remember -- "something unusual or out of the ordinary" [1].

Three methods for creating champions through direct engagement [1]:

- **Run customer service yourself.** Williams and co-founder Evan handled all customer service until Teespring reached $130,000-$140,000 per month in revenue. Williams describes an "emotional reaction where my stomach sinks" when opening the support portal because "it sucks talking to users who have had a terrible experience." But the learning is irreplaceable.
- **Proactively reach out to current, future, and departed customers.** Do not take current users for granted. When a user leaves, reach out to learn why -- sometimes the personal outreach alone brings them back. Even when it does not, the insight prevents future churn.
- **Monitor social media and communities.** Williams reads every tweet about Teespring and monitors all Teespring communities, describing himself as "probably a little bit OCD."

### 3. Finding Product-Market Fit Through Speed

The product at launch will almost certainly not be the product that scales. The founder's job is to iterate as fast as possible to reach that product [1].

Williams provides a concrete example: enterprise customers requested features that would take a month to build properly. Instead, co-founder Evan duplicated the entire codebase and database, built a separate product for enterprise customers in three to four days, validated which features mattered, and then integrated the winners into the core product [1].

"Only worry about the next order of magnitude": when you have 10 users, think about getting to 100, not 1 million [1].

Williams describes months-long stretches where Teespring's servers crashed every night. Every team member slept with their phone on loud under their pillow. "The reality is that it was worth it" [1].

## When to Stop Doing Things That Don't Scale

Never, if possible. Williams is emphatic: "There's not some magical moment. It's not the Series A. It's not when you hit a certain revenue milestone. As long as humanly possible, as long as it is a net positive, you need to be spending time talking to your users. It should be ripped from you" [1].

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Getting Started, Getting Press, and Doing Things That Don't Scale](https://ycombinator.com/library/JL-how-to-start-a-startup-getting-started-getting-press-and-doing-things-that-don-t-scale) -- Stanley Tang, Walker Williams, Justin Kan (2014)

title: Anu Hariharan
body:
# Anu Hariharan

Anu Hariharan is a partner at YC Continuity, the growth-stage investment arm of Y Combinator. She is one of the most prolific contributors to the YC Library on international growth strategies, Chinese technology ecosystems, board governance, and growth team building.

## Worldview

Hariharan's work reveals several consistent themes:

**International case studies as strategic blueprints.** She repeatedly analyzes Chinese technology companies not as distant curiosities but as blueprints for what will eventually happen globally. Her analyses of [[WeChat]], [[Pinduoduo]], and [[Toutiao]] are structured as lesson sets for Western founders, extracting principles that transcend the Chinese market.

**Social dynamics drive commerce and engagement.** Across her work on Pinduoduo and WeChat, Hariharan returns to the idea that social interaction is a fundamental and underserved layer of online commerce and content. She argues that "Web 1.0 platforms -- including Amazon -- optimize for efficiency and don't serve trust-based categories very well" [2] and that shopping is inherently social.

**Network effects at the group level.** Hariharan draws heavily on Reed's law (the value of a group-forming network grows exponentially with the ease of group formation) rather than Metcalfe's law (value proportional to users squared). She identifies Pinduoduo as having "one of the most powerful network effects in the world" [2] because it taps into groups of social relationships rather than one-to-one connections.

**Board management as a learnable discipline.** In contrast to the anxiety many founders feel, Hariharan treats board management as a set of concrete practices: meeting cadence, deck structure, preparation timelines, and communication rhythms [4]. Her approach is operational and template-driven, drawing on real examples from companies like Gusto, Brex, GitLab, Faire, and Segment.

**Data network effects compound product quality.** Her Toutiao analysis emphasizes that algorithms without data are useless and data without an addictive product is unavailable. The virtuous cycle of user engagement, data accumulation, and algorithmic improvement is a core pattern she identifies across multiple companies [3].

**Growth as a scientific discipline.** Hariharan treats growth as a scientific discipline, not a collection of hacks. She is explicit that "growth hacking" is not a term growth experts relate to -- "hacking implies a haphazard/gut-driven approach, and the reality is quite the opposite" [5]. Companies that achieve sustained growth have intentional, metrics-driven teams that thrive on experimentation.

## Distinct Perspective

Hariharan is distinctive in the YC ecosystem for her deep focus on Chinese technology companies as sources of strategic insight for Western founders. While most YC content is US-centric, Hariharan provides detailed case studies with product screenshots, user flow diagrams, and growth metrics from platforms that most Western founders have never used. She consistently argues that Chinese tech innovations will be replicated globally.

Her approach to growth is methodical and benchmark-driven. She provides specific retention targets by vertical, team composition formulas, and organizational structures backed by consensus from 25 growth experts at companies including Facebook, Airbnb, Uber, Stitch Fix, Square, Slack, and Instagram [5].

## Key Frameworks

- **Content Lifecycle** (from Toutiao analysis): Creation, Curation, Recommendation, Interaction [3]
- **Team Purchase model** (from Pinduoduo): Group buying as a social commerce mechanic that creates network effects [2]
- **Board meeting preparation timeline**: A 28-day preparation framework used by Brex [4]
- **Group Effect**: Product features designed around group behavior rather than individual behavior (from WeChat/Pinduoduo) [1] [2]
- **Retention Before Growth**: Do not invest in growth until retention is proven. The leaky bucket problem wastes resources and jeopardizes the company's future [5]
- **Retention Benchmarks by Vertical**: Specific long-term retention targets (e.g., 55% median for social networks at month 12, 29% for travel at year 2) [5]
- **Absolute Goals**: Growth goals must be absolute numbers, not percentages. "Achieve 5M first-time room nights" not "improve conversion by 10%" [5]
- **Growth Team Composition**: Year 1 team = 1 Growth PM + 2-3 Growth Engineers + 1-2 Data Scientists. First hire when ~15 engineers on the product team [5]

## References

1. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) -- Anu Hariharan (n.d.)
2. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) -- Anu Hariharan, Nic Dardenne (2020)
3. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) -- Anu Hariharan (n.d.)
4. [How to Create and Manage a Board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board) -- Anu Hariharan (n.d.)
5. [How to Set Up, Hire, and Scale a Growth Strategy and Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team) -- Anu Hariharan, Gustaf Alströmer (n.d.)

title: Dalton Caldwell
body:
# Dalton Caldwell

Dalton Caldwell is Managing Director and Group Partner at Y Combinator, where he has worked since 2013. Before YC, he founded iMeem (a social media music platform acquired by MySpace in 2009) and App.net (an ad-free social network). He co-hosts the "Dalton & Michael" video series with [[Michael Seibel]], one of the most prolific sources of YC startup advice, covering fundraising, founder psychology, startup strategy, and the cultural dynamics of Silicon Valley. He appears across 34 sources in this wiki.

## Worldview

Caldwell's advice centers on psychological realism about founder behavior. Where other YC advisors focus on product and market strategy, Caldwell consistently diagnoses the mental traps that cause founders to make bad decisions. Several interlocking themes run across his work:

**Don't lie to yourself.** This is the first of two principles Caldwell uses to close his [[Runway Management]] guide, and it pervades all his advice [11]. Whether discussing runway, [[Technical Co-Founders]], or [[Talking to Users]], Caldwell pushes founders to confront uncomfortable truths rather than rationalizing inaction. In his runway essay, he catalogs seven "counterproductive ideas" that founders use to avoid facing that they are default dead [11]. His second principle: "Act quickly and decisively" [11].

**Ego protection is the root problem.** Caldwell sees most founder self-sabotage as ego protection in disguise. He describes talking to users as "the inoculation" against wasted effort: "There's nothing more humbling. There's nothing that makes you feel shittier than seeing real people using this thing that you built... and just like ripping it to shreds" [22]. He argues surveys are insufficient for real validation -- founders need the higher bar of getting someone to pay money or use the product daily [22]. He pushes back on the question behind the question at office hours, pointing out when co-founder disputes or insecurity are the real issue [25]. Every conversation with Caldwell eventually arrives at the same question: are you protecting your ego, or are you doing the thing that will actually move the company forward?

**Speed creates luck.** His framework for [[Creating Luck]] treats startup success as a function of surface area -- the more shots on goal a founder creates, the more likely one will succeed. He cites Brex (pivoted from VR headsets to credit cards in six weeks), Retool, and Magic as examples of companies that "dramatically increased the surface area of luck" through fast iteration [2]. This applies at every stage: pre-company (make things and share them), during YC (iterate through ideas rapidly), and post-Series A (hire fast-moving people to create a culture of luck) [2].

**Self-conviction precedes all other persuasion.** Caldwell argues that the answer to "how do I convince someone to join my startup?" begins with convincing yourself [1]. "If you're not fully committed or if you have grave doubts that your idea is any good, how can you ever convince someone else?" [1]. He has consistently observed that teams with deep conviction survive setbacks that would kill teams without it -- running out of money, complete pivots, team member departures [1].

**Understand others' incentives.** Caldwell consistently emphasizes that founders must understand the incentives of the people they interact with -- investors, acquirers, potential co-founders. He argues investors are not evil but have their own business goals that may diverge from founders' interests [5]. A smart founder "takes a second to think about those incentives when they're hearing the sales pitch, as opposed to just believing things blindly" [5].

**Overthinking is the enemy.** Caldwell's [[The Midwit Meme]] framework argues that the naive beginner and the seasoned expert arrive at the same simple answer, while the founder with intermediate knowledge overthinks themselves into paralysis. This applies to fundraising, MVPs, co-founder selection, and [[Product-Market Fit]] assessment [17].

**Positive-sum games only.** Caldwell argues that startups should create value, not extract it. Zero-sum thinking -- where one party wins only because another loses -- is structurally incompatible with building enduring companies. Caldwell's test: "What is the leave-behind value that is being created? Am I winning because someone else is losing, or is something real?" [18]. Companies solving real problems survive market downturns because "it turns out that if you're solving someone's real problem and the economy goes down, it's not like they still don't have that problem" [18].

**Caring about customers is a competitive advantage.** Caldwell frames customer care not as altruism but as strategy: "The customer knows in their bones" whether you care [13]. He tells the Airbnb photography story -- how Joe Gebbia staying for coffee with a host unlocked a decade of notes that surveys never could have produced [14]. Caldwell also advises founders who do not naturally love their customer segment to reframe it: "Everyone can like people. Everyone can like helping another person" [13].

**Ground truth over validation.** Caldwell consistently redirects founders away from seeking approval (from investors, press, friends, startup competitions) and toward direct evidence from customers. "Authority figures don't have the answers for a lot of these questions. If you're looking for authority figures to tell you your idea is good, you still aren't really getting the point" [30]. He identifies the "useful lie" every founder starts with -- the assumption they know what customers want -- and urges getting in front of users as fast as possible to confront which hypotheses were wrong [30].

**The fear of consulting is wrong early.** In discussing [[Value Creation]], Caldwell identifies a specific fear that prevents founders from deeply understanding their customers: the fear of becoming a consultant. Every founder wants to blitzscale, and solving one customer's specific problem feels like the opposite. Caldwell argues this fear is "exactly wrong when you're early" -- digging in with a few customers teaches you what is generalizable [7].

**Passion follows traction.** Caldwell argues that passion can follow from a business working, rather than preceding it: "Guess what gives you passion? That damn thing working. Success, users, revenue numbers" [21]. He uses a sports analogy: the best athletes are not always the ones who nerded out on the sport earliest, but the ones who generated a positive feedback loop from natural aptitude [21].

**Be a non-conformist.** Caldwell traces the history of Silicon Valley from a domain of non-conformists to an increasingly mainstream destination for status-seekers. The best founders bet on themselves, are excited by unstructured challenges, and do not seek approval from authority figures. "The really hardcore non-conformists I funded that have done great, they'll be like, 'Yeah, I don't get what all these other people are doing, but this is what I'm doing, and it's going great'" [19].

**Technology history as a guide.** On AI, Caldwell draws parallels to previous platform shifts -- the App Store, cloud computing, open source -- to argue that new technology creates opportunities for startups rather than destroying them [10].

## Distinct Perspective

Caldwell is the most psychologically oriented voice in the YC canon. Where [[Paul Graham]] provides theoretical frameworks and [[Michael Seibel]] provides operational processes, Caldwell diagnoses the emotional and cognitive patterns that derail founders. His advice often takes the form of naming a bias or trap and then showing how founders fall into it.

He brings a practitioner's directness. His language is more blunt and colloquial than most YC content. He is the partner most likely to name the specific lie a founder is telling themselves and restate it as a question: "Would you respond to this email?" or "You don't think your customers are smart or something?" [13].

He is also distinctive for his historical perspective on Silicon Valley culture. His observations about conformists invading the startup world, status-seeking replacing problem-solving, and the tech industry becoming the new finance draw on two decades of personal experience in the ecosystem [19].

Caldwell is also the primary voice on the psychology of low-runway situations -- a topic most startup advisors avoid. His essay on low runway is notable for addressing not just what to do but how to manage the founder's own anxiety, and for being explicit about when to shut down [11].

## Key Frameworks

- **Surface Area of Luck**: The founders that get luckiest are the founders that move fastest, creating maximum opportunities for fortunate outcomes [2]. See [[Creating Luck]].
- **Self-Conviction as Prerequisite**: Before recruiting co-founders, employees, customers, or investors, the founder must first genuinely believe in what they are building [1]. See [[Recruiting Early Team Members]].
- **The Audacity Argument**: "It's equally hard to succeed in a startup that you don't care about and one that you do." Given equal difficulty, always choose the more ambitious and exciting path [1].
- **Fear-Based Decision-Making**: Founders who secretly believe their product is bad will fundraise before metrics exist, seek investor validation over customer feedback, and stockpile money instead of building revenue [3]. See [[Fundraising Mistakes]].
- **[[The Midwit Meme]]**: The naive and expert approaches converge on simplicity; intermediate knowledge produces overthinking and paralysis [17].
- **[[Cargo Culting]]**: Three levels of superficial copying, from imitating Google to imitating struggling startups [20].
- **Zero-Sum vs. Positive-Sum**: Framework for evaluating whether a business creates value or merely extracts it [18].
- **Seven Counterproductive Ideas (Runway)**: Specific rationalizations founders use to avoid confronting low runway [11].
- **Leverage Deterioration Curve**: A startup's negotiating leverage decreases over time as runway shrinks [11].
- **Conformist vs. Non-Conformist**: Diagnostic for whether a founder is building for status or for genuine interest [19].
- **Passion Through Traction**: Passion often follows from a business working, rather than preceding it. "Running something that's working, where you're getting customers, is awesome" [21].
- **The Co-Founder Tiebreaker**: Equal equity with one extra share for the CEO to break fifty-fifty deadlocks [4].
- **Co-Founder First, Then Idea**: Find the co-founder before the idea to create shared ownership. When co-founders are added after the idea, "in their mind it's your company, it's not their company" [4].
- **The Founder's Useful Lie**: "Every founder starts their company with a lie in their head that they know what their customer wants." The more you build before showing users, the more the lie grows. Contact with reality is the antidote [30].
- **The "Find a Lead" Soft No**: When an investor says "come back when you find a lead," it usually means no [5].
- **The Non-Attachment Principle**: "When you're overly attached to some particular thing happening in your life, it tends to not happen" [9].
- **The Irreplaceability Test**: If this person disappeared tomorrow, how hosed would you be? [9].
- **Three Capitals Test**: A potential customer's opinion is only valuable if they will spend financial capital (pay), time capital (use regularly), or social capital (share with friends) [22].
- **The Question Behind the Question**: The real issue in any conversation is usually not the stated topic [25].
- **Adventure Pitch**: The right way to recruit a technical co-founder is to offer adventure and partnership, not to pitch an idea and assign work [16].
- **Discipline Over Intelligence**: "You can't really change your raw intelligence, but you can get better at this. Smart people certainly have an advantage, but man, hard workers are dangerous" [30].
- **The Driver's License Analogy**: External validation (investors, press, competitions) does not mean the product works -- just as getting a driver's license does not mean you are a good driver [30].
- **The Bootstrap-VC Non-Debate**: The decision is simple and revisitable; the controversy is manufactured for engagement [35].
- **Commit or Validate**: Founders with expertise should commit to their vision; founders building expertise should validate aggressively [36].
- **VC as the NBA**: Starting a VC-backed company is comparable to making the NBA -- possible but not the standard path to wealth [35].
- **The Mental Story Test**: If an application reader can construct a clear story from your application, it is good [38].
- **Final Fantasy Grinding Analogy**: Defer starting to level up skills; the grind compounds into power later [39].


## On the Bootstrap vs. VC Debate

Caldwell argues the debate is "fake news" -- a manufactured controversy that gets engagement but does not represent a real disagreement. "The vast majority of businesses should not raise venture capital. And I'll extend that point: venture capital is not interested in investing in the vast majority of businesses" [35]. He compares VC-backed startups to the NBA: getting in is extremely hard and not the typical path to wealth [35]. See [[Bootstrapping vs. Venture Capital]].

## On Founder Intuition

With Seibel, Caldwell introduces the [[Commit or Validate]] framework, which argues that founders with domain expertise should trust their gut more and founders without it should validate more [36]. The crossed-wires failure mode: expert founders who second-guess themselves (building AI features they do not believe in to raise money) and novice founders who overcommit to detailed plans before learning anything [36].

## As Startup Terminology Educator

In a Startup School session, Caldwell defines key terms including MVP, venture capital, angel investors, burn rate, SAFE notes, TAM, and product-market fit [37]. His definition of PMF centers on the shift in priorities: pre-PMF, "all you should be worried about is finding product market fit"; post-PMF, "your job is to stay in that state" [37]. See [[Startup Terminology]].

## On the YC Application Process

Caldwell provides the definitive guide to YC applications and interviews. He reveals his internal evaluation process: constructing a mental story from each application and testing whether the basics are clear [38]. He connects applying to YC with his broader framework on [[Creating Luck]]: "Certain people are luckier than others, but one of the ways you can create luck is to put yourself out there more" [38]. See [[Applying to YC]].

## On Student Founders

Caldwell and Seibel address high school students directly. Caldwell introduces the Final Fantasy VII "grinding" analogy for upskilling before starting a company: "Investing and leveling yourself up can produce a lot of benefits later on" [39]. He provides the poetry critic example: his high school girlfriend who loved poetry played the long game and became the New York Times poetry critic decades later [39]. See [[Starting Young]].

## References

1. [How Do You Convince Someone to Join Your Startup?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5b-how-do-you-convince-someone-to-join-your-startup) -- Dalton Caldwell (n.d.)
2. [How Do You Create Luck?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7L-how-do-you-create-luck) -- Dalton Caldwell (n.d.)
3. [YC Founders Made These Fundraising Mistakes](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DY-dalton-michael-yc-founders-made-these-fundraising-mistakes) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)
4. [Co-Founder Mistakes That Kill Companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DZ-dalton-michael-co-founder-mistakes-that-kill-companies-and-how-to-avoid-them) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2022)
5. [Understanding Investor Terms and Incentives](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DX-dalton-michael-understanding-investor-terms-and-incentives) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2022)
6. [How to Live in the Social Media Matrix](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LU-how-to-live-in-the-social-media-matrix) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
7. [How Generosity Built Tech Giants](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IO-dalton-michael-how-generosity-built-tech-giants) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
8. [Do Technical Founders Need a Business Co-Founder?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KV-do-technical-founders-need-a-business-co-founder) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
9. [How to Influence Decision Makers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LQ-how-to-influence-decision-makers) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
10. [Will OpenAI Kill All Startups?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ie-dalton-michael-will-openai-kill-all-startups) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
11. [Advice for Companies with Less Than 1 Year of Runway](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3Z-advice-for-companies-with-less-than-1-year-of-runway) -- Dalton Caldwell (n.d.)
12. [Why Investors (Secretly) Love YC](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Jd-dalton-michael-why-investors-secretly-love-y-combinator) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)

[YC's Group Partners Share Their Favorite Pivot Stories](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iy-yc-s-group-partners-share-their-favorite-pivot-stories) -- [[Harj Taggar]], Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]], [[Jared Friedman]], Gustaf Alstromer, [[Tom Blomfield]], Brad Flora, Aaron Epstein (n.d.)

36. [When Should You Trust Your Gut?](https://ycombinator.com/library/Kw-when-should-you-trust-your-gut) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
37. [Starting A Company? The Key Terms You Should Know](https://ycombinator.com/library/Lk-starting-a-company-the-key-terms-you-should-know) -- Dalton Caldwell (2024)
38. [How to Apply and Succeed at YC](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/It-how-to-apply-and-succeed-at-yc) -- Dalton Caldwell (2024)
39. [The Student's Guide to Becoming a Successful Startup Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IZ-dalton-michael-the-student-s-guide-to-becoming-a-successful-startup-founder) -- Dalton Caldwell, [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)

title: Gustaf Alstromer
body:
# Gustaf Alstromer

Gustaf Alstromer is a Group Partner at Y Combinator. Before YC, he was Head of Growth at Airbnb. He appears across multiple YC sources, primarily on topics related to early-stage customer acquisition, sales, and launching.

## Worldview

Alstromer's advice is grounded in the belief that early-stage growth is a founder execution problem, not a marketing or product problem. His recurring themes:

**Sales is a numbers game that founders must do themselves.** Alstromer argues that "learning how to do sales actually gives you full control of your destiny as a startup" [1]. He teaches founders to work backwards from goals through a sales funnel, track conversion rates, and accept that most recipients of outbound emails are not early adopters. The most common mistake he sees: founders send too few emails, get zero customers, and conclude that "sales is not working" -- when the real problem is insufficient volume [1].

**Founders can learn sales easily.** "It's probably the easiest job to learn in a startup if you know the problem you're solving, if you know your product intimately, if you know the market" [1]. He cites Tony Xu (DoorDash), Tracy Young (PlanGrid), and Steve Jobs as founders who learned to sell [1].

**Start with the easiest customers.** "Your first customers should be your easiest" [1]. Sell to people you know first, then to startups (short decision-making lines, no procurement departments), and accept that most people are not early adopters [1].

**Charge from day one.** "If you don't charge your customers, they are not a customer, and you don't have a company" [1]. For B2B, Alstromer recommends money-back guarantees over free trials [1].

**Launching and talking to customers serve the same purpose.** In the Launching video, Alstromer reinforces that programmers avoid customer interaction because coding is comfortable: "Very comfortable sitting behind a computer writing code" solving "discrete, well-defined problems" where "the code compiles or the code does not compile" [2]. Customer feedback is ambiguous and feels personal. But launching early is how founders discover if they are even talking to the right users [2].

## Distinct Perspective

Compared to other YC partners, Alstromer brings a uniquely data-driven approach to early-stage sales. His sales funnel math -- showing that 100 emails yields zero customers while 500 yields two -- is one of the most concrete tools in the YC curriculum. He is also self-identified as an early adopter: "I remember being one of those people when I worked at Airbnb. I would love when founders emailed me with new products" [1].

His advice bridges the gap between the philosophical ("do things that don't scale") and the tactical (here is exactly how to write the email, here is the CRM tool, here is the math on how many emails to send).

## Key Frameworks

- **Sales Funnel Math**: Work backwards from your goal. If each funnel stage has known drop-off rates, you can calculate exactly how many outreach emails you need to send [1].
- **Early Adopter Search**: Since most people are not early adopters, volume is the only way to find them. "You don't have time to convince anybody to become an early adopter. You have to find early adopters and just go for them" [1].
- **Unscalable-to-Scalable Bridge**: Even if your eventual growth channel is SEO or word of mouth, "that's not how Airbnb started." Every company begins with unscalable founder-led tactics [1].
- **Startup Curve**: TBD (initially drawn by YC, labeled by Trevor Blackwell) -- launch, early adopter buzz, trough of sorrow, crash of ineptitude, wiggles of false hope, promised land of product-market fit [1].

## References

1. [How to Get Your First Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ip-how-to-get-your-first-customers) -- Gustaf Alstromer (2023)
2. [Why Startup Founders Should Launch Companies Sooner Than They Think](https://ycombinator.com/library/LG-why-startup-founders-should-launch-companies-sooner-than-they-think) -- Gustaf Alstromer et al. (2023)

title: Nicolas Dessaigne
body:
# Nicolas Dessaigne

Nicolas Dessaigne is a Group Partner at Y Combinator. He was the co-founder and CEO of Algolia, a search API used by millions of developers that powers over 1.75 trillion searches annually for 17,000+ customers worldwide [1]. His YC content focuses primarily on building and selling dev tools.

## Worldview

Dessaigne brings a practitioner's perspective from building Algolia from two technical co-founders with no business background to a major enterprise product. His core themes:

**Technical founders are the best salespeople for dev tools.** 74% of YC dev tool companies have only technical co-founders [1]. Dessaigne lived this: "We ended up creating Algolia with two tech co-founders. It crossed our mind: 'Hey, we don't know how to sell.' No. It's actually way easier. Learning to sell is way easier than learning how to speak with a developer" [1].

**Developers are the best marketers for developer products.** "I don't know a single dev tool company that is happy with their CMO when they have a traditional marketing background" [1]. At Algolia, every engineer did a "marketing hack" every month. "All of our best marketing at Algolia was done by developers" [1].

**Documentation is product.** Dessaigne treats documentation as first-class: "Documentation should never be an afterthought. It should be part of the product. Documentation is marketing" [1]. At Algolia, features were not considered done until docs were done, and the documentation team existed to help developers write better docs, not to write docs for them [1].

**Support is marketing.** Engineers should do support, which produces two benefits: satisfied developer-customers who interact with peers who speak their language, and engineers who better understand customer pain [1]. Algolia waited until they had hundreds of employees before creating a dedicated support team [1].

**Runtime ideas beat build-time ideas.** Build-time tools (docs, QA, testing) are nice-to-have and crowded. Runtime tools (APIs, infrastructure) are must-have and have usage-based growth dynamics that align incentives with customers [1].

**Open source is a go-to-market strategy.** For libraries, frameworks, and data-sensitive products, open source is mandatory. For others, it is a powerful differentiator. But monetization must be planned: hosting/cloud, open core, or (least recommended) support services [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Dessaigne is the most specific voice on dev tools in the YC canon. Where others give general startup advice, he addresses the unique dynamics of selling to developers: no sales decks (show demonstrations), bottom-up adoption, renamed sales roles ("product specialists"), and the build-time vs. runtime distinction.

He is also notable for his emphasis on Hacker News as the primary launch platform for dev tools and his detailed analysis of open source monetization models. His Algolia examples -- from closing the first $2,000/month contract with a command-line demo to not having a sales deck until $10 million ARR -- provide concrete benchmarks.

## Key Frameworks

- **Build-Time vs. Runtime**: Runtime ideas are must-have, with usage-aligned growth; build-time ideas are nice-to-have and crowded [1]
- **Good/Better/Best Pricing**: Self-serve for developers (low price), team tier for engineering managers (collaboration), enterprise tier for CTOs (security, SLAs, sales-led) [1]
- **Open Source Decision Tree**: Must be open source (libraries, frameworks, sensitive data products), could be open source (APIs, applications), monetized via hosting, open core, or support [1]
- **Documentation as Product**: Features are not done until docs are done; documentation team helps engineers write better docs [1]
- **Founder-Led Sales Until $1M ARR**: Do not hire salespeople until roughly $1M in annual recurring revenue [1]

## References

1. [How To Start A Dev Tools Company](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Lu-how-to-start-a-dev-tools-company) -- Nicolas Dessaigne (2024)

title: Jess Lee
body:
# Jess Lee

Jess Lee is a partner at Sequoia Capital. She studied computer science at Stanford (graduating around 2004), worked as a PM for Google Maps, and then co-founded Polyvore, a fashion and shopping app where she served as CEO for eight and a half years before joining Sequoia [1].

## Worldview

**Grit is the most important founder quality.** Lee identifies multiple forms of grit: deep passion for the problem, demonstrated history of running through walls, naive optimism that keeps people going when they do not know how hard something will be, and a chip on the shoulder from being underestimated [1]. Starting a company is "almost irrational" because the odds are so bad.

**The problem must be explained before the solution.** Lee observes that many founders pitch solutions without explaining the problem. "It should be so crisp and clear what the problem is that you're solving that the solution almost just flows from that" [1]. She experienced this challenge firsthand at Polyvore, a fashion app pitched to investors who were 94% male. Telling them "imagine an app where you can buy dresses all day" did not work. The Polyvore team solved it by stacking September issue fashion magazines on the table and saying: "This stack of magazines is 300 million dollars of revenue in one issue. Imagine that on the internet" [1].

**Investor selection is a 10-year marriage you cannot divorce.** You cannot kick investors off your cap table. Lee advises founders to treat investor selection with the same gravity as choosing a co-founder: "You're partnering with a person who you're going to be married to for 10 years, except you can't get divorced" [1].

## On Fundraising

### What Sequoia Looks For

Three things [1]:
1. **A founder who is special in some way** -- a unique insight, demonstrated grit
2. **A clear understanding of the problem** -- not just the solution
3. **A really big market** -- line of sight to billions of dollars of market cap

### Running a Competitive Process

Lee recommends going all-in on fundraising rather than treating it as a side activity. Prioritize conversations based on desire to work with the investor multiplied by likelihood of success. Create a competitive dynamic by timing conversations to overlap and being transparent about timelines: "I'm hoping to get this done by this date" [1].

### Choosing the Right Investor

Beyond valuation and terms, Lee advises evaluating [1]:
- **The individual partner**: Will they be a shock absorber during lows and a sparring partner during highs?
- **The firm's network and founder community**: Peer support from other founders elevates everyone's game
- **Preparation**: If an investor walks into a meeting knowing nothing about you, that is a bad sign

Lee describes the ideal investor as someone who provides constructive feedback without being either a pure cheerleader or a critic. Her Sequoia partner Brian Shrier was "super supportive about the good things. And on the things that needed fixing, he didn't yell at me, but he pointed them out logically." Investors who just want to be best friends are not optimal. Investors who are totally uninvolved are also not optimal. And investors who are too prescriptive -- "my sister uses your product and thinks the buttons should go here" -- create different problems [1].

### Counterintuitive Advice

Lee advises against taking office space in your investor's offices. The investor's proximity inhibits experimentation: "If you know that your investor is going to come look at your spreadsheet or look at your product, you're not going to try the super weird, crazy idea" [1].

### When to Raise

At the friends-and-family stage, the tipping point is personal commitment: being okay with the near-certainty of losing the money. At later stages, milestones provide fuzzy guidelines -- for SaaS, roughly $1 million ARR growing 20-30% monthly might signal Series A readiness [1].

## Distinct Perspective

Lee brings the rare combination of founder experience (8.5 years as CEO of Polyvore) and venture capital perspective (Sequoia partner). She is particularly insightful on the challenge of pitching to investors who have no personal connection to the problem -- a challenge she experienced repeatedly pitching a fashion product to male investors. Her advice on investor selection carries the weight of having lived through a long founder-investor relationship.

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Raising Money and Succeeding Long-Term](https://ycombinator.com/library/JM-how-to-start-a-startup-raising-money-and-succeeding-long-term) -- Jess Lee, Aaron Harris, Ali Rowghani (2014)

title: Anne Wojcicki
related: [[23andMe: Disrupting Healthcare by Empowering the Consumer]]
body:
# Anne Wojcicki

Anne Wojcicki is the co-founder and CEO of [23andMe](https://www.23andme.com/), a direct-to-consumer genetic testing company. Before founding 23andMe, she spent 10 years on Wall Street investing in healthcare -- small-cap biotechs, pharma, hospital systems, and insurance companies. She got her first Wall Street job by accident after attending a job fair for the free frequent flyer miles [1].

## Worldview

Wojcicki's thinking is shaped by a fundamental critique of healthcare incentives. The system monetizes illness; no one profits from keeping people healthy. "If I successfully get you to never be diabetic, no one makes money" [1]. Her response is to put consumers in the "power seat" by making them direct purchasers of their own health data, bypassing the insurance-doctor-pharmacy chain that typically makes decisions on their behalf [1].

She believes in persistence above almost everything else. 23andMe's Series A OKRs from 2007 were nearly identical to the company's goals a decade later. "One of the most important things I've learned at doing this now is the importance of persistence. You have to stick with it" [1]. She and [[Sam Altman]] agree that the secret to overnight success is ten years of persistence [1].

On regulation, her view evolved from adversarial to respectful. She acknowledges that FDA regulators are public servants with decades of experience and a legitimate mission to protect consumers. The onus is on the startup to communicate in the regulator's language: data, publications, and structured evidence [1].

## Key Frameworks

**The Trough of Sorrow in Consumer Products**: After a strong launch, 23andMe dropped to selling 15-25 kits per day. The fix was education: iterating on public talks (never giving the same one twice), identifying barriers to adoption (people did not understand genetics), and leading with specific value propositions (over 50% of customers get a medically meaningful result) [1].

**Hiring for Humility**: Between smart people who have humility and smart people who want to demonstrate how smart they are, always choose humility. People who are open to feedback and criticism create environments that attract more talent [1].

**Publishing as Credibility**: Science companies cannot sell credibility through claims. "No one believes that. What you need to do is you need to publish" [1]. Wojcicki never argues with scientists; she hands over publications.

## Distinct Perspective

Wojcicki is distinctive for having built 23andMe at the intersection of consumer technology, genetics, and regulated healthcare. Her Wall Street background gave her a systems-level view of healthcare incentives that most founders lack. Her strategy of crowdsourcing health data from millions of consumers to enable drug discovery and personalized medicine is unusual in the YC canon -- most health tech companies sell to providers or payers, not directly to consumers [1].

## References

1. [On starting and scaling healthcare startup 23andMe](https://ycombinator.com/library/5I-on-starting-and-scaling-healthcare-startup-23andme) -- Anne Wojcicki, Sam Altman (2018)

title: Geoff Ralston
related: [[Embark Trucks: From Campus Shuttles to Self-Driving Semis]]
body:
# Geoff Ralston

Geoff Ralston is a partner at Y Combinator. He interviewed the Embark Trucks founding team in late 2015 and stayed in close contact with them through their IPO. His public writing in the YC canon focuses on evaluating young founders and hard tech companies [1].

## Key Perspectives

Ralston demonstrates how YC evaluates very young founders. When interviewing the 19-20 year old Embark team, his initial concern was whether they were "merely playing with golf carts and having a good time while never planning to leave school" [1]. What changed his mind: "Their thinking was startlingly clear and robust. Their answers were direct and to the point. They had a plan and were serious and, by the way, they'd already built the first self-driving vehicle in Canada" [1].

His conclusion: "Those qualities make age irrelevant" [1]. This aligns with the broader YC stance on [[Starting Young]] -- credentials matter less than demonstrated building and clarity of thought.

## References

1. [Embark Trucks' (W16) road to IPO](https://ycombinator.com/library/Co-embark-trucks-w16-road-to-ipo) -- Geoff Ralston (2021)

title: Ali Rowghani
body:
ENRICH EXISTING ARTICLE with the following from source [3]:

## The Pixar Four: Biographical Detail

In his 2014 Stanford lecture, Rowghani provides detailed character portraits of the four Pixar/Disney leaders that inform his later published frameworks [3]:

**John Lasseter**: Extroverted artist. Studied cartoons. Boisterous, fun-loving, emotional. Massive toy collection. Loved wine and good food. Dozens of hobbies. Did not manage his time well. Gave everyone hugs. Wore Hawaiian shirts every day [3].
