The AI team discovered that 100 headlines is a retention threshold: "users that do not retain long-term tend to drop off dramatically after seeing ~100 headlines, similar to Facebook's '10 friends' rule" [1].

For each new user, the recommendation engine blends three signal types [1]:

1. **User profiles**: Built from demographics (age, location, gender, socioeconomic status)
2. **Content analysis**: Natural language processing determines trending status, article length, and timeliness (evergreen vs. short half-life)
3. **Context**: Location-related data (geography, weather, local news)

The system optimizes for two metrics: the percentage of articles a user clicks on and the percentage of articles a user finishes reading (measured by time on page) [1]. The engine learns quickly: "for most users, it takes less than one day to successfully learn their interests (indicated by 80% read rates)" [1]. The result is user retention above 45%, comparable to social networks [1].

### Interaction: Algorithmic Matchmaking

Toutiao developed a question-and-answer feature where AI matches question-askers with people who can answer them [1]. Their "Conditional Focused Neural Question Answering with Large-Scale Knowledge Bases approach" achieved 75.7% accuracy on a dataset of 108,000 questions, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by an 11.8% margin [1].

## Force 3: From Aggregation to Destination

Toutiao transitioned from content aggregation to content destination through two mechanisms [1]:

### Revenue Sharing for Creators

Starting in 2014, Toutiao rolled out incentive programs for content creators: office space, tools, minimum monthly guarantees tied to milestones, and revenue sharing from ad monetization [1]. By the time of writing, Toutiao hosted more than 800,000 Toutiaohao accounts (professional media, bloggers, influencers) [1].

### Algorithmic Audience Reach

For many creators, Toutiao's recommendation engine delivers larger and more relevant audiences than other platforms [1]. The example of "Huanzi TV," a creator of short videos about rural Chinese life, illustrates this: each video averaged 700,000 views on Toutiao versus less than 1/40th of that on their WeChat Official Account [1].

Content diversity is broad: top 20 categories account for only 60% of supply, with no single category exceeding 10% [1].

[![Toutiao content channels](../raw/posts/images/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao/06de1a2b708a.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/10/Toutiao-5-1.png)

## Force 4: Format Flexibility

Rather than being rigid about core format, Toutiao expanded to new formats when data indicated they should [1]. In 2015, observing increased video content supply as connectivity improved, Toutiao added video capability and supported PGC short video content (1-5 minutes) [1].

In March 2016, Toutiao launched a separate short video app (later renamed Watermelon Video), powered by the same recommendation engine [1].

## Force 5: Early Monetization Aligned with Product

Toutiao's advertising model maps directly to its core strength: identifying what users want to see [1]. Ads are matched to users using the same proprietary recommendation technology. Three benefits [1]:

1. **Reduced friction**: Relevant ads feel less intrusive and can actually function as product discovery
2. **Higher ad rates**: Toutiao's native targeting solves the problem advertisers spend enormous sums trying to address
3. **More inventory**: Users reading and viewing content are more receptive to relevant ads

Third-party survey data estimated Toutiao's click-through rates to be 200% better than its peers [1].

## Broader Implications

Toutiao is "chipping away at their end goal, which is essentially to wipe away the concept of search and just serve up aggregated, hyper-relevant content" [1]. The success suggests that content aggregators that previously failed in the US may have been an "idea whose time is yet to come -- and that better algorithms will be the catalyst for success" [1].

## References

1. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) — [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)

title: Athelas
body:
# Athelas

Athelas (YC S16) makes low-cost devices that enable rapid blood diagnostics through computer vision. The company was founded by Tanay Tandon and his co-founder Deepika. It started as a proof-of-concept built overnight at [YC Hacks 2014](https://ychacks.devpost.com/submissions/25781-athelas) and grew into a shippable medical product [1].

## From Hackathon to Product

[![Athelas hackathon prototype](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/dc4f11da762d.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-1.png)

The first version used a rubber piece and spherical magnifier attached to a smartphone camera. A blood sample was held (by a toilet paper roll) underneath, the camera took images, and computer vision rendered malaria cell counts. The design was similar to a [van Leeuwenhoek microscope](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antonie_van_Leeuwenhoek) -- one of the earliest microscopes built, used to see microorganisms for the first time [1].

[![Athelas cell classification](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/21b8f50e25a9.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-2.png)

[![Athelas cell segmentation](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/393ab971f08b.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-4.png)

[![Athelas cell recognition output](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/df9dcd053523.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-5.png)

The hackathon focused on segmentation and template matching combined with a fast random forest model that classified extracted Red Blood Cells. Cell boundaries were recognized, then fed into the classifier to identify parasitic cells like Malaria or Trypanosoma [1].

"While functioning and a neat trick, someone needed to be physically holding the camera in place, the slide had to be moved around, with the lighting often being hard to fix. At the end of the day it was a nice experimental toy you might see someone post as a video on Facebook" [1].

## Technical Evolution

Turning the demo into a product required advances on multiple fronts [1]:

[![Athelas hardware evolution](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/eb2bd58bdaed.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-6.png)

**Hardware**: Higher-resolution optics in a standalone, cheap device with actuation systems and Gaussian edge autofocusing algorithms for consistent cell capture.

**Sample preparation**: Deepika perfected a fast staining mechanism, synthesizing dozens of stain compound versions and coating them on plastic strips for out-of-the-box use. The strip needed to create a "monolayer" (single layer of cells) for statistically representative imaging.

**Computer vision**: Training data assembled from public CDC images and blood smears collected from researchers at Stanford and UCSF, often hand-labeled by Tandon or a pathologist. Traditional computer vision and deep learning approaches classified cell types.

[![Athelas deep learning classification](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/d27965e1b0d8.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-7.png)

[![Athelas training data](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/4daccd321fda.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-8.png)

## Clinical Validation

During YC (full-time, summer batch), clinical validation was conducted locally and at the FEMAP family hospital. The goal: prove the system on White Blood Cell counts. Results across 350 patients showed high-accuracy correlation with the gold-standard Beckman Counter [1].

Athelas's computer vision approach has an advantage over traditional Coulter counting. Coulter counters flow particles through a jeweled aperture and record impedance to classify by particle size. Particulate matter or lymph can confuse a Coulter system, especially in diluted quantities. Athelas's vision classifies such artifacts as non-leukocyte cellular bodies, avoiding false counts [1].

The trial showed 100% 5-class inter-rater agreement between the two systems. Data was submitted to the FDA for Class 2 510(k) approval, and distribution began for the Class 1 version [1].

[![Athelas clinical validation results](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/6ae9b7460c81.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-13.png)

[![Athelas final product](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/104f634a1e0c.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-14.png)

[![Athelas device and app](../raw/posts/images/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project/e9768e42baba.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/01/athelas-15.png)

## Challenges

Progress was slow before YC. College workload at Stanford combined with rising hardware iteration costs made consistent progress difficult. "Finals often meant days going by without any tangible progress whatsoever" [1]. Going from hackathon prototype to shippable product was "a progression in dimensionality at every stage" [1].

The devices shipped at $250 for point-of-care locations, homes, and clinical settings, with plans to integrate concussion monitoring, inflammation tracking, urinary tract infection detection, platelets, and additional cell counts [1].

## References

1. [On Starting a Company from a Hackathon Project](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project) -- Tanay Tandon (January 2017)

title: WeChat
body:
# WeChat

WeChat is a Chinese super app with 889 million monthly active users (as of 2016) who use the app an average of 50+ minutes and 9-11 separate times per day [1]. This usage matches the combined time users spend across the entire portfolio of Facebook apps (Instagram, Facebook, and Messenger) [1]. [[Anu Hariharan]], partnering with China Tech Insights (a Tencent research group), documented the growth strategy that took WeChat from 0 to 800M+ MAUs in less than six years.

WeChat has inspired a new category of "messaging as a platform" globally. Hints of WeChat's influence appear in Apple's iMessage and Facebook's Messenger platform [1]. As Hariharan puts it: "At this point, WeChat is not only an app -- it is simply the internet" [1].

## Lesson 1: Build Your Own Competition

Tencent was already one of China's top internet companies in 2010, with ~650 million monthly active instant messenger accounts through QQ, its desktop messaging app [1]. The company had created a mobile version of QQ in 2008, but it was "weighted down by desktop-centric features" like multiple user statuses, large file transfers, and embedded services [1].

Tencent made a decisive move: it assembled a small skunkworks team from the QQ Mail team (its core product at the time) to build a new mobile app that could potentially compete with other Tencent products [1]. This team of only seven engineers created the first version of WeChat in three months [1]. WeChat launched in January 2011 as a simple messaging and photo sharing app.

WeChat was not the first mobile messenger. Xiaomi's MiTalk led with 5 million registered users [1]. Initial growth was slow. The breakthrough came in May 2011 (three months after launch) when WeChat added voice messaging, allowing users to send short audio notes [1]. This was inspired by TalkBox and Kakao Talk, and was strategic because "the native mobile keyboard to type Chinese characters at the time was not easy to use and consumers found sending voice notes intuitive, personal and convenient" [1]. As one of the first messengers to offer voice, text, and photos in a combined solution, daily downloads spiked to 50,000-60,000 consistently [1].

Mobile QQ continues to operate with 652 million MAUs (as of December 2016), differentiated by entertainment features (animated video stickers, facial beautifying tools), while WeChat focuses on utility services for daily life [1].

The lesson echoes Amazon's cannibalization of book sales with Kindle and Uber's move from town cars to UberX: "If you don't create a culture of disrupting yourself, then a competitor is likely to disrupt you" [1].

## Lesson 2: Design for Groups ("Group Effect")

Allen Zhang, founder of WeChat, was "adamant about developing the product around 'Group Effect'" -- the observation that individual user behavior differs from behavior in groups [1]. The WeChat team focused on how features would be adopted by groups of users, not just individuals. This accelerated adoption rates through bandwagon effects [1].

### Solving the Cold Start Problem

Like all social networks, WeChat faced the zero-friends problem. While Facebook was famous for getting users to 10 friends within 14 days, WeChat took a different approach [1].

**People Nearby** was a location-enabled feature that showed users others nearby who were also using the feature [1]. It satisfied curiosity about what was happening around them and boosted community feeling. User acquisition jumped to over 100,000 per day [1].

WeChat then enabled account linking between WeChat and QQ, allowing users to import their existing social graph. Importantly, the importing feature was only exposed to users already on both platforms, preserving differentiation [1]. This pushed registered users to 50 million by November 2011, overtaking MiTalk [1].

**Shake** used GPS and accelerometer data to let users shake their phones to find random chat partners [1]. It served dual purposes: giving new users engagement and creating an offline-visible phenomenon (similar to Pokemon Go). Shake was used over 100 million times in its first month (October 2011) [1].

**Message in a Bottle** allowed users to exchange random messages by throwing a virtual bottle into a virtual sea [1].

After launching these group interaction features, new user acquisition hit 200,000 per day in November 2011 [1].

## Lesson 3: Extend Features from Users' Inner Desires

Allen Zhang was "passionate about keying into the inner desires behind an exhibited behavior, which often led to features that fit naturally into users' worlds" [1]. WeChat's research approach was observational rather than traditional: the team closely observed how users behaved among groups of friends and strangers in everyday life, rather than relying on surveys, interviews, or competitive analysis [1].

### Moments

Launched in WeChat's second year, Moments (Chinese translation: "friend circles") was a photo-sharing feature for sharing with a private group of friends [1]. Unlike Facebook's News Feed, comments and likes on a post can only be seen by mutual friends, not friends of friends. This was inspired by Path (a US photo-sharing app limited to close connections) but grounded in an old Chinese philosophy called "circle cultures" -- "smaller circles are much stronger at the core and as the circles get bigger, the ties get weaker" [1].

A team of only 10 people spent 4 months making over 30 versions of Moments before deciding on a version to launch [1]. The core product functions remain largely unchanged from the original version.

### Official Accounts (OA)

Released in 2012, Official Accounts allowed a one-way follow (similar to Twitter, competing with Weibo) where fans could subscribe to celebrity content [1]. The differentiator: celebrities could send text, voice, and video updates that looked like regular WeChat messages, "creating a feeling of a personal and private conversation" [1]. This prompted many celebrities to set up WeChat OAs even with millions of Weibo followers.

OAs expanded to brands and businesses, becoming a channel for direct customer communication (booking, customer care, feedback, Q&A) rather than just broadcasting [1]. "Where Twitter/Weibo were tools for businesses to broadcast and brand, WeChat (via OAs) became a channel for direct communication with customers" [1].

### Sticker Store

WeChat created a marketplace for illustrators to sell stickers, with users able to "reward" any amount up to RMB 200 ($25 USD) to creators they liked [1]. This marketplace created competition among illustrators for the top spot, enriching the user experience.

## Lesson 4: Big Ideas Come from Solving Your Own Problems

**WeChat Red Packets** originated from a Tencent company custom: every manager gives employees a red envelope with cash on the first workday after Chinese New Year [1]. As the company expanded, giving out physical red packets became exhausting, so managers asked for a technical solution. The result became WeChat's most transformative feature.

The first version was developed by a team of 20 in 3 weeks [1]. From New Year's Eve 2014 through 4pm the next day, over 5 million users sent Red Packets [1]. Growth exploded in 2015 with over 1 billion Red Packets sent during Chinese New Year [1].

The random Red Packet variant makes receiving money in a group chat like "a lottery combined with a fortune cookie -- you have no idea how much money you will receive before you open the packet, and getting more than others can be a sign of good luck" [1]. This is another example of the Group Effect: since Red Packets are sent in group chats, adoption increased as more users received them.

Red Packets drove WeChat Pay adoption by building trust through close social circles first (friends and family sending money), then extending to merchants, online and offline stores [1]. A partnership with Didi (China's Uber equivalent) expanded the payment network further [1].

By 2016, Tencent had 600 million mobile payment MAUs and over 600 million average daily payment transactions [1].

[![WeChat Pay Growth](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/5b831210586e.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Pay.png)

## Lesson 5: Monetize Subtly

WeChat demonstrates that monetization and user growth are not mutually exclusive [1].

**Game Center** connected WeChat with Tencent's core gaming expertise. A Tencent game called Rhythm Master had 700,000 DAUs after a year, but exploded to 17 million DAUs (>20x growth) after launching in Game Center [1].

**Moments Native Ads** (launched January 2015) limited native ads to one per day in a user's feed, compared to Facebook's roughly one ad per 10 posts [1]. Users interact with and share ads like friend posts. An Airbnb ad on Moments received more than 1.8 million views after sharing, saw a 5x increase in click-through rate, and drove a 600% increase in new sign-ups [1].

[![WeChat Moments Ad Example](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/683b74fa5f3d.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Moment-Ad.png)

[![WeChat Moments Interface](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/507d68f2e342.jpg)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Moment.jpg)

**Social Coupons** adapted the classic coupon model to friend-based endorsement. When a user redeems a coupon, they can opt to share the offer with friends, extending vendor reach through trusted recommendations [1].

## Lesson 6: Measure What You Value, Not What You're Supposed To

WeChat does not measure growth solely by users or messages. Instead, the team also measures "how deeply is the product engaged in every aspect of daily life (e.g., the number of tasks WeChat can help with in a day)" [1]. The daily engineering message is: "are you creating more value for the user?" [1].

WeChat deliberately limits friend counts, promotional messages, and social circle engagement visibility, viewing excess as "noise that may distract from the utility of WeChat" [1].

**QR Codes** became WeChat's bridge between online and offline worlds. Tencent's Pony Ma described QR codes as "a label of abundant online information attached to the offline world" [1]. QR codes failed in the US for three reasons: the dominant phone and social app did not support scanning, dedicated scanner apps added friction, and early use cases focused on low-value marketing content [1].

**Mini Programs** extended Official Accounts to enable in-app service access without downloading or installing apps [1]. A local restaurant or store could operate within WeChat Mini Programs, discovered via social connections or QR code scanning. WeChat claims this drives higher purchasing conversion rates [1].

## Lesson 7: Don't Play Favorites with Features

Allen Zhang actively worried about users spending too much time in any single feature, as this could prevent them from using other valuable services [1]. WeChat's core product tenet is that it is a "tool" with utility-oriented qualities. Zhang compared this to Google: "once the user finds the right link, Google sends the user directly to the desired site" [1].

The team limits marketing content on Moments and restricts broadcast messages from Subscription Accounts to one per day, without push notifications [1].

Despite the deep feature set, WeChat maintains only 4 tabs: chat, contacts, discover, and me [1]. They have been "very disciplined in keeping the app to only 4 tabs, as whenever they experiment with adding one, the left-most tab sees conversion rates drop by 20-30%" [1].

[![WeChat Engagement Metrics](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/fdd1b0a18af3.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Engagement.png)

## Key Growth Metrics Timeline

| Milestone | Date | Key Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Launch | January 2011 | Messaging + photo sharing |
| 50-60k daily downloads | May 2011 | Voice messaging added |
| 100k+ daily acquisitions | Mid-2011 | People Nearby feature |
| 50M registered users | November 2011 | QQ account linking |
| 200k daily acquisitions | November 2011 | Group interaction features |
| 300M MAUs | ~2013 | Moments, Official Accounts |
| 5M Red Packet users in 2 days | January 2014 | WeChat Red Packets |
| 1B Red Packets sent | Chinese New Year 2015 | Red Packet virality |
| 889M MAUs | 2016 | Full platform maturity |

[![WeChat Growth Chart](../raw/posts/images/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world/a260bf04ade6.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/04/WeChat-Growth.png)

## References

1. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) — [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)

title: Lambda School (BloomTech): Reinventing CS Education with Income Share Agreements
body:
# Lambda School (BloomTech): Reinventing CS Education with Income Share Agreements

Lambda School (YC S17), later renamed Bloom Institute of Technology, provides a CS education that is free until the student gets a job. The company was founded by [[Austen Allred]] and uses income share agreements (ISAs) as its core business model: students pay nothing upfront and repay a percentage of their income after they are employed [1].

## Origin: Human Capital as a Mispriced Asset

Allred's founding insight came from moving to San Francisco from rural Utah. He observed that people of equivalent capability were earning $30,000 in Utah and $150,000 in San Francisco. The gap was not intelligence or work ethic but access to opportunity [1]. He frames the problem in financial terms: "If you look at any asset class in the United States, it's being traded on Bloomberg terminals instantly all over the world. You can trade a hundred thousand pounds of pork bellies without anything moving. For some reason humans don't have that" [1]. Human capital, in his view, is "the most unoptimized asset class we've ever had" [1].

The ISA model emerged from this observation. Asking someone making $30,000 a year to spend $10,000 on education with 7% interest represents spending half their annual income on a bet. Most cannot afford that risk [1]. By deferring payment until after employment, Lambda School removed the primary barrier to entry.

## The Anti-University Model

Allred is not a college graduate. He argues that the conventional advice of "go to college, doesn't matter what it costs, get a degree, doesn't matter what you study" is "not only not good advice, it's probably bad advice, net-net" [1]. He points to the structural misalignment of incentives in traditional education: University of Phoenix gets paid $80,000 whether or not a student succeeds because loans are backed by the federal government [1].

Lambda School's nine-month program compresses a four-year degree into one focused period. Allred calculates that by the time a university student graduates, a Lambda School student already has three years of work experience, has paid off the ISA, and is at least a quarter-million dollars ahead [1].

The school is entirely vocational. Allred sees value in liberal arts education but believes it should come after financial stability, not before: "We want to help you make as much money as you can and then when you're making a lot of money, it's a whole lot easier to make decisions around what you'll learn" [1].

## The ISA Is the Easy Part

When competitors began copying the ISA model, Allred argued the business model itself is not the moat. "Plugging, throwing an ISA on the front end is the easy part" [1]. The hard parts are:

1. **Underwriting student risk.** No credit score exists for human potential. Lambda School had to build proprietary models to predict which students would succeed. "Nobody knows how to underwrite student risk" [1].
2. **Training students effectively online.** The entire code bootcamp industry believed online-only education was ineffective. Allred built the instructional design from first principles [1].
3. **Placing students nationally at scale.** Lambda School hired 37 interview sourcers to function as a talent agency for its graduates. Networking skills were built directly into the curriculum starting at week three [1].
4. **Reducing dropout rates.** Initially, dropout was "what was killing us." By the time of this interview, Lambda School was graduating over 85% of students who start day one [1].

## Online Education: A Secret Advantage

Lambda School is fully remote by design. Allred sees this not as a limitation but as a competitive advantage. In-person classes rely on the "crutch" of physical presence, while Lambda School must use "data and software and instructional design to make sure everybody's working on the right thing at the right time" [1].

Allred observed that in his own college experience, at least 50% of students in any lecture were on Facebook. Lambda School's approach flips the traditional CS curriculum: instead of starting with C and memory management (which produces high dropout), students have a site they can see and interact with on day one. "You do need to know how to write C to be a good engineer. You do need to know how memory management works, but maybe let's not start with memory management as an entry level point to software engineering" [1].

## From Bootstrapped to VC-Backed

Lambda School was profitable before joining YC. Allred had sworn off venture capital after a traumatic experience with his first company, where a Series A fell through on December 23rd with docs already signed. The VC's secretary called to cancel, and Allred had to call his entire team to tell them "I think it's over" [1].

Despite this history, Allred chose to raise for Lambda School after modeling the trade-off with a YC partner: the dilution (roughly 15%) would enable 10x growth. By the time of this interview, Lambda School had raised $48 million [1]. The decision framework was explicit: "If you have a company that can grow at extreme scale and capture a market and create way more value, that's why you raise VC" [1].

## Product Quality as Unmeasurable Moat

Allred argues that product quality is systematically undervalued by analysts and investors because it cannot be quantified. "Revenue is a very, very trailing indicator of product" [1]. He points to Airbnb, Stripe, and Dropbox as companies that won despite competitors with more money or market share, specifically because product quality compounds over time through "a whole lot of subtlety built up" [1].

He uses Tesla as a canonical example: "The materials are worse, the build is probably not as good. They're not as good at assembling the pieces, but just the experience of that car versus the other one, it's not even close" [1].

## Expansion Plans

At the time of the interview, Lambda School was exploring expansion into other fields with labor shortages. Nursing was the leading candidate: there is a massive shortage of nurses due to an aging population, but nursing schools are capped by the federal 90-10 rule (only 90% of revenue can come from federal funds). Lambda School's ISA model could bypass this constraint entirely [1].

## References

1. [On starting and scaling Bloom Institute of Technology](https://ycombinator.com/library/5N-on-starting-and-scaling-bloom-institute-of-technology) -- Austen Allred (2019)

title: OpenAI: From Flip Chart to AGI
body:
# OpenAI: From Flip Chart to AGI

OpenAI is the AI research company behind ChatGPT and the GPT series of large language models. Co-founded by [[Sam Altman]], Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, and others, OpenAI launched in January 2016 with a heretical premise: pursue AGI directly through deep learning at scale. In a 2024 interview with [[Garry Tan]], Altman describes the founding decisions, the scientific bets, the many wrong turns, and the conviction required to build on a single thesis when the established AI community called it irresponsible.

## Founding: The Heretical Thesis

OpenAI originated from YC Research, which Altman described as "the coolest retirement job" -- running a research lab [1]. The initial effort was not specifically focused on AI; it funded several different research directions. Altman read histories of Xerox PARC and Bell Labs, and wanted to replicate the model of allocating capital to smart people [1].

AI was having "a mini moment" in late 2014 and 2015. DeepMind had produced impressive results. The book "Superintelligence" by Bostrom sparked serious discussion. Altman had been "an AI nerd forever" and wanted to try something, though "it's very hard to say if it was met out yet" [1].

The founding thesis had two heretical components [1]:

1. **Deep learning works.** This was "somewhat heretical" at the time.
2. **It gets better with scale.** The initial evidence was suggestive: making neural networks bigger made them better. What required "religious level of belief" was that this would not stop.

The established AI community responded harshly. Leaders of the field said OpenAI was "wrong, and this is like a bad thing to believe or bad thing to say." The criticisms: OpenAI would perpetuate an AI winter, waste resources, and pursue an irresponsible path [1].

Altman describes the founding announcement: "We said from the very beginning we were going to go after AGI at a time when in the field you weren't allowed to say that because that just seemed impossibly crazy." This attracted exactly the right people -- young researchers excited by ambition -- while drawing "the derision of the mediocre old people," which Altman took as "a really good sign" [1].

## The Initial Team and the Apartment Meeting

Altman recruited one by one. He found Ilya Sutskever by watching a video online: "He has this incredible presence." Altman emailed him, got no response, then attended a conference where Sutskever was speaking. Greg Brockman was a connection from the early Stripe days [1].

About ten people gathered in Brockman's apartment on January 3, 2016. "It felt like we had done this monumental thing to get it started. And everyone's like, 'So what do we do now?'" Altman compares this to when startup founders raise a round and think they have accomplished something: "That was actually the starting gun, and now we got to run" [1].

An early offsite produced flip charts with three goals: "Figure out how to do unsupervised learning, solve RL, and never get more than 120 people." OpenAI achieved the first two but missed on the third [1].

## Concentration as Strategy

OpenAI was far less resourced than DeepMind. The strategic response was focus: "They're going to try a lot of things, and we've just got to pick one and really concentrate. And that's how we can win here" [1]. This is, Altman notes, "totally the right startup takeaway."

While others spread bets across multiple approaches, OpenAI said: "We don't know what we don't know. We do know this one thing works. So we're going to really concentrate on that." Altman believes other efforts "were trying to outsmart themselves in too many ways" [1].

Tan observes that this mirrors a pattern across successful YC startups: "Most of the world still does not understand the value of a fairly extreme level of conviction on one bet" [1].

## The Path to Language Models Was Not Straight

Altman is candid about the winding path: "I wish I could go tell you, like, 'Oh, we knew exactly what was going to happen, and it was, you know, we had this idea for language models from the beginning.' But obviously, the story of OpenAI is that we did a lot of things that helped us develop some scientific understanding, but we're not on the short path" [1].

Early work included robots, agents, and playing video games. Altman personally "had no idea that language models were going to be the thing" [1].

The critical breakthrough came from Alec Radford, whom Altman describes as "this unbelievable outlier of a human." Radford noticed a single neuron that was flipping positive or negative sentiment as the model generated Amazon reviews. This "unsupervised sentiment" insight was not widely recognized at first -- "it took people a while to fully internalize what a big deal it was." Radford then built GPT-1, which was scaled by others into GPT-2 [1].

## Scaling Laws and Conviction

The scaling results formalized what was initially a hunch. The data showed that model performance improved predictably with scale. "At some point, you have to just look at the scaling loss and say, 'We're going to keep doing this, and this is what we think it'll do'" [1].

Altman describes the tension: "There is a difference between being high conviction just for the sake of it, and if you're wrong and you don't adapt and you don't try to be truth-seeking, it still is really not that effective." OpenAI's approach was to believe whatever the results showed, fully embrace being wrong when data contradicted them, but operate on conviction when data did not yet exist: "Conviction is great until the moment you have data one way or the other. And there are a lot of people who hold on past the moment of data" [1].

## GPT-3 to GPT-4: Finding Product-Market Fit

The transition from research breakthrough to product-market fit was gradual [1]:

- **GPT-3**: "An incredible demo," but with the possible exception of Copilot, "no great businesses were built on GPT-3."
- **GPT-3.5**: YC startups started building interesting things. "It no longer felt like we were pushing a boulder uphill. People actually wanted to buy the thing we were selling."
- **GPT-4**: The moment Altman knew they had something great. CaseText founder Jake Heller (YC) described 3.5 as too hallucination-prone for legal work, but GPT-4 reached the threshold where, with small enough prompts, it could do exactly what he wanted. He built CaseText around it and sold for $650 million -- one of the first to commercialize GPT-4 at scale.

Altman observes: "You never really know if you have a hit product on your hands until you put it in customer hands." The internal team was impressed by GPT-4's capabilities -- rhyming, humor, reasoning -- but "the real test is users" [1].

## The Five Levels of AI

OpenAI developed a framework for AI capability levels because "AGI had become this badly overloaded word" [1]:

1. **Chatbots** -- Conversational AI (existing systems like ChatGPT)
2. **Reasoners** -- Systems that can reason at PhD levels on closed-end tasks (O1, achieved in 2024)
3. **Agents** -- Systems that execute longer-term tasks, interact with environments, ask for help, and work together. Altman expects this "faster than people expect."
4. **Innovators** -- AI that can explore not-well-understood phenomena over long periods and generate novel insights. A hackathon demo at YC showed an O1-based system iteratively improving an airfoil design from non-functional to competitive lift.
5. **Organizations** -- AI operating at the scale of entire companies or organizations.

Altman initially believed the jump from level 3 to level 4 would be "much harder and require some medium-sized or larger new ideas." The YC hackathon demos changed his mind: "You can get a huge amount of innovation just by using these current models in really creative ways" [1].

## Speed-Running a Tech Company

By late 2024, ChatGPT was less than two years old but OpenAI had "speed-run the medium-sized or even pretty big sized tech company arc that would normally take like a decade." Management teams changed. The company's structure and mission evolved. Altman acknowledges "plenty of mistakes" alongside a few things done right [1].

Altman describes the current moment as "the first time ever where I felt like we actually know what to do." The research path, infrastructure path, and product path are all clearer than they have ever been. "When you have that clarity, I think you can go pretty fast" [1].

## Platform Shifts and Startup Advice

Altman draws a direct parallel between the current AI moment and previous platform shifts (mobile, internet, semiconductors): "Big companies have the edge when things are moving slowly. When something like this happens, that's when upstarts have their edge" [1].

His advice to founders [1]:

1. **Bet on this trend.** "We are not near the saturation point. The models are going to get so much better so quickly."
2. **Speed is the edge.** Build something with AI and take advantage of the ability to see a new capability and build something that day, rather than putting it into a quarterly planning cycle. Google is on a decade-long planning cycle; startups can react in hours.
3. **The rules of business still apply.** A new technology does not exempt a company from needing a moat, competitive edge, or real product value. "Everyone can build an absolutely incredible demo right now. But building a business, man, that's the brass ring."
4. **Short-term growth is seductive.** Embracing new technology faster than competitors produces explosive short-term growth, but this is not a substitute for building lasting value.

Tan reinforces the theme: companies that were dominant in one platform shift (e.g., Facebook on web) nearly missed the next one (mobile). "The platform shift is always built by the people who are young with no prior knowledge" [1].

## The Apprenticeship of the Twenties

Altman started his first company, Loopt (a geolocation app), at 19 during YC's first batch in 2005. The iPhone did not yet exist; mobile phones were literally phones. Loopt never found product-market fit. But the experience -- the "rate of experience and education" -- was formative [1].

Altman quotes Paul Graham: "Your 20s are always an apprenticeship but you don't know for what, and then you do your real work later." He reflects that Loopt was "not a successful company" and a "difficult experience," but "there is nothing that I have ever heard of that has a higher rate of generalized learning than doing a startup" [1].

On what he would tell his 19-year-old self: "AI was always the thing I most wanted to do and AI just like -- I went to school to study AI but at the time I was working in the AI lab the one thing that they told you is definitely don't work on neural networks. We tried that, it doesn't work" [1].

## References

1. [How To Build The Future: Sam Altman](https://ycombinator.com/library/Lp-how-to-build-the-future-sam-altman) -- Sam Altman, [[Garry Tan]] (2024)

title: Dropbox: From Thumb Drives to 500 Million Users
body:
# Dropbox: From Thumb Drives to 500 Million Users

Dropbox (YC W07) is a cloud file storage and sharing company that grew to over 500 million users. Founded by Drew Houston and Arash Ferdowsi, the company's origin story illustrates several recurring YC themes: ideas born from personal frustration, the importance of validation through demos, viral growth mechanics, and the difficult transition from product builder to CEO.

## Origin: Personal Frustration

Houston had the idea while riding the Chinatown bus from Boston to New York. He forgot his thumb drive and could not get any work done on the trip [1]. He had a second pain point: collaborating with his co-founder at a prior company (Accolade, an online SAT prep company). He hacked together scripts to solve the problem, but the bus ride was "the last straw" [1].

## YC Application: Rejected, Then Accepted

Houston first applied to YC with Accolade and was rejected without an interview. The reasons: he and his co-founder did not know each other well, neither was full-time, and there were market size concerns [1].

He applied again as a solo founder with Dropbox. Paul Graham responded: "Hey, idea seems interesting, but you really need to find a co-founder" [1]. The application deadline was three weeks away. Through Kyle Vogt (co-founder of Justin.tv/Twitch, later Cruise), Houston was introduced to Arash Ferdowsi. They were floormates at MIT and both from Kansas. Houston and Ferdowsi spent two to three hours together before committing. Ferdowsi dropped out of MIT the next day [1].

Houston describes the co-founder selection: "This kid's clearly smart. A little quiet, but very thoughtful." He did not use a rigorous framework: "I wish I could say, 'Oh yeah, here are the 17 things I was looking for on this 7-point scale'... But no" [1].

## Validation: The Demo Video Strategy

Houston created a three-minute demo video to get into YC, reasoning that every applicant would have a demo link but a video would stand out -- especially one designed for the Hacker News audience [1]. He posted it to Hacker News, where it stayed at the top for two days. It generated the desired email from Paul Graham, along with the unwanted "you also need a co-founder in three weeks" [1].

One comment became infamous: someone called it "the stupidest thing I've ever heard of. Don't these people know that Wget exists?" YC now shares this comment with founders who get demoralized by negative online feedback [1].

Houston used the same video-on-community approach at least twice: first for YC admission, then for building company momentum via Digg and Reddit [1].

## Handling Competition and Negativity

Online storage was "the startup cliche of its time" [1]. Investors pushed back constantly: "Google's going to do this, it's a commodity" [1]. Houston agreed with much of the criticism: Google could probably do it, Dropbox had few structural advantages, the business model was unclear, and they had no users. But he identified a key asymmetry: "I would ask investors, 'Do you use this? Yes, there are 50 of these things out there, but do you use any of them?' They would say no, and I'd say, 'Isn't that interesting?'" [1].

On handling persistent negativity, Houston observes that even Apple posts record-breaking results and the next day "everybody's like, 'But they're really dead now'" [1]. Founders need simultaneously thick skin (to tune out noise) and thin skin (to respond to customer and team unhappiness). "That's a weird dynamic that you have to handle" [1].

Competition did not bother Houston because "our sights were also a lot lower in the beginning. I had a target customer of one, which was really myself, and victory, for me, would be never having to carry a thumb drive" [1]. From idea to a working product that eliminated the thumb drive took six to eight weeks [1].

## Viral Growth Engine

Houston built Dropbox's growth engine using Dave McClure's "Startup Metrics for Pirates: AARRR" framework (Acquisition, Activation, Revenue, Retention, Referral) [1].

**The activation problem**: Four out of five people who signed up did not install the client or put a file in their Dropbox. Houston brought people off Craigslist to watch them install the product. Zero of five succeeded in going from setup to sharing a file. The team watched in real-time from an adjacent room [1].

**The referral program**: A two-sided incentive where both referrer and referred received free storage space. This drove 30% of Dropbox's signups. Shared folders, which are inherently viral, drove another 20%. Half of all signups were viral [1].

Houston credits this approach to studying earlier models: PayPal's referral bonus (late 1990s/early 2000s) and the book "Guerrilla Marketing" for low-budget user acquisition [1].

## The CEO Transition

Houston describes the challenge of transitioning from coder to CEO. Nobody is born a CEO; you learn it. The chessboard expands from product to markets, business models, competition, strategy, team building, and team management -- "pretty tough when you've never done any of it" [1].

Houston's approach to self-education [1]:
- **Reading**: "Probably the single most valuable thing that I did." Key recommendations: "High Output Management" by Andy Grove (scaling organizations), "The Effective Executive" by Peter Drucker, "Poor Charlie's Almanack" by Charlie Munger (building mental models) [1].
- **People at different stages**: Having friends one step, two steps, and five years ahead. Sequoia connected Houston with other entrepreneurs. One early meeting with a more experienced CEO frustrated him -- the CEO talked about mission, values, and culture while Houston was thinking about unfixed bugs. "After hearing that 5 or 6 or 10 times, you realize, 'The only one here who's confused is me'" [1].
- **Cross-domain study**: How Phil Jackson built championship culture on the Bulls and Lakers. How governments solve problems. How consumer packaged goods companies create products like Swiffer [1]. Board member Condoleezza Rice brought perspective from running the State Department (85,000 people) and Stanford (20,000 people) [1].

Houston identifies a key insight for engineer-founders: "You're going from designing a system of code and algorithms, which is very orderly and discrete, to a system of people, and things like culture, values, all kinds of other things, are the programming that makes that work" [1].

Ben Horowitz's line resonated: "The average grade for running a company is a 22 out of 100" [1].

## Strategic Inflection: Expansion and Contraction

Dropbox expanded from file sync into photos (Carousel app) and email (Mailbox acquisition). The logic was sound on paper: many users' primary use case was photo sharing and team collaboration [1].

But the expansion created problems: "Photos and collaboration, it's very different products, very different ideal business model, very different competition, just pretty everything is different" [1]. The company was straddling two worlds, making it hard to build the best product or explain what Dropbox was.

Houston re-read Andy Grove's "Only the Paranoid Survive," which describes Intel's transition from memory to microprocessors. "My stomach started churning, because I'm like, 'Oh my god, this strategic inflection point he's talking about, that's what we're in'" [1]. The book's advice: "Put all your eggs in one basket, and watch that basket" (quoting Mark Twain) [1].

Houston went into the office the next week and killed Carousel and Mailbox. The reaction was mixed: relief from some ("What took you so long?"), disappointment from those working on the products. "Everybody loves the idea of focus, but what focus actually means is shooting stuff you love and turning down things you know you can make work" [1].

## On Going Public

Houston describes the IPO decision as "primarily a financing decision" -- driven by capital needs, liquidity for employees and investors, and branding benefits. Private markets had solved most of these, but going public solves all of them. The downsides: compliance overhead ("a lot of people in your company spending time on things that don't really have anything to do with your customers") and the stock market's manic-depressive nature, which whipsaws team morale [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Dropbox (YC W07)](https://ycombinator.com/library/6S-on-starting-and-scaling-dropbox-yc-w07) -- Drew Houston, [[Sam Altman]] (2017)

title: Slack: Twice Pivoting from Failed Games to Iconic Products
body:
# Slack: Twice Pivoting from Failed Games to Iconic Products

Slack is an enterprise messaging platform that grew past $200 million in GAAP revenue (as of 2017) while still growing rapidly. Stewart Butterfield, Slack's co-founder and CEO, arrived at Slack through two failed attempts to build web-based massively multiplayer games -- the first producing Flickr, the second producing Slack [1].

## The Original Vision: Online Games

Butterfield's fascination began in 1992 when he first used the internet in college -- IRC, Usenet, email. The idea of a game that "lived online" and used "play as a pretext for social interaction" seemed compelling [1].

### Attempt 1: Game to Flickr (2002-2004)

The first game company started in 2002, after the dot-com crash, 9/11, and the Enron accounting scandals. The NASDAQ was down 80% from its peak. "No one wanted to invest in internet-facing stuff" [1]. The team raised friends-and-family money and spent a year building a prototype. The prototype was well-received, but finishing it required another year and a half, and "we literally couldn't raise any money at all" [1]. At one point, only the team member with children was being paid.

The pivot to Flickr reused existing technology. The game client had inventory slots along the bottom of the screen; these became photo slots. The game's messaging server enabled real-time photo sharing. But the initial version was "a terrible product because you had to be online at the same time as someone you cared about" to use it [1]. It demoed well but failed in practice because "you didn't care about any of the people who were actually online" [1]. The product evolved quickly from there.

### Attempt 2: Game to Slack (2009-2013)

The second attempt started in 2009 with $17.5 million in venture capital, plus AWS, better hardware, and many more people online. None of those external improvements changed the underlying problem: "there wasn't a big enough market" for a web-based multiplayer game [1]. The game, called Glitch, attracted an engaged but small user base with a "leaky bucket" -- very difficult to add new users [1].

Butterfield felt stuck between contradictory narratives: "Good entrepreneurs are resilient. When everyone else thinks it's a bad idea, it's probably really a good idea" versus the reality that "I'm the CEO. I'm the chairman of the board. I'm sure that this is not going to work" [1]. The decisive factor: "I had exhausted all of the ideas we had to fix the problems." The team had focused on new user experience and retention. Ideas that once filled whiteboards had been reduced to three bad ones. "When you're out of ideas about how to make it work" is the signal to move on [1].

## The Three-and-a-Half-Year Unconscious Design Process

Slack emerged from the internal communication system the game team had been using [1]. The foundation was IRC, which Butterfield describes as "Slack with all of the things that were missing when it was originally designed in 1989" [1].

The development followed a pattern:

1. IRC could not store and forward messages, so the team built a logging bot
2. With messages in a database, they built search
3. No good iPhone IRC clients existed in 2009, so they built an HTML5 mobile frontend
4. Once they could view messages on mobile, they wanted to post from mobile

Each improvement addressed "whatever problem is the most irritating" with minimal effort, then the team returned to building the game [1]. This continued for three and a half years with 45 people generating real-world usage feedback.

Butterfield argues this process was superior to normal software development because it eliminated ego and speculation. In typical product discussions, "people are very ego-laden" and arguments center on speculation ("I think this could be really valuable"). The proto-Slack development was purely reactive: "an opportunity that was so obvious that we couldn't help but take advantage of it" [1].

The result: "a fully designed product that we were using that was a terrible implementation. Not what you would do if you started from scratch, but it was obvious that this was something that had enormous value. Everyone who worked at the company agreed they would never work without something like this again" [1].

## Underestimating the Market

When pitching Andreessen Horowitz (one of the game's investors) on the pivot, Butterfield projected that Slack could eventually become "a hundred million a year revenue business -- valued at a billion dollars" and thought that was "a decade out" representing 100% of the total addressable market [1]. The company passed that figure in year two. By 2017, it projected $200 million in GAAP revenue while still growing rapidly, with a revised market estimate of "ten billion in revenue, like a hundred billion dollar company" [1].

As of 2017, Slack had 38,000 paid customers. The sales team had never talked to more than 90% of them because they could not reach that many people -- adoption was almost entirely product-driven [1].

## Saying No to Social

Roughly one-third of Slack teams created were for social (non-business) usage. Multiple people proposed pursuing this: "Slack for groups" or pivoting to become "a private version of Twitter" [1]. Butterfield resisted: "You can only do so many things well and there's a lot of shiny objects like that that are very distracting" [1]. He describes himself as "chief no-sayer" [1].

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: How to Get Ideas and Find What's Working](https://ycombinator.com/library/8B-how-to-start-a-startup-how-to-get-ideas-and-find-what-s-working) -- Stewart Butterfield, Adam D'Angelo (2017)

title: Figma: Ten-Year Overnight Success
body:
# Figma: Ten-Year Overnight Success

Figma is the collaborative design tool that replaced Photoshop as the dominant design platform. Adobe acquired the company for $20 billion [1]. Co-founded by [[Dylan Field]] (19 years old at the time) and Evan Wallace in August 2012, Figma's journey illustrates how new technologies create startup opportunities, why management is a skill that must be learned, and why the path to a billion-dollar outcome requires navigating thousands of individual decisions over a decade [1].

## Origin: Technology Plus Problem

In 2011, WebGL 1.0 launched -- a new way for websites to draw and render graphics inside a web browser. Field and Wallace knew they wanted to build something with this technology and cared about making creative tools accessible. They agreed with Bret Victor's statement that "if you can't use a tool because it's too complex, it's a moral wrong" [1].

[[Garry Tan]] frames this pattern as common: Instagram started from experiments with new APIs (HTML5 meets social location sharing). The frontier technology is not enough on its own -- Field also knew he wanted to walk toward the problem of design tools being expensive and hard to use [1].

## The Meme Generator Detour

After exploration mode, Field and Wallace briefly built a meme generator with WebGL. Field describes the moment of realization: "After like a week of working on this meme generator, I remember looking in the mirror and being just like, 'Holy -- I dropped out of Brown for this? What am I doing with my life?'" An hour later, his co-founder took off his headphones and said, "Dude, I just can't do this" [1].

Tan uses this as a cautionary tale about technology-for-its-own-sake: "Meaningless products that don't solve real-world problems but seem adjacent to hot buzzwords" can consume founders. The solution is self-knowledge -- "if that's not yours, you won't be able to recruit, build, and do what it takes to create day in and day out" [1].

## The Management Crisis

A year into Figma, Field was struggling with management. He made hires that were poor cultural fits and was micromanaging rather than empowering people: "When you hire smart people, you got to give them a way to really contribute and to blow you away and to let them do things that you would never think of. And if you micromanage and give people the exact recipe for what to do, they're not gonna appreciate that if they're the right people to hire" [1].

The crisis peaked when employees confronted him directly: "People were really unhappy with me. It was a moment where they said, 'We really need you to get help to make this work'" [1]. This occurred while Figma had not yet launched and while Field's father was dying of cancer [1].

The resolution: hiring the company's first outside manager, from whom Field learned management skills. Field distinguishes management from leadership: "Management is a skill that you have to build just like any other skill. There's muscles and different things you have to figure out about it" [1].

Tan draws the universal lesson: "Every highly talented technical founder who starts and wants to stay in that CEO position for the long haul will end up having to learn what Dylan learned: how to be a manager." He recommends executive coaches, great managers as peers, and investors who can help with management development [1].

Tan also connects this to his own experience at Posterous: "I had reached a point where I needed to switch into a people manager, and I wish I could say I succeeded. But in fact, I ran away from it and I just coded harder. It was the wrong move for Posterous" [1].

## Slow Is Smooth, Smooth Is Fast

Tan frames Figma's later success through the Navy SEAL maxim: "Slow is smooth, and smooth is fast." Reducing chaos and helping the executive team be productive makes things smooth, and "only then can you create something for a billion people" [1].

## References

1. [Figma's $20B, 10 Year Overnight Success](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jp-garry-s-channel-figma-s-20b-10-year-overnight-success) -- [[Dylan Field]], [[Garry Tan]] (2022)

title: Meesho: Social Commerce in India
body:
# Meesho: Social Commerce in India

Meesho (YC S16) is a platform that enables anyone in India to start and grow a social store on WhatsApp, Facebook, or Instagram. By 2019, the platform had close to half a million monthly active social store owners selling across fashion, food, travel, cosmetics, and other categories [1]. The company was co-founded by Vidit Aatrey and Sanjeev Barnwal.

## Origin: From Hyperlocal Fashion to WhatsApp Commerce

Aatrey and Barnwal met at IIT Delhi (class of 2008-2012). They did not plan to start a company in college. In 2015, three years after graduation, Barnwal called Aatrey from Sony in Japan, asking for a reference check on a startup he wanted to join. Aatrey's response: "If you want to work with a startup, let's do something ourselves first. If it doesn't work, then you go there" [1]. They agreed to start a company on that call, with no idea in mind. The next day they created a Google spreadsheet listing ideas and started talking to people around them [1].

Their first idea was FashNear, a hyperlocal fashion delivery service (similar to what Swiggy is for food, but for fashion). They built it because "doing O2O, hyperlocal, was very popular in India" in 2015 [1]. After four to five months, they learned a hard lesson: "In fashion, fulfillment is like the third thing that is most important. The first thing is selection, which you can never have around you" [1].

The discovery that led to Meesho came from the same work. While onboarding small shops in Bangalore as suppliers for FashNear, Aatrey's pitch was "I'm going to take you online." The shopkeepers replied: "We're already online... I sell on WhatsApp" [1]. They had created WhatsApp groups of existing customers and posted photos of new stock, taking orders through the chat. One shopkeeper reported selling 30-40% of his total monthly business through WhatsApp [1].

Aatrey and Barnwal built a mobile-only, India-localized version of Shopify, tailored for doing commerce on WhatsApp and Facebook. They applied to YC and raised an angel round with this product [1].

## The Second Pivot: From Tools for Shops to a Reseller Marketplace

Six months after the tool launched, retention was lower than expected. Speaking to users revealed that more than half were not the intended audience of offline shopkeepers. They were housewives running boutiques on WhatsApp -- creating logos, curating products from suppliers, and selling to their own networks [1]. Most were women based in Gujarat (a manufacturing hub) who sourced products via WhatsApp from local suppliers.

Aatrey created an internal experiment. He split the company into two teams in two rooms: one continuing the original Meesho product, the other building "Meesho Supply" -- a product to enable anyone to run a boutique like these women. Critically, Meesho Supply launched on WhatsApp with no app or website [1]. In six months, the new product was doubling every month. The original product's retention remained stagnant [1].

The decision to shut down the original product and rename Meesho Supply to Meesho was difficult. Aatrey attributes his ability to pivot to co-founder trust: "I have seen so many friends around me who were just not able to pivot because it was a hard conversation with their co-founder... Just because you have a co-founder you trust blindly, it was easier" [1]. The team initially resisted ("You keep changing what we do every six months"), but three months after the pivot, growth was so strong that "everyone was very clear that we made the right decision" [1].

Financial pressure also helped. Meesho was one of the few YC companies that did not raise significant money on Demo Day. The new product generated revenue by charging suppliers; the old product was free. "Everything was pointing in one direction" [1].

## Macro Tailwinds: Jio, UPI, and WhatsApp

Aatrey identifies three macro trends that drove Meesho's growth [1]:

1. **Reliance Jio** (launched 2016): Drastically reduced data costs in India. "Hundreds of millions of people in India have come online just the last two, three years" -- primarily in tier 2 and tier 3 cities [1].
2. **WhatsApp adoption**: WhatsApp was generally the first app most people used when they came online [1].
3. **UPI (Unified Payments Interface)**: Taught millions to transact online for the first time [1].

These new internet users were predominantly in smaller cities with lower incomes. They bought mostly unbranded products -- a category that destination marketplaces like Amazon and Flipkart had never figured out how to sell at scale in India [1].

## The Trust Deficit and Social Selling

India operates as a "trust deficit market." Aatrey explains: "Most people do not believe in corporate companies. They don't believe in their own government. They don't believe in the judicial system. Court cases run for hundreds of years" [1]. Trust is placed in personal communities. For unbranded products -- where quality is uncertain and there is no brand guarantee -- buyers trust people in their community over unknown merchants [1].

This is why 90% of Indian commerce (as of 2019) happens through small mom-and-pop stores, and why social selling through personal networks is a natural distribution channel for unbranded goods [1]. Meesho digitizes the same trust relationship that existed in offline community commerce.

## User Profile: Aspiring Entrepreneurs

Ninety percent of Meesho's resellers are women. More than 80% are from tier 2, tier 3, and smaller cities. Seventy-five percent are housewives [1]. The typical average user earns $150-200 per month; the top 5-10% earn $400-500 [1]. Most use it as secondary household income (15-30% of primary income) [1].

The economic impact matters less to users than the identity it provides. Aatrey describes a woman who had asked her husband for money to start a boutique every year for 20 years and never received it. On Meesho, she started a store with zero investment [1]. Aatrey says: "Most of our most impactful stories are not just about how much money this person makes. It's a lot about what were they doing before... A lot about how you empower me, what identity do you give me, people start giving me more respect" [1].

## Building for Users You Are Not

Aatrey identifies a core challenge: "I am not a woman and I am not a housewife" [1]. His response has been systematic user closeness:

- **CEO as customer support**: From day one, every new user was added to Aatrey's personal WhatsApp. "Any problem, you tell me." By 2019, 100-200 top users were on his WhatsApp [1].
- **"We Hear You" monthly videos**: Aatrey records a monthly video for users describing what Meesho did last month, what it plans to do, and what is not working. Users respond with detailed feedback. "It's like a town hall for our users" [1].
- **Management call center rotations**: All managers sit in the call center for half a day every month, listening to user calls. "Everyone comes out with something new that they never thought about" [1].
- **User celebrations**: Users in Bangalore are invited to company events, including funding round parties [1].
- **Community mentorship**: A Reddit-type community in the app connects new users with experienced sellers. Top 5% of users serve as mentors. "These people are helping each other out" [1].

Aatrey frames this as a general principle: "The best thing to do is build a product that solves your own problem. The second best thing to do is build a product that solves a problem for someone who can't solve their own problem" [1].

## Growth and Quality Cycles

Meesho's growth came in six-month cycles alternating between expansion and quality improvement. "You grow very fast, and then you realize you've carried so much debt around -- a lot of rework, a lot of manual things -- and then you start focusing on how do you improve quality" [1].

During high-growth periods, quality was deliberately compromised. During lean seasons (e.g., post-Diwali), the company focused on fixing accumulated technical and operational debt [1].

On customer support specifically: "If you need customer support, that means something is wrong with the product. You have to go back to the drawing board and understand: why are people calling me?" [1].

## Competing with Facebook and Amazon

Aatrey argues that Facebook (WhatsApp's parent) will not directly compete because social commerce is one of many use cases on the platform, and operating a supply chain is "not in their DNA" [1]. He draws a parallel to Tencent, which invested in commerce use cases on WeChat rather than building them in-house [1].

For Amazon and Flipkart, the issue is different: those marketplaces primarily sell branded products. Selling unbranded, long-tail products at scale requires trust-based distribution -- the reseller model -- which destination marketplaces have not cracked [1].

## CEO Role Evolution

Aatrey describes three phases of his role [1]:

1. **Pre-PMF**: "My whole job was just to figure out what the business model will be in the long term." He did everything on growth and product. Meesho hired its first product manager after Series A and its second after Series B [1].
2. **Post-PMF**: Role shifted to hiring, culture, and course correction. "Nothing else" [1].
3. **At 700 employees**: Ensuring the right people are in place, motivating the team, and occasional direction changes [1].

## The Indian Startup Ecosystem

Aatrey observes a shift in India's startup culture. When Meesho started, "everyone was like, tell me what are you a copy of, either in US or in China." Meesho struggled because there was no obvious Western or Chinese parallel [1]. By 2019, new companies were building bottom-up for tier 2 and tier 3 users, "not thinking top-down, this is how it should be because it exists in US" [1].

He predicts the next decade of Indian startups will focus on serving the newly-online tier 2-4 population across housing, education, healthcare, and entertainment [1].

## Key Mistakes and Lessons

**Listening to VCs instead of users**: Aatrey's biggest early mistake. After deciding to start up, "we just kept listening to VCs." They spent four months chasing VC-defined milestones that kept moving. "After that, I've never gone to them asking what should I do, because I know they don't know" [1].

**Best decision**: Starting the company with Sanjeev Barnwal. "A lot of other mistakes that I've done have not been such big mistakes because I started with him" [1].

**Reversed opinion**: Aatrey initially believed the common advice that founders must solve their own problems. After Meesho's discovery process, "I don't hold that opinion anymore. You can build for others. It's hard, definitely, much, much harder than building a product for yourself" [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Indian Shopping Site Meesho](https://ycombinator.com/library/6M-on-starting-and-scaling-indian-shopping-site-meesho) -- Vidit Aatrey, Adora Cheung (2019)

title: Bump to Google Photos: From Contact Sharing to a Billion Users
body:
# Bump to Google Photos: From Contact Sharing to a Billion Users

Bump (YC S09) was one of the first breakout apps on the iPhone App Store, reaching 150 million downloads and 10 million monthly active users. Its founder, [[David Lieb]], pivoted twice before arriving at the product insight that became Google Photos, one of 17 billion-user apps at Google. The story illustrates the difference between viral adoption and sustainable business, the mechanics of pivoting from user feedback, and how an acqui-hire can become the vehicle for realizing a startup vision at massive scale.

## Origins: A Business School Side Project

Lieb was a full-time MBA student in the fall of 2008, the year the iPhone App Store opened to third-party developers. A classmate had built a WiFi-finder app and earned $200,000 over the summer selling it [1]. The business model was obvious: apps were new, people were buying them.

The product insight came from the friction of exchanging contact information. Lieb recounted doing the manual exchange -- "tell me your phone number, I'll type it in, then I'll call your phone, you miss the call" -- ten times in one day with new classmates. He emailed Andy Huibers, a former colleague from Texas Instruments, proposing they build an iPhone app to solve this. Huibers initially thought Android would be bigger, but they started with iPhone [1].

Lieb, Huibers, and Jake Mintz (a classmate also from Texas Instruments) built Bump in their spare time. They launched on March 27, 2009 [1].

## The Billionth Download and the YC Network

Growth was 100% word of mouth. Lieb estimated the total marketing spend at $42 -- "a video tape to put into a borrowed camcorder and a black piece of felt" for the demo video [1]. The chicken-and-egg problem (you cannot use Bump unless the other person also has it) turned out to be the distribution engine rather than an inhibitor: the novelty of physically bumping phones made people willing to convince friends to download it [1].

Lieb executed a press ladder strategy: he emailed a reporter at the Chicago Tribune, then forwarded that article to David Pogue at the New York Times, then escalated further. The breakthrough came when Bump was the billionth app downloaded from the App Store. Apple called Lieb to confirm his servers could handle the publicity. He called his co-founder: "Andy, are we going to be able to do this?" The answer was "I don't know," which Lieb interpreted as "that sounds like a yes" [1].

During YC, Apple included Bump in its international "There's An App For That" TV campaign. The team first learned about it when their graphs showed "a factor of 1,000 in 10 minutes" and friends started texting them saying they'd just seen Bump on Dancing with the Stars. The servers melted down. Lieb sent an email to the YC founder network asking for an Apache expert. Founders came to their office and said, "Step away from the keyboards. We'll fix it" [1]. This was the moment Lieb understood the value of the YC network, validating what Paul Graham had told him: "I won't be able to explain it a priori, why it's really valuable, but I guarantee you'll see that it's very valuable" [1].

## The YC Interview

The interview was with Paul Graham, Jessica Livingston, Robert Morris, and Trevor Blackwell. Before Lieb could speak, Graham said, "Give us your phones. We want to try it." Morris tried to hack it. Livingston asked about the founders' relationship and backgrounds. Graham put his head down, thought, and asked: "How does this become bigger than Google?" Lieb's reaction: "Dude, are you crazy?" But the question revealed how Graham evaluated ideas -- taking something that seemed frivolous and imagining what it could become [1].

## The Retention Problem: 150 Million Downloads, No Business

Fundraising was "super easy" [1]. Bump raised from Sequoia (Series A) and Andreessen Horowitz (Series B, $17 million), with Ron Conway doing the bridge. The thesis for all investors was the same: mobile is a huge wave, Bump is the most popular app, and it solves a real problem [1].

The fatal flaw was frequency of use. Lieb tracked total users and daily bumps but did not rigorously track cohort retention curves -- "I would be like, 'Oh, it's good,' and then I'd go and Google cohort retention curve" [1]. The reality: long-term retention was actually good (people kept the app on their phones and would use it again), but usage frequency was very low. With 10 million MAU but infrequent use, Bump could extract roughly $1 per user per year. "That's a nice business, but it's not a business that you go raise huge amounts of VC funding to go build" [1].

Lieb describes a broader lesson: "Our plan all to that point was just grow, grow, grow, grow. Facebook did it. Twitter's doing it. We'll just do it, too, and it'll be great. And I think we didn't understand the fundamental differences between our business and those businesses" [1].

In hindsight, Lieb believes Bump could have been profitable without VC funding. A freemium model where 1% of users paid a dollar would have been a viable business. But it would not have been profitable at the scale Sequoia or Andreessen Horowitz needed [1].

Lieb also offered a fundraising lesson: "Raise more money if you know how you're going to use that money today. If you have no idea how you're going to raise it... I'm very skeptical of raising money when you don't know why you need it." He wished he had raised less in the Series B because the extra runway delayed the existential reckoning by about a year [1].

## Pivot 1: Flock (Automated Photo Sharing)

By early 2012, growth was flattening. Lieb recalled PG's advice: "When you're stuck, go talk to your users. They will always tell you what they want" [1]. He pulled a list of the top 100 Bump users in the world and began calling them. The discovery: most heavy users were not using Bump for contact sharing. They were using it to share photos with spouses. "Well, it's just easier, and they get the full-resolution file, and I don't have to worry about attachments and emails bouncing" [1].

Simultaneously, the engineering team made a breakthrough. They had been developing Bump's pattern-matching algorithm (an early AI system that used multiple signals to predict which phones bumped). Engineers discovered they could predict who would bump with whom tomorrow by analyzing photo metadata -- the time, location, and co-occurrence of photos on different phones. If two users took photos at the same time and place, they would likely share them [1].

Flock married the user insight (people want automated photo sharing) with the tech insight (co-location can be detected from photo metadata). It looked at Facebook friends, identified when users took photos at the same time and place, and prompted one-button sharing [1].

The team loved it. Friends and family loved it. Then they launched, and "nobody downloaded it" [1]. The failure came down to the cold-start problem described in Chris Dixon's "Come for the tool, stay for the network" framework: the first user of a product must get value even if nobody else uses it. Flock required your friends to already have the app. "99% of people were like, 'Cool. Home screen,' and they never used it again" [1].

The key learning: to solve automated photo sharing, they needed to "upstream" themselves -- become the camera roll itself. If they were the camera roll, everyone would already be using the product, and sharing features would actually work [1].

## Pivot 2: Photo Roll and the Google Acquisition

The third product was Photo Roll, a better photo gallery app (inspired by how Google's Inbox reimagined email). It had Flock's sharing built in as a feature that would "light up" as friends joined. But they arrived at this insight at the end of their $20 million runway [1].

Lieb assessed the situation honestly: "We looked at it, and we said, 'I wouldn't invest in that.'" Storing all photos in the cloud would be expensive. Building this right required the resources of an operating system provider [1].

Google's acquisition brought the answer. The company's mission -- "organize the world's information" -- mapped directly onto what Lieb wanted to build. He found Google already had large teams working on face recognition, image content understanding, and scalable photo backup. "I just saw all this, and I'm like, 'Oh, this is going to be good'" [1].

What Bump's team contributed was the product insight: how people think about their photos and what the right design of a product should be. The combination of Google's AI capabilities and Bump's product vision became Google Photos [1].

## The Photo Assistant Design Method

Lieb designed Google Photos by imagining a hypothetical human photo assistant with unlimited time and intelligence. "If my co-founder, Andy, was my photo assistant, and all he did all day long was help me with my photos, what would he do?" [1]. The brainstorm produced a list of features:

- Back up every photo automatically (so losing a phone does not mean losing photos)
- Identify important people (who is mom, who is the girlfriend)
- Label every photo with its contents
- Create montage movies for anniversaries and meaningful events
- Edit dark or messed-up photos
- Suggest deleting blurry or low-quality images
- Remind you of meaningful moments from your past

This list became the product roadmap. Lieb describes this as "one of the first examples of AI solving real problems that people could resonate with" [1]. The nostalgia feature -- surfacing old photos on anniversaries -- tapped into what Lieb calls "one of the most powerful things in the human experience" [1].

## Product-Market Fit as a Spectrum

Lieb offers a nuanced view of [[Product-Market Fit]].
