He argues it is not binary but staged: "You can have very great product-market fit with some customer base, but as you try to expand your customer base, you might find, 'Oh yeah, there's not product-market fit for these other people over here'" [1]. Even Google Photos, with over a billion users, has product-market fit for a large group but "there's another group of people who use our product that I wouldn't say is product-market fit necessarily" [1]. As a product scales, the next user who joins is very different from the first, and the product must evolve to serve them.

## AI in Products: Two Vectors

Lieb identifies two vectors for applying AI to products [1]:

1. **Scaling human-solvable problems.** Tasks that a skilled human can do but cannot do at scale or at an affordable price. Google Photos exemplifies this: a personal photo assistant is something any person could do; AI makes it possible for billions of people simultaneously.

2. **Solving human-unsolvable problems.** Tasks beyond human cognitive capacity, like optimizing data center power usage. These are riskier bets with potentially bigger payoffs.

Lieb advises AI startups to identify which vector they are pursuing, and then ensure the solution addresses a genuine user need. "Even though the AI tech is super cool, if the solution is like, 'Yeah, that's nice, but whatever,' then it's not going to work" [1].

## The Meta-Problem of Working on the Wrong Thing

Lieb's biggest mistake at Bump was over-focusing on system performance (optimizing microseconds) while missing the bigger picture. He cites [[Sam Altman]]: "The meta problem that kills most startups is working on the wrong thing. It's not screwing up whatever you're working on. It's not your competitors. It's that you were just focused on the wrong thing" [1].

## Advice on Acquisitions

Having experienced both sides of acquisitions (selling Bump, then acquiring companies at Google Photos), Lieb argues the most important question when evaluating an acquisition offer is: "Can you execute your vision towards a much larger audience in a better way inside this company or not?" [1]. Financial terms are secondary to mission alignment.

He notes that founders spend "laughably small" time with the people they will work with daily, while most discussion focuses on financial outcomes. Without fundamental alignment on product vision, an acquisition "is going to be a disaster" [1].

Lieb also uses a question when investing in startups: "If you could go acquire a big company with the purpose of furthering your vision, which big company would you acquire?" The answer reveals both ambition and long-term thinking [1].

## Consumer Product Durability

Lieb distinguishes between products that are popular temporarily and products that serve a durable human need. The test: "Tell me the thing that humans already do today in a limited, not as great way that you are going to somehow amplify times 10 or times 100" [1]. Facebook amplified social connection. Instagram amplified self-expression and social interaction. Products that introduce a novel format without addressing a fundamental need tend to fail once the novelty wears off. "Instagram already exists. So you're not solving a durable human need" [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling One of the Biggest iOS Apps](https://ycombinator.com/library/5f-on-starting-and-scaling-one-of-the-biggest-ios-apps) -- David Lieb, Gustaf Alströmer (n.d.)

title: Cursor: From Failed CAD Co-Pilot to $100M ARR Code Editor
body:
# Cursor: From Failed CAD Co-Pilot to $100M ARR Code Editor

Cursor (built by Anysphere) is an AI-powered code editor that went from zero to $100M ARR in one year (2024). Co-founder Michael Truell was 24 at the time. The company's story illustrates several principles: the value of years of preparation before a startup "overnight success," the courage to compete against an entrenched incumbent, and the power of product quality as a growth engine [1].

## Origins: A Decade of Preparation

Truell's path to Cursor began in middle school. He and his brother wanted to build a hit mobile game. His brother looked at Objective-C and quit. Truell bought a book and kept going. His most viral creation was not a game but a novelty app that spoofed high scores in Piano Tiles and Flappy Bird -- "the technically easiest thing to build, which was maybe a lesson in startups of, you know, the code isn't everything" [1].

In high school, Truell and a friend built robots with reinforcement learning. They wanted to teach a robot dog to learn from positive and negative feedback, like a real dog. Their naivete was an advantage: unable to use standard ML libraries on memory-constrained microcontrollers, they implemented their own neural network library from scratch. "I have memories of us not really understanding calculus but kind of fumbling our way through reimplementing some important ideas from neural networks" [1].

Truell was inspired from early on by [[Paul Graham]]'s essays and the YC community [1].

## The Founding and Failed Ideas

The four co-founders graduated from MIT in 2022. They shared deep interest in AI and two catalysts excited them: GitHub Copilot (proof that AI coding products had a market) and scaling law research (evidence that AI would predictably improve) [1].

They began with a month-long hackathon in early 2022, exploring AI for various knowledge work domains [1].

### The CAD Co-Pilot (6+ months)

Their first serious idea: an AI co-pilot for mechanical engineers using CAD systems like Solidworks and Fusion 360. They chose it because it seemed "boring and sleepy and uncompetitive" -- an "armchair MBA thing" that was a poor fit because none of them were mechanical engineers and the AI science was not ready for that domain [1].

The work was heavy on data scraping (collecting all CAD models on the internet, converting between file formats), training infrastructure, and hacking extensions into non-extensible CAD applications. The result: effectively no users [1].

### Other Failed Projects

Simultaneously, two co-founders built an end-to-end encrypted messaging system that hid not just message content but metadata (who is talking to whom, when). Technically impressive but no traction as either a consumer or B2B product [1].

### The Pivot to Coding

The co-founders had avoided AI for coding because they thought it was too competitive -- GitHub Copilot was already generating $100M+ in revenue. The pivot came from "the desperation of having worked on ideas for a while and not really being excited about them" [1].

A critical realization: "If we were being really consistent with our beliefs, there was going to be an opportunity for all of coding to change in the next 5 years and for all of software development to flow through models. It felt like no one working on the space at the time was really taking that seriously. They had great products and were making them a bit better, but they weren't really aiming for a world where all of coding as we know it today gets automated" [1].

They considered niche approaches first -- security reviews, tools for quants -- but had too many ideas for the general product. "We just had a ton of conviction about that and we had a ton of excitement about that and so at some point we just decided to go for it" [1].

## Building the Product

### The Custom Editor (First 3 Months)

The team initially built their own code editor from scratch using open-source primitives like CodeMirror and language servers. Within four weeks, they had something they could use as their daily driver. Four weeks later, first beta testers. Four more weeks, public launch [1].

The first version had a single command that pulled up a "universal remote" -- users asked the AI to do something, and the system tried to figure out the appropriate response. "There wasn't a lot of control." They learned that given the state of AI at the end of 2022, the interface needed more structure [1].

### The Switch to VS Code

They also learned that building a feature-complete code editor for the world would take far longer than anticipated. VS Code had been developed over 12 years with many people. "We thought, 'Oh yeah, of course, you can spin something up that's just equivalent for the world in a few months.' We learned very rapidly that wasn't the reality." They switched to basing Cursor on VS Code, similar to how browsers base themselves on Chromium [1].

### Custom Models as a Product Lever

Initially, the team avoided model training, burned by the CAD experience. But as the product scaled, building their own models -- especially for Tab (next-edit prediction) -- became "a really important product lever." Product usage data fed back into model improvement [1].

## The Growth Story

### 2023: Wandering the Desert

Throughout 2023, numbers were small and direction was unclear. The team debated whether to pivot again. There was no clear next step much of the time. Early users pulled in different directions: a vocal segment of non-coders wanted attention; another segment wanted tech-stack-specific tools. The team resisted both [1].

A team member occasionally argued that "the product's already good enough -- let's focus on growth engineering." Two-month sprints on growth "kind of washed away compared to the other stuff we worked on that year" [1].

The company ended 2023 with fewer than 10 employees and roughly $1M in revenue [1].

### 2024: 1 to 100 in One Year

Compounding growth continued because each product improvement showed up immediately in the numbers: codebase awareness, next-action prediction, faster and more ambitious predictions, agent-like behavior within the codebase. "We kind of just focused on making the product better. The compounding continued" [1].

YC batch adoption was a signal: from single-digit percentage Cursor usage in 2023 to roughly 80% in 2024. "It just spread like wildfire. The best builders were using it" [1].

### Growth Channels

Initial traction came from one co-founder who had built a niche following on Twitter by reading AI papers and posting analysis. He evangelized the product at launch. But after the initial push, "we stepped away from that and lived like monks in 2023 and just focused on the product." Growth was almost entirely word of mouth [1].

## Truell's Advice

### For Young Builders

"Work on things that you're interested in and do it with people both that you enjoy being around but that you respect a ton. And take that really seriously" [1].

For students: resist the pull toward checking boxes. "Really focusing on something that you're interested in" matters more than credentials [1].

### On the Future of Coding

Truell holds a middle position between "AGI makes all coding tools irrelevant" and "the current paradigm is permanent." His view: AI will transform software development over the next couple of decades. Code will still matter for professional engineers, but the role shifts toward reviewing, editing, and directing AI output. "There will be this long messy middle where you will be working with the AI more and more. It will become like a colleague more and more" [1].

Programming, like math, is "a good general education. I don't think that goes away" [1].

## References

1. [Michael Truell: Building Cursor at 23, Taking on GitHub Copilot, and Advice to Engineering Students](https://ycombinator.com/library/Ms-michael-truell-building-cursor-at-23-taking-on-github-copilot-and-advice-to-engineering-students) -- Michael Truell, Diana Hu (June 2025)

title: True Link Financial: Profitability Through Underserved Markets
body:
# True Link Financial: Profitability Through Underserved Markets

True Link Financial is a neobank that provides financial services for people with special needs -- the elderly, the autistic, those recovering from addiction, and others who need caregivers to help manage their financial lives. Founded by Kai Stinchcombe (YC/Initialized), True Link claims to be the first neobank to cross profitability [1]. [[Garry Tan]] presents the company as a case study in how targeting a specific underserved audience creates structural advantages in customer acquisition, loyalty, and unit economics [1].

## Origin: Personal Experience with the Problem

Stinchcombe's grandmother had Alzheimer's and was struggling to bank: "If you can't remember what you did yesterday, that means you can't remember if you spent any money, which means your budget is always full. Every person with Alzheimer's will at some stage in their condition have that problem" [1].

## The Power of a Specific Underserved Customer

Investors pushed Stinchcombe to broaden the target: "They say we want you to be for everyone. It's a bigger TAM" [1]. Stinchcombe resisted, arguing that specialization creates advantages rather than limitations: "We don't have to go out and try to be a better version of Chase than Chase is, which is really hard. You're boiling the ocean. We have to find stuff that Chase overlooked, which is very easy when you find an underserved audience" [1].

Serving this specific audience meant True Link had to understand domains that no generic bank would: qualifying for benefits, checking into and out of recovery centers, the role of in-home caregivers, types of residential care facilities [1]. Stinchcombe says the first quality they look for in hiring is humility: "If you are going into this thinking you know all the answers, you've just never walked in every single set of shoes" [1].

## Life-Changing Product as CAC Advantage

The depth of understanding led to features with disproportionate impact. One example: if a person in addiction recovery makes an unexpected late-night cash withdrawal, a caregiver receives a text message and can intervene. Stinchcombe: "There's people who say, 'Oh my God, you saved my life,' which is a neat thing to be able to say as a financial services startup" [1].

Tan frames the unit economics: "When you get the target customer right -- that they are underserved and they have a real need and you can solve it -- that's priceless. You can get in front of them for far less. Lifetime value is super high and cost of customer acquisition is super low" [1].

Stinchcombe makes the distribution point explicit: "If you are targeting the same customer everyone else is -- 'Oh, we want affluent, two-income young professionals' -- that's going to be the most expensive customer to advertise to on the internet. What if we want to go chase down a million people with Alzheimer's? Who's advertising to that person?" [1].

## References

1. [An Interview with Kai Stinchcombe, Founder of True Link Financial](https://ycombinator.com/library/Ju-garry-s-channel-an-interview-with-kai-stinchcombe-founder-of-true-link-financial) -- Kai Stinchcombe, [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: Segment: From Classroom Tool to Customer Data Platform
body:
# Segment: From Classroom Tool to Customer Data Platform

Segment (YC S11) is a customer data platform that helps companies collect data from mobile apps and websites and route it to analytics, marketing, and data warehouse tools. Co-founded by [[Peter Reinhardt]], Ilia, Calvin, and Ian, the company grew from four people to 150 in three and a half years after finding [[Product-Market Fit]]. Segment's story is one of the most detailed case studies of the PMF search process in the YC canon -- including two failures, a near-death experience, and a breakthrough from 580 lines of code [1].

## Attempt 1: The Classroom Lecture Tool (ClassMetric)

Segment's original product was designed for exactly the kind of lecture in which Reinhardt was speaking [1]. The problem: professors standing in front of a class had no idea whether students understood the material. The solution: give students a button to push -- "I'm confused" or "I get it" -- with a real-time graph visible to the professor.

The team wrote "hundreds of thousands of lines of code." They went to Stanford and Berkeley campuses, ambushing professors after class. "We pounced on this professor right after Berkeley" [1]. Professors agreed to test it for a few lectures, "sort of out of pity" [1].

The team believed this was product-market fit. It was not. When they stood in the back of the classroom and looked at student screens, "none of them were using the product" [1]. Students had opened their laptops and immediately turned to other things. The technology itself was the distraction. "Basically, putting a laptop into the classroom was the most distracting thing you could conceivably do" [1].

The deeper signal had been visible earlier: professors did not actively want the tool. They agreed to test it, but that is "not the same thing as product market fit where they're like, 'Holy crap, that solves this problem that I have'" [1].

Four weeks after investors signed checks from YC Demo Day, Reinhardt called them back: "By the way, it turns out this is a terrible idea. We're going to do something else. Do you want your money back?" Most investors did not take their money back, saying they had invested for the team [1].

## Attempt 2: The Analytics Tool

The team pivoted to an analytics tool, reasoning that they should have been able to detect their own lack of PMF through data rather than physically standing in classrooms [1].

They read "The Lean Startup" and went out to talk to customers. Again and again, potential customers were "vaguely interested" -- willing to meet for coffee, willing to receive product updates. "And so we thought, 'This must be it, right? This must be product market fit where people are interested in what we're doing.' Again, this is not product market fit. This is idle interest. Very, very big difference" [1].

Based on this idle interest, the team spent six months writing code. Reinhardt went on one sales trip and found potential customers were happy with Mixpanel and Google Analytics but had edge-case feature requests. "I tricked myself into believing that these little edge cases were actually a really wide gap" [1].

The team kept almost reaching PMF. "If we just ship one more feature, one more thing, we're going to get to product market fit. Again, bad idea" [1].

By December 2012, after a year and a half, $500,000 had been spent with nothing to show. The founders met with Paul Graham at YC. Walking around the cul-de-sac on Pioneer Way, Graham stopped, looked at them, and said: "So just to be clear, you've spent half a million dollars and you have nothing to show for it" [1]. They had $100,000 left -- one more shot.

## The Breakthrough: Analytics.js

The breakthrough came from the least likely source -- a tiny internal tool the team had built during YC's first week [1]. When evaluating analytics tools (Kissmetrics, Google Analytics, Mixpanel), the founders noticed that while the outputs differed, the input data was the same. Rather than choosing one tool, they wrote roughly 100 lines of code to send data to all three simultaneously, translating between their APIs [1].

This library was improved incrementally over months. Later, when trying to sell their own analytics tool, the team kept hitting the objection: "I already have Mixpanel installed." Co-founder Ilia suggested adding their own analytics tool as a fourth destination in the library, using it as a growth hack to overcome the sales objection [1].

Something unexpected happened: people loved the library but did not want the analytics service. "The library is fantastic. I just don't really want to use your analytics service" [1]. The library accumulated GitHub stars and pull requests -- "the first time we'd ever felt like people were just finding this thing and doing something with it" [1].

The day after the Graham meeting, co-founder Ian proposed: "Remember that analytics JS library that has been idling on GitHub? I think that could be a big business" [1]. Reinhardt's reaction: "You've got to be kidding me. That's the worst idea I've ever heard. First of all, it's open source, and second of all, it's 580 lines of code. So who the heck is going to pay for that?" [1].

To kill the idea, Reinhardt proposed a test: build a landing page, put it on Hacker News, and see if anyone signs up [1].

It went straight to the top. 300 upvotes on Hacker News. Thousands of GitHub stars. People reached out on LinkedIn demanding beta access: "What does a brother have to do to get bumped up on your beta list?" [1].

"Full stop. Like, compare this to everything previously. Everything changed" [1]. Every metric went haywire. The team no longer had to search for what to build next -- thousands of users told them exactly what integrations they needed. "It flips from being something that you're like pushing against the customer to all of a sudden the customer is running away with it and you're like wait, hold on, it's not quite ready yet" [1].

## The Business Underneath the Open Source

The open source library alone did not fully solve the problem [1]. The real pain point was organizational: marketing teams kept bringing engineering departments contracts with new analytics tools (email marketing, Adobe Analytics) and demanding implementation. Engineers had roadmaps to execute and resented the interruption. The hosted version of Segment let engineers do a single implementation while marketers pushed buttons in Segment's interface to route data wherever they needed [1].

The open source version still required engineer involvement for each new tool. The hosted version eliminated that. "In some ways we got lucky that the open source version doesn't fully solve the problem" [1].

## Subsequent PMF Discoveries

Segment found product-market fit twice more after the initial breakthrough [1]:

**Redshift integration.** The team noticed all business-tier customers were using the S3 integration. Reinhardt visited five customers in New York and asked what they were doing with the data. Five out of five described the same workflow: a data engineering team taking S3 data, converting to CSV, managing schema translation, and uploading to Amazon Redshift. By the third identical answer, Reinhardt knew what to build. The Redshift integration launch caused "almost every metric in the business to go nuts" [1].

**The customer reaction test.** Five months before the lecture, Reinhardt pitched five product ideas to a large customer. Four received polite interest: "That's great. I totally understand what the value is." On the fifth, the customer interrupted, turned to a colleague, and started scheduling follow-up meetings with multiple teams. "That is the feeling of product market fit" [1].

## Pricing Discovery

Segment underpriced for a long time because the founders conflated code complexity with business value [1]. A sales adviser pushed Reinhardt to ask for $120,000 per year. "I'm like, 'That's crazy.'" The adviser threatened to quit. Reinhardt asked, turned red, and the customer negotiated down to $18,000. Over time, the ask became normal. Some customers now pay over $100,000 per year [1]. "The size of the business problem has almost nothing to do with the amount of code written" [1].

## Vision vs. Market

Reinhardt draws a consistent lesson across all three attempts [1]. With the lecture tool and the analytics tool, the team started with a vision of the future and tried to build a product to match. With analytics.js, they started with a "tiny little library" that solved a real problem and had "no vision associated with it whatsoever at the beginning" [1]. The market does not care about vision. "The market wants what it wants, and it will win every time" [1].

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Finding Product-Market Fit](https://ycombinator.com/library/86-how-to-start-a-startup-finding-product-market-fit) -- [[Peter Reinhardt]] (2017)

title: StarCloud
body:
# StarCloud

StarCloud (formerly Lumen Orbit) is a YC-backed company building data centers in space. In November 2024, the company made aerospace history by launching a satellite carrying an NVIDIA H100 GPU into orbit -- "the first time anybody's tried to launch data center grade terrestrial Earth-based GPUs into space" and "a hundred times more powerful than any computer that's ever operated in the vacuum of space" [1].

## The Idea: From Space Solar to Orbital Compute

Co-founder Philip Johnston initially explored space-based solar power -- large solar panels in orbit beaming energy to Earth. The economics did not work: "You lose 95% of the energy transmitting it from space to Earth," and the break-even launch cost was approximately $50 per kilogram, far below current rates [1].

The pivot: instead of sending power down, send the data centers up. By consuming solar energy in orbit rather than transmitting it to Earth, the break-even launch cost shifted to $500 per kilogram -- "much closer to that today than we are to $50 a kilo" [1]. This became the basis for a white paper and eventually the company.

## The Technical Thesis

Operating in sun-synchronous orbit, StarCloud's satellites draw uninterrupted solar energy, radiate waste heat into deep space via large deployable radiators, and consume zero fresh water [1]. The company argues this solves three terrestrial data center constraints simultaneously:

- **Energy**: Data centers are creating an "absolute tidal wave of demand for energy" that Western infrastructure struggles to supply [1]
- **Water**: Terrestrial data centers cool through water evaporation, "causing huge problems in certain parts of the US where they're just sucking the rivers and the lakes dry" [1]
- **Land**: Orbital data centers scale without land constraints [1]

The core IP is the deployable radiator. Co-founder Ezra Feilden, who has a PhD in engineering and spent a decade designing satellites including NASA's lunar pathfinder mission, leads the thermal dissipation work. "Half our engineering team is building a very large, low-cost and low-mass deployable radiator" [1].

## The Team

The three co-founders have complementary backgrounds [1]:
- **Philip Johnston**: Applied math and theoretical physics (undergrad and masters), five years as a software engineer. Not from a space engineering background.
- **Adi Oltean**: Twenty years building data centers at Microsoft, then principal software engineer at SpaceX. Owns the compute module and radiation hardening.
- **Ezra Feilden**: PhD in engineering, decade of satellite design including deployable solar panels for NASA. Owns all satellite structure.

Johnston describes the team as covering "commercial compute, payload, and satellite structure" -- the three essential domains for the product [1].

## Speed of Execution

StarCloud went from founding to having a designed, built, and tested satellite in 15 months [1]. Johnston states that the previous record for any startup going from day one to having something on orbit was four years [1]. The speed came from building the compute module and antennas in-house by hand, with engineers "working through the night up until the day that we shipped down the payload" [1].

## The YC Path

The team applied to YC three times before being accepted [1]. In their initial application, they applied only for the near-term business of providing cloud services to other satellites. They knew about the larger vision of orbital data centers for all non-low-latency compute but were "maybe even embarrassed to talk about such a grand vision." YC "encouraged us to go for it" [1].

Johnston cites a [[Sam Altman]] talk as inspiration: "It's easier to build a hard company than it is to build an easy company. There's one hard thing, which is: can we operate data centers in space cheaply? If we can do that, everything else is easier. Hiring amazing people is easier. Getting people to write about us is easier. Even fundraising is easier" [1].

## Roadmap

- **StarCloud 1** (launched November 2024): 60 kg satellite with NVIDIA H100. Demonstration workloads including first model training in space, first fine-tuning in space, and first Gemini inference in space [1].
- **StarCloud 2** (planned October 2025): At least 10x more powerful, carrying NVIDIA Blackwell architecture with multiple GPUs and high-bandwidth optical terminals for 24/7 low-latency connectivity [1].
- **Long-term vision**: Full 40-megawatt data centers of approximately 100 tons, fitting in one Starship payload bay. The ultimate goal: "almost all data centers, anything that doesn't require very low latency, is operating in space" [1].

Johnston acknowledges they may be "a decade plus away from their grand vision of massive 5 gigawatt data centers in orbit" but notes the company is no longer alone: "Google, SpaceX, Amazon -- they are all exploring data centers in orbit" [1].

## References

1. [Inside The Startup Launching AI Into Space](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/N6-inside-the-startup-launching-ai-into-space) -- Philip Johnston, Aaron Epstein (2025)

title: 23andMe: Disrupting Healthcare by Empowering the Consumer
body:
# 23andMe: Disrupting Healthcare by Empowering the Consumer

23andMe provides direct-to-consumer genetic testing. Co-founded by [[Anne Wojcicki]], the company built the world's largest genetic dataset with health information (over 5 million people at the time of this interview) and navigated years of regulatory tension with the FDA. [[Sam Altman]] interviewed Wojcicki for YC's "How to Build the Future" series [1].

## Origin: Wall Street to Genetics

Wojcicki spent 10 years on Wall Street investing in healthcare before founding 23andMe. She covered small-cap biotechs, pharma companies, hospital systems, and insurance companies, developing a broad understanding of the healthcare landscape [1].

Her founding insight: healthcare is a system that "really effectively monetizes illness." She frames the misalignment starkly: "If I successfully get you to never be diabetic, no one makes money. But if you do become diabetic, there's all kinds of ways I can make money" [1]. No entity in the system was focused on keeping people healthy.

Two converging trends made 23andMe possible. First, the cost of genome sequencing was plummeting. Wojcicki had invested in genetics since 1996 and saw that consumers would soon be able to afford a scan of their entire genome [1]. Second, social networking proved that crowdsourcing worked. "If I just empowered everyone with their genetic information and I crowdsourced all this information... if I have the world's health information, what could I do?" [1].

Altman observes that many transformative companies emerge because "the founder sees an intersection of two pretty different but really important trends a few years before everybody else does" [1]. Wojcicki adds another dimension: the founder personally wants the product. "To me it was totally obvious" [1].

## The Trough of Sorrow

23andMe sold well on launch day but then slowed to 15-25 kits per day. The problem: "People don't understand genetics" [1]. Basic market research could have revealed this, but the team had to learn through the trough.

Wojcicki's response was relentless speaking. She accepted every conference invitation. She never gave the same talk twice, iterating based on audience feedback. Over time she accumulated roughly 500 different talks [1]. Through this process, she identified three barriers to adoption: people did not understand genetics at a basic level, they did not know the medical utility, and they needed social validation that genetic testing was normal [1].

The conversion breakthrough: when Wojcicki started leading with the statistic that over 50% of customers get a medically meaningful result, conversion improved [1].

## Navigating Regulation

23andMe went from unregulated to FDA-regulated, a transition Wojcicki describes as significant. The company's culture of hiring smart people who question everything clashed with the regulatory world's emphasis on rules and obedience [1].

Her view evolved from adversarial to respectful: "People who work at the FDA or people who work in government, it's a public servant job. They care about public safety" [1]. The key communication lesson for science companies: "You can't just tell you, 'Oh we're great, we have great science.' No one believes that. What you need to do is you need to publish" [1]. She never argues with scientists; she hands over publications.

Regulation, she acknowledges, also has an upside: "When you're regulated, it means there's rules. There's guidelines and you can figure out exactly what that means. In some ways, there's more of a path about actually how you have to execute" [1].

## Hiring for Humility

Wojcicki's first two hires were founding scientists found through a friend-of-a-friend network. They had a similar idea, which made the partnership work [1]. These early hires set the bar for talent throughout the company. Altman reinforces this: "If you screw up the first five, seven hires, it's almost impossible to get through that" [1].

Wojcicki describes a deliberate hiring philosophy: "There are a lot of smart people who have humility and there's a lot of smart people who want to tell you how smart they are. And we have opted for the humility" [1]. She looks for people who are "constantly open to feedback and constantly open to constructive criticism and learning about other areas" [1].

Altman adds his own framework: evaluate the net output a person has on the entire organization, not just their individual contribution. "Even if they do a lot themselves, if they make everybody else miserable" the hire is a failure [1]. Wojcicki reinforces with the Steve Jobs example: hiring the wrong senior leader (Sculley) was disastrous. "If you hire the wrong senior leadership and you don't fix it very quickly, it can sink you" [1].

## Persistence as Strategy

Wojcicki describes persistence as the defining characteristic of 23andMe's success. The company's Series A OKRs from 2007 were "remarkably similar" to current goals. One investor told her: "What's amazing, I look back on everything, you're a broken record" [1].

This consistency is a feature, not a bug: "One of the most important things I've learned at doing this now is the importance of persistence. You have to stick with it" [1]. She sees the long-term payoff of persistence -- kids who saw her speak years ago now tell her she inspired them to pursue PhDs in genetics. "It takes a decade to really see the impact" [1].

Altman summarizes: "That is the secret to overnight success is ten years of persistence" [1].

## Vision: Healthy at 100

Wojcicki's personal success metric is being healthy at 100. 23andMe's five-million-person dataset enables intervention studies at unprecedented scale: a six-arm weight loss study with 70,000 participants, research into whether behavior changes can lower Parkinson's risk in genetically high-risk individuals [1].

The ultimate promise: "At the end of this decade, I will tell you what to do" -- personalized, data-driven health recommendations based on genetics and behavior [1].

## References

1. [On starting and scaling healthcare startup 23andMe](https://ycombinator.com/library/5I-on-starting-and-scaling-healthcare-startup-23andme) -- Anne Wojcicki, Sam Altman (2018)

title: Embark Trucks: From Campus Shuttles to Self-Driving Semis
body:
# Embark Trucks: From Campus Shuttles to Self-Driving Semis

Embark Trucks (YC W16) went public, completing a journey from a YC batch team of 19-20 year olds to a publicly traded self-driving truck company. [[Geoff Ralston]], who interviewed the founders in late 2015, describes the trajectory [1].

## YC Interview: Age as a Red Flag, Then Irrelevant

When Ralston saw Alex Rodrigues, Brandon Moak, and former co-founder Michael apply to YC, the immediate concern was whether these teenagers were serious or "merely playing with golf carts and having a good time while never planning to leave school" [1].

The interview dispelled that concern. "Their thinking was startlingly clear and robust. Their answers were direct and to the point. They had a plan and were serious and, by the way, they'd already built the first self-driving vehicle in Canada" [1]. Ralston's conclusion: "Those qualities make age irrelevant" [1].

## The Original Idea: Constrained Environments

The team entered YC as Varden Labs, building self-driving shuttles for campus environments where roads were controlled and speeds low. This was a deliberate constraint: "the first self-driving vehicles would be delivered in a highly constrained environment because the unconstrained problem was, frankly, too difficult" [1].

Two problems emerged during the batch. Campus environments were less constrained than expected, and each campus presented unique challenges that made generalization difficult [1].

## Resisting a Premature Pivot

An Australian mining company approached the team about building self-driving mining trucks in another constrained environment. The team was tempted but chose to stay the course, continue signing up campuses, and deliver a strong Demo Day. Ralston calls this "one of the early, impressively focused decisions" [1].

## The Highway Pivot

Despite initial success, Alex and Brandon eventually concluded the campus market was too small. They pivoted to self-driving trucks constrained to highways -- "an enormous market and although still fabulously difficult, a more tractable problem" [1]. They persuaded their investors to stay on, built their first self-driving truck quickly, and continued to demonstrate the execution speed that had impressed Ralston from the start [1].

## IPO

Ralston describes the founding team's defining traits as "energy, optimism, and above all, the clarity of their thinking" [1]. The IPO was "but one step" in the company's trajectory [1].

## References

1. [Embark Trucks' (W16) road to IPO](https://ycombinator.com/library/Co-embark-trucks-w16-road-to-ipo) -- Geoff Ralston (2021)

title: CoinTracker: Surviving Crypto's Boom-Bust Cycles
body:
# CoinTracker: Surviving Crypto's Boom-Bust Cycles

CoinTracker (YC W18) is a crypto portfolio management and tax compliance platform. Co-founders Chandan Lodha and John built it to solve their own pain point: tracking crypto assets across exchanges and filing taxes. The company's journey illustrates how to navigate an industry defined by extreme cyclical volatility [1].

## Origin

Lodha and his co-founder had worked together previously and were interested in fintech. They were originally building something unrelated to crypto but became frustrated with traditional financial rails: "They're super slow and they're super inefficient" [1]. That frustration led them to explore crypto in 2017. Their first pain point was basic: "How the heck am I going to keep track of all these assets?" [1]. That kernel became CoinTracker's first product for portfolio tracking, later expanding to crypto tax compliance.

## The Cycle Problem

Crypto companies face a unique challenge. Bull cycles bring massive demand all at once; bear cycles can last years, during which "the entire world is telling us that the entire space that we're working in is irrelevant, dead, and doesn't make sense" [1]. Bitcoin alone has been declared dead over 470 times in the past decade.

Lodha frames the strategic question: "When there's excitement, how do we capture that excitement and push it, use it to propel ourselves forward? And when we're in a bear cycle, how do we hunker down and use our resources effectively to just continue building so that when that next wave of crypto adoption comes, we can ride that wave?" [1].

## The 2020 Near-Death Experience

In 2020, CoinTracker was six months away from shutting down. Lodha describes it as the lowest point of his professional life: "It's just a feeling of desperation of just like, oh my God, like nothing is working and there's nothing we can do about it" [1].

Two things got them through [1]:

1. **The co-founder relationship.** Lodha states: "I have an amazing co-founder and I cannot imagine having gone through this without him."
2. **First-principles thinking.** Rather than reacting to the local climate, they asked: does this mission make sense regardless of where we are in the cycle? Does it improve users' lives? They stuck to those first principles.

They also had direct, vulnerable conversations with the team: "We're not going to lie to you. Here is the reality of the situation. Here's what it means for us and here's what we need to accomplish in order to get back on a growth trajectory" [1].

## The Growth Trap

During DeFi summer in 2021, CoinTracker grew 14x. They focused on driving growth and acquiring users. But as the market pulled back, customer feedback revealed a problem: they had lost their product edge. The crypto ecosystem had evolved -- liquidity pools, Uniswap, advanced DeFi activity -- and CoinTracker had not kept up [1].

"It was a painful realization having users tell us that we weren't solving their problem anymore" [1].

## CoinTracker 2.0: The Nine-Month Rebuild

CoinTracker halted everything for nine months to reimagine the product and company. Two specific changes transformed how the company operated [1]:

### User Obsession

Everyone in the company began talking to users constantly: "From customer support to product design to marketing to the product management team to engineers." Lodha himself talks to users daily and monitors Reddit and Warpcast. "We want to be there. We want to understand what's working and what's not" [1].

### "Think Long-Term, Ship Today"

The company's guiding principle shifted from "think long-term" to "think long-term, ship today." This small change "gave everyone in the company the permission that they needed to take some risks and experiment and just build the thing that they wanted to build and try it out." The result: "The entire execution of the company massively accelerated almost overnight. People were shocked" [1].

## Lessons for Cyclical Industries

Lodha's core advice [1]:

- **Take a long-term view.** The companies that survive bear markets "get to reap the benefits of the really good times."
- **Stay humble when things go well.** During bull runs, do not lose product focus.
- **Stay believing when things are tough.** First-principles conviction in the mission matters more than the current market climate.
- **Use winters to rebuild.** Crypto winters are building seasons. CoinTracker used theirs to become the strongest product in the space by the time the next bull cycle arrived.

## References

1. [How to Survive the Crypto Boom & Bust Cycle](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Km-how-to-survive-the-crypto-boom-bust-cycle) — Chandan Lodha, [[Garry Tan]] (2024)

title: Boom Technology
body:
# Boom Technology

Boom Technology (YC W16) is building supersonic passenger airplanes. Founded by [[Blake Scholl]], the company aims to make high-speed travel economical for airlines and affordable for passengers -- fifty years after the Concorde's design [1].

## Origin

Scholl had been talking about supersonics for 10 years before starting Boom. After his first startup was acquired by Groupon, he made a list of startup ideas in descending order of how awesome it would be if they worked, ignoring feasibility. "I figured, 'I'll cross things off the list and I'll end up working on idea number five.' And, as luck had it, the more I got into supersonics, the more I learned that it's not crazy, and actually the time is now" [1].

His previous work on internet coupons at Groupon was a catalyst: "There's nothing like working on Internet coupons to make you yearn to work on something you really love" [1].

## The Case for Founder-Led Aviation

Scholl observes that supersonic travel represents a unique case in technology where capability went backward. "Our phones are better, our computers are better, we're about to have cars that drive themselves and genetically-engineered medicine, and yet we're still flying at 1960 speeds" [1].

He traces every major breakthrough in aviation to founder-led companies: the Wright brothers (bicycle entrepreneurs), the DC-3 (first practical airliner), and the De Havilland Comet (first jetliner). The last new commercial aircraft company was founded in 1921. The last founder retired from the industry in 1958. Since then, Boeing and Airbus have optimized existing designs but have not advanced human capability [1].

## YC Experience

Scholl was initially skeptical: "Isn't YC for mobile social apps?" But after connecting with other hard tech founders who had done YC, he concluded it would help with traction and raising capital -- the biggest risk for Boom in the early days [1].

YC gave two pieces of advice: get out of stealth mode, and go sell airplanes. Both proved critical [1].

## The Virgin Deal

For demo day, Scholl assessed his pipeline (United, Delta, Lufthansa, Air China) and concluded none would close in eight weeks. He focused on startup airlines and Virgin [1].

The team pursued Richard Branson through two channels: friends inside Virgin Galactic and astronaut Mark Kelly on Boom's advisory board. They secured a 15-minute breakfast meeting by coordinating requests from both avenues and showing up at a Virgin Galactic spaceship rollout [1].

The ask was crafted to be easy: "We're not asking for your money, we're asking, 'When this flies, do you want a Virgin logo on it?'" Using demo day as a deadline, Boom told Virgin, "We are launching the company on demo day, and either you can be part of it or not" [1].

The result: 24 hours before Boom's demo day slot, Virgin committed to the first 10 airplanes with options, plus Virgin Galactic support for building. Boom presented $5 billion in LOIs -- a demo day record [1].

## Lessons

Three sales principles distilled from the experience [1]:
1. Be persistent beyond the point of discomfort
2. Have a believable external deadline
3. Make the deal maximally attractive to the other side

For hardware companies at YC, find progress that "both is and looks like actual, substantive progress." Boom built a physical model of the airplane, 3D-printed the jet engine design, and brought to demo day one of the actual engines that would power the first aircraft [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Aerospace Company Boom](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5Q-on-starting-and-scaling-aerospace-company-boom) -- [[Blake Scholl]] (n.d.)

title: Pinduoduo
body:
# Pinduoduo

Pinduoduo is a Chinese social e-commerce platform that grew from launch in 2015 to 585 million active buyers and over $144 billion in gross merchandise value in just four years [1]. By 2020, it was China's second-largest e-commerce platform by active users, trailing only Alibaba [1]. The company reached a $57 billion market capitalization despite launching into a market dominated by JD and Taobao/TMall, which combined for $433 billion in GMV the year Pinduoduo launched [1].

[[Anu Hariharan]] and Nic Dardenne analyzed Pinduoduo as a case study in how social commerce can create entirely new market categories rather than competing head-on with established transactional platforms.

## The Core Insight: Shopping Is Social

Pinduoduo found space in e-commerce not as a competitor to search-based platforms like JD, but as a new category: interactive and social shopping [1]. The fundamental observation is that in the physical world, "shopping is meant to be 'interactive and fun' and purchases are regularly informed by friends and family" [1]. Traditional e-commerce platforms like JD, Alibaba, and Amazon optimize for efficiency, funnel conversion, and purchase rates. They do not account for the social dimension of shopping.

The evidence for this gap was visible in category penetration data: in 2020 in the US, only 29% of apparel, 11% of health and personal care, and 3% of food and beverage purchases happened online, compared to 55% of books/music and 43% of computers/electronics [1]. Social shopping is particularly important for categories where consumers seek feedback or recommendations from friends.

## Team Purchase: The Core Mechanic

The core of Pinduoduo's experience is team purchase, where buyers form groups to receive discounts from suppliers [1]. The flow works as follows:

1. Merchants set two prices for each item: individual purchase and team purchase [1]
2. A user opting for team purchase can either initiate a new team or join an existing one [1]
3. The initiator shares via [[WeChat]] to recruit friends, or waits for other Pinduoduo users to join [1]
4. The team must form within 24 hours for the order to be confirmed [1]
5. Once formed, the product ships within 48 hours [1]

![Pinduoduo Team Purchase Flow](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/22cf244a2ed3.jpg)

Nearly all Pinduoduo transactions use team purchase [1]. In early days, groups were large (10+ members), but requirements have declined as the platform scaled [1].

### Why Team Purchase Is Not Groupon

The comparison to Groupon is common but misleading [1]:

| Dimension | Pinduoduo | Groupon |
|---|---|---|
| Deal design | By sellers, initiated by consumers | Designed and managed by sellers |
| Product type | Everyday goods (fruits, vegetables, apparel) | One-off products/experiences |
| Merchant geography | Diverse merchants across China and globally | Mostly local sellers |
| Consumer value | Discounts on goods users need anyway | Aggregating demand on low-value/excess inventory |

### Strategic Launch Category

Pinduoduo launched in fruits and vegetables for two strategic reasons [1]:
1. Incumbents were focused on non-perishable items, limiting competition
2. Fruits and vegetables are low order value, high frequency goods, creating habitual usage

When buyers saw good deals, they forwarded them to neighbors and friends via [[WeChat]] to form teams of 10+ buyers. Group initiators received produce for free for helping Pinduoduo acquire 10 incremental users [1]. This drove organic sharing and rapid user growth: only one month after launching their first-party app in January 2016, Pinduoduo had over 10 million customers [1].

For comparison, Alibaba crossed the 500 million active buyer threshold 14-15 years after launch versus just 4 years for Pinduoduo [1].

### Network Effects

Pinduoduo exhibits what Hariharan calls one of the most powerful network effects in the world, governed by [Reed's law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reed%27s_law): "The value of a group-forming network is proportional to number and the ease with which groups form within it" [1]. The comparison is to Slack and WhatsApp Groups, which grew exponentially by tapping into groups of social relationships.

The key takeaway for startups: "New commerce platforms that can leverage social relationships will accelerate the offline to online transition and if you are able to tap into groups of social relationships vs. one-to-one relationship the faster your platforms will scale" [1].

## WeChat as Growth Platform

A significant enabler of Pinduoduo's virality was the widespread use of [[WeChat]] in China. Tencent (WeChat's owner) is a large investor in Pinduoduo and allowed the platform to grow on top of the WeChat ecosystem [1]. Hariharan notes that "it is highly unlikely that Facebook would allow a social commerce application like Pinduoduo to be built on top of Messenger or Instagram in the US" but adds that messaging fragmentation in the US means this is not necessarily a blocker for US-based social e-commerce [1].

## Gamification and Engagement

Pinduoduo operates on the principle that users visit without specific purchase intent, "much like visiting a real-world shopping mall" [1]. Time spent directly correlates with purchases, so Pinduoduo invested heavily in gamified engagement features.

### Daily Check-Ins

Users receive redeemable points for daily check-ins. Rewards accumulate over many months, and users must reach a minimum value (e.g., 30 RMB) to cash out as a no-minimum-spend voucher [1]. This creates a simple daily habit loop that eventually ties back to commerce.

![Pinduoduo Daily Check-In](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/e621e756cab8.png)

### Price Chop

Users can get products for free by sharing a custom link with friends within a 24-hour window [1]. Each friend who clicks and engages with Pinduoduo (no purchase required) drives an incremental discount for the initiator. The difficulty mechanics mirror MMORPG leveling: "it becomes harder to level up as you graduate to higher levels" [1]. Difficulty is customized based on the user (easier for low-engagement users) and the item (expensive items are harder to chop) [1].

Price Chop simultaneously increases customer lifetime value and acquires new customers through user-to-user sharing [1].

![Pinduoduo Price Chop](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/bf7bb9afff8b.png)

### Card Programs

Three key card types drive specific user behaviors [1]:

1. **Free Pass Card**: Loyalty-style card allowing team purchase discounts without joining a team. Earned after two purchases.

![Pinduoduo Free Pass Card](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/cb1438a37151.png)

2. **Brand Black Card**: Encourages reviews by offering discounts on branded products after 2-4 reviews. Improves platform quality for all users.

![Pinduoduo Brand Black Card](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/6ed39bc1ed0c.png)

3. **Brand Card**: Promotes branded products. Users share brand cards with friends via WeChat, leveraging trusted friend recommendations to drive branded product adoption.

![Pinduoduo Brand Card](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/e18ba1c6e7bf.png)

### Mini Games

Pinduoduo hosts in-app games to drive daily engagement. **Duo Duo Orchard** (described as "Farmville except now the rewards are real physical goods") has users nurture a virtual fruit tree that eventually yields a real box of fruit shipped to their address [1]. The game had more than 11 million DAUs. Progress is tied to behaviors that drive revenue: shopping on Pinduoduo yields water droplets for your tree, and team purchases unlock special tools [1].

![Pinduoduo Duo Duo Orchard](../raw/posts/images/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce/b33c6dcf07c3.png)

**DD Bank** is a piggy bank game where users accumulate exchangeable coins over time [1].

The games create "a win-win-win situation for merchants, consumers, and themselves. More specifically, merchants see more volume, consumers have fun and receive unique discounts, and Pinduoduo generates revenue" [1].

## Personalized Recommendations

Unlike search-based platforms, Pinduoduo is recommendation-driven [1]. Based on a user's social graph, category preferences, and trust relationships, the platform personalizes the experience. For new customers, Pinduoduo shows different categories based on what they know about the user and their friends, then uses interactions to build a buying persona [1].

Personalization extends to value perception: based on purchase and browsing history, Pinduoduo determines whether a user values high-cost branded or low-cost non-branded items and adjusts recommendations to match willingness to pay [1].

## Implications for Global E-Commerce

Hariharan argues that while team purchase may be specific to China's commercial landscape, the underlying insight about social integration in commerce is "likely entirely universal" [1]. The core takeaway: "Web 1.0 platforms -- including Amazon -- optimize for efficiency and don't serve trust-based categories very well" [1].

YC portfolio companies already pursuing variations of this model [1]:
- **[Snackpass](https://www.snackpass.co/)**: A food app for college campuses with social features (gift-sending, virtual pet hatching through food orders)
- **[Meesho](https://meesho.com/)**: An Indian reseller marketplace where customers create microbusinesses selling to friends and family via WhatsApp

"Social commerce does not just mean connecting user accounts to Facebook. It means investing in creating physical world experiences online -- specifically bringing the fun of shopping offline to online platforms" [1].

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) — [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne (2020)

title: Astranis: Building Satellites Without Being a Billionaire
body:
# Astranis: Building Satellites Without Being a Billionaire

Astranis (YC W16) builds small satellites for geostationary orbit -- a new category of spacecraft designed to provide telecommunications services at lower cost than traditional large satellites. The company was started by two founders in a San Francisco apartment, went through Y Combinator with a $500,000 investment, and is now worth over a billion dollars with more than 400 employees [1].

## Origin

John Gedmark, an aerospace engineer by background, had spent time on large satellite programs and "knew that that was not for me" [1]. He reconnected with Ryan McKlinko, a co-founder he had known for over twenty years through Students for the Exploration and Development of Space. McKlinko had moved to San Francisco to work at Planet, one of the pioneering space startups [1].

The insight: nobody was taking a new approach to satellites in geostationary orbit, a "super valuable orbit" for telecommunications. They decided to take a crack at it [1].

## The MVP Problem in Hard Tech

As Gedmark puts it: "Turns out minimum viable product for a geostationary telecommunication spacecraft is not very minimal" [1].

The approach was to find the smallest possible proof point. From the earliest days, they drew a line in the sand: "We as a company are going to put something in space and show that we can build a satellite and that it will work in space. Let's just figure out how to do that and do it at the lowest possible cost" [1].

## The YC Batch: Three Months to Build a Satellite

When Astranis joined YC in Winter 2016, their partner told them: "You have three months until you can show up on demo day, and whatever you have on demo day, it better be as impressive as possible. Anything that doesn't get done until after demo day, forget about it. It won't matter" [1].

The team committed to building a first prototype satellite in three months -- work that could take other companies years. Because it was a demo rather than a production satellite, they could be flexible on requirements. The core elements were "fundamentally kind of simple" when building for demonstration rather than deployment [1].

They built the prototype and brought it to Demo Day.

## Scrappy Infrastructure

The seed round raised at Demo Day was one of the largest at YC at the time, but space is expensive, so scrappiness was essential [1]:

- They worked out of an 1,800 square foot apartment
- Built satellites on Ikea desks fitted with ESD mats
- Built a "clean room" from PVC piping and shower curtains, sprayed with a conductive substance called staticide to prevent static discharge
- Proved you could build spacecraft-grade hardware without expensive infrastructure [1]

## Demo Day as Proof

Gedmark's Demo Day pitch became one of the videos YC continues to show to new batches as an example of what a startup can accomplish in three months. The pitch addressed the two main investor objections [1]:

1. **It seems impossible** -- answered by showing they had already built a working prototype
2. **It could be a science project, not a business** -- answered by demonstrating the $122 billion addressable market

Gedmark had practiced the pitch fifty times over three days. It paid off: Astranis raised enough to eventually launch a satellite into space [1].

## Scaling

Eight years after founding, Astranis has [1]:
- More than 400 employees working out of Pier 70 in San Francisco
- In-house testing facilities including vibration tables and thermal vacuum chambers
- A dedicated SpaceX Falcon 9 launch for four new satellites (believed to be the first new company in history to purchase an entire dedicated Falcon 9)
- An operational satellite already in space from 2023
- Next-generation satellite designs in development

The company brought testing in-house as it scaled, including discovering they could perform ion thruster tests inside their existing thermal vacuum chambers -- a discovery that saved the cost and time of sending thrusters elsewhere for testing [1].

## Lessons for Hard Tech Founders

[[Jared Friedman]], who worked with Astranis at YC, frames the broader lesson: "You don't need to be Elon Musk to start a hard tech company." The requirements are domain experience, a great co-founder, a scrappy mindset, and persistence [1].

The 2016 YC batch was a pivotal moment for aerospace at YC: three companies from that era -- Astranis, [[Boom Technology]], and Relativity Space -- are now all billion-dollar companies [1].

## References

1. [You Don't Have to Be a Billionaire to Launch Satellites](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Lm-you-don-t-have-to-be-a-billionaire-to-launch-satellites) — [[Jared Friedman]], John Gedmark (2024)

title: PlanGrid: Building Software for an Industry That Had Never Used It
body:
# PlanGrid: Building Software for an Industry That Had Never Used It

PlanGrid (YC W12) built mobile construction productivity software. The company was acquired by Autodesk for $875 million in 2018, making it one of the largest acquisitions of a YC company at the time. Co-founded by [[Tracy Young]] and four others, PlanGrid grew to 450 employees and solved a problem the founders experienced firsthand as construction engineers [1].

## Origin: The Saddest Story in Silicon Valley

Two of PlanGrid's five co-founders were construction engineers; three were software developers. Young wanted to be an architect but was rejected from architectural programs, leading her to construction engineering. On job sites, she performed QA/QC -- taking specifications and blueprints into the field and checking them against the actual construction [1].

The idea came when co-founder Ryan Sutton-Gee tried to open construction blueprints on a first-generation iPad. The screen displayed "out of memory" because blueprints are 10,000 by 10,000 pixels, far too large for the device to handle. Sutton-Gee's reaction was immediate: "We should build this" [1].

What followed was what Sutton-Gee called "the saddest story in Silicon Valley": having a great idea but no technical co-founders. Young and Sutton-Gee pitched the idea to programmer friends. All agreed it was good. None would quit their jobs [1]. Co-founder Antoine, a high-frequency trading engineer in Chicago, told them: "This is a fantastic idea. You guys should learn how to program" [1].

The breakthrough came accidentally. Young's boyfriend Ralph (now her husband) saw a recurring Thursday night calendar event labeled "peanuts" on Young's shared Google Calendar. When she explained the idea, he was offended: "Do you even know what I do? I'm a full-stack engineer. I'm the best engineer I know. I write iOS and Android apps on the side. I'm a rendering engineer at Pixar Animation. Why have you not told me about this before?" [1]. Ralph became a co-founder and eventually CTO.

## First Users: Lending iPads and Walking Through the App Store

PlanGrid started as a "fun project" with no expectation it could become a business. Young realized it was a real business when they sold their first million dollars without a sales team, roughly two years after starting [1].

The first 20 users were people the founders had worked with on construction projects or gone to university with. Young called them every week: "Hey it's Tracy, just checking in. How's the new update going? Did you load it?" She could see whether they had downloaded updates but called anyway [1]. The signal that the product was working: "At some point people would start using the word, like love" [1].

The product's simplicity was deliberate and essential. PlanGrid's target users -- superintendents, foremen, electricians, carpenters -- had never used software to do their jobs. "How do you design and build software for people who don't even know how to use a computer?" [1]. The team enforced a rule: "If we could build this feature in two buttons, let's make sure we don't do it in five" [1].

The biggest early barrier was hardware, not software. In 2012, construction workers did not have iPads. PlanGrid loaned out iPads for free, then later charged enough to cover hardware costs. Founders sat down with individual users, walked them through the App Store, typed in passwords, and loaded projects. "We knew that it wasn't going to happen because there's so many non-literal fires to put out on a job site every single day" [1].

This was a textbook [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]] approach: educating an entire industry on mobile devices, then educating them on the software, one user at a time.

## Product Breakthroughs

PlanGrid consciously designed one product that worked for everyone in construction -- from project executives to electricians -- across all project types (residential, commercial, roads, bridges). This maximized TAM while avoiding the trap of custom versions for different profiles [1].

Key features that drove adoption:

1. **Sheet overlay and version comparison.** Users could overlay different drawing types (equipment, electrical, plumbing) to see the same room from multiple perspectives, then compare old and new versions with changes highlighted in red [1].
2. **Full sheet search.** PlanGrid was the only construction system that allowed searching for any word across all sheets. A user looking for "magnetic door hold opener" across 5,000 sheets could find the five relevant ones instantly [1].
3. **Automatic version control.** Using machine learning (before it was trendy), PlanGrid could read sheet identifiers like "A101, first floor floor plan" and automatically detect when a new version was uploaded, prompting the user rather than requiring manual version management. The US construction industry loses $20 billion annually to waste caused by building from outdated plans [1].

## Bottoms-Up Sales in a Top-Down Industry

Traditional construction software had existed for 30 years but was written for office workers -- CIOs, VPs of operations -- because the hardware to bring software into the field did not exist. PlanGrid went bottoms-up, selling directly to field workers. "We went directly into the field, which is normally not where software is sold" [1].

## Hiring: Pain Tolerance and Positive Attitude

Young describes two eras of hiring at PlanGrid:

**Sub-50 employees**: Hire generalists with high pain tolerance. "Those five people have to wear multiple hats. They had to not complain about it. There's work like, taking out the garbage because you probably don't have an office manager, and then also code" [1].

She assessed pain tolerance through background signals. One early hire, Taylor, had been canvassing door-to-door, getting cursed at and doors shut on her. Another, Saman, was "literally a door-to-door pencil salesman" for Staples in 2014-2015. He now leads PlanGrid's BD team [1].

**Post-50 employees**: Specialization becomes necessary. For leadership hires, Young looks for authenticity: "If there's ever a moment where you're in an interview and it feels like someone's bullshitting you, they're probably bullshitting you" [1]. She emphasizes reference checking and looks for whether the candidate has done the specific job before.

The cross-country train test: co-founder Ryan's hiring filter was "who would I rather be stuck on a cross-country train with?" [1].

## Firing: The Most Common YC Advice

Young reports that at YC, "almost every single speaker talked about" not firing people fast enough [1]. The blast radius of keeping the wrong person in a leadership role is enormous because "everyone else knows" when someone is not working out.

Her framework: Make it not a surprise. Time-box it. Communicate explicitly: "This is the expectations for this role, you're not meeting the expectations. I think this is what you need to do to fix it. If you don't do this, I will have to ask you to leave in three months from now. That is what you owe your team member" [1].

Young describes her own failure to fire fast enough as one of her deepest regrets: "I still to this day feel this like, it still hurts me at a deep level that I fired someone and it was a complete surprise to them" [1].

## Scaling: Everything Breaks at 150

PlanGrid grew from 5 co-founders to 450 employees. Young identifies 150 employees as the breaking point, consistent with Dunbar's number: "150 was when everything broke" [1].

When hiring executives, the most common mistake was scale mismatch. Executives from very large companies "had never seen a startup of this size. 'Cause it's painful at this size. You don't have 50 recruiters helping you recruit" [1].

## Balancing Parenthood and CEO Responsibilities

Young was pregnant, gave birth, and nursed while leading a 400+ person team, hitting revenue targets, managing a Series C, and navigating an Autodesk acquisition. "You can do all of that. I'm living proof that you can do all of it" [1].

## Negative Ego and Authenticity

Young's executive coach, Stratt, told her: "For someone who doesn't have a lot of ego, you have a lot of negative ego" [1]. She was constantly putting herself down and feeling guilty about mistakes rather than acknowledging them and moving forward. Before every board meeting, she was convinced the board would fire her. When she accidentally mentioned this to board member Carole, "she laughs. This loud cackle almost, like, 'Are you fucking out of your mind?'" The company was doing well; the fear was entirely internal [1].

Her advice to her earlier self: "Learn to be more authentic earlier." In her construction career, she smoked cigarettes to fit in with the crew and even tried chewing tobacco. As CEO, she tried to match what she thought a CEO should look like. "At some point, I don't even know when it happened, but it happened slowly... I just became more and more and more of myself. And the last few years have been the happiest for me" [1].

## References

1. [On starting and scaling construction startup PlanGrid](https://ycombinator.com/library/6L-on-starting-and-scaling-construction-startup-plangrid) -- Tracy Young, Kat Manalac (2019)

title: Box: From Consumer Cloud Storage to Enterprise Platform
body:
# Box: From Consumer Cloud Storage to Enterprise Platform

Box is a cloud content management and file-sharing platform for enterprises. Founded in 2005 by Aaron Levie and three co-founders while they were college sophomores and freshmen, the company pivoted from consumer to enterprise in 2006-2007 and grew to 71,000 customers and 63% of the Fortune 500 [1].

## The Origin: Personal Pain Point

In late 2004, accessing and sharing files was difficult. USB thumb drives, self-emailing files, and FTP accounts were the norm. During an internship, Levie also encountered enterprise content management software that was "way too complicated" [1].

The founders identified three technology trends that could solve the problem: mobile ubiquity (though their original vision was "the entire planet would start to be using Blackberries everywhere" -- "we were wrong"), dropping cost of computing and storage (not yet called "cloud computing"), and increasing internet interconnectedness [1].

They launched box.net with a simple proposition: upload files and access them from any browser or mobile device. Within months, thousands of people signed up. The signup flow required only an email address -- "actually pretty disruptive at the time to traditional IT" where employees needed corporate approval for technology [1].

## The Garage Phase

The founding team dropped out of college and moved to the Bay Area. They lived and worked out of a renovated garage (technically zoned as a garage, "so it was actually pretty illegal") [1]. Levie describes the psychological gap between the glory narrative of dropouts -- "Bill Gates dropped out. Steve Jobs dropped out" -- and the reality of four people iterating on a business model daily, "pivoting almost every single day" [1].

## The Consumer-to-Enterprise Pivot

The critical decision came when the team had to choose between consumers and businesses [1].

**The consumer problem:** The maximum a consumer would pay was about $10 per month. Google, Facebook, Apple, and Microsoft were clearly going to provide free storage. "Not a great business model when you only have about 35% of the market willing to upgrade" [1]. Advertising was considered and rejected: "You really don't want to see ads when you're looking at your tax documents" [1].

**The enterprise opportunity:** Companies were spending millions on legacy content management software. Box could deliver equivalent or better functionality for a fraction of the cost -- potentially a 10 to 20x improvement [1].

The pivot felt unappealing. The founders were 22 and 23. "There's a long list of things that you could do that aren't very cool. Somewhere near the bottom of that list when you're 23 is starting an enterprise software company" [1]. Enterprise software carried connotations of slow innovation, expensive technology, complex interfaces, pushy sales reps, and rare updates [1].

The resolution: "If we're going to forego all the fun and all the exciting times we would have as a consumer company, then we have to find a way to build an enterprise software company that operates and acts and innovates and moves like a consumer company" [1].

## Why Enterprise Was Ripe for Disruption

The traditional enterprise software model had structural weaknesses that new technology trends could exploit [1]:

- Complex technology sold through salespeople (no self-service)
- Only affordable by the largest companies
- Customer bore the security risk (buying separate security layers)
- Customer bore the financial risk (paying for licenses upfront regardless of usage)
- Proprietary, closed platforms

The new model inverted each: simple and end-user-focused, available to all sizes, security baked in, vendor bears the risk (subscription model), and open systems [1].

## The Disruption in Practice

Box's initial product was absurdly simple by enterprise standards. Legacy competitors "would have died laughing" at a product where "all it does is you upload a file and then you access the file" [1]. But that simplicity was the disruption. Enterprise vendors had assumed value correlated with feature density. Box proved that "the fewer features, the simpler set of functionality in one place is actually going to be our value proposition" [1].

## The Ongoing Enterprise Opportunity

Levie argues that the best opportunities in enterprise software are still ahead (as of 2017) [1]. Two factors drive this:

1. Most enterprise technology remains legacy -- "old, slow, complex, expensive" -- and must be retired
2. Every company in every industry recognizes the need to modernize because of digital disruption: "If you're a media company, you're freaking out because of Netflix. If you're a financial services company, you're freaking out because of Stripe" [1]

## Recommended Reading

Levie recommends four books for anyone building a B2B software company [1]:
- *The Innovator's Dilemma* (Clayton Christensen)
- *Crossing the Chasm* (Geoffrey Moore)
- *Blue Ocean Strategy* (W. Chan Kim and Renee Mauborgne)
- *Behind the Cloud* (Marc Benioff)

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Building for the Enterprise](https://ycombinator.com/library/73-how-to-start-a-startup-building-for-the-enterprise) -- Aaron Levie (2017)

title: Flexport: From Solo Founder to Eight Billion Dollar Logistics Platform
body:
# Flexport: From Solo Founder to Eight Billion Dollar Logistics Platform

Flexport is a technology-driven freight forwarding company that helps nearly 10,000 companies move $19 billion of goods across 112 countries [1]. Founded in 2013 by [[Ryan Petersen]], the company was valued at $8 billion and subsequently raised a $985 million Series E while profitable [1]. [[Garry Tan]] invested at the beginning through both Y Combinator and Initialized Capital [1].

## Origin: Eleven Years Before Venture Capital

Petersen started businesses and ran companies for 11 years before raising venture capital [1]. He co-founded ImportGenius.com, one of the largest providers of shipping manifest data, while sustaining himself through three flexible side jobs. He was Flexport's sole employee for four years before applying to YC [1].

His domain expertise came from direct experience. He was one of the first Amazon third-party merchants, selling imported motorbikes from China. He lived in China for two years running supply chain [3]. Tan describes the pitch: "My favorite pitch meetings are with founders who teach you something about the world you didn't know before. Global logistics was one of those things, and he knew it intimately" [3].

When Petersen applied to YC, his vision was narrow: "I want to be TurboTax for customs" [1]. He once believed long-range planning was "a sign you're gonna fail" [1]. The vision expanded through direct customer contact to encompass all of global trade logistics.

## The Core Loop

Flexport onboards importers, who bring their factories onto the platform -- 18 factories within three years of onboarding, 36 within six years [1]. Factories book shipments, Flexport dispatches trucks in 112 countries, clears customs, ships across oceans, clears destination customs, and delivers to warehouses. Petersen describes 80% of effort at Flexport as making this core loop "easier and cheaper" -- specifically, removing human interactions from the chain [1].

Tan categorizes Flexport as a "faster" startup in his [[Better, Faster, or Cheaper]] framework: Flexport speeds air freight by two to three days and resolves insurance claims six times faster by replacing paper with software [2].

## Flexport Capital: The Fintech Layer

Tan identifies Flexport Capital as a potential multi-billion-dollar business built on top of structured logistics data [1]. A lending business needs four advantages: customer acquisition, underwriting, collections, and cost of capital. Flexport has strong advantages in the first three [1]:

- **Customer acquisition**: importers are already on the platform placing orders.
- **Underwriting**: customs declarations require showing wholesale prices; Flexport knows margins, growth rates, payment history, and can meet borrowers face-to-face.
- **Collections**: under maritime law (older than the U.S. Constitution), the freight provider has first lien on cargo. If a borrower defaults, Flexport can seize and sell the goods. Petersen: "We lend at the wholesale rate, so even if you fail and I have your stuff, you'd rather pay me back and sell the stuff. We've had bankruptcies in the portfolio that still paid back" [1].

Tan frames this as "buy now, pay later for global trade" [1].

## The Edge Between Order and Chaos

Petersen's most detailed management thinking concerns the tension between process and agility at scale. Key lessons:

**Money wants to be spent.** After every fundraise -- $20 million Series A, billion-dollar Series D, $985 million Series E -- Flexport lost spending discipline. After the Series A, Petersen committed $100,000 to a trade show booth the next day. After the Series D, "burn went crazy" and they spent two years tightening belts [1].

**Hatred of bureaucracy is not enough.** "You'll actually get more bureaucracy from not having any process and policies and standards than you will from having too much" [1]. He uses the metaphor of an edge between order and chaos that shifts as the company grows.
