**Architecture determines speed.** Service-oriented architecture (teams communicating via APIs) and organizational architecture (single-threaded ownership of metrics) are the same concept applied to different domains. Both slow you down initially but enable speed at scale [1].

**Team of teams.** The organizational ideal is small teams operating like startups, each owning a single metric. Petersen cites the Jeff Bezos API memo and discusses how decomposing net revenue into buying, pricing, and sales teams lets each team move independently [1].

**The founder meddling trap.** Petersen considers himself strong at marketing, and recognizes this as counterproductive: "The area that I think I'm good at, I probably hold Flexport back" because he could not stop meddling, preventing independent development [1].

## On Writing Things Down

Petersen initially viewed mission statements and company values as corporate overhead. His position changed once a layer of managers existed between him and all employees: "You need to start writing these things down to clarify for people what they're doing because it doesn't happen through force of personality at scale" [1]. The risk of over-codification: "If everything in your culture is codified and written down, you might become a culture of rule followers" [1].

## Industry Context

Of the top hundred freight forwarders in the world, Flexport is the only one founded after Netscape (1994) [1]. Most competitors still use mainframe software (IBM AS400). Many pay 100% of cash flow as dividends and buybacks -- "clearly a sign you don't have great things to invest in" [1].

Petersen is not worried about incumbents but is "paranoid" about tech debt creating the same dynamics within Flexport. "We're definitely moving slower than we were six years ago when it comes to product development" [1].

## References

1. [Flexport CEO Ryan Petersen on Scaling a Startup from Zero to $8B](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jo-garry-s-channel-flexport-ceo-ryan-petersen-on-scaling-a-startup-from-zero-to-8b) -- [[Ryan Petersen]], [[Garry Tan]] (2021)
2. [Billion Dollar Startups Are Better, Faster, or Cheaper](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jn-garry-s-channel-billion-dollar-startups-are-better-faster-or-cheaper) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)
3. [Billion Dollar Startup Ideas](https://ycombinator.com/library/Ji-garry-s-channel-billion-dollar-startup-ideas) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: Lob: From Inkjet Printer to Enterprise Mail API
body:
# Lob: From Inkjet Printer to Enterprise Mail API

Lob (YC S13) makes APIs for enterprises to programmatically send physical mail -- letters, postcards, and other mail formats. Founded by [[Harry Zhang]] and co-founder Leore, Lob grew to nearly 70 employees over six years and signed enterprise contracts ranging from $30,000 to millions per year [1].

## Origin: A Problem Found at Microsoft

The idea started from complaining. Zhang was driving back from a ski trip with co-founder Leore and venting about a project at Microsoft. His team had built a system for sending custom training materials and webinar invitations, but email was not allowed and telesales was too expensive. The fallback was a direct mail campaign. It worked so well that Zhang received a large budget to scale it -- then spent three months struggling with the operational complexity of sending physical mail at scale [1].

## The Minimal MVP: An API That Did Nothing

When Zhang and Leore applied to YC, they had almost nothing built. Their MVP was an API that took whatever input it received and mirrored it back. "We were secretly hoping that we wouldn't have to demo, because honestly, we hadn't built a lot of product yet" [1]. [[Kevin Hale]], who evaluated them, confirms: "I would say it was fairly embarrassing in terms of what you guys had" [1].

What they did have was a deep understanding of the problem. They had talked to many potential customers and understood the core pain points of sending physical mail programmatically. Zhang frames their philosophy: "We've always operated from the point of view of we want customers to pay us for something before we actually go and do it" [1].

## First Customer: A 16-Year-Old Team Fortress 2 Merchant

Lob's first customer came from a Hacker News Show HN post. William Whey, a 16-year-old who ran one of the largest Team Fortress 2 stores online, had a fraud problem: when customers bought digital items, he had no proof of delivery, leading to chargebacks. His solution was to also send a physical letter with a confirmation code. He needed an API to automate this [1].

The use case was one Zhang would never have imagined. "I think it goes to show, you can always think you have a way to build a product, but not always" [1]. This early lesson about unexpected use cases shaped Lob's product strategy.

## The Assembly Line: Doing Things That Don't Scale

In the earliest days, the API connected to a database that Zhang serviced with his home inkjet printer. "We printed everything on my home printer out of my apartment" [1]. At one point, he and his co-founder were watching TV while running an assembly line of letters they stuffed by hand. Only when volume exceeded what they could manually handle did they seek commercial printing partners [1].

## The Lighthouse Customer Problem

Zhang describes the enterprise sales flywheel as a chicken-and-egg problem: "You need a lighthouse customer to get other big customers. Getting the first one is always the hardest" [1]. Lob's first major enterprise client was a large New York insurance company, found through cold email. The CEO responded, asked qualifying questions, and connected Zhang to the VP of Data. The insurance company needed HIPAA-compliant printing and had lost confidence in their existing print vendor [1].

Zhang's cold email approach: short, personalized, specific about the problem. "This is what we do, here's how we're different, and I think we can help you with your current problem in these three ways" [1]. He did not know about their specific HIPAA problem -- the timing was lucky, but the targeting was deliberate.

For subsequent enterprise deals, Lob maps out each target organization before outreach: who are the key stakeholders, what motivates each person, what's the right message [1]. They combine cold outreach with conference attendance and marketing that makes "Lob pop up everywhere" for the target account [1].

## The Mugs API: A Cautionary Tale

During YC, a customer requested coffee mugs printed on demand through the API. The revenue number looked enormous for a small company. Zhang's team scrambled for two weeks, built a fully functioning mugs API, then went to the customer. "He was like, 'Awesome, we'll get right on it.' He went dark, he stopped responding" [1].

Total mugs sold through the API: two. One went to [[Dalton Caldwell]], who was running App.net at the time [1].

The lesson: "You always want to be selling a little bit ahead of where you are, but you also want to align with the customer what you actually have and don't have" [1]. The team had built without getting commitment in writing.

## Booking.com: The Right Way to Sell Ahead

The mugs lesson paid off when Lob closed Booking.com, one of their largest customers. Booking.com was already using Lob's address verification API when GDPR prompted them to reevaluate all print vendors. They needed European operations, which Lob did not yet have [1].

Instead of over-promising, Zhang was upfront about current capabilities and negotiated a timeline for European expansion as part of the deal. "Our customer actually wanted to help us get things going faster" [1]. The transparency turned the deal into a partnership rather than a gamble. "If the customer really wants to work with you, they're going to find a way to make it work with you, and that's the best position to be in" [1].

## Pricing: The Platform Fee Innovation

Lob's biggest pricing mistake was structural. Initially, customers bought bundles of mail pieces (e.g., 100,000 letters per year) at a per-piece price. The problem: enterprise customers compared Lob's price directly to traditional mail providers and asked "why are you five cents more expensive?" [1]. The five cents represented Lob's technology value, but it was invisible in the per-piece comparison.

The fix: separate the technology from the commodity. Lob introduced a platform fee of $25,000-$30,000 per year for API access, with mail pricing at or below market rates for the physical product. "Now we can focus the conversation on how do you value Lob's technology, is that worth 25 to 30,000, instead of having a conversation about what is the cost of a mailpiece" [1].

This worked because enterprise customers were already paying agencies separately to manage mail campaigns -- money from a different budget line. Lob's platform fee captured the same value but made it legible within the customer's existing budget categories [1].

## HIPAA as a Pricing Lever

Regulated industries provided natural pricing power. HIPAA compliance costs Lob more to provide, but the pool of competitors capable of handling sensitive information programmatically is small. "We actually charge more for those customers, because it costs us more. And also because there's less offerings that are able to do something like that programmatically" [1].

The strongest signal for feature prioritization: "We had customers that will be like, 'We'll pay you if you guys can do X for us.' That's when you really know you're on to a feature that's really important" [1].

## API Documentation as Product Design

Hale observes that API companies are a pure expression of interface design: "the interface is just documentation" [1]. For Lob, documentation is the number one product priority. "When your core product is an API, you better have the best documentation on the planet" [1]. Hale compares this to Stripe, which set the standard for developer-facing API documentation.

## Vertical Integration: Why Lob Does Not Own Printers

Hale pressed Zhang on whether Lob would eventually vertically integrate and buy printing facilities, as Amazon eventually built its own warehouses. Zhang's answer: no. Printing is a commodity with hundreds of thousands of specialized providers. The network model gives Lob flexibility to support different mail formats and customer needs without massive capital expenditure [1].

The team almost bought an HP Indigo commercial printer before YC. They met a sales rep at Starbucks, went through the specifications, and then asked the price. "Something like over a million dollars. And I sort of looked at Leore, I was like, 'Dude, we're not doing that'" [1]. Zhang acknowledges they might eventually consider vertical integration, but only if it provides a competitive advantage that the network model cannot [1].

## RSUs Over Options

Lob uses a non-traditional RSU (restricted stock unit) structure instead of standard options. Zhang's reasoning: options require employees to spend their own money to purchase shares without knowing the outcome. "That puts you in this weird scenario where you want to buy your shares, but you're not sure if you want to" [1]. Lob's RSUs vest on a one-year cliff with monthly vesting thereafter, and taxes are owed only at a liquidity event -- when the employee already has money from the RSUs [1].

The retention mechanism: at the 2.5-year mark and every year after, employees receive additional retention grants. "The longer you stay at the company, the more equity you should be able to acquire. There shouldn't be a point where you ever stop earning equity" [1].

## The Founder's Evolving Role

Zhang describes the founder's role as perpetually shifting: from product builder, to sales leader, to marketing driver. "Your role is going to continue to change. That's something you have to get comfortable with as a founder" [1]. The shift is a positive signal -- it means the company has gotten good enough in one area to hire someone else. But the implication is clear: "If you as the founder of the company can't sell the product, nobody else is going to be able to either" [1].

## References

1. [On starting and scaling direct mail automation startup Lob](https://ycombinator.com/library/6a-on-starting-and-scaling-direct-mail-automation-startup-lob) -- Harry Zhang, Kevin Hale (2019)

title: Lever: Building Recruiting Software from the Inside Out
body:
# Lever: Building Recruiting Software from the Inside Out

Lever (YC S12) builds recruiting software for teams to source, interview, and hire talent. Co-founded by Sarah Nahm, Nate Smith, and others, the company raised its Series A in October 2014 after spending two years (2012-2014) finding [[Product-Market Fit]] [1]. Nahm became CEO on the eve of the Series A, transitioning from individual contributor designer to company leader through a collective team decision [1].

## The Founding Path: Non-Linear and People-First

Nahm grew up in Birmingham, Alabama, "as far from Silicon Valley" as possible [1]. She studied design at Stanford, where her freshman class was the first to start with Facebook. She planned to go into design consulting at IDEO. Instead, she ended up at Google through a chance encounter at Stanford's Career Development Center, where someone asked if she wanted to sign up for Google interviews [1].

At Google, her first role was speech writer for Marissa Mayer -- "a job that nobody had had before" [1]. She later worked on the Chrome team under Sundar Pichai. Nahm describes her Google experience as a "crash course in a lot of what it is to be a founder, because I had to kind of create something out of nothing and figure out what I was doing and be really bad at it for a while" [1].

Leaving Google was easy. Starting Lever was harder. Nahm did not leave to start a company; she left to grow and experience more things [1]. The co-founders "were almost trying out working with each other for a while before we actually officially went for it." Nahm credits co-founder fit for much of Lever's success: "Making sure that you're finding the right people is like probably the hardest to predict but most important part of that super, super early stage" [1].

## The Thesis: Knowledge Work Eating the World

Lever's founding thesis, crystallized around 2012, was that "just like software was eating the world, knowledge work was eating the world" [1]. As technology and digital transformation hit every industry -- even manufacturing -- organizations needed people who could "program a robotic arm or rethink manufacturing process," not undifferentiated assembly line workers [1].

This transformed how companies needed to think about hiring. Career tenure was shrinking (three to five years, 18 months in Silicon Valley). The companies winning were the ones with 10x talent driving leapfrog innovation [1]. Recruiting, as a function, had not kept up with these shifts.

## Building and Throwing Away

Lever spent two and a half years on the "blurry period" of product development [1]. They built an entire first product, gathered customer feedback, and realized they had built "something incremental... just a better version of what was there before" [1]. They threw it out.

Nahm frames the discipline that saved them: "What we did know was how we were going to hold ourselves accountable, and that was through listening to our customers" [1]. The founding team did not know the exact product they would build, but they knew the standard they would hold themselves to -- customer honesty over internal conviction.

## Proactive Recruiting: Lever's Own Playbook

As a recruiting software company, Lever applied its own principles to its hiring. Nahm's co-founder Nate Smith built the engineering organization with 81% of the team proactively sourced (outbound, not inbound applicants) [1].

### The Nurture Pipeline

Lever's most distinctive hiring strategy was relationship nurturing. Many hires were people the team had met two to three years before their eventual start date [1]. Nahm frames this not as building a "database of talent" but as "investing in relationships" that pay returns when the candidate is ready for their next move. These nurtured candidates were hired 35% faster than people meeting Lever for the first time [1].

### Motivation Fit

Lever's first stage of the hiring process is not a phone screen. It is called "motivation fit" -- explicitly named as such in their process [1]. Questions include:
- Where are you taking your career?
- What past career decisions have been most meaningful to you?
- What are you doing at your current job that you'd like to do more of?
- What are you not getting to do that you hope to do next?

Nahm listens for whether candidates thrive with ambiguity, whether they have specific self-awareness about what "impact" means for them personally, and whether they are at the right stage for early-stage startup life [1].

### Behavioral Interviewing and Career Trajectory

Nahm advocates behavioral interviewing for early-stage startups because founders are "hiring for a lot of roles you have never actually had personal experience doing" [1]. Lever uses a "career trajectory" interview that walks through a candidate's career from college onward, step by step, looking for patterns of success and failure [1]. This is a modified version of "top grading," a well-documented methodology [1].

### Impact Descriptions, Not Job Descriptions

After raising its Series A, Lever took a company off-site (the entire company fit in a van) to design its hiring process [1]. They created an internal exercise: define what someone in each role needs to achieve in 1, 3, 6, and 12 months -- not tasks, but outcomes and impact [1].

Candidates who saw these documents "would just light up" [1]. It took Lever six months to realize they should replace traditional job descriptions with these impact descriptions externally. The result: more top-percentile candidates applied, and the descriptions attracted more diverse applicants because they did not broadcast assumptions about what kind of person should apply [1]. "By having this lengthy list of skills and requirements, you're basically broadcasting to the world assumptions about what kind of people should apply" [1].

## Diversity and Inclusion: Culture Before Demographics

Nahm was the only woman at Lever for two to two-and-a-half years [1]. She argues that the biggest roadblock to inclusive hiring is "thinking that it's about demographics and thinking that they have to reflect the demographics of diversity before they're allowed to make their culture more diverse or inclusive" [1].

Her advice: start by making the culture inclusive, then diversity in hiring follows. "What's the point of hiring all this quote diverse talent if when they get there they're not ready to succeed?" [1].

### Concrete Inclusion Practices

**Dish duty and cultural surface area.** Early at Lever, "a very obvious group of people did the dishes and a very obvious group of people did not." After someone shared research about women disproportionately doing office chores, the team built a Slackbot that assigned rotational dish duty. It became part of the culture and a talking point on candidate tours [1].

**Holiday schedule as culture.** Lever shipped a holiday calendar that did not include Martin Luther King Day (it had included Presidents' Day in the same month). Employees pushed back. The team realized "our holiday schedule was surface area to our culture" and revised it [1].

**Technical/non-technical divide.** Lever deliberately rejected the Silicon Valley stereotype of "technical" versus "non-technical" people. They ran an internal training called "I'm Technical and So Are You." Nahm, who has a BS in Mechanical Engineering from Stanford, had herself internalized being the "non-technical founder" [1]. Engineering teams needed to understand customers; non-engineering teams needed to design scalable systems. The divide was holding both sides back [1].

**Employee resource groups with policy input.** Sixty-five percent of Lever employees belonged to an employee resource group. Lever's parents ERG was asked to help revise the parental leave policy. "Using these groups to inform real decisions is kind of the next level" [1].

**Colors at onboarding.** During "Ramp Camp" (Lever's week-long onboarding), all new hires take a personality assessment that outputs a color. This creates shared language for discussing working-style differences without resorting to stereotypes. At the Ramp Camp graduation happy hour, new hires wear leis matching their colors [1].

Holly Liu, interviewing Nahm, observes that this creates both common language and permission to discuss differences. Nahm notes that most of these practices emerged bottom-up from the team, not top-down from leadership [1].

## Advice for YC Batch Companies

Nahm's summary advice: "Your culture is your people. It's who you hire, it's who you fire, it's also who you're recognizing, who you're promoting, who you're rewarding and how you do all those things." She argues that building a great team "is the shortest path to building a strong culture" and "the shortest path to building your results." Her framing: "A founder's number one job is recruiting" [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Recruiting Software Startup Lever](https://ycombinator.com/library/6I-on-starting-and-scaling-recruiting-software-startup-lever) -- Sarah Nahm, Holly Liu (2019)

title: Rippling: Building the Same Company Twice
body:
# Rippling: Building the Same Company Twice

Rippling is an all-in-one HR, finance, and IT software company valued at $13.5 billion [1]. Co-founded by Parker Conrad and Pano, the company is a direct continuation of Conrad's earlier company Zenefits, rebuilt from scratch with deeper conviction and a different operational philosophy. Conrad's journey spans three companies over roughly 15 years and includes one of the more dramatic founder narratives in recent YC history.

## SigFig: Seven Years of Grinding Failure

Conrad's entrepreneurial career began at Amgen in Los Angeles, where career progression was slow. His college roommate from Harvard (who worked at Amazon) proposed starting a company together. They made "exactly the wrong" move: deciding to start a company first, then trying to figure out what to build [1].

Their first idea was a Wikipedia-style platform for stock research, launched around the time Wikipedia took off. "It wasn't super clear why people would do that, but they were doing it for Wikipedia, so why not do it here?" [1]. It never worked. Conrad identifies a broader lesson: "A lot of consumer businesses end up being kind of random. Whether things work or not feels like very much outside the control of the company. It's one of the reasons I hate consumer businesses" [1]. Many entrepreneurs who start in consumer end up pivoting their careers to B2B for this reason [1].

They raised a $2.5 million seed round at a $7.5 million valuation off a pitch deck. "I was like, 'Oh my God, we are rich!'... It was very exciting and heady, and it was all downhill from there" [1].

The company (later renamed SigFig) went through seven years of pivots. Conrad describes the cycle: "We would always have five ideas that we thought would work... four of them would just completely fail, and one of them would mostly fail, but maybe there was some glimmer of hope" [1]. They pitched approximately 70 investors during the 2009 downturn with no success.

Conrad emerged from this experience "deeply cynical about fundraising." He observed that investor enthusiasm shifts every six to nine months -- "Facebook apps," "social local mobile," Web3, AI -- while building a company takes years. "There's no way the attention span that investors have on this stuff is always so much shorter than the time that's required to build a business" [1].

## Fundraising Philosophy: Don't Optimize for It

Conrad's hard-earned fundraising principle: "You should just basically wait until you're getting investors that are emailing you term sheets that are signed with a blank for the valuation, and they say, 'Just fill in the valuation and send it back to us.' And when they do that, then it's time to fundraise. And until then, you should probably just focus on making your business better" [1].

He argues that most advice-seekers should "not spend any time trying to optimize for a fundraising outcome" [1]. This directly shaped his decision to do YC with Zenefits: if he could get a few hundred customers making insurance commissions, he would never need to talk to VCs again [1].

## Zenefits: Meteoric Rise

The core insight behind Zenefits was that many separate administrative tasks for running a business -- insurance, benefits, payroll, offer letters -- all needed to work together but did not. Doing them all in one system eliminated enormous manual work: "This kind of magic button to hire an employee, and then they sort of got set up everywhere" [1].

Conrad describes [[Product-Market Fit]] by contrast with his SigFig experience. At SigFig, one idea out of five would mostly fail. At Zenefits, "they all worked unbelievably well. Just unbelievably well. Wildly exceeded every expectation." Even things they thought probably would not work "would work unbelievably well as well." The market was "pulling the product into existence because it was so clearly what everyone wanted" [1].

Conrad's advice on PMF is stark: "If it's not working, give up immediately. It very rarely happens that people with something that's not working end up pivoting the business in a way that eventually it starts working. There's always that Airbnb story, but most of the time that's not what happens" [1].

Growth numbers: $0 to $200K revenue exiting YC (about six weeks). $0 to $1M in the first year. $1M to $20M in year two. $20M to $65M in year three (plan had been $100M) [1].

### PG and Launch Urgency

Conrad joined YC as a solo founder with a prototype and little built. PG told him he needed to launch the first week of February. Conrad protested that it was three weeks away. PG's response: "What I'm telling you is you're screwed then. I don't see how -- you might as well give up now and go home" [1]. Coming off seven years of failure, this was devastating.

They launched in the second week of February, a few weeks ahead of a competitor. The TechCrunch narrative cemented: Zenefits was the innovative company; the competitor was "trying to copy what Zenefits is doing" [1].

Conrad credits YC's value primarily in creating urgency: "It is the right level of urgency and pressure into the system to get you going early on" [1]. Starting a company "feels a lot like being unemployed... If you don't get anything done, nobody really yells at you" [1].

## The Fall: Zenefits to Zero

At $65M ARR, Conrad was fired within a week of the fiscal year-end. "Everything that was working so well for us top of funnel stopped working" coincided with investors who had invested at high multiples "underwriting an enormous amount of future growth that suddenly was not materializing" [1]. Compliance issues surfaced in this context.

David Sacks became CEO but, according to Conrad, "hired a comms person... and they just focused on attacking me over and over again for like six months" [1]. Conrad was under legal restrictions and could not respond publicly. The press narrative became one-sided [1].

[[Garry Tan]] describes the context from YC's perspective: "I saw that you had created Zenefits -- Zenefits would not have existed or created any of that enterprise value without you. And to see what they did to you, it was deeply unfair" [1].

## Rippling: The Rebuild

Conrad had "either the advantage of or the misfortune to build the same company twice" [1]. He and co-founder Pano (who had worked with him at Zenefits) shared a conviction: "There's a hundred billion dollar opportunity just sitting right there on the floor, and nobody can see it except for us. Everyone's just walking by oblivious to it" [1].

Key differences in the rebuild:

**No operations, no customer support initially.** Conrad believed the "critical flaw at Zenefits" was scaling too much on manual operations and support rather than building true end-to-end software. Rippling spent roughly two years building with mostly an engineering team before launching [1].

**Expanded scope.** Rippling extended the Zenefits insight beyond HR: "For the same reason you want this one place to manage payroll and benefits... you actually also want to be able to ship someone their computer or get them set up with access to email and Salesforce and GitHub" [1]. Employee data became the center of a broader business software thesis, not just an HR product [1].

**Deep conviction over exploration.** "I felt like I had a lot of insights about the market and what was needed and very little self-doubt this time around versus the first time" [1].

## The Compound Startup Thesis

Conrad coined the term "compound software business" to describe Rippling's approach. The thesis: many deeper organizational problems cannot be solved by narrow point-solution software. A suite of seamlessly interoperable applications can solve problems that individual tools cannot [1].

He traces this to older software companies -- SAP, Oracle, Microsoft, and to some extent Salesforce -- which were compound businesses. The SaaS era created a moment where narrow point solutions could each become low-single-digit-billion-dollar companies. But that era created an overcrowded, fragmented landscape where "the types of problems that you can solve for customers tend to be much more limited" [1].

Rippling invests heavily in platform capabilities that cut across applications: analytics, approvals, permissions, and workflow automations. "Most of our competitors just can't go as deep on that stuff" [1].

Conrad is candid about the thesis's limitations: "It's actually a deeply cynical view of software markets because I think it's possible that compound software businesses are the wave of the future. And also, there will be three of them" [1].

## AI in Enterprise Software

Conrad argues that AI's biggest impact on enterprise software is not generative -- it is the ability to read and ingest information at scale. "The fact that these systems can read and their context windows are so large is actually much more powerful for B2B software than the fact that they can write" [1].

Rippling launched an AI performance management feature that analyzes an employee's first 90 days -- pull requests, sales calls, support tickets -- to predict whether they are on track for success or need intervention [1].

On CRM and AI: Conrad argues AI should not replace the rep's forecast. Instead, AI should provide a second opinion (based on listening to all sales calls) and flag disagreements between the rep's assessment and the AI's. "If you only have time to look at five deals or five new hires, these are the ones that you should look at" [1].

On AI SDRs: "They don't work... But the second that it flips and they can, it won't have the impact that people are hoping. What'll happen is it'll just destroy outbound as a channel" [1].

## Founder Mode

Conrad agrees with the concept that founders need to "go all the way to ground" when something is broken -- "review the support tickets, listen to the sales calls, do the job on the factory floor" [1]. But he warns against misinterpretation: "You need really good executives, and you don't want to do the founder mode thing unless something's broken... You can't do it everywhere" [1].

AI extends this capability. An LLM can read across the entire organization, effectively expanding the CEO's context window. "The powerful thing about LLMs inside of businesses is that it can flatten organizations and give executives and managers just a larger sort of window into what's going on" [1].

## References

1. [How To Build The Future: Parker Conrad](https://ycombinator.com/library/M2-how-to-build-the-future-parker-conrad) -- Parker Conrad, [[Garry Tan]] (2025)

title: Perplexity: Building the Next Search Engine
body:
# Perplexity: Building the Next Search Engine

Perplexity is an AI-powered search engine that grew to a $9 billion valuation in under three years. Co-founded and led by Aravind Srinivas, the company started as a Twitter search tool, pivoted to general web search powered by LLMs, and now competes directly with Google. The story illustrates how ignorance of competitive moats can be an asset, why the "dumb approach" sometimes beats the smart one, and how the innovator's dilemma protects challengers in ad-dependent markets.

## Ilya's Two Circles

Srinivas came to the US from India for a PhD at Berkeley, studying AI and deep learning. His intellectual trajectory was reshaped by an internship at OpenAI, where Ilya Sutskever "listened for five minutes and said, 'All this research is useless'" [1].

Sutskever drew two circles. The large circle was unsupervised learning. Inside it, a smaller circle was reinforcement learning. He pointed to the inner circle and said, "This is AGI. Every other research doesn't matter" [1]. This was during GPT-1 development, before the name "GPT-1" existed.

Srinivas returned to Berkeley and shifted his focus from reinforcement learning (the trend, driven by AlphaGo and DeepMind) to generative models and unsupervised learning. During a later internship at Google, he read Steven Levy's "In the Plex" and realized that Google's founders were once grad students like him. The idea took shape: build a company at the intersection of AI research and product building [1].

He discussed this with Sutskever, and they identified two problems where AI research and product development could form a flywheel: search and autonomous driving. In both, product usage generates data that improves the underlying AI, which makes the product better, which attracts more users. Critically, the company must be "on the AI completeness path" -- better AI should continuously make the product better, so the company grows alongside AI progress rather than being run over by it [1].

## The Twitter Search Prototype

Srinivas's first pitch to seed investor Elad Gil was about disrupting Google "from pixels, from a glass" -- a multimodal, camera-based approach. Gil advised against it [1].

The team pivoted to building search over specific databases and verticals. They could not get enterprise data (Pitchbook, Crunchbase refused to share), so they turned to Twitter's publicly available academic data. In one month, three people built a conversational search tool for Twitter: you could chat, query, and plot data. Users loved it, particularly the social search dimension -- discovering who follows whom, who liked what, who unfollowed whom [1].

They attempted to replicate this for other databases (GitHub, LinkedIn) but kept hitting data access walls. Then Srinivas encountered a key insight, which he attributes to the "Polymath tweet": when you try to solve a harder version of a problem, you sometimes end up with a simpler, more general, more scalable solution [1].

The choice was between two architectures [1]:

1. **Structured approach**: Index each domain's data into specific formats (tables), write templates, have the LLM generate SQL queries. Reliable but domain-specific and labor-intensive.

2. **Unstructured approach**: Keep data unstructured, let the LLM do the heavy lifting at inference time. Less reliable with current models but more general and future-proof.

The team chose the second path, betting that models would get smarter. This also meant Perplexity would be architecturally different from Google's heavily indexed system, providing a structural advantage if the bet on improving models was correct [1].

## The "Dumb Approach" That Worked

OpenAI's WebGPT (built by John Schulman's team) had already demonstrated web-search-plus-LLM, but it was slow and agentic -- an RL agent that decided whether to click links, scroll, and browse. Srinivas built a simpler, faster version [1]:

- Always take the top-K links from a search API
- Only use the cached summary snippets (no scrolling, no clicking)
- Feed all links into the LLM prompt
- Ask the model to write a summary with academic-style source citations

Srinivas describes this as "the dumb approach," but it worked because models had improved enough for reliable instruction-following. "One year ago, John and his team tried, like the models were just so much worse that if you tried the dumb approach it just wouldn't work" [1]. Better models fixed the latency problem, which was the core product UX issue. Even so, the first version took seven seconds because they did not implement streaming, and had to hardcode prompts like "Only write five sentences" to control verbosity [1].

## Finding Traction: People Search for Themselves

The first moment of virality came from an error. An academic searched for herself, and Perplexity wrote her biography in the past tense -- because a person with the same name had died. The model's reasoning was "pretty clever" but lacked the higher-order understanding that these were different people. The mistake went viral and triggered a wave of people searching for themselves, screenshotting AI-generated summaries of their internet presence, and sharing them [1].

Srinivas recognized a universal pattern, confirmed by a conversation with Instagram's Mike Krieger: people love to search for themselves. On Instagram, even though users can tap their own profile icon, they frequently search for their username in the search bar [1].

The product breakthrough came when they added follow-up questions, which "doubled the engagement time on the site and also increased the number of questions every day." The number of queries per day was growing exponentially. This convinced Srinivas not to pivot to enterprise [1].

## The Bing Chat Scare

On the day Srinivas agreed to a term sheet with NEA, The Verge leaked screenshots of Bing Chat. One competing investor extended the due diligence period from 30 to 45 days. Another texted asking for a call the next morning [1].

Srinivas told his co-founder they should consider selling the company. But the NEA investor called and said: "Look, I'm not going to ask you to pivot. I'm not going to ask you to do anything different. You guys keep going." The following week, Google announced Bard. Srinivas's assessment: Microsoft "was never really good at consumer products for a long, long time. You can't suddenly change that." They "messed up the opportunity." Google would have its own internal challenges [1].

## The Innovator's Dilemma as Competitive Moat

Srinivas articulates why Google cannot easily build Perplexity's exact product on its homepage: if people stop clicking on links, the ad economy declines. Google's search page is already cluttered with answer boxes, knowledge panels, ads, social cards, and perspectives. The difference between using Google and Perplexity on informational queries is "fast food versus healthy meal" [1].

Google's search revenue is close to $200 billion per year, with the highest margins in the company. YouTube is not high-margin (creator payouts, no ads on subscribers). Cloud only recently became profitable. The stock price depends on search revenue: "Wall Street just automatically panics if search revenue goes down" [1].

Srinivas argues that whoever wins the next generation of search will need a new business model. The current ad model is structurally incompatible with directly answering questions. He envisions a product that researches products, books hotels, completes purchases -- end-to-end from question to action. The monetization comes from transaction facilitation, not from ad clicks on intermediate links [1].

## Larry Page's "User Is Never Wrong" Principle

Srinivas deliberately modeled Perplexity's culture on early Google principles, particularly Larry Page's maxim that "the user is never wrong" [1]. When a feature fails for a user, the product should clarify ambiguity rather than blame the user:

"It should have come and clarified to me, 'Hey, I'm not sure. Either it's this or this, which one did you actually want?' And then I should have clarified and then it should have gone and done, instead of saying, 'I don't know'" [1].

The opposite approach -- blaming users and telling them to become better prompt engineers -- is how enterprise software works. Magical consumer products work the other way. Srinivas draws the analogy to Google's spell-check and auto-suggest: Larry Page was not good at spelling, so Google handled typos. The product adapted to the user, not the other way around [1].

## Operating the Company

Perplexity's primary metric is number of queries per day, the same metric Google used in its early days. Srinivas reviews it weekly at All Hands meetings, analyzing growth rates and investigating any declines. He rejected the idea of a always-visible TV dashboard as distracting [1].

The organizational culture is flat: "There's no hierarchy. If there is some bug to be fixed, if I know some particular person's working on it, I can go and talk to the person directly." Srinivas personally files roughly 50 bugs per day. The team understands this is about product quality, not performance review [1].

For user feedback, Srinivas uses X (Twitter) as the primary channel because "people are just super brutally honest there." Email is too polite. In-person feedback is worst: "You go show someone something and they're just going to tell you good things, even if they hate it" [1].

## Fighting Entropy at Scale

Srinivas acknowledges the company is already slowing compared to its earliest days. The causes are not just headcount but the cost of things breaking in production, which erodes trust and makes people cautious. Not every new engineer has full context of the codebase. There is tension between moving fast and maintaining quality for mass-market usage [1].

His strategy: maintain personal awareness of who is working on what, file bugs directly, and rely on co-founders who share the obsessive detail-orientation. "We're trying to fight the entropy here. I think that's the only thing you can try. And it's an uphill battle" [1].

## The Next Google: An Orchestration Problem

Srinivas's vision for what wins in 10 years is not just better answers but a universal orchestrator: small models for quick facts (weather, scores, ages), knowledge graphs, widgets, LLM streaming for complex answers, multi-step reasoning for research queries, clarifying questions for ambiguous intent, and transactional capabilities for shopping and booking. The user should not need to specify when to use what -- the system decides. "Whoever builds that and can operate that at a scale of billion users and also knows how to monetize some of those queries really well is going to be the next Google" [1].

He argues Perplexity is better positioned for this than OpenAI or Anthropic: "We have it in our DNA to care about the user and the product. We're not just talking about reasoning and models" [1].

## References

1. [How To Build The Future: Aravind Srinivas](https://ycombinator.com/library/MC-how-to-build-the-future-aravind-srinivas) -- Aravind Srinivas, [[David Lieb]] (2025)

title: Snapdocs: Navigating Market Cycles
body:
# Snapdocs: Navigating Market Cycles

[Snapdocs](https://www.snapdocs.com/) is the leading digital closing platform for the mortgage industry. As of the interview, the company touches 25% of all US real estate transactions and is valued at $1.5B. Founder and CEO [[Aaron King]] (YC W14) built the company with minimal outside funding through volatile market conditions [1].

## Capital Efficiency as a Discipline

Snapdocs started as a side project years before [[Aaron King]] applied to YC. By the time he applied, the company had a live product, customers, and revenue. Even after YC, the company did not raise much immediately [1].

Three years after YC, Snapdocs raised its Series A. At that point, the company had spent about $1M of its seed round and was at a $5M revenue run rate. The lead investor later told King how capital efficient Snapdocs had been compared to peers [1].

King frames capital discipline not as a constraint but as a virtue: "I've learned that you should take the same approach even when you do have the capital to be less disciplined" [1]. Capital constraints kept the team focused on going deep with paying customers, avoiding the common trap of building products no one wanted [1].

The approach also forced the team to think about the smallest version of every product in order to ship quickly, maintaining a short feedback loop with users [1]. King acknowledges the cost: "We built many things that weren't engineered for scale or flexibility. However, now we can afford to reengineer those unscalable solutions because we built something people want" [1].

## Product Development

Before raising the Series A, Snapdocs was heavy on customer involvement. The team spent time in customers' offices watching them use the product and understanding their work [1]. They also kept prospects in the loop as they built new features:

> "Some of the best feedback came from people who had chosen to not yet work with us. Responding to that feedback with a killer feature was a great way to ultimately get them on board." [1]

The in-person interactions built trust and rapport. Customers would call the team the moment they spotted a problem or saw a competitor doing something compelling. Customer churn has always been low as a result [1].

Despite small size, the team maintained a disciplined product release process while shipping code daily, sometimes multiple times per day [1].

### The Value of Low Investor Pressure

Not having heavy investor pressure early on allowed Snapdocs to experiment in an underappreciated part of the market. The Serviceable Available Market (SAM) of the initial product was roughly only $20M, but King believed it would allow expansion into more critical parts of the mortgage ecosystem [1]. He notes: "It was the type of opportunity that would be hard to discover through market analysis or spreadsheet exercises. You had to get deep into the problem set to see the opportunity" [1].

## Hiring Under Capital Constraints

Before the company could afford full-time employees, King worked with contractors and leaned on friends. Friends would come over to whiteboard or work through code problems [1].

When King could afford to hire, he treated early employees like founding team members — generous with equity and transparent about the business's potential and challenges. "Getting a few really good people into the company early on was foundational to the company's success" [1].

The first full-time hire was an engineer from a previous role (and one of the friends who helped in the early days). The second and third hires came from Hacker News job postings. All three turned out to be excellent [1].

### The Recruiting Regret

King's biggest early hiring mistake was being too timid to approach people he respected:

> "I should have tried to convince them to quit their successful jobs and join our small (yet risky at the time) startup. I'm fearless on this approach now, but back then I was intimidated to try to convince a friend to join a company that might fail. In hindsight I did them a disservice by not trying to recruit them." [1]

His lesson: smart people can evaluate the opportunity for themselves. Be honest about inherent challenges, but give them the opportunity to take a risk on you [1].

The team only hired when there was a clear and painful need. This made running the company harder but kept everyone working on the most important things [1].

## Navigating Market Cycles

A core lesson from Snapdocs is how to selectively respond to market changes. King describes the approach as "selectively ignoring some market changes, while reacting quickly to others" [1]. The filter is how a change aligns with resources, vision, and the north star metric of market share growth.

### Fluctuations in Mortgage Volume

The biggest dynamic change Snapdocs faces is fluctuations in monthly/annual mortgage volume, driven by economic factors. When well-resourced with momentum, the team ignores market downturns and stays focused on market share growth. Other times they scale up or back based on the fluctuation's size [1].

### Resisting Trendy Technology

Industry appetite for new technologies changes quickly. There were times when the market demanded a technology but King believed underlying industry factors would prevent it from scaling. Building it would pull resources from long-term priorities [1]:

> "Sometimes to the protests of our sales team, we ignored it or invested minimally in these trendy areas. By doing so, we were able to stay focused on the things that were truly going to transform the industry." [1]

### The Cost of Change Scales with Company Size

King notes that navigating change was relatively easy in the first few years when the team could all fit in one room, product cycles were short, and the cost of making a change was low. As the company grew, changes to speed or direction became more expensive: investments take longer to play out, headcount changes take longer to scale, and there are more layers to communicate through [1].

## The COVID Pivot (March 2020)

COVID accelerated demand for Snapdocs' product but shifted what customers wanted. The team had to expand quickly and pivot the roadmap immediately [1].

Decision-making worked in concentric circles [1]:
1. **Inner circle (3-4 people)**: Where the hardest and messiest conversations took place. Quickly defined problems, opportunities, and direction.
2. **Senior leadership team**: Further discussion and arming leaders with everything needed to communicate changes.
3. **Company-wide meeting**: Shared new direction and answered questions.

This entire process took about two weeks. For ongoing rapid changes, the company used temporary pods of 4-5 team members focused on specific challenges that would spin up for a few weeks and dissolve. Company-wide all-hands went from monthly to weekly [1].

### Intellectual Honesty About Growth

The team worked hard to be intellectually honest about COVID-driven growth:

> "This industry works in cycles. We're in an up cycle now and that's great. There's going to be a down cycle. We don't know when or how strong it's going to be. But we should not overly congratulate ourselves for the current situation, just as we shouldn't be too hard on ourselves when we're fighting through an inevitable downturn in the future." [1]

## Shifting Gears: From Efficient to Effective and Back

When Snapdocs raised its Series D in 2021, transactions had grown to millions of closings per year with thousands of lenders and title companies using the platform monthly. The company shifted from frugal to aggressive, raising larger rounds and hiring seasoned executives [1].

King describes the key question: "Is what I did yesterday the thing that's going to get me to where I need to be tomorrow?" The answer required changing parts of the culture and capital investment strategy [1].

But when mortgage demand cooled later that year, the company returned to its roots: ditched the motto of being effective over efficient, returned to ruthless prioritization, and shifted away from investing in future scale that would not be needed for years [1].

King's summary: "Market fluctuations are normal and unavoidable. Startups should scale up at times and scale back at others. It's hard and painful... But these ups and downs are natural and a necessary part of building an enduring company" [1].

## References

1. [Snapdocs' Aaron King on Navigating Market Cycles](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Gu-snapdocs-aaron-king-on-navigating-market-cycles) — [[Aaron King]], Lindsay Amos (2022)

title: Gobble: Five Years in the Trough of Sorrow
body:
# Gobble: Five Years in the Trough of Sorrow

Gobble (YC W14) is a dinner kit delivery service that helps anyone cook dinner in 10 minutes. Founded by [[Ooshma Garg]], the company spent five and a half years iterating through four different product versions before finding [[Product-Market Fit]] and raising a Series A from Trinity Ventures and Andreessen Horowitz. [[Paul Graham]] [tweeted](https://twitter.com/paulg/status/659749829040410624) about Garg: "Ooshma Garg does not give up" [1].

## Origin: Solving Her Own Problem

Garg was running a previous startup at Stanford, working 16-18 hours a day and eating nothing but takeout and Starbucks. She grew up with home-cooked North Indian food; her father would sing while cooking traditional meals. As an adult, recreating those meals took two hours after a full workday [1].

She posted an ad on Craigslist: would any chef make a home-cooked meal for $8 a plate? She received 70 responses in 24 hours. "There are so many people in the Bay Area that are proud of their food and want to earn a living from it" [1]. She chose favorite chefs, created a calendar of what they would cook each day, emailed friends asking if they wanted to order, and personally drove deliveries. The entire business was prototyped with email, PayPal, and her car [1].

## Searching for a Technical Co-Founder

Garg is a mechanical engineer with no software experience. She searched for a technical co-founder through coffee dates and friend introductions but "no one was as passionate about the idea as I was, they weren't willing to quit their job, and we just didn't connect in a deep way" [1].

She decided to proceed without one: "Screw it. I'm just gonna do it anyway" [1]. The investors agreed. She had the passion, a proof of concept, and previous startup experience. The co-founder fear did not need to be a blocker. Gobble raised a [seed round](http://techcrunch.com/2011/05/11/marketplace-for-home-cooked-food-gobble-raises-1-2m-from-founder-collective-reid-hoffman-and-other-angels/) from Founder Collective, Reid Hoffman, and other angels [1].

## The Multi-Year Trough

### Snake 1: First Engineer Quits Before Launch

Weeks before launch, Garg's first engineer quit. "He walked in and said, 'This is too much pressure. I can't handle it anymore. I quit'" [1]. She found Ruby on Rails consultants through investor introductions, begged one to learn the codebase, and eventually hired replacement engineers -- but the launch was delayed [1].

### The Marketplace Phase

Gobble launched as a chef marketplace and was covered by TechCrunch. But after the honeymoon period, Garg had to fight for every customer: "I put coupons in Palo Alto coffee shops and went to mom's events and pitched our company, and every time somebody signed up, I personally emailed them, followed up, and gave them a promo code to order again" [1].

### Snake 2: Failed Series A (First Attempt)

An investor offered to lead a Series A. Garg structured her entire fundraising process around this one firm, meeting only five investors. After the partner meeting, the deal fell apart. Lessons learned: "I didn't meet many different investors. I met five, not forty. I thought that a deal was done before it was done. Next time, I would know that the deal isn't done until the money is in the bank" [1].

### The On-Demand Experiment

Customer feedback said they wanted to order on demand rather than planning in the morning. In 2012, before most on-demand companies existed, Gobble launched "Gobble Instant." It failed: the target market was suburban families, delivery took 30+ minutes, food wilted in transit, and the economics were negative [1].

### Snake 3: Investor Pressure to Sell

Some investors were losing confidence. One suggested selling the company to a clothing startup for an acqui-hire. Garg refused because "our mission was still alive and well. Busy people still were not eating home-cooked food" [1].

### The Couch Revelation

Garg laid on the couch in her office, surrounded herself with blank paper, and "stopped listening to our investors and other people, and started listening to myself" [1]. She reflected on accumulated insights: dinner is daily, tastes are stable, timing is predictable. Dinner should be a subscription, personalized like Netflix or Pandora but for food [1].

### The Subscription Model

The personalized dinner subscription was Gobble's third product version. Revenue was five times higher than any previous version. But when Garg tried to raise Series A again, the food startup space had become competitive. Investors had already placed bets or wanted to wait. The second Series A attempt failed [1].

### Near Death

With no money left and existing investors unwilling to provide more (too old a company, no space in the fund, Thanksgiving and Christmas tanking numbers), Garg's bank account screenshot showed she had made her last full payroll. "Nobody knew this but me" [1].

## YC as Lifeline

A friend suggested Y Combinator. Garg's initial reaction: "What?! We've already raised seed money, and we've already launched a product. Why would I join a startup incubator?" [1]. But the alternative was death. "When you look at it that way, you put your pride aside and you keep the company alive" [1].

YC helped Gobble get back to basics. The team visited customers' homes, watching them cook and eat. The key observation: when people microwaved the prepared meals, "they would cringe" [1]. Microwaving was not a dignified way to feed a family.

## The Dinner Kit: Mise en Place for Families

Executive Chef Thomas Ricci taught Garg about *mise en place* -- the restaurant concept where prep cooks handle chopping, saucing, and marinating so executive chefs can finish and serve. Gobble applied this to home cooking: the company does the prep work, and the customer finishes the meal in one pan in 10 minutes [1].

The team tested the concept by giving TaskRabbit workers dinner kits to deliver to friends in Palo Alto. "Our social media blew up, the word of mouth blew up, the pride and the smiles blew up, and the growth blew up. We had hit the promised land" [1].

At the time of this talk, Gobble had zero marketing hires. All growth was organic. "That's what product market fit looks like" [1].

## Series A: The Same Firms That Said No

Gobble raised its Series A from Trinity Ventures and Andreessen Horowitz -- both of which had rejected Garg in her two previous fundraising attempts. "The lesson there is that it's important to accept rejection with grace. Understand that startups are a marathon and a long game, and the same people you may not work with right now, you may work with in the future" [1].

## Negative Press as Motivation

During the trough, a journalist wrote: "Gobble was puttering about on the peninsula with its paltry funding and ever-evolving business model." Garg's response to her team: "Let's put it in a box. We are the underdog. No one knows what we're up to, and we are about to come out like a storm" [1].

## Three Takeaways: Be a GEM

Garg's framework for founder resilience:

1. **Grit**: "The thing that comes from your core. It's this place inside of you when no one believes in you that tells you whether you should keep going." Compete with yourself, not others [1].
2. **Experiment**: "A company is not just a product, a company is solving a problem. It's a mission. And if the mission is alive, you should keep working on it." Gobble launched four different versions to find what worked [1].
3. **Mission**: Gobble's mission is to "create love through food." The mission outlasted every product iteration [1].

## References

1. [On starting and scaling meal kit company Gobble](https://ycombinator.com/library/69-on-starting-and-scaling-meal-kit-company-gobble) -- Ooshma Garg (2016)

title: Coinbase: From Bitcoin at Five Dollars to a Hundred Billion Dollar Company
body:
# Coinbase: From Bitcoin at Five Dollars to a Hundred Billion Dollar Company

Coinbase is the largest cryptocurrency exchange in the United States, going public at a nearly $100 billion market cap [1]. Founded by [[Brian Armstrong]] in 2012, the company started when Bitcoin was worth approximately $5 and almost no one believed in it [1]. [[Garry Tan]] was one of the first investors, turning a $300,000 seed check into over $2.4 billion [2].

## Origin: Solving a Problem the Founder Experienced

Armstrong's path to Coinbase began with a specific frustration. He downloaded the Bitcoin desktop client, which had a command-line interface and required syncing a node on his MacBook. "I knew that just wasn't going to scale to a large number of people" [1]. He drew analogies to email servers (nobody wants to run their own; they want Gmail) and Git (hard to use until GitHub made it easier). He recognized that "someone's going to make the hosted wallet in the cloud thing for crypto" [1].

He initially tried to resist the idea because of its scope: "That's a really big responsibility. You're going to be storing people's money. You're going to have to have regulation" [1]. His first prototype was a Bitcoin wallet for Android phones, but it required running a full Bitcoin node on the phone and took hours to sync even on WiFi. He realized the cloud version was inevitable and "couldn't help myself" [1].

In March 2012, Armstrong sent Tan 0.05 bitcoin (worth about two cents at the time, worth about $2,500 by 2021) using his prototype, then called BitBank [2]. The only thing that changed from day one was the name -- unlike most startups, Coinbase never pivoted [2].

## Why Tan Said Yes When Everyone Else Said No

Tan identifies three reasons he invested when nearly everyone else passed [2]:

1. **Primed for the problem.** Tan had tried to buy Bitcoin on Mt. Gox, the first major exchange, and experienced a terrible UX. The bad design "felt so wrong, and I knew that couldn't be the thing that brought bitcoin all the way" [2].
2. **Primed for the idea.** In 2005, working as an engineer at Peter Thiel's hedge fund Clarium Capital, Tan read "The Dollar Crisis" by Richard Duncan, which described the consequences of Nixon taking the U.S. off the gold standard in 1971. "Twenty-five percent of all dollars in circulation today were printed just in the last year, in 2020" [2]. This had primed Tan for Bitcoin's thesis.
3. **Brian was a builder.** Armstrong had been head of anti-fraud at Airbnb, which Tan describes as "being part of the special forces" -- he was attacked by hackers daily. Armstrong could build and secure the first version himself [2].

## Being Better: The Clean, Well-Lit Place

Tan categorizes Coinbase as a "better" startup in his [[Better, Faster, or Cheaper]] framework [3]. Compared to Mt. Gox (the dominant option in 2012), Coinbase was dramatically better: Mt. Gox required wiring money via Western Union to an overseas bank, and the website had originally been built to trade Magic: The Gathering cards -- that is why it was called "Mt. Gox" (Magic: The Gathering Online eXchange) [3].

Armstrong's first version of Coinbase used Twitter Bootstrap and "wasn't by any means the most beautiful site, but it was functional and it had something about it that wasn't sketchy" [1]. He describes the vision as making "a clean, well-lit place to actually buy this stuff and interact with it" [1].

## Building Credibility From Nothing

Armstrong made a conscious decision to be transparent and non-anonymous, breaking from the cypherpunk culture of early Bitcoin. He put his real name on the about page, linked to his LinkedIn, created email addresses for press and sales (even though he was the only person), and sought credibility signals like YC and established investors [1].

He framed this as thinking from the outcome backward: "If I want to build a thing that helps the whole world use crypto... there's no way I can be anonymous. I need to do this above board from day one" [1]. Tan observes that this backward-from-outcome thinking was rare: most founders "focus on looking like a startup" rather than building as if success were certain [1].

## Innovation at Scale: Coinbase 10

Post-IPO, Armstrong launched Coinbase 10, allocating 10% of time and resources to new products, modeled partially after Y Combinator [1]. Products including Coinbase Wallet (self-custody) and Coinbase Commerce emerged from this program.

Armstrong recognized the danger of founder-as-bottleneck: "There have been instances where people inside Coinbase pitched me, I said no, they've left, and those have become huge companies" [1]. The program lets employees pitch ideas with multiple paths: stay at Coinbase, leave and get funded, or hybrid arrangements [1].

## Three Phases of Crypto

Armstrong describes crypto's evolution [1]:
1. **Investment** -- Bitcoin as digital gold.
2. **Financial system** -- DeFi reinventing lending, insurance, exchanges.
3. **Application platform** -- new identity, social media, governance, DAOs.

## Key Lessons

- **Be contrarian and right.** Find something fringe but ultimately correct. "Every crazy idea will have its day, but only if it has a truly compelling pitch" [2].
- **Be a builder.** "The more you can do, the less you leave to chance, and the more people will want to fund you, join you, or use your product" [2].
- **Send a compelling first email.** Armstrong sent Tan 0.05 bitcoin with his prototype -- "he literally sent me free money. It got my attention, and it showed me Brian was serious about building something genuinely useful" [2].

## References

1. [Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong on Cryptocurrency and the Future of Decentralization](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jk-garry-s-channel-coinbase-ceo-brian-armstrong-on-cryptocurrency-and-the-future-of-decentralization) -- [[Brian Armstrong]], [[Garry Tan]] (2021)
2. [How I Turned $300k into $2 Billion](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jf-garry-s-channel-how-i-turned-300k-into-2-billion) -- [[Garry Tan]] (2021)
3. [Billion Dollar Startups Are Better, Faster, or Cheaper](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jn-garry-s-channel-billion-dollar-startups-are-better-faster-or-cheaper) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: DoorDash: Creating a Market from Scratch
body:
# DoorDash: Creating a Market from Scratch

DoorDash (YC S13) is the largest food delivery platform in the United States, commanding mid-60% market share as of 2025, with over seven million drivers on the platform. Co-founder and CEO [[Tony Xu]] built DoorDash from a static HTML page with eight PDF menus into a public company. The story illustrates how doing every delivery yourself for years produces irreplaceable customer insight, why contrarian geographic strategy can beat better-funded competitors, and how crisis moments clarify mission.

## The Macaroon Store Origin

Before DoorDash, Xu and his co-founders (Andy Fang, Stanley Tang, and Evan Moore) worked on several projects together at Stanford GSB. One project -- a tablet-based survey app that sat at the point of sale to help merchants measure marketing effectiveness -- met one criterion (the founders enjoyed working together) but not the other (they did not love the idea). They cancelled it [1].

The founding insight came from shadowing a macaroon store owner. The founders' approach was not surveys but immersion: "Can we follow you around for a day? We'll go and pack boxes with you, do your accounting with you, make salads with you" [1]. Xu explains the rationale: "Sometimes it's very, very hard for any customer to tell you exactly what is inside their brain when you ask them what problems they have. And so we wanted to feel and maybe trying to figure it out ourselves" [1].

At the end of the visit, the store owner showed them "a booklet of orders she had turned down. All of them were delivery orders." It was a thick booklet, representing significant lost revenue for a one-person shop. The founders asked why she was not pursuing them, and began "unraveling that thread" [1].

As they studied the problem further, the thread expanded: not just this macaroon store, but all bakeries, all restaurants, all retailers. There were roughly one million restaurants in the United States, but only about 20,000 offered delivery -- the ones that incumbents like GrubHub and Seamless worked with, all of which had their own delivery fleets. DoorDash's question: "What would happen if you could enable the other nine hundred eighty thousand to do it?" [1].

## Palo Alto Delivery: The MVP

The founders shipped paloaltodelivery.com in under an hour -- "maybe forty-five, fifty minutes." It was a static HTML page with eight PDF menus from restaurants in Palo Alto that they frequented as students and a Google Voice number. When a customer called, all four founders' phones would ring simultaneously. Whoever picked up first took the order [1].

All four co-founders did every delivery themselves. The total investment: less than ten dollars for the domain name [1].

## YC Summer 2013: Answering Three Questions

The founders entered YC with three open questions [1]:

1. **Would consumers pay for delivery?** Delivery is not a new idea -- "it's been around since horses." In a highly capitalistic market like the US, if something does not exist, there may be a good reason.

2. **Would merchants (restaurants) pay?** The founders knew restaurants had the need, but not whether they would pay for a delivery service.

3. **Would a driver workforce exist?** Would people want to work as delivery drivers?

### Consumer Validation

The earliest customers were families with young children, typically mothers making meal decisions. They told the founders what they wanted, which restaurants they preferred, and what was important. The critical signal: "They kept coming back again without our throwing advertising at them, coupons, or discounts at them" [1].

### The Driver Experiment

Because the founders had done every delivery themselves, they could speak credibly when recruiting drivers through Craigslist ads. But Xu was uncertain whether a driver workforce would form, since transporting people (Uber) presumably paid better than transporting food. He ran a test [1]:

He recruited 20 DoorDash drivers and 20 Uber X drivers, both earning $20/hour. He offered each group $25/hour guaranteed to switch platforms. Out of 40 drivers, exactly one said yes [1].

The insight: these were fundamentally different populations. DoorDash drivers skewed younger and more female. Today, almost 60% of DoorDash's 7 million Dashers are women. They come from hundreds of industries and often deliver by scooter, bicycle, or motorcycle rather than car -- very different from ride-hailing [1].

## Demo Day Failure and Near-Death

Internally, the team felt they had answered their three questions with positive evidence. Demo day, however, "was not successful." DoorDash was not among the favorites [1].

The company almost went to zero. The initial YC grant was small, and Xu could not raise seed financing. People were not convinced a 10-week dataset proved a business. For investors, DoorDash "would be a conviction bet on the team and on the idea that there would be a potential market" at a time when the market did not yet exist [1].

### The Stanford Football Incident

In the fall of 2013, the first home game for Stanford football ended at an awkward time, and everyone in Palo Alto ordered DoorDash simultaneously. Every delivery was at least an hour late, probably closer to 90 minutes. The founders could not shut off the website and had no way to fulfill the demand [1].

That night at 9 or 10 PM, the founders calculated that refunding everyone would cost 40% of their nearly depleted bank account. They made the decision in 10 seconds: refund everyone. Then they stayed up all night baking cookies and delivered them at 5 AM before customers woke up [1].

Xu describes this as the origin story of DoorDash's core company value: "customer obsessed, not competitor focused." He attributes it partly to survival bias, but says the founding team "always had this desire to at least do things the right way, even if we wouldn't have made it" [1].

A few weeks after the incident, they raised their seed round. "Luckily, all you need is one investor to say yes" [1].

## The Suburban Contrarian Bet

As the market grew, competitors focused on expected geographies: San Francisco, New York, dense urban cores where order density seemed necessary for economics. DoorDash took a different path [1].

Because the founders had done every delivery themselves for two years (and continue to do deliveries annually to this day), they kept hearing that need was strongest outside city centers. The logic was simple when viewed from the customer's perspective: "If you and I lived in New York City and we walked outside of the elevator of the building, we probably could walk into hundreds of restaurants. In a place outside of New York City -- Long Island, for example -- you would be walking for miles probably before you would see the first restaurant" [1].

The unit economics confirmed the intuition, line by line [1]:

- **Higher baskets**: Suburban areas had more families, more mouths to feed, larger orders, larger revenue per delivery.
- **Easier parking**: Drivers spent less time finding parking.
- **Simpler deliveries**: Single-family homes versus high-rise buildings in Manhattan.

"The first instinct was just listening to what the customers had told us -- where the need was. But it was very easy to prove out very, very quickly thereafter" [1]. Over the next decade, the majority of growth in the food delivery industry came from places outside of city centers [1].

## Three Years of Fundraising Rejection (2016-2018)

Even as internal metrics moved in the right direction across every dimension, Xu received hundreds of investor rejections across 2016, 2017, and 2018, including a down round for the Series C [1].

Xu's analysis of why investors passed: "There are moments where it's just before that moment" -- the point right before repeatable profitable growth becomes undeniable. In a market without an obvious winner, with better-capitalized competitors, investors were "gunshy" [1].

The turning point came with the Series D in March 2018. The internal team could see what was coming: DoorDash had consistently superior retention and frequency. "One of the hardest things to really fool is the retention and the engagement of a product." Every marketing dollar went further for DoorDash than for competitors. Once they received the capital to launch across geographies, "it was just a matter of time" [1]. By 2019, DoorDash surpassed Uber Eats and GrubHub to become the category leader.

## COVID-19: Crisis as Clarity

COVID-19 shelved DoorDash's 2019 plans to go public. The period felt like "DoorDash in YC" again: seven days a week, 10 AM to 2 AM, multiple all-company meetings per day [1].

Xu found that crisis simplified decision-making. The priorities were immediate and obvious [1]:

1. **Keep everyone safe.** Acquire tens of millions of units of PPE. Ship contactless delivery in four to five days.
2. **Get everyone liquid.** The average merchant has 17 days of cash on hand; every hour matters. Dashers, many furloughed or laid off, needed instant liquidity.
3. **Take care of the community.** Partner with dozens of large hospital networks (UCSF, Stanford, Mount Sinai) to provide free delivery to hospital workers.

Two additional decisions were controversial internally [1]:

- **National TV campaign advertising for competitors.** DoorDash spent millions on a campaign saying "whether you order on us or any of our peers, just order. The dining rooms may be closed, but the kitchens are open."
- **Cutting commissions in half.** DoorDash was the only platform to do this, at a cost of over $100 million, while the company was still unprofitable and considering an IPO.

Xu framed the decision through the lens of mission duration: "Are we just here to build a company that ends in 2020 or ends in an IPO? No. We're here to build a company that will grow and empower every physical business and grow the GDP of every city. That's not going to happen in one year, two years, five years, ten years." From that perspective, the costs were "a drop in the bucket in the grand scheme of the journey" [1].

## Doing the Work as Expertise

Xu's advice to his 18-year-old self: "Especially when you're on the cusp of something new -- take AI -- there's no better way to be the expert than just to do the work. And you might be surprised at how quickly you get to become the expert" [1]. He had no background in logistics or delivery, but by doing deliveries for years, he developed a strong point of view on how the physical world works. The principle applies regardless of field -- AI, biotechnology, or any emerging domain [1].

## The Physical World Opportunity

Xu argues the physical world remains an untapped frontier: "Where is the last parking spot in a rainstorm here in San Francisco? How many apples does the local grocery store have in aisle six? These are questions that actually nobody has the answer to. No LLM has the answer to" [1]. Physical businesses -- small, medium, and large -- produce the vast majority of jobs and GDP in any society. DoorDash's long-term mission is to grow the GDP of every city by empowering these businesses [1].

## Stanley Tang's Origin Account

Co-founder [[Stanley Tang]] provides a complementary account of DoorDash's origin from the Stanford perspective. While [[Tony Xu]]'s account (source [1]) emphasizes the macaroon store shadowing and the strategic questions answered during YC, Tang's account (source [2]) emphasizes the speed of the initial experiment and the validation signal [2].

### The Macaron Store Meeting

Tang was passionate about building technology for small business owners. During his junior year at Stanford (Fall 2012), he sat down with Chloe, the owner of a macaron store in Palo Alto, to interview her about her problems. Chloe produced a "really, really thick booklet" showing pages of delivery orders she had to turn down because she had no drivers and was personally delivering orders herself [2].

Over the following weeks, the founders talked to 150-200 small business owners. When they raised delivery, "they kept agreeing -- this is a really big problem for us. We don't have delivery infrastructure. There's not any good solutions out there" [2].

### The One-Hour Launch

To test whether consumer demand existed, the founders created paloaltodelivery.com in "about an afternoon." Tang found PDF menus from Palo Alto restaurants online, uploaded them, and added a phone number at the bottom -- their personal cell phone. "We weren't really expecting anything" [2].

The first call came while they were driving home. Someone wanted Thai food. "We're like, we're not doing anything right now. Might as well swing by, pick up some Pad Thai, and deliver it to this person" [2]. The customer handed over a business card identifying him as the author of "Weed the People" -- "the best first delivery slash worst delivery you could have asked for" [2].

Two calls the next day, then five, then seven, then ten. The key validation: people were willing to endure a terrible experience -- looking up PDF menus, calling a phone number -- to get delivery. "When people are willing to put up with all this, we knew we were on to something" [2].

### The Unscalable Infrastructure

The co-founders served as delivery drivers, customer support, and marketers simultaneously [2]:
- **Payments**: Square (shut down on suspicion of money laundering due to rapid small transactions; resolved by co-founder Tony Xu emailing former Square colleagues)
- **Order tracking**: Google Docs
- **Driver tracking**: Apple's Find My Friends
- **Customer acquisition**: Passing out flyers on University Avenue; taking phone calls during lectures
