- **Customer support**: Personally emailing every new customer each night with personalized messages

### The Mobile Insight

When asked why nobody had solved delivery before, Tang's answer was mobile: "What if you could design a delivery system that was entirely based off mobile? Instead of hiring drivers full-time, purchasing vehicles, what if you could tap into an on-demand pool of independent contractors and only send orders to them when they have time?" [2].

### The Ridiculousness of the Journey

Tang captures the absurdity of building DoorDash while still a Stanford student by describing a single moment: walking home the night they raised their Series A from Sequoia, holding CS247 homework, tax forms, a speeding ticket, and "a $15 million piece of paper" in the same hands [2].

## References

1. [How To Build The Future: Tony Xu](https://ycombinator.com/library/MJ-how-to-build-the-future-tony-xu) -- Tony Xu, [[Garry Tan]] (2025)
2. [How to Start a Startup: Getting Started, Getting Press, and Doing Things That Don't Scale](https://ycombinator.com/library/JL-how-to-start-a-startup-getting-started-getting-press-and-doing-things-that-don-t-scale) -- [[Stanley Tang]], Walker Williams, Justin Kan (2014)

title: nVision Medical: From Ovarian Cyst to $275M Acquisition
body:
# nVision Medical: From Ovarian Cyst to $275M Acquisition

nVision Medical (also referred to as Envision Medical) was a medical device startup founded by Surbhi Sarna that developed the first and only FDA-cleared device for collecting cells from the fallopian tube for cancer evaluation. Boston Scientific acquired the company for $275 million [1]. Sarna is now a Group Partner at Y Combinator, where she mentors healthcare and biotech founders.

## Origin: A Personal Health Crisis

At thirteen, Sarna experienced sudden, severe abdominal pain. She was nearly misdiagnosed with appendicitis before doctors determined she had repeat complex ovarian cysts [1].

The diagnostic limitations were stark. Ultrasound could identify that a mass existed but not whether it was cancerous. The available blood test was "no better than a coin toss" for accuracy. Biopsy was not an option because "if it is cancerous, you risk spreading cancer cells all over the body." Surgery to remove the cyst often meant removing the entire ovary, with impacts on fertility and ovarian function [1].

Her family faced a binary choice: invasive surgery with lasting consequences, or wait and see if she had "one of the most lethal cancers impacting women" [1]. They chose to wait. After months, the cyst turned out to be benign. But the experience revealed how "antiquated everything to do with women's health was" [1].

As a high school senior, Sarna wrote in her college personal statement that she wanted to start a company in women's health. That statement became her career [1].

## Founding at 24

Sarna worked as an engineer at Abbott Vascular and then at a smaller medical device startup. At 24, even though her plan was to get more industry experience, "there was just something in me that did not feel satisfied. There was this energy that I couldn't contain" [1]. She left to start the company.

## Talking to Users: The Gynecologist Visit

Sarna needed to validate her device concept with gynecologists but did not know any personally. Her solution: she called her own gynecologist's office, "pretended to have a stomach ache, asked to see her as soon as possible, and in the middle of a pelvic exam said, 'Hey, I have this new catheter concept I wanted to talk to you about'" [1].

Dr. Cook was startled but agreed to meet at a cafe in Saratoga. She liked the concept and introduced Sarna to physicians at UC Davis and Stanford. "It really blossomed from there" [1].

Sarna's advice on early networking: "Right when you graduate from college or even a few years out, no one's expecting you to have a huge Rolodex. Instead, focus on the personal network that you've already built and just ask for introductions to people who are in the industry that you're trying to do something in. If you try hard enough and you do it for long enough, you will get to the right people" [1].

## Fundraising: 50 Rejections and Bias

Sarna was rejected at least 50 times by investors [1]. She identifies multiple barriers: "I'm a brown woman, I'm a woman of color, and I didn't have any one of the many higher degrees that they like -- MBA, PhD, MD. So there I am with my little bachelor's degree trying to go out and start a company" [1].

Investor reactions to women's health specifically included statements like "women's health isn't a big enough market" and referring to it as "bikini medicine" [1]. Sarna's internal response: "I guess 50 percent of the population isn't big enough for you" [1].

Two women investors finally committed, each conditional on finding one other investor. The seed round closed and enabled the first phase of development [1].

Sarna's framing for underrepresented founders: "It's hard to be a founder no matter who you are, but I would be lying if I said it's equally challenging for a woman." Her advice: "These days I sincerely believe that there are enough people who believe in your potential that you can just ignore the folks who are being biased for whatever reason. Don't let it impact your confidence and find the right people to work with" [1].

## From Prototype to FDA Clearance

After the seed round, Sarna attracted a "world-class team" and raised approximately $20 million more in venture capital. The device went through prototype development, multiple clinical studies, and FDA clearance -- becoming "the first and only device cleared to collect cells from the fallopian tube, which you can then evaluate for malignant features" [1].

## The $275M Acquisition

Sarna had no interest in an acquisition. Her goal was getting the product to patients as quickly as possible. But several companies, including Boston Scientific, read about the clinical data and initiated conversations [1].

The offer came via email on an ordinary workday. "I cannot describe just the pure disbelief that I felt in that moment. Can you imagine just being rejected over and over again, struggling to raise any amount of money to push this life-saving device forward, and then all of a sudden having that much validation from a company that knows the industry so well?" [1].

She signed the acquisition papers holding her son on his first birthday, with her mother on one side and her husband on the other [1].

## Transition to YC

After the sale, Sarna was introduced to [[Jared Friedman]] at YC. He was looking for YC's first healthcare and biotech partner. Sarna spoke at a YC event in front of 60-100 founders: "It was the first time that I had relayed my story, and the energy in the room was electrifying" [1].

She realized that "everything I went through, all of the struggle, all the failures, all the mistakes that I made, could actually be put in a productive place" [1]. She joined YC as a visiting partner and later became a full Group Partner.

Her evaluation framework for healthcare founders: "As long as you really know your stuff, I am not going to look at whether or not you have an MD or PhD and make my decision exclusively on that. I want to know that there's a big problem that needs solving and you are the founder to do it, because no one believes in the solution as much as you do" [1].

## References

1. [Backstory: Surbhi Sarna](https://ycombinator.com/library/K2-backstory-surbhi-sarna) -- Surbhi Sarna (2024)

title: Default Alive or Default Dead
aliases: do you make it to profitability
body:
# Default Alive or Default Dead

"Default Alive or Default Dead" is a diagnostic framework introduced by [[Paul Graham]] in October 2015 [1]. It poses a single question to startups past their first 8-9 months: assuming expenses remain constant and revenue growth continues on its current trend, does the company make it to profitability on the money it has left?

"Or to put it more dramatically, by default do they live or die?" [1]

## The Diagnostic

The question is binary. If default alive, you can discuss ambitious new things. If default dead, you need to talk about how to save the company [1].

Half the founders Graham talks to do not know the answer [1]. Trevor Blackwell built a [calculator](http://growth.tlb.org/#) to help founders determine their status.

The reason founders don't know: it is not a question that makes sense to ask early on, "any more than it makes sense to ask a 3-year-old how he plans to support himself" [1]. But as the company grows older, the question switches from meaningless to critical, and that switch often takes people by surprise [1].

Graham proposes starting to ask too early rather than too late. "It's probably not that dangerous to start worrying too early that you're default dead, whereas it's very dangerous to start worrying too late" [1].

## The Fatal Pinch

The most dangerous state is what Graham calls the "fatal pinch": default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it [1]. Founders end up there by not realizing that is where they are headed.

A secondary cause: founders assume it will be easy to raise more money. "But that assumption is often false, and worse still, the more you depend on it, the falser it becomes" [1].

Graham suggests making the assumption explicit: "Say 'We're default dead, but we're counting on investors to save us.' Maybe as you say that, it will set off the same alarms in your head that it does in mine" [1].

## The Investor Dependency Problem

It would be safe to be default dead if you could count on investors. But you cannot. Investor interest is a function of growth, and sometimes something about your business will spook investors even if growth is great [1].

The rule: fundraising should never be more than a plan A. "You should always have a plan B as well: you should know (as in write down) precisely what you'll need to do to survive if you can't raise more money, and precisely when you'll have to switch to plan B if plan A isn't working" [1].

Even with steep revenue growth (over 5x a year), you can only start to count on investor interest, never rely on it [1].

## Growth vs. Spending: The False Dichotomy

"In practice there is surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows" [1]. When growth is fast, it is because the product hits a nerve. When spending is high, it is because the product is expensive to build or sell, or the company is wasteful [1].

These are independent variables, not a tradeoff. The implication is that cutting spending rarely hurts growth and often does not affect it at all.

## Overhiring: The #1 Killer

"Don't hire too fast. Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money" [1].

Three reasons founders overhire:
1. There is so much work to do that they assume hiring will get it done
2. Successful startups have lots of employees, so it seems necessary
3. They do not want to face the real reason for slow growth: the product is not appealing enough [1]

VCs encourage overhiring because kill-or-cure strategies are optimal for portfolios. "VCs want to blow you up, in one sense of the phrase or the other. But as a founder your incentives are different. You want above all to survive" [1].

Sam Altman added: "I think it's roughly correct to say that YC's most successful companies have never been the fastest to hire, and one of the marks of a great founder is being able to resist this urge" [1].

## The Common Death Spiral

The typical pattern [1]:

1. Startup makes something moderately appealing
2. Decent initial growth
3. First round raised easily (founders seem smart, idea sounds plausible)
4. Growth is ok but not great
5. Founders convince themselves hiring will boost growth; investors agree
6. Growth still doesn't come (product is only moderately appealing)
7. Running out of runway with high expenses and slow growth
8. Unappealing to investors for next round
9. Company dies

What they should have done: address the fundamental problem with the product. Hire slowly. Seek growth by [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]], or by redesigning the product in the way only founders can [1].

## The Airbnb Counter-Example

Airbnb waited 4 months after raising money at the end of YC before hiring their first employee [1]. The founders were terribly overworked, but they were overworked evolving Airbnb into the organism it became. This is the model Graham holds up: founders doing the work themselves, staying lean, letting the product evolve before adding headcount [1].

## Premature Scaling

Paul Buchheit identifies a related pattern: "founders take a small business that isn't really working (bad unit economics, typically) and then scale it up because they want impressive growth numbers. This is similar to over-hiring in that it makes the business much harder to fix once it's big, plus they are bleeding cash really fast" [1].

## The Alternative Path

If you are default dead, instead of hiring to boost growth, Graham recommends [1]:
- Doing things that don't scale (see [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]])
- Redesigning the product -- something only founders can do effectively at the early stage
- Operating cheaply to extend runway
- Having an explicit written plan B for survival without more funding

The alarm bells from asking "am I default alive or dead?" can counteract the forces pushing you to overhire and instead compel you toward the paths to growth that actually work for most startups [1].

## References

1. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)

title: Gene Pool Engineering
body:
# Gene Pool Engineering

Gene Pool Engineering is a framework introduced by [[Vinod Khosla]] for systematically maximizing a startup's chances of success through the composition of its early team. The core premise: "A company becomes the people it hires, not the plan it makes" [1]. The framework has two components: the strategic composition of the founding team (gene pool engineering proper) and the cross-functional evaluation of senior hires (the kitchen cabinet).

## The Kitchen Cabinet

Khosla defines the "kitchen cabinet" as the group of people a founder turns to when facing hard, ambiguous, uncertain decisions -- the people you "coalesce around your dining table when you have a really hard, ambiguous, uncertain decision to make" [1]. Building this cabinet is the most important task in a company's first six months.

The kitchen cabinet determines the quality of all future decisions. A business plan is "completely irrelevant, other than to judge how somebody's thought about a problem. Not what they're going to do" [1]. The plan will change constantly; the people who help evolve it determine where the company ends up.

## Equity as the Primary Weapon

Khosla argues that founders systematically under-allocate equity, which is the primary constraint on building a great team [1].

**The Sun Microsystems model**: The first three or four founders kept roughly 25-27% of common stock. An equal or larger portion was reserved for future hires. Investors held about 40% after the Series A. This enabled Sun to recruit Eric Schmidt, Carol Bartz, Bill Joy, and Andy Bechtolsheim among the first 15-20 employees -- each of whom went on to build or lead billion-dollar companies [1].

**The Curai model** (Khosla's son's AI startup): The founder kept 15% instead of a potential 45%, gave a co-founder 15%, and reserved the remainder for hiring engineers who were making million-dollar salaries at Google and Uber. Individual engineers received 3-5% each. In AI, where the best talent can start their own company, standard equity allocations (basis points) are insufficient: "If you don't do that, you're including only the people who couldn't start their own company" [1].

**The magnet principle**: Some early hires are worth their equity even if they contributed zero direct work, because they attract other exceptional people. Bill Joy at Sun was "an incredible magnet." Khosla does not believe Eric Schmidt would have joined a 25-year-old CEO without the draw of working alongside Joy and Bechtolsheim [1].

[[Sam Altman]] reinforces this as "the most important piece of advice" from the interview: "Being super generous with early employee equity in getting founder quality people as the first 10 employees. All the evidence is on the side of doing this, and yet almost no one does, so there's a huge edge if you're willing to do it" [1].

The counterargument -- "I can hire an engineer for X per basis points, so why would I ever pay 5X or 10X?" -- is short-sighted. Altman's response: "Wait, in two years ask me again if that was the right decision" [1].

Khosla identifies insufficient option pools as his "single biggest beef with YC." Most investors and founders resist larger pools because it is not intuitive: "They would like to own a higher percentage, but the pie is much smaller" [1].

## Hiring for Rate of Learning

The most important quality in a hire is rate of learning, not domain experience. "Experience doesn't matter, the rate of learning matters. First principle's thinking matters" [1].

Khosla's hiring evaluation method [1]:
- Give candidates a brand-new problem they have no experience with and observe first-principles thinking
- Ask: "If I gave you $10 million today, what three startups would you consider, and what are the reasons as an investor you wouldn't want to invest in those?"
- For founders: measure rate of change over a defined period ("How fast did they evolve their plan, change their plan?")

Altman confirms this is his "number one" signal for evaluating founders [1].

## Cross-Functional Hiring

For senior hires, functional competence is necessary but secondary. The primary question: "What are the questions he will ask? How will he make the CFO better or the VP of engineering better through the questions they ask?" [1]. The best senior hires improve the thinking of everyone around them, not just their own function.

## Unreasonable People at the Top

Khosla's organizational model separates the strategic layer from the execution layer. "If you're reasonable, you won't do unreasonable things. Great managers will take a great idea and turn it into a good one, and execute a decent idea at lower risk" [1]. For ambitious companies, you need "a think tank of unreasonable people" at the top, with reasonable people layered below to micro-optimize within the macro ideas the kitchen cabinet generates [1].

## Further Reading

Khosla references two published pieces: "Gene Pool Engineering" and "The Labor of Love: The Art of Hiring," both previously published in TechCrunch. He describes them as "essential reading for entrepreneurs" [1].

## References

1. [Vinod Khosla on How to Build the Future](https://ycombinator.com/library/6N-vinod-khosla-on-how-to-build-the-future) -- [[Vinod Khosla]], [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)

title: Pricing Thermometer
body:
# Pricing Thermometer

The Pricing Thermometer is a framework introduced by [[Kevin Hale]] that makes the forces behind pricing visible and actionable [1]. Three variables sit on a vertical axis: cost at the bottom, price in the middle, and value at the top.

## The Two Gaps

**Gap between cost and price = Incentive to Sell.** The larger this margin, the more the company is motivated to push the product -- invest in salespeople, marketing, and distribution [1].

**Gap between price and value = Incentive to Buy.** The larger this gap, the easier it is for the customer to say yes. The customer perceives they are getting a good deal [1].

Most startups fail by placing their price too close to cost (leaving little incentive to sell) or too close to value (leaving little incentive to buy) -- or, most commonly, they place it too close to cost and too far below value, undercharging significantly [1].

## Two Approaches to Setting Price

**Cost-plus pricing**: Start with cost, add a margin. Simple but leaves money on the table [1].

**Value-based pricing**: Start with the value the customer perceives, work backward. Allows charging more and manipulating the customer's incentive to buy. Hale states this is the approach "almost consistently across all businesses, everyone will tell you, you should strive for" [1].

The challenge: startups often do not understand their costs, their value, or both -- so the price ends up in an arbitrary location [1].

## The 10/5/20 Rule

Hale provides a concrete heuristic for founders who do not know where to begin [1]:

1. **10X**: Set a price that is one-tenth of the perceived value. A $10 product should be easily perceived as worth $100.
2. **5%**: Raise prices by 5% at a time.
3. **20%**: Keep raising until 20% of deals are lost. That is the approximate sweet spot.

## Application

The thermometer explains why charging less than competitors rarely works for startups. Early adopters are not price-sensitive -- they care about benefits, not bargains. A low price creates reputation risk ("why is it too good to be true?") and shrinks the incentive to sell, limiting what the startup can invest in distribution [1]. See [[Startup Pricing]] for the full treatment.

## References

1. [Startup Pricing 101](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6h-startup-pricing-101) -- [[Kevin Hale]] (2019)

title: Commit or Validate
body:
# Commit or Validate

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] argue that general startup advice fails when applied uniformly to two fundamentally different types of founders: those who bring deep domain expertise and those who are building expertise from scratch [1]. The framework determines how much a founder should trust their gut versus how much they should validate through customer research.

## The Two Archetypes

**Founder with expertise**: Someone who worked at a company, built something valuable there, saw it deployed and used, and left to productize that tool for a broader market. Or: someone who has built and launched consumer products before and has developed taste for what works [1].

**Founder without expertise**: Two people fresh out of college, technically capable and motivated, but without deep knowledge of any specific problem domain. They are smart but "don't have a lot of preferences. Or to the extent you have preferences, they're the same as everyone else" [1].

Seibel observes these two patterns constantly at YC, and the advice for each is "precisely different" even when the startup idea is identical [1].

## When to Trust Your Gut

Founders with expertise should lean more heavily on their intuition [1]. Seibel's guidance: "If you were inside of a company, you built a tool, everyone at the company uses the tool, it's still being used even though you don't work there anymore, you probably know more than you think" [1].

The advice for such founders is: "Make something that would impress you. Make something that you would be proud of." When you can tell whether the product is good because it solves a problem you personally have or have solved before, that self-evaluation is a reliable signal [1].

Caldwell extends this to arcane technical domains: "If you wanted to build a new compiler, if you wanted to build something that's really arcane, but that you know a lot about and you have a lot of taste, again a lot of opinions about, a lot of expertise on, yes, often you should listen to that expertise" [1].

### The Concrete Example

Caldwell illustrates with a real estate software startup [1]. Category A: a founder who spent ten years at a mortgage underwriting company building software. "Your judgment about knowing what mortgage underwriters want, if you've been building software inside of a mortgage underwriting company for ten years, it's probably pretty good." Such founders should trust their gut [1].

Category B: founders with no industry experience beyond having bought a house. "These folks can still be successful, but those folks should probably trust their gut a little bit less that they know precisely what to build" [1].

## When to Validate

Founders without deep expertise should focus on building expertise through rapid learning [1]. Seibel advises: "If you can start in a good general idea area, a good place where building expertise here will be valuable, and if you don't over hamper yourself with a complicated plan, you give yourself opportunity to build, to figure something out" [1].

The failure mode for founders without expertise is overconfidence: planning out their hiring, reading Goldman Sachs reports about market sizes, and budgeting two years to build a "comprehensive solution." The better approach: "Build the smallest thing possible and learn some more" [1].

Seibel also recommends choosing a domain you are excited to learn about, because "you have a lot of learning to do." Choosing an area you hate because you think it will raise money is "not the best play" [1].

## The Crossed-Wires Problem

The most common mistake is when the two archetypes behave like each other [1]. Caldwell identifies both failure modes:

**The expert who acts like a novice**: An experienced founder with deep domain knowledge hesitates and over-validates. Common rationalizations include: "Maybe the thing I built was only useful at my company and no one else will want it," or "I know what to build and they want it, but I don't think VCs will fund it" [1]. Seibel adds a specific version: founders who abandon their actual expertise because it does not involve AI, building something AI-related instead "because we think we need to say it's AI related to raise money" [1].

**The novice who acts like an expert**: A founder with no domain knowledge builds an elaborate plan as if they already know the answer. They are "planning out their hiring and talking about how much money they need and reading Goldman Sachs reports about market sizes" instead of learning the basics of their chosen domain [1].

## How to Self-Diagnose

Seibel offers a self-diagnostic: if you find you "don't have a lot of opinions about what to work on" or your opinions are "very similar to the fiftieth percentile of other people," you are probably in the validate-first category [1]. The question is whether you are bringing expertise or building expertise, and then implementing a plan based on that honest assessment [1].

For founders who do have expertise, Seibel's test: "Would you have liked this? Would you have bought it? Would you have talked to yourself? Would you have read this email that you just sent about this thing?" These tests all work when you have pre-existing experience [1].

## References

1. [When Should You Trust Your Gut?](https://ycombinator.com/library/Kw-when-should-you-trust-your-gut) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)

title: Equity Equation
body:
# Equity Equation

The Equity Equation is a formula introduced by [[Paul Graham]] in July 2007 for deciding whether any trade of company stock -- for investment, an employee, or a deal -- is worth making [1]. The formula is:

**1/(1 - n)**

where n is the fraction of the company you are giving up. You should make the trade if what you receive improves your average outcome enough that the (100 - n)% you have left is worth more than the whole company was before [1].

## How the Formula Works

If an investor wants to buy half your company, the investment must more than double your average outcome for you to break even. You have half as big a share of something that must be worth more than twice as much [1].

In the general case, if n is the fraction you are giving up, the deal is good if it makes the company worth more than 1/(1 - n) [1].

### Example: Y Combinator

At the time of writing, YC took about 7% of a company. n = 0.07, and 1/(1 - 0.07) = 1.075. So the deal is worth taking if YC can improve your average outcome by more than 7.5%. If they improve it by 10%, you are net ahead: the remaining 0.93 you hold is worth 0.93 x 1.1 = 1.023 [1].

Graham comments: "This is why we can't believe anyone would think Y Combinator was a bad deal. Does anyone really think we're so useless that in three months we can't improve a startup's prospects by 7.5%?" [1]

### Example: Sequoia

Greg McAdoo from Sequoia said at a YC dinner that when Sequoia invests alone they like to take about 30% of a company. 1/0.7 = 1.43, meaning the deal is worth taking if Sequoia can improve your outcome by more than 43% [1].

For the average startup, this would be an extraordinary bargain. "It would improve the average startup's prospects by more than 43% just to be able to say they were funded by Sequoia, even if they never actually got the money" [1]. The reason Sequoia is such a good deal is that their percentage is "artificially low" -- they limit holdings to leave founders enough stock to feel the company is still theirs [1].

The catch: Sequoia gets about 6,000 business plans a year and funds about 20. The odds of getting this deal are 1 in 300. The companies that make it through are not average startups [1].

## Applying the Formula to Employee Equity

The formula works in reverse for employees. If i is the average outcome multiplier with the addition of a new person, they are worth n such that i = 1/(1 - n), which means n = (i - 1)/i [1].

### Worked Example

Suppose you are two founders and want to hire a hacker so good you estimate he will increase the average outcome by 20%. n = (1.2 - 1)/1.2 = 0.167. You would break even trading 16.7% of the company for this person [1].

But 16.7% is not the right amount to offer because stock is not the only cost. There is salary and overhead too. Graham suggests multiplying the annual salary-plus-overhead rate by about 1.5 to translate it into stock terms. The reasoning: most startups grow fast or die. If they die, you don't pay. If they grow fast, next year's salary comes out of next year's (presumably 3x higher) valuation. So the total stock-equivalent cost is about 1.5 years at the present valuation [1].

Full example: the employee is worth 16.7% but the company wants 50% profit on the hire, so his "retail" price is about 11.1%. If he costs $60k/year in salary and overhead, times 1.5 = $90k total. At a $2M valuation, $90k is 4.5%. So the stock offer is 11.1% - 4.5% = 6.6% [1].

Graham notes: "notice how important it is for early employees to take little salary. It comes right out of stock that could otherwise be given to them" [1].

## The General Principle

"Whenever you make any decision involving equity, run it through 1/(1 - n) to see if it makes sense. You should always feel richer after trading equity. If the trade didn't increase the value of your remaining shares enough to put you net ahead, you wouldn't have (or shouldn't have) done it" [1].

The formula does not eliminate guesswork -- "ultimately you always have to guess" -- but it ensures you know what you are guessing about. Whether you use gut feel or a VC-supplied table of typical grant sizes, understand that those are estimates of the multiplier in the equity equation [1].

## Limitations

The formula assumes you can estimate the outcome multiplier, which is inherently uncertain. For VC deals, there are always factors beyond a straight trade of money for stock [1]. The present valuation (typically the post-money of your last round) probably undervalues the company because (a) the company has presumably grown since the last round and (b) the valuation of an early round usually reflects some other contribution by the investors [1].

## References

1. [The Equity Equation](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8u-the-equity-equation) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2007)

title: Better, Faster, or Cheaper
related: [[Coinbase: From Bitcoin at Five Dollars to a Hundred Billion Dollar Company]]
[[Flexport: From Solo Founder to Eight Billion Dollar Logistics Platform]]
body:
# Better, Faster, or Cheaper

[[Garry Tan]] argues that being first to market is overrated. Facebook was the 20th social network. Snapchat was not the first messaging app. Discord was not the first chat app. "You don't have to be first, but you do need to be different" [1]. Every startup must be better, faster, or cheaper than existing alternatives. "If you don't have that, you can't succeed, your startup will die" [1].

## Being First Can Be a Disadvantage

Before Instacart, there was Webvan. Before Facebook, there was Friendster and Six Degrees. Tan uses his own experience: Posterous launched five years after WordPress but still grew fast because it was better in one specific way -- post by email [1].

Investors do favor novelty, which creates a bias founders must navigate. Brand new business models are usually driven by new technologies, new online behaviors, new demographics, new capabilities, or new regulation. But not every startup can find a blue ocean. "Sometimes you have to wade into a red ocean: a space with lots of competitors" [1].

## Better

Being better means providing a clearly superior experience. Tan identifies several patterns [1]:

- **Technical excellence**: Google was better than AltaVista through PageRank. Dropbox was better than a USB drive through seamless syncing.
- **Underserved audience**: Discord focused on gamers (low CPU requirements so games could still run). Airbnb targeted conference goers when no hotels were available. [[Coinbase: From Bitcoin at Five Dollars to a Hundred Billion Dollar Company|Coinbase]] served early crypto believers when the only alternative was the sketchy Mt. Gox.

## Faster

Being faster means reducing time for the customer. Tan's examples [1]:

- **Software replacing paper**: [[Flexport: From Solo Founder to Eight Billion Dollar Logistics Platform|Flexport]] speeds air freight by two to three days and resolves insurance claims six times faster by replacing paper-and-pencil processes with software. "It's easy to be faster than your competitors when your competitors are literally using paper and pencil."
- **Supply liquidity**: Uber used software to scale the world's largest mobile workforce. More drivers meant faster response times.
- **Technical performance**: Facebook stayed fast while Friendster choked on unoptimized MySQL and Java Server Pages. "Speed matters a lot, especially for consumer scenarios."

## Cheaper

Being cheaper means delivering the same or better value at lower cost. Tan's examples [1]:

- **SaaS replacing infrastructure**: Shopify charged $29/month versus running your own Magento server, enabling "a new generation of brand and store that just couldn't have existed without them."
- **New business model**: Lyft applied Uber's model but with citizen drivers instead of black cars, making rides far cheaper. Instacart did asset-light grocery delivery, avoiding Webvan's warehouse and fleet costs.
- **Margin elimination**: Amazon's mantra -- "their margin is our opportunity" -- cut retail margins by removing physical stores. Robinhood brought trading fees to zero by selling order flow data.

## Combining the Framework

These three axes are not mutually exclusive. Many successful startups combine two or three. Flexport is both faster (software vs. paper) and cheaper (efficiency gains). The key diagnostic question: "Is it better, faster, or cheaper than alternatives?" If the answer is no on all three, "you should find a way to become that, or you need to find a new idea" [1].

## References

1. [Billion Dollar Startups Are Better, Faster, or Cheaper](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jn-garry-s-channel-billion-dollar-startups-are-better-faster-or-cheaper) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: Learn, Earn, or Quit
body:
# Learn, Earn, or Quit

[[Garry Tan]] proposes a two-axis framework for evaluating any job or career position: are you learning, and are you earning? [1]. If both, you are in a great place. If one, that can be acceptable. If neither, "it's time to move on. You're wasting your life" [1].

## Learning

Tan recommends prioritizing learning first, especially early in a career. His friend Steven Sinofsky calls this "the protein before the carbs" [1].

Learning starts with self-knowledge: "What do you like? What do you enjoy? What allows you to enter a state of flow?" Then acquiring skills: investing the effort to become genuinely good at something [1].

Tan divides skill sets into two categories [1]:

- **Makers**: design, product, and engineering
- **Hustlers**: management, sales, and marketing

### How to Start Learning

For hustlers: learn to market or sell. Marketing means "helping other people communicate their problem and how their solution is the best." For sales: "go get those customers. Without even asking, send those customers to a founder, and they'll always take your email" [1].

For builders: find a problem, build a first version that solves it. "When you're starting out, it sort of maybe doesn't even matter what that problem exactly is." Tan notes that several successful founders started by making gradebook software because they were in school and had access to teachers frustrated with their grade books. They did not stay in education -- "they ended up making billion dollar companies in lots of other spaces, but they learned to create in gradebook software" [1].

### Building a Portfolio Over Credentials

Tan argues that a demonstrated ability to create is more powerful than any credential. "If you can point to a beautiful app or website or GitHub repo with elegant code that you created, you have done a thing that will almost always set you up to be a top ten percent player off the bat with no credentials" [1].

Tan's personal example: at fourteen, he got his first job cold-calling the Yellow Pages internet section. His credential was a website for his junior high school underground newspaper that had won design awards [1].

## Earning

Earning means putting money in your pocket [1]. Tan identifies equity as the highest-leverage form of earning: "To make the most amount of money, you need to create true wealth by owning the stock of a company that became worth a lot of money" [1].

### For Founders

Starting a company means starting with 100% of a pie that may be worth zero. "If you do manage to get to [[Product-Market Fit|product-market fit]], you could have a company with an exit in the tens of millions, hundreds of millions, or billions of dollars. If you own ten to thirty percent of that company by then, you could be a centimillionaire to billionaire" [1].

### For Employees

Early employees may hold 0.25% to 2% or more. "It can still be an outrageously good outcome, and you might not even have to take as big a risk." Being early at a fast-growing company means the equity may already be worth something meaningful even if the pie was not started from zero [1].

### The Hedonic Treadmill Warning

Tan warns about the earning trap: earning money to spend it on bigger houses, nicer cars, and lifestyle inflation. "Before you know it, you're running on a hedonic treadmill." For founders-in-waiting, the advice is: save the money. "If you're young and still thrifty, don't change your lifestyle. That money you spend needs to be saved so that you can invest your time in creating your own startup" [1].

## Learn and Earn Together

The best position is learning and earning simultaneously [1]. Tan uses his own career as an illustration:

- At Palantir: learning to run teams and build product while earning equity in a $40 billion company [1]
- At Y Combinator: learning to invest and work with earliest-stage startups while earning carry [1]
- At Initialized Capital: learning from founders daily while building his own venture firm [1]

## Neither Learning Nor Earning

The worst position: not learning, not earning, and not enjoying the work. "Sometimes these high-paying jobs that feel meaningless are jobs." Tan references David Graeber's concept of "bullshit jobs" -- work that exists for political or structural reasons rather than productive ones [1].

Tan recalls interning at a bad startup during college: an enterprise software company that raised money but never closed customers. "I didn't earn much because interns don't get paid a lot, but I didn't learn very much either. It was basically a wash" [1].

The prescription: "If you're not learning, you don't have a way to earn equity or even a good salary, and especially if it's not fun or meaningful for you, you gotta quit" [1].

## The Recommended Sequence

Tan recommends learning first, then earning [1]. Reversing the order -- earning first -- means slower learning and risk of lifestyle lock-in. "If you try and do it in reverse and you try to earn first, you'll probably still learn but maybe slowly, and you don't know what you're going to give up" [1].

## References

1. [LEARN, EARN or QUIT: My Job/Career Advice](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jg-garry-s-channel-learn-earn-or-quit-my-job-career-advice) -- [[Garry Tan]] (2021)

title: Feature Prioritization Frameworks
body:
# Feature Prioritization Frameworks

[[Emmett Shear]], co-founder of Justin.tv and Twitch, describes three mental frameworks for deciding what to build next. In his experience, startups progress through each framework in order as the type of challenge shifts [1].

Justin.tv started as a "Built For Me" product -- the founders wanted to make a live reality TV show and built the underlying technology for themselves. Twitch began the same way, as the only part of Justin.tv Shear personally enjoyed was video game streaming. When Twitch development started in earnest, the approach shifted to "Switch To Us" because the primary customers were streamers and Shear was not a streamer. Later, Twitch developed a "Three Numbers Matter" approach that drove most subsequent development [1].

## Built For Me

The founder is the primary exemplar user of the product. Deeply introspect and figure out what you wish the product did. Build that [1].

**When it works best:**
- When the founder truly loves the product. Shear managed the viewer side of the Twitch experience this way because he genuinely loved watching Twitch [1].
- With small teams. Intuitive, introspective understanding is hard to communicate to others. A small team inside a big company can use it, but a medium-sized organization cannot easily align on it [1].
- When the founder has good intuitive access to their desires. Some people want what they "should" want rather than what they actually want. Human introspection is unreliable -- "I think I want this because it's what I believe I'm supposed to want" feels subjectively identical to "I want this" [1].

**Why it gives startups an advantage:** Big companies cannot easily use this method because it does not scale. This explains why many of the biggest startups started with Built For Me -- it provides an automatic advantage against large incumbents. The ongoing popularity of "hack weeks" within large organizations is an attempt to unleash the Built For Me capacity latent in the organization by reverting to a more startup-like approach with small teams and intuitive decisions [1].

## Switch To Us

People are already doing the behavior the product enables, using competitors. The goal is to get them to do it with your product instead [1].

**The method:**
1. Interview 5-6 dedicated users each from your service, each competing service, and closely adjacent services
2. Accumulate approximately 50 interviews
3. Categorize responses and score them in a spreadsheet
4. Look for patterns. Consider doubling down on strengths and shoring up obvious weaknesses
5. Target one competitor at a time
6. Prioritize things that can be built or changed fast -- customers do not care how hard something is to build, only how effective it is for their problem [1]

**The critical principle for interviews:** Use interviews to understand the problem and generate ideas for solving it. "Validating an idea you already have is an anti-goal. You can't improve an idea through any amount of validation" [1]. The goal is to have better ideas as much as to prioritize them. If ideas are generated from customer problems and opportunities, both quality and intuitive prioritization improve [1].

**When it works best:**
- When the product is important to customers and they can articulate their needs. Twitch streamers had strong opinions about streaming services and could weight tradeoffs like "prosumers" or "SMBs." This would not work for something like Snapchat, where users may have no idea what they specifically like about it [1].
- When selling a better mousetrap, not inventing the category. Twitch was a better mousetrap for streamers on other platforms. It would not have worked for Justin.tv when there were no existing live streamers because they were the first live streaming service [1].

**Good interview questions:**
- What do you like about your current solution?
- What do not you like about it?
- How did you choose your current solution?
- What were you doing before?
- What would you do if you were the CEO of the company whose product you are using?
- What is the single most annoying thing about your current solution?
- What is the single coolest new thing about your current solution?
- Is there anything that would convince you to switch to us immediately if we built it?
- If you had a magic wand and could create any experience you wanted, how would it work?
- What is one thing about your current solution that is surprisingly hard?
- Follow up with "interesting, tell me more" or "why is that?" as appropriate. "People know more than they think they do, they just need a good interviewer to draw it out of them" [1]

## Three Numbers Matter

There are a few key drivers that make using the product better or worse. Identify the three most important and focus the entire organization on moving them [1].

**Examples:**
- **Amazon**: lower prices, more product selection, faster/more reliable shipping. Ideas that move those three numbers get priority [1].
- **Twitch**: audience size, positive interactions, money. Twitch focuses on helping streamers grow their audience, interact in more fun and connected ways, and earn more money per viewer [1].

**Why three:** "Three seems to be the maximum number of focus areas that people can keep in mind consistently." A list of "X, Y, and Z" feels natural; "W, X, Y, and Z" does not [1].

**The bonus fourth area: Stuff You Have To Do.** PCI compliance, uptime, security -- things that are not company focus areas but will kill the company if neglected. These should be done but not included in the top-level three metrics [1].

**The recursive property:** Each of the three top-level metrics can be further decomposed into three sub-drivers, and so on. Start with a metric for each area (these evolve -- revisit every few months), choose a resource split by intuition (maybe 33/33/33, maybe 50/25/25), and start trying to move the numbers [1].

**When it works best:**
- When people choose the product for measurable reasons (price, audience size, speed)
- Poorly suited for difficult-to-measure reasons (fun, connection, love, community). "Good for making utilitarian things, less good for making games and toys and beautiful UX" [1]

**Operational excellence:** Important for every company, but for a startup, winning through pure operational excellence is rare. Usually, the startup needs "good enough" operational excellence while focusing on what customers really care about. Sometimes one aspect of operational excellence (low latency, high security, super reliability) becomes a key goal, but even then most aspects should run on a "good enough" basis early on [1].

## Transitions Between Frameworks

Twitch periodically returned to Switch To Us for new customer segments even while running primarily on Three Numbers Matter. When launching a music category, the team talked to artists who livestreamed elsewhere to understand their needs. As the category grew, they began identifying key metrics and transitioning back to Three Numbers Matter [1].

## The Hard Problem

Certain products do not fit any framework: a brand-new category, where people like the product for hard-to-measure reasons, and the founder is not the end user. "It's really really hard!" [1]. This constrains big companies more than small ones because Built For Me does not scale. Big companies end up working on products with easily measurable drivers, or on incremental innovation. The exception is when the CEO is the lead designer (Steve Jobs at Apple) -- a big company escaping the dilemma to do ground-breaking category creation for products that are great for hard-to-measure reasons [1].

## References

1. [How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) -- [[Emmett Shear]] (n.d.)

title: Three Traits of Great Leadership
body:
# Three Traits of Great Leadership

The Three Traits of Great Leadership is a measurement framework developed by [[Ali Rowghani]] based on his experience working closely with Ed Catmull, Steve Jobs, John Lasseter, and Bob Iger at Pixar and Disney [1]. Despite these four leaders being radically different in style and temperament, Rowghani found they all built extraordinary trust through the same three traits.

The framework's core claim: "You cannot be a great leader without [these three traits] because you cannot build trust without them" [1].

## The Three Traits

### 1. Clarity of Thought and Communication

Great leaders think clearly and describe a compelling vision [1]. If employees are "confused about your mission and strategy, or do not find it motivating or credible, they will not follow you with the focus and determination necessary to succeed" [1].

**How to improve**: Spend more time thinking about what is truly important, then practice expressing it simply [1]. Clarity of thought always precedes clarity of language.

**Exemplar**: Jeff Bezos's retail strategy at Amazon, built on three enduring customer preferences: lower prices, bigger selection, faster delivery. As Bezos said: "You can build a business strategy around the things that are stable in time" [1].

**At scale**: Great leaders spend hours preparing internal communications, regardless of natural talent. Tobi Lütke of Shopify spends hundreds of hours preparing for the annual employee Summit because "if we communicate well at the Summit, we achieve great alignment by the end" [1].

### 2. Judgment About People

Great leaders have strong intuition about who to empower. They "see hidden potential in people and detect cases where ambition exceeds ability" [1]. They also have the courage to correct hiring and promotion mistakes.

**The stakes**: "Nothing does more damage to an organization or to the standing of a leader than picking the wrong leaders or failing to correct these mistakes" [1]. But "leaders who fire too many of their own also lose a lot of credibility and trust" [1].

**How to improve**: Meet the best people in every field to sharpen pattern recognition. Do extensive reference checks. Learn from mistakes [1].

### 3. Personal Integrity and Commitment

Integrity means "standing for something meaningful beyond oneself" [1]. It includes admitting mistakes, receiving critical feedback openly, and avoiding trust-eroding behaviors (favoritism, conflicts of interest, inappropriate relationships).

**The test**: "if your team had full transparency into your private communications and behavior towards employees, would you be embarrassed by anything you have done or said?" [1].

Commitment goes beyond time investment. Great leaders "make their work into their core life mission in ways that inspire others" [1].

## Measuring Leadership with Surveys

Rowghani proposes structured employee surveys organized around the three traits. Startups should begin this at approximately 50 employees [1]. The survey design compares CEO self-assessment with employee perception to identify alignment gaps.

### Clarity of Thought Questions

**For the CEO** [1]:
- Write down your company's mission, strategy, and key metrics in less than 2 minutes
- Write down 2-3 themes you consistently emphasize in communications

**For employees** [1]:
- What is the company's mission and strategy?
- What are the most important operating metrics?
- How does your work contribute to key success metrics?
- How often has the definition of mission, strategy, and metrics changed in the past 24 months?
- What do you think is really important to the CEO?
- How effective is the CEO in written, large group, and small group communication?

### Judgment About People Questions

**For the CEO** [1]:
- Rate the effectiveness of each leader you have promoted or hired
- Name any leader you don't think is the right person for their role
- Have you exited the right employees?

**For employees** [1]:
- Has the CEO chosen good leaders?
- Which leaders do you respect and why?
- Are there leaders you think are weak and why?
- Were any recent leadership changes good decisions?
- Have high performers left the company in the past year? Why?
- (For departing employees) Are you leaving because of concerns about senior leadership?

### Personal Integrity Questions

**For the CEO** [1]:
- Have you taken actions that may have diminished employee confidence in your integrity?
- Do you ask for performance feedback? Can you cite examples of behavior change from feedback?
- How do you rate your commitment level? Your direct reports'?

**For employees** [1]:
- How would you rate the CEO's integrity/moral compass?
- Does the CEO listen well and respond to feedback?
- Have you seen favoritism, inappropriate relationships, conflicts of interest, or unethical behavior?
- What do employees feel motivates the CEO?
- How would you describe the CEO's personal commitment to the mission?
- Have you seen examples of lack of commitment from the CEO or other leaders?

## Why Not Measure by Business Results

Rowghani rejects measuring leadership through company performance because "timing and luck can be confounding factors" and because performance metrics provide "no clues as to how someone can improve as a leader" [1]. The three-trait framework is both diagnostic (where are you strong and weak?) and actionable (each trait has specific improvement paths).

## The Trust Equation

Trust is built through both competence (the "science") and character (the "art") [1]:

- **Science of trust**: Making accurate predictions about products, investments, and competitive landscapes. Choosing the right people for leadership roles. Getting things done.
- **Art of trust**: Saying the right thing at the right time. Standing for ideals bigger than yourself. Being honest about what you don't know. Not trying to be someone else.

"This is why you can't try to copy Steve Jobs or Ed Catmull in your quest to be a great leader. You can only be yourself" [1].

The operational lesson for difficult decisions: "ask yourself which course of action and which style of communication will increase the trust that employees have in them. When faced with a difficult challenge, they optimize for trust" [1].

## References

1. [How to Measure Leadership](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3j-how-to-measure-leadership) — [[Ali Rowghani]] (n.d.)

title: The Compound Startup
body:
# The Compound Startup

The compound startup is a concept articulated by [[Parker Conrad]] to describe a software company that builds multiple interoperable applications on a shared platform layer, rather than focusing on a single point solution [1]. Rippling is the canonical example: it is not just payroll, not just benefits, not just IT provisioning, but all of these working together through a unified employee data layer [1].

## The Thesis

The core argument: many of the deeper problems within organizations -- business process management, decision-making, coordination -- cannot be solved by narrow software that addresses only one function. A suite of seamlessly interoperable applications can solve problems that individual tools cannot [1].

Conrad traces this approach to older enterprise software companies -- SAP, Oracle, Microsoft, and to some extent Salesforce -- which were compound businesses before the term existed. The SaaS era created a temporary window where "you could go out and do this really narrow thing and turn it into a SaaS company," with most such companies reaching low-single-digit billions in value [1].

That window is closing. The landscape is overcrowded with point solutions. Sales and marketing has gotten harder and more expensive: "Public companies are spending 50% more on sales and marketing but they're adding 10% less in new ARR with that increase in spend" [1].

## Advantages Over Point Solutions

1. **Deeper problem-solving**: Compound software can automate business processes that cross functional boundaries, something no point solution can do alone [1].
2. **Platform investment**: Shared capabilities like analytics, approvals, permissions, and workflow automations benefit all applications. "Most of our competitors just can't go as deep on that stuff" [1].
3. **Higher wallet share**: When all functions are in one system, revenue per customer is much higher. One of Conrad's early Rippling memos calculated that combining multiple functions raised per-user-per-month pricing from $115 to hundreds of dollars [1].
4. **Stronger moat**: "There's this island of product-market fit that's kind of over the edge of the horizon line that's sort of harder to get to. But if you can get there... it's much harder to displace" [1].

## Limitations and Risks

Conrad is candid about the narrowness of the opportunity: "It's actually a deeply cynical view of software markets because I think it's possible that compound software businesses are the wave of the future. And also, there will be three of them" [1].

The approach requires enormous R&D investment, years of building before launch (Rippling spent roughly two years with mostly engineers before going to market), and founders with deep domain conviction. Conrad advises caution: "I'm not sure that it's like good advice" for most companies [1].

## Relationship to Asian Tech Companies

[[Garry Tan]] observes that compound software resembles Asian super-app strategies more than typical American startup approaches. Conrad agrees it has parallels but locates the closer analogy in pre-SaaS enterprise software: companies built 20+ years ago that were compound from the start [1].

## References

1. [How To Build The Future: Parker Conrad](https://ycombinator.com/library/M2-how-to-build-the-future-parker-conrad) -- Parker Conrad, [[Garry Tan]] (2025)

title: Mission-to-Metrics Framework
body:
# Mission-to-Metrics Framework

The Mission-to-Metrics framework is a three-level alignment tool described by [[Ali Rowghani]] as one of the three tasks that only a startup CEO can perform [1]. It connects the company's purpose (Mission) to its direction (Strategy) to its performance measurement (Metrics), creating a chain that allows every employee to understand how their work contributes to the company's goals.

## The Three Levels

[![Mission-to-Metrics Diagram](../raw/posts/images/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo/cb2bc3e4792d.png)](https://blog.ycombinator.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/11/Artboard-2white_wborder.png)

### Mission (Purpose)

The mission should feel "ambitious and permanent" and find its roots in the reasons the company was founded [1]. It should not change often.

### Strategy (Direction)

Product strategy and go-to-market strategy should be revisited at least twice per year to ensure relevance [1]. Rowghani strongly recommends writing strategy in long form rather than relying on slide decks: "the CEOs who are most effective in developing and communicating strategy take the time to write their strategy out, in long form" [1]. He cites Jeff Bezos's practice at Amazon of requiring 6-page memos for strategic meetings, though he notes founders need not go that far.

### Metrics (Pace and Performance)

A common mistake is equating key internal metrics with top-line results like revenue or user growth [1]. This fails because top-line results are usually not directly actionable. Instead, CEOs must dig deeper to find the drivers. Rowghani cites Facebook's discovery that connecting a new user to 10 friends within 14 days correlated with retained usage. Facebook then set "number of new users with 10 friend connections" as the key product metric [1].

"If you don't know what drives revenue, customer acquisition, or user growth, you aren't likely to be successful anyway" [1].

## The SpaceX Example

Rowghani's most vivid illustration comes from a friend who visited the SpaceX manufacturing floor [1]. He stopped to ask an employee assembling a large part, "What is your job at SpaceX?" The employee answered:

> "The mission of SpaceX is to colonize Mars. In order to colonize Mars, we need to build reusable rockets because it will otherwise be unaffordable for humans to travel to Mars and back. My job is to help design the steering system that enables our rockets to land back on earth. You'll know if I've succeeded if our rockets land on our platform in the Atlantic after launch."

The employee could have simply said he built a steering system. Instead, he traced his specific task through the strategy to the mission. "That is alignment" [1].

## Communication Is Constant

The framework only works if it is communicated relentlessly. Rowghani warns that founders must "reiterate the Mission-to-Metrics much more than what feels reasonable, which may run counter to your instinct to be efficient" [1].

The test is not whether employees can repeat the framework, but "whether they can make good decisions in your absence based on the context you have provided" [1].

This aligns with Aaron Epstein's advice on management communication: "You should feel like you sound like a broken record about the things that are important to your company" (see [[Startup Management]]).

## How to Build It

Rowghani's practical process [1]:

1. Write your Mission, Strategy, and Metrics in long form
2. Get feedback from your leadership team and key employees
3. Communicate it to everyone regularly
4. Test alignment by checking whether employees can make good decisions without you

## References

1. [The Second Job of a Startup CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo) — [[Ali Rowghani]] (n.d.)

title: Cargo Culting
body:
# Cargo Culting

Cargo culting in startups is the practice of superficially copying another company's tactics without understanding why those tactics worked [1]. The term originates from Pacific Islander communities that built fake landing strips hoping airplanes would return -- mimicking the visible artifact without understanding the underlying system [1]. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] identify three levels of cargo culting, each progressively more dangerous.

## Classic Cargo Culting: Copying Successful Companies

The first generation of cargo culting involved copying demonstrably successful companies -- Google, Facebook, and Uber -- without understanding the context that made their strategies work [1].

### Google Cargo Culting

In the 2000s, Google was the archetypal startup. The cargo cult copies [1]:
- Open offices with bright colors and free snacks (the conclusion: this is what made Google great)
- Flat organizational structures with no managers
- "Hire as many smart engineers as possible" regardless of need
- Cute company names with dropped vowels (started by Flickr)
- Logos with primary colors and lens flares

Seibel explains why these tactics worked for Google: they were solving a genuinely hard technical problem (the conventional wisdom pre-Google was that search was a commodity and Yahoo was good enough). They hired during the post-dot-com crash when great talent was available cheaply. These contextual factors did not transfer to the average startup copying Google's office layout [1].

### Facebook Cargo Culting

Facebook's influence produced [1]:
- "Going viral" as a strategy for every product, including ones people use five minutes per month
- Never charging users; building an ads business
- Share buttons on everything
- Ignoring privacy concerns

Seibel explains why this worked for Facebook: going viral is a viable strategy when users spend two hours a day on your product. Facebook's ad business worked because of unprecedented scale and engagement. Caldwell adds: "Not clear that every business should be an ads business" [1]. Seibel notes: "The second Facebook-style profile you had was delivering infinitely less value than the first one" [1].

### Uber Cargo Culting

The Uber cargo cult was distinctive because many of the beliefs about Uber were not even accurate [1]:
- "Spend as much money as possible"
- Expand to as many cities as possible before the product works in one
- Ignore unit economics
- Ignore laws
- "Blitzscale" (a book was written about it)

Caldwell explains the reality: investors funding Uber were looking at markets where Uber had excellent unit economics, then funding rapid expansion into those proven markets. "It wasn't people looking at Uber serving no markets well, burning money left and right" [1]. Caldwell recalls: "The first time you took Uber, it was a huge moment. It was immediately successful in San Francisco." Seibel draws the conclusion: "Superficially copying Uber and pretending that you're Travis doesn't work. It's just like wearing a black turtleneck does not make you Steve Jobs" [1].

## Modern Cargo Culting: Copying Unproven Companies

The second level is more dangerous because the company being copied has not succeeded. A founder bases their strategy on a fundraising announcement and a podcast interview, with no knowledge of the target company's revenue [1].

Seibel describes the pattern: "You're the CEO of a company. The strategy you're betting the farm on is based on you read a fundraising announcement and you have no other context?" [1].

Seibel provides examples: WeWork and Zume Pizza (robotic pizza making) attracted cargo cult followers based on high valuations, not business results. Both eventually flamed out, taking their imitators with them [1].

## Super-Modern Cargo Culting: Copying Struggling Startups

The third and most dangerous level: copying another startup that is itself struggling. Caldwell: "I love when someone references a startup that we know and we're like, no" [1]. Founders copy strategy from companies they believe are succeeding based on superficial signals (GitHub stars, social media presence) without any evidence of actual business performance.

## The Startup Facade

Modern cargo culting also manifests as imitating what a startup "should look like" rather than building something that works [1]:
- Raising money (regardless of need)
- Having impressive advisors
- Filing patents
- Creating a beautiful pitch deck
- Attending conferences
- Getting press

"These are things that founders do to impress themselves, to impress other founders. The user is not essential" [1].

## Influence vs. Plagiarism

Caldwell draws an analogy to academic writing: plagiarizing a paper by copy-pasting from three sources without understanding them is not original work. Reading many sources, digesting the information, and synthesizing it into your own original work with cited sources is [1].

Seibel extends the analogy to music: ripping off someone's song is plagiarism; being influenced by sounds and integrating them into new music is how the industry works. "Everyone's being influenced by everyone else" [1].

The key distinction: copying completely without thought versus being influenced and borrowing good ideas to integrate into your own thing [1].

## The Right Starting Point

Seibel argues that cargo culting is avoidable when founders start from the user's perspective. "What does the user want?" leads to understanding which company tactics are essential versus superficial [1]. Do people care about Google's logo or about getting good search results? Do they care about which city Uber expanded to, or that Uber shows up in five minutes? Starting with the user makes the essential visible and the cargo cult artifacts obviously irrelevant [1].

Starting from a fundraising announcement makes cargo culting almost inevitable [1].

## References

1. [Dalton & Michael: Silicon Valley's Cargo Culting Problem](https://ycombinator.com/library/IR-dalton-michael-silicon-valley-s-cargo-culting-problem) — [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2022)

title: The Midwit Meme
body:
# The Midwit Meme

The midwit meme, as applied to startups by [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]], captures a recurring pattern: the naive beginner and the experienced expert arrive at the same simple conclusion, while the person with intermediate knowledge overthinks themselves into paralysis [1]. The framework is a diagnostic for recognizing when founders are overcomplicating decisions that should be straightforward.

## The Structure

In the meme format, three levels of intelligence are depicted. The "idiot" and the "genius" reach the same simple answer. The "midwit" in the middle constructs an elaborate, multi-variable analysis that leads to worse outcomes [1].

Caldwell explains why this maps to startup life: founders feel lost, have no boss, have no constraints, and "tend to overthink everything. In every decision, you kind of think in circles over and over again" [1].

## Elon Musk as the Canonical Example

Seibel introduces Elon Musk as the central example, with Caldwell extending it, to illustrate that the "idiot" approach and the "genius" approach are often indistinguishable [1]:

- **SpaceX**: Seibel describes Musk going to Russia to buy rockets. The Russian government could not tell if he was brilliant or insane. Seibel: "Is that genius or stupid? I can't tell" [1].
- **Tesla**: Caldwell frames the midwit analysis. The simple version: "Electric cars are good." The midwit version: no one has successfully started a car company since the 1950s, the infrastructure does not exist, every prior attempt failed. The genius version: "Electric cars are good" [1].
- **The Boring Company**: Seibel presents this as the funniest example: "Traffic is bad. What if we dug a hole under the ground?" The midwit response involves bus system optimization and transportation theory. "Drill tunnel to avoid traffic is certainly at the extremes" [1].

Seibel draws the key insight: "If he was an overthinker, he would rule all this stuff out. All this stuff is obviously a bad idea. And anyone in the midwit territory would rule out all these ideas" [1]. The hard work is in the implementation, but the initial idea is simple.

## Applied to Startup Decisions

### Fundraising

Caldwell created an actual midwit meme for YC founders nervous about fundraising. The naive founder says: "I need $800K, so I'll raise $800K." The experienced founder says: "I need $800K, so I'll raise $800K." The midwit wants to "construct the perfect round," worries about signaling risk, allocation for value-added investors, and spends 15 levels deep in a hypothetical for an offer they do not have [1].

### MVPs and Launching

Extremes: build something fast and ship it. The midwit worries about bad reputation from bugs, believes they only have one shot to launch, fears competitors will copy the idea, debates between TechCrunch, New York Times, and Hacker News coverage, and considers rebranding because a similar name exists [1].

### Co-Founders

Extremes: "It's fun to work with my friends, so I'll work with my friend." The midwit wants a co-founder with five to seven years at Google who reached at least level seven. The founders of Twitch chose their co-founder because "this guy helped us find an apartment" [1].

### Product-Market Fit

Extremes: "Did I sleep last night?" (If you have time to wonder whether you have PMF, you probably do not.) The midwit creates 15 different metrics and a complicated framework to determine if they might have "early product-market fit" [1].

### Applying to YC

Extremes: fill out the application and submit it. The midwit spends months on application strategy, seeks introductions to alumni, gets recommendations from strangers, and sends drafts to 15 people for feedback. "Not correlated with success" [1].

## Beginner's Mind

Seibel connects the midwit meme to the Zen concept of beginner's mind. At the close of the conversation, Seibel states: "What would an idiot do? That's actually how you can divine the genius approach. Everyone can reach for the idiot approach" [1].

Seibel describes the advantage of youth: young founders "think everything is easy and none of the innumerable reasons why something might be hard even occurs to them" [1]. Caldwell identifies the worst position as knowing just enough to be afraid: "You know just enough to know why the things you're trying are hard and the odds are stacked against you. And that freaks you out" [1].

## Trap Door Decisions: When to Overthink

Not everything should be simplified. Caldwell identifies categories where careful thinking is warranted [1]:

- **Legal issues**: "Don't break any law. Saying you didn't know there was a law is not a great legal defense"
- **Regulatory issues**: Especially in healthcare, finance, and regulated industries
- **People's health and well-being**: "Let's measure twice, cut once" for online pharmacies and similar products
- **Distinguishing high-stakes from low-stakes**: Good founders are skilled at determining which decisions fall into the overthink-worthy category and which do not

The framework is not "never think." It is: most of the work is not in optimizing decisions. "Most of the work is launching, getting the first customer, selling. It's doing these things yourself" [1].

## Why Brains Reject Simplicity

Caldwell argues that unless you have personally witnessed a successful startup from the inside, "your brain rejects the idea" that the beginning was silly and simple. Seibel adds: "It had to be lots of strategy meetings and whiteboarding, flow charts, Gartner Magic Quadrants" [1]. The reality of how big companies actually got built is hard to believe for people who have not seen it firsthand.

## References

1. [Dalton & Michael: Elon Musk and the Midwit Meme](https://ycombinator.com/library/IW-dalton-michael-elon-musk-and-the-midwit-meme) — [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)

title: Legible Simple Obvious
body:
# Legible, Simple, Obvious

A framework from [[Kevin Hale]] for designing pitch deck slides that investors can understand and remember [1]. The framework is built on a single premise: before anyone can remember your ideas, they have to understand them. If a founder just communicates clearly, they will do better than 99% of startups.

## The Three Principles

![Legible, Simple, Obvious](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/35131887eed3.png)

**Legible**: The audience can physically read the slide. Use large type, bold text, a simple font, and good contrast from the background. Text at the top is easier to read from the back of a large room. At Demo Day, the room holds over 500 people, many of whom are older with imperfect eyesight [1].

**Simple**: Each slide expresses one idea. The word "simple" shares a root with "complex" -- the Latin "plex" meaning braid, twist, or fold. Simple ideas are "one fold." A simple slide does not intertwine multiple ideas. Since there are only five to seven key points, the deck should have only about five to seven slides [1].

**Obvious**: The slide can be understood at a glance. Test: show it to a stranger and ask what it means. If they do not immediately say your idea, you lose. Obviousness matters because Demo Day presentations are 2 minutes and 30 seconds, and investors are easily distracted. "If they don't get your point right away, they will check their email" [1].

## How to Apply

1. Start with legibility and simplicity.
2. Make the idea explicit. Add a direct caption or label that states the takeaway. Hale calls this "CliffsNotes right on the slide." A growth graph without labels requires study; a growth graph captioned "10x growth in 6 months" is instant [1].
3. Remove information distractions. Each additional piece of information increases comprehension time logarithmically ([Hick's Law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hick%27s_law)). Diagrams are "little mazes for ideas" [1].
4. Avoid screenshots, screencasts, and videos. They are almost always illegible, complex, and non-obvious. Replace with simplified bulleted steps describing the user flow [1].

## The One Exception

Deliberate complexity is acceptable when the point is to overwhelm the audience to illustrate how hard a problem is. Hale cites Magic (YC company), which showed a grid of every on-demand service logo across every industry to illustrate the scope of the problem their software solves [1].

## Anti-Patterns

- Too much text
- Excessive explanations and caveats
- Excessive branding per slide
- Photos without titles or captions
- Animations and transitions
- Memes, subtle humor, accidental humor

"Investors invest in teams not slides. Your slides should make your ideas more clear. Don't let your slides distract investors from what you're saying out loud" [1].

## References

1. [How to Design a Better Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck) — [[Kevin Hale]] (December 2015)

title: Schlep Filter
body:
# Schlep Filter

The Schlep Filter is a concept introduced by [[Paul Graham]] to describe an unconscious mental filter that prevents founders from seeing good startup ideas because those ideas involve tedious, unpleasant, or frightening work [1]. Graham wrote a [separate essay](http://www.paulgraham.com/schlep.html) on the condition it induces, which he calls "schlep blindness."

## The Mechanism

Most programmers wish they could start a startup by just writing brilliant code, pushing it to a server, and having users pay them lots of money [1]. They would prefer not to deal with tedious problems or get involved in messy ways with the real world. This is a reasonable preference, because such things slow you down. But this preference is so widespread that the space of convenient startup ideas has been stripped pretty clean [1].

"If you let your mind wander a few blocks down the street to the messy, tedious ideas, you'll find valuable ones just sitting there waiting to be implemented" [1].

## Stripe as the Canonical Example

Stripe is the most striking example of what happens when you turn off the schlep filter [1]. Thousands of programmers were in a position to see the payments opportunity. Thousands knew how painful it was to process payments before Stripe. But when they looked for startup ideas, they did not see this one, because unconsciously they shrank from having to deal with payments [1].

Dealing with payments is a schlep for Stripe, but not an intolerable one. The result of most people avoiding the idea: Stripe had comparatively smooth sailing in areas that are sometimes painful, like user acquisition. "They didn't have to try very hard to make themselves heard by users, because users were desperately waiting for what they were building" [1].

## The Unsexy Filter

The unsexy filter is a related but distinct concept [1]. While the schlep filter prevents you from working on problems you fear, the unsexy filter prevents you from working on problems you despise or find boring.

Graham and his co-founders overcame the unsexy filter to work on Viaweb. There were interesting things about the architecture of their software, but they were not interested in ecommerce per se. They could see the problem was one that needed to be solved [1].

Turning off the schlep filter is more important than turning off the unsexy filter for two reasons [1]:
