1. The schlep filter is more likely to be an illusion -- the feared schleps may not be as bad as imagined
2. It is a worse form of self-indulgence -- "Starting a successful startup is going to be fairly laborious no matter what. Even if the product doesn't entail a lot of schleps, you'll still have plenty dealing with investors, hiring and firing people, and so on"

The unsexy filter, while still a source of error, is not entirely useless. If you are at the leading edge of a rapidly changing field, your ideas about what is sexy will be somewhat correlated with what is valuable. And if you find an idea sexy, you will work on it more enthusiastically [1].

## How to Overcome the Filters

Graham recommends several specific tactics [1]:

1. **Actively seek schlepy ideas.** Instead of merely turning off the filter, seek out ideas that are unsexy or involve schleps. "Don't try to start Twitter. Those ideas are so rare that you can't find them by looking for them. Make something unsexy that people will pay you for" [1].

2. **Ask what you wish someone else would build.** "What would you pay for right now?" This bypasses both the schlep and unsexy filters because you are imagining yourself as the customer [1].

3. **Look for dying industries.** "Since startups often garbage-collect broken companies and industries, it can be a good trick to look for those that are dying, or deserve to, and try to imagine what kind of company would profit from their demise" [1].

4. **Find disdained markets.** When big players ignore or look down on a market, that disdain often misleads them. HP turned down the Apple I because it used a TV as a monitor, which seemed intolerably declasse [1].

## The Connection to Starting a Startup Generally

Graham argues in his 2014 Stanford lecture that startups involve unglamorous but necessary schleps [2]. This is distinct from the bogus work that plagues big companies and school. "In a startup you have to spend a lot of time on schleps, but this sort of work is merely unglamorous, not bogus" [2].

The schlep filter is one of several reasons that the method of deliberately trying to think of startup ideas produces worse results than organic discovery. When you try to think of ideas, the schlep filter is active. When you naturally notice problems in your own life, you have already accepted the schlep because you are living with the problem [1].

## References

1. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (November 2012)
2. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)

title: Creating Luck
body:
# Creating Luck

[[Dalton Caldwell]] argues that luck in startups is not random. It is manufactured through speed. "The way to create luck is to move much faster than everyone else and to give yourselves as many opportunities for that lucky thing to happen to you" [1].

## The Speed Principle

If everyone is running at five miles an hour, imagine running at fifteen. You can run circles around everyone. A startup moving fast gets more opportunities to talk to more customers, change ideas more often, and write code more quickly. "Everything gets easier if you move much faster" [1].

The founders that get the luckiest are the founders that move the fastest, giving themselves "as much surface area as possible to get super lucky" [1].

## Brex: From VR Headsets to Credit Cards

Brex is the canonical example. When YC initially funded the Brex founders, they were working on a VR headset idea. If they were not fast movers, "they would have worked on that idea, realized it wasn't gonna work, and given up" [1].

Instead, they realized the VR idea was not working within about six weeks. They did not simply pick one new idea. They iteratively went through half a dozen or more different ideas, quickly talking to experts about each one and crossing them off as non-viable. "By doing this really fast process, really quickly during the batch, they zeroed in on what became Brex very quickly" [1]. See [[Startup Ideas]] for more on the idea iteration process.

## Retool and Magic: The Same Pattern

Retool followed the same pattern -- changing ideas quickly to find a much better one [1]. Magic (originally funded to do something completely different) moved so fast that they built full prototypes of ideas and even launched them to customers, eventually finding an idea that took off [1].

In all three cases, the companies "dramatically increased the surface area of luck" through speed [1].

## Creating Luck Pre-Company

For people who have not yet started a company, Caldwell identifies two strategies [1]:

**Know interesting people.** Meet and learn from as many interesting people as possible [1].

**Do uncomfortable things.** "A lot of the times, founders that may not get as lucky just stay at home and they wait for the world to come to them and they wait to be recognized for their secret genius. Whereas the folks that are able to create luck just go out there and interact with people and tell other people about their ideas" [1].

### Mark Zuckerberg as a Case Study

Caldwell cites Zuckerberg's pre-Facebook history as an example of luck-creation through prolific making. Before Facebook, Zuckerberg "was constantly creating prototypes and giving them to users. He was creating music plug-ins for Winamp. He was making hacks for his friends. He was always just kind of making stuff and giving it to people" [1].

This habit of building and sharing created the surface area from which Facebook emerged. "You can dramatically increase the chance that you will get lucky if you just make things. You learn how to make things really fast. You learn how to make things by yourself or with other people that are interesting. And you give them to other people. You don't just hide them to yourself. And if you just do that a lot, things start happening for you" [1].

## When to Play to Strengths vs. Go Outside Comfort Zone

Caldwell draws a nuanced line [1]:

- If you have the technical skills to build the product you want to build, it is acceptable to "get in way over your head" in terms of domain knowledge. Sometimes outsiders see opportunities that insiders have given up on [1].
- If you have "absolutely no relevant skills" and are trying to break into something that requires technical skills, "you're gonna have a pretty hard time" [1].
- Knowing nothing about biology and deciding to cure cancer is "a pretty tall order" [1].

## Creating Luck at Scale (Series A+)

The speed-as-luck principle applies beyond the earliest stage. At Series A and later, it manifests as a culture of fast-moving experimentation. "Hire people that move so fast that they get lucky too" [1]. If the founder is fast-moving and continues to hire people who iterate through ideas quickly, the entire culture becomes "continuously lucky" [1].

## References

1. [How Do You Create Luck?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7L-how-do-you-create-luck) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (n.d.)

title: Board Management
body:
# Board Management

Board management is one of the most anxiety-inducing responsibilities for startup founders. As [[Anu Hariharan]] describes it, the anxiety is natural: "this is the first time the founder/CEO is subject to external supervision, and the board has powers that include the firing of the CEO and the senior management" [1]. This article covers the full lifecycle of board management: composition, meeting cadence, deck preparation, leveraging board members, and handling difficult board dynamics.

## Purpose of a Board

The main role of a board is to guide the company through major decisions: hiring and firing senior management, approving corporate actions (compensation, stock options, budget), and offering guidance on strategic decisions that impact the business long-term [1]. Every Delaware corporation must have a board once it starts operating, but in the early days it consists of founders and functions mainly as a technical body for approvals like option grants [1].

When people refer to "setting up a board," they usually mean adding outside directors after raising money, either investor directors or independent directors [1].

## Board Composition

### Size and Evolution

- Early-stage companies: 3-5 board seats [1]
- Public companies: 7-9 seats [1]
- Series A and Series B lead investors typically join the board; later-stage growth investors generally do not ask for seats [1]
- Composition changes significantly in the years before going public, starting with recruiting independent board members, then forming committees (audit, compensation) [1]

### Selecting Board Members

The primary attribute to select for in early-stage board members is trust, and "the only way you build trust is with time" [1]. Hariharan recommends getting to know VC partners on your target list at least 6 to 9 months before your fundraise. Mathilde Colin, CEO of Front, documented her approach to this in detail [1].

Before adding new board members, founders should assess their strengths, weaknesses, and whether they would want to work with them for 10+ years, given that the median time to IPO can produce board relationships of that duration [1].

Reference checking is critical. However, many founders observe that this has caused some VCs to become less transparent in feedback with their portfolio companies, since they fear bad references affecting future deals. This dynamic has led some founders to bring on independent directors sooner [1].

### The Rise of Trusted Independents

Several examples illustrate the trend toward deeply trusted independent board directors [1]:
- **Atrium**: Founder Justin Kan added [[Michael Seibel]] (a former co-founder at Justin.tv) to the board at Series B
- **Brex**: Founders Henrique and Pedro added Victor Lazarte (TFG founder/CEO), who had known them since age 16
- **Faire**: Founders added Brian Grassadonia (Square Cash leader), their former manager at Square

These relationships are "likened to family relationships, where the family privately shares the kind of direct feedback no one else will" [1]. The caveat: "this does not mean you add your friend to your board. You need someone you really trust, who is 100% independent, and has the ability to help both you and your team as you scale the company" [1].

## Running Board Meetings

### Meeting Cadence

At the Series A stage, meetings tend to be informal (like 1:1s), ranging from bi-weekly to bi-monthly [1]. By Series B, establish a regular quarterly cadence with all meetings scheduled a year in advance to ensure attendance. In-person meetings are strongly preferred [1].

### Agenda and Length

Board meetings typically last about 3 hours [1]. The critical mistake to avoid is treating board meetings as status update meetings. Hariharan advises:

- First 45 minutes: highlights, lowlights, and KPIs [1]
- Remaining time: deep dives on no more than 1-2 strategic topics [1]

She shares the actual agenda from a Gusto board meeting as a model [1]:

| Agenda Item | Duration |
|---|---|
| Highlights / Lowlights | 10 min |
| Performance / KPIs | 50 min |
| Strategic topic 1: 3 Year Strategic Plan | 45 min |
| Strategic topic 2: Engineering Org | 45 min |
| Closed Session | 30 min |

The result: no more than one-third of the meeting on the state of the business, with the majority on strategic issues [1].

Hariharan recommends identifying and aligning on key KPIs with board members early, as this "trains" board members to process operational data quickly and focus on the "why" behind hits and misses [1].

### Pre-Meeting Preparation

What makes Gusto's approach work happens outside the board meeting [1]:
1. Extensive prep on areas where input is sought (ideally topics already being discussed with the executive team, so materials serve double duty)
2. Pre-read deck sent at least one week in advance
3. Questions collected from each board member in a Google Doc three days before
4. Questions answered in the Google Doc by the morning of the meeting

Brex follows an even more structured 28-day preparation timeline [1]:

| Timing | Activity |
|---|---|
| T-28 days | Founders/Chief of Staff identify strategic topics; align with executive team |
| T-18 days | Outline topics, write content, share draft deck with executive team |
| T-11 days | Execs provide feedback; Finance finalizes deck |
| T-7 days | Final review; distribute deck to board |
| T-4 days | Board submits questions (especially on KPIs) |
| T-2 days | Brex consolidates and answers all board questions |
| T-0 | Set expectations upfront on where to spend time and seek feedback |

### Board Deck Structure

The board deck is "just a tool for discussion, not an end in and of itself" [1]. Its structure should align with the meeting agenda. Gusto's decks are 30-50 pages and follow this structure [1]:

- **Highlights / Lowlights**: 1 page
- **Performance / KPIs**: 15-20 pages
- **Strategic Topic 1**: 5-10 pages covering options considered, decision framework, product roadmap (including what they plan NOT to pursue), known vs. unknown risks, economic value assessment, discussion questions
- **Strategic Topic 2**: 5-10 pages covering current org, hiring scale, future org (18 months), talent assessment, retention/development/recruitment plan
- **Appendix**: 10 slides on organizational health (hiring metrics, close rates, employee pulse survey results)

### Involving Executives

Post-Series B, Hariharan recommends having specific executives join only for their agenda item (about 45 minutes) rather than attending the entire meeting [1]. The first approach (all executives attending) tends to turn the meeting into a presentation rather than a discussion and makes it harder for the CEO to discuss sensitive matters [1].

The targeted approach achieves three things: deeper discussion on strategic issues, executives hearing board feedback directly, and more time for the CEO and board to discuss sensitive matters privately [1].

General counsel and/or outside counsel should attend all board meetings to capture notes and handle issues requiring attorney-client privilege [1].

## Monthly Updates

In addition to quarterly meetings, a 2-page monthly update email at the beginning of every month is best practice [1]. The email typically includes: last month's performance summary, hiring update, burning issues, and specific asks for help.

Practical examples of effective asks [1]:
- **Brex**: Always makes two specific asks in monthly emails: introductions to business partners/customers, and help with key open roles
- **GitLab**: Asks for introductions to specific individuals at potential customer companies, and publicly applauds investors who have helped, "putting pressure on the entire board to do the same"

Monthly updates accomplish three things [1]:
1. Board members go above and beyond to help with specific asks
2. The board arrives better prepared for quarterly meetings
3. The CEO is forced to step back and objectively assess the business

Hariharan cites Jeff Bezos's advocacy for written narrative over slides: "written narrative is more compelling in sharing ideas than bullet points and slides" [1].

### Letter to the Board

Some CEOs use the board meeting as an occasion for deeper reflection [1]:
- **Peter Reinhardt** (Segment): Writes a 3-5 page memo covering highlights, lowlights, and time allocation analysis, including his own strengths and weaknesses as CEO
- **Faire**: Uses written memos to discuss strategic priorities, articulating decision frameworks and documenting uncertainties and blind spots. These memos help the board prepare and create a record of decision-making quality

## Navigating Board Dynamics

### Mixed Messages

Conflicting viewpoints are productive: "The job of the board is to push you on your strategy and to ask questions that help sharpen your thinking" [1]. CEOs should welcome disagreement and not feel pressure to reach consensus.

When conflict stalls progress, take the conversation offline with 1:1 or small group meetings. Use a decision-making framework (e.g., economic value, resources, cash needs, competitive threat) to guide the path forward [1].

A critical principle: "Your board members will never be as close to the business to make that decision. While board members provide important input all CEOs should seriously consider, the CEO has the authority to make the best decision on behalf of the company" [1].

### Asking for Help

Not every board member needs to be in the loop on every topic. "It is a poor use of time to update every board member 1:1 before every board meeting" [1]. Instead, follow up directly with specific board members on topics where they can be most helpful.

### Balancing Helpfulness vs. Control

"Be cautious of board members who are trying to make decisions for you. A board member's job is to help you think through the issues by offering tools or decision frameworks -- not to give you all the answers on how to run your business" [1].

Hariharan cites Micky Malka of Ribbit Capital as an exemplary board member: when the Brex CEO asked about typical credit loss rates, rather than giving a definitive answer, Malka shared examples from 10 startups in Ribbit's portfolio with their trajectories and pros/cons of each approach, empowering the founders to decide [1].

### Problem Board Members

If a board member is unhelpful or overbearing [1]:
1. First attempt a 1:1 meeting with constructive, specific feedback
2. Work with other board members to understand and address the issue
3. If behavior is destructive (meddling, micromanaging, leaking to press), consider either approaching someone senior at the board member's firm for a replacement, or adding more board members to create counterweight

The warning: if all board members echo the same feedback and the CEO is not listening, "the problem is likely you" [1]. Some CEOs bring executive coaches to board meetings to calibrate their perception of board dynamics.

### Removing Board Members

This is a last resort with two options [1]:
1. **Approach the firm for a replacement**: Burns bridges but works if the company is performing well (high leverage)
2. **Add more independent directors**: Creates counterweight without direct confrontation

Companies that are top performers in a VC's portfolio have more leverage than they realize in requesting board member changes [1].

## References

1. [How to Create and Manage a Board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board) — [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)

title: Technical Co-Founders
body:
# Technical Co-Founders

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] state that based on the thousands of companies YC has funded, "companies lacking a technical co-founder underperform" [1]. The video's title answers its own question with a single word: Yes.

## The Core Argument

Caldwell frames the key insight: "The best business founders recruit amazing technical co-founders, and that is how they distinguish themselves. That is the number one distinction" [1]. It is not what is on the resume, the prior executive role, or the quality of the idea. The distinguishing act is the recruiting [1].

Seibel draws two overlapping sets: "There's the set of people that are great business founders. And there's the set of people that can recruit amazing technical co-founders. You want to be in the intersection of those two sets" -- and "there's not many people" in that intersection [1].

## Why Founders Resist This Advice

Caldwell identifies the most common pattern: "I can't find this person, so I'm just going along" [1]. Business founders frame going ahead without a technical co-founder as entrepreneurial spirit -- "no barrier can prevent me from getting my product to market." They hire engineers, use dev shops, or build with no-code tools [1].

Caldwell's response: "One, the percentage chances of death just went through the roof. But two, I bet if you would put the same amount of effort you put into getting a product to market without a great technical founder into finding a great technical founder, at least half of people would be able to do it" [1].

## The Rocket Ship Analogy

Seibel offers a vivid comparison: "I'm going to build a rocket to the moon. But no one here knows anything about physics. I have such a big heart. I have such hustle. I'm going to the moon" [1]. He notes the absurdity is clearer in other domains: a five-foot-four person who cannot jump or run rarely claims they will make the NBA. "Whereas we see the equivalent of that in startups all the time" [1].

## The "Tech-Enabled" Objection

A common retort is: "We're not building a software business. Software is in there, but it's a tech-enabled marketplace / a simple DoorDash clone / we can use white-label software" [1].

Seibel acknowledges this is hard to win in theory. But the facts are clear: "Literally DoorDash or literally Airbnb, they lived and died by their speed of shipping software. And if what they did was buy white label software or have some dev shop in a foreign country making edits to the website, there's a zero percent chance those companies would have worked" [1].

## The Compounding Effect

Seibel draws from his personal experience at Justin.tv and Twitch. Even when competitors raised more money, had nicer offices, more employees, or cooler users, "having better engineering is the gift that gives you more shots every day. Even if you need to pivot, no matter what happens, good or bad, you're in a better position to react to these changes" [1].

The compounding works in the negative direction too. A team of business people assessing engineers will "naturally start by hiring worse engineers" -- and that quality cascades through every subsequent engineering hire [1].

## Even Technical Founders Benefit

Seibel was a "technical CEO" but still benefited enormously from having three additional technical co-founders. "Without my three other co-founders, none of that happens. Ideas are a dime a dozen" [1]. The advice is not only for non-technical founders.

## How to Find a Technical Co-Founder

### Stop Disqualifying People

Caldwell describes a common failure: "I obviously need a CTO with ten years of experience, who've managed teams of at least fifty to one hundred." He asks: "You're twenty-three years old with no years of experience. Why do you need that?" [1]. The founder imagines premature scale. In reality, a talented engineer who cares about the mission is far more valuable than a seasoned executive expecting a large team to manage [1].

### Change Your Network

Sometimes the right move is not to start the company yet. "Go get a job at a startup. Go change the people you know" [1]. Taking a sideways step to build the right network may be "the easiest and best path to starting the journey with the highest likelihood of success" [1].

### The Visualization Exercise

Caldwell recommends a thought experiment: "Visualize the best person you have ever worked with, either in school or in your job. The best engineer that everyone knows is the best person. Can you get that person to be your co-founder?" [1].

Founders immediately negotiate against themselves: "Oh, they would never. They're making lots of money." The response: "Have you talked to them?" Almost always, the answer is no [1].

### Pitch Adventure, Not Ideas

The biggest recruiting mistake: pitching the idea as a finished plan and offering the technical person the role of builder. "Here's my idea for a social network for dogs, will you build my website?" This is offering employment, not partnership [1].

The correct pitch: "Let's build a company together" [1]. Seibel notes: "You're offering adventure. You're offering the unknown." The best engineers do not have "six adventures on the table." Being pitched a genuine co-founding adventure is rare and compelling [1].

Seibel uses Justin Kan as an example. Kan recruited Seibel, Emmett Shear, and Kyle Vogt to co-found a startup where Kan wore a camera on his head, streaming his life 24/7, on a site named after himself, having raised only $50,000. "How did he build that team? He promised adventure, not that the idea was going to work" [1].

Ask the target engineer what startup ideas they have. Come together on the idea as co-owners. "It's shocking how often that appears to have never occurred to someone. They think finding a tech co-founder is to find someone who will basically submit to their whims" [1].

## References

1. [Does Your Tech Startup Really Need a Technical Co-Founder? Yes.](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KO-does-your-tech-startup-really-need-a-technical-co-founder-yes) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)

title: Dev Tools
body:
# Dev Tools

Dev tools are software that developers use to build products -- covering coding, testing, debugging, documenting, deploying, and running applications [1]. The category includes IDEs (VSCode), APIs (Stripe, Twilio, Algolia), libraries and frameworks (React, Next.js, LangChain), infrastructure (AWS, Vercel), and workflow tools (Docker, Terraform, DataDog, GitHub) [1]. [[Nicolas Dessaigne]], former CEO of Algolia and YC Group Partner, provides the comprehensive guide to building a dev tools company [1].

YC has funded hundreds of dev tools companies, including two that became public (GitLab, PagerDuty) and many others: Stripe, Docker, Heroku, Supabase, Segment, PlanetScale, and Apollo [1].

## Founding Team

The founding team for a dev tool should be technical. The product is built for developers, so the founders must be developers [1]. Dessaigne provides a striking statistic: 74% of YC dev tool companies had only technical co-founders, compared to 45% for all other YC companies [1].

A common mistake is thinking the team needs a business co-founder. Dessaigne's own experience at Algolia: "We ended up creating Algolia with two tech co-founders. But it crossed our mind: 'Hey, we don't know how to sell.'" They did not need one. Learning to sell a dev tool is easier than learning to speak the language of developers [1].

## Good vs. Bad Ideas

### Build Time vs. Runtime

The distinction between build-time and runtime ideas is one of the most important filters for dev tool startups [1].

**Build-time ideas** (docs, QA, testing) are tools used when building software. They are "mostly nice to have. You could build your product without them." These are also the most obvious ideas -- every developer encounters them daily -- which creates a crowded market [1].

**Runtime ideas** are tools that run in production. They are "must-have products. If you bet on an API, for example, you cannot run your own product if it's down." Runtime ideas also have a natural usage-based growth dynamic: as customers grow, they use more of the product. "Think about Stripe. If their customers sell more products, they make more money. Everyone is happy" [1].

### Libraries and Frameworks

Libraries and frameworks can be great but are challenging to monetize. Pandas, for example, is widely used but very difficult to make money from. The main monetization path is a hosting service: Next.js and Vercel illustrate this model [1].

### The AI/LLM Wave

The LLM wave has created many new dev tool opportunities, but also extreme noise. LLM observability is the obvious example: "Anyone building with LLMs, they want to know how they are doing. They want to be able to evaluate them. Guess what? Many other companies think the same thing" [1]. Dozens to hundreds of such companies exist. Starting in a crowded space is not fatal, but founders need a clear differentiation plan [1].

A counterpoint: "With the advent of LLMs and AI in general, it's actually very difficult to know for sure today what's a good or bad idea. The bad ideas, the ideas of yesterday, could actually be possible to make successful now thanks to LLMs" [1].

### Common Idea Mistakes

1. **Waiting for the perfect idea.** "You are going to wait for a very long time." Start, learn, and iterate. "You learn as you go, and eventually you'll be able to pull it off. Or you'll change your idea" [1].
2. **Sticking with the wrong idea too long.** 50% of YC companies pivot from their first idea, and this is also true for dev tools [1].
3. **Thinking you need a business founder.** See above [1].

## Building: Prototype to MVP

The two activities at this stage are building and talking with users, in no particular order [1].

### Don't Over-Engineer

"The most important thing here is not to over-engineer it. You should do that quick and dirty" [1]. Dessaigne acknowledges this is hard for experienced engineers who pride themselves on robustness. But the reality: "I would assume that you'll throw away 90% of all the code you write. So your goal at that stage is just to identify the 10% of what you're building that's actually valuable" [1].

### Start Narrow

"It's much better to be 10x better on a very tiny thing for someone who cares about it. Working for a niche is completely okay" [1].

Algolia started as "a glorified autocomplete. Very minimal." The first customer demo used a command line to index content -- no API client, no admin UI, "just a command line and then a very simple webpage to show the search." That was enough to close a $2,000/month contract [1].

### Talk to Users Early

Developers are often introverts who dislike sales. Dessaigne reframes this: "You are a developer yourself. You speak the language of your customers. You are uniquely qualified to understand them" [1]. Do not wait for the product to be ready. "Right away, if you are solving a real problem, then people will be ready to pay for it" [1].

### Finding Users: Outreach and Launches

Nobody knows a new dev tool exists, so inbound will not work initially [1]. Two approaches:

**Outreach:** Start with your network (colleagues, classmates), then expand to friends of friends and LinkedIn. Personalize messages. "Ask yourself: would you be excited to open your message?" [1]. See [[Sales for Founders]].

**Launches:** The best place to launch a dev tool is Hacker News, specifically the "Show HN" section [1]. The key: "Don't do marketing. Don't try to sell your product. Just explain plainly and simply what's new and interesting in what you are building" [1]. Engage with comments, including hostile ones. "Your goal is not to convince them. It's just to convince any other readers who are going to read how you reply to these haters" [1].

**Segment** validated its idea by launching on Hacker News. The post blew up with hundreds of votes and comments, confirming they were onto something [1]. **Ollama** started as a comment on another HN post, attracted interest, then launched its product to massive engagement. They launched again and again every few months: "Each time they had new things to share. And that continued to fuel their growth" [1].

### Common Building Mistakes

- Choosing a tech stack because it is cool rather than because the team is fast with it [1]
- Not talking with users [1]
- Overbuilding before getting feedback [1]
- Misunderstanding developer feedback. If users say "I could build this in a week," ignore it -- "You just built it yourself in a week. You shouldn't mind about that" [1]
- Hiring too early. "Please don't hire anyone until you have convinced yourself you're working on the right idea" [1]

## Open Source as Go-to-Market

Open source is one of the main go-to-market strategies for dev tools [1].

### When You Must Be Open Source

- **Libraries and frameworks.** "Developers wouldn't implement on top of a new framework that is not open source" [1]
- **Products dealing with sensitive data.** A new database or an API connecting to CRM or EHR data needs to be open source [1]

### When Open Source Is Optional

APIs and applications (like bug trackers) do not necessarily need to be open source, though it can serve as differentiation: "You could be the open source version of X" -- PostHog vs. Amplitude, Supabase vs. Firebase, Airbyte vs. Fivetran [1].

### Benefits of Open Source

- Developers prefer open source tools [1]
- Creates community awareness [1]
- Can be a differentiation strategy [1]
- Community contributions (though Dessaigne warns: "Good contributions are actually pretty rare" and managing contributors can be more work than expected) [1]
- Trust, especially with enterprises. Med Plum, an open-source EHR, likely shortened its enterprise sales cycle by a year or more through being open source [1]

### Monetization

Open source must be monetized eventually. Three common approaches [1]:

1. **Hosting/Cloud.** Free self-hosted version, paid cloud version with additional features like team management [1]
2. **Open Core.** An enterprise tier with advanced features not in the open source version: SSO, OAuth, disaster recovery, SLAs [1]
3. **Support and Services.** Dessaigne discourages this: "Your incentive becomes to build the most complex product possible so you can charge for support and services, which is a bad incentive" [1]

### Pricing for Non-Open-Source Dev Tools

Two approaches [1]:

- **Usage-based** (like APIs). Algolia and Stripe charge based on customer usage, with possible volume discounts [1]
- **Good/Better/Best tiers.** "Good" is self-serve for individual developers (low price). "Better" targets engineering managers who care about collaboration (self-serve, team features). "Best" targets CTOs and requires sales -- security, audit logs, SLAs [1]

## Developer Marketing

The goal is awareness that drives inbound [1].

### Find Your Community

Hacker News, relevant subreddits, Discord servers. "Your goal is not to sell your product just yet. Here, just be helpful. Just establish yourself as the expert" [1].

### Launch Repeatedly

Supabase runs "launch weeks" once a quarter, concentrating all news into one week to maximize noise [1].

### Documentation Is Marketing

"Documentation should never be an afterthought. It should be part of the product. Documentation is marketing" [1]. Stripe raised expectations industry-wide. "Developers have become very demanding" [1].

At Algolia: "A feature was not ever done until the docs were done. And documentation should be written by developers" [1]. The documentation team's role was to help developers write better docs, not to write docs for them. They built tools that automatically inserted API keys for logged-in users so copy-pasted code worked immediately [1].

### Support Is Marketing

"Engineers should do support" [1]. Two benefits:

1. Developers interacting with other developers who speak their language are more satisfied. "I have examples where someone would reach out to support, and then the developer doing the support was able to actually fix the bug and deploy in production in like 20 minutes" [1].
2. Engineers doing support better understand customer needs [1].

Algolia waited until they had hundreds of employees before building a dedicated support team, and then asked their best engineering manager to lead it [1].

### Developers Should Do Marketing

Dessaigne's most provocative claim: "I don't know a single dev tool company that is happy with their CMO when they have a traditional marketing background" [1]. Traditional marketers do not understand developers. "Developers really hate being marketed to" [1].

At Algolia, every engineer did a "marketing hack" every month: writing a blog post, speaking at a meetup, or building a hack [1]. "All of our best marketing at Algolia was done by developers" [1].

On developer advocates: the concept is sound (developers in the marketing team) but the role is "ill-defined with often a lot of very fuzzy expectations." Wait until Series A or later, and hire from within or from the community -- contributors or active Discord members make the best candidates [1].

## References

1. [How To Start A Dev Tools Company](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Lu-how-to-start-a-dev-tools-company) -- [[Nicolas Dessaigne]] (2024)

title: Building Products Users Love
body:
# Building Products Users Love

[[Kevin Hale]], co-founder of Wufoo and YC Group Partner, argues that building products users love is fundamentally about relationships. Growth is a function of two variables -- conversion and churn -- and the gap between them determines how fast a company grows [1]. Most founders focus on conversion through calculated, mathematical means. Hale proposes a different lens: treat acquiring new users like dating and retaining existing users like maintaining a successful marriage [1].

Hale's philosophy: "The best way to get to a billion dollars is to focus on the values that help you get that first dollar, to acquire that first user. If you get that right, everything else will take care of itself" [1].

## Wufoo: The Case Study

Wufoo was an online form builder that looked "like it's designed by Fisher Price" [1]. Despite being a database app at its core, the company reached customers across every industry and a majority of the Fortune 500. Wufoo raised only $118,000 total and returned approximately 29x per dollar to investors -- compared to the startup average of $2.5 million raised and 6.76x return [1]. The team was 10 people, all working remotely from Florida with no office, when SurveyMonkey acquired them in 2013 [1].

The company's growth curve over its first five years was driven entirely by word of mouth. Wufoo spent zero dollars on advertising or marketing [1].

## Acquiring Users: The Dating Metaphor

### First Impressions Are Everything

Humans are "relationship manufacturing creatures" who anthropomorphize the tools and software they use [1]. First impressions in products function like origin stories in relationships -- they are told and retold. The threshold for failure is extremely low in first encounters, just as catching someone picking their nose on a first date is disqualifying but the same behavior after 30 years of marriage is forgiven [1].

Most companies identify a few first moments -- the landing page, the marketing site. Hale argues that great product builders discover many more: the first email, the first login, the first error message, the first customer support interaction. "All of those are opportunities to seduce" [1].

### Taken-for-Granted Quality vs. Enchanting Quality

Hale borrows from Japanese design philosophy, which distinguishes two types of quality [1]:

- **Atarimae hinshitsu** ("taken-for-granted quality"): the product works as expected. The pen writes.
- **Miryokuteki hinshitsu** ("enchanting quality"): the product delights beyond function. The pen's weight, ink flow, and the experience of reading the handwriting are all pleasurable.

Enchanting quality is what transforms a product from something people use into something people love [1].

### Examples of Enchanting Quality

Hale provides a series of examples of products that create delight through small, intentional details [1]:

- **Wufoo's login link**: A dinosaur icon with a tooltip that "basically reassures you" rather than explaining what the button does. In usability studies, it universally put a smile on people's faces.
- **Vimeo's login page**: Visually distinctive, signaling "this is going to be something different." Searching for the word "fart" produces fart noises as you scroll.
- **Circa's signup form**: Each field contains witty microcopy. "Email address -- it's also your username and it has to be legit. First name -- what your mom calls you. Password -- something you remember but hard to guess." The form reads like a poem.
- **Heroku's signup page**: Dragging knobs and levers communicates that scaling backend services will be easy and intuitive.
- **Chocolat** (code editor): When the trial expires, all features remain the same except the font changes to Comic Sans -- a punishment precisely calibrated to its developer audience.
- **Stripe's documentation**: If a user is logged in, API credentials are automatically inserted into code examples, eliminating the most common pain point of copy-pasting keys.
- **MailChimp**: Redesigned help guides to look like magazine covers. Readership increased overnight and customer support volume for those topics decreased.

Hale's caution: humor is difficult to execute. "What you want to shoot for is something witty." And functionality must come first -- "if you don't have taken-for-granted quality, don't try to do anything witty, because it will backfire on you" [1].

## Retaining Users: The Marriage Metaphor

### The Gottman Research

John Gottman, a marriage researcher in Seattle, can predict with 85% accuracy whether a couple will divorce within four years by watching 15 minutes of videotape of them fighting. If he extends the tape to an hour and includes discussion of hopes and dreams, accuracy rises to 94% [1]. Marriage counselors, psychiatrists, priests, and successfully married couples shown the same tapes predict at random chance [1].

Gottman's key finding: successfully married couples fight about the same things as everyone else -- money, kids, sex, time, and "others" (jealousy, in-laws). The difference is how they fight [1].

### The Four Horsemen of Customer Churn

Gottman identifies four warning signs that predict relationship failure. Hale maps each directly to customer support dynamics [1]:

1. **Criticism**: The customer moves from specific complaints to sweeping generalizations ("You never listen to users").
2. **Contempt**: The customer is purposefully insulting.
3. **Defensiveness**: The company refuses to take accountability, making excuses instead.
4. **Stonewalling**: The company simply stops responding.

Stonewalling is the most dangerous in startups. Hale sees it constantly: support requests pile up and founders think they do not need to respond. "That act of just not even getting back to them is one of the worst things you can do. And it's probably some of the biggest causes of churn in the early stages of startups" [1].

### The Broken Feedback Loop

A natural evolution occurs in technical startups. Before launch, the founder experiences "bliss, Nirvana, and opportunity" -- every line of code feels perfect [1]. After launch, reality sets in. Other tasks appear: customer support, sales, marketing. Technical founders want to return to the pre-launch state, so they silo off these tasks to other people, treating them as inferior work [1].

This creates a broken feedback loop: the people building the software are divorced from the consequences of their decisions [1].

## Support-Driven Development

Hale's solution is what he calls support-driven development. The principle is simple: everyone in the company does customer support [1].

Paul English at Kayak implemented a version of this by installing a red customer support phone that rang on the engineering floor. When asked why he would pay engineers $120,000+ to do work that could be done by call center employees at a fraction of the cost, English replied: "After the second or third time that phone rings and the engineer gets the same problem, they stop what they're doing, they fix the bug, and we stop getting phone calls about it" [1].

### How It Worked at Wufoo

When Wufoo was acquired, 500,000 users were on the system, with 5 million people using Wufoo forms. All support came from the same 10-person team, with usually one person dedicated to support per day. Response times: 7-12 minutes during 9 AM to 9 PM, one hour from 9 PM to midnight, and no longer than 24 hours on weekends [1].

### The Emotional State Dropdown

Wufoo ran an experiment: they added a dropdown to their support form asking "What's your emotional state?" The hypothesis was that nobody would fill it out [1].

The result: the emotional state field was filled out 75.8% of the time -- nearly identical to the browser type dropdown at 78.1%. "People were basically telling us: how I feel about this problem is just as important as all the technical details you need to debug it" [1].

An unexpected byproduct: people started being nicer in support tickets. Text analysis showed decreases in exclamation marks, curse words, and all caps. "Once people had a simple outlet for their emotions, people were a lot more rational and made our jobs a lot more pleasant" [1].

### The Research Behind Direct Exposure

Jared Spool at User Interface Engineering found a direct correlation between time spent directly exposed to users and design quality [1]. The exposure must be:
- **Direct** (not through reports or graphs)
- **Minimum every six weeks**
- **At least two hours per session**

Otherwise, software quality degrades over time. Wufoo developers were exposed to users four to eight hours every week through customer support [1].

## Reducing Churn Through Knowledge Gap Reduction

Hale uses Spool's knowledge gap framework (also referenced in [[Conversion Rates]]). Every interface problem reduces to a gap between the user's current knowledge and the target knowledge needed to use the product. There are only two ways to close the gap: increase the user's knowledge or decrease the knowledge required [1].

Engineers instinctively want to add features, which only increases the knowledge gap. Wufoo focused on the opposite direction: 30% of engineering time went to internal tools for customer support and self-service help. One documentation redesign reduced customer support volume by 30% overnight [1].

## Keeping the Passion Alive

Gottman found that some couples stayed together 10-15 years, then suddenly divorced with no prior warning signs. The cause: no passion. "When it comes to relationships, they kind of follow the second law of thermodynamics. In a closed energy system, things tend to run down" [1].

### The "Since You've Been Gone" Feature

Wufoo built an alert system that timestamped every new feature. When a user logged in, the system calculated the difference between their last login and current time, then displayed: "Hey, since you've been gone, here's all the awesome stuff that Wufoo did for you" [1].

This became the most talked-about feature Hale ever shipped. Users told him: "Even though I pay the same amount every single month, you guys are doing something for me almost every week. It makes me feel I'm getting maximum value" [1].

### Handwritten Thank You Notes

Every Friday, the Wufoo team gathered to write handwritten thank you notes to users -- simple cards on index cards with a sticker and a dinosaur [1].

The practice originated from co-founder Chris Ryan's suggestion. They wrote handwritten Christmas cards to all users the first year. The second year, they had too many users and switched to writing only to highest-paying customers. In January, a longtime user wrote: "I really loved that Christmas card you sent me the first year. I just wanted you to know I haven't received my second one yet, and I'm looking forward to it" [1].

Hale's lesson: "The best way to exceed expectations is not to send any to begin with." They shifted thank you notes from an annual event to a weekly ritual [1].

## The Three Paths to Market Dominance

Hale closes with research from Michael Treacy and Fred Wiersema in the Harvard Business Review, identifying three paths to market dominance [1]:

1. **Best price** -- focus on logistics (Walmart, Amazon)
2. **Best product** -- focus on R&D (Apple)
3. **Best overall solution** -- focus on customer intimacy (luxury brands, hospitality)

The third path -- customer intimacy -- "is the only one that everyone can do at any stage of their company. It requires almost no money to get started with -- usually just requires a little bit of humility and some manners" [1].

## The King for a Day Ritual

Hale dislikes internal hackathons because 99% of projects never reach production. Wufoo replaced hackathons with "King for a Day": a randomly selected employee got to direct the entire company's resources over a weekend to build whatever they wanted -- features, fixes, improvements. The "king" directed engineering, marketing, and design resources toward their priorities [1].

The practice boosted morale because "what people most love is working on things where it's like, 'I made a difference to the app'" [1].

## Remote Work at Wufoo

Wufoo operated with a four-and-a-half-day work week. Half of Friday was reserved for all meetings and business discussions. One day per week was dedicated to customer support. This left three days of focused building time [1].

The 15-minute rule governed the three building days: any discussion between team members was capped at 15 minutes. If unresolved, the item was tabled until Friday. Hale reports that 90% of the time, tabled items were never brought up because "people would sleep on it and then they just magically say, 'Hey, I found a solution'" [1].

Accountability was managed through shared to-do lists in simple text files stored in Dropbox. Every night, each person logged what they did. Every Friday, they reviewed: "This is what you said last week you were going to do. This is what you actually got done" [1].

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Building Products Users Love](https://ycombinator.com/library/JK-how-to-start-a-startup-building-products-users-love) -- [[Kevin Hale]] (2014)

title: University Spinouts
body:
# University Spinouts

[[Jared Friedman]], YC partner, provides advice for people who have done scientific research at a university and are considering starting a company to commercialize it. YC has funded more than 75 companies in this situation. YC also conducted a [Bio Tour](https://blog.ycombinator.com/yc-bio-tour-2020/) visiting research universities to talk with hundreds of students and professors in the life sciences about commercialization [1].

## Deciding Who Should Spin Out

In a typical spinout, several people worked on the research -- a mix of students, post-docs, and faculty. The first decision is who works on the company and who stays at the university. Friedman identifies four common misconceptions [1]:

### Misconception 1: You Can Start a Company While Continuing Your Academic Career

"Your company has little chance of success unless someone who worked on the original research is willing to leave their university role to start this company" [1]. Students graduating soon can wait until graduation -- a perfect time to start. Otherwise, an intentional decision to leave must be made at some point [1].

### Misconception 2: You Should Find a CEO to Run the Company

Scientists who remain in academia and try to find a CEO to build a company around their invention almost always fail. Great CEOs are scarce and have better opportunities than running an idea-stage startup with no funding. Most scientists either never find a CEO or settle for a mediocre one. It is even worse if the university tries to find a CEO [1].

Even with any person in the world available, an outside CEO would likely be a bad choice. The people who did the original research are far more invested and far more qualified -- their domain knowledge is more valuable than general business skills. The common belief that "the research is done and all that's left is commercialization" rarely holds. Usually the market wants something different from what was invented, requiring more research. Original inventors can adjust; an outside CEO will be stuck [1].

### Misconception 3: You Need Someone With Business Experience on the Founding Team

"In the first couple of years, there is typically very little 'business' to be done, and whatever business skills you need you will pick up along the way" [1]. Most scientists YC funds have no prior business experience. "People who work in business like to make it sound hard, as if business were like quantum physics, a field that needed to be studied for years to master. The fact is, it's not even close" [1].

### Misconception 4: You Should Raise Money First, Then Leave the University

Scientists often pitch to local VCs hoping for validation and a smooth transition. VCs occasionally fund spinouts this way but usually do not. Founders who get rejected conclude the idea is bad and give up, when the actual issue is that it is too early for VC money [1].

Typically founders need to work full-time for 1+ years before a multi-million dollar VC round is feasible. In the meantime, they sustain themselves through savings, government grants, friends and family, or small pre-seed rounds from angels, accelerators, or seed funds [1].

Founders who refuse to quit before raising face a catch-22: "They are waiting for an investor to take a bet on them before they quit their job. But the investors are waiting for the founders to believe enough in their own company to quit their job" [1].

### The Recommended Approach

The ideal situation: two or more people from the lab who did the work leave together as cofounders. One full-time founder is also acceptable. One of the people who leave should be CEO. Others who remain at the university can serve as "academic cofounders" or "scientific cofounders" and contribute, but the full-time founders are the most important [1].

## Deciding When to Spin Out

In the early stages of developing a new technology, progress is faster at the university, taking advantage of university resources. At some point that flips, and being at the university slows things down because universities are not set up to commercialize technologies [1].

Leaving too early is possible, but the far more common mistake is waiting too long. Most founders delay because leaving is scary. "No one is pressuring you to leave and leaving seems risky so the natural thing to do is to keep delaying it" [1]. The temptation is to make the technology perfect before spinning out, with always "one more experiment" to do. Programs like NSF I-Corps can test market demand from inside the university [1].

After leaving, founders often realize that much of the last year's work was wasted because assumptions about market needs were wrong, and that they are now moving so much faster that they could have saved months by spinning out a year earlier [1].

## Splitting Equity

Friedman's framework has two rules [1]:

1. **Full-time founders get equal or nearly equal equity.** (He references [[Michael Seibel]]'s [essay](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5x-how-to-split-equity-among-co-founders) on this.)
2. **Full-time founders get much more than academic cofounders.** Academic cofounders should typically own no more than 10% unless they remain hands-on.

The biggest conceptual mistake: thinking equity allocation rewards past contributions, when it actually anticipates future ones. "If you are going to make a new company successful, you will probably have to work on it for 7-10 years post spin-out" [1].

Friedman uses a marathon analogy: at spinout, founders may feel halfway done, but they are actually on mile 2 of 26. Academic founders may have been instrumental in mile 1, but full-time founders take the other 25. The equity split must reflect expected contributions over the entire marathon [1].

This means equity in the new company may have no relation to academic seniority. Junior people who leave often get much more equity than their former senior faculty boss. "This can be an awkward conversation, but it's entirely sensible" [1].

## Negotiating with Tech Transfer Offices

Commercializing university research requires negotiating intellectual property rights with the technology transfer office (TTO) [1].

TTOs historically had a bad reputation for being slow, bureaucratic, and forcing onerous terms that strangled young startups. Things have improved. TTOs at universities in major startup hubs (Harvard, MIT, Stanford) now give startups reasonable terms, though they still take too long. At universities with fewer successful spinouts, quality is hit-or-miss. Some universities now use "[express license agreements](https://ctl.cornell.edu/entrepreneurs/fasttrack/)" -- preset agreements requiring little to no negotiation [1].

### Four Key Terms

1. **Equity**: University gets equity in the company. 3-5% is typical and acceptable. Above 10% causes problems [1].
2. **Royalty**: Percentage of revenue or profits paid to the university. If too high, it affects the company's ability to raise money and operate. Ideally zero; keep it as low as possible [1].
3. **Milestone payments** and **sublicensing terms** (details truncated in source) [1].

## References

1. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) -- [[Jared Friedman]] (n.d.)

title: Building Movements
related: [[Coinbase: From Bitcoin at Five Dollars to a Hundred Billion Dollar Company]]
body:
# Building Movements

[[Garry Tan]] argues that every billion-dollar startup begins as a movement -- "a team and a direction and a thing that helps millions and possibly billions of people" [1]. He draws parallels between punk rock, the personal computer revolution, and startup founding to explain how movements emerge, grow, and eventually change the world.

## Steve Jobs on Shaping the World

Tan opens with Jobs's 1994 observation: "Everything around you that you call life was made up by people that were no smarter than you. The minute that you understand that you can poke life and actually something will pop out the other side -- that you can change it, you can mold it -- that's maybe the most important thing" [1].

## Stage One: Emergence

Movements begin as "social ferment" -- small groups of passionate people working on something nobody else cares about [1]. The personal computer started this way: in the 1970s, only governments, universities, and large businesses had computers. Hobbyist clubs like the Homebrew Computer Club in San Francisco were where enthusiasts showed off home-brewed machines. "From these modest beginnings came a series of startup companies" [1].

Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak were two of those hobbyists. Wozniak designed the computer; Jobs worried about which parts to use and how to make it buildable. "I was not designing a computer with any idea we'd ever start a company. It was just to go down to the club and show off" [1]. Jobs saw the interest and started thinking about how to turn it into a product.

Tan distills the lesson: "What do you want that nobody else cares about yet? What problem do you see first? Try to solve it for yourself. Then you might find that a lot of other people need it too" [1].

## The First Follower

Citing Derek Sivers's TED talk, Tan argues that the first follower is more important than the leader: "The first follower is what transforms a lone nut into a leader" [1]. Critical dynamics:

- The leader must embrace the first follower as an equal -- "So now it's not about the leader anymore. It's about them, plural" [1].
- New followers emulate followers, not the leader [1].
- Three is a crowd, and a crowd is news. After three, there is momentum [1].
- As more people join, it becomes less risky for fence-sitters to participate [1].

The implication for startups: "You could be the first leader or the first follower, but either way, you've got to have intrinsic motivation -- really wanting it from the bottom of your heart, not because someone else told you to" [1].

## DIY: Do It Yourself

Tan draws the deepest parallel between punk rock and startups through the DIY aesthetic. Punk rock's power was not musical complexity (many songs used only three chords) but authenticity and self-sufficiency [1]:

- "You don't wait for someone to give you something. You don't wait for someone to teach you how to play. You don't wait for a record deal. You just pick up a guitar and you do it. You pick up a keyboard and you start writing code" [1].
- Kurt Cobain of Nirvana: "We usually just play really cheap equipment, and a lot of times before a show we're scrambling for guitar parts and gluing things together just to have a guitar for that night" [1].

Tan connects this directly to [[Brian Armstrong]] building the first version of Coinbase with Twitter Bootstrap -- "he begged, borrowed, and stole the skills necessary to get a website up. He didn't look for a designer. He used the free, open-source Twitter Bootstrap library, and it worked great" [1].

The principle: "Humans have a visceral reaction to experiencing authenticity, even at the cost of perfection. Everyone is so focused on being perfect these days, and to that I say, fuck perfection. Do it yourself" [1].

## You Don't Need Permission

Tan identifies waiting for perfect conditions as the most common obstacle: "I can't tell you how often it is that people waiting for perfect conditions is what actually prevents them from starting in the first place" [1].

His historical examples: "If Steve Jobs looked to Hewlett-Packard for a playbook, he never would have started Apple. If Brian Armstrong looked to big finance for approval, he never would have started Coinbase. If the Velvet Underground just copied whatever was mainstream at the time, the rock and roll greats like the Ramones and Nirvana would never have emerged. To be first, you have to really want it. You can't ask for permission" [1].

## References

1. [How REAL Movements Start, Then Change the World](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jm-garry-s-channel-how-real-movements-start-then-change-the-world) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: Founder Mindset
body:
# Founder Mindset

[[Paul Graham]] returns repeatedly across a decade of essays to the question of what makes a good startup founder. His answer is consistent: determination matters more than intelligence, and the best two-word description of the ideal founder is "relentlessly resourceful" [1]. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Garry Tan]] extend this with complementary frameworks: Caldwell on conviction as the foundation for all persuasion [7], and Tan on the tension between visionary belief and radical honesty about reality [8].

## Relentlessly Resourceful

In a March 2009 essay, Graham distilled the quality he looks for in founders into two words [1]. The opposite of a good founder is "hapless" -- passive, battered by circumstances [1].

A good founder is not merely relentless but resourceful. In any interesting domain, the difficulties are novel. "You don't know initially how hard they are; you don't know whether you're about to plow through a block of foam or granite. So you have to be resourceful. You have to keep trying new things" [1].

Graham makes the relationship between his two most famous pieces of advice explicit: "'Make something people want' is the destination, but 'Be relentlessly resourceful' is how you get there" [1].

## Self-Conviction as Foundation

[[Dalton Caldwell]] argues that the founder mindset begins with genuine belief in what you are building [7]. "If you're not fully committed or if you have grave doubts that your idea is any good, how can you ever convince someone else? And people can tell if you don't believe in the thing you're doing" [7].

Caldwell has observed two patterns at YC: teams that secretly do not believe give up when they hit rough patches, while teams with deep conviction "overcome almost every kind of setback you can overcome -- you can run out of money and somehow survive, you could have to completely pivot and change your idea" [7].

The practical test for conviction: "Do you ever know that feeling where an idea enters your head and you can't sleep at night and you can't think about anything else?" [7]. If yes, pursue it -- even over ideas that seem easier to raise money for or more commercially viable. "It's equally hard to succeed in a startup that you don't care about and one that you do" [7]. See [[Recruiting Early Team Members]] for the full treatment.

## Confronting Reality While Holding the Vision

[[Garry Tan]] adds a critical dimension: the founder mindset requires holding two contradictory beliefs simultaneously [8]. You must believe in a future that does not yet exist (the "dreamland"), while maintaining honest assessment of present reality.

Tan experienced the failure mode firsthand at Posterous. After two years of 20-30% monthly growth, growth went flat. He could not accept it. "I looked for statistics that were indicating we might still be okay. It was just a dip" [8]. The result: denial, then death.

The solution is not to abandon the vision but to surround yourself with people who challenge your thinking. Tan draws on Ray Dalio: "Whatever success I've had in life has had to do more with my ability to know how to deal with my not knowing than even the things I know" [8]. See [[Confronting Reality]] for the full treatment.

## Determination Over Intelligence

Graham's 2006 essay states it plainly: "The most important quality in a startup founder is determination. Not intelligence -- determination" [2]. [[Elon Musk]] exemplifies this through deliberate fatalism -- accepting that SpaceX had less than a 10% chance of success and proceeding anyway because the goal was important enough. [[Diane Greene]] frames the same idea through sailing: "you give it your all, not because you're supposed to, not because that's what makes you win, but because you have respect for your goal and you enjoy the process."

For [[First-Time Founders]], the mindset challenge is distinct: inexperience can be an advantage (fresh eyes, willingness to try unconventional approaches) but first-timers must resist the urge to compare themselves to repeat founders with existing networks and track records.

"You can lose quite a lot in the brains department and it won't kill you. But lose even a little bit in the commitment department, and that will kill you very rapidly" [2].

## The Half-Hearted Effort Problem

The most common type of startup failure is not the spectacular flameout but the one that never really gets going [5]. Graham argues that statistically, if you want to avoid failure, the most important thing is to quit your day job. Most founders of failed startups don't quit their day jobs; most founders of successful ones do [5].

"Most startups fail because they don't make something people want, and the reason most don't is that they don't try hard enough" [5].


## Persistence as the Defining Trait: New Case Studies

Four new sources independently reinforce persistence as the defining trait of successful founders:

**[[Ooshma Garg]]** spent five and a half years iterating through four product versions of Gobble before finding product-market fit. She nearly ran out of money twice, failed to raise Series A twice, and was publicly mocked by a journalist. [[Paul Graham]] tweeted: "Ooshma Garg does not give up" [9].

**[[Anne Wojcicki]]** persisted with 23andMe for over a decade. [[Sam Altman]] adds: "That is the secret to overnight success is ten years of persistence" [10].

**[[Austen Allred]]** was told by a past investor: "You don't have any marketable skills, you're never going to make more than 60,000 dollars a year." He made sure his next job offer was double that "just out of spite" and went on to build Lambda School [11].

**[[Tracy Young]]** describes the psychological toll of scaling PlanGrid over seven years. Her framework for survival: "Our ability to manage our own emotions is actually a big key to just surviving the next day" [12].

## Negative Ego

Young's executive coach identified a pattern called "negative ego" -- constant self-diminishment. Young felt guilty about mistakes instead of fixing them. Before every board meeting, she was convinced the board would fire her, even when the company was growing [12]. The antidote was becoming more authentic over time [12].

## The Founder as Last Line

Garg describes the loneliness of founder knowledge: at her lowest point, she had just made the last full payroll she could afford. "Nobody knew this but me. Starting and running a company, as a founder, is a very lonely experience" [9].

## The Evangelical Founder (Sam Altman)

[[Sam Altman]] identifies a specific founder trait: the evangelical founder, usually the CEO, who "recruits, sells the product, talks to the press, raises money" [13]. This person "infects with enthusiasm the whole world about what the company is trying to do." An ambitious vision supports this evangelism: "Ambitious visions are exciting. They're fun to work on." In 2018, Altman argues, it is easier to start a hard startup than an easy one [13].

## Confident and Definite View of the Future

Altman observes that the best founders have "a confident and definite view of the future. They may be wrong. And so we say, it's good to be confident and flexible" [13].

## The Blessing of Inexperience

Altman describes what YC calls "the blessing of inexperience": startups that accomplish things because "no one told them it was hard." He quotes Steve Wozniak: "All the best things he ever did came from having no experience whatsoever and having no money" [13].

## Never Lose Momentum

Altman identifies momentum as one of the most critical founder responsibilities. "If you have momentum, people keep delivering results beyond what they think they're capable of. If you lose momentum, it's very difficult to get it back" [13].

## Frugality, Focus, Obsession, and Love

Altman credits Paul Buchheit with distilling the traits of the best YC founders into four words: "frugality, focus, obsession, and love" [13].

## Resilience Over Confidence (Harj Taggar)

[[Harj Taggar]] adds a diagnostic insight: confidence is not a reliable proxy for founder resilience. He has seen "someone could appear very confident initially, but not be resilient when their startup hit roadblocks" -- and conversely, "some of the quietest and least confident seeming founders at first turned out to be the most resilient" [14]. See [[Becoming a Founder]] for the full treatment.

# Founder Mindset

## The Builder Ethos (Garry Tan, Brian Armstrong)

[[Garry Tan]] reinforces the builder mentality through his investment criteria. He funded Coinbase because [[Brian Armstrong]] was "a builder" who had coded the entire first version himself and secured it himself [15]. Armstrong had been head of anti-fraud at Airbnb -- "being part of the special forces" against hackers daily [15].

Armstrong extends the principle: almost no one gets the first version of a product right. If you hire a firm to build it and get version one wrong (which "everybody does"), then version two wrong, "you're on the third version, you're out of money and you're dead." Building it yourself lets you iterate cheaply [15].

Tan describes the builder mindset as what separates wealth creators from wage earners: "The people most likely to grasp that wealth can be created are the ones who are good at making things -- the crafts people who can create it. Being an engineer, designer, or product person is the easiest way to be a craftsperson in modern society" [16].

## DIY and Authenticity

Tan draws a parallel between punk rock's DIY ethic and startup founding: "You don't wait for someone to give you something. You don't wait for someone to teach you how to play. You don't wait for a record deal. You just pick up a guitar and you do it. You pick up a keyboard and you start writing code" [17].

Armstrong exemplified this by building Coinbase with Twitter Bootstrap: "He begged, borrowed, and stole the skills necessary to get a website up" [17]. Nirvana's many songs had only three chords, but they became one of the most influential groups in history [17].

The principle: "Humans have a visceral reaction to experiencing authenticity, even at the cost of perfection" [17]. Waiting for perfect conditions is the most common obstacle: "I can't tell you how often it is that people waiting for perfect conditions is what actually prevents them from starting in the first place" [17].

## Persistence Across Failures

Armstrong tried approximately ten different startups. Eight of nine failed. "Try something, try anything. It almost certainly won't work, but you'll learn something. Try something else. It almost certainly won't work. Try it again" [15].

## The Six Skills That Increase Founder Success

Tan identifies engineering, product, design, sales/marketing, finance, and leadership as the six skills that make startup success more likely. "You don't have to have all of these things figured out. Starting a startup isn't deterministic like that. You're taking risk, and these skills make it a more calculated risk" [16]. See [[Six Founder Skills]] for the full treatment.

## References (new)

15. [Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong on Cryptocurrency and the Future of Decentralization](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jk-garry-s-channel-coinbase-ceo-brian-armstrong-on-cryptocurrency-and-the-future-of-decentralization) -- [[Brian Armstrong]], [[Garry Tan]] (2021)
16. [6 Skills for Successful Startup Founders](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jl-garry-s-channel-6-skills-for-successful-startup-founders) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)
17. [How REAL Movements Start, Then Change the World](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jm-garry-s-channel-how-real-movements-start-then-change-the-world) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

# Founder Mindset

## Startups Are Counterintuitive (Paul Graham)

In his Stanford "How to Start a Startup" lecture, [[Paul Graham]] frames the entire founder mindset challenge as a problem of counterintuitive knowledge [15]. "Startups are very counterintuitive. And I'm not sure exactly why. It could be simply because knowledge about them has not permeated our culture yet" [15].

Graham compares startups to skiing: "When you first try skiing and you want to slow down, your first impulse is to lean back, just like in everything else. But lean back on skis and you fly down the hill out of control" [15]. Startups similarly require suppressing natural instincts. "If you remember nothing more than that -- when you're about to make a mistake, you may at least pause before making it" [15].

At YC, "we used to joke that our function was to tell founders things they would ignore." Batch after batch, partners warn founders about mistakes, founders ignore them, and they return a year later saying "I wish we'd listened" [15].

### Trust Your Instincts About People

There is one exception to the counterintuitive rule: people. "Your life so far hasn't been much like starting a startup, but all the interactions you've had with people are just like the interactions you'll have with people in the business world" [15].

Graham warns that founders -- especially those from engineering backgrounds -- mistrust their own read on people because they assume business is "supposed to be this sort of slightly distasteful thing." When they meet someone smart but distasteful, they assume that is normal. It is not. "Pick people the way you would pick people if you were picking friends" [15].

### Gaming the System Stops Working

Graham identifies a core mindset shift: "Starting a startup is where gaming the system stops working" [15]. Students have been trained for their entire lives to optimize for artificial tests -- extracurricular activities, exam questions, the right-sounding answers. Graham himself gamed college exams by predicting questions rather than mastering material [15].

This instinct carries into startups as "playing house" -- founders "go through the motions of starting a startup" because that is what 20 years of education trained them to do. "How do I convince investors?" becomes the new exam question. The YC partners' answer: "The best way to convince investors is to start a startup that's actually doing well, meaning growing fast, and then simply tell investors so" [15].

The uncomfortable implication: "There is no boss to trick. There's only users. And all users care about is whether your software does what they want. They're like sharks. Sharks are too stupid to fool. You can't wave a red flag at a shark and fool it. It's meat or no meat" [15].

Graham acknowledges this loss is real: "You're deprived of one of your most powerful weapons, something you have spent 20 years mastering." But he finds it exciting: "There even exist parts of the world where gaming the system is not how you win" [15].

### Not Expertise in Startups

Graham's second counterintuitive point: "What you need to succeed in a startup is not expertise in startups. What you need is expertise in your own users" [15]. He cites Mark Zuckerberg, who "succeeded despite being a complete noob at startups" because he understood college social dynamics. "If he was even paying attention when Ron Conway wrote him the big check, he has probably forgotten about it by now" [15].

Graham worries that studying startup mechanics may be "possibly somewhat dangerous" because it encourages "playing house" -- the imitation of external forms without the substance [15].

## References

1. [Be Relentlessly Resourceful](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/94-be-relentlessly-resourceful) -- [[Paul Graham]] (March 2009)
2. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
3. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (March 2007)
4. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)
5. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
6. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
7. [How Do You Convince Someone to Join Your Startup?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5b-how-do-you-convince-someone-to-join-your-startup) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (n.d.)
8. [The Main Function: How to Go from Startup Dream to Reality](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/JG-the-main-function-how-to-go-from-startup-dream-to-reality) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)
9. [On starting and scaling meal kit company Gobble](https://ycombinator.com/library/69-on-starting-and-scaling-meal-kit-company-gobble) -- [[Ooshma Garg]] (2016)
10. [On starting and scaling healthcare startup 23andMe](https://ycombinator.com/library/5I-on-starting-and-scaling-healthcare-startup-23andme) -- [[Anne Wojcicki]], [[Sam Altman]] (2018)
11. [On starting and scaling Bloom Institute of Technology](https://ycombinator.com/library/5N-on-starting-and-scaling-bloom-institute-of-technology) -- [[Austen Allred]] (2019)
12. [On starting and scaling construction startup PlanGrid](https://ycombinator.com/library/6L-on-starting-and-scaling-construction-startup-plangrid) -- [[Tracy Young]], Kat Manalac (2019)
13. [How to Succeed with a Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/89-how-to-succeed-with-a-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (August 2018)
14. [Should You Start a Startup?](https://ycombinator.com/library/GV-should-you-start-a-startup) -- [[Harj Taggar]] (2022)
15. [How to Start a Startup: Before the Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/JI-how-to-start-a-startup-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (2014)

title: Biotech Startup Funding
body:
# Biotech Startup Funding

[[Jared Friedman]], YC partner, argues that biotech startup fundraising in 2019 looks like tech startup fundraising looked in 2005, and the same forces that transformed tech fundraising are now transforming biotech [1]. The parallel is structural: new infrastructure has collapsed the cost to start, which enables new funding models, which shifts power to founders.

## How Tech Fundraising Changed (2005-2019)

In 2005, when YC started, venture capital access was limited.
