VCs preferred companies that already seemed like a sure bet, favored MBAs with executive experience over unproven technical founders, asked for onerous financial terms, and often replaced founders with favored executives. The only model of institutional seed funding was the "business incubator" where VC firms funded well-connected founders and incubated them in their offices [1].

Then the cost to start a tech company plummeted. Open source software, modern web frameworks, SaaS developer tools, cloud hosting, and better distribution channels meant technical founders could launch a product and get users with minimal funding. Once they proved the idea had merit, they could use traction to raise capital [1].

These companies needed only a small amount of money to get started, but no institutional investors made small investments. This insight led to the creation of YC and hundreds of institutional seed funds. Easy access to flexible institutional seed funding led to an explosion of tech startups [1].

Because founders raised VC only when they were further along and had leverage, the balance of power shifted. Founders increasingly retained control. Investors lost the power to fire founders and bring in executives. And when founders did run their companies, investors discovered something surprising: "despite their inexperience, the founders were often the right people to run the company" [1]. (Friedman notes this trend was accelerated by institutional seed funding, building on the logic Ben Horowitz [articulated in 2010](https://a16z.com/2010/04/28/why-we-prefer-founding-ceos/) about preferring founding CEOs.)

## The Biotech Parallel

Early-stage biotech funding in 2019 is dominated by the "venture creation model." The VC firm creates the company, assembles a team of favored executives (often from their entrepreneur-in-residence pool), incubates the startup out of the VC's offices, invests a large amount of money upfront, and takes a controlling ownership stake [1].

This model made sense when the cost to start a biotech company was high -- when no one could make progress before a VC wrote a $10M check. But new infrastructure has brought down the cost of doing biology dramatically. Founders can now make real progress proving a concept for as little as $100K [1].

### Infrastructure That Lowered Costs

Friedman catalogs the new infrastructure [1]:

- **Low-cost CROs** (contract research organizations): [WuXi Biologics](http://wuxibiologics.com), [Evotec](http://evotec.com), [ChemPartner](http://chempartner.com)
- **CRO access platforms**: [Science Exchange](https://www.scienceexchange.com/) for instantaneous, cost-effective access to CROs and scientific supplies
- **Lab space by the bench**: [MBC BioLabs](https://mbcbiolabs.com/), [Berkeley BioLabs](http://www.berkeleybiolabs.com/)
- **Lab management**: [Quartzy](https://www.quartzy.com/), [Happilabs](https://www.happilabs.org/)
- **Lab automation**: Affordable lab robots from [OpenTrons](https://opentrons.com/) for automating batch experiments
- **Computational drug discovery**: [Atomwise](https://www.atomwise.com/) for in silico experiments
- **Patent filing**: [Cognition IP](https://www.cognitionip.com/) for lower-cost patents
- **FDA submission**: [Enzyme](https://www.enzyme.com/) for streamlined regulatory submission

### YC Bio Companies as Evidence

Bio companies routinely clear major scientific hurdles during YC's short program. Therapeutics companies show effectiveness in animal models. Diagnostic companies show success with human samples. Synthetic biology companies successfully engineer cell lines [1].

**Shasqi** (2015): Jose Mejia Oneto, an MD/PhD who left orthopaedic surgery residency, had developed a technique for localizing chemotherapy delivery in academia but had not tried it in animal therapeutics. Using just YC's funding, he showed in less than three months in a breast cancer mouse model that localized delivery outperformed conventional chemo [1].

**Athelas**: Founders Tanay Tandon and Deepika started the company while still in college, making a device for at-home blood tests for oncology patients using computer vision. They built a working prototype with $40K in investment. During YC they ran a 350-patient initial study showing very good results. The device is now FDA-cleared and serves thousands of patients [1].

Friedman notes that running clinical trials for drugs remains very expensive, and biotech companies ultimately need to raise large amounts. But this is not different from top tech companies: the biggest YC software companies each raised over $1B. The key is that these companies could get started with less than $100K and de-risk enough to raise more [1].

## Predictions

Because startups can start cheaply, the tech startup model is now available for biotech: raise money incrementally, keep control, and work on your own idea rather than VC-originated ideas [1].

This path has drawn a new kind of biotech founder. Many are grad students or postdocs whose previous options were staying in academia or joining big pharma. Starting a company is now a viable third option [1].

If the pattern plays out as it did in tech [1]:
- An explosion of funding options for biotech companies
- Traditional biotech investors' controlling legal terms will go out of vogue, as happened in tech in the early 2000s
- A new crop of biotech and tech/biotech crossover seed funds will create a vibrant seed ecosystem (YC bio companies already typically raise $1-5M seed rounds after each batch)
- More biotech companies will be run by founders who care about their ideas, not by VCs and hired executives

## References

1. [How Biotech Startup Funding Will Change in the Next 10 Years](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4L-how-biotech-startup-funding-will-change-in-the-next-10-years) — [[Jared Friedman]] (February 2019)

title: China Tech Ecosystem
body:
# China Tech Ecosystem

The Chinese technology ecosystem has produced several of the world's most innovative consumer technology companies, many of which pioneered product strategies years before Western equivalents adopted them. [[Anu Hariharan]] has documented three Chinese tech case studies in detail for the YC Library: [[WeChat]] (messaging as a platform), [[Pinduoduo]] (social commerce), and [[Toutiao]] (AI-powered content). Together, these case studies reveal patterns that are instructive for founders globally.

## Key Structural Differences from the US Market

### Mobile Leapfrogging

China's mobile revolution was more dramatic than the West's. Smartphone penetration went from near zero in 2010 to 65% by 2014 [3]. Many consumers skipped the desktop internet entirely, creating massive demand for mobile-first services. Toutiao exploited this gap: by mid-2012, there were only six significant news apps on the Chinese Android platform [3].

### Platform Openness

[[WeChat]] functions as the operating system of Chinese mobile life, and Tencent has been willing to let third-party services grow on top of it. Pinduoduo's rapid growth was significantly enabled by access to WeChat's social graph and messaging infrastructure [1]. This contrasts with the US, where Hariharan notes that "it is highly unlikely that Facebook would allow a social commerce application like Pinduoduo to be built on top of Messenger or Instagram" [1].

### QR Code Adoption

QR codes became the bridge between offline and online commerce in China through WeChat, but failed in the US for three reasons: the dominant phone and social app did not support scanning, users needed dedicated scanner apps, and early use cases focused on spam-like marketing content [2].

### Unorganized Retail

More than 80% of retail in China's developing cities is unorganized [1]. This creates a "trust deficit" that social commerce platforms address by embedding purchases within social relationships. WeChat Red Packets similarly built payment adoption through trusted social circles first, then extended to merchants [2].

## Recurring Growth Patterns

Three patterns appear across all three case studies:

### 1. Self-Cannibalization

Tencent launched WeChat as a potential competitor to its own QQ, with a small skunkworks team of seven engineers from the QQ Mail division [2]. This echoes Amazon's self-cannibalization with Kindle and Uber's move from town cars to UberX. The lesson: "If you don't create a culture of disrupting yourself, then a competitor is likely to disrupt you" [2].

### 2. Group-Level Network Effects

Both [[WeChat]] and [[Pinduoduo]] designed features around group behavior rather than individual behavior [1] [2]. WeChat's Allen Zhang was "adamant about developing the product around 'Group Effect'" [2], and Pinduoduo's team purchase model follows Reed's law (value proportional to group formation ease) [1]. Toutiao achieved similar engagement levels without a social graph, but its content lifecycle creates an analogous data network effect [3].

### 3. Subtle Monetization

All three platforms aligned monetization with user experience rather than extracting value from it. WeChat limited Moments ads to one per day [2]. Toutiao's ad targeting uses the same recommendation engine as content, making ads feel like product discovery [3]. Pinduoduo's gamification features tie engagement to commerce without requiring immediate purchases [1].

## Implications for Western Founders

Hariharan consistently argues that Chinese tech innovations preview global trends. The success of social commerce "will be replicated by others globally" [1]. Content recommendation engines like Toutiao's suggest that "content aggregators" may be "an idea whose time is yet to come -- and that better algorithms will be the catalyst for success" [3].

The specific mechanics (team purchase, Red Packets, QR codes) may be culturally specific, but the underlying insights about social dynamics in commerce, group-level network effects, and AI-driven personalization are "likely entirely universal" [1].

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) — [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne (2020)
2. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) — [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)
3. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) — [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)

title: Equity Split
body:
# Equity Split

Equity split refers to how ownership of a startup is divided among co-founders and early contributors. It is one of the most consequential early decisions a startup makes and one of the most common sources of [[Co-Founders|co-founder]] conflict.

## The Default: Equal Splits

Y Combinator's general guidance favors equal or near-equal splits among co-founders. [[Michael Seibel]] presents the case for this position in detail, drawing on his own experience across multiple startups [3]. [[Kirsty Nathoo]], YC's Managing Director of Finance, reinforces this position from the operational side, noting that equity disputes are the number one reason YC sees co-founders break up [6].

### Why Founders Get It Wrong

Seibel identifies common reasons founders cite for unequal equity splits, and argues that all of them fail the same test [3]:

- "I came up with the idea for the company"
- "I started working n months before my co-founder"
- "My co-founder took a salary for n months and I didn't"
- "I am older/more experienced than my co-founder"
- "I brought on my co-founder after raising n thousands of dollars"
- "I brought on my co-founder after launching my MVP"
- "We need someone to tie-break in case of founder arguments"

All of these reasons over-weight early contributions relative to future work. Seibel's rebuttal rests on four points [3]:

1. **It takes 7 to 10 years to build a company of great value.** Small variations in year one do not justify massively different founder equity splits in years two through ten [3].

2. **More equity equals more motivation.** Almost all startups fail. The more motivated the founders, the higher the chance of success. Getting a larger piece of the equity pie is worth nothing if the lack of motivation on the founding team leads to failure [3].

3. **If you don't value your co-founders, neither will anyone else.** Investors look at founder equity split as a signal for how the CEO values the co-founders. Giving a co-founder 10% or 1% communicates that they are not important. "Why communicate to investors that you have a team that you don't highly value?" [3].

4. **Startups are about execution, not ideas.** Dramatically unequal splits often give undue preference to the co-founder who initially came up with the idea, as opposed to the team that got the product to market and generated initial traction [3].

### The Forward-Looking Principle (Kirsty Nathoo)

Nathoo frames the same argument from the operational perspective. When founders tell her they deserve 70% because they came up with the idea, built the prototype, or started three months earlier, her response: "Everything is ahead of you. This is the early days of the company. If you've worked on it for three months, you've probably got five, ten, fifteen years of this company ahead of you, if it's going to be a success. So think about forwards, not backwards. All the effort is in front of you. You're going to be iterating on the product. The prototype is probably going to bear no resemblance to the final product that becomes successful" [6].

The only valid reason for a slight imbalance is preventing founder deadlock on votes. But Nathoo notes: "If you're at the point where the founders are having to vote their shares to make decisions, then there's probably something more fundamentally broken in the relationship that this is not going to solve" [6].

### The Motivation Framework

Seibel frames equity split primarily as a tool for long-term motivation, not as compensation for past work [3]. As CEO, the first thought should not be "how do I negotiate the best deal?" but "how do I come up with an equity split that's going to maximize the motivation of my teammates?" [3].

The equity stake should be the thing that "gets them to wake up in the middle of the night, gets them to work on the weekends, gets them to work late, gets them to recruit their friends. It gets them to feel like they are true owners of the company and not just employees" [3].

Seibel notes that while he has historically recommended equal splits as a simple rule of thumb, the rule cannot be applied universally. The guiding question remains: "If you're not really interested in the future motivation of your co-founders, if you don't think you're gonna need them in the long term, why are you making them co-founders at all?" [3].

## Vesting as the Safety Mechanism

The primary risk mitigation tool for generous equity grants is vesting with a cliff [3][6]. The standard structure:

- **Four-year vesting**: The co-founder earns equity over four years
- **One-year cliff**: If a co-founder leaves or is fired within the first year, they receive nothing
- After the cliff, 25% of shares vest, then an additional 1/48th of total stock vests each month [3][6]

Seibel calls vesting and the cliff the CEO's "get-out-of-jail-free card": if the co-founder choice was incorrect, as long as it is corrected within one year, there is no long-term harm to the company [3]. Because this safety mechanism exists, it benefits founders to be more generous with equity, not less [3].

### Why Vesting Matters (Nathoo's Three Reasons)

Nathoo provides three distinct justifications for vesting [6]:

1. **Protection for remaining founders**: Without vesting, a co-founder who leaves after six months retains the same ownership as founders who work for another decade. In a $500 million acquisition, the departed co-founder would receive the same payout despite contributing nothing after month six [6].

2. **Skin in the game**: Creates incentive to stay and work hard. Investors care deeply about this because they are investing in the founders specifically [6].

3. **Sets an example for employees**: "It's very unfair to say to your employees, 'You must have vesting,' but I don't as a founder." Same standards for everyone signals fairness [6].

### Board Seat as Additional Protection

Seibel recommends that only the CEO hold a board seat before a significant equity fundraise. This prevents board disputes during tough decisions, including the unlikely event that the CEO has to fire a co-founder [3].

## The 83(b) Election

When shares are subject to vesting, the 83(b) election determines when the founder is taxed. Without filing the 83(b), founders are taxed on the increase in value of their shares every time shares vest. This can create enormous personal and company tax liabilities [6].

Filing the 83(b) election with the IRS within 30 days of the share purchase eliminates this problem. It is the one thing in the incorporation process that cannot be fixed retroactively. Nathoo has seen both funding rounds and acquisitions fall apart because proof of 83(b) filing was missing [6]. See [[Startup Legal Mechanics]] for the full treatment.

## The Co-Founder Conversation

The [[Co-Founders]] article covers the broader question of co-founder selection. Equity split should be one of the earliest conversations, addressed before the company is incorporated. Key considerations include:

- Relative contributions (past work on the idea vs. future contributions)
- Full-time vs. part-time commitment
- Vesting schedules (standard is 4-year vesting with 1-year cliff)
- What happens if a co-founder leaves

## University Spinouts

[[Jared Friedman]] notes that equity split is particularly fraught in [[University Spinouts]], where the question of how much equity the university, the professor, and the founding team each receive can become contentious. Universities typically demand significant equity (often 5-15% or more) plus licensing fees, which can create conflicts with venture investors who expect clean cap tables [2].

## Co-Founder Breakup Guidelines (Pre-PMF)

Seibel provides specific guidelines for what happens when a co-founder leaves or is fired before [[Product-Market Fit]] [4]:

- **Before the one-year cliff**: Departing founder retains a token amount (2-5%)
- **After the one-year cliff but pre-PMF**: Departing founder retains no more than 5%, which often means giving back some equity
- **If fired**: One to three months severance is reasonable
- **If they leave voluntarily**: Severance is not typical
- **All departures**: Resign from board, sign a release, grant proxy voting rights to remaining founders

The rationale for the 5% cap: "When a company's pre-product-market-fit, so much work is left to be done. You want to motivate the people who are remaining to distribute that equity to people who can actually help" [4].

## CEO Must Have Firing Authority

"The CEO should have the ability to fire founders who are not performing. There has to be a captain of the ship. If you're not willing to join a team under these circumstances, maybe you should go start a company and be the CEO" [4].

## Bad Advice to Avoid

**Performance-based equity**: Impossible to set meaningful goals at the start of a startup, and goals change with pivots. "This is probably not an area that you should be innovating" [4].

**Part-time founders**: "A part-time founder isn't a founder and shouldn't really be considered in this equation" [4].

**Dynamic equity agreements**: Complicated structures where equity shifts based on milestones. "If you're trying to motivate people, it's really nice for them to know what they have" [4].

## The Equity Equation for Non-Founders

[[Paul Graham]]'s [[Equity Equation]] provides a mathematical framework for evaluating any equity trade: if someone joining the company makes your stock worth more than 1/(1-n) where n is the fraction you are giving up, the trade is worth it. This applies to early employees, advisors, and anyone receiving equity.

## Employee Equity (Nathoo)

Nathoo provides specific guidance on employee equity allocation [6]:

- Reserve approximately 10% of the company for the first 10 employees, on a sliding scale
- For the first 5 employees, target 7-8%
- All employee equity should vest on the standard four-year schedule
- Stock options (the right to buy shares at today's price in the future) are the standard instrument for employees
- Be generous: early employees work nearly as hard as founders, are paid below market rate, and set the cultural DNA

The critical communication requirement: "You need to understand the number of shares they're getting and what that represents in terms of percentage of the company. Because if you only give them one and not the other, it gets very confusing" [6].

## Be Radically Generous with Early Employee Equity (Vinod Khosla)

[[Vinod Khosla]] argues that insufficient equity allocation is the single most important failure mode in a company's first six months [5].

**Sun Microsystems**: The first three or four founders kept roughly 25-27% of common stock. An equal or larger chunk was reserved for future hires. This enabled recruiting Eric Schmidt, Carol Bartz, Bill Joy, and Andy Bechtolsheim in the first 15-20 hires [5].

**Curai (AI healthcare)**: Khosla's son kept 15% instead of 45%, recruited a co-founder at 15%, and reserved the remainder for engineers making million-dollar salaries at Google and Uber. Individual engineers received 3-5% each [5].

**The magnet principle**: Some hires are worth their equity even with zero direct contribution, because they attract other exceptional people. Bill Joy at Sun "helped attract Eric Schmidt" [5].

[[Sam Altman]] calls this "the most important piece of advice": "Being super generous with early employee equity in getting founder quality people as the first 10 employees. All the evidence is on the side of doing this, and yet almost no one does" [5].

Khosla identifies this as his "single biggest beef with YC" -- option pools are too small [5]. See [[Gene Pool Engineering]] for the full framework.

## References

1. [10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Hj-10-questions-to-discuss-with-a-potential-co-founder) -- Y Combinator (2022)
2. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) -- [[Jared Friedman]] (n.d.)
3. [How to Split Equity Among Co-Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5x-how-to-split-equity-among-co-founders) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)
4. [Co-Founder Equity Mistakes to Avoid](https://ycombinator.com/library/LP-co-founder-equity-mistakes-to-avoid) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
5. [Vinod Khosla on How to Build the Future](https://ycombinator.com/library/6N-vinod-khosla-on-how-to-build-the-future) -- [[Vinod Khosla]], [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)
6. [How to Start a Startup: Startup Mechanics](https://ycombinator.com/library/JR-how-to-start-a-startup-startup-mechanics) -- [[Kirsty Nathoo]] (2014)

title: First Principles Thinking
body:
# First Principles Thinking

[[Garry Tan]] argues that the default education system trains people to think by analogy -- "you learn the history, you learn how it's been done, and then you're programmed to believe that is how they will be forever" [1]. First principles thinking is the antidote: starting from what you are absolutely sure is true and reasoning upward, rather than copying existing approaches. Tan identifies three prerequisites: curiosity, empathy, and serenity [1].

## Curiosity: The Five Whys

Curiosity means asking questions and digging further to understand how systems actually work. Tan highlights the Five Whys analysis from the Toyota Production System, using an example from Eric Ries (The Lean Startup) [1]:

1. Why was the website down? CPU utilization on front-end servers hit 100%.
2. Why did CPU spike? Code contained an infinite loop.
3. Why was that code deployed? A programmer made a mistake.
4. Why did the mistake get checked in? No unit test was written.
5. Why was there no unit test? The new employee wasn't trained in test-driven development.

Ries observes: "Because the most common problems keep recurring, your prevention efforts are automatically focused on the 20 percent of your product that needs the most help" [1]. The Five Whys are why the Toyota Production System made Toyota's cars so reliable -- "they had to come up with a process to get there" [1].

## Empathy: Direct Experience with Users

Empathy means developing an intuitive sense for the lived experience and feelings of customers. Tan uses Airbnb's founding story as the canonical example: Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia needed money for rent, so they listed air beds during a design conference. They hosted three strangers and discovered something unexpected: "The arc of a friendship gets contracted from a year to a day. In other words, if you were to meet somebody, how much time does it take to invite them over your house? It might take months, even a year. What it did -- it contracted this year-long friendship into a couple days. These people came as strangers; they literally left as friends" [1].

This direct, unmediated experience -- sleeping under the same roof as users -- gave Airbnb's founders conviction that could not have come from market research or surveys. "They had direct experience, which gave them direct belief and conviction that this was something that was going to work" [1]. Airbnb is now worth over $100 billion.

## Serenity: Accepting What You Cannot Change

The most counterintuitive prerequisite. Tan references the Serenity Prayer by Reinhold Niebuhr: "God, grant me the serenity to accept the things I cannot change, courage to change the things I can, and the wisdom to know the difference" [1].

[[DoorDash: Creating a Market from Scratch|DoorDash]] illustrates this principle. Facing competitors like Uber (with 50x their marketing budget) and Seamless (with a 17-year head start), DoorDash could not win a head-on battle in major cities. Instead, they "retreated to Long Island, New Jersey, Westchester, and Connecticut. They found product-market fit there and were profitable in a matter of months" [1].

The key insight: "There are two ways to win. One is just to raise more money. But what can you do if you can't raise more money? Be more profitable, outflank, and outsmart. Don't fight a losing battle; fight the battle you can win" [1]. DoorDash took suburban profits and used them to win the cities, eventually reaching 50% U.S. market share and a $55 billion+ market cap.

## Breaking the Script

Tan frames first principles thinking as escaping the societal script: "A whole system, a whole society, a whole civilization dedicated to telling you how to live and what to do and how to think" [1]. The classroom and workplace train thinking by analogy. First principles thinking challenges preconceived notions by demanding direct experience and reasoning from ground truth rather than precedent.

## References

1. [3 Steps for Billion Dollar Startups (How Airbnb & DoorDash Succeeded)](https://ycombinator.com/library/Js-garry-s-channel-3-steps-for-billion-dollar-startups-how-airbnb-doordash-succeeded) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: Conversion Rates
body:
# Conversion Rates

[[Kevin Hale]] teaches that conversion is one of the two main drivers of [[Growth]], alongside churn. Growth is the gap between conversion and churn [1]. Working on churn is easier than working on conversion, but conversion is the starting point -- the thing founders must get right first [1].

## When to Work on Conversion

Hale advises that conversion optimization should only be prioritized when the startup has a "leaky bucket" -- visitors arriving but not converting [1]. He provides industry benchmarks:

| Category | Conversion Rate |
|----------|----------------|
| Shareware (pre-internet free software) | ~0.5% |
| Casual download games | ~2% |
| Freemium SaaS (average) | 1.5-5%, median ~3% |
| Flickr (heyday) | 5-10% |
| AdultFriendFinder | 10-22% |
| Children's social networks | High (parents paying to occupy children) |
| TurboTax Online | ~70% |

Once a landing page converts at approximately 3% (2-3 out of 100 visitors sign up), Hale advises that "you probably don't need to spend that much more time on this. There's probably other things you should work on instead" [1].

## The Knowledge Gap Framework

Every conversion problem is a user interface problem. Hale uses a framework called the Knowledge Spectrum, created by interface designer Jared Spool [1]. It places all knowledge on a spectrum from zero to complete. The user sits at a "current knowledge point" and the interface sits at a "target knowledge point." The space between them is the "knowledge gap" [1].

Every interface problem reduces to closing this gap. There are only two approaches [1]:
1. **Increase the user's knowledge** (teach them what they need to know)
2. **Decrease the knowledge required** to use the product (simplify the interface)

## The One-Button Interface Exercise

Hale's most practical design exercise: imagine reducing the entire landing page to a single button [1]. Then ask:
- What is the minimum information needed on this page to get someone to press the button?
- Is there any information that would prevent someone from pressing it?
- Is there any missing information whose absence prevents pressing it?

The answer to these three questions produces the ideal landing page content [1].

## Seven Questions for Every Page

Hale evaluates every landing page and conversion interface using seven questions [1]:

### 1. What Is the Call to Action?

Is the button -- the thing the founder most wants the user to do -- obvious? The call to action should be placed as close as possible to what Hale calls the **magic moment**: "the experience, the knowledge, the information, the interaction that someone has with your startup and all of a sudden they get tingly inside, the light bulb goes off, they go, 'Holy fuck, I've been waiting for this my whole life'" [1]. The number of steps between clicking the call-to-action button and experiencing the magic moment should be as close to zero as possible [1].

### 2. What Is This?

Hale's test: "Can I copy-paste a sentence on this page that I can put into an email and send it to my mom and my mom goes, 'I understand what this is'?" [1]. Ninety-nine percent of the time, landing pages are filled with jargon and marketing language, and no single sentence clearly explains what the company does [1].

### 3. Is It Right for Me?

Visitors quickly try to identify themselves on the page. They want to see a reflection of themselves or their problems. Without this recognition, they leave [1].

### 4. Is It Legit?

The threshold is low: the site cannot look like a spam website. Beyond that, templates and modern design tools make clearing this bar straightforward [1].

### 5. Who Else Is Using It?

Users want to know others have adopted the product. This serves as a shortcut for both legitimacy and self-identification. Customer logos, testimonial counts, or usage numbers all serve this purpose [1].

### 6. How Much Is It?

Hale is emphatic: "How many times do you go to a website and go like, 'Well, I'll use this without knowing how much it costs. Sounds good to me. Let's just do it.' No one does that" [1]. B2B and enterprise companies are often afraid to list pricing, but hiding it damages conversion rates. If the product is free, explain the business model -- otherwise users feel paranoid about the catch [1].

### 7. Where Can I Get Help?

A percentage of users will always want to talk to a human, regardless of how thorough the documentation is. Some want to verify a real person exists behind the product. Others cannot be bothered navigating the site. If contacting someone is difficult, these users will not convert [1].

## Common Design Failures

Hale identifies several patterns that damage conversion through his live critiques of startup landing pages [1]:

- **Competing calls to action**: Multiple buttons on a single page create confusion about what the user should do next [1]
- **Magic moment buried behind many steps**: If the core product experience requires navigating through seven-step carousels or multiple sign-up pages, conversion drops [1]
- **Carousels**: "Carousels don't work very well because you're hiding information that I might want to have to answer any one of my seven questions" [1]
- **Beta language**: Phrases like "open beta" or "closed beta" telegraph unreliability and undermine the legitimacy question [1]
- **Logos without customers**: Press logos, partner logos, and award logos are not substitutes for evidence of actual users [1]
- **Affordance problems**: When the clickable element (the call to action) does not look like a button, users miss it [1]
- **Copy that buries value**: When the most compelling features are hidden in dense paragraph text rather than highlighted prominently, users miss what makes the product special [1]

## Design Review: Conversion Pitfalls in Practice

[[Pete Koomen]], YC Group Partner and co-founder of Optimizely, and Aaron Epstein review real startup websites and identify conversion failures in practice [2]. Where [[Kevin Hale]]'s framework is diagnostic (seven questions to ask), the design review provides concrete examples of what goes wrong and why.

### Competing Calls to Action

Rivet (a multiplayer infrastructure tool) displayed five different calls to action on a single page: "Click to Start," "Sign Up," "Tutorials," "Templates," and "Open Rivet." Koomen identifies the paradox of choice: "The more things you have competing for somebody's attention, the less likely it is that they're going to take any of those actions" [2]. Worse, each CTA led to a different experience -- one opened a game demo, one opened a community registration, one opened a beta signup via Typeform. The recommendation: "Reducing down to a single call to action and seeing what impact that had" [2].

### The Two-Question Test

Koomen evaluates every page with two questions [2]:
1. **Does this convince me that I want to sign up?** (Does the page explain what it is and why I should care?)
2. **Does the signup process talk me out of it?** (Once convinced, does friction kill the conversion?)

### Repeating the Call to Action

Decoherence (an AI video generator) demonstrated a best practice: repeating the same call to action throughout the page. "Start Free Trial" appeared at the top, as "Try Now" in the middle after showing examples, and again at the bottom. "If you're at the top, you're like, 'Oh yeah, this sounds cool.' If I start scrolling down, oh, and now there's a 'Try Now' right there" [2]. This pattern keeps the conversion path available regardless of where the user is in their discovery process.

### Reducing Friction to the Aha Moment

Koomen and Epstein emphasize mapping the full conversion funnel -- every click from landing to the "aha moment" where the user experiences the core value [2]. Epstein describes the diagnostic: "Map out your conversion funnel, every single step that a user needs to take from when they first land on your website until they get to that aha moment... and figure out which ones you can actually cut" [2].

A practical example: founders whiteboard the funnel and discover the aha moment is at step seven. Steps two, four, and five are unnecessary. Cutting them moves the aha moment to step two, "and things improve a lot from there" [2].

### Social Login Reduces Friction

Decoherence used Google login exclusively. Koomen notes that offering existing login options (Google, Facebook) "can increase conversion rates a lot" because users do not need to create a new username and password [2].

### Addressing Objections Proactively

Decoherence's "No credit card required" text next to the "Start Free Trial" button addresses the most common objection before the user raises it. Epstein explains: "If there's some objection that people have, then you can address that right out of the gate, right there on the page" [2].

### Social Proof Matters for Trust

For Solve Intelligence (AI patent writing), Koomen notes the absence of social proof is especially damaging when the stakes are high: "If I write a patent with them and I spend all this money to do it and it gets rejected because the AI wasn't good enough, I'm going to be upset." Something like "100 patents have been approved that were written by this" would build confidence [2].

### Carousels and Hidden Content

InEvent buried important features behind a 10-slide carousel. Koomen and Epstein confirm the Hale principle: "When you bury things behind tabs or a slider, nine times out of 10 it's not going to be seen" [2]. Better approach: distill to the most important features and put them directly down the page. "People will scroll" [2].

### Specificity Over Breadth

InEvent's headline "Go beyond in-person events" was ambiguous -- it could mean either improving live events or replacing them with virtual ones. Koomen's fix: "Instead of 'go beyond live events,' just 'event management software for live events, webinars' -- even that would have made it much clearer" [2]. This echoes Hale's "What is this?" question [1].

### Sticky Navigation

InEvent demonstrated a positive pattern: the "Book a Meeting" CTA button stuck to the top of the page during scrolling, ensuring it was always accessible. Combined with embedding the email form directly on the page (rather than behind a click), this reduced friction significantly [2].

### Auto-Scrolling Fights the User

InEvent's carousel auto-advanced even when the user's mouse was hovering over it. Koomen notes: "People use their mouse to kind of follow along with where they are. And so it can be a good indicator for where people are actually paying attention" [2]. Fighting the user's browsing behavior damages conversion.


## References

1. [How to Improve Conversion Rates](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6l-how-to-improve-conversion-rates) -- [[Kevin Hale]] (2019)
2. [How to Convert More Visitors into Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KD-how-to-convert-more-visitors-into-customers) -- [[Pete Koomen]], Aaron Epstein (2024)

title: Startup Website Design
body:
# Startup Website Design

[[Garry Tan]] and [[Aaron Epstein]] review five AI startup websites in a design critique that distills actionable principles for any startup homepage [1]. The core insight: a website is a startup's first conversation with a potential user, and that conversation must answer two questions immediately -- "What is this?" and "Is it for me?" [1].

## The Two Questions

Every visitor arriving at a startup website asks two things in rapid succession [1]:
1. **What is this?** What does this product or company do?
2. **Is it for me?** Does this solve my problem? Am I the target customer?

If the homepage cannot answer both within seconds, the visitor leaves. Tan warns against clever taglines that sacrifice clarity: "You don't want to be clever if clever equals confusing" [1]. One reviewed startup had three different concepts in its hero section -- "AI generated game assets," "game development," and "GPT to game" -- leaving both reviewers unsure what the product actually did [1].

## Single Opinionated Call to Action

Multiple calls to action of equal visual weight confuse users. Epstein recommends identifying the primary KPI and making that the single prominent CTA, with secondary options as plain links rather than equally weighted buttons [1].

Tan reinforces this: "Usually you want a single call to action that you're driving people towards." When a site offers four different product links, "I don't know which to click" [1].

## Show, Don't Tell: The Feat of Strength

For AI startups in particular, Tan recommends showing a live demo or impressive output rather than describing capabilities abstractly: "Show the product and a feat of strength... Those are the types of demos that wow people. That's actually why people are so obsessed with this technology now -- the demos and the technological feats of strength that people are capable of putting out" [1].

## Don't Block the Experience

"Try it now" buttons that lead to login walls destroy trust. Epstein recommends letting users experience value before asking for commitment: "Don't force people to create an account. If somebody wants to save it or use it, that's when you prompt them to actually create an account because they're already invested in the product" [1].

Tan frames the competition: "You're always fighting the urge for the back button or to go look at Hacker News" [1].

## Specific Customer, Specific Language

Being too general about who the product serves is "a little bit problematic" [1]. Tan recommends writing the homepage for the ideal customer profile (ICP), not for "anyone who might need our technology" [1].

Epstein suggests a practical tactic: "Go talk to your existing users, ask them 'how would you describe my product?' And then take the exact phrase that they say and just put that up at the top of your site because that's ideally the same thing that will resonate for other potential customers" [1].

## Social Proof

Customer logos and quotes should be woven throughout the page rather than concentrated in one section. The most effective pattern shows a logo, a specific quote from a named person at that company, and their photo -- "it feels like real people rather than something that's made up" [1].

## Animation and Visual Design

Tan and Epstein caution against overusing animation. "If a lot of things are moving, then it just becomes overwhelming. Be selective -- what's the thing that you really want to draw people's attention to? Put animation around that" [1]. Animation should direct the eye to specific elements, not create sensory overload [1].

Spacing and fit-and-finish matter more than many founders realize. Poor spacing between elements "ruins the illusion that this is something that I can trust at the enterprise level" [1].

## The "How It Works" Section

Tan favors putting a "how it works" section early in the page because "it brings you to a much higher understanding of what's going on. It's kind of like when you do YC interviews and you get to do a demo" [1].

Concrete claims like "30 percent cheaper and 25 percent faster" are effective because they sound like they came from user conversations and proactively address concerns about using the product [1].

## FAQ as Trust Builder

A well-placed FAQ section that addresses the top two or three questions can be highly effective. "Within two clicks you can get the answers" creates a good experience and builds trust [1].

## References

1. [Critiquing AI Startup Websites with YC President Garry Tan](https://ycombinator.com/library/Iv-critiquing-ai-startup-websites-with-yc-president-garry-tan) -- [[Aaron Epstein]], [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: Leaving Big Tech
body:
# Leaving Big Tech

Many aspiring founders work at large technology companies (Facebook, Apple, Amazon, Netflix, Google, and similar) and struggle to leave. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] describe this as one of the most common career conversations they have at YC -- not an expertise either ever wanted, but one forced on them by repeated exposure to technically gifted people trapped in jobs they do not like [1].

## The False Assumptions

Caldwell and Seibel identify four assumptions that potential founders treat as facts rather than debatable claims [1]:

### "I'll work on the hardest technical problems at the largest scale"

Seibel calls this "quoting the recruiter." Most employees at large tech companies, when honest, describe working on ad servers, one pixel of one corner of one product, or projects that will be killed. The minority who work on core search infrastructure or other genuinely hard problems may find this claim accurate. For the majority, "it's been presented in brand to them, branded to them, as a very cool thing" when the actual work is unremarkable [1]. Caldwell adds that the way to find out is to talk to someone doing the job, not the recruiter, whose compensation depends on you signing [1].

### "I'll learn lessons I can apply to my startup"

Caldwell reports that many founders tell him "nothing they did in their job translates at all to their startup. Like, could not be less relevant versus their college coursework was more relevant" [1]. The reason: large companies provide proprietary tooling -- Bigtable, BigQuery, internal code review systems, deployment infrastructure. Founders learn to depend on those tools. When starting a company with none of it, "you're like, 'Wait, what happened to all those tools I was used to using?'" [1].

Seibel adds that FAANG experience creates things founders must actively unlearn. Google engineers' standards for shipping to beta are ten times what an MVP requires. "I've had to fight with founders on that where it's like, 'Oh, well, this would never see the light of day at Google.' And it's like, 'Yeah, that's not the standard'" [1].

### "VCs fund Google engineers easily"

There is some truth here -- the brand name helps for a period. But Caldwell warns the signal degrades over time. "If you stayed there for eight years, that doesn't apply anymore. You kind of have to get out to get the benefit of it" [1].

More importantly, Seibel warns against the illusion that VCs are "walking the halls being like, 'Engineer, here's term sheet.'" The process of getting from MIT CS degree to Facebook job is nothing like the process of raising venture capital. "You don't read about the unsuccessful fundraisers. And so if all you read is the successful fundraisers, it looks like everyone is raising." The math does not pencil out: "There's a lot of Google employees and ex-Googlers now" [1].

### "FAANG experience is essential before starting"

The most dangerous assumption. Most FAANG recruiter messaging is written for people who want to be employees or managers, not founders. "If you want to be an employee or a manager in a large tech company, that's great. All those guides are written for you. The problem is, what should the founders do?" [1].

## The Retention Trap

Caldwell describes the retention mechanism as an engineered system, iterated over a decade or more, designed to prevent departure [1]. The core mechanism is equity vesting:

1. **Initial equity package** vests over four years. Leaving at any point means forfeiting unvested stock.
2. **Refresh grants** arrive every six to twelve months, also vesting over four years. The result: at any point, the amount of unvested equity ahead is disproportionately larger than what has already vested, creating a constant pull to stay "a little bit longer."
3. **Loss aversion** compounds the effect. Behavioral economics shows that people irrationally overvalue things they already have when threatened with losing them. The unvested equity feels like savings that would be lost by leaving.

The social environment amplifies the trap. Colleagues are optimizing for employee or manager careers, not founder careers. They spend their salaries on lifestyle upgrades -- nicer apartments, cars, vacations. If the founder-aspirant follows suit, they never build the savings needed to start a company. "If they can keep you spending your entire salary and they can keep the money that you have invested yet larger than the amount of money that you have in your savings by like two to three X, you never leave" [1].

Caldwell compares the gamification to a video game: "Oh, I can get my next level. I want to get my next level upgrade and then I unlock my booster pack." It is the same behavioral design these companies apply to retain users on their products [1].

## Warehouse Talent

Seibel identifies a darker dynamic: large tech companies sometimes warehouse talented people to prevent competitors from using them. The engineer works on "speculative science projects that get axed and that never ship." Facebook built a chip and never shipped it. PayPal had a research lab and laid everyone off. "If you're not on the core thing, you have no promises that your work will ever turn into anything" [1].

Often the speculative project was what attracted the person in the first place. "That was the moonshot. So if you're getting trapped, it was the moonshot that was the bait" [1].

## When to Leave

Seibel describes the signal in stark terms: "We all know these people that want to just like tell you their darkest secret, which is they wake up every day and they like dream of quitting. Like, they have fantasies of quitting every day. Those are people that probably should quit" [1].

He compares it to talking to someone with an addiction: "When someone's just like, 'I don't know why I'm doing this, but I'm still doing it, and I can't stop, but I hate my life.' If that sounds like you or your friend talking about your job at Facebook, that's probably a good sign" [1].

This applies to roughly one percent of FAANG developers. For the other 99 percent, the message is irrelevant. "We're not trying to argue with you. We're just saying though, if you know someone that's deeply unhappy because of decisions they're making, they can just make different decisions. You can break the spell" [1].

## The Tactical Playbook

For those who decide FAANG is a stepping stone, not a destination [1]:

1. **Decide how long you will stay before starting.** Have a plan for what you are trying to accomplish: getting a visa, saving money, putting the brand on your resume. Set a departure date.
2. **Keep personal burn low.** Do not get hooked on the compensation. The lifestyle inflation is the primary mechanism that traps you.
3. **Do not work on projects that will make you hate technology.** Seibel knows many ex-Facebook employees who feel they "did evil and they made money doing it" and are unhappy about it. The psychological cost prevents them from being optimistic about tech, which is a prerequisite for being a startup founder [1].
4. **Remember you can always go back.** "If you're a good programmer and you leave in a nice way, no matter what happens to you, you can come back. There's actually not that much loss you have to take" [1].
5. **Have the plan before you are looking a bonus in the eye.** "It's a lot easier to have a plan when you're not looking a bonus in the eye. If you come in with the plan, when the bonus thing happens, you'll see it coming" [1].

# Leaving Big Tech

## The $200 Million Mistake (Garry Tan)

[[Garry Tan]] provides the most personal case study of the cost of staying at a big company. In 2003, at age 23, he was earning $72,000/year at Microsoft. Peter Thiel offered him a check for his full annual salary and told him to quit: "Cash this check. Quit your job. This is a zero-risk opportunity for you." Tan declined -- "Thank you very much, Mr. Thiel, but I might get promoted to level sixty next year" [2]. The company was Palantir, now worth $20 billion+. The missed equity was worth at least $200 million [2].

Tan also turned down a Google job interview in 2004, when "it was obviously useful, but it wasn't clear that it was going to make money" [2].

### The Value Gap

Tan frames the structural problem: Google's pure profit per employee is $1.6 million per year after all costs. Engineers on "the builder side of that organization" create far more value than that -- "possibly ten times, maybe a hundred times that value." But the only way to capture that value is through equity, which means "either being a founder or working at an early stage startup that gives significant equity" [2].

### Pros and Cons

Tan acknowledges legitimate reasons to work at big companies [2]:
- Ship products to millions of people
- Stable, with best-practice product techniques
- Meet smart potential co-founders

Downsides [2]:
- Building new things is harder -- requires negotiation, politics, and favors
- You spend time maintaining the status quo rather than making something new
- Skills in politics are separate from skills in building

### The Direct Feedback Loop

Tan identifies what small companies offer that large ones cannot: "Very direct access to users and customers and their problems, which means you can actually have empathy for what's actually going on with them. And then you can directly solve it. That cycle is so powerful. The sooner you learn how to make that cycle happen in your career, the better off you'll be" [2].

His advice: "I'm not really saying that you shouldn't go work at a big tech company. I am saying you should probably leave before it makes you soft. Don't make my mistake. Make all new mistakes" [2].

## References (new)

2. [My $200 Million Startup Mistake](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jh-garry-s-channel-my-200-million-startup-mistake) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

## References

1. [Dalton & Michael: Why You Should Leave Your FAANG Job](https://ycombinator.com/library/Ih-dalton-michael-why-you-should-leave-your-faang-job) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2022)

title: Six Founder Skills
aliases: engineering product design sales finance leadership
body:
# Six Founder Skills

[[Garry Tan]] identifies six skill areas that increase a founder's probability of success [1]. No single person can master all six, but being deep in one or two and "broad enough to be dangerous" in the rest is the target. "Any given single human being could probably only get truly great at one or two of these skills, but you need a team of people who are great at all of these in order to build a startup or business that really matters" [1].

## 1. Engineering

Technical prowess is the "ultimate moat" in startups. "When you have a piece of code or an algorithm figured out that nobody else has, when you have something like PageRank, that's the ultimate moat for Google, and it protects something that is worth billions of dollars. Someone can't just walk in off the street and take that from them" [1].

A CS degree helps but is not required. For non-technical founders, Tan recommends learning enough to be dangerous: "If you understand the craft and you can build a good but not perfect version of what you ultimately want to build, you can get the ball rolling. It'll immediately distinguish you against the sea of non-technical people who are also trying to find a [[Technical Co-Founders|technical co-founder]]" [1].

## 2. Product

Product is about knowing what to build and for whom. Tan frames it as "being able to run the process on a machine to continually find and refine [[Product-Market Fit]]. This isn't a one-time thing. This is a constant process" [1].

There is no obvious shortcut to learning product. Tan's recommendation: "Build something and release it. Release it to users and see if they stick with it. Talk to users and see why they did or didn't take to it" [1].

## 3. Design

Design has two components [1]:

- **Interaction design (UX)**: how it works. Where to put the buttons, what to say on them, how to break complex processes into manageable steps. This is "most directly related to product" and is the more important of the two.
- **Visual design**: how it looks. A skill like drawing or painting. Having "taste" -- knowing when a design works or does not -- is more important for founders than being able to execute visual design themselves.

Tan recommends Steve Krug's "Don't Make Me Think" as an entry point [1].

## 4. Sales and Marketing

[[Ryan Petersen]] of Flexport calls sales "the most underrated profession" [1]. Tan adds that "every business today is a meme. It's never been easier for a startup to access exactly the people who have that problem" [1]. Brand, attention capture, and the ability to sell are essential.

## 5. Finance

Not critical at the outset -- "engineering, product, sales, marketing are really the key to getting [[Product-Market Fit]]" [1]. But financial literacy becomes essential post-PMF. Tan cites Matt McGinnis from Rippling: "Once I understood that my company was worth the net present value of all future cash flows, I started making better decisions. Also, all the investor tropes started to make a lot more sense" [1].

Finance enables resource allocation decisions: should you hire an executive, invest in a new office, and when will those investments pay back? [1]

## 6. Leadership and Management

Leading by example is step one. But the harder skill is holding people accountable -- "having very crucial conversations, hard discussions about when things didn't go right, ideally as constructively as possible, while also having the impact you need to have" [1].

Tan cites Barbara Kellerman: "Followers are more important to leaders than leaders are to followers." Leading from empathy creates more connected, resilient teams [1].

For those still in school, Tan recommends finding leadership positions that require coordinating people toward numerical outcomes -- clubs, sports teams, or projects at work. He recommends Dale Carnegie's "How to Win Friends and Influence People" as a surprisingly practical guide [1].

## The Team Imperative

Founders are "the speck of dust that forms the crystalline structure that actually solves a real problem" [1]. The six skills explain why teams are so crucial -- and why founders should assess which skills they need in co-founders and early hires. Being deep in one or two areas helps a founder recognize who is truly excellent in the others [1].

## References

1. [6 Skills for Successful Startup Founders](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jl-garry-s-channel-6-skills-for-successful-startup-founders) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: Investor Advice Pitfalls
aliases: why investors can't fix your company
body:
# Investor Advice Pitfalls

Investors -- including YC -- cannot fix a startup. [[Michael Seibel]] frames this as the central message, comparing the realization to learning Santa Claus is not real: "For some folks, they're like, 'Hey, we finally get some time with you. No one wants our product. What should we do?' And I have to tell them the unfortunate news, which is I have no idea" [1]. Seibel draws on his own experience: "I really believed that once I raised from the top investors, they would tell me whatever secrets they were holding out on the world" [1].

The problem is not that investors are unhelpful. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and Seibel argue that each type of investor brings systematic biases rooted in their background, and founders who follow advice blindly often end up in predictable failure modes.

## The Seven Investor Types

### 1. The Finance Background Investor

**Background**: pure finance -- never operated a company.

**Systematic bias**: When all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail. Solutions involve money: raising more, spending more, throwing money at problems [1].

**Common bad advice**: Asking pre-product-market-fit startups for five-year financial projections. Caldwell asks Seibel: "You're pre-product market fit. Do you have five-year financial projections?" Seibel recalls: "To even ask me if I had financial projections, I was like, 'What's a financial projection?'" He resisted learning what a balance sheet was for years, telling his co-founder Kevin: "You know what's important for us to learn? How not to lose money every month before we die" [1].

**Failure mode**: Caldwell identifies the concrete results: scaling negative unit economics, spending on advertising with no payback period, getting founders to work on things that are not making the product good. Seibel cites Patrick Collison (Stripe founder), who addressed this at YC: "Too many founders treat product as an afterthought. They're basically trying to do financial engineering and the product is delegated" [1].

**The nuance**: This strategy is not always wrong. For successful companies, there is usually a point where deploying capital is the right move. The problem is giving this advice universally, including to pre-PMF startups [1].

### 2. The Big Company Executive

**Background**: employee number 100-1000+ at a major tech company.

**Systematic bias**: Excellent at scaling a successful product further. Very different from getting the first 100 users [1]. Seibel references Paul Buchheit, Google employee #18, who was "the king of dirty hacks." The tactics of employee 1000 are fundamentally different from those of the first 20 [1].

**Common bad advice**: Seibel describes the pattern: hiring executives before product-market fit. "You need a marketing executive. The engineering team isn't productive enough -- we need a VP of engineering." Pre-PMF, the mistake is usually that the wrong person is in the company, not that there aren't enough people [1].

**Failure mode**: Building org charts for a company that has zero real customers. Seibel notes: "What failure usually looks like for a lot of our companies is they get zero real customers. Not that the failure was they couldn't get a Series A. They just bombed, like complete disaster" [1].

**The nuance**: Seibel contrasts the first five employees of YouTube before acquisition with the team that scaled it post-acquisition. Both groups are valuable. Caldwell adds that there is a supply-demand imbalance: "There's so many more people that helped scale Instagram than there are people that founded Instagram" [1].

### 3. The Non-Tech Industry Entrepreneur

**Background**: Made their money in real estate, franchises, or other non-tech industries.

**Systematic bias**: Caldwell introduces this category as especially common internationally. Seibel describes the behavior: they treat the startup investment like a Subway franchise -- calling to yell when things go wrong, demanding onerous control terms, and being "really stressed out all the time that you're going to lose their money" [1].

**Common bad advice**: Asking for franchise-style terms: more control, more reporting, terms that would make sense for a strip mall investment but are toxic for a tech startup [1].

**The nuance**: Seibel notes these investors are among the most forgivable. They are successful people applying principles that worked for them. "You can't be mad at them. They're just taking the principles that they learned to make their money and applying them to a different industry" [1].

### 4. The Junior Investor

**Background**: Early-career, needs to establish a track record quickly.

**Systematic bias**: Their KPI is having investments marked up. If the startup raises more money at a higher valuation, the junior investor looks good to their own colleagues and LPs [1].

**Common bad advice**: Constant encouragement to raise more money. "Everything's working great and you do have product market fit and you should scale faster." This keeps the junior investor's metrics looking good, regardless of whether the startup should actually be raising [1].

**Failure mode**: The startup may have serious product or market problems that need fixing, but the investor cannot afford to engage with those problems because it would break the illusion. "It's harder for them to go and spend the time in the dirt with you on that stuff, because it's going to break their illusion" [1].

### 5. The Influencer / Famous Person

**Background**: Built a large distribution network (social media following, celebrity status).

**Systematic bias**: They understand distribution and treat startups like other ad deals -- "give me some stuff and I'll promote you" [1].

**Common bad advice**: Advisor shares in exchange for promotion. The promise of distribution via celebrity posts [1].

**Failure mode**: Founders expect that a celebrity tweeting about their product will solve distribution. In most cases, the clicks are far less helpful than hoped. Seibel says founders "kind of feel like they got a raw deal on these" [1].

**The nuance**: The best, most reputable influencer investors do not promise distribution. They say "I'll try to help." The unrealistic expectation is the founder's: "It's the founder once again looking for the silver bullet. Someone else to solve the problem for me. Silver bullets don't exist" [1].

### 6. Other Founders

**Background**: Built and sold (or failed at) their own startup.

**Systematic bias**: Advice is heavily autobiographical. If they struggled with fundraising, all advice centers on fundraising mistakes. If they struggled with distribution, all advice is about distribution [1].

**The nuance**: Seibel admits this was true of himself: "A lot of my advice was heavily based on my personal experience, and it took years of me working at YC to break out of that trap." The YC partners' real value comes from condensing the learnings of thousands of founders, not from their own founder experience alone [1].

### 7. The Extremely Young Investor

**Background**: Just out of college or still in college, often a scout or angel.

**Systematic bias**: They model their behavior on what they read from other investors -- repeating whatever the hot trend is, whatever people are tweeting about, whatever essay they most recently read [1].

Seibel compares it to how founders sometimes "watch movies about how founders are supposed to act": these young investors read about investing and pattern-match to what they have consumed [1].

## YC's Own Failure Modes

Seibel raises this candidly. Caldwell acknowledges that YC partners give bad advice too. The most common failure: overweighting the lean startup playbook. Caldwell states: "Sometimes it works right out of the gate and sometimes you can spend two years building a product in a cave and never talk to a single user and it's perfect and you immediately get product market fit" [1].

YC has enough data points of this succeeding that they cannot claim it never works. But statistically, recommending the build-in-a-cave strategy would be "insane." Seibel adds: "The best YC partners are very careful with how forcefully we give the YC advice because the longer you're here, the longer you have a running record of the time I told someone to do it like this and the opposite worked" [1].

## The Best Investor Advice

The best advice Caldwell received as a founder: an outside perspective that cut through the noise. "I was swimming in my head with too many ideas at once. And someone from the outside could look at everything and be like, 'oh, this is working. You should do more of this.' They could take all of this consternation and complicated stuff" and synthesize it into something simple [1].

The best advice Seibel received: brutal honesty from someone with no financial interest. A friend named Gideon visited after Justin.tv had reached $8M in annual revenue. "He basically said, folks, congratulations. But your company sucks if you don't change stuff. It's all going to die." There was no prescribed solution -- just an accurate diagnosis. "The best investors point out the problem. They don't give you a solution" [1].

Seibel notes the key enabler: Gideon had no interest in investing. "You can get a lot more honesty out of somebody when they're not financially incentivized to blow smoke up your ass" [1].

## The Meta-Lesson: Founder Accountability

The overarching message is that the founder is ultimately responsible. Seibel states it as: "The founders that made it work believed in themselves and they knew that we couldn't fix it. They took bits and pieces. They took information from the outside world, but they took personal accountability that they're the ones who are gonna have to make it work" [1].

Seibel describes the YC kickoff: "The first thing we say is that we're experts in failure. We know a lot of paths to not take. But if you want us to sit next to you at your desk and tell you everything to do to win, to become a billion-dollar company, we can't do it" [1]. They even offer to return equity to any founder who wants to leave after hearing this.

Seibel frames the success attribution: "You're going to be able to deserve 99.9% of the credit if you figure it out because you figured it out. All the rest of the people around who helped you, they didn't figure it out" [1].

## References

1. [Dalton & Michael: Why Investors Can't Fix Your Company](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IT-dalton-michael-why-investors-can-t-fix-your-company) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)

title: Company Culture
body:
# Company Culture

Culture is rarely the most imminent risk of death for a startup. It will not kill you this week. But Kathryn Minshew, co-founder of The Muse (YC W12), argues it is one of the most important things to think about at every stage -- when you are 2 people, 10 people, 25 and beyond [1]. The Muse grew from 12 to over 80 employees in two years, and the lessons she extracted center on a principle: culture is not what you say it is. Culture is how you behave when no one is watching [1].

[[Patrick Collison]] of Stripe frames culture as a solution to a bandwidth problem. At two people, a founder can be involved in every decision. By five or ten, this is already impossible. By fifty, it is "completely hopeless." Culture is "what the strands are that you want to have, the invariances that you want to maintain, as you can get specifically involved in fewer and fewer decisions over time" [3].

## The Four Dimensions of Culture (Patrick Collison)

Collison defines culture along four dimensions [3]:

1. **Who you hire and what those people value**
2. **What you do every day and why**
3. **What you choose to communicate**
4. **What you choose to celebrate** (and its converse: what you choose to punish)

"Running a company based on what you celebrate is more exciting than what you punish" [3].

## Culture Is Tough Choices

The core of culture is not perks, office design, or stated values on a wall. It is the willingness to make hard decisions that align with those values [1].

Minshew describes the test: finding a candidate who has every skill the company needs, a pedigree that would make investors swoon, and who "kind of might be a jerk." The culture decision is not to hire that person and to keep looking, "because you realize that the impact of one really bad person is far more important than their behavior, and that toxic behavior spreads to the rest of your team" [1].

The Muse codified six core values when they reached 25 people. Every interview candidate is tested against all six. The most well-known value: "No assholes" [1]. Minshew acknowledges the skepticism -- "A lot of companies say that" -- but argues that the difference is whether you enforce it when it costs you something.

## Culture Is Behavior, Not Words

"Culture is how the founders behave, how the executives behave" [1]. Putting values on a wall is not culture. Living them under pressure is.

Minshew tells a cautionary story: a company that served steak and sushi at Friday lunches, then gradually downgraded to hot dogs during the 2008 recession. When an employee asked if the company was cutting costs, the office head replied: "No, it's Americana themed." Minshew's assessment: "That's bullshit, and everybody knows it" [1].

The alternative: be honest. "When you treat your employees like kids, they're going to behave as such, versus being open and honest, even if the answer is, 'The economy's tough, so we're going to be a little bit more careful'" [1].

## Transparency as a Cultural Practice

### The Muse's Approach

The Muse practices transparency through several mechanisms [1]:

- **Board deck sharing**: The entire company sees board decks at All Hands after every board meeting
- **Full budget review**: A 20-slide presentation walking through every line in the budget -- total spend, revenue, burn, payroll, health insurance, office supplies, marketing expenses, and the rationale for each allocation
- **Open trade-off discussions**: After the budget review, the company discussed trade-offs openly ("Why aren't we providing free lunch? What did we decide to spend that money on instead?")

The budget review produced an unexpected benefit: executives self-edited their requests. After seeing the full picture, several said "I asked for this much, but actually, I can do what I need with less" [1]. Transparency generated buy-in for scrappiness without mandating it from above.

### Stripe's Radical Email Transparency

Stripe placed extraordinary emphasis on transparency as a core cultural value. Collison explains the reasoning: if everyone believes in the mission and has "really good access to information and a good picture of the current state of Stripe, then that gets you a huge amount of the way there in terms of working productively together. And it kind of forgives a lot of the other things that tend to break as you grow a startup" [3].

In the early days, Stripe BCC'd every other person at the company on every outgoing email unless the sender opted out [3]. The rationale: fewer meetings needed if everyone could stay abreast of what was happening. As the company grew past 50 people, this created problems -- new employees had 2,000 emails in their inbox on the first week and Gmail itself broke at one point from the volume [3].

Stripe evolved the system through increasingly sophisticated tooling: mailing lists, Gmail filter generation programs, weekly all-hands with decks, and a tool called Hackpad (described as "Google Docs but with a news feed") where all documents were public by default [3].

The challenge of transparency at scale: when someone contacts a partner with an idea and 170 people can see it, "maybe a person sitting across the way from you thinks that's the stupidest idea they've ever heard." Stripe developed cultural norms around when it is appropriate to jump into discussions and how to give drive-by feedback without discouraging sharing [3].

Collison's assessment: "I can't give a much stronger endorsement of it than it has worked so far pretty good" [3].

## Scrappiness After Raising Money

A common warning in the startup world: "Don't raise too much money, because you will spend it frivolously." Minshew pushes back: "That takes away the agency from the founders, from the leadership, and I think that's a little bit silly" [1].

The Muse raised $10 million for their Series A when they had targeted $5-6 million. Rather than letting the extra capital erode their scrappy culture, they used the budget review to maintain discipline. They surveyed employees to understand which benefits mattered most, ensuring money went where it counted [1].

The lesson: raising more money than planned does not automatically destroy culture if leadership is intentional about communicating how the money will be used and why discipline still matters.

## Perks Are Not Culture

"Say no to ball pits. I think that there's a lot of these startup floor things, and frankly, I think if you need something like this to recruit, you probably have other problems" [1].

## The First Ten Hires Set Everything

The first ten employees are disproportionately important because each one effectively brings ten more people with them. Collison frames it as a compounding decision: "You're not just hiring those first ten people. You're actually kind of hiring a hundred people because you should think of each one of those people as bringing along sort of another ten people with them" [3]. See [[Hiring at Early Stage]] for specific tactics on finding and evaluating early hires.

## Hiring for Culture: What Silbermann, Collison, and Collison Look For

### Ben Silbermann (Pinterest)

[[Ben Silbermann]] describes culture as more like gardening than architecture: "You plant some seeds, and then you pull out weeds that aren't working, and they sort of expand" [3].

His filter for early hires [3]:
- **Hard work, high integrity, low ego**
- **Creativity expressed through curiosity**: "Some of our first employees are probably some of the quirkiest people I've ever met." One had created his own board game with elaborate rules. Another was into magic tricks and had coded a magic trick app for iPhone and shot the production video himself.
- **Desire to build something great without arrogance**

In the early days, the horrible office and zero pay were natural selection mechanisms: "There was no external reason other than being excited about building something to join. In fact, there was every reason not to" [3].

For evaluating talent in unfamiliar domains, Silbermann developed a preparation ritual: before interviewing candidates for a role he did not understand (head of finance, head of marketing), he would first talk to people he knew were world-class in that discipline. He asked them: "What are the key traits? What questions do you ask? If you're looking for the next person as good as you, where are they working right now? And what's their phone number?" [3].

### Patrick and John Collison (Stripe)

The Collisons identified three common traits in their first ten hires [3]:

1. **Genuine and straightforward**: People others trust. Intellectual honesty in how they approach problems.
2. **Enjoyed finishing things**: "There's a lot of people who are really excited about tons of things. Only a subset of those are actually excited about completing things." Collison is skeptical of hiring based on GitHub activity, because it rewards starting many things over going deep on one.
3. **Cared intensely about quality**: "It was offensive to them when something was just a little bit off." Early Stripe routed every single API error to all inboxes and phones. Team members copied each other on all outgoing emails and pointed out grammar or spelling mistakes.

For finding early talent, the Collisons describe thinking like value investors: "You're looking for the human capital that's significantly undervalued by the market." Their first designer was 18 and in high school in Sweden. Their CTO was in college. "You can relax one constraint -- relax the fact that it's apparent that they're talented" [3].

A practical test for talent: "Is this person probably the best out of all of their friends at what they do?" [3].

### Work Trial Before Hiring

Collison recommends working with candidates before committing, especially for the first ten hires. For a majority of Stripe's first ten, the team worked with them for about a week before extending an offer. "It's pretty hard to fake it for a week" [3].

[[John Collison]] adds that founders should have the confidence to interview in ways that work for them, not cargo-cult standard processes. When Stripe hired its first engineer, they flew the candidate out and spent a weekend coding with him -- "the only way we could really tell" [3].

## Onboarding

### The Small-Team Version

Silbermann describes Pinterest's early onboarding: "Here's your computer. We already set up your environment. This is the problem that we have to solve together." Because the team worked from a tiny two-bedroom apartment, relationship-building happened automatically [3].

The one critical addition at every stage: remind new hires of the long-term vision. "It's really easy when you first join to drop someone into a problem, and then they think the whole world is like this little problem in front of them. We'd always say, 'Someday we want to do for discovery what Google did for search'" [3].

### The Scaled Version

As Pinterest grew, onboarding became a week-long program with function-specific tracks. They measure two output metrics: new hire satisfaction (surveyed immediately and at 30 days) and manager/peer assessments of ramp-up speed. A critical rule: if a team is not doing a good job bringing new people up to speed, that team should not be hiring more people [3].
