Silbermann emphasizes learning about the person: "What are their aspirations? What's their working style? How do they like to be recognized? Do they really prefer being in total silence? Are they a morning person or night person?" [3].

### Push Off the Cliff (Stripe)

John Collison advocates getting new hires doing real work immediately: engineers committing code on day one, business hires in real meetings on day one. "Sometimes there's a tendency to be tentative and help ease people in. We're much more of the 'push people off the cliff' school" [3].

The second priority is rapid cultural feedback. When Stripe's culture was more written (people sitting next to each other with headphones on, emailing each other), new hires needed explicit coaching on these norms. "Everything from high level -- how you're doing at your job -- to minor cultural pointers, the more feedback you can give them, the better they'll do" [3].

## Scaling Culture

### Maintaining a "Startup of Startups" (Silbermann)

Silbermann describes Pinterest's goal as making the company feel like "a startup of many startups, rather than this monolithic organization with a set of foreign processes that cut horizontally through it" [3]. Each team should control the resources it needs, know what the most important thing is, and know how it is measured. This makes the management problem "somewhat tractable" [3].

The challenge at Pinterest: each team needs a strong designer, a strong engineering leader, often a writer, and often a community manager -- all self-contained. This cross-functional structure is harder to build but supports the company's product philosophy of putting curious people with diverse skills together, anchoring them on a single project, and removing barriers to speed [3].

### Time Horizons Shift (Patrick Collison)

As a company ages, the time horizon for planning lengthens correspondingly. After one month, you plan one month ahead. After one year, you plan one year ahead. After four years, four years ahead [3].

This affects hiring: "In the really early days, you kind of have to hire people who will be productive essentially immediately. You don't have the luxury of hiring people who are really promising but aren't quite going to be up to speed for another year or two." After two or three years, making longer-term investments in people becomes not just reasonable but necessary [3].

### Growing Early Hires into Leaders

Collison states that several of Stripe's first ten people now hold leadership roles. The key: "People don't necessarily come out of the womb being good at managing or being good at leadership. And being able to develop that in people is a lot of work at a time when everyone is running around with their hair on fire. But it's also sort of damaging if the company can't develop that skill" [3].

John Collison adds nuance: some people try management and succeed. Others try it and say "I'm really glad I tried it because I never want to do that again." The company must ensure both paths -- management and individual contribution -- offer equal impact. "My strong preference is that you give as many people a shot as possible" [3].

## Referencing: The Quantitative Back-Channel

Silbermann describes reference checks as one of the most important and underused hiring tools [3]. The key principles:

- **Test self-awareness**: "If I asked Jonathan what you're best at, or what you'd be most proud of, what would he say?" This creates social accountability because the candidate knows you will actually talk to the reference.
- **Force scarcity**: Ask references to rank the candidate: "Top 1% of people you've ever worked with, top 5%, or top 10%?" Without this forcing function, references default to generic positives.
- **Spend real time**: 15 minutes on the phone, not a quick confirmation call.
- **Use references as a recruiting source**: "References are also a tremendous source of new recruits" [3].

## Authenticity in Recruiting

All three founders reject whitewashing difficulties when recruiting. Silbermann shares his interview technique: when a candidate says "I'm really passionate about what you're doing," he asks "Where else are you interviewing?" If they list seven unrelated companies at the same stage, "that's a sign that they're probably not being authentic about what they care about. Those folks, often when things get really hard, they won't stick it out" [3].

Collison cites the PayPal and iPhone recruiting stories as models: PayPal told recruits "Visa and MasterCard want to kill us and we might be doing something illegal. But if you succeed, you'll redefine payments." The iPhone team told recruits "You won't see your families for three years, but when you're done, your kids' kids will remember what you built" [3].

The principle: be transparent about why the opportunity is amazing and lay out in detail why it will be hard. "The right people select in, or they select out" [3].

## Culture Is What You Make Surface Area (Sarah Nahm)

[[Sarah Nahm]] introduces the concept of "cultural surface area" -- the boundaries of what a company considers part of its culture. At Lever, dish duty was not part of the culture until someone pointed out that a disproportionate group of people (mostly women) did the dishes. The team built a Slackbot for rotational dish duty [2].

Similarly, Lever's holiday calendar was not cultural surface area until the team pushed back on not including Martin Luther King Day. "We realized in that moment that our holiday schedule was surface area to our culture" [2].

## Inclusion Before Diversity

Nahm argues: "What's the point of hiring all this quote diverse talent if when they get there they're not ready to succeed?" Start with an inclusive culture; diverse hiring follows [2]. See [[Diversity and Inclusion]] for a detailed treatment of DNI at early-stage startups.

## The Technical/Non-Technical Divide

Lever rejected Silicon Valley's dichotomy with a training called "I'm Technical and So Are You" because the divide was holding both sides back: engineers did not understand customers, and non-engineers did not think in scalable systems [2].

## Employee Resource Groups with Policy Power

At 65% participation, Lever's ERGs were asked to help build company policies -- the parents ERG co-designed the parental leave policy [2].

## It Does Get Easier

Minshew challenges the common advice that "it doesn't get easier." In the early days of The Muse, she cold-emailed 800 student groups for the first users, pitched over 150 VC firms, and got 148 rejections in six months [1]. People told her constantly that it only gets harder.

Her rebuttal: it never gets easy, but the risk of death recedes. With a team of executives, a single departure does not kill the company. "Yes, the challenges keep going, and yes, it's always going to be hard. But it does, in some ways, get a little bit easier" [1]. The early stage is "blood, sweat, tears and Ramen. And now, sometimes, I eat in restaurants" [1].

## Focus Changes Over Time

Minshew describes a critical transition at The Muse. The company was beloved by users -- people emailed daily saying the product changed their lives. But growth had slowed. Revenue was flat. "We had to have a really tough moment where we said, 'If we don't start focusing on the things that matter, on moving our growth, on figuring out revenue, we're going to be the most loved startup that nobody ever gets to use anymore, because it's dead'" [1].

The shift: hiring a sales leader, figuring out pricing and revenue model, while continuing to build the user experience. The lesson: what is most important changes over time, and culture must support the willingness to make those shifts honestly.

## Remember Why You Started

Minshew's advice for sustaining motivation over the long haul: remember why you started [1]. For her, it was the experience of being in a job she did not love, going online to look for a new one, and thinking "This is the best there is? I'm almost offended by how bad that experience is" [1].

When energy flags at four and a half years in, she reads user emails or talks to someone who found their job through The Muse. "Being a startup can be one part one of the most meaningful experiences of your life, and one part a total slog. But I think when you remember why you got into it, you keep your focus on building for the long haul" [1].

## Airbnb: Culture as Competitive Moat (Brian Chesky and Alfred Lin)

[[Brian Chesky]] and [[Alfred Lin]] provide one of the most detailed treatments of how culture is built from day one and how it influences strategic decisions at scale [3].

### Three Things Nobody Tells You About Culture

Chesky identifies three reasons culture is systematically neglected [3]:

1. **Nobody talks about it.** "There's tons of articles about building a great product. There's very few things about culture."
2. **It's hard to measure.** "Things that are hard to measure often get discounted."
3. **It doesn't pay off in the short term.** "If you wanted to build a company in one year and sell it as quickly as possible, the number one piece of advice I give you is forget the culture." Culture makes you hire slowly and be deliberate about decisions that slow near-term progress. "It's really about building a company for the long term."

### Culture Must Be Designed

Chesky studied companies that endured -- Apple, Amazon, Nike -- and concluded they all had a clear mission and clear values. "Culture needs to be designed" [3]. He and Gebbia wrote Airbnb's core values before hiring their first employee, spending four to five months finding that first engineer. "I kind of felt like your first engineer was like bringing in DNA to your company. There were going to be a thousand people just like him or her" [3].

### Core Values Interviewers

Airbnb uses dedicated core values interviewers who are never from the candidate's function. An engineering candidate is assessed for cultural fit by non-engineers, to prevent technical skill from biasing the values assessment. "We've said no to a lot of really great people because we just didn't feel right about them being with us long term" [3].

Chesky's hiring bar: "If you could hire anybody in the world, would you hire the person sitting across from you?" Every hire should raise the standard [3].

### Missionaries vs. Mercenaries

In 2011, the Samwer brothers cloned Airbnb, raised $90 million, and hired 400 people in 30 days. Airbnb had 40 employees and $7 million. Multiple advisors recommended acquiring the clone. Chesky refused: "I felt like we were missionaries and they were mercenaries. I didn't think they were doing it for the beliefs" [3].

His logic: "I believed that missionaries would outlast and out-endure mercenaries." He also observed that "the best revenge against an internet clone was just to make them run the company long term." Today, more than 50% of Airbnb's revenue comes from Europe [3].

### Brand Is Culture Turned Outward

Chesky argues that culture and brand are "two sides of the same coin." Culture is the internal principles and beliefs. Brand is the external promise. "Whatever happens inside the company eventually comes out" [3].

He cites Steve Jobs's 1997 Think Different campaign as the model: instead of talking about bits and bytes, Apple declared "We believe people with passion could change the world." The campaign preceded Apple's renaissance into the most valuable company in the world. "If that doesn't happen, you're a utility. And utilities get sold at commodity prices" [3].

### The CEO's Three Jobs

Chesky distills the CEO role to three functions [3]:
1. Articulate the vision
2. Develop the strategy
3. Hire people who fit the culture

"If you do those three things, you basically have a company" [3].

## Alfred Lin's Culture Framework

[[Alfred Lin]] provides the analytical scaffolding for Chesky's stories [3].

### Culture Defined

Lin defines company culture as: "Every day, the [core values] and [actions] of each member of the team in pursuit of our company [mission]" [3].

### Why Culture Matters: The FIRST Framework

Lin organizes benefits into an acronym [3]:
- **F**irst principles for decision-making
- **I**dentity alignment on values
- **R**esilience and stability
- **S**howing what not to do (more important than showing what to do)
- **T**alent retention of the right employees

### The Evidence: Stock Returns

Lin cites research showing Fortune 100 Best Companies to Work For returned 11.8% annually from 1997-2003, nearly double the S&P 500 (6.8%) and Russell 3000 (6.04%) [3].

### Culture as Daily Habit

Lin compares culture to fitness: "You just can't be fit if you don't do it as a daily habit. Eventually you get out of shape, then you get fat." Culture must be integrated into hiring (interview for culture fit, not just skills), performance reviews, and daily operations [3].

### Zappos: One Year to Write Ten Values

At Zappos, Lin and Tony Hsieh asked all employees what core values they identified with. They received 37 submissions and spent a year whittling them to 10. The year was necessary because single words are meaningless: "If you just come up with the word 'honesty,' I mean, give me a break. Everybody wants the culture to be honest" [3].

Two Zappos examples [3]:
- **"Deliver WOW through service"**: Specifically committed to a "wow experience," supported by a paragraph defining what "wow" meant for employees, customers, partners, and investors.
- **"Be humble"**: Derived from the founders not liking arrogant people. Flip the negative to find the value.

### Patrick Lencioni's Five Dysfunctions

Lin references "The Five Dysfunctions of a Team" to illustrate how culture failures cascade [3]:
1. Absence of trust prevents honest debate
2. Fear of conflict prevents arriving at right answers
3. Lack of commitment follows from unclear answers
4. Avoidance of accountability follows from weak commitment
5. Inattention to results follows from no accountability

## References

1. [Creating Good Company Culture (and Sticking to It)](https://ycombinator.com/library/5p-creating-good-company-culture-and-sticking-to-it) -- Kathryn Minshew (2016)
2. [On Starting and Scaling Recruiting Software Startup Lever](https://ycombinator.com/library/6I-on-starting-and-scaling-recruiting-software-startup-lever) -- [[Sarah Nahm]], Holly Liu (2019)
3. [How to Start a Startup: Building Company Culture, Part II](https://ycombinator.com/library/JO-how-to-start-a-startup-building-company-culture-part-ii) -- [[Ben Silbermann]], [[Patrick Collison]], [[John Collison]] (2014)
4. [How to Start a Startup: Building Company Culture, Part I](https://ycombinator.com/library/JN-how-to-start-a-startup-building-company-culture-part-i) -- [[Brian Chesky]], [[Alfred Lin]] (2014)

title: Scaling and Later-Stage Operations
body:
# Scaling and Later-Stage Operations

[[Sam Altman]] outlines common issues founders face when their startups are 12-24 months in and doing well. The central shift: before [[Product-Market Fit]], the number one job is building a great product. As the company grows past approximately 25 employees, the main job shifts from building a great product to building a great company [1].

## Management

### The Transition Point

There is very little management in the beginning, and that works well. When there are fewer than 20 employees, most companies have everyone report to the founder. That is optimal for the early stage. But what works at 20 employees can be disastrous at 40. Structure must be in place before it is painfully necessary [1].

The starting structure is simple: every employee knows who their manager is, everyone has exactly one manager, every manager knows their direct reports, and people are clustered into sensible teams. "Clarity and simplicity are important -- I wouldn't try to innovate on management structure" [1].

A clear mission and clear goals reduce the burden of management. The better the job a founder does with these, the less management feels overbearing [1].

### Common Management Failures

**Avoiding senior hires**: In the early days, hiring senior people is usually a mistake. As the company scales, adding executives who have built companies before becomes valuable [1].

**Not delegating**: Many founders take on too much personally. This works temporarily but creates a breaking point as the company grows. Delegation is essential [1].

**Delegating poorly**: Some founders delegate grunt work but retain all decisions. This fails for scalability and for making employees effective. Instead, empower employees to own decision processes. "Let them know how you think about a problem, but let them own the decision process" [1]. Delegate the things you do not want to do. Retain involvement in the one thing you care most about [1].

**Personal disorganization**: Developing a system to track tasks and follow-ups is not critical when focused purely on product, but becomes crucial as the founder becomes a manager [1].

**Undercommunicating**: After a certain point, one-on-one conversations with everyone are impossible. The solution: learn to communicate in clear, succinct writing and share it with everyone. "It's one of the highest leverage things you can do, and it scales to thousands of people" [1]. All-hands meetings at least monthly remain important, as do one-on-ones with direct reports [1].

### The Alignment Test

Altman describes a classic diagnostic: "If I asked 10 employees what the top three goals are for the company, would they say the same thing?" Founders always say yes. When Altman actually asks the employees, no two say the same thing. Founders consistently overestimate how effectively they communicate their ideas. Reiterating the roadmap and goals must become habitual [1].

## HR

Most founders ignore HR early, but continuing to ignore it as the company scales is a major mistake. Good HR means three things: a clear management structure, a way for people to raise workplace concerns, and pathways for career evolution [1].

### Performance and Compensation

Performance feedback should happen frequently, even if light. People benefit from regularly hearing how they are doing [1].

Compensation must be structured with bands to keep things fair. "People talk, and they will eventually find out co-workers' compensation levels. If it's all over the place, it can be a complete disaster" [1].

### Equity

YC company data suggests that the most successful companies give out significant equity. Altman advises planning to give away 3-5% of the company each year for ten years from founding. This includes [refresher grants](https://blog.wealthfront.com/how-long-should-you-stay-at-your-job/) for employees approaching vesting cliffs. "You never want an employee in a place where they start thinking about leaving because they've vested three out of their four years. Always stay in front of people's vesting schedules" [1].

### Hiring Process and Diversity

At the 12-24 month mark with [[Product-Market Fit]] achieved, hire a full-time recruiter. Before that is premature. Additional practices [1]:
- **Build a diverse team**: Unity of vision, diversity of backgrounds. Myopic culture is less effective.
- **Announce offers internally**: Up to a few hundred employees, share potential job offers on an internal mailing list. Often someone will know something relevant about the candidate.
- **Structure onboarding**: Have a program so every new employee's first week is planned.
- **Build paths for early employees**: The original 10-15 employees may not fit new executive roles but are loved by peers and highly productive. Proactively discuss their career paths.

### Burnout Monitoring

Building a company is a marathon. Working 100-hour weeks is not sustainable. Monitor the team for burnout, ensure people take vacations, have new challenges, and do new things [1].

## Company Productivity

Building a company that can innovate repeatedly is the hardest thing in business. Most companies do one great thing then stop. Alignment is the key lever -- companies become unproductive when people are not on the same page or do not understand priorities [1].

The solution is a clear mission, roadmap, and goals. Everyone should know the roadmap for the next 3-6 months or a year. Transparent communication through weekly management meetings with direct reports and monthly all-hands meetings is critical [1].

### Longer-Term Productivity Practices

**Offsites**: Take the best people outside the normal workspace for a weekend of big-picture thinking [1].

**Legal document organization**: Assign someone to collect every agreement the company has signed. This saves headaches later [1].

**FP&A**: Building a great financial model of the business enables optimization that most founders miss. Most companies do not hire for this until they have a few hundred employees, but doing it sooner is worthwhile [1].

**Dedicated fundraiser**: Hiring a full-time fundraiser after the B round, whose job is to prepare for the C round, yields better results and less dilution than hiring an investment banker later [1].

**Unit economics**: Figure out how to make more money from each user than you spend, sooner rather than later. Most great companies historically had good unit economics soon after monetization, even if the company as a whole lost money. "It can be tempting to paper over a problem with the business by spending more money instead of fixing the product or service, but this is a major trap" [1].

**Runway management**: Even with great unit economics, never get down to just a couple months of cash in the bank [1].

## Founder Psychology

As the company succeeds, the highs get higher but the lows keep getting lower. People start rooting against the founder. Journalists and internet critics find ways to pick apart the work. Learning to ignore critics early is important [1].

Altman recommends making a genuine long-term commitment: "Have a strategy that assumes you will work on your company for the next 10 years -- it can be an extreme advantage that few founders leverage" [1].

Taking vacations is non-negotiable. Founders who go three or four years without a real vacation will burn out [1].

Focus must be maintained. It is what made the company successful in the first place. Common focus-killers include burnout and talking to potential acquirers [1].

## Marketing and PR

Ignore marketing and PR for a long time. "Press will not make your startup." But once the product is working, shift to caring about it a little [1].

Critical rules:
- Never outsource key messaging to a PR firm or head of marketing. The founder must figure out the company's message [1].
- Pick three or four journalists to develop close relationships with. "No journalist wants to talk to a PR firm; they're much happier to hear from the founder" [1].

## Dealmaking

Business development can start to matter at 12-24 months, assuming a great product exists. Key principles [1]:
- Develop personal connections with counterparts. No one wants to feel used transactionally.
- Set up competitive situations. This is basic negotiation -- you get good terms when you have alternatives and stay persistent.
- Ask for what you want. "Most of the time you won't get laughed out of the room, and you might actually get it" [1].

## References

1. [Later Stage Advice for Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4q-later-stage-advice-for-startups) -- [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)

title: Recruiting Early Team Members
body:
# Recruiting Early Team Members

How do you get a co-founder, a first employee, or an early partner to join your startup? [[Dalton Caldwell]] argues that the answer begins with self-conviction. Before you can convince anyone else, you must first convince yourself [1].

## Convince Yourself First

"If you're not fully committed or if you have grave doubts that your idea is any good or it's even worth trying, how can you ever convince someone else?" [1]. People can tell when a founder does not believe in what they are doing. Genuine conviction creates a magnetism that flows outward to co-founders, employees, customers, and partners alike [1].

The signal that you are convinced: "Do you ever know that feeling where an idea enters your head and you can't sleep at night and you can't think about anything else but it? And it starts to pervade your thoughts? That's a good sign" [1]. Conversely, if you are immediately cynical about the idea, not excited, or able to set it aside easily, that is a sign you are not convinced [1].

## Why Conviction Matters: Resilience Through Setbacks

Caldwell explains the mechanism through which self-conviction translates to startup survival. He has consistently observed two patterns at YC [1]:

**Teams that secretly don't believe** in what they are doing give up when they hit rough patches -- and every startup hits rough patches [1].

**Teams that are deeply convicted** overcome almost every kind of setback: running out of money, having to completely pivot and change the idea, losing team members. "If there's something deeply held in the mind of the startup founders about why they're doing what they're doing, they can keep going" [1].

This connects directly to [[Founder Mindset]] and [[Paul Graham]]'s concept of determination as the most important founder quality.

## Work on What Excites You

Caldwell makes a specific argument about idea selection that runs counter to conventional wisdom [1]. He encourages founders to work on the thing they are most excited about, even at the expense of:

- An idea that seems easier to raise money for
- An idea that seems more commercially viable
- An idea that impresses other people

The reasoning: "It would appear that it's equally hard to succeed in a startup that you don't care about and one that you do. And it's almost equally hard to succeed in a startup that's a very ambitious, audacious idea as it is to do something super incremental that's not that exciting" [1].

If the difficulty is roughly equal, always choose the more ambitious or exciting path. Caldwell uses a travel metaphor: "If you have to travel a thousand miles to go somewhere you want to visit versus travel a thousand miles to somewhere you're ambivalent about -- always go to the place you're much more excited about" [1]. Your excitement and enthusiasm increase the probability of arrival [1].

## Can Conviction Be Built Over Time?

Caldwell acknowledges that not everyone starts with a burning obsession. Conviction can grow through iteration [1].

The process: work on something as a side project. Pay attention to whether it feels like fun or like work. "If it feels like not work to work on your startup or your side project or what have you, and it's just something you want to do because it's entertaining or enjoyable to you, that's a good sign" [1].

External validation creates a positive feedback loop. When you put something into the world and people recognize and appreciate it, the positive response makes you more excited about spending time on it. "You can definitely get data points from the external world that cause you to take your side project or your idea more seriously" [1].

This connects to [[Dalton Caldwell]]'s broader advice on [[Creating Luck]] through building things and sharing them with people.

## References

1. [How Do You Convince Someone to Join Your Startup?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5b-how-do-you-convince-someone-to-join-your-startup) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (n.d.)

title: Convincing Investors
body:
# Convincing Investors

Convincing investors is not a performance problem. It is a truth problem. [[Paul Graham]] argues that inexperienced founders make the same mistake as people who hurt themselves lifting heavy things: they try to lift with their back instead of their legs. Founders try to convince with their pitch rather than letting the startup do the work [1]. The correct approach is to first understand why the startup is worth investing in, then explain that clearly.

[[Dalton Caldwell]] extends this principle beyond investors to all stakeholders: co-founders, employees, customers, and partners. "If you really have that fire, that you're onto something that's worth doing and you're convinced, then it's much easier to convince other people to join you" [3]. The mechanism is the same -- genuine conviction is more persuasive than polished performance. See [[Recruiting Early Team Members]] for the full treatment.

## What Investors Are Looking For

Investors seek startups that will be very successful. Because startup outcomes follow a power law with a steep curve, most investors treat "big success" as binary. The conventional wisdom is that roughly 15 startups per year become the big successes, and investors are interested in a founder only if there is some chance, however small, of being one of those 15 [1].

Three things make a startup look like a sufficiently good bet [1]:

1. **Formidable founders**
2. **A promising market**
3. **Some evidence of success so far**

## Formidable Founders

Most investors decide within the first few minutes whether a founder seems like a winner or a loser, and once that opinion is set, it is hard to change [1]. If investors think you are a winner, they focus on reasons to invest. If not, they focus on reasons not to [1].

Graham defines "formidable" as someone who seems like they will get what they want, regardless of obstacles. The way to seem formidable as an inexperienced founder is to stick to the truth [1]. Graham writes:

> "Convince yourself that your startup is worth investing in, and then when you explain this to investors they'll believe you." [1]

This requires being a domain expert. Investors can tell quickly whether founders truly understand their market by how they answer questions [1].

## The Three-Step Recipe

Graham distills the approach for founders who are not naturally formidable [1]:

1. Make something worth investing in.
2. Understand why it is worth investing in.
3. Explain that clearly to investors.

Use the same matter-of-fact language used to convince yourself. Conciseness is a test many investors explicitly use [1]. Alfred Lin emphasizes this is doubly important because the explanation must work at one remove -- it must convince not just the partner you speak with, but that partner when re-telling it to colleagues [1].

## Handling Rejection

Getting rejected is common -- even for the best companies. Graham advises against trying to bluff about other investors' interest [1]. The better approach is to explain why investors rejected you and why they are mistaken. Experienced investors know that the best ideas are also the scariest [1].

Dropbox was part of a YC Boston batch. All the Boston investors passed. A couple weeks later, Dropbox raised a Series A from Sequoia [1].

## Authenticity and Motivation

The defining quality of founders who build billion-dollar companies is that they are genuinely interested in what they are building. The company is their project. Every billionaire founder could have stopped sooner. They kept working because there was nothing else they would rather do [2].

## References

1. [How to Convince Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors) -- [[Paul Graham]] (August 2013)
2. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) -- [[Paul Graham]] (December 2020)
3. [How Do You Convince Someone to Join Your Startup?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5b-how-do-you-convince-someone-to-join-your-startup) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (n.d.)

title: Doing Things That Don't Scale
body:
# Doing Things That Don't Scale

One of the most common types of advice given at Y Combinator is to do things that don't scale [1]. [[Paul Graham]] wrote the definitive essay on this in July 2013, arguing that startups take off because the founders make them take off. The belief that startups either take off or don't -- that you build something, make it available, and either people beat a path to your door or the market doesn't exist -- is wrong [1].

"Actually startups take off because the founders make them take off. There may be a handful that just grew by themselves, but usually it takes some sort of push to get them going. A good metaphor would be the cranks that car engines had before they got electric starters. Once the engine was going, it would keep going, but there was a separate and laborious process to get it going." [1]

## Recruit Users Manually

The most common unscalable thing founders must do at the start is recruit users manually. Nearly all startups have to do this. You cannot wait for users to come to you [1].

Stripe is the canonical example. Despite solving an urgent problem -- one where you might think users would find them on their own -- the Collison brothers were famous within YC for aggressive early user acquisition [1]. They invented what YC calls the "Collison installation": when anyone agreed to try Stripe, instead of saying "Great, we'll send you a link," the Collisons would say "Right then, give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot [1].

Two reasons founders resist manual recruitment [1]:
1. **Shyness and laziness.** They would rather sit at home writing code than go out and talk to strangers and probably be rejected by most of them. But for a startup to succeed, at least one founder (usually the CEO) will have to spend a lot of time on sales and marketing.
2. **The numbers seem too small.** Founders think this cannot be how the big, famous startups got started. They underestimate compound growth. If you have 100 users and grow 10% a week, after a year you have 14,000 users; after two years, 2 million [1].

Airbnb is another classic example. In the early days, Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia went door to door in New York, recruiting new users and helping existing ones improve their listings. Graham recalls them always showing up to Tuesday YC dinners with rolly bags because they had just flown back from somewhere [1].

## The Fragility of Early Startups

Almost all startups are fragile initially [1]. Airbnb now seems like an unstoppable juggernaut, but early on it was so fragile that about 30 days of going out and engaging in person with users made the difference between success and failure [1].

Inexperienced founders, investors, reporters, and know-it-alls on forums unconsciously judge larval startups by the standards of established ones. "They're like someone looking at a newborn baby and concluding 'there's no way this tiny creature could ever accomplish anything'" [1].

The right question about an early-stage startup: not "is this company taking over the world?" but "how big could this company get if the founders did the right things?" [1]. Even Bill Gates underestimated Microsoft -- he returned to Harvard for the fall semester after starting the company [1]. Microsoft did not seem impressive when it was just a couple of guys in Albuquerque writing Basic interpreters for a market of a few thousand hobbyists [1].

## Delight Your Users

Founders should take extraordinary measures not just to acquire users but to make them happy [1]. Wufoo sent each new user a hand-written thank you note, for as long as they could (which turned out to be surprisingly long) [1].

Three reasons founders do not focus enough on delighting individual users [1]:

1. **Engineering training.** Engineers are trained to build robust and elegant things, not to be slavishly attentive to individual users. But at an early-stage startup, the founder is Kirk, not Scotty.
2. **Worry about scalability.** In their current state, they have nothing to lose. "See if you can make it happen" -- having too many users to personally delight would be a great problem to have.
3. **Low standards from experience.** Their standards for customer service have been set by big companies. Tim Cook does not send you a hand-written note after you buy a laptop. He cannot. But a small startup can, and that is an advantage of being small.

Graham states: "I have never once seen a startup lured down a blind alley by trying too hard to make their initial users happy" [1].

## Insanely Great Experience, Not Product

Steve Jobs's phrase "insanely great" applied to early startups means something different than it did at an established Apple [1]. For a larval startup, it is not the product that should be insanely great, but the experience of being your user. The product is just one component. You can and should give users an insanely great experience with an early, incomplete, buggy product, if you make up the difference with attentiveness [1].

This over-engagement is not just a permissible technique for getting growth rolling. For most successful startups it is a necessary part of the feedback loop that makes the product good [1]. The feedback from engaging directly with your earliest users will be the best you ever get. When you are big enough to resort to focus groups, you will wish you could go over to users' homes and offices like you did when there were only a handful of them [1].

## The Contained Fire Strategy

Sometimes the right unscalable trick is to focus on a deliberately narrow market. "It's like keeping a fire contained at first to get it really hot before adding more logs" [1].

Facebook did this. At first it was just for Harvard students -- a potential market of a few thousand people. But because they felt it was really for them, a critical mass signed up. When Graham interviewed Mark Zuckerberg at Startup School, Zuckerberg said that while it was a lot of work creating course lists for each school, doing that made students feel the site was their natural home [1].

Among companies, the best early adopters are usually other startups. They are more open to new things by nature and because, having just been started, they haven't made all their choices yet. When they succeed they grow fast, and you with them [1].

## Pulling a Meraki: Hardware Startups

For hardware startups, there is a variant called "pulling a Meraki" [1]. The Meraki founders (Robert Morris's grad students) got started by assembling their routers themselves. Hardware startups face a catch-22: the minimum order for a factory production run is usually several hundred thousand dollars, but without a product you cannot generate the growth to raise that money [1].

Pebble assembled the first several hundred watches themselves. If they had not, they probably would not have sold $10 million worth on Kickstarter [1]. Eric Migicovsky said one of the things he learned was "how valuable it was to source good screws" [1].

## The Consulting Technique

For B2B startups, Graham sometimes advises taking over-engagement to an extreme: pick a single user and act as if you were consultants building something just for that one user [1]. The initial user serves as the form for your mold. Keep tweaking till you fit their needs perfectly, and you will usually find you have made something other users want too [1].

At Viaweb, when merchants would not use the software themselves, Graham and Morris offered to make stores for them. "We felt pretty lame at the time. Instead of organizing big strategic e-commerce partnerships, we were trying to sell luggage and pens and men's shirts" [1]. But it was exactly the right thing because it taught them how merchants would use the software. The feedback loop was sometimes near-instantaneous: in the middle of building a merchant's site, Graham would find he needed a feature they did not have, spend a couple hours implementing it, and resume building the site [1].

## Being Your Own Software

A more extreme variant: when you only have a small number of users, you can sometimes do by hand things you plan to automate later [1]. This lets you launch faster, and when you finally automate yourself out of the loop, you know exactly what to build because you have muscle memory from doing it yourself.

Stripe delivered "instant" merchant accounts to its first users by having the founders manually sign them up for traditional merchant accounts behind the scenes [1].

## Big Launches Usually Don't Work

Graham explicitly warns against the "Big Launch" -- launching simultaneously in 8 publications with embargoes on a Tuesday [1]. Think of successful startups: how many of their launches do you remember? All you need from a launch is some initial core of users. How well you are doing a few months later will depend more on how happy you made those users than how many there were [1].

Partnerships also usually do not work as a way to get growth started. "Six months later they're all saying the same thing: that was way more work than we expected, and we ended up getting practically nothing out of it" [1].

## Startup Ideas as Vectors

Graham proposes thinking of startup ideas not as scalars but as pairs: what you are going to build, plus the unscalable thing you are going to do initially to get the company going [1]. In most cases the second component is: recruit users manually and give them an overwhelmingly good experience [1].

In the best case, both components of the vector contribute to the company's DNA. The unscalable things you do to get started are not merely a necessary evil but change the company permanently for the better [1]. If you have to be aggressive about user acquisition when you are small, you will probably still be aggressive when you are big. If you have to work hard to delight users when you only have a handful, you will keep doing it when you have a lot [1].

## Connection to Other Principles

The essay on [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]] connects directly to Graham's essay on [[Default Alive or Default Dead]]. In that essay, he argues that instead of hiring to boost growth, startups should seek growth "by doing things that don't scale, or by redesigning the product in the way only founders can" [3]. Airbnb waited 4 months after raising money at the end of YC before hiring their first employee. The founders were terribly overworked, but they were overworked evolving Airbnb into the astonishingly successful organism it became [3].

Graham's earlier essay on the hardest lessons also reinforces this: "Release early" and "keep pumping out features" -- get a version 1 out fast, then improve it based on users' reactions [2]. Wufoo released their form-builder before the underlying database, getting 83,000 people to sit in the driver's seat. Linux users complained about Flash, so Wufoo rewrote their software. If they had waited to release everything at once, they would not have discovered the problem until it was deeply wired in [2].


## DoorDash: Deliveries as Strategy (Tony Xu)

[[Tony Xu]] and all four DoorDash co-founders did every delivery themselves for the first two years. This was not just a phase -- twelve years after founding, every person at DoorDash, including Xu, still does deliveries every year [4].

The practice produced three categories of insight no dashboard could provide:

1. **Customer insight**: The earliest customers were families with young children. The founders learned what they wanted, which restaurants they preferred, and that they kept coming back without marketing spend or discounts [4].
2. **Geographic strategy**: By personally experiencing deliveries in different areas, the founders discovered that need was strongest in suburbs, not city centers. This became DoorDash's contrarian strategy against better-funded competitors targeting urban density [4].
3. **Driver recruitment credibility**: Having done every delivery gave the founders firsthand knowledge of the job, making them credible when recruiting the first drivers on Craigslist [4].

## Bump: One-Person Customer Support (David Lieb)

[[David Lieb]] was the sole customer support person at Bump from founding through the Google acquisition, replying to every email and blocking 1-1.5 hours daily for it. "It taught me that you listen to people, and generally, they will tell you what you should do" [5]. When seeking product direction, Lieb called the top 100 Bump users globally -- an unscalable act that produced the pivotal insight that users were sharing photos, not contacts [5].

## PlanGrid: Lending iPads and Walking Users Through the App Store (Tracy Young)

[[Tracy Young]] describes one of the most extreme examples of unscalable tactics. PlanGrid's target users -- construction workers -- did not own iPads in 2012. PlanGrid's solution: loan out iPads for free, then sit with each user to walk them through the App Store, type in passwords, set up accounts, and load their construction projects [6]. Young called every user weekly: "Hey it's Tracy, just checking in. How's the new update going?" [6].

## Lob: The Inkjet Printer Assembly Line (Harry Zhang)

[[Harry Zhang]] ran Lob's initial fulfillment on his home inkjet printer. Orders came through the API into a database, and Zhang printed letters from his apartment. At peak early volume, he and his co-founder watched TV while running an assembly line of letters they stuffed by hand [7].

## Gobble: CEO as Delivery Driver (Ooshma Garg)

[[Ooshma Garg]] prototyped Gobble with email, PayPal, and her personal car. She created a calendar of chefs and their daily menus, emailed friends asking if they wanted to order, collected payments via PayPal, and personally drove deliveries [8]. When Gobble pivoted to dinner kits, the team tested the concept by having TaskRabbit workers deliver prototype kits to friends in Palo Alto [8].

## DoorDash: The One-Hour Launch (Stanley Tang)

[[Stanley Tang]] provides one of the most extreme examples of launching fast. DoorDash's first version -- paloaltodelivery.com -- launched in under an hour. It consisted of PDF menus from Palo Alto restaurants and a phone number (the founders' personal cell phones). There was no backend, no dispatch system, no driver network [9].

The first customer called wanting Thai food. The founders, driving home, decided to personally pick up and deliver the order. The customer turned out to be the author of a book called "Weed the People" [9].

The validation signal was not the quality of the product but the strength of the need: "When people are willing to put up with all this" -- PDF menus, phone calls, no app -- "we knew we found the need" [9].

The co-founders used Square to charge customers (until Square shut them down for suspected money laundering), Google Docs to track orders, and Apple's Find My Friends to monitor driver locations. Tang personally emailed every new customer each night with personalized messages: "If I see someone order chicken skewers from Orange Hummus, I would say, 'I love Orange Hummus. How were the chicken skewers?'" [9].

Tang's three lessons: treat startup ideas like experiments, launch fast (under an hour), and do things that don't scale because "doing things that don't scale is one of your biggest competitive advantages when you're starting out" [9].

## Teespring: The Boulder Metaphor (Walker Williams)

[[Walker Williams]] of Teespring describes unscalable tactics through three phases [9]:

### Finding First Users
There is no silver bullet for user acquisition. Williams equates early user acquisition to pushing a boulder uphill: "the incline is the steepest" at the beginning, gets easier with progress, and eventually the boulder rolls on its own [9].

Williams advises against giving products away for free: "People have a different -- they treat products that are free in a much different way than a paid product. And oftentimes it can give you a false sense of security" [9].

### Turning Users into Champions
A champion is a user who talks about and advocates for the product. Williams creates champions through customer service (he and his co-founder handled all support until $130,000-$140,000/month in revenue), proactive outreach to current and departing customers, and obsessive monitoring of social media [9].

On detractors: "One detractor who's had a terrible experience on your platform is enough to reverse the progress of ten champions" [9].

### Speed Over Scalability
The product at launch is almost certainly not the product that scales. Williams's co-founder Evan, faced with enterprise features that would take a month to build properly, duplicated the entire codebase and database, built a separate product in three to four days, validated which features mattered, then integrated the winners [9].

Williams's rule: "Only worry about the next order of magnitude" [9].

### Never Stop
Williams is emphatic that doing things that don't scale should continue as long as possible: "There's not some magical moment. It's not the Series A. It's not when you hit a certain revenue milestone. It should be ripped from you" [9].

## Airbnb: The Photography Service as Scaling Pattern (Brian Chesky)

[[Brian Chesky]] describes the full lifecycle of a single unscalable tactic scaling into a global service. A New York host could not transfer photos from her camera to her computer. Chesky asked: "What if you could press a button and a professional photographer showed up?" She said it would be magic. "Next day I knocked on her door and said, 'I'm here'" [10].

The progression [10]:
1. Chesky personally photographed homes, renting a camera in Brooklyn
2. Started hiring contract photographers
3. Got an intern to manage the contractors
4. The intern became full-time, managing other interns managing contractors
5. Only after the process was fully understood did they build the technology

Airbnb now operates one of the largest on-demand photography networks in the world -- 5,000 photographers, hundreds of thousands of homes photographed. "We did it only after we knew exactly what the perfect service was" [10].

Chesky credits [[Paul Graham]]'s advice: "It's better to have 100 people that love you than to have a million people that just sort of like you." The logic: you cannot make a million people care, but 100 passionate users will each tell 100 others [10].

## References

1. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) -- [[Paul Graham]] (July 2013)
2. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
3. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)
4. [How To Build The Future: Tony Xu](https://ycombinator.com/library/MJ-how-to-build-the-future-tony-xu) -- [[Tony Xu]], [[Garry Tan]] (2025)
5. [On Starting and Scaling One of the Biggest iOS Apps](https://ycombinator.com/library/5f-on-starting-and-scaling-one-of-the-biggest-ios-apps) -- [[David Lieb]], Gustaf Alstromer (n.d.)
6. [On starting and scaling construction startup PlanGrid](https://ycombinator.com/library/6L-on-starting-and-scaling-construction-startup-plangrid) -- [[Tracy Young]], Kat Manalac (2019)
7. [On starting and scaling direct mail automation startup Lob](https://ycombinator.com/library/6a-on-starting-and-scaling-direct-mail-automation-startup-lob) -- [[Harry Zhang]], [[Kevin Hale]] (2019)
8. [On starting and scaling meal kit company Gobble](https://ycombinator.com/library/69-on-starting-and-scaling-meal-kit-company-gobble) -- [[Ooshma Garg]] (2016)
9. [How to Start a Startup: Getting Started, Getting Press, and Doing Things That Don't Scale](https://ycombinator.com/library/JL-how-to-start-a-startup-getting-started-getting-press-and-doing-things-that-don-t-scale) -- [[Stanley Tang]], [[Walker Williams]], Justin Kan (2014)
10. [How to Start a Startup: Building Company Culture, Part I](https://ycombinator.com/library/JN-how-to-start-a-startup-building-company-culture-part-i) -- [[Brian Chesky]], [[Alfred Lin]] (2014)

title: B2B Pricing
body:
# B2B Pricing

Pricing is one of the most common questions early-stage founders ask, and one of the easiest to overthink. [[Tom Blomfield]], YC Group Partner, presents a framework built on three elements -- value, cost, and competition -- with the value equation as the dominant driver [1].

## The Value Equation

The value equation is by far the most important element. It accounts for 80-90% of getting the price right [1]. The method: sit down with the champion (the person at the customer who wants the product), and together write down what value the product will deliver to their company. That value falls into three categories: cost savings, time savings, or revenue increases [1].

### Worked Example

A customer service AI tool sold to a company with 100 support agents. Each agent costs $100,000 fully loaded (salary plus offices, health insurance, overhead). Total customer service cost: $10 million. If the AI tool eliminates 20% of queries, that is $2 million of potential savings [1].

The pricing rule: charge 25-50% of the value delivered. The customer keeps roughly two-thirds, the startup keeps roughly one-third [1]. In the example, $2 million in savings yields a contract price of roughly $700,000 [1].

The value equation also produces the success metrics for a pilot. In the example, if a one-month trial with 10 agents shows at least 20% query reduction, the value equation holds. If the pilot shows 15% or 25%, the pricing can be adjusted accordingly [1].

### Why Value Comes First

Blomfield warns against starting with cost-plus pricing, which "always ends up with you underpricing your software" [1]. Founders who have spent months building a product often calibrate against the last software they personally bought -- a $19/month GitHub subscription or ChatGPT -- and propose ludicrously low numbers. "Asking for tens or even hundreds of thousands of dollars can feel very uncomfortable. You almost can't say it with a straight face" [1]. The value equation provides the justification that makes large numbers defensible.

The value equation is also a sales tool: the champion takes it to their boss or CFO to justify the purchase [1].

## Cost as a Floor

Cost should never be the starting point for pricing, only a floor [1]. If the value equation yields a $700,000 contract and costs (OpenAI fees, AWS) are $200,000, margins are healthy. If the value equation yields $150,000 but costs are $200,000, the business is unsustainable -- either demonstrate more value, change what you are building, or exit the business [1].

The target for software margins is 80-90% [1].

### A Note on Credits

Startups receive large credits from AWS, Microsoft, and OpenAI. Blomfield warns to treat these as cash costs: "Don't assume you'll have unlimited credits forever. It'll totally mess up your margins" [1].

### Pricing Below Cost

Pricing at or below cost is a "really, really risky maneuver" typically used in land-grab situations where the founder bets costs will decline dramatically [1]. With LLM costs dropping rapidly from providers like OpenAI and Anthropic, there may be a case for slightly lower pricing in anticipation of margin improvement, but Blomfield advises startups to maintain 80-90% gross margins [1].

## Competition

If a direct competitor enters at half your price, the instinct is to start a price war -- undercutting them, then they undercut you, in a race to the bottom. Blomfield warns against this: "Competing solely on pricing really is not a winning maneuver" [1].

The airline industry illustrates the endpoint: a commodity product (a seat across the country) with extreme competition produces an average net profit margin of 2.7%. "It's a brutal, brutal business" [1].

The alternative: differentiate the product. "It can't be an apples-for-apples comparison. Your product needs to be differentiated" [1]. Pick a niche, focus on specific integrations or industries, and make the product incomparable to competitors.

## Pricing Structure

### Mirror What Customers Know

Ask the champion how they pay for other similar software. Are they used to monthly flat fees, per-seat pricing, usage bands, or credits? "Pick a pricing strategy they're used to" [1]. People are wary of uncapped usage-based pricing, so caps are advisable [1].

### Committed Recurring Revenue Over Usage

Monthly or annual recurring revenue (MRR/ARR) is preferable to pure usage-based pricing because it protects revenue during economic downturns [1]. A practical technique: start with usage-based pricing for new customers, run it for a month or two to establish a baseline, then offer a minimum monthly commitment with volume discounts. If the customer averages $15,000/month in usage, offer a $12,000/month flat fee on a 12-month contract [1].

### Signing Authority Hack

Ask the champion what dollar amount they can personally sign off on without CFO or legal approval. If their limit is $15,000, keep pilot pricing at $14,999 to keep things moving quickly [1].

## Published Prices vs. "Contact Sales"

Software developers often prefer transparent pricing with a "click to buy" button. But for enterprise contracts, the value equation differs for each customer. A single published price either overprices for low-value customers (losing them) or underprices for high-value customers (leaving money on the table) [1].

The standard approach: one or two cheaper plans (individual, small team) with basic functionality, and an enterprise plan gated behind "contact sales." Features commonly gated behind enterprise pricing include SOC 2 audit reports, single sign-on, audit logs, compliance reports, and data residency requirements -- things individuals do not care about but enterprises cannot live without [1]. This allows pricing enterprise customers up to 10x more than small customers [1].

## Pricing Dictates Sales Channels

A useful rule of thumb: 5:1 ratio between new signed ARR and total salesperson compensation (base plus commission) [1]. If a salesperson earns $100,000/year, they should close $500,000 in new ARR.

This creates very different sales models depending on deal size [1]:

| Annual Contract Value | Deals Needed per Year | Sales Model |
|---|---|---|
| $500,000 | 1 | Whale hunting, 4-6 active deals |
| $25,000 | 20 | ~2 deals per month, traditional AE |
| $1,000 | 500 | ~42 per month, inside sales/call center |

At $83/month contract values, a traditional sales team is not viable. The motion shifts to self-serve or inside sales [1].

## Free Trials and Pilots

Long free trials are counterproductive because the customer has not committed to using the product [1]. Keep pilots to two to four weeks with clear success criteria from the value equation.

A stronger technique: push for an annual contract from the start with a 30-60 day money-back guarantee. "By default it becomes a recurring contract. You can count this as recurring revenue pretty much straight away" [1].

## Playing to Startup Strengths

Founders sometimes ask whether they should fabricate a larger team on their website. Blomfield advises against it. Instead, play to the strengths of being small: "Say to your customers, 'You can have the phone number of the founders, and we're on call 24/7 to come and fix your problems.' You're certainly not going to get that from Salesforce or Oracle" [1].

## When All Else Fails: The Ratchet Method

If the value equation is too uncertain, pick a number similar to other software the customer buys, then increase by 50% for each subsequent customer [1]. Start at $10,000; if they say yes, pitch $15,000 to the next customer, then $22,000. "When you start to lose more than 25% of potential deals based solely on price, you're now probably in the right ballpark" [1].

If every deal closes immediately, the price is almost certainly too low [1].

## Do Not Over-Optimize Early

The first two or three sales are the hardest the company will ever have to make. These initial five or ten customers will be a tiny fraction of future revenue. "Over-optimizing for pricing early is a big mistake. Pick a number" [1]. Prices can always increase as the product improves, new modules are added behind paywalls, and validation logos accumulate on the homepage [1].

## References

1. [How To Price For B2B](https://ycombinator.com/library/LI-how-to-price-for-b2b) — [[Tom Blomfield]] (2025)

title: Pricing and Monetization
body:
# Pricing and Monetization

Every batch, Y Combinator group partners find themselves repeating the same advice: start charging. [[Michael Seibel]], [[Harj Taggar]], and [[Brad Flora]] discuss why founders resist charging, why they should charge anyway, and the narrow exceptions where free is legitimate [1].

## Why Founders Resist Charging

The partners identify several common reasons founders talk themselves out of charging [1]:

**Optimizing for feedback over revenue.** YC pushes founders to get feedback and get users. Founders conclude it is easier to do this if the product is free, so they optimize for feedback at the expense of revenue [1].

**Perfectionism about pricing.** Founders believe they cannot go to market with a price and then change it. Taggar notes this is plainly false: "Every week you can change your pricing. If you look at some of the most successful startups, they have new pricing all the time" [1].

**Fear of losing the deal.** Founders selling to enterprises sometimes argue that charging will make it harder to close. They fail to realize the anti-signal of not charging: the enterprise customer wonders "Is this going to work? Are they going to be able to support us?" Putting a price on it removes those objections [1].

**The no-redos fallacy.** Founders believe they will never be able to change the price after setting one. [[Brad Flora]] points out that you can always grandfather existing users into their current plan and raise prices for new users. "If you're confident and if things go well, the users you have today should hopefully represent a small fraction of the users you have in six months or a year" [1].

## Just Pick a Number

The partners regularly pick a price for founders in office hours -- sometimes a number pulled out of thin air -- and tell them to start charging that evening. The look of fear on founders' faces is common, but it gets them moving [1].

Seibel tells the story of Drew Houston from Dropbox. When asked how he decided on $9.99 a month, Houston smiled and said he just picked a reasonable number. Later analysis suggested it might have cost hundreds of millions in revenue. "But it was fine. The point was to pick something reasonable that got them up and running" [1]. Good enough for Drew, good enough for you [1].

## The Three-Second Feedback Test

Seibel describes charging as one of the most powerful feedback mechanisms available: "The best feedback you're gonna get about your product is in the three seconds after you tell them the price, and the facial expression that they make or the tone of voice that they use -- that's often the most honest reaction you're gonna get about your product" [1].

## The Danger of Free Pilots

Taggar warns that free pilots, especially with large companies, can waste years of a founder's life. Founders believe they have customers -- "Oh yeah, Apple's a customer" -- but when pressed on how much Apple pays, the answer is nothing [1]. Without revenue, the founder has no way to know whether the product solves a real problem. Revenue is the ultimate validation signal.

Flora adds that with enterprise sales, the goal is to prove that you can change behavior at the company. The champion on the other side must spend some capital -- political capital, financial capital -- to use your product. "If you're just selling free pilots, you're not really proving that much" [1].

## Legitimate Exceptions to Charging

The partners identify three business models where a free tier is a core component, not an excuse [1]:

### Open Core

Companies like GitLab use an open-source product to drive adoption, then upsell enterprise features. The difference from lazy free pilots: "The open core companies are actually very sophisticated about this and know exactly what they're looking for when they're going out there and getting these quote-unquote free users" [1]. There is a clear plan and everything is tracked.

### Freemium

Slack is the canonical example. The freemium model is legitimate when two conditions hold [1]:
1. There is an actual constraint users want to get around (more users, more storage, more messages)
2. The founder has the discipline to track whether free users convert to paid users and over what time frame

The problem arises when founders say "We're giving the product away for free and we think people will pay for it at some point." Without a conversion plan, free users build and build and increase the product surface area without ever generating revenue [1].

### Consumer Advertising Models

YouTube, Facebook, and Twitter needed massive user bases before advertising revenue made sense. This is a legitimate exception. But the founder must be honest about wanting that model. Seibel uses Airbnb as a counterexample: a less confident founder might have framed it as "a social network for travel" and avoided charging. That company was called Couchsurfing. Airbnb's willingness to handle the awkwardness of money on the platform was the right move [1].

## Core Takeaway

Flora summarizes: it is acceptable to be free in certain cases where there is a structured, intentional plan and path to getting paid. "But on the whole, the trick is to make sure that you're not giving away for free out of fear" [1]. If you do not put pricing out there and start charging, you prevent yourself from learning whether your product is something people want [1].


## The Platform Fee Model (Harry Zhang / Lob)

[[Harry Zhang]] describes a structural pricing mistake that affected Lob for years. Initially, Lob sold bundles of mail pieces at a per-piece price. Enterprise customers compared this directly to traditional mail providers: "Why are you five cents more expensive than everybody else?" [2]. The five cents represented Lob's technology value, but it was invisible.

The fix: split pricing into a platform fee ($25,000-$30,000 per year for API access) plus competitive per-piece mail pricing. This restructured the buying conversation. The insight: "It's important to differentiate and associate the value that customers see in your technology with what they pay for versus something that could be a commoditized product" [2].

## HIPAA as a Pricing Lever

Regulated compliance creates natural pricing power. Lob charges more for HIPAA-compliant offerings because it costs more and fewer competitors can provide it programmatically [2].

## References

1. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: Turning Users Into Paid Customers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J3-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-turning-users-into-paid-customers) — [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Brad Flora]] (2021)
2. [On starting and scaling direct mail automation startup Lob](https://ycombinator.com/library/6a-on-starting-and-scaling-direct-mail-automation-startup-lob) -- [[Harry Zhang]], [[Kevin Hale]] (2019)

title: Product-Market Fit
related: [[Segment: From Classroom Tool to Customer Data Platform]]
body:
# Product-Market Fit

Product-market fit describes the state where a startup's product satisfies strong market demand. It is one of the most referenced concepts in the YC canon, cited across 11+ articles in this wiki as the prerequisite for nearly every subsequent startup activity -- from [[Growth]] investment to [[Series A Fundraising]].

## The Concept

Product-market fit is the moment when a product resonates so strongly with its target market that demand begins to outpace the company's ability to supply. [[Sam Altman]] frames it as the precondition for growth: founders who focus on growth before building something people love are unlikely to succeed. Facebook had a product people talked about obsessively from the start; Airbnb slogged for 1,000 days before discovering how to make their product loved [1].

[[Paul Graham]] approaches the concept from the other direction: "In a sense there's just one mistake that kills startups: not making something users want" [2]. The organic method of finding PMF is to live in the future and build what is missing, rather than generating ideas on demand [3].

[[Michael Seibel]] grounds the concept in Marc Andreessen's original definition, which he calls "the single most influential post for me as an entrepreneur" [7]. Andreessen's formulation: "The customers are buying the product just as fast as you can make it -- or usage is growing just as fast as you can add more servers. Money from customers is piling up in your company checking account. You're hiring sales and customer support staff as fast as you can" [7]. Seibel restates this more directly: you have reached product-market fit when you are overwhelmed with usage, usually to the point where you cannot even make major changes to your product because you are swamped just keeping it running [7].

## The 80% Failure Rate

[[Peter Reinhardt]], co-founder and CEO of [[Segment: From Classroom Tool to Customer Data Platform|Segment]] (YC S11), states the stakes bluntly: "Eighty percent of all founders fail to find product market fit. So four out of five attempts to found a company just fail at this sort of earliest stage of even finding a problem that you can solve in a unique way" [14]. The remaining one in five then face the struggle of building the company from that foothold.

## Signals of Product-Market Fit

Several speakers describe how to recognize PMF:

**Seibel's test**: Look for "a frantic founding team trying to deal with ever-growing numbers of happy, loyal, and ideally paying customers" [7]. Founders often try to signal PMF by pointing to headcount or a major funding round. These are not the signals that matter. Until you are overwhelmed by demand, stay lean, keep burn low, and "resemble a Navy SEAL team instead of an Army battalion" [7].

**Altman's test**: When users actively recommend your product to friends, you are in the right place. Net Promoter Score can be a useful measurement [1].

**[[Anu Hariharan]]'s quantitative test**: If the retention curve flattens and runs parallel to the x-axis after an initial dip, and the long-term retention rate is in line with vertical benchmarks, the product has achieved fit [4]. See [[Growth]] for specific retention benchmarks by vertical.

**[[Emmett Shear]]'s feature test**: Once PMF is established, the question shifts from "does anyone want this?" to "what should we build next?" -- a qualitatively different problem [5].

**Reinhardt's "stepping on a land mine" test**: Reinhardt credits the Dropbox founders with the most vivid description: "Product market fit feels like stepping on a land mine" [14]. The defining characteristic is that it is unmistakable. "If you are at all questioning whether you have product market fit or not, you don't" [14]. When Segment found PMF with its analytics.js library, "literally every single metric went totally haywire" [14]. People were reaching out on LinkedIn demanding beta access. Thousands signed up. Pull requests appeared within 48 hours. The experience flipped from "pushing against the customer" to "the customer is running away with it and you're like, wait, hold on, it's not quite ready yet" [14].

**Reinhardt's customer reaction test**: When pitching five product ideas to a large customer, the first four generated polite interest: "That's great. I totally understand what the value is." Reinhardt translates: "Doesn't care is what that is code for. That's someone being nice" [14]. On the fifth idea, the customer interrupted: "Wait, sorry, you can do what?" Then turned to a colleague and said, "Set up a follow-up meeting with this team, this team, and this team. We also need to tell Joe about this." Reinhardt: "That is the feeling of product market fit" [14].

## Finding PMF: Market First, Solution Second

Seibel argues that founders "often hold too tightly onto solutions and too loosely onto problems" [7]. The problem -- the market -- is the real opportunity. The founder's unique v1 idea on how to solve that problem is usually wrong. Only through launching, talking to customers, and iterating will a product reach product-market fit. As Andreessen wrote, "the market pulls product out of the startup" [7].

Seibel extends Sequoia's metaphor of finding customers whose "hair is on fire" [7]. If your friend's hair was on fire, that fire would be the only thing they cared about. If you handed them a hose (the perfect solution), they would put the fire out immediately. But if you handed them a brick, they would still grab it and try to hit themselves on the head to extinguish the flames [7]. The implication: "You need to find problems so dire that users are willing to try half-baked, v1, imperfect solutions" [7].

This connects directly to [[Startup Ideas]]: choose a market where users have a real, meaningful problem, launch quickly, and listen to your users. An MVP in a good market is all you need to get initial customers in the door [7].

Reinhardt extends this with a direct rebuttal of the visionary method. He references Alan Kay's lecture on inventing the future -- imagining the future and pulling it back to the present. Reinhardt argues this approach is "amazing for research" but that "not a single successful company has actually been founded by doing that" [14]. Instead, finding PMF is "a very inductive process. You're going back and forth with customers, and you eventually find something that works" [14]. The market does not care about the founder's vision. "The market wants what it wants, and it will win every time" [14].

## The False PMF Trap

Seibel warns that founders frequently believe they have found product-market fit when they have not [7]. This is dangerous because it triggers premature scaling: hiring people, increasing burn, and optimizing the product before discovering what actually needs to be built [7]. See [[Early-Stage Mistakes]] for more on premature scaling.

Reinhardt provides the most detailed anatomy of false PMF signals from his own experience at Segment [14]:

**Idle interest is not PMF.** Segment's second product (an analytics tool) generated vague interest from potential customers. People agreed to coffee meetings. They expressed willingness to receive product updates. "Again and again they were vaguely interested. And they had willingness to meet and chat with us, and they said they wanted product updates. And so we thought, 'This must be it, right?'" [14]. It was not. Reinhardt distinguishes sharply between idle interest and genuine demand.

**Bullying customers is not PMF.** Segment's first product (a classroom lecture tool) was tested by convincing professors to try it. Professors agreed "sort of out of pity." When the founders stood in the back of the classroom and looked at student screens, "none of them were using the product" [14]. Students had opened their laptops and done other things. "Basically, putting a laptop into the classroom was the most distracting thing you could conceivably do" [14].

**Edge-case feature requests are not PMF.** On a sales trip for the analytics tool, Reinhardt found potential customers were happy with Mixpanel and Google Analytics but had "edge case features" they hoped Segment could solve. Reinhardt "tricked myself into believing that these little edge cases that we might be able to solve were actually a really wide gap that we could fill" [14]. The team then spent six months writing code based on this false signal.

**A single positive interaction is not PMF.** Reinhardt shows the transcript of a website chat at 3:00 AM where a stranger expressed interest. "This is idle interest" [14].

The emotional danger of false PMF is that founders "convince yourself into seeing mirages of it" [14]. At Segment, Reinhardt lost ten pounds in three weeks and went to the hospital twice for panic attacks during the search. Sam Altman developed scurvy during the founding of Loopt by forgetting to eat properly [14].

## Learning from Failure vs. Success

Reinhardt cites research showing that founders who fail to find PMF the first time are no more likely to succeed the second time (odds remain around 22%). But founders who succeed at finding PMF once see their odds rise to 34% the second time [14]. The implication: "There's actually not that much information encoded in failing to find product market fit," but "there's quite a bit encoded in feeling the success of actually understanding what did work" [14].

Without positive examples of PMF, founders lack a reference point. Reinhardt frames this using machine learning terminology: "I had a bunch of negative training set examples and I had no positive training set examples. And so of course my machine learning algorithm was like, 'I don't know'" [14].

## Kill Bad Ideas Fast

Reinhardt argues the biggest problem is not generating ideas but killing bad ones quickly enough. He cites Codecademy as exemplary: "They tried like 12 ideas in seven weeks. It was something totally absurd. But they legitimately tried them and they killed them so fast. And then four days before demo day, they started a new idea, which was Codecademy. And then it worked. On demo day, they had 300,000 users" [14]. That is the land mine effect: "nothing, nothing, nothing, nothing, nothing, nothing" -- then everything.

## The Foothold Principle

Reinhardt addresses the concern that an initial PMF might be in too small a niche. He endorses Paul Graham's view: "It's actually harder to find the first problem and solve any problem well than it is to find a route out" [14]. Once a startup solves one problem for a customer, the customer brings more problems. "You just keep solving adjacent problems. So once you find one thing, it's actually almost trivial to find the next thing" [14].

Segment's own history illustrates this. The initial foothold was 580 lines of open-source JavaScript code. From that foothold, Segment expanded into a customer data platform with customers paying over $100,000 per year. "The size of the business problem has almost nothing to do with the amount of code written" [14].

## Building Category Leaders

Reinhardt outlines the path from PMF to category leadership [14]. Category leaders (Amazon, Facebook, Salesforce) are ten to a thousand times larger than the next competitor. They achieve this through platforms -- exposing data and functionality so other businesses build on top of them. Salesforce has the AppExchange; Amazon has the reseller program.

To build a platform, a company needs roughly $100 million in revenue, so that capturing a few percentage points of the customer base represents a viable business for partners [14]. Jason Lemkin's framework breaks the path: zero to one million in revenue is "impossible" (finding PMF), one to ten million is "improbable" (a brutal grind for founders), and ten to one hundred million is "inevitable" (momentum carries the company forward) [14].

## PMF and Growth

The relationship between PMF and growth is sequential, not simultaneous. The consensus across multiple speakers is that growth investment before PMF is wasteful and dangerous. Hariharan's growth team guide is explicit: do not build a growth team before you have proven Retention [4]. Altman warns that growing on a mediocre product creates a "leaky bucket" -- users come in the door but do not stay [1].

Once PMF is achieved, users become "a free marketing and sales force." Word-of-mouth growth is powerful even for enterprise and hard tech companies [1].

## PMF and Fundraising
