PMF is increasingly the threshold for [[Series A Fundraising]]. Compared to the seed round, it is harder to raise a Series A on promise alone -- investors want to see results that demonstrate the product works [6]. The traction slides in a Series A deck are fundamentally evidence of PMF.

## PMF as a Spectrum, Not a Binary (David Lieb)

[[David Lieb]] argues product-market fit is staged, not binary: "You can have very great product-market fit with some customer base, but as you try to expand your customer base, you might find, 'Oh yeah, there's not product-market fit for these other people over here'" [8]. Even Google Photos, with over a billion users, does not have universal PMF. This challenges the common framing where PMF is a threshold you cross. In Lieb's view, it is a continuous frontier that expands with each new user segment [8].

## DoorDash: Organic Retention as PMF Signal (Tony Xu)

[[Tony Xu]] describes DoorDash's clearest PMF signal: organic consumer retention without marketing spend, coupons, or discounts. The earliest customers "kept coming back again without our throwing advertising at them" [9]. Later, when DoorDash had strong metrics but could not raise capital (2016-2018), Xu identified retention as the decisive metric: "One of the hardest things to really fool is the retention and the engagement of a product" [9].

## Perplexity: Follow-Up Questions Doubling Engagement (Aravind Srinivas)

[[Aravind Srinivas]] describes the feature that confirmed PMF: adding the ability to ask follow-up questions "doubled the engagement time on the site and also increased the number of questions every day." Most follow-up queries were "completely irrelevant to the first query because they just want to keep continuing the session" -- evidence of genuine engagement rather than frustration [10].

## PMF by Contrast (Parker Conrad)

[[Parker Conrad]] provides one of the sharpest descriptions of PMF versus non-PMF. At SigFig (no PMF): "four of them would just completely fail, and one of them would mostly fail." At Zenefits (PMF): "They all worked unbelievably well. Just unbelievably well" [11]. Conrad's test: "If you have product-market fit, are you asking yourself that?" The answer is no [11]. His recommendation: "If it's not working, give up immediately" [11].

## PMF Through Customer Honesty (Sarah Nahm)

[[Sarah Nahm]] describes Lever's path to PMF as a two-and-a-half-year process. The team built an entire first product, gathered customer feedback, and realized they had built "something incremental." They threw it out. The discipline: "What your customers say about you or what they say to their friends when they're talking about your software -- that's ultimately who you are" [12].

## PMF Signals at Meesho (Vidit Aatrey)

[[Vidit Aatrey]] describes PMF signals at Meesho: a WhatsApp-only product with no app or website that doubled every month for six months, while the original (more polished) product stagnated [13].

## Bump: Viral Growth Without PMF (David Lieb)

Bump represents a cautionary case: 150 million downloads and 10 million MAU, but Lieb does not describe it as having achieved PMF. Long-term retention was good but usage frequency was very low. The business could extract roughly $1 per user per year. Viral growth and PMF are not the same thing [8].

## References

1. [Before Growing Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4p-before-growing-your-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)
2. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
3. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (November 2012)
4. [How to Set Up, Hire, and Scale a Growth Strategy and Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team) -- [[Anu Hariharan]], Gustaf Alstromer (n.d.)
5. [How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) -- [[Emmett Shear]] (n.d.)
6. [How to Build a Great Series A Pitch and Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck) -- Janelle Tam (n.d.)
7. [The Real Product-Market Fit](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5z-the-real-product-market-fit) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)
8. [On Starting and Scaling One of the Biggest iOS Apps](https://ycombinator.com/library/5f-on-starting-and-scaling-one-of-the-biggest-ios-apps) -- [[David Lieb]], Gustaf Alstromer (n.d.)
9. [How To Build The Future: Tony Xu](https://ycombinator.com/library/MJ-how-to-build-the-future-tony-xu) -- [[Tony Xu]], [[Garry Tan]] (2025)
10. [How To Build The Future: Aravind Srinivas](https://ycombinator.com/library/MC-how-to-build-the-future-aravind-srinivas) -- [[Aravind Srinivas]], [[David Lieb]] (2025)
11. [How To Build The Future: Parker Conrad](https://ycombinator.com/library/M2-how-to-build-the-future-parker-conrad) -- [[Parker Conrad]], [[Garry Tan]] (2025)
12. [On Starting and Scaling Recruiting Software Startup Lever](https://ycombinator.com/library/6I-on-starting-and-scaling-recruiting-software-startup-lever) -- [[Sarah Nahm]], Holly Liu (2019)
13. [On Starting and Scaling Indian Shopping Site Meesho](https://ycombinator.com/library/6M-on-starting-and-scaling-indian-shopping-site-meesho) -- [[Vidit Aatrey]], Adora Cheung (2019)
14. [How to Start a Startup: Finding Product-Market Fit](https://ycombinator.com/library/86-how-to-start-a-startup-finding-product-market-fit) -- [[Peter Reinhardt]] (2017)

title: Simple Products
body:
# Simple Products

Many of the largest companies in existence started as products that did exactly one thing. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] argue that founders systematically overestimate the number of features needed to launch and underestimate the danger of building a multi-feature product that does not work reliably [1]. The pattern across OpenSea, Gusto, Amazon, and others is consistent: start with one use case, make it work, then expand.

## Boiling the Ocean

Seibel calls the instinct to build a complete, elaborate product before acquiring a single user "boiling the ocean" -- attempting something basically impossible or intractable [1]. Caldwell identifies the underlying cause: "Every founder starts their company with a lie in their head that they know what their customer wants and they know how to solve the problem. And the more you build before putting your product in front of a user, the more you just let that lie go wild" [1].

The antidote is contact with reality. Getting something -- anything -- in front of a user reveals how many hypotheses were correct and how much was "just the lie that got me started doing my company. A useful lie, but maybe not the best lie if you want to solve your user's problem" [1].

## Googleitis

Seibel coined the term "Googleitis" for the pattern of looking at companies as they exist today and not understanding what they looked like at the beginning [1]. Caldwell illustrates with Amazon: "When Amazon started, it was a really simple website that just sold books. It didn't do anything else. I think it didn't even do that that well" [1].

## Case Studies

### OpenSea: A Simple NFT Marketplace

OpenSea was in YC in 2018. Caldwell recounts: the founders pivoted from a different idea. They spent one to two years grinding with "a really simple website that just let you buy and sell NFTs and like barely worked" [1]. They shipped constantly -- daily or weekly -- and deliberately did not build their own wallet (users had to use a third-party wallet). By focusing on the core use case and building network effects, "we all woke up one day and this is one of the hottest companies in the world, and everyone wants to invest, and they're worth billions of dollars" [1].

Seibel emphasizes the obscurity period: "Don't forget the two or three years they were grinding away in obscurity just trying to make the dang thing work, doing the simplest thing that had basically no features" [1].

### Gusto (ZenPayroll): Online Payroll

Gusto today offers payroll, benefits, org charts, and 401k management. Caldwell recalls that when it started in YC Winter 2012 (his second batch) as ZenPayroll, it did exactly one thing: payroll [1]. The status quo was calling ADP, having someone come to your office to sign paperwork, then calling every month to confirm you wanted to run payroll. Gusto said: "We can just do that online. And that's all it's going to do. And you're going to love it" [1].

Caldwell uses Gusto to illustrate the asymmetry of simplicity vs. complexity: "A product that doesn't work with lots of features is infinitely worse than a product with one feature that works." A hypothetical Gusto that offered healthcare, benefits, 401k, and payroll but occasionally lost employees' money would be universally hated. Seibel adds: "That sounds like Verizon or something. That's a great way to learn a hated company -- have a lot of features that don't reliably work" [1].

## The Copy-and-Paste Fallacy

A related mistake is assuming that product-market fit transfers across geographies [1]. A YC founder wrote: "We copied another successful business model from an overseas market and wrongly assumed that product-market fit was a given. If the product works in country X, it's going to work in country Y. We scaled up hiring quickly and burnt a lot of money before we realized that the users we were selling to did not want our product" [1].

Caldwell identifies the deeper problem: founders substitute external validation for ground truth. "What we want to believe is that by getting validation from investors or the press or my friends or employees or winning a startup competition, that meant it was good." He compares it to a driver's license: "Lots of people can get into trouble with their driving habits even though you have a driver's license" [1]. Having permission to start (funding, press, a plausible thesis) does not mean the product works.

Seibel traces this to how founders learn. In school, knowledge comes from teachers, not first principles. "What we're basically saying is that all of the ways that you learned in the past don't work here. You actually have to go observe the problem, talk to the people who have the problem, try to figure out what's going on, and iterate" [1].

## Ground Truth Over Authority

Caldwell makes a meta-point about advice itself. The purpose of their guidance is to direct founders toward customers, not to serve as the authority. "People email us after these videos... Our whole point is, 'Go talk to customers, go figure it out.' And they email us and they're like, 'Can you give me advice?' They kind of aren't getting the point of these videos" [1].

## Discipline Over Intelligence

The conversation turns to why some founders execute and others do not. Seibel argues that discipline -- not intelligence -- is the primary differentiator among founders. "If you looked at their calendar, if you shadowed them, the folks that are really good at this are just better at doing focused work. There's kind of nothing else" [1]. He adds that "genius is mistaken for discipline" -- many assume successful founders were smarter, when the real difference was that they avoided distractions like hiring, conferences, and press [1].

Seibel illustrates with the low-runway effect: "When the runway gets down to about 12 months, the magical thinking starts fading." Founders do their best work when their back is against the wall [1]. Caldwell compares it to writing a paper in college -- great work happens in the final 24 hours that could have happened at any point during the semester [1].

The encouraging implication: unlike raw intelligence, discipline is improvable. Caldwell states: "You can't really change your raw intelligence, but you can get better at this. Like, this is a little bit like working out. If doing focused work for someone who likes to procrastinate is a little bit painful, the more they do it, the less painful it gets." He concludes: "Smart people certainly have an advantage, but man, hard workers are dangerous" [1].

## Crowdfunding as a Cautionary Tale

Caldwell and Seibel discuss hardware crowdfunding as an instance of the same mistake -- building elaborate plans before validating fundamentals [1]. Caldwell lays out the failure mode:

1. No idea how to build the product
2. No idea what it should cost
3. No idea whether it solves the user's problem
4. But a lot of money up front and enormous expectations

The result, as Seibel describes: "There's no surer way to make strangers on the internet hate you." Campaigns that fail to ship on time or at all create thousands of angry backers who track founders down and demand refunds. Caldwell adds: "We've seen this ruin people... It never leaves you. You get that permanent Wikipedia page that's not what you want" [1].

Seibel's revised best practice for hardware: "Do it after you've shipped and manufactured a couple of versions already" -- use crowdfunding at V2 or V3, not V1 [1].

The broader principle extends to any model where money is collected before value is delivered: "If you take money from people at scale on the internet, they rightfully will hold you accountable" [1].

## References

1. [Dalton & Michael: Simple Products That Became Big Companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DT-dalton-michael-simple-products-that-became-big-companies) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)

title: Starting Young
body:
# Starting Young

The question of when to start a startup -- and particularly whether to start one in or immediately after college -- is one [[Paul Graham]] has revisited multiple times, with his position evolving from encouragement (2005-2007) to more nuanced caution (2014). The tension between the advantages of youth and the dangers of starting too early runs through several of his most important essays.

## The Sweet Spot: Mid-Twenties

Graham's considered view, expressed in his 2006 student guide, is that the sweet spot for startup founders is the mid-twenties [1]. The question is what advantages 25-year-olds have over 21-year-olds, and why the answer is not older still.

**Advantages of young founders [1]:**

1. **Stamina.** Long hours are universal in successful startups. Younger founders need this even more because they are probably less efficient than they will be later.

2. **Poverty.** This sounds counterintuitive, but poverty means you can live cheaply, which is critically important because nearly every startup that fails does so by running out of money. A low burn rate gives more opportunity to recover from mistakes. "The guys with kids and mortgages are at a real disadvantage" [1]. Even more important than living cheaply is thinking cheaply. The Apple II was popular partly because Woz designed it for himself and couldn't afford anything more [1].

3. **Rootlessness.** Young people are more mobile -- no house, less stuff, fewer serious relationships. Many startups involve someone moving, and a startup hub matters enormously. Houston, Chicago, and Miami have microscopically small numbers of successful startups per capita [1].

4. **Colleagues.** The number one question at YC is "Where can I find a cofounder?" At 30, your school friends have either lost touch or are tied down by jobs. At a good college, you are concentrated with ambitious, technical people more than you will ever be again [1]. "Co-founders really should be people you already know. And by far the best place to meet them is school" [1]. Google, Yahoo, and Microsoft were all founded by people who met in school [1].

5. **Ignorance (innocence).** Jessica Livingston's interviews with startup founders revealed a pattern: "One after another said that if they'd known how hard it would be, they would have been too intimidated to start" [1]. Steve Wozniak put it strongly: "All the best things that I did at Apple came from (a) not having money and (b) not having done it before, ever" [1].

**Disadvantages of young founders [1]:**

The core problem is that young founders build things that look like class projects -- lacking (1) an iterative definition of a real problem and (2) market-level intensity [1]. See [[Early-Stage Mistakes]] for the full analysis.

The 24-year-old's real advantage over the 20-year-old: "they know what they're trying to avoid" [1]. To the average undergrad, getting rich means buying Ferraris. To someone who has learned about the relationship between money and work, it means opting out of the brutal equation governing 99.9% of people's lives. "Someone who gets this will work much harder at making a startup succeed -- with the proverbial energy of a drowning man" [1].

## The Excuses for Not Starting

Graham's 2007 essay systematically dismantles 16 common reasons people give for not starting a startup [2]. His goal is explicitly to increase self-confidence, but with the constraint that he is motivated to be honest -- encouraging people who shouldn't start startups makes his own life worse [2].

**Excuses that are mostly bogus:**

- **Too young.** The most successful founder YC had funded at the time, Sam Altman, was 19. But Altman is an outlier: "he seemed like he had a 40-year-old inside him" [2]. The real test: whether you have crossed the adult threshold, not your chronological age.

- **Too inexperienced.** "The best way to get experience if you're 21 is to start a startup" [2]. The Kiko founders started right out of college, made a lot of mistakes, and by the time YC funded their second startup a year later, they had become "extremely formidable" [2]. Getting a normal job may actually make you less able to start a startup by "turning you into a tame animal who thinks he needs an office to work in and a product manager to tell him what software to write" [2].

- **Not smart enough.** "If you're smart enough to worry that you might not be smart enough to start a startup, you probably are" [2]. Starting a startup does not require exceptional intelligence. Enterprise software companies are sales companies, and sales depends mostly on effort [2].

- **Know nothing about business.** The coefficient of this variable should be zero. "You don't need to know anything about business to start a startup. The initial focus should be the product" [2]. Pretty much 100% of startups that make something popular manage to make money from it. Acquirers buy strategic value, meaning something people want [2].

- **No idea.** In the average YC startup, 70% of the idea is new at the end of the first three months, sometimes 100%. YC is "so sure the founders are more important than the initial idea" that they accept applications with no idea at all [2].

- **No room for more startups.** There is no economic limit on the number of startups. "Startups make wealth, which means they make things people want, and if there's a limit on the number of things people want, we are nowhere near it. I still don't even have a flying car" [2].

- **Fear of uncertainty.** "If you start a startup, it will probably fail. Seriously, though, this is not a bad way to think about the whole experience." No employer will hold it against you. "In America, companies, like practically everything else, are disposable" [2].

**Excuses that are real:**

- **Not determined enough.** Determination is probably the single best predictor of success. But most hackers underestimate their own determination [2].

- **No cofounder.** "A startup is too much for one person to bear." All investors, without exception, are more likely to fund you with a cofounder. If you don't have one, get one -- "It's more important than anything else" [2].

- **Family to support.** Graham won't advise anyone with a family to start a startup. An alternative: start a consulting business and gradually turn it into a product business, or join an existing startup as an early employee [2].

- **Not ready for commitment.** If a startup succeeds, it will consume at least three or four years. This is a legitimate reason to wait, though Graham notes you will probably spend just as long at a regular job with less spare time than you expect [2].

## The 2014 Recalibration

By October 2014, Graham had shifted to more cautionary advice for students [3]. His Stanford lecture argues explicitly: "Do not start a startup in college" [3].

The reasoning: starting a startup at 20 is "like a brutally fast depth-first search. Most people should still be searching breadth-first at 20" [3]. You can do things in your early 20s that you cannot do before or after -- "plunge deeply into projects on a whim and travel super cheaply with no sense of a deadline" [3].

Mark Zuckerberg will never get to bum around a foreign country. "Success has taken a lot of the serendipity out of his life. Facebook is running him as much as he's running Facebook" [3].

The key insight: there is not even a tradeoff. You are not sacrificing anything by waiting, because you are more likely to succeed if you do [3]. The usual way startups take off is for founders to make them take off (see [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]]), and "it's gratuitously stupid to do that at 20" [3].

What students should do instead: "just learn" [3]. Become good at things that matter. Work on problems that interest you. The optimal preparation is not a vocational version of college focused on entrepreneurship but "the classic version of college as education for its own sake" [3].

Graham flags university entrepreneurship programs as misguided. "They go out of their way to ensure their students are well supplied with contraceptives, and yet they're setting up entrepreneurship programs and startup incubators left and right" [3]. The university cannot teach the essential thing -- understanding user needs -- because that requires actually starting the company, at which point you are no longer really a student [3].

## The Google Trap

Graham observes that while Google seems like the ideal place to work, "zero startups come out of there. In that respect it's a black hole" [1]. People like working there too much to leave. If you hope to start a startup one day, a pleasant employer can lull you into staying indefinitely even if leaving would be a net win. You need "activation energy" to start a startup, and a comfortable job suppresses it [1].

The best place to work if you want to start a startup is probably a startup. It is the right sort of experience, and one way or another it will be over quickly: you will either end up rich, or the startup will get acquired and start to suck, or it will blow up and you will be free [1].

## The Default Shift

Graham sees the shift from employment as default to startups as an option in historical terms. "Getting a job" is only about a hundred years old as the default way to make a living -- before that it was farming [2]. The shift from farming to manufacturing would have seemed insanely risky to medieval serfs [2].

"How grim it would be to commute every day to a cubicle in some soulless office complex, and be told what to do by someone you had to acknowledge as a boss -- someone who could call you into their office and say 'take a seat,' and you'd sit! Imagine having to ask permission to release software to users" [2].

Graham believes startups may represent another such historic shift in how wealth is created [2].

## Dalton & Michael: The Student's Guide

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]], both of whom started their first companies in their early 20s, provide specific advice for high school students who want to become founders [6]. The opening message: "The startup game isn't going away. You've got time" [6].

### Level Up Before You Play the Boss

Caldwell uses a Final Fantasy VII analogy: you can fight the boss at your current level and get destroyed, or you can grind for ten more hours and crush it [6]. "Investing and leveling yourself up can produce a lot of benefits later on. Like deferring a little bit of that satisfaction of getting started right now so you can upskill makes you ten times more powerful later" [6].

But the grinding has to be real: "If I just stood there and didn't grind, my character got no better" [6].

### Skills to Develop

**Learn to code.** Caldwell and Seibel are emphatic. Even if you think "I'm not going to be a coder my whole life, I want to be a founder, I want to be a product person" -- learn to code anyway [6]. Seibel, who was the "business guy" at Justin.tv: "There are many moments in the early stage of a company where I felt borderline useless and certainly I didn't have the power to impact the product anywhere near as much as my other three co-founders, who were software engineers" [6].

Seibel pushes harder: "Get this complicated view of 'I don't want to be a coder' out of your head. The coders are the most powerful people inside of software companies. They're the most highly compensated. They're the hardest to replace. They often have the best ideas" [6].

Resources: AP Computer Science, Replit, Code Academy. "It doesn't matter" if you were not a thirteen-year-old prodigy. Learning at nineteen or twenty is fine [6].

**Learn design.** Caldwell recalls needing to use Photoshop when no designer was available: "If a button needs to be made and we didn't have a designer, make the button. It didn't even have to look that good" [6].

**Launch products.** Going through the full cycle of conceiving, building, and releasing a project is irreplaceable practice [6]. Seibel's example: Mark Zuckerberg and Adam D'Angelo built a music recommendation plugin for Winamp in high school called "the Brain." It got millions of downloads. "It had nothing to do with what they did later on in life, but they knew how to write code, they knew how to make something they themselves wanted, and they released it" [6].

Caldwell observes this pattern in successful YC founders: "You ask them, 'Well, why were you doing that?' And they almost don't understand the question. It's their nature to build things and release them" [6].

**Learn to talk to people.** "This is 100 percent a learnable skill, and anyone who says otherwise is really doing you a disservice" [6]. Caldwell notes that high school identities feel permanent ("That's a nerd. That's a jock") but they are not. "You can experiment. You can play. You can be whoever you want to be" [6]. And "everyone else feels shy too. Even the people who you think don't feel shy" [6].

**Learn to help people.** Caldwell recounts tutoring a ninth grader in high school -- initially to check a box for the National Merit Scholarship. When the parents smiled at him with genuine gratitude, something clicked: "If you actually help someone, they'll really appreciate it. If you care, and I actually took it more seriously" [6]. He connects this to customer relationships: even when you screw up, if customers know you genuinely care, "they're not gonna fire you. It is so rare to find products or people who actually care" [6].

### Play the Credential Game (Strategically)

Caldwell acknowledges that credentials matter in the real world, despite not being a meritocratic filter [6]. He warns against both extremes: embracing the system uncritically ("the teacher's pets") or rejecting it entirely ("rage against the machine"). "Either extreme is bad" [6].

Seibel's framing: the system is somewhat arbitrary. With that understanding, you can reject it (and be held back by missing credentials), or you can fail to see its arbitrariness (and be crushed when credentials do not equal personal merit), or you can use the system as a tool to accomplish your goals [6].

### Distinguish Fads from Value

When young people get excited about something (crypto, stock trading, AI), they should ask: does this create real value, or is it passing? [6] "Fads stick when they really add value and fads pass when they don't" [6]. Zuckerberg's Winamp plugin was part of the Napster fad -- but he was building something, not just consuming the trend.

The startup-world version: COVID triggered a wave of Zoom competitors; Wall Street Bets triggered Robinhood clones. "The Zoom guys thought people need a video call way before COVID" [6]. The fad is often hiding the real underlying value.

### Stay Excited About Technology

Seibel notes a generational shift. When he was young, journalism was uniformly tech-optimistic. Now the default narrative is tech-skeptical: "This is bad. A lot of these people are bad. Every new technology -- how is it going to try to screw us?" [6]. Both extremes are wrong, but Seibel argues the pessimistic frame is especially corrosive for young people: "Your job is to be an optimist. Your job is to believe amazing things about what you can do with your life and what you do in the world. When you're young, that's the point. That's why the world needs young people" [6].

### Learn to Be Honest

Caldwell argues that self-honesty is the most important habit: "If your own relationship with yourself -- if you can't be honest about things, what do you really feel, what do you really think, who are you really -- it's going to be rough" [6].

He connects honesty to the startup ecosystem: "A lot of the reasons why early adopting customers will use you is because they think you're going to be honest. A lot of reasons why investors might give you money, why your co-founders might work with you -- a lot of reasons why people make things happen in startup world is because we're in a high trust environment" [6].

Seibel adds the positive reframing of "nerd honesty": the person who raises their hand to say the product sucks is doing something valuable, even if polite society considers it impolite [6].

### Learn to Love Long Games

Seibel recounts a formative experience: his startup lawyer, at age 30, told him the average startup takes seven to ten years to exit. "I just thought that couldn't be the case." The lawyer worked at a firm representing roughly a quarter of all tech companies -- he had the data. Seven years later, when the company sold, Seibel was 30 and the lawyer was 37. "The guy was right. This is a long game" [6].

Caldwell provides a non-startup example: his high school girlfriend loved poetry and became the New York Times poetry critic. "Probably not everyone that likes poetry when they're like a six-year-old is gonna go there. But if you actually put in your whole life towards something and you have a dream and you're not ashamed of the dream, and you keep the dream going into your thirties -- a lot of people have dreams when they're young, and they lose their dreams" [6].

### The Deferred Gratification Paradox

Seibel recalls being a poor startup founder in his 20s, watching friends in New York banking and finance live it up. "The fun that a 25-year-old has in New York? To be honest, it's pretty shitty. A wealthy 30-year-old? Way more fun" [6].

# Starting Young

## Do Not Start a Startup in College (Paul Graham, 2014)

In his Stanford "How to Start a Startup" lecture, [[Paul Graham]] delivers his most emphatic advice on timing: "Do not start a startup in college" [7]. This represents a sharpening of his earlier positions.

Graham's logic: "Starting a startup is something you can intrinsically only learn by doing it. And you can't do that in college for the reason I just explained: that startups take over your entire life. If you start a startup in college, if you start a startup as a student, you can't start a startup as a student, because if you start a startup you're not a student anymore" [7].

### The Opportunity Cost of Starting Too Early

Graham frames the decision within the broader question of "how to have a good life" [7]. Starting a startup could be a good thing at some point, but "20 is not the optimal time to do it." Activities uniquely available to people in their early twenties -- plunging deeply into projects on a whim, traveling cheaply with no deadline -- are foreclosed by a successful startup. "Mark Zuckerberg will never get to bum around a foreign country. If he goes to a foreign country, it's either as a defacto state visit, or he's hiding out incognito at the George Cinq in Paris" [7].

Graham argues there is "not even a trade-off" because waiting makes success more likely: "You'll be more likely to succeed if you wait" [7]. The only exception is the "astronomically unlikely case" where a side project takes off organically at age 20, like Facebook did.

### Universities Cannot Teach Entrepreneurship

Graham compares the situation to learning French. A French class teaches you French. An entrepreneurship class teaches you about entrepreneurship -- "linguistics" rather than fluency in a language [7]. What founders need is not expertise in startups but expertise in their users. Mark Zuckerberg "succeeded despite being a complete noob at startups" because he understood his users [7]. Facebook was first incorporated as a Florida LLC -- "even you guys know better than that" [7].

Graham actively warns against studying startup mechanics: "It's not merely unnecessary for people to learn in detail about the mechanics of starting a startup, but possibly somewhat dangerous" [7]. The danger is that students will "go through the motions of starting a startup" -- come up with a plausible idea, raise money at a nice valuation, rent a nice office, hire friends -- "and then gradually realize how completely fucked they are. Because while imitating all the outward forms of starting a startup, they have neglected the one thing that's actually essential which is to make something people want" [7]. Graham and Sam Altman coined a term for this: "playing house" [7].

### What to Do Instead

The optimal path is "the classic version of college as education for its own sake" [7]. Learn about things that matter. Work on problems that interest you. These activities unconsciously prepare the mind to generate startup ideas. "The component of entrepreneurship that really matters is domain expertise. Larry Page is Larry Page because he was an expert on search" [7].

Graham illustrates with a Harvard grad student in the mid-1990s who wrote his own voice-over-IP software to call his girlfriend in Taiwan without paying long-distance fees. "It wasn't meant to be a startup. He never tried to turn it into one." But it was the kind of insight that startup ideas are made of, arrived at through genuine expertise rather than entrepreneurial ambition [7].

## The All-Consuming Nature of Startups (Paul Graham)

Graham emphasizes a point he considers under-appreciated: "If you start a startup, it will take over your life to a degree that you cannot imagine" [7]. If the startup succeeds, this lasts for years, possibly decades, possibly the rest of a working life. Larry Page "started running as fast as he could at age 25 and he has not stopped to catch his breath since" [7]. Every day brings problems that only the CEO can handle. Vacations create backlogs. As a billionaire, he gets "zero, actually less than zero sympathy" for having a difficult life [7].

Every YC batch hears the same message from successful founders: "It never gets any easier. The nature of the problems change" -- construction delays in London offices replace broken air conditioners in studio apartments -- "but the total volume of worry never decreases, if anything, it increases" [7].

## You Cannot Tell If You Are Good Enough (Paul Graham)

Graham states the fifth counterintuitive truth about startups: "You can't tell" whether you are up to the challenge [7]. "Your life so far has given you some idea what your prospects might be if you wanted to become a mathematician or a professional football player. But unless you have had a very strange life indeed, you have not done much that's like starting a startup" [7].

After nine years of trying to predict which YC founders would succeed, Graham reports his accuracy as limited. "The founders sometimes thought they knew. Some arrived feeling confident that they would ace Y Combinator, just as they had aced every one of the few, easy, artificial tests they had faced in life so far. Others arrived wondering what mistake could cause them to be admitted." But "there's little to no correlation between these attitudes and how things turn out" [7]. Graham compares this to the military, where "the swaggering recruits are no more likely to turn out to be really tough than the quiet ones" [7].

## References

1. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
2. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (March 2007)
3. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)
4. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
5. [Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2005)
6. [The Student's Guide to Becoming a Successful Startup Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IZ-dalton-michael-the-student-s-guide-to-becoming-a-successful-startup-founder) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
7. [The Student's Guide to Becoming a Successful Startup Founder](https://ycombinator.com/library/IZ-dalton-michael-the-student-s-guide-to-becoming-a-successful-startup-founder) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2022)
8. [How to Start a Startup: Before the Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/JI-how-to-start-a-startup-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (2014)

title: Competing with Big Companies
body:
# Competing with Big Companies

YC group partners [[Michael Seibel]], [[Harj Taggar]], and [[Brad Flora]] observe that one of the most common failure modes among founders doing well is psyching themselves out about competitors [1]. Founders replace excitement about their own company with fear about a competitor -- usually triggered by a competitor's fundraising announcement, press coverage, or a new hire from a marquee company [1].

## The Lies Founders Tell Themselves

### "My competitor raised more money, so they'll win"

Seibel frames the depressing but useful truth: "Most likely everyone's gonna die. You and your competitors, none of you will make something that people want" [1]. The second thing founders fail to account for is that many markets support multiple winners. "How many banks are there? One bank doesn't look at the other banks and say, well, I guess that's it, we got to fold up shop" [1].

### "My competitor must be doing great"

[[Brad Flora]] notes that TechCrunch announcements hide internal chaos. "Years later you talked to someone that worked at that company and they say, 'Oh, man, what a mess. The CEO was on a bender and was all sad because we lost all of our big customers'" [1]. Founders are exposed to their own company's flaws every day but see only competitors' carefully crafted exterior -- their marketing [1].

Seibel describes the shift in perspective from founder to YC partner: "As a partner, you work with these companies and you start realizing that all of them are kind of really badly flawed somehow. Literally in any YC batch, I could take every single company and pick out some fatal flaw. And you know that some of them are going to turn out to be worth billions of dollars" [1].

### "We're in a land grab situation"

The "land grab" framing is "definitely a bit of a meme" at YC [1]. [[Harj Taggar]] plays devil's advocate by voicing the founder fear: "We're in a land grab situation. If I don't get those customers unprofitably as fast as possible, my competitors will beat me" [1]. Taggar's diagnostic questions cut through it: "Are your customers talking about them? Are you losing deals? Have you looked at the product? How do you stack up?" The answers are usually reassuring -- customers are not talking about the competitor [1]. Seibel calls it "a confusion of interfaces" -- founders think a competitor in the New York Times means their customers are having a worse experience [1].

### "My competitor must know something I don't, so I should copy them"

Seibel tells the story from his own experience at Justin.tv competing with Ustream and Livestream. All three companies were copying each other's features. Justin.tv built a feature exclusively for copyright owners to take content off the site -- a feature with "no clear user value at all." Two weeks later, a competitor released the same feature. "We were like, 'Oh crap, this is clearly the blind leading the blind.' They don't even know why we have this" [1].

The company only started working when Emmett Shear and Kevin Lin "started actually talking to the users and figuring out what they wanted instead of the years where all the competitors just looked at each other, copying each other's dumb features" [1].

## When Competition Actually Matters

### No competitors at all

Hearing "we don't have any competitors" in a YC interview is a red flag [1]. "That shows a lack of understanding of the customer and what the customer is doing right now to solve the problem" [1]. Founders should always know what alternatives exist.

### Your competitor objectively has a better product

Seibel acknowledges this happened to him personally. The day after Socialcam was acquired, a close friend messaged saying he could finally delete the app because he had been using something else the entire time -- "I guess we didn't have the best" [1].

### Structural platform advantage

The most dangerous competitive situation is when a competitor owns the platform. [[Brad Flora]]'s company, Perfect Audience, used the Facebook Exchange API along with eight or nine competitors. When Facebook launched Custom Audiences and started selling the same service directly, "Facebook was now a competitor, and they own the platform. They wrote all the code. They control pricing. So that's a time when you should worry about competition. And so we sold the company" [1].

Seibel draws from another example: his former co-founders Justin Kan and Emmett Shear created a calendar company that was crushed six months in when Google Calendar launched. "What happens when someone has an actual product structural advantage?" [1].

Seibel also notes the Microsoft Teams vs. Slack dynamic: Teams is probably not as good as Slack, but Microsoft's massive sales distribution advantage lets a "good enough" product win in the enterprise [1].

### Structural advantage with a bad product loses

Taggar observes that a structural advantage paired with a poor product usually fails. Microsoft's music player, mobile operating system, and many Facebook product launches demonstrate this. "A structural advantage with a crap product doesn't win. A structural advantage with a product that's good enough can win" [1]. But "just as often, they don't nail it" -- and Taggar notes that the partners struggle to find examples of FANG companies launching products that actually killed fast-growing successful startups [1].

Flora points to Snap as evidence: "Snap lives, right?" Seibel adds TikTok and notes that for founders, the takeaway is simple: "Build a better product, and if you do that, then all of these other advantages are just kind of moot points. It's really rare for the company that has the structural advantage to also put out a great product" [1].

## The Instacart Example

Taggar raises Instacart as a case study. Instacart faced the "what about Amazon?" question from day one. Taggar asks: "How the hell does anyone compete with one of the greatest companies of all time on the thing that they're experts at?" The answer: "just picking the right avocado was too big of a challenge for this trillion-dollar company" [1]. The lesson is that even in spaces dominated by massive incumbents, specific execution challenges create opportunities.

## The Cruise Example

When Kyle Vogt started Cruise, Google was already a decade into self-driving cars. An investor told Seibel that "Kyle's screwed from day one." Seibel's response drew on his direct experience as Kyle's co-founder at Justin.tv: "Kyle has built the most complicated products inside of our company. Whenever there was a ridiculously stupid technical challenge that no one else could do, Kyle volunteered for it. I would never bet against Kyle. And the crazier the more I wouldn't bet against him" [1].

## The Real Strategy: Build a Better Product

Flora summarizes the partners' collective advice: "Instead of worrying about all these stupid things like land grabs and whatever all the other crap, man, just build a better product for your users. And you might get surprised at how well that strategy goes. Stay informed. Know what your competitors are building and how you stack up against them. But don't let it deflate you" [1].

Seibel's approach in office hours is to redirect founder anxiety to the fundamentals. "What's your growth rate? Customers churning? What's the pipeline on sales look like? And if they can answer all those questions well, you're like, 'Let's get back to it. Keep going. Why are we stopping?'" [1].


## The Innovator's Dilemma in AI Search (Aravind Srinivas)

[[Aravind Srinivas]] provides a detailed case study of competing with a dominant incumbent through structural advantage. Google cannot easily build Perplexity's product because if users stop clicking links, ad revenue (~$200 billion/year) declines. "Wall Street just automatically panics if search revenue goes down" [2]. Srinivas's edge: "Obsession about the user and good product taste" [2].

## Outcompeting with Superior Retention (Tony Xu)

[[Tony Xu]] competed against better-funded rivals (Uber Eats, GrubHub) with a structural advantage: superior retention and frequency. "One of the hardest things to really fool is the retention and the engagement of a product" [3]. Every marketing dollar went further for DoorDash because retained users compound [3].

## Concentration as Competitive Strategy (Sam Altman)

[[Sam Altman]] describes how OpenAI, far less resourced than DeepMind, used focus as a weapon: "They're going to try a lot of things, and we've just got to pick one and really concentrate" [4]. The startup advantage is not resources but conviction and speed [4].

## Three Structural Advantages for Startups (Sam Altman)

[[Sam Altman]] identifies three specific areas where startups repeatedly beat big companies [5]:

### Ideas That Sound Bad But Are Good

At a big company, a product manager with an idea that sounds bad but is actually good must get "everybody from your boss, sometimes all the way up to the CEO to say yes. So one no can kill you." At a startup, any of thousands of investors at Demo Day can say yes. "For those ideas that sound bad but are good, because of this phenomenon, this one no versus one yes, startups can win" [5].

### Fast-Changing Markets

A startup's great advantage is agility and speed. "The more a market is changing the higher the number of decisions you get to make and the higher number of tweaks to your product and strategy you get to make." Big companies will make those decisions "on average, worse and certainly much slower" [5].

### Big Platform Shifts

Startups come in clusters, usually after a major platform shift. Large companies operate on annual cadences and "are not good at making a big enough strategic pivot. The battleship just turns too slowly." A startup can commit fully to a new direction overnight [5]. See [[Why Now]] for Garry Tan's complementary treatment of platform shifts.

# Competing with Big Companies

## Innovation Complacency: The Google Cash Problem (Garry Tan)

[[Garry Tan]] presents a structural argument for why big companies fail to innovate, using Google as the primary example [6]. In 2012, Peter Thiel confronted Google CEO Eric Schmidt with the observation that Google had $30-50 billion in cash and "no idea how to invest that money in technology effectively" [6]. By the time of Tan's video, Google held $135 billion in cash [6].

Tan identifies the root cause: "There are limits that are not cash. There are limits of recruiting, limits of real estate, limits of regulatory limits" [6]. Big companies cannot simply convert money into innovation. Their size creates organizational drag -- bureaucracy, politics, drama -- that prevents them from executing on new ideas [6].

The former CEO of Waze, Noam Bardin, experienced this firsthand after Google acquired Waze for $1 billion. He described "an ever-increasing percent of our time went to non-user value creation tasks. And that changes the DNA of the company quickly from customer-focused to corporate guidelines-focused" [6].

Tan's conclusion: "A small startup can beat even the largest companies on the planet. You care a lot more. You can hire better. You can deliver a better product" [6]. The big company CEO spends most of their time keeping plates spinning. A founder only has to spin one plate really well [6].

### Amazon as the Exception

Tan identifies Amazon as the rare large company that consistently reinvests rather than hoarding cash. Amazon barely turned a profit for almost twenty years despite growing revenue every year. The result: 50% of all U.S. e-commerce and 32% of cloud infrastructure [6].

The key difference is Jeff Bezos's approach to resource allocation. Bezos refused the job of "driving up the stock price." Instead, he worked backward from free cash flow to find controllable inputs. For established businesses, this means 1-2% improvements. But Amazon also took "big risks on brand new products like AWS, Kindle, and Alexa" [6].

Tan contrasts this with Google's approach: "$5 to $20 billion per year in profit that they actually couldn't figure out what to do with. It's not a picture that is building the future in an aggressive way" [6].

### Steve Jobs on Interpersonal Computing

Tan includes a 1990 Steve Jobs clip where Jobs predicted that electronic organizations would replace management hierarchies because they can be formed in "literally fifteen minutes worth of setup" and can "live for as long as they're needed and then vanish" [6]. Tan frames this as the computer revolution creating "a new kind of management, a new type of hiring, a new type of company, and a new way to come together to create" [6].

## References (new)

6. [YOU Can Beat Google the Way Amazon Does. Here's How.](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jj-garry-s-channel-you-can-beat-google-the-way-amazon-does-here-s-how) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

## References

1. [How to Compete with Amazon and Google](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iz-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-how-to-compete-with-amazon-and-google) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Brad Flora]] (2021)
2. [How To Build The Future: Aravind Srinivas](https://ycombinator.com/library/MC-how-to-build-the-future-aravind-srinivas) -- [[Aravind Srinivas]], [[David Lieb]] (2025)
3. [How To Build The Future: Tony Xu](https://ycombinator.com/library/MJ-how-to-build-the-future-tony-xu) -- [[Tony Xu]], [[Garry Tan]] (2025)
4. [How To Build The Future: Sam Altman](https://ycombinator.com/library/Lp-how-to-build-the-future-sam-altman) -- [[Sam Altman]], [[Garry Tan]] (2024)
5. [How to Succeed with a Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/89-how-to-succeed-with-a-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (August 2018)

title: AI and Startups
body:
# AI and Startups

The current wave of AI -- particularly large language models (LLMs) -- represents a technology shift comparable to mobile (2007-2012) and cloud (early 2010s). [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] address the macro question of whether OpenAI and similar companies will kill all startups [1], while YC Group Partners argue that every new startup should use AI but that simply pivoting to AI without customer insight is not sufficient [2]. Separately, "vibe coding" -- using AI coding assistants to build products -- has become a core productivity tool that founders can measurably improve at [3].

## The AGI Question Is Out of Scope

Caldwell and Seibel set a boundary: companies like OpenAI and Anthropic are trying to build AGI, not AI-powered CRMs or better search. Whether they will succeed is a debate for AI researchers, not startup advisors [1].

Seibel invokes a version of Godwin's law: once a conversation reaches "AI could become God" or "Skynet is coming," nothing useful can be said. "We don't know whether or not Skynet is coming. It's outside the scope of this video" [1].

## Lessons from Technology History

What can be discussed is how major technological changes have historically affected startups. The pattern is consistent across farming, electricity, and the internet [1]:

1. **More businesses, not fewer.** Major innovations enable more businesses than they destroy.
2. **Startups are advantaged over incumbents.** The greater the technology change and the shorter the time period, the more startups are advantaged relative to established companies. The entire venture capital industry exists because internet innovation happened so quickly that incumbents were disrupted [1].

If history repeats, someone looking at LLM tools today and thinking "real problems can be solved right now that couldn't be solved six to twelve months ago" is in the right position [1].

[[Brad Flora]] compares the current AI moment to the mobile wave he witnessed firsthand. He arrived at YC in 2007, the same week Steve Jobs announced the iPhone. The apps did not come until the following summer, and the permissions needed did not arrive until 2009. But the following five years produced most of the mobile companies people use today [2].

The pattern: "Big companies generally are preceded by big technology shifts" [2]. Gustaf Alstromer extends the analogy to cloud migration in the early 2010s: "There were just a lot of good ideas lying around because you had the opportunity to replace some legacy piece of software that was on-prem." He cites a conversation with the CEO of Workday, who saw the same pattern when PeopleSoft's on-prem model was vulnerable to cloud-native replacements. "Similar moment now for a lot of companies" [2].

## Cargo Culting vs. Real Value

Seibel introduces the distinction, with Caldwell elaborating [1]:

**Cargo culting AI**: Caldwell defines this as adding "AI" to a product description when it is tangential to what the product does. The AI does not help customers, does not improve the product, and exists primarily to help raise money.

**Real AI value**: Seibel describes seeing companies add AI features that dramatically increase retention, increase product quality, or make it much easier to charge. "That stuff is real. That is not hype" [1].

The analogy is to when the App Store launched. There was a hype cycle around apps and many companies cargo-culted the label. But building a high-quality mobile app that increased user retention was genuinely transformative. Facebook almost died because it did not move to mobile fast enough [1].

Similarly, people who were "haters on cloud computing because it was overly hyped" missed the point. Just because something is overhyped does not mean you should not evaluate it on its merits [1].

## Do Not Fear Big Companies

Despite the obvious power of incumbents, their execution speed is slow. "ChatGPT is like two years old, and we still have a dumb Alexa" [2]. This gap creates opportunities for startups. Flora advises founders not to fear big company speed of execution [2].

Until AGI is solved, it will be the first, second, and third goal of companies like OpenAI. Using the existing tools to make people's lives and businesses better is "not on their list" [1]. That gap is the opportunity for startups [1].

## Should You Pivot to AI?

Gustaf Alstromer offers a two-part answer: "No, you should not pivot to AI" just because it seems like a good idea. "But yes, you should almost absolutely be working on something that's using LLMs at the heart of it today" [2]. The distinction is between chasing a trend and leveraging a technology platform.

Pete Koomen draws the analogy to cloud: "If you are building a cloud company, obviously you're going to use the cloud. Same thing with AI. It doesn't make sense for anyone creating a new company today to not leverage AI within their company" [2].

## Who Is Starting AI Companies

Seibel identifies two groups of founders who are well-positioned [1]:

**Domain experts in ML**: People who have been doing machine learning for years and now see "all of the things I imagined were going to happen are starting to happen. I can predict the future" [1].

**Enthusiastic early adopters**: People who love using LLMs and are learning deeply about them while most people know nothing. Caldwell notes this group is analogous to the teenagers who jailbroke iPhones when the App Store was new -- the Brex founders were among them [1].

Caldwell identifies a third group that is not well-positioned: people who are not excited about LLMs and have no ML experience but want to make money fast. "Those people are not being attracted to this," which Caldwell sees as a positive signal for the quality of companies being built [1].

## Where AI Ideas Come From

### Automate Specialized Skills

Alstromer describes two companies from the Fall 2024 YC batch that automated previously specialized skills: Replex (automatic UI localization) and Gecko Security (AI security engineer). Both take a specialized skill, automate it with AI, put it behind an intuitive interface, and give software engineers the ability to manage it independently as part of their release cycle [2].

### Domain Expertise Matters

A YC company whose founders all worked at an insurance technology company built an AI co-pilot for Medicare Advantage insurance agents. "This is a relatively obscure workflow. Most software engineers don't have much experience with that. But because of that experience, they were able to build an AI co-pilot" that is doing very well [2].

This echoes [[Startup Ideas]] principles: the best ideas come from the intersection of domain expertise and a new technology capability.

### Healthcare as an AI Goldmine

Alstromer describes US healthcare as a $4 trillion system with $1.3-1.4 trillion in administrative costs. "If you look at the companies that we funded in YC, it seems like much of this admin is legacy software system plus a human who's moving data from one system into another" [2]. Most of these tasks are now fully automatable with LLM agents.

The challenge: founders do not know what these tasks are. Alstromer's advice is concrete: "Go to your friend who works in this and go and sit next to them and ask them how they do their job and watch their screens and you will get ideas right away. I'm very convinced that you don't have to spend more than a few hours in front of their screens" [2].

## Second-Order Effects Are Where the Opportunity Lives

Seibel worries that low-hanging-fruit AI ideas will be executed well enough by OpenAI itself. The real opportunity lies in second-order effects [1].

The analogy: when the iPhone launched, it was obvious that Maps would come to the phone. It was not obvious that Uber would come. "Uber was a consequence of the iPhone" [1]. Similarly, the most valuable AI startups will likely be consequences of LLMs that are not yet obvious.

"Fart apps" were the first-order ideas on the iPhone. Uber was the second-order idea. The former were not the big business [1].

## The "Thin Wrapper" Defense

When critics dismiss a startup as "a thin wrapper on OpenAI," Seibel asks: is that the destination or the starting point? The comparison is Dropbox, which was dismissed as "a thin wrapper on top of S3" [1].

Seibel's advice to founders absorbing criticism about being a wrapper: "Ignore those people. Understand that many good things started like toys and make something the customers really love" [1].

## The Importance of San Francisco

Multiple partners emphasize that the AI ecosystem is concentrated in San Francisco in a way that makes physical presence valuable. Alstromer compares it to his time at Airbnb: when they wanted to learn SEO, the world's best SEO team (Pinterest) was literally downstairs. "That is the state of LLMs here right now" [2].

The advice: at minimum, come to SF for three to four weeks and embed in the community. The long-term recommendation: consider moving permanently [2].

## Pivoting Done Right: The Bland Story

Flora tells the story of a company that applied to YC in Fall 2020 as "InvestBetter" (financial investment platform), pivoted during COVID to a Zoom productivity tool called Superpowered, grew to thousands of DAUs and became default alive, then recognized the LLM wave and made two decisions: move from Toronto to San Francisco, and stop working on the old product. The company, renamed Bland, grew from nothing to powering a significant portion of B2B voice AI in the YC portfolio in under 15 months [2].

## Pivoting Done Wrong

Koomen describes the failure pattern: companies that were not working pivoted to AI by becoming "customer support agent company number 50" without new insights, without changing their environment, without embedding in communities, and without deeper customer understanding. "The fundamental things that founders still need to do to enable the technology to produce value for customers are the same. And if you're not doing that, just switching your idea over to something that makes calls to OpenAI is not going to change your fate" [2].

## Vibe Coding: Getting the Most Out of AI Development Tools

[[Tom Blomfield]] spent a month building side projects with Claude Code, Windsurf, and Aqua, and compiled a playbook for getting better results from AI coding assistants [3].

### Start with a Plan, Not Code

Before writing any code, work with the LLM to write a comprehensive plan in a markdown file. Step through it section by section. Mark completed sections. Keep an "ideas for later" section to explicitly tell the LLM what is out of scope. "I probably wouldn't expect the models to oneshot entire products yet" [3].

### Version Control Religiously

Use Git as the safety net. Start each new feature from a clean Git state. If the AI goes off on a "vision quest," `git reset --hard` and try again. Do not accumulate layers of failed fixes -- each failed attempt adds cruft. If you eventually find the solution after several attempts, reset and re-implement the clean solution from scratch [3].

### Write Tests (High-Level)

Get the LLM to write tests, but keep them high-level -- simulate someone clicking through the site end to end rather than testing individual functions. LLMs have a bad habit of making unnecessary changes to unrelated logic, and test suites catch these regressions early [3].

### Bug Fixing

First step for any bug: copy-paste the error message straight into the LLM. "Often this error message is enough for the AI to identify and fix a problem. You don't even need to explain what's going wrong" [3]. For complex bugs, ask the LLM to think through three or four possible causes before writing any code. After each failed fix attempt, `git reset` and start again. If one model cannot solve it, try a different model [3].

### Write LLM Instructions

Put instructions in Cursor rules, Windsurf rules, or a Claude markdown file. Some founders have written hundreds of lines of instructions for their AI coding agent, making them "way, way, way more effective" [3].

### Choosing a Tech Stack

Blomfield found AI performed remarkably well with Ruby on Rails -- a 20-year-old framework with a ton of consistent, high-quality training data. Languages with less training data online (Rust, Elixir) produced less reliable results [3].

### Use Voice Input

Voice input through tools like Aqua (a YC company) allows input at roughly 140 words per minute, double typical typing speed. LLMs are tolerant of minor transcription errors [3].

### Refactor Frequently

Once code works and tests pass, refactor freely. Ask the LLM to identify repetitive code or refactoring candidates. Keep files small and modular -- easier for both humans and LLMs [3].

### LLMs Beyond Code

Blomfield used LLMs for DNS configuration, Heroku setup, favicon design and resizing, and documentation lookup. "The AI is now my designer as well" [3].

### Keep Experimenting

The state of the art changes weekly. Different models excel at different tasks: Gemini for whole-codebase indexing and planning, Sonnet 3.7 for implementation, though rankings shift constantly [3].

## Conclusion

Unless short-term AGI is imminent, OpenAI is not going to kill all startups. The tools available now could produce "an explosion of new amazing startups. This could be the moment that we've been waiting for since mobile, where people can actually do cool new things" [1].


## The Platform Shift: Founder Perspectives

[[Sam Altman]] frames the AI moment as "the best time yet" to start a technology company. "Big companies have the edge when things are moving slowly. When something like this or mobile or the internet or semiconductor revolution happens, that was when upstarts had their edge" [4]. [[Garry Tan]] observes that "the world is still sleeping on all of this to such an astonishing degree" [4].

### Concentration as Strategy

Altman's experience at OpenAI provides a direct case study. OpenAI was less resourced than DeepMind and chose to concentrate on one bet rather than spreading across many. "They're going to try a lot of things, and we've just got to pick one and really concentrate. And that's how we can win here." This is "totally the right startup takeaway" [4].

### The Five Levels of AI (Sam Altman)

A framework to replace the overloaded term "AGI" [4]: (1) Chatbots (achieved with ChatGPT), (2) Reasoners (achieved with O1), (3) Agents (expected soon), (4) Innovators (early signs), (5) Organizations (future).

## AI Product Design: Two Vectors (David Lieb)

[[David Lieb]] identifies two vectors for applying AI to products [5]:

1. **Scaling human-solvable problems**: Tasks that skilled humans can do but cannot do affordably at scale. Google Photos exemplifies this.
2. **Solving human-unsolvable problems**: Tasks beyond human cognitive capacity (e.g., data center power optimization). Riskier but potentially bigger payoff.

## AI Can Read, Not Just Write (Parker Conrad)

[[Parker Conrad]] argues that generative AI is a misnomer for the most valuable enterprise application. "The fact that these systems can read and their context windows are so large is actually much more powerful for B2B software than the fact that they can write" [7].

Practical application: Rippling's AI performance management feature ingests an employee's first 90 days of work product and predicts whether they are on a success trajectory or need intervention [7].

Conrad frames AI as CEO context window expansion: it can read everything that everyone is doing, effectively flattening organizational layers. AI will help "2,000-person companies be run more like 200-person companies, and 200-person companies be run more like 20-person companies" [7].

### AI SDRs Will Destroy Outbound

Conrad's contrarian take: AI SDRs currently do not work. But "the second that it flips and they can, it won't have the impact that people are hoping. What'll happen is it'll just destroy outbound as a channel" because every company will simultaneously flood it [7].

## Perplexity: AI Search as Case Study

[[Aravind Srinivas]] chose search because it sits on the "AI completeness path" -- the company improves as AI improves, creating a flywheel. Perplexity's approach was deliberately simple: take top-K search results, feed cached snippets to an LLM, generate answers with citations [6]. See [[Perplexity: Building the Next Search Engine]].

## References

1. [Will OpenAI Kill All Startups?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ie-dalton-michael-will-openai-kill-all-startups) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
2. [How To Use AI In Your Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/M3-how-to-use-ai-in-your-startup) -- [[Brad Flora]], Pete Koomen, Gustaf Alstromer (2025)
3. [How To Get The Most Out Of Vibe Coding](https://ycombinator.com/library/MN-how-to-get-the-most-out-of-vibe-coding) -- [[Tom Blomfield]] (2025)
4. [How To Build The Future: Sam Altman](https://ycombinator.com/library/Lp-how-to-build-the-future-sam-altman) -- [[Sam Altman]], [[Garry Tan]] (2024)
5. [On Starting and Scaling One of the Biggest iOS Apps](https://ycombinator.com/library/5f-on-starting-and-scaling-one-of-the-biggest-ios-apps) -- [[David Lieb]], Gustaf Alstromer (n.d.)
6. [How To Build The Future: Aravind Srinivas](https://ycombinator.com/library/MC-how-to-build-the-future-aravind-srinivas) -- [[Aravind Srinivas]], [[David Lieb]] (2025)
7. [How To Build The Future: Parker Conrad](https://ycombinator.com/library/M2-how-to-build-the-future-parker-conrad) -- [[Parker Conrad]], [[Garry Tan]] (2025)

title: Startup Management
body:
# Startup Management

Most startup founders begin managing teams with zero management experience. Building the right [[Company Culture]] early is closely tied to effective management -- both shape how people behave when no one is watching. As Aaron Epstein, founder of Creative Market, describes it: "One of the most difficult things about starting a company is that you have to create both a product that people love and a company where people want to work at the same time" [1]. This article compiles practical management advice for founders who are building and leading teams for the first time.

## The Core Tension

The dual mandate of startups creates a fundamental challenge: "Great people build great products, and great people won't tolerate a bad environment for very long" [1]. The commonly cited observation that "people leave managers, not companies" applies with particular force to startups, where many first-time founders "raise a bunch of money and start building a team without any management experience at all" [1].

## Delegation and Scaling

Delegation is the foundational management skill for founders. Epstein frames it as a necessary evolution: "As a founder you're used to doing it all, and it can be scary to shed responsibilities you've always owned, but you can't scale yourself and focus on the highest leverage opportunities until you get things off your plate" [1].

The practical signal for when to delegate: "If you think you're going to have to work especially late or can't get to the things you need to in a given day, take that as a signal to delegate to your team" [1].

The flip side of delegation is growth opportunity creation. Great employees often value personal growth more than compensation: "Don't hoard [new responsibilities] all for yourself" [1]. This connects to the broader principle from [[Startup Leadership]] that a CEO's role transitions from Doer-in-Chief to Company-Builder-in-Chief.

## Investing in Star Employees

Epstein draws an analogy to customer retention: "Similar to how it's much easier to retain an existing customer than to acquire a new one, it's much easier to keep an existing star employee happy than find a new star" [1]. The advice is to go "above and beyond" to make stars feel valued, appreciated, and rewarded.

## Setting Standards and Accountability

### High Bar

"Great people want to do big, meaningful things. Push your team with aggressive goals, and you might be surprised what they will accomplish" [1].

### Deadlines and Follow-Through

When assigning tasks, always ask "by when?" and add a calendar note to follow up [1]. When standards or deadlines are not met, give direct feedback to change future behavior.

### Verbalizing Feedback

Epstein emphasizes the power of simply stating feelings: "'I'm disappointed in this work' is extremely powerful in helping people directly understand where you stand. People aren't mind-readers, so rather than just jumping into solutions when giving feedback, state your feelings out loud" [1].

## Leading by Example

"Teams embody the characteristics of their leaders, and the things you care about will be the things your team cares about" [1]. If a founder pays attention to details, the team will learn to do the same. This extends to credit and blame: "Use 'I' when talking about a screw-up, and use 'we' (or better yet, specific contributors' names) when talking about successes" [1].

## Communication

### Sharing the Big Picture

Employees need to understand how their work fits into the company's larger goals. "It's easy to take it for granted that your team is clear on this, but this is one of those things where you want to constantly make that connection" [1]. This reinforces the [[Mission-to-Metrics Framework]] that [[Ali Rowghani]] describes as one of a CEO's three non-delegable tasks.

### Repetition

"You should feel like you sound like a broken record about the things that are important to your company -- your mission, your vision, your KPIs, etc." [1]. The test for adequate repetition: "every single person on your team will be able to explain them to a complete stranger exactly how you would've said it" [1].

## Hiring

### Hire Slow, Fire Fast

"It's the thing everyone says and few have the discipline to do" [1]. The reasoning: "Great people want to work with other great people, so resist the temptation to fill open positions with mediocre candidates, and don't settle. You should be thrilled about each new hire" [1].

### Hire for Hunger

Skills can be learned; passion cannot be manufactured. "Passion for your mission, business, and team will do more to drive an employee to make a big, long-term impact than any specific skills they may have" [1].

### Onboarding

"It's unfair to expect even the best people to hit the ground running on day one without being properly onboarded" [1]. Managers must invest early in setting expectations, showing the ropes, teaching the business, and providing clear direction.

### Earning Trust

New employees should not be given full autonomy immediately: "Make them prove themselves first by working more closely with them and frequently reviewing their work until they've earned your full trust" [1].

## Adapting Management Style to Individuals

Epstein's favorite management interview question is "How would you describe your management style?" and "the best possible answer is 'it depends on the employee'" [1]. Each person has a unique combination of experience, motivations, and personality. The assumption that everyone is self-motivated like the founder is a common mistake: "Get curious to figure out what drives and motivates each individual on your team, rather than assuming everyone's like you" [1].

## Decision-Making

### Including the Team

"If you've hired great people, you'd be crazy not to include them in important decisions" [1]. The key is defining the process upfront: will the decision be made by committee, delegated to someone, or made by the founder after gathering input? "Even if your team disagrees with the final call, by including them in the process they will better understand the decision, feel like their opinion was valued, and be able to get on board" [1].

### Showing Your Work

"Explain your decision-making process" [1]. When taking controversial actions or making difficult decisions, explaining the rationale earns buy-in and teaches the team how to make similar decisions.

### Admitting Uncertainty

"There's nothing wrong with saying you'll need some time to think or learn more before making an important decision" [1].

## Healthy Tension

"Differing perspectives help make teams and products better, so a little professional tension can be really valuable" [1]. The limit: tension must be net-positive. If it forces decisions to stall or shifts from professional to personal, "you need to take action to eliminate it quickly before it drags down the team or blows up" [1].

## One-on-Ones

"Have consistent 1-on-1s every week" [1]. These meetings are for the employee's benefit, so they should drive the agenda. The manager's role is to ask open-ended questions: "How are things going?", "How are you feeling?", "What's your opinion on X?" Prioritizing these meetings and refusing to cancel them "will send a signal to your team that you care about their happiness" [1].

## The Maker-to-Manager Transition

As organizations grow, the best individual contributors are promoted to managers. Epstein warns that "just because someone is an outstanding individual contributor doesn't mean they'll be an outstanding manager right off the bat, because it requires a completely different set of skills and experience" [1].

The common failure mode: new managers try to do both their old maker work and their new management duties, "spending 75% effort on making and 75% effort on managing -- a 150% workload resulting in less than 100% output in each area" [1]. The solution is to "put structure in place to allow them to focus on being a manager first and foremost, and take maker duties off of their plate" [1].

## Career Development

"Every 6 months, ask your employees where they want to be in their career in the next 2-3 years. Work with them to put together a plan, help them get the skills and experience they need, and guide them on a course to get there" [1].

## References

1. [Advice for New Managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers) — Aaron Epstein (n.d.)

title: Passion and Motivation
aliases: solution in search of a problem
body:
# Passion and Motivation

The advice to "follow your passion" is among the most common and least useful counsel given to aspiring founders. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] dissect what passion actually means in the context of startups, when it helps, when it misleads, and what to do when you do not have a burning problem to solve [1].

## The Solution in Search of a Problem

The failure case that prompted this discussion: a YC founder wrote, "We chose to create a company around a hot technology we were really excited about. We eventually raised funds because there were enough investors excited about it too, but now looking back, we had it backwards. We were the classic Silicon Valley startup in search of a problem" [1].

Caldwell defines a solution in search of a problem (SISP): founders build something and then try to find someone who wants it. "As long as technology has existed, this has been an issue" [1]. Caldwell identifies the school metaphor at the root: students are trained to accomplish specific tasks and are rewarded when doing what their smart friends are doing. Applied to startups, this produces founders who treat investor approval as the equivalent of a good grade, and a "cool" idea as the equivalent of getting into a prestigious school [1].

Seibel adds the diagnostic: founders see the "superficial similarity between what you're doing and things that appear to be working" and "try to hide from the fact that you have no idea what you're doing." The difference between going through the motions and "the damn thing actually working" requires looking in the mirror [1].

## When Passion Works

Passion is valuable when it is genuine and specific -- when the founder would work on the problem even without money or approval from authority figures [1].

Caldwell traces Microsoft and Apple back to their origins: people who were "really into this weird niche thing" of microcomputers. "No one told them to go work on microcomputers. They probably would be working on that stuff even if there was no money in it" [1]. He defines passion as "finding stuff that you care about even if there was no money in it, and that you're doing it even if there's no approval from authority figures.
