2. [On Starting and Scaling Direct Mail Automation Startup Lob](https://ycombinator.com/library/6a-on-starting-and-scaling-direct-mail-automation-startup-lob) -- [[Harry Zhang]] (n.d.)
3. [How to Start a Startup: Building for the Enterprise](https://ycombinator.com/library/73-how-to-start-a-startup-building-for-the-enterprise) -- Aaron Levie (2017)

title: Becoming a Founder
body:
# Becoming a Founder

[[Michael Seibel]] addresses the question of how to prepare for founding a startup from two angles: advice for college students on decisions that will help or hinder their ability to start a company later, and a framework for understanding the three career paths in tech [1] [2].

## Decisions That Block Future Founders

Seibel frequently talks to people in their late 20s and 30s who want to start startups but cannot. A decision they made when they were younger is either blocking or creating a serious barrier. The most common blockers [1]:

- They did not learn how to write code
- They never met anyone in college who knew how to code
- They significantly increased their expenses early in life, did not save, and now cannot afford to begin a company
- They created roots post-college in a city that is not startup-friendly

## Advice for College Students

Seibel frames this as what those blocked founders would tell their younger selves [1]:

**Learn to code.** This does not mean majoring in Computer Science. College offers a chance to learn the basics. Being a programmer gives the ability to work and start a company in any industry. "You don't have to be the best developer, and you don't have to write code your entire life if you don't want to -- but knowing how to code gives you the proverbial golden ticket." The number one barrier to starting a tech startup is not knowing how to code [1].

**Meet people with real skills.** College puts you next to designers, programmers, salespeople, and scientists. Make friends with people who have different skills. These people could become cofounders or early employees [1].

**Have fun.** For most successful founders, college grades and accolades had almost nothing to do with their future success. "Personally, the greatest career opportunity college gave me was the chance to make friendships with really smart people" [1].

**Take responsibility.** Join the leadership of a club where you must execute on a real-world task -- organizing a conference, creating a publication, volunteer work, playing on a sports team. Learn what you are good and bad at when working with others [1].

**Be ready to hit the ground running.** Seibel disagrees with the cultural norm of using your 20s to find yourself. "Your 20s are your most valuable working years: You often have very few obligations (no family), a very low burn rate (you're used to living in a dorm), and you are still young enough to meet new people and learn new skills." Use college to get partying out of your system and spend your 20s working hard [1].

**Be your own career counselor.** Career counseling at most universities is "really really bad." Use some college time to figure out what you want to do after graduation. Having a plan, even one you change, is better than no plan [1].

**Ignore parental pressure if needed.** Many people pursue careers because of parental pressure and poor career counseling. "The number of doctors and lawyers I know who really don't love their jobs or their lives is astounding" [1].

**Be careful with student debt.** Student debt forces people away from startups and into big companies. Once in a big company, expenses spin up (car, nice apartment) and the person prices themselves out of starting a company. Expensive private schools without good financial aid are worth avoiding. The best private schools are generous with financial aid [1].

Seibel states that the prime startup years are ages 22 to 32. "Every year you are not doing a startup you are losing 10% of this prime time" [1].

## The Three Paths in Tech

Seibel outlines three primary career paths in the tech industry: founder, executive, and employee. He leaves out investor because the best path to becoming an investor starts with being successful (or failing) at one of these three [2].

### Founder

**Pros**: Work on something you are passionate about; bring something new into the world; high responsibility inspires extreme productivity; choose who you work with; learn new skills at an extremely fast pace [2].

**Cons**: Incredibly stressful (even success hurts); probably will not maximize personal earnings; significant financial, skillset, and location hurdles to get started; large-scale success often requires a decade-plus commitment; commitment level can significantly hurt personal relationships [2].

**Strategy**: The initial goal is to accumulate four prerequisites [2]:
1. Potential teammates with the technical skills to build the MVP
2. A financial plan (savings, friends/family investment, bootstrapping, or reduced spending to create a 6-12 month runway)
3. A problem that you and your potential teammates are passionate about solving
4. An idea for how to solve the problem (the least important prerequisite, but still required)

A popular mistake is trying to hustle around a missing prerequisite instead of solving the underlying issue. If the team lacks technical skills, do not work with a dev shop -- make friends with people who have those skills and convince them to join [2].

When hard barriers prevent starting immediately, Seibel recommends moving to a tech hub (preferably the Bay Area) and working for a tech company until you can save money, meet the right teammates, or discover the right problem. "It usually takes at least 10 years to build a large and impactful company. It's fine to delay your start date in order to give yourself the best chance of success" [2].

**Experience is overvalued.** "In almost all cases, no matter what knowledge you bring to the table, you will learn most of what you need to know about your problem, your customer, and the best solution after you start your company" [2]. For a deeper look at why inexperience can be an advantage, see [[First-Time Founders]].

### Executive (Senior Manager at a Large Company)

**Pros**: Stable income and benefits; high prestige (only very successful founders have more); a higher likelihood of having a huge impact (given most startups fail); does not require building a team and acquiring money [2].

**Cons**: Producing results is not necessarily how you move up -- internal politics are as important or more; success can be blocked by others inside the organization; requires picking companies that will be growing and successful for a long time; takes a long time to get significant responsibility [2].

**Two strategies** [2]:
1. **Pick a fast-growing company early.** If the company grows rapidly for years, opportunities to become an executive emerge. This is similar to a VC's task of picking winners. Example: being one of the first 100 people at Facebook and staying ten years.
2. **Move diagonally up between established companies.** Start at Google, get hired at Dropbox as a team lead, move to Yahoo to become a director, move back to Google. This requires constantly watching for better opportunities inside and outside the current company.

### Employee (Individual Contributor / Middle Manager)

**Pros**: Stable income and benefits; more work and fewer meetings; more often directly affecting the customer; flexibility in where and how much you work with a high-demand skill set; more time for friends and family [2].

**Cons**: Productivity can be blocked by bad management; often no control over what you work on; no voice in major decisions; harder to become very wealthy; can be boring; easier to be fired if skills or productivity decline [2].

**Strategy**: Similar to the executive path. Either spot and join a quickly growing company, or find a way into a well-known successful company. It is much easier to move between brand-name companies when you start at a brand-name company. The path is easier to optimize as a software developer [2].

### The Cost of Exploration

Seibel warns against overextending the discovery phase. If it takes 5-10 years to get truly good at something and 10 years are spent discovering what to get good at, "it's going to take a long time for you to feel like a highly skilled productive person." Exploration is not wrong, but its costs must be understood and planned for [2].

## The Three Types of People (Michael Seibel)

Seibel identifies three groups when it comes to the startup question [3]:

**Group 1: Natural entrepreneurs (~1% of the population).** "The only thing they can really do is work in a radically entrepreneurial job. They will not enjoy their life inside of a big company." Historically, these people started small businesses [3].

**Group 2: Fence-sitters (also rare).** Equally capable as a founder or as an effective big-company operator. "A lot of people confuse themselves for this group when in reality this group is also probably pretty small" [3].

**Group 3: Big-company operators (the largest group).** "They want a system to optimize. They want an existing path, an existing set of rules they can operate and play by" [3].

Seibel warns against guilt-tripping in either direction. He admits his own past bias: "When I first started giving people advice, I really assumed that because you could sort of start a startup, you should start a startup. I quickly realized working at YC that's a bad idea" [3].

## The Self-Assessment

Seibel's diagnostic questions [3]:

- **Where am I at my best?** Not just resilient, but actually delivering peak results.
- **Where am I organically applying effort?** Where is motivation 100% self-generated?
- **When do I outperform?** For Seibel, his high-gear moments never happened in structured K-12 settings.

Three yes answers suggest the startup path: (1) Do I like being the underdog? (2) Do I seek the hard challenges that most people shy away from? (3) Do I thrive when I take personal responsibility for success or failure? [3]

## The Underdog as Motivation

Seibel uses his own biography to illustrate the underdog drive [3]. In high school, he was placed in the "regular" track instead of the "gifted" track. At Yale, he was kicked out for poor grades. Two months later, the anger returned. At Justin.tv/Twitch: "Doing startups was never my plan but looking at my history in high school and college I really needed to be an underdog in order to motivate myself to succeed. Startups are the classic underdog, 99% of the time -- they fail. This was so motivating that I didn't even need to believe in our first idea to stay excited" [3].

The Justin.tv survival story: on five separate occasions over five years, the company nearly died. "All of these adversities were exactly what we needed to stay hungry" [3].

## Beware Biased Advice

Seibel identifies three sources of biased career advice [3]:

**Big companies.** Google's recruiting machine has three purposes: (1) get smart people to work on hard tasks, (2) get smart people so they are not working for competitors, and (3) get smart people so they are not creating companies that will compete with Google [3].

**Peers.** In school through college, peers are excellent information sources. Then suddenly, with career advice, "they almost are horrible." The shift happens because college goes "from one track to a thousand tracks" [3].

**Prestige culture.** "Do you actually want to do the work of a lawyer? No, of course not" [3].

## Should You Start a Startup? (Harj Taggar)

[[Harj Taggar]] addresses the question from the perspective of someone who has worked with almost a thousand startup founders at YC [4].

### Resilience Is the Key Trait

Taggar identifies resilience as the single most important quality for startup founders. He warns against using confidence as a proxy: "Someone could appear very confident initially, but not be resilient when their startup hit roadblocks" [4]. He cites Sajith of Benchling as an example of hidden resilience: quietly spoken, struggled at Demo Day, made no revenue for over a year, now runs a $6 billion company [4].

### Motivations Can Change

Taggar argues that initial motivations matter less than people think. "It's fine to start a startup to become rich" [4]. The enduring motivations are genuine interest in the problem and love for the people you work with [4].

### The Worst-Case Analysis

Taggar's practical framework: "Ask yourself, What do I have to lose?" -- not rhetorically, but as a concrete exercise. The calculus differs by situation. A recent college graduate faces minimal risk. A FAANG employee nine years in stands to lose more [4].

### The Learning Value of Failing

Taggar argues the learning from starting a company is undervalued in worst-case analysis. Rippling hires former founders to run product divisions. Nick Grandy went from a failed startup to Airbnb's first employee to $3 billion Outschool [4].

### When to Quit and Start

The signal: "If you find your job is draining, but you have energy to work on your side projects on evenings and weekends, that's a sign it may be time to quit" [4].

## How to Succeed (Sam Altman)

[[Sam Altman]] adds that ambitious visions make it easier, not harder, to build a team. "In 2018, at least in Silicon Valley, it's easier to start a hard startup than it is to start an easy startup." Employee 20 joins because the mission matters [5]. See [[Founder Mindset]] for more on Altman's founder trait framework.

## References

1. [A Letter to College Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4d-a-letter-to-college-students) — [[Michael Seibel]] (March 2017)
2. [Three Paths in the Tech Industry: Founder, Executive, or Employee](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4h-three-paths-in-the-tech-industry-founder-executive-or-employee) — [[Michael Seibel]] (September 2017)
3. [Should I Start a Startup?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/60-should-i-start-a-startup) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2018)
4. [Should You Start a Startup?](https://ycombinator.com/library/GV-should-you-start-a-startup) -- [[Harj Taggar]] (2022)
5. [How to Succeed with a Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/89-how-to-succeed-with-a-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (August 2018)

title: Technical Founders
body:
# Technical Founders

[[Diana Hu]], YC Group Partner and former CTO of Escher Reality (acquired by Niantic, makers of Pokemon Go), provides the comprehensive guide to being a technical founder at the earliest stages [1]. The role evolves through three phases: ideating, building to launch, and iterating to [[Product-Market Fit]].

## What a Technical Founder Is (and Isn't)

A technical founder is not "somebody to build my app" [1]. Hu is direct: "A technical founder is a partner in this whole journey of a startup, and it requires a really intense level of commitment" [1].

In the early stages, the role looks like being a lead developer with key differences [1]:
- **You do all the tech things.** Front end, back end, DevOps, website, UX, even provisioning Google accounts. For hardware, if you know electrical engineering, you will also need to learn mechanical [1].
- **You talk to users.** Building in isolation produces the wrong product [1].
- **You bias toward "good enough" over perfect architecture.** Big companies reward the perfect architecture; startups do not [1].
- **You decide with incomplete information.** Comfort with technical debt, inefficient processes, and ugly code is required [1].
- **You do whatever it takes.** The employee mindset of "this is not in my pay grade" does not apply [1].

Whether the technical founder is the CEO or CTO depends on the product, the industry, and the team composition. Hu has seen technical founders in both roles [1].

## The Three Stages

### Stage 1: Ideating (Days)

Build a prototype as fast as possible with the singular focus of showing it to users. It does not have to fully work. See [[MVP Planning]] for detailed examples [1].

### Stage 2: Building to Launch (Weeks)

Build an MVP that gets commitment from users, ideally payment. The three principles (do things that don't scale, create a 90/10 solution, choose tech for iteration speed) are covered in detail in [[MVP Planning]] [1].

### Stage 3: Launch and Iterate to PMF

Once launched, the technical founder's role shifts to iteration [1]:

**Quickly iterate with hard and soft data.** Set up a dashboard tracking the main KPI. Use simple analytics: Google Analytics, Amplitude, or Mixpanel. Do not reach for LogStash or Prometheus -- "these are great for large companies, but not at your stage" [1]. Combine hard data with soft data from ongoing user conversations to understand why users stay or churn [1].

**Continuously launch.** Segment is the canonical example. They launched five times in roughly a month, each time adding features users had requested (Node support, PHP support, WordPress). Their first Hacker News post in December 2012 got strong engagement -- a hint of product-market fit. They kept launching, kept iterating, and eventually reached a $3.2 billion exit to Twilio [1].

**Balance building and fixing.** Technical founders must make thoughtful choices between fixing bugs, adding features, and addressing technical debt. "Tech debt is totally fine. You gotta get comfortable, a little bit, with the heat of your tech burning" [1].

Hu illustrates with Pokemon Go. When it launched in 2016, nobody could log into the game -- the load balancer and backend could not handle the traffic (Pokemon Go reached Twitter's active user count in one month, compared to Twitter's ten years). The technical failure did not kill the company. To this day, Pokemon Go generates over a billion dollars in annual revenue [1].

**WePay** illustrates the pivot pattern. They launched as a B2C payments product similar to Venmo. Analytics showed no one used features like messaging. They talked to users and discovered their biggest customer, GoFundMe, did not care about the B2C UI -- they just wanted the payments API. WePay pivoted to an API model, built a first version without even having technical docs, and that version achieved product-market fit [1].

## Common Post-Launch Mistakes

1. **Building like a big company.** "Asking 'what would Google do?' is almost certainly a trap" [1].
2. **Hiring to move quickly.** This is nuanced and can backfire [1].
3. **Focusing too much on refactoring.** "Sometimes I see CTOs like, 'Okay, we launched. I'm gonna hunker down and just get into building.'" The technical founder must stay involved with users and product insights [1].
4. **Only building product features.** "You also need to build tech to grow. Some of the best growth hacks are where engineers pair up with sales and growth folks who are non-technical" [1].

## How the Role Evolves Post-PMF

After [[Product-Market Fit]], the technical founder's role changes significantly [1]:

- **Now you can build to scale.** Identify which pieces of the tech need to be reworked and refactored. This is the right time -- not before PMF [1].
- **Expect things to break.** Breaking because of demand is a good problem [1].
- **Define engineering culture.** This is when the engineering culture gets established [1].
- **Hire, but expect less coding time.** At 2-5 engineers, the technical founder can still code ~70% of the time. At 5-10 engineers, less than 50%. Beyond 10, "you probably won't really have time to code" and must decide between an architect role and a people-management (VP Eng) role [1].

## The One Takeaway

Hu closes with a single principle: "Startups move quickly" [1]. Every decision about architecture, tools, hiring, and process should be evaluated against that standard.

## References

1. [Tips for Technical Startup Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Is-tips-for-technical-startup-founders) -- [[Diana Hu]] (2024)

title: Network Effects
body:
# Network Effects

Network effects occur when a product becomes more valuable as more people use it. The concept is central to understanding why certain startups achieve winner-take-most dynamics. [[Anu Hariharan]]'s case studies of Chinese technology companies provide some of the most detailed analyses of network effects in the YC canon.

## Types of Network Effects

Hariharan draws a distinction between Metcalfe's law and Reed's law in her analysis of [[Pinduoduo]]:

**Metcalfe's law** holds that the value of a network grows proportionally to the square of the number of users. This applies to one-to-one connection networks like telephones or messaging platforms [1].

**Reed's law** holds that the value of a group-forming network grows exponentially with the ease of group formation. Hariharan identifies Pinduoduo as having "one of the most powerful network effects in the world" because it taps into groups of social relationships rather than one-to-one connections [1]. The team purchase mechanic means every user has an incentive to recruit others, and the recruiting happens through existing social groups.

## Network Effects in Practice

### WeChat

[[WeChat]]'s network effects compounded across layers. The messaging platform created a base network effect, then the Moments social feed added a content layer, then WeChat Pay added a financial layer, then Mini Programs added an application layer. Each layer reinforced the others, making switching costs enormous [2]. WeChat grew from 0 to 889 million MAUs by stacking network effects on top of each other.

### Toutiao

[[Toutiao]] demonstrates data network effects: more users generate more engagement data, which improves the recommendation algorithm, which makes the content more addictive, which attracts more users [3]. Algorithms without data are useless, and data without an addictive product is unavailable. The virtuous cycle compounds product quality over time.

### Pinduoduo

Pinduoduo's team purchase model creates network effects at the group level. Users recruit friends and family into purchasing groups to unlock lower prices, which brings new users to the platform, who then form their own groups [1]. The social graph becomes the distribution channel.

## Network Effects and Growth

[[Paul Graham]] notes that raising money lets founders "choose their growth rate," and the most successful startups take VC money even when profitable because it lets them grow faster and fend off competitors, "especially in businesses with network effects" [4]. Network effects create a strategic imperative for fast growth: the first company to reach scale often locks in its advantage.

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) -- [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne (2020)
2. [How WeChat Grew to Be the #1 App in the World](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3t-how-wechat-grew-to-be-the-1-app-in-the-world) -- [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)
3. [The Hidden Forces Behind China's Content King Toutiao](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3x-the-hidden-forces-behind-china-s-content-king-toutiao) -- [[Anu Hariharan]] (n.d.)
4. [Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) -- [[Paul Graham]] (September 2012)

title: Startup Outcomes and Optionality
body:
# Startup Outcomes and Optionality

[[Sam Altman]] addresses a common but rarely discussed situation: the startup that is successful but is not on a trajectory to be a billion-dollar company. Using a metaphor attributed to David Weiden, he describes starting a startup as being pushed off a cliff with a bag of aerospace parts [1].

## The Airplane vs. Spaceship Framework

Sometimes the parts make a spaceship -- huge market, great product, natural monopoly, credible path to being the winner. Plenty of advice exists for that case. Sometimes the parts make nothing at all. But "a reasonable amount of the time, it turns out you have the parts for an airplane" [1].

An airplane is a company with profitability within reach, on track to be worth $100 million with several more years of hard work. "This is nothing to be ashamed of -- quite the opposite, obviously. This is a triumph by almost any metric." It is what happens to "most very lucky, very smart, very determined people who decide to start a startup and have everything go their way except happening upon a perfect market" [1].

The founder has two choices: try to get a new bag of parts (new market, new product, very different strategy) and keep trying to build a spaceship, or decide to have a really good airplane [1].

## Founder-Investor Misalignment

The most important thing to understand: this is where founders and investors often get misaligned. Investors are looking for spaceships; airplanes do not matter much to them, though they matter quite a bit to founders [1].

Existing investors will usually be willing to take almost any amount of risk to turn an airplane into a spaceship. The typical advice offered is little more than "point your plane at the moon, burn more fuel, and let's see what happens" [1].

Altman's recommendation: "Be frank and realistic with your existing investors; you will eventually persuade them that a small multiple is better than a loss." The best investors understand how venture investing works and do not fight over such outcomes -- many treat founders extra-generously hoping to fund their next company. The longer founders delude their investors, the worse shape they end up in [1].

## The Overvaluation Trap

Raising too much money at too high a valuation severely limits optionality. "Very often I've seen cases where founders know in their hearts they have an airplane but are able to convince good investors it might still be a spaceship. This really causes a lot of heartache, and often precludes your opportunity for a good acquisition later" [1].

The answer is simple in theory but difficult in practice: do not raise a lot of money at a high price when you think the company will probably not increase 10x in value and you would be happy selling soon. This may require changing how the company is run and finding a path to profitability with current capital. "It's far less painful to deal with this now rather than putting the company in a position that will preclude the option you think is best" [1].

## Positioning for Acquisition

Companies get bought, not sold. It is very difficult to force an acquisition, and nearly impossible if the company has not built something legitimately valuable. "Somehow, this fact surprises founders who decide 'we'd like to get acquired' almost every time" [1].

What founders can do: begin developing relationships with potential acquirers early. It usually takes a long time, and personal relationships help [1].

The outcome is still worth pride. "Silicon Valley is filled with people who had one or several mid-success companies before eventually hitting it out of the park" [1].

## References

1. [Navigating Success for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4s-navigating-success-for-your-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)

title: Pivoting
body:
# Pivoting

A pivot is changing what your startup works on. YC group partners define it simply: "If you're not making something people want, you usually need to change your idea or your startup will fail" [1]. A huge number of the most successful startups had to pivot, and the process of working on the wrong thing is often what teaches founders enough about the space to find the right thing [1].

## Pivoting Is Normal, Not a Failure

Multiple YC group partners independently reinforce that pivoting is not a sign of weakness. [[Harj Taggar]], [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Jared Friedman]], Gustaf Alstromer, [[Tom Blomfield]], [[Brad Flora]], and Aaron Epstein all share pivot stories from billion-dollar YC companies [1]. The message: many of the biggest YC successes started as completely different companies.

## Pivot Stories from Billion-Dollar Companies

### Brex: VR Headsets to Corporate Cards

Brex's founders, Henrique Dubugras and Pedro Franceschi, applied to YC with an augmented reality headset idea. The two founders had no hardware experience -- they had built a Stripe-like payments business in Brazil as high schoolers [1]. Taggar recalls: "I thought there was no way that that idea would stick." Three or four weeks into the batch, after consulting hardware experts, they realized they were not the team to build a VR headset. They went back to payments, the domain where they had deep expertise [1].

The pivot illustrates what Taggar calls the "smart person trap": smart people feel that worthy problems must feel hard. An idea in their area of expertise -- payments -- felt too easy, too "been there, done that." The harder, shinier VR challenge was more appealing but a poor fit [1].

### GOAT: Group Dinners to Sneaker Marketplace

Dalton Caldwell describes GOAT's pivot. YC funded the team over ten years ago as "Grub with Us," a site for meeting local people through group dinners. The concept worked within niche communities -- San Francisco's investor community loved it -- but retention was awful [1].

Two years after YC, the founders realized they were personally obsessed with collecting sneakers and that the secondary market for limited-edition sneakers was growing. In 2015, they pivoted to a sneaker marketplace [1].

Caldwell emphasizes the timing: "In 2015, this was so far outside the norm of conventional wisdom to decide to pivot your company into sneaker collecting." The really great pivots often come when founders are "years ahead of consensus" [1].

The psychological dynamic matters: founders sometimes need morale to drop low enough that they stop optimizing for what sounds impressive and start working on what genuinely interests them. "They've internalized, 'I'm gonna fail. So I might as well do something cool on the way down.' That's what lets them take the blinders off" [1].

### GoCardless: Bill-Splitting App to Recurring Payments

[[Tom Blomfield]] describes his first company. He and his co-founders applied to YC with Group.io, a bill-splitting and group payments app -- "a classic fintech tar pit. Every college kid thinks this is the best idea in the world" [1]. Retention was terrible. After Paul Graham told them to stop building features and just try to get users, four founders spent two weeks cold-calling hundreds of sports club treasurers in the UK at 3-4 AM Pacific time. They got one new user [1].

The pivot: they took the core payments infrastructure and applied it to businesses instead of consumers. Direct debit for small businesses collecting recurring payments from customers turned out to be a perfect use case. Twelve years later, GoCardless is a multi-billion dollar company doing the same thing [1].

Blomfield reflects: "When you're young, you think of the entire world as consumer, and the older you get, the more you're like, 'Oh, I guess businesses buy software'" [1].

### Clipboard Health: Nurse Job Board to Staffing Platform

Gustaf Alstromer describes Clipboard Health's journey. Founder Wei Deng came in with a hiring platform for nurses -- "almost like an Indeed but for nurses." The idea was common and had no clear "why now" [1].

Deng spent years selling to hospitals and talking to everyone in the ecosystem. The key insight came from observing that when a nurse called in sick, hospitals always turned to staffing agencies. This was the real pain point -- not job placement but shift filling [1].

Alstromer praises Deng's commitment to "doing things that don't scale": she manually coordinated getting nurses to hospitals before writing any software. "She literally spent time trying to understand exactly what is the problem that I need to solve -- and like maybe I can just manually solve this problem" [1]. Only after doing this repeatedly did she build software to automate it.

### Azure Reality: AR Marketing to AR SDK

Diana (co-founder of Azure Reality) describes starting with AR technology and no clear market. They were "a cool technology in search of a problem" [1]. Their first customers were marketers doing splashy AR campaigns. It took getting into YC to realize that marketers were just being polite -- "that wasn't real feedback" [1]. Through user interviews, they discovered that game developers were the real market. Azure Reality built an AR SDK for game developers and was eventually acquired by Niantic (creators of Pokemon Go) [1].

### Creative Market: Design Community to Design Marketplace

[[Brad Flora]] describes pivoting from Color Lovers, a creative design community with over a million members, to Creative Market, a marketplace for graphic design assets. For a year and a half after YC, they tried multiple monetization approaches (software sales, ads, contests) without a clear primary KPI. "The reason it took so long was because we didn't have a main KPI that we were focused on" [1].

Once they pivoted to a marketplace, the KPI became obvious: are people buying? "I became obsessed with it. I was like refreshing it every 15 minutes" [1].

Flora's advice: "Make sure you have a main KPI that you're focused on, and make sure that you're tracking it on a regular basis." If you do not immediately know what your main KPI is -- the thing you chase when you wake up every morning -- you probably need to pivot [1].

## When to Pivot

Several signals suggest it is time [1]:

1. **Retention is terrible** -- users try the product but do not come back (Group.io, Grub with Us)
2. **Users are being polite, not enthusiastic** -- they say nice things but do not actually use or pay for the product (Azure Reality's marketers)
3. **No clear primary KPI** -- the founders cannot identify the single metric that defines whether the business is working (Color Lovers)
4. **Sustained inability to acquire users despite effort** -- four founders cold-calling for two weeks and getting one user (Group.io)

The basketball analogy, attributed to Aaron Epstein: "When you pivot you have to keep one foot stationary. You may keep the type of product that you're building, but maybe the audience is going to change. Or you might build a different product for the same audience." Good pivots build on learnings from prior ideas [1].

## Pivot Hell

A company can pivot too much. YC has a term for it: **pivot hell** -- changing to a completely new idea every week. Not a slightly different idea, but an entirely new startup concept each time [1].

[[Jared Friedman]] explains the root cause: perfectionism. "If you're too much of a perfectionist up front and you want everything planned out perfectly... I think that keeps you in this loop of constantly iterating instead of just saying, 'Look, there's a bunch of unknowns, and I know that, but the only way I'll truly learn is if I sink my teeth into one idea and push it forward'" [1].

The paradox: no startup idea is ever perfect. Founders searching for the perfect idea keep switching because every idea reveals flaws, and they mistake those flaws for fatal problems [1].

Friedman shares a story of a company so deep in pivot hell that they had to defer demo day because they had nothing to present. But six months later, they hit on a high-quality idea: a regulatory change had created an opportunity, and their time exploring the industry -- working through bad ideas -- had given them the domain knowledge to spot it [1].

The lesson: "In order to have this idea, they had to go through a whole bunch of bad ideas first. But in the process of doing this, they got to know the industry really well" [1].

## The Founders Are the Location

The analogy from real estate -- "location, location, location" -- maps to startups as "the founders, the founders, the founders." Everything else can change. The same founders working on a different idea can look like a completely different company. "Oftentimes, the founders even act like different founders. They become more formidable when they're working on an idea that they're excited about and they're well suited for" [1].

YC group partners describe their role as helping founders reach the moment of clarity faster -- sometimes in the interview, sometimes early in the batch. The realization that the current idea does not work is not the end; it is the prerequisite for finding the right idea [1].

## The Process

Seibel frames the meta-lesson: "If you're already a builder, the process is truly about finding a problem you can solve, and often you won't know what that problem exactly is until you've actually made contact with real customers. That's the knowledge you're looking for" [1].

Alstromer adds: "One of the big awesome questions that founders should always ask themselves is: 'What do I understand or what do I know, and what do I believe that nobody else knows or believes?' That's actually the core of a lot of the pivots that really work incredibly well" [1].

## More Pivot Stories

### Bump to Google Photos: Three Pivots (David Lieb)

[[David Lieb]]'s journey from Bump to Google Photos involved two pivots that illustrate how each failed product teaches you what the right product needs [2].

**Bump to Flock**: Bump had 150 million downloads but infrequent usage and no sustainable business model (~$1/user/year). Lieb called the top 100 users and discovered they used Bump for photo sharing, not contact sharing. The engineering team simultaneously discovered they could predict sharing behavior from photo metadata. Flock combined these insights but failed at launch because it required friends to already have the app (the cold-start problem) [2].

**Flock to Photo Roll**: The failure taught Lieb that to solve automated photo sharing, they needed to "upstream" to the camera roll. Photo Roll was a better gallery app with Flock built in -- but they arrived at this insight at the end of their $20 million runway. The product was right; the timing was wrong. Google's acquisition provided the resources [2].

Each pivot preserved the learning from the previous attempt. The photo metadata technology from Bump became Flock's core feature, which became Google Photos' sharing intelligence.

### Perplexity: Twitter Search to General Search (Aravind Srinivas)

[[Aravind Srinivas]]'s Perplexity started as a structured search tool for specific databases (Twitter data organized into SQL-queryable tables). Enterprise data sources refused to share data. The pivot came from the realization that a simpler, more general approach -- letting the LLM do the work at inference time over unstructured web data -- would be more scalable than building domain-specific indexes [3].

The pivot was also influenced by a key insight only realized after launching the general product: if AI answers eliminate link-clicking, the ad economy declines, giving Perplexity a structural advantage against Google that did not exist in the enterprise search model [3].

### Gobble: Four Product Versions to Find PMF (Ooshma Garg)

[[Ooshma Garg]] launched four different versions of Gobble over five years [4]:
1. **Chef marketplace**: Connecting home chefs with customers. Got press but not sustained growth.
2. **On-demand delivery (Gobble Instant)**: Failed because the target market was suburban families, delivery was slow, and food wilted in transit.
3. **Personalized dinner subscription**: A Netflix-for-food model. Five times more revenue than previous versions, but the competitive landscape prevented fundraising.
4. **Dinner kits (mise en place)**: The company does the prep work; the customer finishes the meal in 10 minutes in one pan. This version produced organic growth with zero marketing hires.

The key lesson: Garg separated the mission from the product. "A company is not just a product, a company is solving a problem. It's a mission. And if the mission is alive, you should keep working on it" [4].

### Embark Trucks: Resisting One Pivot, Then Making Another (Geoff Ralston)

[[Geoff Ralston]] describes Embark's pivot discipline. During YC, the team discovered that an Australian mining company offered a potentially lucrative contract for self-driving mining trucks. The team was tempted but chose to stay the course with campuses and demonstrate growth [5].

Later, after succeeding with the original idea, the founders concluded the campus market was too small and pivoted to self-driving trucks on highways -- "an enormous market and although still fabulously difficult, a more tractable problem" [5]. The distinction: they resisted a premature distraction but later made a deliberate, evidence-based pivot [5].

### Meesho: Two Pivots Enabled by Co-Founder Trust (Vidit Aatrey)

[[Vidit Aatrey]] took Meesho through two pivots. The first: from FashNear (hyperlocal fashion delivery) to a Shopify-for-WhatsApp tool (inspired by discovering shopkeepers already selling on WhatsApp). The second: from the tool for existing shopkeepers to a reseller marketplace for housewives (inspired by discovering that more than half of users were not the intended audience) [6].

The second pivot required shutting down a product that was "not doing bad" in favor of an experiment running on WhatsApp with no app or website. Aatrey attributes his ability to pivot to co-founder trust: "I have seen so many friends around me who were just not able to pivot because it was a hard conversation with their co-founder" [6].

### Lever: Throwing Out a Whole Product (Sarah Nahm)

[[Sarah Nahm]] describes building an entire first product at Lever, gathering customer feedback, and realizing it was "something incremental... just a better version of what was there before" [7]. They threw it out. The discipline was accountability to customer reactions, not internal conviction [7].

### Dropbox: Strategic Contraction (Drew Houston)

[[Drew Houston]] describes Dropbox's expansion into photos (Carousel) and email (Mailbox), then the painful decision to kill both products. The catalyst: re-reading Andy Grove's "Only the Paranoid Survive" and recognizing that Dropbox was in a strategic inflection point. "Everybody loves the idea of focus, but what focus actually means is shooting stuff you love and turning down things you know you can make work" [8].

This is not a pivot in the traditional sense but rather a strategic contraction -- returning to the core after an expansion that diffused the company's identity and execution [8].

### SigFig: The Case Against Pivoting (Parker Conrad)

[[Parker Conrad]] describes seven years of pivots at SigFig, where the recurring pattern was: five ideas, four fail completely, one mostly fails with a glimmer that leads to the next pivot. The lesson Conrad draws is the opposite of "keep pivoting": "If it's not working, give up immediately" [9]. The Airbnb pivot story is the exception, not the rule [9].

## References

1. [YC's Group Partners Share Their Favorite Pivot Stories](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Iy-yc-s-group-partners-share-their-favorite-pivot-stories) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Jared Friedman]], Gustaf Alstromer, [[Tom Blomfield]], [[Brad Flora]], Aaron Epstein (n.d.)
2. [On Starting and Scaling One of the Biggest iOS Apps](https://ycombinator.com/library/5f-on-starting-and-scaling-one-of-the-biggest-ios-apps) -- [[David Lieb]], Gustaf Alstromer (n.d.)
3. [How To Build The Future: Aravind Srinivas](https://ycombinator.com/library/MC-how-to-build-the-future-aravind-srinivas) -- [[Aravind Srinivas]], [[David Lieb]] (2025)
4. [On starting and scaling meal kit company Gobble](https://ycombinator.com/library/69-on-starting-and-scaling-meal-kit-company-gobble) -- [[Ooshma Garg]] (2016)
5. [Embark Trucks' (W16) road to IPO](https://ycombinator.com/library/Co-embark-trucks-w16-road-to-ipo) -- [[Geoff Ralston]] (2021)
6. [On Starting and Scaling Indian Shopping Site Meesho](https://ycombinator.com/library/6M-on-starting-and-scaling-indian-shopping-site-meesho) -- [[Vidit Aatrey]], Adora Cheung (2019)
7. [On Starting and Scaling Recruiting Software Startup Lever](https://ycombinator.com/library/6I-on-starting-and-scaling-recruiting-software-startup-lever) -- [[Sarah Nahm]], Holly Liu (2019)
8. [On Starting and Scaling Dropbox (YC W07)](https://ycombinator.com/library/6S-on-starting-and-scaling-dropbox-yc-w07) -- [[Drew Houston]], [[Sam Altman]] (2017)
9. [How To Build The Future: Parker Conrad](https://ycombinator.com/library/M2-how-to-build-the-future-parker-conrad) -- [[Parker Conrad]], [[Garry Tan]] (2025)

title: Selling to Startups
body:
# Selling to Startups

Selling to startups has been a formula for success at YC. Stripe started as a payments processor for YC companies. PagerDuty's early customers were startups, still referenced in their S-1 filing years later [1]. But the strategy fails when founders cargo cult it without understanding why it works for certain businesses and not others.

## When Selling to Startups Does Not Work

### The Long Sales Cycle Escape

The most common mistake: a product naturally belongs in large enterprises, but the sales cycle seems long, so the founder sells to startups instead [1]. [[Brad Flora]] describes the pattern: a founder who spent four years at a large company, saw a problem only large companies have, was funded because of that enterprise insight, and then in the first office hours says "I'm going to sell this enterprise-grade DevTools system to startups for $100 a month" [1].

Snowflake only makes sense with massive data volumes. Workday's early customers were doing six and seven-figure deals because comprehensive HR suites are only needed with hundreds of employees. Trying to sell either product to startups would have been a waste [1].

### Startups Are Not Easier Customers

Founders assume startups will be easier to sell to than enterprises. Flora pushes back: "Why would it be easier to sell to a customer who doesn't have the problem than to sell to a customer who has the problem but takes a long time to make decisions?" [1]. A startup willing to talk about a problem it does not actually have is not evidence of an easier sale -- it is evidence of misdirection [1].

Furthermore, startups can be high-maintenance while paying very little. Enterprise end users may be equally demanding, but the contract size justifies support resources [1].

### The Churn Problem

The most dangerous assumption: that customers acquired as startups will stay as they grow. In some categories, churn is nearly guaranteed [1]:

- **HR tools**: Almost every early-stage HR product gets churned as customers scale
- **Heroku-type products**: Customers churn once engineering teams outgrow simplified platforms
- **CRM**: Startups pick lightweight CRM tools, then switch to Salesforce the moment a VP of Sales wants to scale to 500 people
- **Triplebyte's experience** ([[Harj Taggar]]'s company): The product was built for engineers screening engineers, but every company eventually hires a recruiting team with completely different needs, requiring a brand new product [1]

Flora illustrates this with Perfect Audience, his own startup: early on they could sell to startups because the buyer was a founder or a single marketing person, but as the customer grew into a full marketing team, the requirements changed completely [1]. The underlying pattern: when a product scales with people rather than revenue, the buyer changes as the company grows. The CTO or technical founder who evaluated the tool is replaced by a non-technical department head with entirely different requirements [1].

### When Churn Does Not Happen

Stripe does not churn because payments do not scale with people in the same way. Companies do not want to manage payments in-house regardless of size. The product grows seamlessly from two people to IPO. The test: will the core buyer and their needs remain the same as the company scales? [1]

### Avoiding Sales

Sometimes founders target startups not because the strategy makes sense but because they are afraid to sell. Flora calls this the "Civilization fallacy": "I just want to turn my startup into a real-time strategy game where I can sit at my computer and click on things and watch numbers go up" [1].

The manifestation: building a self-serve flow and waiting. "MVP self-serve flow, dot dot dot, riches" [1]. Founders ping accessible founders in their YC batch but never develop the actual sales skills -- how to get replies to cold emails, what messaging works, how to change behavior. "Building a great self-service interface is not necessarily a strategy for getting customers" [1].

## When Selling to Startups Works

### Grow-With Strategy (Stripe Model)

Get companies when they are two people, and the product sticks with them through IPO and billions in revenue. This requires that the product does not need to be rebuilt as the company scales [1].

### Bridge to SMB (Gusto Model)

Gusto used startups to build a great payroll product, then expanded into the broader small business market. The product was similar enough across segments that startup learnings transferred directly [1].

### Bottoms-Up Enterprise (AWS Model)

Selling to startup engineers creates a path to large enterprises. [[Harj Taggar]] explains: Amazon did not have Microsoft's existing enterprise relationships, so they sold to smart engineers at startups who loved the product. Those engineers later moved to larger companies and advocated for AWS. Amazon was training an entire generation of developers on cloud software [1].

The key caveat: bottoms-up adoption is a booster, not a replacement for enterprise sales. AWS, Stripe, and similar companies still built real enterprise sales teams. "Walmart's not just gonna walk into your self-serve flow" [1].

## Know Which Game You Are Playing

Seibel frames the biggest-picture takeaway: founders must know which strategy they are executing and how selling to startups fits into it [1]. The Stripe game (grow with customers forever), the Gusto game (use startups as training wheels for SMB), and the AWS game (bottoms-up into enterprise) are all viable. "The game of 'I'm gonna sell to big companies by first selling to people that have nothing in common with big companies' -- that's not a good game" [1].

These strategies are well-documented. Seibel advises founders to study the companies that have nailed each path directly. "You don't have to learn all the hard mistakes that they did. You can learn from them" [1].

## References

1. [Inside the Group Partner Lounge: The Ideal Customer](https://ycombinator.com/library/J5-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-the-ideal-customer) — [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Brad Flora]] (2021)

title: Wealth vs. Money
body:
# Wealth vs. Money

[[Garry Tan]] argues that founders should pursue wealth, not money. Drawing on [[Paul Graham]] and philosopher Alan Watts, Tan distinguishes between money (an integer in a database) and wealth (the capacity to create value and generate more value over time) [1].

## Money Is Not Wealth

Paul Graham: "If you're in the middle of Antarctica where there's nothing to buy, it wouldn't matter how much money you had. Wealth is what you want, not money" [1]. Alan Watts made the same point in the 1970s: money "has the same relationship to real wealth -- actual goods and services -- that words have to meaning. As words are not the physical world, money is not wealth" [1].

Tan warns that focusing on money traps people in a consumptive cycle: "You'll end up focused on that next promotion or the trappings of upward mobility" [1]. He describes his own Microsoft years as an example: "My employer was buying my time, and working there was tiring. I got this money, and I'd consume -- buy a new shirt at Banana Republic or a nice martini. I was stuck in a consumptive mode" [1].

## The Path to Wealth: Skills

Wealth creation begins with acquiring skills, particularly skills that are rare and valuable. Tan uses Dennis Rodman as an illustration: Rodman started as a janitor at an airport, then had a growth spurt and pursued basketball. But what made him a Hall of Famer was developing a skill nobody else had -- studying the trajectory and spin of every shooter's ball to position himself for rebounds. "He did something nobody else did or even knew that they should do, and that won his team five NBA championships" [1].

In technology, the most valuable skills are those closest to actual creation: engineering, design, and product. "Being an engineer, designer, or product person is the easiest way to be a craftsperson in modern society. These are the next generation construction workers of our era" [1].

## Above the API vs. Below the API

Tan cites Venkatesh Rao's framework: you either live above the API or below it [1]. An Uber driver works below the API -- following directions from an app, using no specialized skills. The people who created Uber work above the API -- building and maintaining the systems that give people what they want. "If you can do things nobody else can do, you'll have skills that are literally priceless. And to be frank, this is one of the more brutal facts of capitalism" [1].

## The Wealth Machine

Tan introduces the concept of a "wealth machine" -- a business that takes money in, turns the crank, and spits out more money than it started with [1]. The key property: the output can be reinvested to create more wealth.

He contrasts four types:
- **Wealth machine** (money + wealth): a business with virtuous cycles. "You can take the money that comes out and feed it back in" [1].
- **Get-rich-quick scheme** (money, no wealth): a one-time transaction with no repeatable mechanism. "Lambos can be bought with money, but they can't actually be converted into even more money" [1].
- **LARPing startup** (no money, no wealth): hiring people, spending money, and wasting it all. "A special form of this is when startups decide to sell twenty-dollar bills for ten dollars or even give them away for free" [1].
- **Truly great startup** (both): generates both more money and more of the capacity to generate money. Three virtuous cycles work together: talent (better teams attract better people), customers (happy users tell others), and capital (growth enables more fundraising). "That's what [[Product-Market Fit]] is, and that's the key to this whole startup thing" [1].

## Leverage: Products That Work While You Sleep

Tan frames his personal career arc through the lens of leverage. At age 14, he got his first job cold-calling the Yellow Pages. He was happy getting $7, then $10, then $20 per hour to write code. "But what I wish I knew earlier was that I have a really finite number of hours in the day and in my life. You can get to sixty or seventy hours per week of work, but beyond that you run out. But if you make a product that can make money for you while you sleep, that's leverage" [1].

He uses the Apple founding story to illustrate how leverage emerges naturally: Jobs and Wozniak made computers for themselves, then their friends, then the circle got bigger. "We didn't think about starting a company. We were just doing it for ourselves" [1]. Naval Ravikant frames it as escaping finite games: "Wealth is about positive sum games" and "getting out of the sort of finite games like selling your time" [1].

## References

1. [Stop Chasing Money -- Chase Wealth](https://ycombinator.com/library/Je-garry-s-channel-stop-chasing-money-chase-wealth) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

title: AI-Native Software
body:
# AI-Native Software

Most AI apps today are "horseless carriages" -- they pack powerful AI models into outdated interfaces designed for humans to do work in, rather than redesigning from the ground up to offload work [1]. [[Pete Koomen]], YC partner and founder of Optimizely, argues that the fundamental design question should shift from "How do I insert AI into my tool?" to "How would I design this tool from scratch to offload as much repetitive work from the user as possible?" [1].

## The Horseless Carriage Problem

The term references early automobile designs that looked like carriages with the horse replaced by an engine. The carriage form factor created problems at higher speeds -- poor suspension, high center of gravity, bad turns. "Inventing the motor was only a small part of what was needed to produce a vehicle that could take advantage of it. It only became useful once you redesigned the entire vehicle" [1].

The same pattern has repeated with every major technology transition [1]:
- **Internet**: The first search engines were digitized yellow pages (literal directories)
- **Mobile**: The first mobile apps were websites wrapped in a native app wrapper, ignoring GPS and multi-touch

Koomen argues we are in the horseless carriage stage of AI: developers are slotting AI into applications designed for humans to do work in, rather than redesigning around what AI can actually do [1].

## The Gmail Problem: A Case Study

Koomen uses Gmail's AI draft-writing feature as a detailed case study of the failure pattern [1].

**The prompt**: "Let my boss Gary know that my daughter woke up with the flu this morning and I won't be able to come into the office today."

**The output**: "Dear Gary, I'm writing to inform you that my daughter woke up with the flu this morning. As a result, I won't be able to come into the office today. Thank you for understanding. Best regards, Pete."

Two problems: (1) it does not sound like Pete -- it sounds like no human, and (2) the prompt is roughly as long as the output, defeating the purpose [1].

### The Root Cause: Hidden, Generic System Prompts

Every AI agent combines the user's prompt with a system prompt -- text that tells the AI who it is and what its job is. Gmail's system prompt is hidden from the user, cannot be edited, and is generic: "Use a formal businessy tone and correct punctuation so that it's obvious the user is really smart and serious" [1].

The system prompt is generic because developers treat it as they have treated code for decades: one-size-fits-all, hidden behind an interface, owned by the developer. "For as long as we've had a software industry, there's been a division of labor between me, the user, and you, the developer. The user does not see the code" [1].

### The Fix: Editable System Prompts

If Gmail allowed users to edit the system prompt, Koomen could write: "You're Pete. You're a 43-year-old husband, father, YC partner. You're busy, and so is everyone you correspond with, so you do your best to keep emails as short as possible" [1].

Using this custom system prompt with the same user prompt, the draft becomes: "Hi Gary, my daughter's sick with the flu, so I can't come in today. Thanks." That sounds like Pete [1].

The insight: "By editing this system prompt, I'm able to explain to the AI model how I write emails in general so that I don't have to do it every single time" [1].

## From Email Writer to Email Reader

The real opportunity is not making AI write emails -- it is making AI handle the inbox. Koomen built a demo of an email reading agent with a user-editable system prompt that assigns labels, archives messages, sets priorities, and drafts replies [1]:

- If the email is from my wife: draft a reply, label it personal
- If it is from my boss: draft a reply, priority one
- Anyone else at YC: YC label, priority two
- From a founder who needs help: founders label
- Tech-related: label it tech
- Someone trying to sell me something: archive it

This is the code. The user wrote it in plain English. "The LLM technology is actually good enough to let non-programmers program these apps" [1].

[[Tom Blomfield]] frames the potential: "Steve Jobs describes software as a bicycle for the mind. This feels like a rocket ship for the mind" [1].

## The Developer's New Job: Build Tools, Not Prompts

If users can program agents through natural language, what should developers build? Koomen's answer: tools [1].

Tools are the capabilities that agents need to take action -- labeling an email, archiving it, writing a draft, making a calendar entry, sending a Slack message, updating a Jira ticket. "The thing that these agents need in order to be able to do anything useful, we call tools" [1].

With the right tools, an email agent could handle bill payments, introductions, handoffs, BCC management -- the transactional work that fills most inboxes but does not require original thinking [1].

MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are emerging as the standard way to provide tool access to agents. A YC batch company called Den is building "Cursor for knowledge work" -- chaining together MCP servers so agents can operate across Slack, Google Docs, email, and GitHub from a single interface [1].

## Why Coding Agents Are So Far Ahead

Koomen and Blomfield identify two reasons Cursor, Windsurf, and Claude Code feel so much more powerful than AI agents in other domains [1]:

1. **LLMs are good at processing text and producing code.** If you can describe what you want in English, the model can produce functional code. This is a domain where the core capability aligns perfectly with the use case.

2. **Developer tools give full access to the model.** Developer tool teams did not spend effort preventing users from doing embarrassing things. "They allowed me full access to have this agent do whatever I wanted." Other domains still use a "kid glove mentality" -- limiting the model's power to prevent bad outputs [1].

## Can Non-Technical Users Write System Prompts?

Koomen acknowledges that today most people cannot write system prompts. But he argues this will change quickly, analogizing to computer literacy: "When I was growing up, computers were still seen as a power tool that only nerds really knew how to use. Today that's just no longer the case" [1].

Writing a prompt is more accessible than operating a file system. "You don't need to understand much except to be able to explain yourself in English or whatever language you speak" [1].

For the near term, AI can help. Blomfield describes the pattern: the product reads 20 years of email history, generates a draft system prompt customized to the user, and iterates based on feedback -- exactly how a new human assistant would learn [1].

Koomen built a demo where users can edit system prompts and immediately rerun them, creating a feedback loop: "As long as you can watch it do the job and give it feedback by adjusting this prompt, it's pretty intuitive" [1].

## The Chatbot Paradigm Is Wrong

Blomfield expresses strong dissatisfaction with the chatbot paradigm: "I kind of hate the chatbot paradigm. It was the easiest way to bring the most basic LLM experience to the whole world. But now every product is just embedding this chat agent in it. That's not the way this stuff should be used in almost every case" [1].

LLMs are capable of automating work on behalf of users -- accomplishing things in the world, not just answering questions. The chatbot interface obscures this capability [1].

## Implications for Founders

Koomen's advice: "This is one of the most exciting times to be a founder because almost every tool that we've been using for decades can be rethought from the ground up with AI. The AI-native version of a lot of tools will look different from the versions we're used to" [1].

The shift: instead of asking "How do I insert AI into my tool?" ask "How would I design this tool from scratch to offload as much repetitive work from the user as possible so they can focus on what's important?" [1].

## References

1. [AI Apps Are Broken -- Here's How To Fix Them](https://ycombinator.com/library/MR-ai-apps-are-broken-here-s-how-to-fix-them) -- [[Pete Koomen]], [[Tom Blomfield]], [[David Lieb]] (2025)

title: Getting First Users
body:
# Getting First Users

Getting a new product off the ground requires finding people willing to use a product from a startup they have never heard of. Ankit Gupta, YC General Partner, frames this as a search problem rather than a persuasion problem: "You're looking for the Gustafs and Ankits of the world, the ones who try new things or have a burning need that you can solve" [1].

Most people are not early adopters. For most people, the number of products they used as one of the first 10 users is zero. Almost no one wants to be a startup's first paying customer. Yet every great product manages to find people willing to take that leap [1].

## Finding Early Adopters Is a Search Problem

Two types of people become first users [1]:

1. **Habitual early adopters.** Gupta describes his colleague Gustaf (formerly at Airbnb) who enjoyed trying products from startups and bringing them into the company [1].

2. **People with a burning need.** When Gupta's team needed to ship their first inference API quickly without dealing with billing or public endpoints, he found and paid a startup within three days whose product solved the problem. "Their size or reputation didn't matter. Our problem did, so we took a chance on them, and they delivered" [1].

## Counterintuitive Principles

Understanding that user acquisition is a search problem leads to several non-obvious strategies [1]:

### Charge Real Money Early

Early adopters and people with burning problems are rarely price sensitive. The goal is not revenue -- it is feedback. "Paying customers give sharper feedback than free users ever will. You're more likely to get feedback from an angry customer paying a lot of money than a nobody who isn't willing to pay" [1].

### Use Targeted Personal Outreach

The ways you find early adopters do not look like how you find normal people. "A billboard is way less likely to reach them versus something like a targeted cold email or a knock on their door" [1]. See [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]] for more on manual, unscalable acquisition tactics.

### Launch Early

In the early days, you do not know much about who your early users are. Launching early creates a wide surface area for them to find you [1]. This is one of YC's longest-standing principles.

### Study Your Early Users Like an Anthropologist

"You should be like an anthropologist that's discovered a hidden civilization. How do they make decisions? Why would they make the strange choice to trust you?" [1]. Understanding how early users think and what they want is essential input for product evolution.

### Experiment Fast and Don't Fear Churn

Run constant experiments: pricing, landing pages, onboarding, features. Talk to early users and try to make them love the product, but do not stress if you lose one. "If you annoy someone, you can usually fix it because the relationship is personal. And if they churn, that's fine, too. There are plenty of others who haven't even heard of you yet" [1].

Startups have an advantage over big companies here: "When you run a bad experiment, no one writes about it. You're fighting irrelevance, not headlines" [1].

## B2B vs. Consumer in the Early Stage

The dynamics of finding first users shape who many founders choose to build for in the beginning. The average person's personal software spend is small (Gupta estimates his at about $150/month total), while his corporate card carries multiple tools that each cost more than that [1].

In the AI era, this gap matters more. Consumer apps can struggle because ads often do not cover AI costs and subscriptions must squeeze into small personal budgets. This is why many AI founders start by selling to prosumers, businesses, or targeting users with high advertising value (like doctors) [1].

## The Minimum Evolvable Product

Gupta updates the classic MVP concept. When starting out, you do not just need a minimum viable product. You need a **minimum evolvable product** -- something that can adapt to the needs of critical early customers [1].

The earliest version of the product only needs to do one thing: survive contact with a tiny group of people who might actually try it. "You're not building the final form. You're building something that can evolve" [1].

## Early Users Steer Product Evolution

Early users do not just give feedback. They steer how the product evolves over time [1]. Gupta uses the metaphor of a phylogenetic tree: every product on the market has run an evolutionary process, morphing from an amoeba (basic functions, exposed to external pressures) to a complex organism (millions of users, refined sales pitch, clear value). The founders run "an evolutionary search through the tree of potential future directions" [1].

### Tesla as Case Study

The Tesla Roadster illustrates how early users shape product evolution. Tesla's first product was an impractical $150,000 car that did not go far, did not fit much, could not publicly charge anywhere, and looked strange. The conventional narrative is that Tesla needed a high-margin product to fund capex for the Model S and eventually the Model 3 [1].

Gupta offers a second interpretation: Tesla was searching for early adopters -- "the people crazy enough to buy an impractical $150,000 car" [1]. What those early adopters cared about shaped the entire product line.

The Tesla Model Y, a mass-market vehicle, has a faster 0-to-60 time than a Lamborghini and better tech than a BMW, but worse suspension and comfort than a Toyota. Why? Because early adopters cared more about tech and acceleration than comfort [1]. "If early adopters were willing to pay $150,000 for a slow, plush vehicle, I bet Tesla's cars would look very different today" [1].

Product evolution is path-dependent on what early adopters want. This is why the choice of initial users is so consequential -- it determines the trajectory of the product itself [1].

## It Doesn't Have to Be Perfect

The minimum evolvable product framework is freeing: "It's freeing to know that the product will change a lot, so it doesn't have to be perfect from the start. Ultimately, what it becomes will depend on where you begin and who you begin it with" [1].

## References

1. [How To Get Your First Users](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/ND-how-to-get-your-first-users) -- Ankit Gupta (n.d.)

title: Dev Shops
body:
# Dev Shops

[[Michael Seibel]] warns that using a development shop to build a startup's initial product is one of the most common traps for non-technical founders [1]. While some companies have succeeded after using dev shops, Seibel states that this is "by far the exception, not the rule" [1].

## The Trap Pattern

The dev shop trap follows a predictable sequence [1]:

1. **A non-technical founder** believes they have a problem worth solving and understand their customer, but cannot build the solution.
2. **They are advised** to use a dev shop instead of recruiting a [[Technical Co-Founders|technical co-founder]]. The reasoning: build the first version, get traction, then hire a CTO.
3. **Development takes longer than expected.** The founder has little experience estimating software timelines, and the dev shop is working on other projects. The dev shop's team members are "not as motivated as they would be if they were a founder with a lot of equity in the product."
4. **Costs exceed projections.** The founder budgets $15,000-$30,000 and ends up spending far more. This is "extremely frustrating, especially if they've raised a little bit of angel money and they're pouring all of it into the building of the initial product."
5. **Fundraising becomes harder.** Many good tech investors "do not want to invest in companies that are outsourcing development unless those companies are absolutely taking off and just growing ridiculously quickly, which is very rare."
6. **The code is thrown away.** If the founder eventually hires an engineer, the new hire often disagrees with the dev shop's architecture and rebuilds from scratch.

## Why It Feels Like Progress

The trap's power lies in the fact that it initially feels like progress. "You often go from nothing being built before you have the dev shop to things happening once you have the dev shop. So you feel like you're making all this progress" [1].

The reality becomes clear after the first version ships. The MVP almost never solves the problem on the first attempt. The founder needs to iterate, which means spending more money and more time with the dev shop. "Usually this is when your funds run low and you realize, 'Oh man, I can't make it'" [1].

## What Investors Actually Want

Seibel argues that founders who use dev shops misunderstand what early-stage investors look for. "The vast majority of good early stage investors care more about whether you can build something than what you've built" [1]. At the early stage, there is very little proof the company will succeed. The investor's real question is: "Can you iterate through multiple versions of your product until you can find a version that solves the problem that you want to solve?" [1].

Doing that with a dev shop takes longer and costs far more than doing it with a technical co-founder. True early-stage investors want iteration speed, not traction. Series A investors want traction [1].

## The Technical Co-Founder Alternative

Seibel's recommendation is clear: recruit a [[Technical Co-Founders|technical co-founder]] instead. "Typically what ends up happening is you have to spend far less than you'd have to spend for that dev shop, you have a product that's released far faster, and you're much better set up to iterate on the solution as you get feedback from your customers" [1].

The common objection is that finding a technical co-founder is too hard. Seibel's response: "So many companies fall into this trap that they should really reconsider whether or not spending that extra time recruiting a technical co-founder is going to be far more worth it than going with the dev shop" [1].

## Scope

Seibel limits this advice to venture-scale tech startups -- "startup that's going to generate a lot of value for a lot of people and, you know, be a multi-billion dollar company and that wants to raise money from the venture capital world." There are "lots of other situations where working with a dev shop is totally fine" [1].

## References

1. [Should I Use a Dev Shop?](https://ycombinator.com/library/7v-should-i-use-a-dev-shop) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (2019)

title: Hiring at Early Stage
body:
# Hiring at Early Stage

One of the most common things founders tell YC they need to do during a batch is hire. One of the most common pieces of advice YC gives back is: do not hire. [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], and [[Brad Flora]] describe this tension as a recurring source of friction, because the advice runs counter to the visible signal that successful companies have thousands of employees [1].

The distinction that resolves the tension is [[Product-Market Fit]]. Almost all hiring advice written online is for post-PMF companies scaling a product users already love. Most startups applying to YC are pre-PMF. "If you apply that advice to your pre-product market company, you might accelerate your death" [1].

## The Lies Founders Tell Themselves

### "If we had more people, we could get more things done"

Taggar hears this every week. Founders want more features, an Android app and an iOS app, specialists for each platform [1]. Flora pushes back: "I've even had people tell me that we need to hire more people so that we can reach profitability faster. And do those people bring -- are they somehow paying the company instead of collecting salaries?" [1].

### "Having employees is a marker of progress"

Founders feel investors and customers will be more impressed with larger teams. Employee count becomes a KPI. "It feels good when you tell your friends. 'Oh, how's the company doing?' 'Oh yeah, we're at like 100 employees now.' Things must be going great" [1]. Seibel notes that second-time founders react differently: "After managing people, I'm like, 'Oh God, if I could just do it with fewer people'" [1].

### "That senior hire will fix our problem"

A VP of Sales from a recognized company looks like the answer to a sales problem. But Flora warns: "The tenured VP of Sales might be amazing at managing a team of 100 salespeople. But they are probably not as good as you are at figuring out how to make the first sales call, how to get the first ten customers" [1]. At the pre-PMF stage, founders have a unique skill set that cannot be hired for: knowing the problem, knowing the product, and having the capacity to iterate both simultaneously [1].

Taggar describes what this looks like in office hours: "Someone shows me a plan to hire some people and we have to go down the list and I have to say over and over again, 'That's your job. No, no, that's your job too. That's your job right now'" [1].

### "I need an exec team"

The "cargo cult" pattern. Founders look at the about page of Airbnb, see Chief Operating Officer and other titles, and conclude they need to fill those squares [1]. Flora adds that often the relationship already exists -- a former coworker who wants a fancy title. "It doesn't mean that it's a good idea to bring them on right now before you have product-market fit" [1].

### "We know we'll need this person eventually, so hire now"

Seibel addresses this with the fire analogy. Every startup has one or two six-alarm fires and fifty smoldering ones. Hiring for a smoldering fire feels proactive, but only one or two of those fifty will become real problems. The other forty-nine will not. "It's still better to fight the fire after it's like a massive fire" than to pre-hire for all fifty potential fires [1].

## The Airbnb and Stripe Precedents

Seibel recalls that Airbnb did not hire its first non-founder employee for eighteen months after launch and six months after raising money from YC. "Two designers, one engineer. And Nate was like drowning. And they still took six months to find someone who was great and that helped establish the culture" [1].

Stripe was similar: roughly three people for the first year to eighteen months, then rapid scaling when product-market fit was clear [1].

Both companies now have thousands of employees. But the hiring was "pretty back-loaded" [1].

## When the Specialist Is Worth It

Seibel acknowledges a legitimate case from Justin.tv/Twitch. They hired John, a YouTube veteran who specialized in video infrastructure scaling. John taught them about free peering and saved enormous bandwidth costs. But critically, "John wasn't the first person that worked on video." Co-founder Kyle built the video system first. John came on only when the specific problem of spending too much on bandwidth was identified. "Our first problem wasn't spending too much money. Our first problem is nobody cared to watch video on our product" [1].

## The Real Costs of Over-Hiring

Three consequences that founders underestimate [1]:

1. **Higher burn, lower runway.** "Whenever we see a company post-YC that's running low on money, it's almost always employee burn. It's always salary burn" [1].
2. **Reduced agility.** "The bigger the ship is, the more people you've got to now coordinate to get them rowing in the same direction and turn the ship. And if it takes you like three months to turn the ship, you might just be dead" [1].
3. **Burnout from people problems.** Managing a growing team drains energy. Seibel estimates a five to ten percent chance that any reasonable hire turns out to be toxic. With 25 employees, "at any given time, you probably have toxic people in your inbox every day" [1].

The cultural insight from successful founders: "Every hire should increase the average IQ of the company. And whenever you start thinking about new people that way, you're like, 'Huh, maybe we could do it ourselves'" [1]. Each new hire also shapes [[Company Culture]] -- Airbnb's deliberate slowness in hiring its first employee was as much about establishing cultural DNA as about finding the right skills.

## Protecting Technical Co-Founders
