Tan identifies climate change regulation as the biggest enduring regulatory "why now" today [1]. The Inflation Reduction Act is "the first of its kind, the largest climate package the United States has ever done" and is "going to lead to lots of different incentives around decarbonizing our entire economy" [1].

## Real Trends vs. Fake Trends

Altman provides a complementary framework for evaluating timing [2]. A real trend is one where early adopters use a new technology "obsessively and tell their friends how much they love it." A fake trend is one where people may buy the product but do not use it -- "or at least not enough" [2].

The iPhone was a real trend: early owners "used it for hours every day, it became central to their lives." VR (as of 2018) was a fake trend: "most people that I know that own a VR headset use it never or very rarely" [2].

## Exponentially Growing Markets

Altman argues that one of the biggest mistakes investors make is evaluating a startup's market by its current size rather than its growth rate [2]. "11 years ago, the market for iPhone apps: absolute $0. It's not huge. And I think if you only think about the TAM today, you'll make a big mistake. What you really wanna do is identify a market that's gonna grow every year and be able to ride that up elevator" [2].

## Platform Shifts and Startups

Both Tan and Altman identify platform shifts as the primary mechanism through which startups beat big companies. Altman explains that most large companies operate on annual planning cycles. When a huge platform shift occurs, "the battleship just turns too slowly, whereas a startup can say, 'Wow, woke up this morning. The world is fundamentally different than it was six months ago. We're gonna go all in on this new direction'" [2]. See [[Competing with Big Companies]] for more on this dynamic.

## References

1. [Why Now? The Key to Million Dollar Startup Ideas](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jt-garry-s-channel-why-now-the-key-to-million-dollar-startup-ideas) -- [[Garry Tan]] (2022)
2. [How to Succeed with a Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/89-how-to-succeed-with-a-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (August 2018)

title: Survivorship Bias in Startup Advice
aliases: do as I say not as I did
body:
# Survivorship Bias in Startup Advice

Prominent technology leaders frequently give advice that contradicts the path they actually followed to become successful. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] call this the "do as I say, not as I did" problem and argue that founders who take famous people's advice at face value -- without researching their actual histories -- are making a dangerous mistake [1].

## The Pattern

Caldwell introduces the pattern: a successful person gives advice that sounds reasonable but does not reflect what they did to become successful. The audience, often in their twenties, only knows these people as successes. They have no reason to question the advice. Seibel, who has known many of these people personally, describes the disconnect: "We saw what they did. We know their story. And we're just like, 'That's weird. You didn't do that'" [1].

## Three Examples

### Sam Altman

Seibel describes Altman recently suggesting that the YC-style approach of starting small may not be optimal -- that founders should think bigger, raise more money, hire teams, and build labs [1]. Caldwell traces Altman's actual path: his first startup was Loopt, a mobile social product to see where your friends were in the city. He went to Stanford, did YC, raised a small amount of money, had a small team, and iterated. Caldwell describes it as "a pretty garden variety path" with a "pretty normal outcome" [1].

Caldwell argues Altman could not have started OpenAI as his first company. He needed the network, credibility, and experience that Loopt and YC provided. "If you actually wanted to emulate Sam, you would not immediately go try to start a lab and raise a hundred million dollars. Because that's not at all what he did" [1].

Seibel adds that the people most influenced by Altman's "raise a hundred million" advice are precisely the people who will not be able to do it. "If you're one of the people out there that can't do a startup because they're like, 'If I can't raise a hundred million dollars, why even bother?' -- that's not real" [1].

### Elon Musk

Seibel describes Musk advocating for ambitious hardware projects -- atoms over bits, going to space, fixing the world. Seibel recounts a direct conversation with Musk about his actual path. Musk said he wanted to go to space, knew it required a lot of money, and came to Silicon Valley during the dot-com boom specifically to make money fast through conventional software startups (Zip2, then X.com/PayPal). Caldwell notes: "His ideas were pretty conventional. It wasn't hard tech. It wasn't deep tech" [1].

Caldwell explains that Musk leveraged his wealth and network from those exits to start SpaceX, and repeatedly bailed out Tesla with personal funds. "If he were not an extremely wealthy person, there's no way Tesla could have existed" [1].

Caldwell concludes: "Most mere mortals out in the world need to do step one and can't skip to step two, even though step two sounds cooler. Mere mortals, including Elon -- literally Elon himself -- was a mere mortal" [1].

### Peter Thiel

Seibel describes Thiel as known for critiquing college. But he attended Stanford for both undergraduate and law school, worked at a famous law firm and a famous bank. "He went to college. And not only did he go to college -- he went to college" [1].

Seibel acknowledges Thiel's critique is valid in parts. But the A/B test does not exist. "My sense is there was some aspect of those experiences that had a profound impact on his later success" [1].

## Why Leaders Give This Advice

Caldwell assumes positive intent. These are successful people trying to help. Three mechanisms explain the disconnect [1]:

1. **Blindness to backstory.** Caldwell observes that they are so used to their own history that they "almost become blind to it, just like we can." What they focus on is what they would do now, given what they know now.

2. **Advice they wish they could tell their younger selves.** Seibel suspects much of this advice is what these people wish they had done differently. Altman might have raised more money sooner. Thiel might have skipped the law firm. But those were formative experiences that may have been necessary.

3. **Context collapse.** Seibel explains that videos and blog posts cannot be personalized. YC office hours are personalized -- "we read their application, we interviewed them, we accepted them." Public advice is aimed at the void. The nuance that would make it useful for a specific person is lost.

## The Billion-Dollar Origin Stories

A complementary YC discussion reveals that the biggest companies all had humble, indistinguishable beginnings [2]. Multiple group partners share stories of interviewing companies that became massive.

**Solugen** (industrial chemicals, now a major company) showed up to their YC interview with a beaker of hydrogen peroxide -- most of the hydrogen peroxide they had ever produced. During the batch, they sold bottles from their garage. [[Jared Friedman]], who interviewed them, was convinced not by the technology but because "these guys were doers. They weren't waiting for anyone's permission to go do the thing. They had made the hydrogen peroxide and brought it in, and they were trying to find somebody to sell it to. They clearly had a bias for action" [2].

**Amplitude** (now a public analytics company) applied with a voice-to-text Android app. Paul Buchheit "hammered" the founder Spencer, arguing Google would crush them. Spencer was "just so intense in his comebacks and just determined to work on this idea." They pivoted a year and a half after YC but that intensity was the signal [2].

[[Michael Seibel]] identifies a key trait shared by these founders: obstinance. "There's almost like a sort of obstinance about the best founders where they're just, whatever they're working on, they don't do it at like 60 or 70 or even 80 percent. They're always just like 100 percent committed" [2].

**Segment** ($3.2 billion acquisition by Twilio) applied with a tool for professors to poll students during lectures. Seibel recounts: "No one wanted this. It had zero traction." The partners pushed them to work on something technically hard, but the founders were obstinate -- "not obstinate without direction. They were like, 'What we are really passionate about is fixing education, and we think this is a really good place to start.'" Eventually they found the Segment idea, but it took time [2].

**Brex** started as a VR startup. Seibel also cites this as an example of a founder entering YC with an objectively bad idea but possessing the intensity to find the right one [2].

Diana Hu and Seibel discuss the founder archetype that succeeds at these pivots. Hu argues that the best founders are "very not well-rounded. They're very quirky people for a good reason" -- by definition, building a successful large company requires being "out of multiple standard deviations out of the norm." Seibel adds that the strategy that got founders into elite schools and FAANG jobs -- hedging, being well-rounded -- "is not going to be the strategy that actually gets them to be successful as a startup founder" [2].

**[[Brad Flora]]** adds that the best founders at interview are clear, succinct, and honest about what they do not know. He uses the Jeeves interview as an example: the application had two sentences for progress: "We've completed a deal with our first bank partner and are ready to start onboarding initial customers." Flora was impressed by the confidence to say so little: "All of those things are in your control. You can write succinctly if you choose to." During the interview itself, the Jeeves founders were "pretty upfront about things that they did know and didn't know. And they didn't try to razzle-dazzle us with fake traction" [2].

Aaron Epstein warns founders against comparing themselves to successful companies in their current state. "The stories that you read in the press and in the media, has this PR gloss over it where they're only showing the positives." He advises founders going through tough times to "imagine the story that they want to tell in ten years, and how boring it would be if everything was just up and to the right and successful the whole time" [2].

## How YC Tries to Avoid the Trap

Three practices [1]:

1. **Tell their own full stories.** Caldwell describes how at the beginning of each batch, partners share their complete backstories -- everything they screwed up, every mistake. "It helps people get to know us, to contextualize us as humans. And it helps to know how many mistakes you can make and still do something interesting."

2. **Give advice they would give their own children.** Caldwell states: "If I tell this to my kids, probably it's good for you too." Seibel attended Yale and was not impressed, but recognizes it is where he met his co-founders: "Without them, I wouldn't be here right now. So if that's the only thing Yale did for me, it changed my life."

3. **Personalize advice in office hours.** Seibel explains that in private, YC partners sometimes give advice that contradicts the public YC canon because the founder's specific situation warrants it. A multi-time founder known to be a strong fundraiser might be advised to raise more money. "That doesn't apply to most people" [1].

## References

1. [Dalton & Michael: Why You Shouldn't Copy Your Tech Idols](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jw-dalton-michael-why-you-shouldn-t-copy-your-tech-idols) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
2. [How Do Billion Dollar Startups Start?](https://ycombinator.com/library/Li-how-do-billion-dollar-startups-start) -- [[Jared Friedman]], Diana Hu, [[Michael Seibel]], [[Brad Flora]], Aaron Epstein (2024)

title: Influencing Decision Makers
body:
# Influencing Decision Makers

Many of the most important decisions in a founder's life -- fundraising decisions, promotions, budget approvals, partnerships -- are made in rooms where the founder is not present. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] discuss what founders can do to influence those decisions beyond making strong factual arguments [1].

## Table Stakes: A Coherent Argument

A strong logical case is the starting point, not the differentiator. "Let's just assume you've made an amazing factual argument. That's table stakes" [1]. The question is what else influences the decision when you are not in the room.

## Attitude and Demeanor

Long-term demeanor matters more than any single interaction. Seibel notes that people develop personal brands, sometimes based on a single incident: "It's like how you get a nickname. You do one thing once when you're 18, and for the rest of your life that's your nickname" [1].

Negativity is particularly damaging. The partners distinguish between being realistic and being "always negative about everything." Even when negative people are making excellent factual points, they can tip over the line. The question decision makers ask is: "Do we want other people on the team to act like this person?" [1].

## The "More Time" Test

When decision makers are in the room, they are implicitly deciding whether they want to spend more time with the person. If someone is doing a Series A, the investor is signing up for board membership and years of interaction. If someone is getting promoted, it may mean more meetings and closer collaboration [1].

This does not mean founders must be nice. But decision makers think about what they are signing up for [1].

## Being Irreplaceable

Seibel introduces the concept of irreplaceability as the strongest form of influence. The thought experiment: "If this person disappeared tomorrow, how hosed are you?" [1].

He applies this test in hiring: 90 days after a new hire starts, put a calendar event asking "How would you feel if that person said today is my last day?" Anything other than distraught means the person did not make themselves irreplaceable [1].

"If you really want to influence the room where it happens, you can't be a B player. You can't be like, 'Well, they get some stuff done.' Irreplaceable is really where it happens" [1].

The same test applies to companies and [[Product-Market Fit]]: "If your company went away tomorrow, how sad would people be and how hosed would their business be?" If AWS disappeared, websites go down. That is irreplaceability at the product level [1].

## Heart vs. Mercenary

Caldwell identifies another factor: whether someone gives more to an organization than they take, or the reverse. Decision makers try to assess motivation: "Is this a mercenary? If I give them something, are they going to try to figure out how to take more?" versus someone who cares about customers, direct reports, or the product [1].

Mercenaries are easy to detect: "Man, mercenaries are easy to sniff out" [1].

## The Lobbying Trap

Both partners warn strongly against aggressive lobbying or political maneuvering. The margin for error is razor-thin: "It's a hair trigger -- if you go over the line of doing things, oh man, you lost. You lost the decision makers completely. In a very memorable way. Often it's disqualifying" [1].

Caldwell notes that weird lobbying attempts for YC admission never work: "There's never a case where we're like, 'Wow, this is a really good email, and I'm definitely going to take this seriously'" [1].

The risk is environment-dependent. In some organizations, politics may be the only way to get seen. But in every environment, going over the line is "worse than nothing. Way worse than nothing. If you just don't do it, you're ahead" [1].

## The Desperation Signal

When founders become obsessed with getting one particular investor to say yes, they tend to get rejected. Caldwell describes a Zen-like pattern: "When you're overly attached to some particular thing happening in your life, it tends to not happen" [1].

Seibel connects this to why YC changed its standard deal. When YC gave companies $125,000, founders approached Demo Day feeling desperate, and investors could sense it. With $500,000 and eighteen months of runway, the same founders had a "take it or leave it" attitude. "Investors rightly pattern match that attitude with being effective" [1].

People who seem fine regardless of the outcome -- "Hey, I'll figure it out whichever way this goes" -- tend to be rewarded. Frantic, anxious people tend to struggle [1].

## How You Handle Rejection Matters

Seibel closes with a point about the repeated-game nature of influence: how you respond to a decision not going your way is itself a factor in future decisions. "Don't go all in necessarily in situations that don't require it" [1]. The reaction to a loss becomes part of the reputation that follows you into the next room [1].

## References

1. [How to Influence Decision Makers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LQ-how-to-influence-decision-makers) — [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)

title: Series A Term Sheets
body:
# Series A Term Sheets

Jason Kwon and [[Aaron Harris]] of YC describe a common problem: founders seeing their first term sheet do not know what "good" looks like [1]. This puts them at a disadvantage because VCs see term sheets constantly. For guidance on the overall [[Series A Fundraising]] process -- building the pitch, preparing the data room, and closing -- see the companion article. YC, having seen hundreds of Series A term sheets, works with founders to identify where terms diverge from standard, what can be done about divergences, and when negotiation makes sense.

YC drafted a standard, clean Series A term sheet representing what they see most often from good Silicon Valley VCs. The term sheet fits on a single page [1]. This brevity was not always the norm but became common over the last decade as some investors shortened their legalese as a signal: "We aren't going to get bogged down in the minutiae. We're going to make this easy, friendly, standard and fast" [1].

A [downloadable Word version](https://www.ycombinator.com/series_a_term_sheet/) of the standard term sheet is available from YC.

## Pricing

The lead investor in a Series A round generally wants 20% of the company, but pricing can flex depending on leverage held by each side. YC considers price important but too specific to each raise to standardize. Their focus is on control and structure terms that are less familiar to founders and more prone to cause confusion [1].

## Control Terms: What the Term Sheet Tells You

The term sheet is a signal about the investor. A contract allocates risks between parties, so the terms an investor insists on reveal their perceived risks [1].

### Board Composition

The standard term sheet is founder-friendly because founders retain board control at 2-1. The most common way founders lose control at Series A is through a 2-2-1 board structure: two founders, two investors, and one independent board member. Losing board control is significant because it means founders can be fired from their own company [1].

Another control risk is a separate provision requiring the investor director's approval for operational decisions such as setting annual budgets, hiring/firing executives, pivoting the business, or adding new lines of business. When investors structure boards to take power away from founders, the outward justification is governance or accountability. Kwon and Harris state plainly: "when an investor says that they're committed to partnering with you for the long-term -- or that they're betting everything on you -- but then tells you something else with the terms that they insist on, believe the terms" [1].

### Standard Investor Vetoes

The standard voting rights section includes vetoes on financing (clauses covering issuance of new equity) and sale of the company. Kwon and Harris note these are standard across most term sheets and use similar technical jargon, but their concrete implications are not facially obvious. Founders should have a lawyer walk through the scenarios [1].

Founders should ensure their lawyer and the investor's lawyer both specialize in startups and venture deals, where "standard" terms have accepted practice [1].

## Dirty Economic Terms to Watch For

The standard term sheet is instructive for what it does not contain. Non-standard or "dirty" economic terms include [1]:

- **Liquidation preference greater than 1x**: The investor gets back more than its invested capital first.
- **Participating preferred**: The investor double-dips by getting money back plus pro rata portion of exit proceeds, rather than choosing between the two.
- **Cumulative dividends**: The investor compounds its liquidation preference every year by X%, increasing the economic hurdle before founders and employees see value.
- **Warrant coverage**: The investor gets extra fully-diluted ownership without paying for it at the agreed valuation.

These are all ways of adding structure to reduce typical venture risk. The investor is "essentially saying, 'I'm sort of afraid of losing my money.'" It can foreshadow behavior when things go badly, such as pushing founders to sell when they do not want to, or pulling back on risk when risk is needed [1].

Good investors address economic risks by negotiating on valuation and are otherwise happy to give standard terms because "the real money in venture is not made with structure, but by building long-term value" [1].

## Series A Terms as Foundation

The Series A documents set a foundation and precedent for future rounds. Good foundations make the next term sheet and financing round fast and simple, as future investors step into the same straightforward terms. Bad foundations complicate everything: future investors ask for the same heavy terms, existing investors refuse to drop terms that new investors want removed. "Unwinding bad terms is difficult, and oftentimes impossible" [1].

## Don't Over-Optimize

The goal is a clean deal, not a perfect deal. "No one ever built an enduring company just by winning their Series A negotiation." Even if terms are not ideal, execution can outrun suboptimal terms or establish leverage to renegotiate later. The ultimate goal is closing fast and getting back to work [1].

## References

1. [A Standard and Clean Series A Term Sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet) — Jason Kwon, [[Aaron Harris]] (September 2018)

title: Co-Founders
body:
# Co-Founders

The co-founder relationship is one of the most consequential decisions in a startup's life. Of YC's [top companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/topcompanies), only 7.9% were founded by solo founders [3]. [[Paul Graham]] notes that exploitation of co-founders is often the first sign of a founder who will fail -- the co-founder relationship is the foundation of the company [8]. Failed co-founder relationships are the number one reason startups fail at YC [7].

## Why Co-Founders Matter

[[Harj Taggar]] provides three specific reasons [7]:

1. **Workload and quality.** Building a startup means competing against companies with hundreds of employees. A good co-founder with complementary skills improves the quality of work. A smart co-founder challenges ideas, talks you out of bad ones, and helps find good ones.
2. **Emotional support.** "Startups are a real emotional roller coaster. You have moments where you think you're going to take over the world, and moments where you're convinced you're about to die." A co-founder who is equally invested provides support that no employee or advisor can. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] agree: "You need someone to offer emotional support because this thing is going to be hard" [6].
3. **Pattern matching.** The most successful startups had co-founding teams: Facebook (Zuckerberg + Moskovitz), Apple (Jobs + Wozniak). One founder becomes famous, but the partnership was essential. "Steve Jobs literally couldn't have built the first Apple computer if he didn't have Steve Wozniak" [7].

## How Co-Founders Meet

Data from YC's top companies shows school and work are the dominant channels. Working on projects or extracurricular activities together is one of the best starting points. College is described as "an ideal place to find a co-founder" [3].

A survey of 50+ founders from a recent YC batch reveals the full range [5]:

**School connections** remain the most common. Multiple founders met as college classmates. Some met even earlier: one pair became friends at Jewish sleepaway camp at age ten, another did choir together in high school [5].

**Workplace connections** are the second most common path. Founders who worked at the same company discovered shared passions through day-to-day collaboration [5].

**Personal and family relationships** also produce co-founders. Sisters, married couples, and close friends all appear in the data [5].

**YC's co-founder matching platform** has become a meaningful channel. Multiple founders from the batch met through the platform. One founder compared the experience to dating apps: "Some swiping right, some swiping left" [5].

### The Always-Be-Scanning Approach

Taggar recommends building a set of potential co-founders long before planning to start a startup [7]. During school and work, work on projects with others -- weekend projects, side projects, purely-for-fun projects. These reveal what someone is actually like to work with.

The number one blocker: assuming everyone you know is unavailable. "You can never make any assumptions about who's available. You have to always make the ask" [7]. The branching tactic: if the person says no, ask who they would pick as their co-founder if they were starting a company, and ask for an introduction [7].

## What to Look For

### Stress Handling (The #1 Criteria)

Taggar identifies the single most important quality: ability to handle stress well. "Even if they have the perfect set of complementary skills, if they don't handle stress well, it's very unlikely your relationship is going to last the stress and rigors of founding a company" [7].

The best test: having worked with someone under real stress previously. Working at Google together does not involve startup-level stress. Socializing with friends does not test stress responses. No co-founder relationship is a sure bet, but someone you have worked with under pressure is safer than a stranger [7].

### Relationship Quality Over Skill Complementarity

Caldwell observes that the biggest error is founders looking for someone with a skill match versus someone they actually have a deep relationship with. He has never seen a co-founder breakup caused by a skills mismatch: "You'd rather work with someone you really like and learn together than work with someone you don't know at all and get into fights and then break up" [4]. Seibel reinforces this: co-founder breakups are never "My co-founder is excellent, A-plus, they just don't have the skills that we need" -- they are always "My co-founder is a living nightmare and I can't take it anymore" [4].

### Shared High-Level Goals

Co-founders need alignment on what kind of company they want to build. A founder who wants a fast-growing, venture-backed company is a bad match for someone who wants a lifestyle business. "Before you start a company with someone, talk about this stuff" [7].

### Trajectory Over Current Skills

Do not fixate on specific current skills. "The meta skill you care about is: is your co-founder smart? And are they willing to learn new things and willing to adapt as your startup grows and changes?" [7].

## 10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder

YC compiled the 10 most important questions to discuss before committing, derived from analyzing the most common reasons founding teams break up [2]:

1. **Why do you want to do this startup?** Personal goals, financial and non-financial.
2. **Roles, titles, and division of responsibilities.** Who will be CEO?
3. **How will you split equity?** See [[Equity Split]].
4. **Where will the company be based?** In-person or remote?
5. **What idea will you work on?** Are you willing to pivot?
6. **What needs to happen for each person to go full-time?**
7. **Personal financial situation.**
8. **Working schedule.**
9. **Team building philosophy.**
10. **Conflict resolution.** What happens if you decide not to work together anymore?

YC emphasizes that no questionnaire substitutes for spending a lot of time together in person. It usually takes several months to know if you are compatible [2].

## Co-Founder Discovery Through Brainstorming

[[Michael Seibel]] suggests that brainstorming about problems with friends is one of the best ways to discover co-founder compatibility [9]. The process of talking about a problem, elaborating on it, and building on each other's thinking reveals whether two people "trade ideas well." The idea generation phase doubles as a co-founder evaluation phase [9].

## Find the Co-Founder First, Then the Idea

Caldwell recommends finding a co-founder before coming up with an idea or raising money [4]. When founders come up with the idea first and then add a co-founder, "in their mind it's your company, it's not their company." If both founders shepherd the idea through the earliest stages together, "you're going to see a lot more ownership and people stepping up when you go through hard times" [4].

## Co-Founder Mistakes That Kill Companies

### The Communication Breakdown Pattern

Caldwell describes a pattern he sees repeatedly: founders who spend eight or twelve hours a day together but have not had a real conversation in a week, a month, or even a year. Once things get that bad, the relationship is likely beyond repair. The CEO's job shifts from trying to fix the relationship to managing the separation "in the most effective and least destructive way" [4].

Seibel adds that founders in this situation typically know the right thing to do -- separate -- but cannot bring themselves to do it because it feels bad. "They're willing to go through years of pain or actually reduce the chance the startup's going to work than to face that head-on" [4]. Caldwell warns that delay makes it worse: "The longer this relationship persists in this way before there's a breakup, the worse the breakup is going to be" -- vested stock, litigation, and a damaged cap table [4].

### The Do-Over Illusion

Caldwell warns against the temptation to abandon a co-founder relationship and start fresh: "It's so easy to start a new company. And then you speak to them a year or two later, and it's really hard to get back to where they were in the moment" [4]. Seibel emphasizes that the co-founder relationship is "one of the most important things you're doing in the beginning of the company" and cautions against investing only fifty percent effort [4].

### Multiple People Wanting to Be CEO

The CEO title designates the person trusted to make final decisions. When multiple founders want that role, it signals "I don't trust and respect the other co-founders to have that" [7].

### Different Work Ethic Expectations

One founder wants to work all the time; the other wants work-life balance. These differences become unbearable under startup pressure [7].

## Equity and Deadlock Prevention

Seibel raises the question of how to structure equity so co-founder fights are not fatal. Caldwell advises keeping equity as close to equal as possible. However, a straight fifty-fifty split creates a deadlock problem. His recommendation: the CEO holds one extra share so that ownership is effectively equal but there is a tiebreaker vote [4]. See [[Equity Split]] for full guidance.

Taggar's default: split equity equally. "If this startup is successful, you will end up working on it for the next 10 years or more. Even if someone's been thinking about the idea for a little bit longer or done a little bit more work, that will be quite insignificant over the long term" [7].

## Maintaining the Relationship

Taggar's practical advice: set up regular one-on-ones with your co-founder. Monthly dinners or drinks to check in: "Do we think things are going well? Are we excited? How are the two of us working together?" [7].

The purpose: "It gives you an opportunity to regularly release some of the pressure that every startup co-founder relationship has, slowly over time, rather than having it all build up and come out in one big burst" [7].

Avoiding hard conversations is "death by a thousand cuts" [7].

## Do Technical Founders Need a Business Co-Founder?

The short answer: no, but someone must do the work. See [[Technical Co-Founders]] for the full treatment. The critical variable is not ability but appetite. Caldwell and Seibel point to evidence from the most valuable companies: Google, Nvidia, Microsoft, Facebook, Stripe, and Dropbox all had primarily technical founding teams [6].

## Solo Founders

While YC does fund solo founders, the data is not in their favor. Only 7.9% of YC top companies were solo-founded [3]. Taggar advises spending all effort on finding a co-founder in 90% of cases. The 10% exception requires: a specific idea the founder is passionate about with domain experience, and the founder is an engineer who can build the first version [7].

## YC's Co-Founder Matching Platform

Y Combinator runs a [co-founder matching platform](https://www.ycombinator.com/cofounder-matching) where founders describe themselves and what they are looking for [1]. The platform has produced companies accepted into YC, including Sequin (YC S21, raised $5.7M) and Kiwi Biosciences (YC S21, raised $1.5M) [1].

## Co-Founder Stories from the Field

### The Technical Co-Founder Search

Two sources illustrate the "saddest story in Silicon Valley" -- having a great idea but no technical co-founder:

**PlanGrid**: [[Tracy Young]] and Ryan Sutton-Gee pitched their construction blueprint idea to programmer friends. All agreed it was good; none would quit their jobs. Co-founder Antoine, a high-frequency trading engineer, said: "This is a fantastic idea. You guys should learn how to program." The breakthrough came when Young's boyfriend Ralph discovered a recurring calendar event labeled "peanuts" and was offended that she had not asked him -- a full-stack engineer and Pixar rendering engineer -- to help [10].

**Gobble**: [[Ooshma Garg]], a mechanical engineer with no software experience, searched for a technical co-founder through coffee dates and friend introductions. "No one was as passionate about the idea as I was, they weren't willing to quit their job, and we just didn't connect in a deep way" [11]. She decided to proceed without one and raised seed funding based on her passion, market proof, and previous startup experience [11].

### Co-Founder Romantic Relationships

Young addresses the challenge of co-founding with a romantic partner. She and Ralph (CTO, then husband) maintained PlanGrid for eight years. Her framework: clear delineation of responsibilities, complete trust in each other's domains, and rules like no work talk after 8 PM (which they "broke all the time") [10]. Her advice: "If you have the choice, you just wouldn't risk your relationship in that way." But if it works, the alignment can be powerful [10].

### The Decade-Long Friendship: Meesho (Vidit Aatrey)

[[Vidit Aatrey]] and Sanjeev Barnwal met at IIT Delhi and worked on projects together for years. They decided to start a company with no idea, on a phone call, purely because "I know I can work with Sanjeev for hundred years of my life" [12]. Aatrey credits this deep trust for enabling two pivots: "I have seen so many friends around me who were just not able to pivot because it was a hard conversation with their co-founder. The belief that me and Sanjeev have is not because of what we are doing right now, it's because we know each other for more than 10 years" [12]. His framing: "My best decision ever was to start this company with Sanjeev" [12].

### The Three-Hour Co-Founder: Dropbox (Drew Houston)

[[Drew Houston]] and Arash Ferdowsi "probably knew each other for maybe two or three hours" before committing to co-found Dropbox [13]. They were introduced by Kyle Vogt three weeks before the YC application deadline, after [[Paul Graham]] told Houston he needed a co-founder. Houston's assessment: "This kid's clearly smart. A little quiet, but very thoughtful." There was no rigorous framework: "I wish I could say, 'Oh yeah, here are the 17 things I was looking for'... But no" [13]. Ferdowsi dropped out of MIT the next day [13].

### The Trial Period: Lever (Sarah Nahm)

[[Sarah Nahm]] describes a more deliberate co-founder discovery. The Lever co-founders "were almost trying out working with each other for a while before we actually officially went for it" [14]. Nahm's hierarchy for what matters most at the founding stage: (1) believing in the people you are working with, (2) believing in a change happening in the world, (3) a distant third -- believing you have what it takes [14].

### The Second Time Around: Rippling (Parker Conrad)

[[Parker Conrad]] and his co-founder Pano had worked together at Zenefits before co-founding Rippling. The relationship was forged through shared experience of building the same product. When Conrad decided to rebuild, Pano shared his conviction: "There's a hundred billion dollar opportunity just sitting right there on the floor, and nobody can see it except for us" [15]. This represents a co-founding pattern where the relationship and the idea are inseparable [15].

## References

1. [Does Co-Founder Matching Work?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Cq-does-co-founder-matching-work) -- Catheryn Li (2021)
2. [10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Hj-10-questions-to-discuss-with-a-potential-co-founder) -- Y Combinator (2022)
3. [Startups for Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/D0-startups-for-students) -- Tamanna Khemani, Kat Manalac (2021)
4. [Co-Founder Mistakes That Kill Companies and How to Avoid Them](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DZ-dalton-michael-co-founder-mistakes-that-kill-companies-and-how-to-avoid-them) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2022)
5. [Founder FAQ: How Did You Meet Your Co-Founder?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/JD-founder-faq-how-did-you-meet-your-co-founder) -- Y Combinator (n.d.)
6. [Do Technical Founders Need a Business Co-Founder?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KV-do-technical-founders-need-a-business-co-founder) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
7. [How to Find a Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Ld-how-to-find-a-co-founder) -- [[Harj Taggar]] (n.d.)
8. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) -- [[Paul Graham]] (December 2020)
9. [How to Get and Test Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7x-how-to-get-and-test-ideas) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)
10. [On starting and scaling construction startup PlanGrid](https://ycombinator.com/library/6L-on-starting-and-scaling-construction-startup-plangrid) -- [[Tracy Young]], Kat Manalac (2019)
11. [On starting and scaling meal kit company Gobble](https://ycombinator.com/library/69-on-starting-and-scaling-meal-kit-company-gobble) -- [[Ooshma Garg]] (2016)
12. [On Starting and Scaling Indian Shopping Site Meesho](https://ycombinator.com/library/6M-on-starting-and-scaling-indian-shopping-site-meesho) -- [[Vidit Aatrey]], Adora Cheung (2019)
13. [On Starting and Scaling Dropbox (YC W07)](https://ycombinator.com/library/6S-on-starting-and-scaling-dropbox-yc-w07) -- [[Drew Houston]], [[Sam Altman]] (2017)
14. [On Starting and Scaling Recruiting Software Startup Lever](https://ycombinator.com/library/6I-on-starting-and-scaling-recruiting-software-startup-lever) -- [[Sarah Nahm]], Holly Liu (2019)
15. [How To Build The Future: Parker Conrad](https://ycombinator.com/library/M2-how-to-build-the-future-parker-conrad) -- [[Parker Conrad]], [[Garry Tan]] (2025)

title: Hard Tech Startups
body:
# Hard Tech Startups

Hard tech startups -- those building physical products, aerospace technology, medical devices, or other complex engineering -- face distinct challenges around demonstrating progress, raising capital, and navigating regulatory environments. Multiple YC founders and partners have shared lessons on how to make hard tech work within the accelerator model and beyond.

## Why Founders Matter in Hard Tech

[[Blake Scholl]], founder of [[Boom Technology]] (YC W16), argues that all the big breakthroughs in aviation came from founder-led companies. The first airplane came from bicycle entrepreneurs. The first practical airliner (DC-3) and the first jetliner (De Havilland Comet) were created by companies run by their founders. The last new commercial aircraft company was founded in 1921. The last founder retired from the industry in 1958 -- "which, coincidentally or not, was also the year we had the first jetliner" [1].

Since then, Boeing and Airbus have optimized: making existing designs more efficient and safer, but not improving human capability. Boeing's CEO publicly said their policy is "no more moonshots" -- from the company that was literally in the moonshot business. "I believe very strongly that if we want more human capability, we need entrepreneurs, we need founders, and we need new companies" [1].

Scholl identifies what founders bring that managers do not: "Founders have vision. Founders start things to make the world a better place. Founders take risks. They make bold bets" [1].

## Demonstrating Progress in Hardware

Hardware companies at YC must figure out what they can build and show at demo day. Software companies can ship an MVP relatively quickly; hardware has longer lead times. The product may not be done, in market, or even have a working demo [1].

Scholl's advice: "Figure out really early on something that both is and looks like actual, substantive progress towards the product" [1]. This means avoiding two traps:

1. **Meaningful but unappreciable progress**: Boom's simulation results hitting critical lift-drag ratios were technically significant but "no one's gonna understand it or believe it or get excited about it" [1].
2. **Progress that is just for show**: Building a flight simulator would have been impressive-looking but empty [1].

Boom's solution: they completed the high-level aerodynamic design, built a model of the airplane, 3D-printed a model of the jet engine, and physically brought to demo day one of the three actual engines that would power the first airplane. "People, of course, were like, 'OMG, jet engine.'" The key was that it was real -- they had found and purchased the engine they would fly [1].

### The Athelas Path: Hackathon to Shippable Product

Tanay Tandon describes how [[Athelas]] (YC S16) went from a proof-of-concept built overnight at YC Hacks 2014 to shipping medical devices to patients and hospitals [2].

The first version used a rubber piece and spherical magnifier attached to a smartphone camera with a blood sample held by a toilet paper roll -- similar in design to a [van Leeuwenhoek microscope](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antonie_van_Leeuwenhoek). "While functioning and a neat trick, someone needed to be physically holding the camera in place, the slide had to be moved around, with the lighting often being hard to fix. At the end of the day it was a nice experimental toy" [2].

The progression from hackathon prototype to shippable product required [2]:
- An automated blood processing mechanism for stained peripheral smears
- More robust computer vision for different cell types (deep learning plus traditional CV)
- Automated imaging without manual slide manipulation
- Clinical validation (350 patients tested against gold-standard Beckman Counter)
- FDA Class 2 510(k) submission

The path was slow because of college workload and rising hardware iteration costs. "Finals often meant days going by without any tangible progress whatsoever" [2]. During YC (summer batch, full-time), clinical validation was completed at the FEMAP family hospital, demonstrating high inter-rater agreement (100% 5-class inter-rater agreement) [2].

The device shipped at $250 per unit for point-of-care locations, homes, and clinical settings [2].

## Selling Before Building

Scholl's experience challenges the assumption that hard tech companies cannot sell before the product exists. YC's advice to Boom: "Get your ass out of stealth mode, it's not helping you" and "Go sell some airplanes" [1].

Scholl's reaction: "I don't know how 10 guys in a basement who say they're building supersonic jets are going to sell anything to airlines, but I could try. And it turns out you can" [1].

The group partners' mandate: "Show up on demo day with sales or your goose is pretty cooked." Scholl assessed his sales pipeline (United, Delta, Lufthansa, Air China) and concluded none could close in eight weeks. He focused instead on startup airlines and Virgin [1].

### The Virgin Deal

Boom pursued Richard Branson directly, ensuring two different avenues to him so neither could be blocked: friends inside Virgin Galactic, and astronaut Mark Kelly on Boom's advisory board. The team crashed a Virgin Galactic spaceship rollout to get 15 minutes with Branson over breakfast with his mother [1].

The critical ask: "Look, we're not asking for your money, we're asking, 'When this flies, do you want a Virgin logo on it?'" Scholl emphasizes: "Ask for the right thing. When you have a deal that's probably gonna be hard to close -- ask for what's really gonna help you" [1].

Using demo day as a deadline was equally critical. Virgin cared about its brand and publicity. "We are launching the company on demo day, and either you can be part of it or not" [1]. Virgin took demo day more seriously than expected.

The email from Virgin arrived 24 hours before Boom's stage slot: "You can announce the following -- We'll take the first 10 airplanes, and we've got options on them. And through Virgin Galactic, we're gonna help you build it" [1].

Boom went from potential embarrassment to a record-setting $5 billion in LOIs at demo day [1].

### Sales Lessons for Hard Tech

Scholl distills three principles:
1. **Be persistent beyond comfort**: "Be so persistent that you're sure you're being annoying. And you'll feel like you must be annoying, but when you have that feeling that means you're probably doing it right" [1].
2. **Have a deadline**: demo day provided a believable, external forcing function [1].
3. **Make the deal sweet for the other side**: when in a hurry, maximize the value proposition for the counterparty [1].

## The Motivation Advantage of Big Ideas

Scholl argues that bigger ideas can actually be easier because they motivate the founder, attract better talent, and energize everyone involved. "The team we have around us at Boom is phenomenal, and we wouldn't have it if we were building an app or a small airplane, even. We're able to get these people because everyone looks at what we're building, and they're like, 'I can tell my grandkids about this! It's either we're going to fail or we're going to change the world. There's no in between'" [1].

His advice for choosing what to work on: "Find something in the world that you desperately want to exist." All startups go through hell; the question is whether it is worth it. "If you haven't found that, don't start a startup just to start a startup. I did that my first time and it was a horrible idea" [1].

## Definition and Scope

Friedman provides a two-criteria definition of hard tech [4]:
1. It will take a lot of time and money to build the first product
2. Even with lots of time and money, it is not clear if building it is possible at all

The key distinction: **market risk vs. technical risk**. A website or mobile app has mostly market risk (will people want this?) but little technical risk (building apps is a solved problem). A hard tech company probably has something people clearly want -- the question is whether it can be built at all [4].

## The Sam Altman Paradox: Hard Companies Are Easier

[[Sam Altman]] observed: "In many ways, it's easier to start a hard company than an easy company" [4]. Friedman uses [[Boom Technology]] to explain. Scholl's first company was a mobile shopping app. Launching was easy, but getting press, attracting talent, and getting investor meetings were hard. With Boom, building the product is incredibly hard, but everything else is easy. "From the very beginning, back when Boom was like an idea, Blake was able to get some of the most talented people in the world to want to help him" [4].

## YC Hard Tech Stats

YC has funded 250+ bio companies and 200+ hard tech companies. YC is the largest bio seed investor and the largest hard tech seed investor in the world. Hard tech applicants have a roughly 10x higher acceptance rate than other application types [4].

## The Heavy MVP Problem

The biggest hard tech problem: you need millions of dollars to build the first product, and you do not have millions of dollars. The answer: "Figure out some way to make some progress on your idea that doesn't require millions of dollars" [4].

Seven examples of creative MVPs [4]:
1. **Boom**: Built computer simulations, assembled advisors, built a plastic model, used the model to get airline interest
2. **Solugen**: Applied to YC with a beaker that could produce one cup of hydrogen peroxide using synthetic biology
3. **AirX**: Used an existing approved medical device and wrote software around it, launching during YC with no FDA approval needed
4. **Notable Labs**: Started by providing tumor-screening services to pharma companies, generating revenue and data
5. **Astranis**: Built a test satellite during YC for under $50K in under three months
6. **Ginkgo Bioworks**: Closed contracts first ("If Ginkgo makes these organisms, we will pay you") then raised money to actually make the organisms
7. **Cruise**: Built an MVP self-driving car during YC in less than three months. Later acquired by GM for $1B

## Proving Demand Before Building

### Letters of Intent (LOIs)

For situations where pre-sales are not possible, YC developed the LOI format. An LOI is a non-binding contract to buy the product when ready [4]. Despite being non-binding, LOIs are effective because customers take them seriously. The specificity principle: the more specific the LOI (quantity, price, timeline, technical requirements), the more valuable it is [4].

## Fundraising Strategy: The Staircase, Not the Wall

The worst fundraising plan: "I need $50M, I'll go pitch until someone gives it to me." The correct plan: split $50M into five discrete raises, starting very small. Each raise requires specific milestones. The critical principle: "No step should be too large" [4].

## Pushing the Frontier: Space Computing

The hard tech category continues to expand. [[StarCloud]] (formerly Lumen Orbit) launched the first data-center-grade GPU (an NVIDIA H100) into orbit in November 2024, putting a hundred times more compute power in space than any previous mission. The company represents a new generation of hard tech startups applying the same scrappy, founder-led approach to domains that were recently considered impossible for startups.

## Regulatory and Validation Challenges

Hard tech startups often face regulatory hurdles that software companies do not. Athelas needed FDA approval (Class 2 510(k)) to ship its blood diagnostics device [2]. Friedman notes that for hardtech and biotech companies, [[Series A Fundraising]] traction sections should focus on technology efficacy, patents, LOIs, and specific regulatory milestones like 510(k) or IND filings [3].


## Navigating Regulation (Anne Wojcicki / 23andMe)

[[Anne Wojcicki]] provides a nuanced view of operating in a regulated industry. Her initial framing: regulation means rules, and rules mean a defined path. But the transition from unregulated to regulated was painful [5]. Key lessons: communicate in the regulator's language, respect institutional knowledge ("FDA employees are public servants who have been doing their jobs for 20 years"), and establish credibility through publications, not assertions [5].

## Young Founders in Hard Tech (Geoff Ralston / Embark)

[[Geoff Ralston]] demonstrates that age is not a disqualifier. The Embark founders were 19-20 years old and had already built the first self-driving vehicle in Canada. Their "startlingly clear" thinking and demonstrated building made age irrelevant [6]. Embark's original insight -- that self-driving vehicles would first succeed in constrained environments -- proved correct even as their specific choice proved wrong [6].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Aerospace Company Boom](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5Q-on-starting-and-scaling-aerospace-company-boom) -- [[Blake Scholl]] (n.d.)
2. [On Starting a Company from a Hackathon Project](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project) -- Tanay Tandon (January 2017)
3. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) -- [[Jared Friedman]] (n.d.)
4. [Advice for Hard Tech and Biotech Founders](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8G-advice-for-hard-tech-and-biotech-founders) -- [[Jared Friedman]] (2019)
5. [On starting and scaling healthcare startup 23andMe](https://ycombinator.com/library/5I-on-starting-and-scaling-healthcare-startup-23andme) -- [[Anne Wojcicki]], [[Sam Altman]] (2018)
6. [Embark Trucks' (W16) road to IPO](https://ycombinator.com/library/Co-embark-trucks-w16-road-to-ipo) -- [[Geoff Ralston]] (2021)

title: Investor Incentives
body:
# Investor Incentives

Understanding investor incentives is one of the most important meta-skills for founders. [[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] argue that investors are not evil, but they have their own business goals that may diverge from a founder's interests. Smart founders take a moment to think about those incentives when hearing a sales pitch, rather than believing things blindly [1].

## Terms Matter as Much as Valuation

A YC founder submitted this advice: "You should care just as much about the terms of your fundraise as you should care about the economics, the valuation, and the amount of money you raise. There are lots of rights, there are lots of terms inside of a funding deal that can screw you no matter how high the valuation is" [1].

Seibel describes the dynamic: investors know that founders want high valuations and press-release-worthy numbers. One way investors exploit this is to say "Great, we'll give you your ask around valuation or amount, but you need to give us all this other stuff" [1]. The "other stuff" is jargon-filled terms that first-time founders may not understand: participating preferred, super pro rata rights, board control provisions [1].

Seibel has seen founders give up board control -- such that they could be fired and were fired -- to raise a million dollars [1].

### The Experience Asymmetry

Founders cannot be as good at negotiation as professional investors who do it every day across many companies. The defenses are: use standard paperwork (like YC's SAFE), use a lawyer who has done significant startup work, and raise from investors with track records of public company outcomes [1].

Investors who have funded billion-dollar companies optimize for the founder building a massive company. Investors who have never seen a deal become a billion-dollar company optimize for base hits -- structuring documents around $10-20 million outcomes. "I don't think the investors are being evil. I think that they're basically saying, 'I've never seen a deal that's become a billion dollar company, and so I've gotta optimize for these base hits'" [1].

### The "Find a Lead" Soft No

One of the most common soft rejections: "This is great. We love it. When you find a lead, come back." Seibel explains the investor's reasoning, and Caldwell underscores why it means no [1]:

If the founder did have a lead investor, the lead would typically want to put in the entire amount to hit their ownership target. Other investors would want to invest alongside. The "come back when you find a lead" line gives the investor a free option: if someone impressive invests, they can jump in; if not, they have politely passed while appearing supportive [1].

Caldwell offers a job analogy: "It's like you apply to a job at Facebook and they're like, 'This is great. Come back to us with your job offer from Google and Apple.' That's not a job offer" [1]. Seibel adds: "It's almost as if they have to do this because this is their job. It's almost as if they get to run this game every day with multiple companies and all you're trying to do is raise money and get back to work. Of course they're going to be better at it" [1].

### The "Take More Money" Incentive

Seibel observes that as the seed funding world has professionalized, seed funds have obligations to their LPs and internal ownership targets. This creates pressure to convince founders to take more money -- not because more money helps the company, but because the fund needs to own 10% [1].

"Money is what the investors are selling. When there's a lot of money out there and a lot of investors out there, the easiest way to compete is: 'Hey, I'll give you more money. You need more money. Let me try to make you afraid that you don't have enough money so you'll take my money'" [1].

### Growth Pressure and Misalignment

Caldwell notes that for companies investors have already funded, there is pressure to grow fast because investors want huge outcomes. This manifests as "You should be growing faster. Your revenue looks flat. You should be spending more on sales and marketing" [1].

The worst scenario for an investor is a portfolio company that is profitable but not growing fast enough. Caldwell observes that for a founder, profitability without hypergrowth "ain't so bad" [1]. Seibel adds context: the professionalization of seed funds has created new misalignments. Ten years ago, smaller funds and angels had interests more aligned with founders. As seed funds have grown, they have obligations to LPs and internal ownership targets that can diverge from what is best for the company [1]. This is a fundamental misalignment that founders must understand. See [[Startup Outcomes and Optionality]] for more on the airplane vs. spaceship dynamic.

## Why Top Investors Secretly Love YC

Caldwell and Seibel reveal a gap between how investors talk about YC publicly and how they actually invest [2].

### The Numbers

Seibel presents the numbers showing top venture firms invest heavily in YC companies [2]:
- Andreessen Horowitz: 234 investments in YC companies
- Sequoia: 139 investments
- Founders Fund: 104 investments

Caldwell adds: looking at the YC top companies list, "it's all the biggest investors" [2].

### The Service YC Provides to VCs

Caldwell explains the VC filtering problem: the average VC does one or two deals per year. Most startups that approach them are "too early" -- not enough signal to justify investment. YC provides a filtering service: taking 20,000+ applications and reducing them to roughly 200 companies. The companies are financed (they do not just run out of money), advised on pivots, and helped with sales and pitching [2].

Caldwell notes that many of the companies on YC's top 100 list pivoted during YC. "That's a value-added service we're providing to the investor market" [2].

Seibel offers an analogy: the best VC firms are like high-end restaurants. They give the impression that the chef personally farms the ingredients. In reality, most ingredients come from purveyors who identify, prepare, and deliver them in a nice package [2].

### Why the Love Is Secret

Caldwell explains: VCs want to be the first check. If a company does YC, the VC will not be the first check. YC also encourages founders to talk to many investors in parallel rather than one at a time -- "not their favorite thing" [2].

Caldwell describes the ideal scenario for a VC: meet every company, have the founder love them exclusively, think all other investors are bad, and come back every six months to pitch again with updated metrics [2]. YC companies are less likely to do any of those things [2].

### Seed Fund Dynamics

Seibel explains the seed fund perspective: these funds face a more zero-sum situation. Their primary opportunity is the first round, and they know they will be diluted from that round forward. Outside of YC, they might invest at a $5-10 million valuation. After Demo Day, the same company costs $15-25 million [2].

Caldwell adds: "You can't blame seed funds for saying we don't want you to do YC" from their perspective. But as a founder, understanding these dynamics is essential to evaluating advice [2].

### The Key Signal: Are They Investing?

Caldwell's practical test for evaluating investor advice: "Are they offering to invest?" If someone is literally offering to invest money and saying you should not apply to YC, that is a reasonable offer to consider. But the common pattern -- someone aggressively not investing while giving advice not to raise from other sources including YC -- is a red flag [2].

"Spend a little bit more time looking at what people do rather than what they say or they meme or they tweet" [2].


## Most Investors Add Negative Value (Vinod Khosla)

[[Vinod Khosla]] offers the most extreme assessment of investor value in the YC canon: "90% of investors add no value. 70% of investors add negative value to a company" [3]. The negative value comes from advising companies without having earned the right to advise. Khosla frames investor selection as: "An investor is an employee who you can't fire, and that's how you should think about it" [3].

[[Sam Altman]] adds that the worst board behavior is sending press clippings of competitors during the most stressful moments. The best investors are "calming you down, supporting you" during hard risk decisions [3].

## Investors Who Are Right vs. Right Timing (Tony Xu)

[[Tony Xu]] offers a founder's perspective on investor rejection. DoorDash's internal metrics moved in the right direction from 2016-2018, yet Xu received hundreds of rejections. His analysis: growth investors struggle with timing. "It's really easy to invest when the numbers are clear... Then there are moments where it's just before that moment" [4]. The resolution: "All you need is one investor to say yes" [4].

## References

1. [Understanding Investor Terms and Incentives](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/DX-dalton-michael-understanding-investor-terms-and-incentives) — [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2022)
2. [Why Investors (Secretly) Love Y Combinator](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Jd-dalton-michael-why-investors-secretly-love-y-combinator) — [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)
3. [Vinod Khosla on How to Build the Future](https://ycombinator.com/library/6N-vinod-khosla-on-how-to-build-the-future) -- [[Vinod Khosla]], [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)
4. [How To Build The Future: Tony Xu](https://ycombinator.com/library/MJ-how-to-build-the-future-tony-xu) -- [[Tony Xu]], [[Garry Tan]] (2025)

title: Series A Fundraising
body:
# Series A Fundraising

The Series A is the first major priced equity round for most startups. It involves two distinct challenges: building and delivering a compelling pitch, and navigating the legal and organizational complexity of closing. This article covers both, drawing on Janelle Tam's guide to Series A pitches and [[Aaron Harris]] and Jason Kwon's diligence checklist.

## The Series A Pitch

Janelle Tam, YC's Series A Program Manager, compiled feedback from hundreds of hours working 1:1 with founders on their Series A pitches and decks. Every piece of advice in her guide was given to at least one YC founder internally [2].

### Purpose and Principles

The purpose is to show investors why they should invest. The pitch provides a clear and concise overview of the business already built, then paints a picture of where it could go and why raising money will help get there [2].

Six principles underlie this:

1. **It is an investor pitch, not a customer pitch.** Investors may not have encountered the problem being solved. Good pitches explain the problem and its impact, show how the product solves it, and demonstrate why it is a good business [2].
2. **Clarity is paramount.** Investor attention is a limited resource. Make the pitch as simple and straightforward as possible. The best pitchers use plain English, avoiding jargon. Where jargon is necessary, they define each term clearly on first use [2].
3. **Give an overview, not a download.** The goal is to tantalize investors with enough information to keep them interested, leaving room for questions. A pitch should be 15-20 minutes, setting up a 40-45 minute discussion. If investors are not asking questions, the slides are probably too complicated [2].
4. **Start with what you have built.** Compared to the seed round, it is harder to raise a Series A on promise alone. Investors want to see results [2].
5. **Paint a picture of where it is going.** Once you establish credibility through what you have already built, show how it could grow into a massive company [2].
6. **Show that money is the obstacle.** The deck should make clear that capital -- not incompetence, poor product quality, or lack of [[Product-Market Fit]] -- is what stands between the company and the next level [2].

### The Starting Exercise

Tam describes a structured process for building the pitch [2]:

1. Write the narrative as 10-15 bullet points -- the "fundraising vertebrae"
2. Under each bullet, list supporting data points
3. Build the deck around this list: each bullet is one slide. If more than one point per slide, the slide is too complicated. The deck should be 10-15 slides plus appendix
4. Write out every question investors could ask, especially about metrics, and prepare clear, credible, data-backed answers

### Deck Structure

**Title (1 slide):** Company name or logo and a one-sentence description. The one-liner must be concrete ("We build and operate robot greenhouses" not "Reinventing agriculture"), understandable to a layperson, and specific to what the company does [2].

![Title slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/cd9e75fb29a3.png)

**Traction Teaser (1 slide):** Placed early to grab attention and convince the investor the presentation is worth listening to [2].

![Traction teaser example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/332f6e6e9236.png)

**Problem (1 slide):** The problem must match the solution. Present from the customer's perspective. Show, don't tell -- "Medical billing is broken" is vague; "Here's how medical billing works today: patients need to do X steps, which takes Y months and results in Z% claims approved" is concrete [2].

![Problem slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/085dc4c55fa0.png)

**Solution (1 slide):** Side-by-side comparison with status quo. Quantify impact with numbers. Focus on what exists now, not future plans. Show how the customer uses it, not feature lists [2].

![Solution slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/370a5e22f179.png)

**Traction In-Depth (a few slides):** Numbers should tell a story, starting with the most important metric -- usually revenue. For enterprise: revenue growth, sales cycle, ACV, salesperson efficiency, churn. For consumer: revenue plus growth rate, organic vs. paid acquisition, CAC/LTV, engagement and retention. For hardtech/biotech: technology efficacy data, patents filed, LOIs, specific milestones [2].

Show at least 4-6 months of trend data. YC's [benchmarks](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/1k-benchmarks) assume Series A fundraising 18-24 months after seed [2].

Three traps in presenting numbers: information overload (identify "hero facts"), too few numbers (at Series A you need data), unclear numbers (cumulative numbers almost always hide weak monthly data) [2].

**Market (1 slide):** Bottoms-up calculation: number of prospective customers x value of each customer. Use real numbers from the current business, not top-down report figures [2].

![Market slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/24ae1bce2667.png)

**Competition (1 slide):** Show why the company is 10x better than alternatives. Convince the investor the moat is defensible [2].

![Competition slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/2eff98b228dc.png)

**Vision (1 slide):** Show how the company becomes a $10B company. "Here's where you can dream and talk about all of the hypotheticals you've judiciously kept from the first part of your deck" [2].

![Vision slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/0831048590eb.png)

**Team (1 slide):** If the team is a comparative advantage, place as the second or third slide. Otherwise, show who the founders are, why they are the right people, and quality of team recruited. Signaling risk: including a Series A fund among existing investors prompts the question of whether they are leading the round [2].

![Team slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/169ca6b8734c.png)

**Use of Funds (1 slide):** The climax. Cover how much is being raised, what it will fund, and where the company will be in 18-24 months [2].

![Use of Funds slide example](../raw/posts/images/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck/1c1028d09c46.png)

**Appendix:** Ammunition for the Q&A. Include slides answering anticipated investor questions, financial projections, and detailed use of funds [2].

### Design and Practice

Optimize for clarity, not beauty. "Keeping it as simple and bare-bones as possible" is usually best [2].

Schedule 1-hour practice sessions: 20 minutes pitch, 20 minutes conversation, 20 minutes feedback. A quick test for individual slides: flash it to someone who has never seen it and ask what point it makes [2].

For video pitches: turn on video with good lighting and audio, one person should pitch, record and review yourself, keep a bullet-point script visible but avoid reading it [2].

### Iterating During Fundraise

Set aside 1-2 hours daily to review each meeting. After every pitch, ask: What excited investors most? Where did they zone out? What objections arose? [2]

## The Closing Process

### The Closing Timeline Problem

Founders generally underestimate how long closing a Series A takes. [[Aaron Harris]] and Jason Kwon (General Counsel of YC Continuity) observe that closing an A "can take more than a month, much of which is often spent tracking down documents for lawyers" [1]. Having all diligence materials organized in a single data room before signing a term sheet can cut as much as a week off the closing process [1].

### The Series A Diligence Checklist

Kwon assembled this checklist based on involvement in hundreds of financings with "nearly every law firm and VC fund in the valley" [1]. Everything listed below should be assembled in a data room before signing a term sheet.

**Corporate Records and Charter Documents:** All minutes of directors' and stockholders' meetings, written consents, Certificate of Incorporation, Certificates of Designation, Rights, Bylaws, subsidiary documentation, and organizational charts [1].

**Business Plan and Financials:** Current business plan, financial projections, and most recent financial statements [1].

**Intellectual Property:** List of all trademarks, patents, copyrights, and domain names with filing/registration documentation. Documentation of IP assignments to the company [1].

**Securities and Cap Table:** List of stockholders with issuance dates and prices. List of option holders with grant dates and exercise prices. Agreements relating to outstanding options, warrants, conversion rights, preemptive rights. Documents evidencing registration rights or shareholder agreements. Vesting schedules including acceleration provisions. Evidence of qualification under Rule 701 and state blue sky laws [1].

**Material Agreements:** Standard terms of service, agreements exceeding $25,000, property leases, debt documents, IP licenses, insurance policies, judgments, joint venture agreements, consulting contracts, and list of officers and directors [1].

**Disputes and Litigation:** Correspondence relating to pending or threatened actions, IP infringement allegations, labor disputes [1].

**Employee Information:** Complete employee and consultant list with title, salary, bonus, commission plan, classification, and state of residence. Standard offer letter template. Employment agreements with severance or acceleration provisions. Equity incentive plans. Employee handbook. Loans to employees. For foreign employees, country-by-country benefits listing [1].

### The Equity Grant Timing Trap

Harris and Kwon flag a critical timing issue [1]: once a term sheet is signed, most practitioners believe the company can no longer use its existing 409A valuation for option grants because a "material event" has occurred that implies a higher valuation.

The consequences cascade: team members hired earlier who have not yet received grants will get options at a higher strike price, reducing their upside, creating a morale issue at the worst possible time [1].

The solution is preventive: "make sure there isn't a significant backlog of equity grants while you are in the middle of your fundraise process" [1]. Clear all pending or promised equity grants before beginning the Series A process.

## References

1. [Series A Diligence Checklist](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3h-series-a-diligence-checklist) -- [[Aaron Harris]], Jason Kwon (n.d.)
2. [How to Build a Great Series A Pitch and Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck) -- Janelle Tam (n.d.)

title: Startup Legal Mechanics
body:
# Startup Legal Mechanics

[[Kirsty Nathoo]], Managing Director of Finance at Y Combinator, has worked with thousands of companies through all stages of their life, from incorporation to exits. She presents the basic mechanical and legal issues that nearly all startups face in their earliest days [1]. None of this is glamorous, but getting it wrong creates expensive problems that surface at the worst possible moments -- during fundraising rounds or acquisitions.

## When to Incorporate

Incorporation creates a separate legal entity that pays its own taxes, holds its own assets and liabilities, signs its own contracts, and can sue and be sued independently of the founders [1].

The timing question has two failure modes [1]:

**Too soon**: The founders are still throwing around ideas, treating the project as a side project, uncertain about long-term commitment, or unsure about their co-founder relationship. In these situations, incorporating adds administrative burden without benefit [1].

**Too late**: The founders have created significant IP (especially with multiple people contributing), built a product ready to charge for, or entered a situation where personal liability protection matters. At this point, not having a corporate entity creates risk [1].

Stripe Atlas, a YC company product, helps founders form a company, get a bank account, and start accepting payments [1].

## Delaware C Corp: The Default

In the US, the standard structure for a venture-backed startup is a Delaware C Corporation. Nathoo is emphatic: "Investors can only really invest in C Corps" [1]. Other structures (LLCs, etc.) may seem appealing based on advice from non-startup lawyers, but they create conversion headaches later.

Why Delaware specifically [1]:
- Delaware corporate law is highly developed and well understood by all startup lawyers
- Investors expect Delaware companies
- Delaware law provides the most flexibility for issuing shares
- Best privacy protections for owners
- Founders can incorporate in Delaware without being US citizens or physically present in the US

Nathoo's advice: "There's no point trying to do something different. Just go with the herd on this" [1].

## The Connecticut Lawyer Horror Story

A YC company came in as an LLC formed in Connecticut because the founders' friends were Connecticut lawyers. When they needed to convert to a Delaware C Corp, they used those same Connecticut lawyers. Three funding rounds later, a Valley law firm discovered the conversion had been done incorrectly and was legally invalid. The fix took six months, involved four law firms, and cost $500,000 [1].

The moral: use Silicon Valley lawyers who understand startup corporate law, or use Clerky [1].

## How to Incorporate

Incorporation is a two-step process [1]:

**Step 1**: File formation documents with Delaware (about 24 hours to process).

**Step 2**: Adopt bylaws, create a board, appoint directors and officers, assign IP to the company, and issue shares to founders.

Two recommended paths [1]:

**Lawyers**: Costs $3,000-$5,000 plus filing fees. Most Valley startup lawyers defer fees until the company raises money. Best for non-standard situations or high-touch needs.

**Clerky** (YC company): A platform that generates all formation documents through a simple interface. Costs a few hundred dollars plus filing fees. Documents are signed on the platform and stored there. This is where YC sends companies that have been accepted but have not yet incorporated [1].

## Assigning Equity

The equity conversation forces co-founders to discuss expectations and commitment levels. It surfaces issues early -- such as whether one founder expects to work part-time [1].

Nathoo argues for near-equal splits among co-founders. Founders commonly push back with reasons like: "I thought of the idea," "I built the prototype," "I closed the first $20K in sales," or "I started three months before my co-founder" [1].

Nathoo's response: "Everything is ahead of you. This is the early days of the company. If you've worked on it for three months, you've probably got five, ten, fifteen years of this company ahead of you, if it's going to be a success. So think about forwards, not backwards" [1]. See [[Equity Split]] for the full treatment of this topic.

The only potentially valid reason for a slight imbalance is preventing founder deadlock on votes. But Nathoo notes: "If you're at the point where the founders are having to vote their shares to make decisions, then there's probably something more fundamentally broken in the relationship" [1].

Equity split is the number one reason YC sees co-founders break up. Resentment builds in stressful environments when splits feel unfair, and the resulting dispute often consumes so much energy that the company itself struggles to survive [1].

## Stock Purchase Agreements and Paperwork

Founders buy their shares via a stock purchase agreement, typically exchanging a few dollars of cash deposited from their personal bank account into the company bank account [1]. The transaction must be properly documented.

Another YC company horror story: founders incorporated, set up shares, then had to merge into a new company. The share swap appeared to work, but new lawyers discovered years later that the original share purchases had never been properly papered.
