The founders did not legally own the original shares, which meant the swap was invalid, which meant they did not own the new shares either. Everything done since -- including multiple funding rounds -- was legally questionable [1].

## Vesting

Vesting means earning the right to permanent ownership of shares over time. The standard schedule is four-year vesting with a one-year cliff [1]:

- **Day 1 to Day 365**: All shares are subject to the company's repurchase option
- **Day 365 (the cliff)**: 25% of shares vest immediately
- **Months 13-48**: An additional 1/48th of total shares vest each month
- **End of Year 4**: 100% of shares are vested

If a founder leaves, the company repurchases unvested shares at the original purchase price [1].

Three reasons vesting matters [1]:

1. **Protection for remaining founders**: Without vesting, a co-founder who leaves after six months retains the same ownership as founders who work for another decade. "Meanwhile, the ex-founder is sitting on a beach, drinking cocktails" [1].

2. **Skin in the game**: Creates incentive to stay and work hard. Investors care about this because they are investing in the founders [1].

3. **Sets an example for employees**: Founders cannot credibly require employee vesting while exempting themselves [1].

## The 83(b) Election

The single most important piece of tax paperwork in the incorporation process. Without it, founders are taxed on the increase in value of their shares every time shares vest -- potentially creating enormous personal and company tax liabilities [1].

Filing the 83(b) election with the IRS within 30 days of the share purchase eliminates this problem [1]. It is the one thing in the incorporation process that cannot be fixed retroactively. Nathoo has seen both funding rounds and acquisition deals fall apart because proof of 83(b) filing was missing and the buyer or investor was spooked by potential tax liabilities [1].

"Follow the instructions. Either when you're dealing with lawyers or when you're dealing with Clerky, they will give you instructions about how to complete the form. They will give you instructions about where to send it, how to do it. Just follow those instructions and all will be fine" [1].

## Fundraising Mechanics

### Priced Rounds vs. SAFEs

Startups can raise money on priced rounds (shares sold at a specific price) or unpriced/convertible rounds (money now, shares later). In the US, the typical sequence is: raise first money on SAFEs, then raise a priced round (Series A) later [1].

**SAFE** stands for Simple Agreement for Future Equity. It is a YC-created instrument that is genuinely simple -- a few pages long, no lawyers required, signable and trackable on Clerky [1]. The investor gives money now in exchange for the right to receive shares at a future priced round, with a valuation cap that rewards them for investing at an earlier, riskier stage.

**Example walkthrough** [1]: A company with 9 million founder shares raises $400K on SAFEs with a $4M cap. Later, it raises a $2M Series A at an $8M pre-money valuation. The SAFE conversion price uses the $4M cap ($0.44/share), while Series A investors pay based on the $8M valuation ($0.89/share). The SAFE investor gets twice as many shares per dollar as the Series A investor -- their reward for investing earlier.

**Priced round example** [1]: The same company raises $2M at $8M pre-money. Price per share = $8M / 9M shares = $0.89. The investor buys 2.25M shares. Post-money valuation is $10M. Each founder goes from 33% to 26.7% ownership. The investor holds 20%.

### Dilution

Dilution is inevitable. The key metric is the area of your slice, not the percentage [1]. "In the early days you have a massive slice of a very small pie. And then as the company grows, the size of the pie grows, but your slice becomes smaller" [1].

A common early mistake: selling too much equity at a low valuation when the company does not need the money. Most early spending goes to hiring, but if the company is still finding [[Product-Market Fit]], hiring may be premature [1].

### Get the Money in the Bank

Nathoo emphasizes a practical point that founders overlook: a commitment is not cash. "You've got to keep working on your investors until the money is in the bank" [1].

## Hiring Mechanics

### Contractors vs. Employees

Both contractors and employees assign IP to the company. The key differences [1]:

**Contractors**: Set their own hours, work on defined projects with end goals, use their own equipment, are not involved in day-to-day operations. The company pays them directly with no tax withholding. At year-end, the company issues a Form 1099. Contractors do not need US work authorization if working remotely [1].

**Employees**: Use company equipment, work company hours, have less autonomy, require more supervision. The company withholds taxes and pays them to the IRS, state, and sometimes city agencies. At year-end, the company issues a Form W-2. Employees must have US work authorization [1].

For payroll, Nathoo recommends using a payroll service provider rather than calculating withholdings manually. Gusto (a YC company) is the most commonly used by YC startups [1].

California law requires that employees be paid at least minimum wage. Unpaid labor producing work for the company is not legal [1].

### Employee Equity

Startups should give employees equity to incentivize alignment and compensate for below-market cash salaries [1].

**Rule of thumb**: Reserve approximately 10% of the company for the first 10 employees, on a sliding scale (employee #1 gets more than employee #10) [1]. For the first 5 employees, Nathoo suggests the 7-8% range [1].

All employee equity should vest on the same standard schedule as founder equity. This protects the company if a hire does not work out [1].

Companies typically issue stock options (the right to buy shares in the future at today's price) rather than outright shares to employees. This avoids immediate tax consequences for the employee [1].

**Communication matters**: Founders must understand equity themselves before explaining it to employees. Always communicate both the number of shares and the percentage of the company they represent. One without the other is meaningless [1].

### Spending Responsibly

Company funds belong to the company. Nathoo tells a story of a founder who took company money to Las Vegas, "had a great time according to Facebook," and was fired. Criminal charges were possible [1].

## References

1. [How to Start a Startup: Startup Mechanics](https://ycombinator.com/library/JR-how-to-start-a-startup-startup-mechanics) -- [[Kirsty Nathoo]] (2014)

title: Online Presence
body:
# Online Presence

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] discuss how founders should think about their online presence. Both have personal experience with social media companies -- Seibel co-founded Justin.tv/Twitch, and Caldwell is still involved with one -- and frame the issue as understanding the value exchange between users and platforms [1].

## The Social Media Matrix

Social media companies do not create content. They host platforms where users post things, which generates page views, which can carry ads. The business is simple: "It costs you nothing. You don't pay anyone to create those page views, and you make slivers of a penny on every ad that's displayed" [1]. The platform needs users to post a lot.

Caldwell frames the key question: "We're all living in this society where these very large and powerful businesses need us all to post a lot. And we have to ask ourselves the question: What is the value exchange?" [1].

## The Permanent Record

Three realities founders must internalize [1]:

**Everything you post is forever.** Seibel states the principle; Caldwell explains the mechanism. Every AI company has been trained on Reddit posts, Twitter posts, and blog posts. Caldwell: "Even if you think you delete it, there's real scrapers looking for stuff. And even if it gets deleted, it's in the database" [1].

**De-anonymization tools are improving.** Seibel raises this point, and Caldwell elaborates: language patterns, time-of-day posting, and other signals can identify anonymous posters. Seibel goes further: "You should be careful about your thoughts" -- not just your photos. Caldwell agrees: "Your thoughts can be used against you" [1].

**Your internet persona and your real-life persona are not separate.** Caldwell recalls thinking it was weird when Twitter first launched and people he met in person knew him from the internet. The split between "internet life" and "real life" is collapsing: "Things on the internet are real" [1].

Seibel states directly: "You have a permanent record on the internet. And to pretend like you don't, I would argue is being pretty naive" [1].

## The Strategic Upside

The permanent record is a double-edged coin [1]. If used strategically, it becomes an asset:

- **Build a corpus of quality work.** Thoughtful writing, essays, or code posted over time becomes discoverable and memorable. Caldwell cites [[Paul Graham]]'s essays as an example: written years ago, "he's still changing people's lives by his writings" [1].

- **GitHub as career infrastructure.** Seibel points out that "if you just keep posting good code to GitHub over a long amount of time, it's going to pay huge dividends for you in your career as a programmer." Recruiters already scrape GitHub to find engineering talent [1].

- **Meet interesting people.** Putting quality content on the internet -- anonymously or not -- can lead to connections that benefit your career and life [1].

## The Platform Is Indifferent

Caldwell describes the platform as "charitably, indifferent." As long as users generate ad views, the platform does not care whether the user is harming themselves [1]. "You have to care. The platform won't care for you" [1].

## Practical Guidelines

The partners offer several rules [1]:

1. **Post things you would be proud to defend.** Ask whether you would be comfortable explaining this post to your future employer, your kids, or a reporter.

2. **Showcase talents and thoughtful perspectives.** Writing, code, and demonstrated expertise create positive permanent records.

3. **Distinguish public from private thoughts.** "Sometimes there's time for public thoughts, and sometimes there's time for private thoughts" [1].

4. **Learn from others' mistakes, not your own.** The lesson about online reputational damage is "easy to learn post-getting burned" but unnecessary to learn the hard way. "You can just take people's word for it" [1].

## References

1. [How to Live in the Social Media Matrix](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/LU-how-to-live-in-the-social-media-matrix) — [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)

title: Startups for Students
body:
# Startups for Students

When Y Combinator launched in 2005, starting a startup was an unusual career path for recent graduates. It was hard to be taken seriously by investors without an MBA or years of business experience. TechCrunch published about 40 company launches per month in 2005, and YC received only a couple hundred applications for its first batches [1]. By 2021, YC received tens of thousands of applications per year, and on any given day Product Hunt shows dozens of new company launches [1].

## Why Start a Startup

### The Three Self-Assessment Questions

[[Michael Seibel]] recommends asking three questions to determine if a startup is right for you [1]:

1. **Do I like being the underdog?**
2. **Do I seek hard challenges most people shy away from?**
3. **Do I thrive taking personal responsibility for success or failure?**

Seibel writes: "There is a certain type of person who only works at their peak capacity when there is no predictable path to follow. The odds of success are low, and they have to take personal responsibility for failure" [1]. If you answer yes three times, a startup may be right for you.

### Find a Problem You Are Obsessed With Solving

Some of the most successful YC companies formed when founders confronted real-world issues [1]:

- **Airbnb** (2007): Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia were having trouble paying rent, so they bought air mattresses and charged people $80/night during a San Francisco convention
- **Stripe** (2010): Patrick and John Collison started a side project after Patrick became frustrated with how hard it was to accept financial payments online
- **Gusto**: Founded because the founders were frustrated by payroll inefficiencies for small businesses

The advice: look at your own life, identify a problem, and build a solution [1].

## Why Not to Start a Startup

### Legitimate Reasons

- **You are still in school.** Startups demand enormous time and energy. Grades and social life will suffer. Aaron Feurer, co-founder of Panorama Education, spent over 40 hours a week building the company as a Yale undergraduate and had amassed hundreds of thousands of dollars in revenue by graduation — but this is "very difficult to achieve and highly uncommon" [1].
- **It is difficult.** Nine out of 10 startups fail in their first year. Building a company is mentally and emotionally taxing [1].
- **You have no co-founder.** Only 7.9% of YC's top companies were solo-founded. See [[Co-Founders]] [1].
- **You are not ready for the commitment.** Exits and IPOs typically take years — prepare for up to a decade of work [1].
- **You need structure.** Large corporations provide managers as mentors and collaborative teams [1].
- **You hate uncertainty.** There is no clear linear path to success at a startup [1].

### Bad Reasons Not to Start

- **"I don't have an idea."** YC puts more emphasis on the team than the idea. Reddit, Brex, and Segment were all accepted as completely different concepts before pivoting [1].
- **"My parents want me to get a real job."** YC notes that *The Social Network* (2012) caused a massive spike in applications and may have been the cultural inflection point where parents realized startups could be a viable career. Point skeptical parents to companies founded by people in their 20s: Brex, Stripe, Embark, Scale AI — all now worth billions [1].
- **"I have student loans."** Student debt makes a well-paying corporate job more enticing, but startup experience is a force multiplier for earning potential even if the company fails [1].

## What to Do in College

### Skills to Develop

1. **Coding.** Learn to code and befriend technical people. You cannot build a software company without software [1].
2. **Communication.** Learn to convey ideas clearly and simply — even enormously complex ones should be explainable to grandparents [1].
3. **People skills.** Build your network, meet potential [[Co-Founders|co-founders]], and develop deep understanding of people's needs. Talking to users is a critical founder skill [1].

### How to Spend Time

College is one of the best places to meet a potential co-founder. Work on projects and extracurricular activities with classmates. Experts in technology, business, and science are usually happy to help college students [1].

YC tells most students to finish their degree. The exception: "if the only thing you can do is think about your startup and you're spending all your free time working on it, it might be worth considering dropping out" [1].

### Non-Technical Founders

YC has funded startups without a technical co-founder. Roughly 10% of companies in each batch are founded by non-technical founders. But among YC's top companies, only three were founded by non-technical founders: Flexport, Snapdocs, and UpKeep. YC strongly recommends finding a technical co-founder for software companies. Even non-technical founders should learn the basics of coding [1].

## Working at a Startup Instead

For those not ready to found, working at a startup — especially a YC startup — provides experience building products, talking to customers directly, and transparent views of the business [1].

### Choosing the Right Company

- Find a startup solving a problem interesting to you
- For internships, a post-Series A company is recommended for more structure and mentorship
- For self-directed learners, a seed-stage company could work

### Questions to Ask Founders

- What will I work on during this internship? (Bonus if the project ships)
- Will I get to see how the business is doing? How much interaction with customers?
- What will I learn from the people I am working with?
- For full-time roles: ask about runway and burn rate [1]

## Getting First Users as a Student

1. **College campus** — easy access to a large pool of potential customers likely to try something new [1]
2. **Friends and family** — helps determine ideal demographic [1]
3. **Charge as soon as possible** — even small amounts help validate how much value you are creating [1]

## Applying to YC

YC accepts applications year-round. No specific school, degree, or business plan is required. The application is free. About half of the Summer 2021 batch was internationally based [1].

What YC looks for [1]:
- **Technical ability** — teams that can build, usually meaning a technical founder
- **A drive to build** — about 60% of funded companies do not have a launched product, but founders show inclination for action and fast progress
- **Persistence and resilience** — about 50% of funded companies have applied more than once; showing progress between applications signals seriousness

## References

1. [Startups for Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/D0-startups-for-students) — Tamanna Khemani, Kat Mañalac (2021)

title: Value Creation
body:
# Value Creation

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] argue that the fundamental business equation underlying all technological progress is simple: sell someone a tool, and if they can use the tool to make more money than it costs them, you are creating value [1].

## The Basic Equation

Seibel provides the examples [1]:

**Google Ads**: When Google first launched ads, buyers paid per click and could sell products for far more than the click cost. "Very straightforward. Makes sense" [1].

**Microsoft Office**: Seibel notes that Word and Excel made office and financial workers literally 10 to 100 times more productive. Caldwell adds that a computer costing five or ten thousand dollars was still massively positive ROI because spreadsheets were that transformative [1].

## The Common Failure Mode

Seibel describes the common failure mode: many founders build products that deliver no or minimal value to the user. The pattern: the founder believes the most important thing is to grow in order to raise money, so they build something that "doesn't solve anyone's problem and just try to get money for it" [1].

Caldwell calls this "make something VCs want" -- a perversion of the YC motto. "Superficially this resembles a software product that superficially people pay for" but the specifics of what it does are treated as details [1].

## The Fear of Being a Consultant

Caldwell identifies a deeper failure: founders resist deeply understanding customer problems because of a fear of becoming a consultant [1].

When a user describes their specific problem, the founder fears: what if this user is different from every other user? What if I have to build something just for them? Caldwell notes that every founder, "in the back of their head, thank you Reid Hoffman, is thinking, when can I get to blitzscale?" If they are not building a simple thing everyone uses, it will not scale [1].

Seibel agrees there is a kernel of truth in the fear. But Caldwell argues it is "exactly wrong when you're early" [1]. Before you deeply understand the customer's problems, your assumptions about how their business works may be mistaken. Caldwell's counter-advice: "Forget your fear about being a consultant early. Dig in with some customers. Actually solve their problem" [1].

In the process, you will learn about the actual problem, develop insights about what is generalizable, and understand the levers in their business that drive economic value. Caldwell adds: "You can even say: how can this help you make more money? Sometimes founders are afraid of asking the dumb question, but that's a worthwhile question to ask" [1].

## Give Value in Outreach, Not Just Products

Seibel extends the principle to customer outreach. Most founder emails ask for more than they give: "Hey, I'm building XYZ software. Do you have 20 minutes to jump on the phone with me to talk about it?" [1].

One YC company found a better approach: they recorded videos analyzing each prospect's customer onboarding flow and provided specific advice for improvement. This was consulting. It did not scale. But when they sent these emails, prospects replied: "Thank you, I can use that. Yeah, I'm happy to talk to you" [1].

## The History of Generosity in Tech

Caldwell provides the historical context. The history of computing involves giving away enormous amounts of value for free [1]:

- The original business model of computer companies was to sell hardware; software was given away free to make the hardware useful
- Bill Gates pioneered proprietary software in the late 1970s
- The free software movement created GCC, Linux, every web browser engine (Gecko, WebKit), HTML, SQLite, and Postgres
- "There'd be no Google, there'd be no YouTube without Linux powering all the stuff" [1]
- "Every web browser, Gecko, WebKit, HTML" -- all free
- SQLite is "in everything. I'll bet to just watch this video stream, SQLite is involved like 50 different ways" [1]

The history of software is a history of giving more value than creators received. This dynamic is why technological progress accelerates: "If you take some of their inspiration when you're building your products, maybe you can have some wild success" [1].

## The Core Insight

Caldwell summarizes: "If you leave a lot of the value on the table for your customers instead of trying to take it all, then you're in the game" [1]. But the prerequisite is that "your product actually has to solve the customer's problem." All those free products, and all the products people pay for, actually work. "If you can do that in the early stage, instead of being afraid of consulting or afraid of fundraising, bam, you have an advantage" [1].

## References

1. [How Generosity Built Tech Giants](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IO-dalton-michael-how-generosity-built-tech-giants) — [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)

title: Pitch Deck Design
body:
# Pitch Deck Design

[[Kevin Hale]], YC partner, teaches a presentation to YC startups before they create their Demo Day slide decks [1]. His core argument is that founders already have everything they need to create a great presentation. The challenge is not finding things to say but communicating them so investors can understand and remember.

## The Fundamental Constraint

There are probably 100 reasons why a company is great, but people can only remember a few after a short presentation. YC helps each startup identify the five to seven most important ideas investors should know. On Demo Day, with over 100 companies presenting, investors will be lucky to remember even one or two points from any given company [1].

The standard is not creativity or flair. If a founder just communicates their points clearly, they will do better than 99% of startups. Before anyone can remember, they have to understand [1].

## The Three Principles: Legible, Simple, Obvious

Hale's framework for making ideas easy to understand is the [[Legible Simple Obvious]] framework [1]:

![Legible, Simple, Obvious](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/35131887eed3.png)

- **Legible**: Even old people sitting in the back row with bad eyesight can read it.
- **Simple**: Each slide expresses one idea.
- **Obvious**: Can be understood at a glance.

The inverse produces failure: illegible, complicated, and subtle slides [1].

![Illegible, Complicated, Subtle](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/7f82892467cb.png)

### Legibility

At Demo Day, the room fills with over 500 people. They cannot all sit in the front row, and many are older with imperfect eyesight. Legible slides use large type, bold text, a simple font, and good contrast from the background. Text at the top of the slide is easier to read from the back [1].

![Legibility principles](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/fb3a8a6a0309.png)

Hale shows an evolution of the same slide through three versions. The first has text with poor contrast and bad positioning:

![Slide with poor contrast](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/6e447d5bfc3f.png)

The second improves but is still constrained by a screenshot:

![Improved but constrained](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/fa95fa8743e4.png)

The third strips away the screenshot and makes the text dominant and readable from anywhere in the room [1]:

![Final legible version](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/bf6463530e43.png)

He uses an example from Shred Video (YC company): their Prototype Day slide had text that was "too small and too skinny," with the explanation of what they actually do hidden "in light gray at the bottom of the slide." Their technology -- software that automatically edits hours of raw video footage into professional-looking edits in seconds -- was buried:

![Shred Video Prototype Day slide](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/ac4165ec4f6c.png)

By Demo Day, the description was simplified and the text was "large and in charge" [1].

### Simplicity

The word "simple" shares an origin with "complex." The root "plex" means braid, twist, or fold. Simple ideas are "one fold" -- one idea. A simple slide expresses one idea. Crowding a slide with multiple ideas makes it complex [1].

![Simple vs Complex etymology](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/c05df6011960.png)

![Plex means braid, twist, fold](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/8aaef1a06fa4.png)

Since there are only five to seven key ideas, there should be only about five to seven slides:

![5-7 slides](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/25c73d797368.png)

Hale shows a slide from Afrostream (YC company) that tried to pack all the nuances of their business onto one slide, resulting in too much text that no one could easily remember:

![Afrostream early version](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/f43a60dfdede.png)

Their Demo Day version was "short, sweet, and easy to recollect" [1]:

![Afrostream Demo Day version](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/3394a3d6fc83.jpg)

### Obviousness

Obvious slides can be understood at a glance. The test: show it to a stranger and ask them what it means. If they don't immediately say your idea, you lose [1].

![Obvious slides test](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/375759af9166.png)

Speed of understanding matters because Demo Day presentations are 2 minutes and 30 seconds, and investors are easily distracted. "If they don't get your point right away, they will check their email" [1].

To make slides obvious, Hale advises making them legible and simple first, then making the idea explicit. He shows a growth graph without labels -- the viewer can guess it represents growth but it is not immediately clear:

![Growth graph without labels](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/9531057ae80a.png)

Adding axis labels helps:

![Growth graph with labels](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/93712f10cafe.png)

But adding a direct caption like "10x growth in 6 months" makes it unmistakable. "It's like I put CliffsNotes right on the slide" [1]:

![Growth graph with caption](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/0f3b663f4851.png)

Another variation from a YC startup, where the caption eliminates the need to study the graph:

![Graph with explanatory caption](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/8d68036b1cb5.png)

Hale references [Hick's Law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hick%27s_law): each piece of information added to a problem increases the time to make a decision logarithmically. A Dropbox diagram illustrates excessive information:

![Dropbox diagram](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/e297b4cf14ba.png)

Each piece of information in the diagram creates comprehension overhead:

![Information overload annotated](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/d2bf3d3fa7dd.png)

Diagrams are "little mazes for ideas" that make the path to understanding too long [1].

**Distractions to avoid** [1]:
- Too much text
- Excessive explanations and caveats
- Excessive branding per slide
- Photos with no titles or captions
- Animations, transitions
- Memes
- Subtle humor, accidental humor

"Investors invest in teams not slides. Your slides should make your ideas more clear. Don't let your slides distract investors from what you're saying out loud" [1].

![Investors invest in teams not slides](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/9da12d19d674.png)

### The Exception: Deliberate Complexity

One exception exists: when the point is to show complexity or overwhelm the audience, usually to illustrate how hard a problem is. Hale uses Magic (YC company) as an example. Magic's software abstracts thousands of on-demand services into a single interface:

![Magic single interface](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/99c0944f2327.png)

Their slide showed a grid of every on-demand service they could find across every industry, market, and vertical. Each logo represented a separate account a user would need, illustrating the discovery and usability problem:

![Magic grid of services](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/0c0085e4610a.png)

"It's overwhelming for a reason and that's usually when it's okay to bend the rules" [1].

## On Screenshots

Hale states he "usually hates screenshots in Demo Day presentations." Screenshots are almost always illegible (interface text is too small), complex (interfaces do multiple things), and non-obvious (take longer than a glance to understand). "They break all 3 rules" [1].

A Google image search for "screenshots" demonstrates the problem:

![Screenshots search](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/0124f324ff52.png)

Instead of showing what a product does through screenshots, Hale recommends showing the most simplified version. A bulleted list of steps is his favorite technique. He cites SparkGift's slide explaining their product through three simple steps as "far better at explaining the ease of use" than any workflow from the actual interface:

![SparkGift simplified steps](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/5bfdee5c72af.png)

Meadow's two-point explanation of their product was "far better than any combination of buttons, links and chrome they could have put up there":

![Meadow simplified explanation](../raw/posts/images/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck/38414710dbcc.png)

The same caution applies to screencasts and videos [1].

## References

1. [How to Design a Better Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck) — [[Kevin Hale]] (December 2015)

title: Product Development
body:
# Product Development

[[Michael Seibel]] describes a product development process that he developed at Socialcam (previously Justin.tv) after years of running a broken process [1]. The broken version had meandering product meetings where decisions were not written down, specs were loose enough that team members had different ideas about what was being built, the team always wanted to build fully-formed products instead of MVPs, analytics were rarely specified for new features, development cycles ran months, and product decisions were made exclusively by founders in a non-transparent process [1].

This guide assumes the company has already shipped an MVP and is figuring out what to do next, which is where most startups spend most of their time [1].

## Define the Development Cycle

The cycle length should be dictated by the product. At Socialcam, building for iOS, the team settled on a two-week cycle, which allowed thorough testing before App Store releases. Web apps can use shorter cycles; hardware might need longer ones. The key constraint is that teammates must stay excited and still feel like they can brainstorm new ideas [1].

## The Product Meeting

Seibel's team ran one and only one recurring team meeting: the product meeting, held on the first day of each development cycle. These meetings sometimes lasted five hours [1].

Every product meeting focused on exactly one of three goals [1]:

1. Increasing content creation
2. Increasing new users
3. Increasing retention

Whichever goal was chosen became the focus of the meeting and the next two weeks. The product lead's role was to protect and improve the development cycle and moderate the meeting to ensure all team members felt comfortable contributing. "Oftentimes just getting the opportunity to voice your idea and having it written on the board -- even if it isn't built -- massively increases buy-in of the process" [1].

## Organized and Inclusive Brainstorm

During brainstorming, ideas were written on the whiteboard in three categories: new features/feature iterations, maintenance, and A/B tests. Everyone was expected to contribute. Debates or putting down other people's ideas was not permitted. This was the time for free contribution without fear of judgment [1].

Each brainstormed item was graded by the engineer in the meeting with the most experience in that specific area [1]:

- **Easy**: Several can be done in a day
- **Medium**: Half a day for one person
- **Hard**: Most of the development cycle

No item could be so hard it would spill into the next cycle. If it was, the team broke it into smaller chunks. This grading system had a critical side effect: it helped non-technical people understand which of their ideas were easy to build and which were hard. "With this realization they often got better at thinking up easier and easier MVPs of their ideas. These easy ideas would then get built and, if they worked, would be iterated upon" [1].

## Building Consensus

The team picked what to work on through consensus, starting with hard ideas (easy to reach consensus because only one could fit), then medium, then easy. Consensus was not difficult because everyone had contributed their own ideas, there was a clear goal, and difficulty estimates were objective. "This process allowed you to grade the quality of your own idea and didn't allow personalities to bully their pet ideas through" [1].

## Clear Spec and Measurements

After consensus, each item was specced in detail, assigned to team members, and paired with the specific stats needed to measure effectiveness. The team would never release a feature without releasing the analytics for that feature and understanding what specific measurable result they wanted to create. Need-to-haves were separated from nice-to-haves; if time ran short, nice-to-haves were dropped [1].

After speccing, the team photographed the whiteboard and erased it. There was no product roadmap outside of these two weeks. Every product meeting started from scratch with a new goal, new analytics data from the last two weeks, and often new insights from monthly in-person user testing [1].

## Working During the Cycle

After the first Monday, work was quiet. The product lead handled business and operations tasks, then dug through Mixpanel looking for product insights or potential bugs, and ran monthly user testing sessions. Engineers and the designer worked quietly on well-scoped, well-specced projects [1].

During the last three days of every cycle, the entire team stopped building and tested. They maintained a testing list in Excel with manual tests for all basic functionality, adding tests for new features each cycle and running all tests twice. Everyone tested, and competitions for fastest testing and most bugs found helped share the burden of an inherently tedious task [1].

## Results

Socialcam did not achieve the team's dream of being the "Instagram for Video" (Seibel notes that what they should have built looks closer to Snapchat). But the process allowed extremely fast iteration: video filters, borders, titles, soundtracks, feed optimizations, multiple visual redesigns, user profiles, recommended channels, front/back camera switching, and more. Growth experiments produced 16 million downloads in about three months and over 100 million people watching video on their website during the same period. The work was done "quickly, efficiently, without major arguments, issues with founder commitment, or really any team problems at all" [1].

## References

1. [Guide to Product Development](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4e-guide-to-product-development) — [[Michael Seibel]] (August 2016)

title: Business Models
body:
# Business Models

A business model is how a company makes money. [[Aaron Epstein]], YC Group Partner and founder of Creative Market, analyzed the YC Top 100 companies list and found that nearly every billion-dollar company uses one of nine proven business models. Rather than inventing a new way to make money, founders should copy a proven model and innovate on their product [1].

## The Nine Business Models

### SaaS (Software as a Service)

Cloud-based subscription software. Customers pay monthly or annually. Primary metrics: MRR/ARR, growth rate, net revenue retention, and customer acquisition cost (CAC). SaaS benefits from recurring revenue predictability and is usually sold to businesses, ideally on annual contracts [1].

### Transactional

Companies that facilitate transactions and take a cut. Often fintech and payments businesses. Primary metrics: gross transaction value (GTV), net revenue, user retention, CAC. The best transactional businesses have extremely consistent revenue from high repeat usage. One-time transactions rather than recurring, often high volume with a low fee (1-3% is common) [1].

### Marketplaces

Facilitate transactions between buyers and sellers. Primary metrics: gross merchandise value (GMV), net revenue, growth rate, user retention. Hard to get off the ground due to the chicken-and-egg problem of needing both supply and demand simultaneously. But when they work, [[Network Effects]] at scale drive exponential growth and they often become dominant winner-take-all players [1].

### Subscription

Products or services sold on a recurring basis, usually to consumers. Similar metrics to SaaS (MRR/ARR, growth rate, user retention, CAC) but typically lower price points from a higher volume of customers. Growth driven by scalable, self-serve acquisition channels [1].

### Enterprise

Large fixed-term contracts sold to big companies (5,000+ employees). Primary metrics: bookings, recognized revenue, annual contract value (ACV), and pipeline (top of funnel to demo to close). Very few customers, much larger deals ($100K+/year). Long sales cycles with many gatekeepers. The buyer is not always the end user. Growth is lumpy -- measuring month-over-month growth rate is less meaningful [1].

### Usage-Based

Pay-as-you-go based on consumption. Primary metrics: monthly revenue (not recurring), growth rate, revenue retention, gross margin. Epstein warns: do not confuse usage-based revenue with recurring revenue. Charges per API request, number of records, data usage, or similar. Products and pricing scale from tiny startups to large enterprises [1].

### E-Commerce

Selling products online. Primary metrics: monthly revenue, growth rate, gross margin/unit economics, CAC. Includes D2C brands and Shopify stores. Not marketplaces -- the company keeps 100% of each sale. Higher COGS means lower margins. Products are often commoditized. Must excel at user acquisition and operations [1].

### Advertising

Sell ads to monetize free users. Primary metrics: DAU, MAU, user retention, CPM/CPC. Typically consumer social products with huge scale. The customer is the advertiser, not the end user. "Users are the product being sold." Need billions of impressions each month. Registered users is a vanity metric [1].

### Hard Tech / Bio / Moonshots

Hard businesses with lots of technical risk and long time horizons. Primary metrics: milestones (progress toward vision), signed contracts, letters of intent (LOIs). Often take years to reach a live product because of technical or regulatory risk. Revenue is often years away, so LOIs are the best way to show customer interest [1].

## Lessons from the YC Top 100

Epstein matched each of the top 100 YC companies to its primary business model and found several patterns [1]:

### SaaS Dominates the List

31% of the top 100 are SaaS businesses -- nearly a third. Recurring revenue makes them great businesses: customers keep paying until they explicitly stop, producing predictable, compounding growth [1].

### Transactional Businesses Are in the Flow of Funds

22% of the top 100 are transactional. They outperform because they are directly in the flow of funds. Stripe processes money; Brex is the corporate card companies use to spend. "If you're a company like Stripe that literally processes money for companies, then you're directly in that flow of funds. And so it's really easy to take your cut" [1].

Epstein received this advice during his own YC batch in 2010: "Get as close to the transaction as possible." Companies far from the transaction (affiliates, for example) depend on multiple intermediary steps before getting paid [1].

Transactional businesses become critical infrastructure. "The thought of ripping that out sounds terrible" -- which is why churn is low [1].

### Marketplaces Create the Most Value per Company

14% of the top 100 are marketplaces, but they create 30% of the overall value. Five of the top 10 YC companies by value are marketplaces: Airbnb, Instacart, DoorDash, OpenSea, and Fair. Once marketplaces hit their inflection point, network effects make them dominant -- each new user increases the value for everyone else [1].

### Advertising Businesses Rarely Make the List

Only 3% of the top 100 use advertising as their primary model. They need organic virality to win -- catching lightning in a bottle. "You should not use ads as your primary business model unless you expect to be a top 10 site on the internet" [1].

### The Power Law Applies Within the Top 100

50% of the overall value of the top 100 comes from just the top 10. The power law means the biggest winners outperform all others by orders of magnitude [1].

## What Is Not on the List

Several business types are absent from the top 100 [1]:

- **Services/consulting**: Non-recurring revenue, scales with people not software, low margins. Starting with consulting to learn is fine, but consulting businesses are not venture scale.
- **Affiliate businesses**: Too far from the transaction. Multiple things must happen before you get paid.
- **Hardware businesses**: Require lots of capital, have low margins. Hard to start and scale.
- **Platform-dependent businesses**: High platform risk. "If your business is built on top of another big successful platform and your business starts to work, then it's in the interest of that platform to shut you down."

## Recurring Revenue Wins

Recurring revenue consistently creates winners because [1]:
- It is highly predictable
- Higher customer lifetime values vs. one-off transactions
- Lower customer acquisition costs (no need to re-acquire existing customers)

But recurring revenue only works with strong retention. Epstein illustrates: with 95% monthly retention, starting with 100 customers, only 54 remain after one year. With 90% monthly retention, only 28 remain. "Just that five percent difference in monthly retention can actually be the difference between life and death for a startup" [1]. See [[Cohort Retention]] for how to measure and improve this.

## Moats Build Winners

The biggest winners are built with defensible moats [1]:
- **Network effects**: Each new user increases value (marketplaces)
- **Lock-in and switching costs**: Stripe as payment processor; customer data on SaaS platforms
- **Recurring revenue**: Customers keep paying; inertia works in your favor
- **Technical innovation**: Cruise (self-driving), Boom (supersonic jets) -- years of development create barriers
- **Economies of scale**: DoorDash, Instacart have driven costs down at scale that new entrants cannot match
- **Organic distribution**: Virality and word of mouth. Companies growing organically can outpace competitors who must pay for every customer

## The Core Formula

Epstein summarizes: "The best businesses generate recurring revenue, have high retention, build defensible moats, are as close to the transaction as possible, they scale with software not people, and they're proven and use business models that are familiar to customers." Innovate on the product, copy the business model [1].

## Pricing Insights

See [[Pricing and Monetization]] and [[Startup Pricing]] for detailed guidance. Epstein contributes five insights from the YC top companies [1]:

### 1. You Should Charge

The most common mistake: founders afraid to charge. Charging teaches you whether users will pay, which segments are most willing, and how much value they see. Even universal refusal is valuable information [1].

**The Stripe example**: Early Stripe set prices at 5% per transaction -- nearly double competitors charging 3% -- to test how much value customers placed on one-click signup and developer-friendly API documentation. "Rather than trying to undercut the competition, they did the exact opposite and set a really high bar" [1].

### 2. Price on Value, Not Cost

Cost-plus pricing (cost to serve + markup) ignores the full value customers perceive. The gap between price and perceived value is the opportunity to raise prices. If cost exceeds price, margins are negative. If price exceeds value, customers will not buy [1].

To find value, ask users: "What problem were you hoping our product could solve?" Their answers typically fall into four categories: make more money, reduce costs, move faster, or avoid risk [1].

Alternatively, keep incrementally raising prices until customers complain but still pay. "The ideal scenario is when you tell the customer a price, they say they have to think about it, they go back, and then they come back a week later and say, 'All right, that seems good'" [1].

### 3. Most Startups Are Undercharging

Lower prices are not a sustainable advantage. "All that means is that your large competitor can underprice you even way lower than your cost because they have way more money." Pricing implies value: if your price is lower than competitors, customers may assume your product is less valuable [1].

Raising prices is the easiest way to grow revenue: "Changing a number on the website" vs. acquiring the same number of customers again [1].

### 4. Pricing Is Not Permanent

Common fear: nailing the price the first time. Strategies for raising prices: exclude existing customers (grandfather them), or give advance notice. Netflix has raised prices repeatedly across 221 million subscribers. "If Netflix is able to find a way to increase their prices on 221 million customers, you should be able to figure out how to do it on your handful of early customers" [1].

### 5. Keep It Simple

Complex pricing pages with five buy buttons and crossed-out prices create friction that decreases conversion. GitLab's three clear plans with simple pricing is the model to follow [1].

**The Segment Story**: Segment started free, then charged $120/year. Customers responded: "I hope you would charge me more than that. Otherwise, I'm worried about keeping my customer data with you." A sales advisor told them to charge $120,000/year. Their CEO nervously quoted that price; the customer countered at $12,000; they settled at $18,000. A 150x price increase because they asked. They continued growing deal sizes to six figures and beyond, leading to a $3 billion acquisition by Twilio [1].

## References

1. [YC Guide to Business Models](https://ycombinator.com/library/Gh-yc-guide-to-business-models) -- [[Aaron Epstein]] (n.d.)

title: Bootstrapping vs. Venture Capital
body:
# Bootstrapping vs. Venture Capital

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] argue that the bootstrap-versus-VC debate is a "confusingly controversial topic" that is, in reality, not much of a debate at all [1]. The vast majority of businesses should not raise venture capital. Venture capital is not interested in investing in the vast majority of businesses. Neither of these facts is controversial among people who understand how venture capital works [1].

## The Scale of the Distinction

The percentage of businesses started each year that receive venture funding is in the single digits, possibly less than 1% [1]. The mismatch between reality and perception arises because VC-backed companies dominate media coverage, YouTube content, and Twitter discourse. Caldwell observes: "If you're just consuming content, watching media, watching YouTube, Twitter, you don't realize that" VC-backed companies are a "weird outlier, freak occurrence" [1].

Seibel identifies a specific source of confusion: shows like Shark Tank portray fundraising in a way that has "nothing to do with reality" in the venture capital industry. "If you're starting a business that can appear on Shark Tank, for the most part you probably shouldn't raise venture capital" [1].

## Venture Capital as a Product

Venture capital exists for a specific purpose: investing in something where the investment could be worth 100x to 1,000x more [1]. Seibel frames the consequence plainly: "Trying to put that jet fuel into something that isn't going to grow to be big, everyone is going to be sad and lose" [1]. No one wins -- not the investor, not the founder, not the users [1].

Caldwell extends the point: "Starting a VC-backed company is really hard. Not the best way to get rich. Not the best path." He compares it to professional athletics: "It's like getting into the NBA is really hard. Like, not like the best path to have a good career is to be like, 'Oh, I'll just make the NBA'" [1].

Most people who are rich did not raise venture capital. Real estate investing, the stock market, law, medicine, and finance all produce more wealthy people than venture-backed startups [1].

## Bootstrapping Is a Win

Caldwell and Seibel are explicit that building a profitable non-VC-backed software business is winning. Seibel shares a personal example: a friend who built a product generating $30,000 to $50,000 per month that required only 7-10 hours per month of maintenance. "The entire time I was working on my startup, he was living life. I would watch his life on Facebook and be like, 'This is amazing. He traveled the world, he got married, he had kids'" [1]. Seibel admits: "97 percent of the time I was watching that Facebook, I was like, 'Am I the idiot?'" [1].

Caldwell also defines bootstrapping in a separate talk: starting a company using personal funds or the business's revenue, without raising venture capital [2]. He identifies it as a "great option" for businesses expected to reach $5 to $10 million per year in revenue and likely not grow beyond that. "It's entirely within your control. You don't need the approval of an investor to go bootstrap a company" [2].

## When VC Makes Sense

Seibel identifies the specific conditions under which venture capital is the right tool: "There are some businesses where you actually need money upfront." If a software business needs millions of dollars or tens of millions of dollars to reach break-even, "there aren't other mechanisms in our economy to give that to you" [1].

Caldwell uses Google as an example: "I don't know how you could have bootstrapped that." The founders of Google could not have bought the servers and built the infrastructure without outside capital [1].

Seibel frames VC as enabling incremental entrepreneurship that would not exist otherwise -- "not as cannibalizing anything" [1]. The transaction is straightforward: "I give you money now. You give me a lot more money later, or at least you try really hard to" [1]. If the business cannot plausibly return a lot of money, the transaction does not make sense for either side [1].

## The Empirical Record

Seibel states a fact: "Empirically, none of the trillion-dollar software companies are bootstrapped with no VC dollars ever" [1]. This does not mean founders should aspire to build trillion-dollar companies. It means that if someone claims bootstrapping can produce outcomes at that scale, the historical record does not support the claim [1].

## The Decision Is Revisitable

Caldwell notes that the bootstrap-vs-VC decision is not permanent. "If you don't want to start a VC-backed company now, but your bootstrap product starts doing well and you want to do it in the future, great. You can. There's no one who is going to say, 'No, you said you were bootstrapped on Twitter. I will not give you money'" [1].

## The Fake Controversy

Caldwell argues the entire debate is "fake news" -- a manufactured controversy that serves content creators who monetize engagement. "I don't think anyone thinks it's more moral or good or right to start a VC-backed company or a non-VC-backed company. I don't think this is an actual debate" [1].

Seibel identifies the pattern that fuels outrage: people point to a silly company that raised a lot of money and ask, "If they funded that, why won't they fund my social network for dogs?" Caldwell's response: "Don't fall for the rage bait. Don't let yourself get enraged by obvious engagement bait" [1]. The person who funded that silly company probably made a mistake. That does not invalidate the logic of venture capital [1].

Seibel also notes that wanting to raise venture capital should come from an internal drive, not from external pressure: "That should come from inside of you, not from YouTube." Caldwell agrees: "If you need to be talked into it, that's a bad sign" [1].

## References

1. [Should Your Startup Bootstrap or Raise Venture Capital?](https://ycombinator.com/library/KZ-should-your-startup-bootstrap-or-raise-venture-capital) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2024)
2. [Starting A Company? The Key Terms You Should Know](https://ycombinator.com/library/Lk-starting-a-company-the-key-terms-you-should-know) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (2024)

title: Handling Investor Rejection
body:
# Handling Investor Rejection

YC group partners [[Michael Seibel]], [[Harj Taggar]], and [[Brad Flora]] state that one of their most common activities is "pumping belief back into founders who have had their confidence shaken by investor rejections" [1]. The YC mantra for handling rejection is: **"Believe the no. Don't believe the why."**

## Why Founders Overweight Investor Feedback

Taggar describes the asymmetry: founders are the genuine experts -- they think about their product, market, and customers day in and day out. Yet a single 30-minute Zoom call with an investor, "while like on Slack, doing email, maybe not the most engaged," can shake everything [1].

The lies founders tell themselves after a rejection [1]:

1. **"This investor is an expert."** They follow the founder on Twitter, they tweet about the market, they worked at a company in the space. "Therefore, they know everything about what I'm doing, and whatever they told me in that rejection email, that must be the reason and the problem with my startup" [1].

2. **"If I address their concern, they'll invest."** Founders believe they can convince an investor to change their mind with a follow-up email or another call. Taggar is blunt: "It's never up for debate really" [1].

3. **"The reason they gave is the real reason."** When an investor says the market is too small, the founder fixates on market size -- even when the real issues are more fundamental: no technical co-founder, negative margins, about to run out of money, no growth since launch, or building a non-software product for a software-focused investor [1].

## The Danger of Acting on Investor Feedback

Flora highlights the paradox: an investor spends two minutes writing a rejection email. Later, the founder has "pivoted your entire company because of it. Not a huge signal of conviction" [1].

Seibel describes a worse scenario: a founder comes to YC saying an investor suggested they pursue a different market, so they are pivoting. The partners' reaction: "I wish I could explain to you how this is gonna make you less attractive. Investors are aware they're not experts. If a founder actually acts on your feedback in a material way, it makes you less likely to want to invest. You don't want to invest in someone who is relying on you for product insights" [1].

## When the Same Feedback Comes from Everyone

When 23 out of 25 investors give the same reason for rejection, the advice remains nuanced. Seibel: "The takeaway still isn't 'believe that.' It's 'think about it. Assess it. Dig into it'" [1]. Do not take it at face value, but do not ignore it either. Write it down. But "don't internalize it and absorb it into your mindset for the company. You should know about it, but not soak it in" [1].

Taggar describes his personal algorithm: "The first time they tell me there's a rejection and a why, I ignore it completely. If they've gotten 15, 20, 25 rejections, I then ask, 'Are the whys consistent?' And only if then the whys are consistent do I even think this is a data point to be considered at all" [1]. This requires pitching many investors -- one or two rejections produce no useful signal.

## The Real Reasons Investors Say No

The partners identify the actual (usually unspoken) reasons investors reject founders [1]:

**The founder does not seem like a leader.** Flora: "You sit across from the person, and in the course of the conversation, you think, 'I have a really hard time seeing this person building and running and leading a large organization'" [1]. This does not mean the founder must be that person today, but they must seem like they are aspiring to become one [1].

**Pattern matching and stack ranking.** Seibel makes the underlying mechanics explicit: investors do two things. First, they pattern match: "Do you pattern match to founders I've had success with in the past?" Second, they stack rank: "Of all the founders I've worked with and are currently pitching me, where do you rank?" [1]. The honest but brutal reason is often "you might be like the kind of founders I've done well with, but you're just not as good as the other ones I'm talking to right now" [1].

**Communication failures.** Taggar notes that the reason can be as simple as "you're not communicating clearly. I don't really understand what you're saying" [1]. Flora adds: "I don't believe your numbers. You gave me conflicting numbers and cast some doubt" [1]. These are self-inflicted wounds that are fixable.

## Founders Change Too

Seibel gives investors credit: founders improve between meetings. "You might reject a company when the founders haven't actually gone out and spoke to any users, or they're two weeks into it. But then a month of real work, they come back, and the same founders can just seem different. They've got real insights. They see more. Their confidence has grown" [1].

This is why 60% of the companies that do YC were rejected at some point in the past [1]. Seibel doubts any other fund, school, or selective institution looks back at failed applicants and gives them another try [1].

## The Practical Takeaway: Change the Facts

The partners converge on a single piece of advice: change the facts of the business, not the words of the pitch [1].

Taggar advises founders to keep a list of investors who said no but seemed reasonable, and send them monthly updates. "Shockingly, if things start going well, those same investors will reach out, change their mind" [1]. But founders must understand: "You can't convince them to change their mind with words, but you can with actions" [1].

Seibel illustrates the disconnect: "The founder says, 'What do I say to this investor to get them to change their mind?' And we say, 'Tell them about the 10 new customers you just closed.' And it takes a second for that to land, realizing that they need to go get 10 more customers and then tell them about it. Not just write something different back to the investor" [1].

Flora frames it as the secret behind YC's model: "With multiple applications, we can see progress. And progress is exciting to invest in" [1].

## References

1. [Investors Said No, Now What?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/J1-inside-the-group-partner-lounge-investors-said-no-now-what) -- [[Harj Taggar]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Brad Flora]] (2021)

title: Growth
body:
# Growth

Growth is the defining characteristic of a startup. [[Paul Graham]] argues that a startup is not merely a newly founded company or one that works on technology -- it is a company designed to grow fast [1]. Everything else associated with startups -- venture funding, exits, technology focus -- follows from this single property. [[Sam Altman]] adds a critical caveat: growth is not step one. Founders who focus on growth before building something people love are unlikely to succeed [2].

## Growth as the Defining Property of a Startup

Graham draws a sharp distinction between startups and ordinary businesses. A barbershop is not a startup. A search engine is. The difference is not about technology but about growth potential. For a company to grow fast, it must (a) make something lots of people want and (b) reach and serve all those people [1]. Most businesses are constrained in one or both dimensions. Startups are the rare companies that are not.

This distinction matters because it shapes founder behavior. "Startup is a pole, not a threshold," Graham writes. Starting one is a declaration of ambition -- a commitment to search for one of the rare ideas that generates rapid growth [1].

The growth of a successful startup follows an S-curve with three phases: an initial period of slow or no growth while the startup figures out what it is doing; a period of rapid growth as it finds [[Product-Market Fit]] and reaches its audience; and an eventual slowdown as internal limits and market saturation set in [1]. The second phase -- the ascent -- is what defines the startup. Its length and slope determine how big the company becomes.

He sees startup founding as analogous to scientific research: "A startup founder is in effect an economic research scientist. Most don't discover anything that remarkable, but some discover relativity" [1].

## Growth Rate as a Compass

Graham advocates using a target growth rate as a decision-making compass. At YC, growth is measured weekly, partly because the batch is short and partly because early-stage startups need frequent feedback [1].

Benchmarks during YC:
- **5-7% per week**: a good growth rate
- **10% per week**: exceptionally well
- **1% per week**: a sign you have not yet figured out what you are doing [1]

The best metric to measure growth is revenue. For startups not yet charging, active users serve as a reasonable proxy because revenues will likely be a constant multiple of active users [1]. Getting the pricing right is itself a growth lever -- see [[Pricing and Monetization]] for when and how to start charging.

The compounding effect of small differences in weekly growth rate is dramatic:

![Growth rate compounding](../raw/posts/images/8s-startup-growth/03a34df22371.png)

| Weekly Growth Rate | Annual Multiple | 4-Year Outcome (from $1K/mo) |
|---|---|---|
| 1% | 1.7x | $7,900/month |
| 5% | 12.6x | $25 million/month |

Graham notes: "Our ancestors must rarely have encountered cases of exponential growth, because our intuitions are no guide here" [1].

Setting a weekly growth target converts the bewildering problem of starting a startup into an optimization problem. "Anything that gets you the growth you need is ipso facto right," Graham writes -- should you attend a conference, hire a programmer, focus on marketing, court a big customer? Whatever hits the number [1]. This narrow focus works because founders' intuitions about which hill to climb are usually better than they realize, and the maxima in the space of startup ideas are not spiky and isolated -- most fairly good ideas are adjacent to even better ones [1].

## The Power of Compound Growth

Founders often dismiss manual user acquisition because the absolute numbers seem so small. Graham counters with simple math [5]: if you have 100 users and grow 10% a week, after a year you have 14,000 users. After two years, 2 million. The mistake founders make is to underestimate the power of compound growth [5].

Patrick Collison described the transition at Stripe: "At some point, there was a very noticeable change in how Stripe felt. It tipped from being this boulder we had to push to being a train car that in fact had its own momentum" [5].

## Do Not Grow Before the Product Is Loved

Altman is explicit: focusing on growth before you have a product people love leads to the "leaky bucket" problem. Users come in the door but do not stay and likely will not return [2].

Facebook had a product people talked about obsessively from the start. Airbnb slogged for 1,000 days before discovering how to make their product loved. Once they had that, it grew exponentially by word of mouth [2].

When the product is loved, users become "a free marketing and sales force." Word-of-mouth growth is powerful even for enterprise and hard tech companies -- the sales process becomes easier when word has spread that what you are making is great [2].

The danger of growing on a mediocre product is that reliance on inorganic means (ads, marketing, PR) becomes unsustainable. The larger the company grows on a weak foundation, the harder it becomes to course correct. "Once your company is a big moving battleship, it becomes really hard to be nimble" [2].

Altman's test for readiness: "When they actively recommend your product to friends, you're in the right place." Net Promoter Score can be a useful measurement [2].

## Growth vs. Conversion

The industry term for turning casual visitors into users is "conversion" [4]. Graham frames it simply: either your site is catching on or it isn't, and you must know which. "If you have decent growth, you'll win in the end, no matter how obscure you are now. And if you don't, you need to fix something" [4].

The metaphor: users are a fickle wind, but more powerful than any other. "If they take you up, no competitor can keep you down" [4]. To catch this wind, do two things: (1) explain, as concisely as possible, what your site is about -- a startup should be able to explain in one or two sentences exactly what it does [4]; (2) give people everything you have, right away -- put the impressive stuff on the front page because that is the only one most visitors will see [4].

## Release Early, Keep Pumping

Graham's most repeated advice to startups: get a version 1 out fast, then improve it based on users' reactions [4]. "By 'release early' I don't mean you should release something full of bugs, but that you should release something minimal. Users hate bugs, but they don't seem to mind a minimal version 1, if there's more coming soon" [4].

Continuous improvement is itself a form of marketing. "Users love a site that's constantly improving. In fact, users expect a site to improve" [4]. If you improve in response to user comments, you generate fanatical loyalty because customers are used to companies ignoring them [4].

The test for whether your product is finished: "If your product seems finished, there are two possible explanations: (a) it is finished, or (b) you lack imagination. Experience suggests (b) is a thousand times more likely" [4].

## Growth vs. Spending: A False Dichotomy

One of the most dangerous misconceptions is that growing fast and operating cheaply are opposed [6]. Graham argues there is "surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows" [6].

When a startup grows fast, it is usually because the product hits a nerve -- hitting some big need straight on. When a startup spends a lot, it is usually because the product is expensive to develop or sell, or simply because they are wasteful [6]. These are independent variables.

The implication: if growth is slow, hiring more people is almost never the answer. The real problem is usually that the product is not appealing enough [6]. The right approach is to seek growth "by [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]], or by redesigning the product in the way only founders can" [6].

## Premature Scaling

Paul Buchheit warns about premature scaling: founders take a small business that is not really working (bad unit economics, typically) and then scale it up because they want impressive growth numbers [6]. This is similar to overhiring -- it makes the business much harder to fix once it is big, and they are bleeding cash fast [6].

The common death pattern: a startup makes something moderately appealing, raises easily because the founders seem smart, then hires aggressively to try to boost growth. Growth never comes because the product is only moderately appealing. With high expenses and slow growth, they become unappealing to investors for the next round. The company dies [6].

## The Contained Fire

The [[Doing Things That Don't Scale]] essay introduces the "contained fire" strategy: focus on a deliberately narrow market first, get it really hot, then expand [5]. Facebook started with only Harvard students. Microsoft started with only Altair owners. The narrowness is not the goal -- depth is. But in practice narrowness comes as a byproduct of optimizing for depth and speed [5].

Any startup that could be described as a marketplace usually has to start in a subset of the market [5]. Among companies, the best early adopters are usually other startups -- they are more open to new things and grow fast, pulling you with them [5]. See [[Selling to Startups]] for a detailed treatment of when and how to use startups as your initial customer base.

## Big Launches Don't Work

Graham explicitly warns against relying on a "Big Launch" for growth [5]. There may even be an inverse correlation between launch magnitude and success -- the only launches he remembers are famous flops like the Segway and Google Wave [5].

All you need from a launch is some initial core of users. How well you are doing a few months later depends more on how happy you made those users than how many there were [5]. Partnerships also usually fail as growth strategies: "Six months later they're all saying the same thing: that was way more work than we expected, and we ended up getting practically nothing out of it" [5].

## Building a Growth Team

[[Anu Hariharan]], with contributions from Gustaf Alströmer, synthesized advice from 25 growth experts at companies including Facebook, Airbnb, Uber, Stitch Fix, Square, Slack, and Instagram [3].

### When to Invest

The consensus is clear: do not build a growth team before you have proven Retention. Investing in growth before nailing retention wastes money and jeopardizes the company's future [3]. Stitch Fix, for example, hired a retention-focused PM to run experiments improving retention before investing in new customer acquisition [3].

### Retention Benchmarks

To determine if retention is good enough to justify growth investment, three checks apply:

![Retention - Cohort Analysis](../raw/posts/images/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team/f39d6a8e86db.png)

1. **Stable long-term retention**: the retention curve should flatten and run parallel to the x-axis after an initial dip
2. **In line with vertical benchmarks**: stable 10% long-term retention is poor for a social network
3. **Newer cohorts perform better**: improving cohorts signal product improvement [3]

Retention benchmarks by vertical:

| Vertical | Period | Long-Term Target | Median |
|---|---|---|---|
| Social Network | Monthly (Month 12) | 45-65% | 55% |
| On-Demand | Monthly (Month 12) | 20-30% | 22% |
| Travel | Annual (Year 2) | 20-35% | 29% |
| E-commerce | Monthly (Month 12) | 10-25% | 16% |
| Subscription | Monthly (Month 12) | 25-35% | 33% |

### The Year 1 Growth Team

The initial team composition: 1 growth-focused PM + 2-3 growth engineers + 1-2 growth data scientists [3].

Timing: most companies made their first growth hire when they had about 15 engineers on the product team. The growth PM is typically the 3rd or 4th PM. The most common mistake CEOs make is waiting too long before hiring a growth-focused PM [3].

100% of growth experts said the first few hires are "magnets" for scaling the team. Eric Colson (former VP of data science at Netflix) was one of Stitch Fix's early hires and attracted accomplished data scientists to the company [3].

### Setting Goals and Metrics

The growth team's first priority is setting an **absolute goal** -- not a percentage change. For example, "achieve 5M first-time room nights this year" rather than "improve conversion by 10%" [3]. Casey Winters (former Growth Product Lead at Pinterest) wrote extensively about this approach [3].

The absolute goal breaks into sub-goals. Facebook's growth accounting equation: Monthly Active Users = New MAUs + Retained MAUs + Resurrected MAUs. Airbnb's: Room Nights = Room nights from new users + Room nights from existing users [3]. Jonathan Hsu (Partner at Social Capital, former Facebook data scientist) published this [growth accounting equation](https://medium.com/swlh/diligence-at-social-capital-part-1-accounting-for-user-growth-4a8a449fddfc) [3].

100% of experts said the CEO must be aligned on the absolute goal and that it must be communicated to the entire company [3].

### Growth Channels

The most common framework for identifying channels is based on existing user behavior. Aatif Awan (Head of Growth at LinkedIn) mapped user behaviors to channels [3]:

| User Behavior | Channel | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Product requires connecting with another user | Product itself | Facebook, PayPal, Slack |
| Existing users talk about the product | Referrals, Community | Uber, Airbnb, Dropbox |
| Users search for a solution | SEO, SEM | Airbnb |
| Users look for expert inspiration | Affiliate bloggers, Partnerships, Content | Stitch Fix, Glossier, Intercom |
| High LTV users | Paid acquisition | Airbnb, Expedia, Uber |

Most products find 1-2 relevant channels early on. Approximately 70% of experts said referrals were the top channel in the first year [3].

### Experimentation at Scale

The four essential elements for a growth team: a clean data set, segmentation tools, a rigorous experiment dashboard, and a peer review process [3].

A company at scale typically runs **1 experiment per growth engineer per week**. Growth teams running 100+ experiments per year report only a third turn out positive. The success rate of 20-30% is expected -- the point is to encourage engineers to take more risks [3].

A common heuristic: "Don't test things you wouldn't ship to everybody" [3].

100% of experts emphasized the decision to build their own internal tools at scale. Early on, tools like [Mixpanel](https://mixpanel.com/), [Optimizely](https://www.optimizely.com/), and [Chartio](https://chartio.com) suffice [3].

### Where the Growth Team Sits

This remains the biggest source of debate. Facebook pioneered the separate growth team -- Growth as its own department, with the Head of Growth reporting to the CEO. The rationale: if no one owns MAU growth, no one drives it. Facebook grew from 50 million to 2 billion MAUs under this model [3].

Other companies (Uber, Airbnb, Slack) started with separate teams but later merged them with product. Because growth experiments often require subtle product changes, advocates argue product and growth must be in the same org [3].

The least-favored option among experts is housing growth under marketing [3].

At scale, a growth team can exceed 100 people, roughly composed of: 50% engineers, 10% PMs, 10-15% data scientists, 10% product marketing, 10-15% designers, and ~5% researchers [3].

## There Is Always Room

A common fear among would-be founders is that the market for startups is saturated. Graham dismisses this [4]. "At every point in history, even the darkest bits of the dark ages, people were discovering things that made everyone say 'why didn't anyone think of that before?'" [4].

The argument that there are only so many startups Google and Yahoo can buy conflates the limit on acquisitions with the limit on wealth creation. "There is no limit on the amount of newly created wealth consumers can absorb, any more than there is a limit on the number of theorems that can be proven" [4]. The only limit on the number of startups is the pool of people willing to work that hard.

## Growth, Fundraising, and Acquisitions

Growth explains why VCs invest in startups rather than ordinary businesses. The returns when startups succeed are so high that the ratio of return to risk passes any investment test. But VCs also prefer startups because capital gains are easier to manage than dividends from private companies, where owners can funnel revenues to themselves [1].

Raising money lets founders "choose their growth rate." The most successful startups take VC money even when profitable because it lets them grow faster and fend off competitors, especially in businesses with network effects [1].

Growth also explains why successful startups get acquisition offers. A rapidly growing company is not merely valuable but dangerous to incumbents. "Most product acquisitions have some component of fear," Graham writes. Acquirers often pay more than ordinary investors would because a startup is doubly valuable -- for what it is and for what it could become in a competitor's hands [1].


## Dropbox: Viral Growth Mechanics (Drew Houston)

[[Drew Houston]] built Dropbox's growth engine on Dave McClure's AARRR framework [8]. Four of five new signups did not put a file in their Dropbox or install the client. Houston brought five people off Craigslist and watched them try to install -- zero succeeded [8].

The referral program (both sides get free storage) drove 30% of all signups. Shared folders drove another 20%. Half of all Dropbox signups were viral [8]. The demo video posted on Hacker News stayed at the top for two days and became a repeatable tactic [8].

## Meesho: Growth-Quality Cycles (Vidit Aatrey)

[[Vidit Aatrey]] describes Meesho's growth in six-month cycles alternating between expansion and quality improvement. Growth was driven by progressively expanding the addressable market: first existing WhatsApp sellers, then new resellers (housewives), then expanding categories, then cross-border supply chains from China [9].

## Rippling: Growth Beyond Sales (Parker Conrad)

[[Parker Conrad]] argues software sales and marketing is fundamentally broken: "Public companies are spending 50% more on sales and marketing but they're adding 10% less in new ARR" [10]. The compound startup thesis reduces dependency on expensive acquisition by serving more of a customer's needs [10].

## References

1. [Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) -- [[Paul Graham]] (September 2012)
2. [Before Growing Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4p-before-growing-your-startup) -- [[Sam Altman]] (n.d.)
