Altman argues this is "the number one secret to having a really great career" because it produces the fastest rate of personal growth [2]. Careers have a compound-interest dynamic: "the work you do now, the learning you do now, the improvement you make early in your career gets to pay off for all the rest" [2].

Startups are where this opportunity exists: "You can get jobs you are widely unqualified for" [2]. When someone takes a role they are unqualified for, "it either goes way worse or way better than expectations. And a lot of the times it goes way better" [2].

## Evaluation Criteria

### Users Who Love the Product

The single most important piece of diligence according to both Taggar and Altman: a core group of early users who are fanatical about the product [1] [2]. Altman's version: "Is this product so good that people are telling other people they've got to use it?" [2]. A small number of users who love the product matters much more than a large number who kind of like it. Airbnb's early users were few but fanatical [1].

Taggar advises asking to talk to some users directly. If the depth of their passion is high, even in a small group, that is a strong signal [1].

### Growth Trajectory Over Absolute Numbers

The most important factor is not the absolute numbers but the growth trajectory. "A startup that has a million dollars in monthly revenue but it's had a million dollars in monthly revenue for the last twelve months in a row -- that's less exciting than a startup that has ten thousand in monthly revenue but the previous month it only had five" [1].

Altman extends this: trust exponential growth even when it is hard to intuit. "I've been investing in startups for eight or ten years now. I still have not managed to get good at intuiting exponential growth in my head. So I have to do it in a spreadsheet every time" [2].

### Growth Rate of the Market, Not Its Size

Both speakers warn against evaluating the size of the market. This is the most common mistake investors make, and it is how they miss the really big ones [1] [2].

Altman uses the Uber deck as an example: their initial TAM was stated as approximately $2 billion. "But it turns out that the market of people that want to move around cities easily grows quickly when you have a better product" [2].

### The Founders and Team

Only join a startup where you are genuinely impressed by the founders and their early hires [1]. Taggar specifically warns against evaluating founders by traditional credentials. Instead, evaluate founders relative to their starting point. A 19-year-old from Milwaukee who managed to raise a seed round and build a company "might be more impressive than an engineer who worked at Google for fifteen years and is in sort of the same place" [1].

Altman describes exceptional founders as "so good that they end up bending the world to their will. And they can do this without a lot of business experience" [2].

### Real Trends vs. Fake Trends

Altman's test: "Real trends are ones where a new technology platform comes along and the early adopters use it obsessively and tell their friends how much they love it. A fake trend is one where people may buy the product, but don't use it" [2]. The iPhone was a real trend: early owners "used it for hours every day." VR (as of 2018) was a fake trend [2].

### Ideas That Sound Bad But Are Good

Most ideas that sound bad are bad. But a class of ideas that sound bad but are good is "where YC has made most of its money" [2]. A startup only needs one "yes" out of many possible investors; a big company needs every person in the chain to say yes [2].

## Employee Equity

Altman states bluntly: "You probably aren't getting enough" [2]. Most startups are not generous enough with employee equity. "The amount of value that you create as an early employee versus a founder is not the one-hundred-x difference that you usually see reflected on a cap table" [2].

## Choosing Who Gives You Advice

"Most people will bring whatever biases they have about what a career should look like or what a safe choice is" [2]. Find a small group of people who are "open-minded and intellectually rigorous thinkers" [2].

## References

1. [How to Choose a Startup to Join](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9t-how-to-choose-a-startup-to-join) -- [[Harj Taggar]] (n.d.)
2. [How to Pick Which Startup to Work At](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/K0-sam-altman-on-how-to-pick-which-startup-to-work-at) -- [[Sam Altman]] (2018)

title: Inventing the Future
body:
# Inventing the Future

Alan Kay, one of the pioneers of personal computing and a Turing Award winner, delivered a two-part lecture at Stanford (for Sam Altman's "How to Start a Startup" series) on how transformational technology gets invented. Part 2 covers the role of context in intelligence, the psychology of human perception and blindness, and why the most important ideas from Xerox PARC still have not been adopted [1].

## Context Is Worth 80 IQ Points

Kay opens with a comparison: Leonardo da Vinci, arguably the most brilliant person of his era, could not invent a single motor. Henry Ford, with nowhere near Leonardo's IQ, built millions of cars and sold them for $800 each. "The difference between the two was that Ford was born into the right century and Leonardo was born into the wrong century" [1].

The accumulated knowledge of the 19th century -- ultimately traceable to the context shift that Newton initiated in the 17th century -- made more difference than any level of individual smartness [1].

Kay formalizes this as **Principle Zero**: "To make progress, context or point of view is worth 80 IQ points. If you've got the context of science and math, you are gazillions of times smarter, effectively, than the smartest people of antiquity" [1].

His summary: "Knowledge is silver, context is gold, and IQ is just lead" [1].

The trap for smart people: being the smartest in a classroom of 30 or 100 feels meaningful, but "if you happened to be in a room that had the hundred smartest people in the world and you were the smartest person in that room, you're still not as smart as the other ninety-nine working together" [1].

## The Pink Plane and Blue Worlds

Kay uses a visual metaphor to explain paradigm shifts [1]. Most people live on a "pink plane" -- their default worldview. Thoughts are like ants crawling over it. Everything they see is pink, but they do not know it is pink because they have never experienced anything else. "This is like a fish not knowing it's in water" [1].

Occasionally, someone has an "outlaw thought" -- a blue thought -- that reveals an orthogonal world. Newton's shift from medieval to scientific thinking. ARPA's shift from mainframes to personal computing. Each is a move from one colored plane to another. "Once you're in that blue world, you realize there's probably a lot of them" [1].

**Einstein at five**: Einstein's father gave him a compass while he was recovering from measles. Most children would accept the compass as it is. Einstein remembered it as the most important moment of his childhood: "There must be something deeply hidden behind things." He did not take the world at face value [1].

## Human Perception Is Broken

Kay uses hands-on demonstrations to show that human perception routinely manufactures false information [1]:

### The Blind Spot

Every human eye has a region with no light-sensitive cells (where blood vessels pass through). The brain fills this gap by making up what it thinks should be there -- an unconscious confabulation. "We have a blind spot in our eyes that our brain makes up stuff to fill in. It's just making it up" [1].

**Principle 22**: We dream while awake. The brain fills in its blind spot the same way it generates dreams. Most people think they only dream at night, "but in fact, human brains are set up so that we actually dream" continuously [1].

**Principle 23**: "We can't learn to see until we realize and admit that we're blind. And the biggest problem with most human beings is they don't understand that they are blind because they think they can see" [1].

### Size Constancy

Holding two identical poker chips at different distances, the retinal image of the farther chip is half the size. But the brain's belief system overrides the retinal data and presents both chips as the same size. This "size constancy" illusion means the mind's eye -- beliefs about the world -- overrides the sensory eye [1].

Artists learn to work around this by measuring with outstretched arms. Most people never discover the discrepancy [1].

### The Shepard Tables

Two tabletops that appear to be completely different shapes are in fact identical. Even knowing this, the visual system refuses to see it. "The natural state of humanity is to be crazy, because crazy is not having good models in our mind for what's going on out there" [1]. First demonstrated by Roger Shepard at Stanford [1].

## What Made It Out of Xerox PARC

Kay provides a detailed scorecard of which PARC inventions successfully transferred to the broader world [1]:

| Invention | % Adopted |
|---|---|
| Laser printer | ~90% |
| PostScript | 100% (Adobe founders were PARC alumni) |
| Ethernet | 100% |
| GUI | ~70% (missing: unlimited desktop/project model) |
| Desktop publishing | ~70% |
| Internet protocols | 100% |
| Programming (Smalltalk) | ~10% |
| CPU architecture | 0% (Intel resisted) |
| Dynabook concept | ~10% |

The pattern: "What came out most successfully were the things for which people out there didn't think they already knew how to do it." Everybody thought they already knew how to program, so Smalltalk's radical approach met maximum resistance [1].

Kay shows a restored 1978 Smalltalk system (recovered from Xerox's dump, where disc packs had been thrown out) with capabilities still absent from modern operating systems: persistent project-based workspaces, live editing of running animations, gesture recognition, and the ability to connect any two objects via messaging. "Wouldn't you like to be able to do that today? This is completely natural. It's what you get very easily if you just have unlimited objects" [1].

### What Is Still Missing

Not a single mainstream operating system includes the collaborative content-sharing capability that Engelbart demonstrated in 1968 [1]. The ability to share any content with everyone else, allowing them to interact with it in real time, requires operating-system-level support that macOS, Linux, and Windows lack.

Kay attributes this to operating systems that predate 1965 in their fundamental design: "All three operating systems we have today that are the main ones are old, old ideas" [1].

## Darwinian Processes Do Not Optimize

Kay corrects a common misconception: evolution does not optimize. It fits organisms into niches. "If that environment isn't the right kind of environment, the process of evolution is not going to give you something that's very interesting" [1]. As computing becomes less interesting (lower expectations, less ambitious goals), the selection pressure produces less interesting software.

This is why innovations like Sketchpad's direct manipulation -- which Sam Altman looked at and asked "Why aren't they doing it today?" -- disappear. "Nobody even thinks about that. Nobody even thinks of doing WYSIWYG on web media" [1].

## Human Universals vs. Inventions

Anthropologists studying thousands of traditional cultures identified traits present in every culture (human universals -- virtually all genetic) and traits absent from many (inventions that had to be discovered) [1].

**Genetic universals** (things humans want): hunting/gathering, social bonding, language, stories, news, theater, revenge. Every one is "a legal drug." Entrepreneurs can build technological amplifiers for any of these cravings, and people will buy them [1].

**Inventions** (things humans need but are not genetically prepared for): agriculture, the idea of progress, writing/reading, scientific representation, slow thinking, similarities over differences, legal systems. These are hard to learn because they are not genetically prepared [1].

Kay's challenge to entrepreneurs: "Try to do it without appealing to the parts of their nature that they're helpless in front of." The technologies of today are "the informational equivalent of cave people with nuclear weapons" because educational systems have not kept pace with technological capability [1].

## Principles Summary

- **0**: Context/point of view is worth 80 IQ points
- **1**: Debugging is harder than programming -- don't use all your cleverness writing the program
- **2**: The goodness of results correlates most with the goodness of the funders (good funders give gold medals ahead of time, knowing 70% will turn to lead)
- **21**: Reality is a low-pass filter -- you need enormous ideas so that filtering retains something useful
- **22**: We dream while awake (the brain fills in blind spots with confabulations)
- **23**: We cannot learn to see until we realize we are blind
- **24**: The mind's eye is different from the sensory eye; we act according to beliefs, not data

## References

1. [How to Invent the Future (Part 2)](https://ycombinator.com/library/76-how-to-invent-the-future-part-2) -- Alan Kay (2014)

title: Startup Pricing
body:
# Startup Pricing

[[Kevin Hale]] argues that pricing is the most neglected lever for growth, yet it produces the highest return on effort [1]. A survey of over 500 SaaS companies found that a 1% improvement in pricing optimization yields a 12.7% return, compared to 3.32% for acquisition efforts and 6.7% for retention [1]. Despite this, most startups avoid touching their pricing out of fear that getting it wrong will drive away all their customers [1].

## The Pricing Thermometer

Hale introduces a framework called the [[Pricing Thermometer]] that makes the forces behind pricing concrete [1]. Three variables sit on a vertical axis: cost at the bottom, price in the middle, and value at the top.

- **The gap between cost and price** is the startup's margin -- its incentive to sell. The larger this gap, the more the company is motivated to push the product to customers, fund better salespeople, and invest in distribution [1].
- **The gap between price and value** is the customer's incentive to buy. The larger this gap, the easier it is for customers to say yes [1].

There are two approaches to setting price. **Cost-plus pricing** starts with the cost and adds a margin. **Value-based pricing** starts with the value the customer perceives and works backward. Hale states that "almost consistently across all businesses, everyone will tell you, you should strive for value-based pricing" because it allows charging more and manipulating the incentive to buy [1].

## The Four Pricing Mistakes

Because founders do not understand the relationship between cost, price, and value, they place their price in an arbitrary location, leading to four common mistakes [1]:

1. **Pricing too low.** "You consistently undercharge" is the number one pricing advice YC gives to startups [1]. This is the most common mistake.
2. **Underestimating costs.** The result is margins too thin to cover customer acquisition costs [1].
3. **Not understanding value.** Either the founder does not understand how the customer values the problem being solved, or the founder cannot convince the customer of the value the product offers [1].
4. **Focusing on the wrong customers.** Founders assume that building a better product and charging half the competition's price will win. It almost never does, because early-stage startups are selling to early adopters, not mainstream customers [1].

## Early Adopters Are Not Price-Sensitive

Hale draws on the product lifecycle curve to explain why the "cheaper wins" logic fails for startups. Startups in the product development and introduction stages are targeting the first 2-5% of potential buyers -- early adopters [1]. These customers behave fundamentally differently from mainstream buyers:

- They care about **benefits above all else** -- getting an edge over competition, doing something much better [1].
- They are **willing to take risks** on unproven products [1].
- They are **not price-sensitive**. If anything, a low price creates reputation risk: "It's like, why is it too good to be true? What is the catch?" [1]

The implication: do not undercharge when selling to early adopters. A higher price signals quality and seriousness. Go after early adopters first, and do not take it personally when mainstream customers are not ready for the product -- "They were never going to be" [1].

## Price Optimization in Practice

Hale demystifies price optimization by reducing it to a simple table [1]:

| Price Point | Conversion Rate | Sales Volume | Revenue |
|-------------|----------------|--------------|---------|
| $X | Y% | Z units | $Total |

Try different price points, measure conversion rate and sales volume for each, and calculate total revenue. The winner is the row with the highest revenue [1]. At lower price points where revenue is still positive, those represent opportunities for discount pricing or tiered pricing [1].

## The Danger Zone: Pricing and Customer Acquisition

Hale introduces a framework linking price to acquisition strategy. The vertical axis represents price; the horizontal axis represents sales process complexity [1]:

**Self-Serve (under $2,000/year):**
- Marketing must be almost entirely inbound [1]
- Support must be self-serve or minimal [1]
- No sales team -- cannot afford one at this price point [1]
- Conversions should happen same-day in a self-serve model [1]

**Transactional ($2,000 to $10,000/year):**
- Marketing can generate qualified leads [1]
- Support can offer SLAs and onboarding training [1]
- Can have inside sales reps, SDRs, and product demo staff (but not dedicated field sales) [1]
- Sales cycle should not exceed one to three months [1]

**Enterprise (over $25,000/year):**
- Marketing can invest in branding and trust-building [1]
- Support is high-touch: phone support, dedicated customer success managers [1]
- Sales structure includes sales managers, territory divisions, and sales engineers [1]
- Sales cycle of six to twelve months [1]

**The Garbage Zone** sits between self-serve and transactional: if it takes months to close a deal but the revenue per customer is low, acquisition costs will be unsustainable. "All of your work should be towards increasing the perceived value of your product or service" to escape this zone [1].

### The Billion-Dollar Math

Hale uses a concrete exercise: to build a billion-dollar company, the rule of thumb is $100 million per year in revenue. At each price point, calculate how many customers are needed to reach $100M [1]:

- **$100/year** -- need 1 million users (consumer territory) [1]
- **$1,000/year** -- enterprise territory [1]
- The SMB range in between is where companies "really, really struggle" because these customers "treat their money like consumers but look like they might be an enterprise" [1]

## The 10/5/20 Rule

Hale closes with a practical heuristic for founders who do not know where to start with pricing [1]:

1. **10X value**: Set a price that is one-tenth of the perceived value. If the product costs $10, the customer should easily perceive it as worth $100. If the 10X value is not immediately obvious, the incentive to buy is too low [1].
2. **5% increases**: Once a starting price exists, practice raising it by 5% at a time [1].
3. **20% loss threshold**: Keep raising prices until 20% of deals are lost. That is the approximate sweet spot between pricing too low and too high [1].

## Pricing as a Growth Driver

Hale summarizes: "If you've never touched the pricing of your product, then you're losing out on lots of potential growth" [1]. The key questions founders should be able to answer: Do you understand your costs? Do you understand why your price is where it is? Do you understand the value your customer perceives? When entering a sales meeting, a founder should be able to say: "I know exactly what this is going to be worth to you. So when I tell you what the price is going to be, you're going to be like, 'Damn, that's totally worth it'" [1].

## References

1. [Startup Pricing 101](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6h-startup-pricing-101) -- [[Kevin Hale]] (2019)

title: CEO Role and the Co-CEO Mistake
body:
# CEO Role and the Co-CEO Mistake

[[Garry Tan]] draws on his personal experience co-founding Posterous to argue that the co-CEO structure is a mistake for nearly all startups, and that founders must honestly assess whether they need to be CEO [1].

## The Co-CEO Failure

In 2010, Tan and his co-founder were pitching at Andreessen Horowitz. Ben Horowitz asked: "So who's the CEO?" Tan and his co-founder looked at each other and said "Both." Tan recalls: "The meeting might as well have ended at that moment" [1].

Horowitz later wrote about this in 2013: "Shared command always seems really attractive to the people at the top of the organization... It looks much less attractive to those who do all the work in the organization. To them, it looks like frustration, chaos, and delay" [1].

The operational problem is speed. With co-CEOs, every hard decision requires both people to agree, which slows decision-making to the point where "your startup will die" [1].

Tan identifies the archetype that tempts founders: Larry Page and Sergey Brin of Google. "What was good enough for Google, surely could be good enough for us, right? Whoa, whoa, no. Look, most people are not Google at their Series A" [1].

## How to Know If You Should Be CEO

Tan's self-diagnostic question: "If it was broken, I needed to be able to fix it. If things are broken, are you okay with someone else being in charge of fixing it? If you're going to be incredibly frustrated that you don't have the authority and can't fix it, then maybe you do need to be CEO" [1].

Tan frames this as a matter of self-knowledge, not ego: "It doesn't have to be an ego thing. It's just something that you need in your life. It's okay to need that. It's okay to need agency" [1].

## Tan's Personal Mistake

After the Andreessen Horowitz meeting, Tan discussed the CEO question with his co-founder. His co-founder became CEO after their Series A. Tan admits: "If I'm being honest with you, I regret that. I learned you may not be interested in authority, but authority is interested in you" [1].

The root of the mistake: Tan wanted to keep coding. "I had to decide whether I wanted to be CEO or if I could keep designing and writing code and shipping product. Writing code and shipping product, those felt really good to me in the moment. I chose sticking with the things that made me feel good" [1]. It took him twenty years to realize he needed to be CEO [1].

## What the CEO Must Do

Tan identifies the specific responsibilities that only a CEO can handle, which become critical after [[Product-Market Fit]] [1]:

- Convince people to follow you
- Hire and manage
- Fundraise and manage investors
- Drive the vision of the company
- Be "the magic that brings all of those things together to build a great product or service"

Tan is direct: "Being CEO is a very specific skill set. And unless you are signing up to do those things and do them to the best of your ability, don't" [1].

## The Three Types of Founders

Tan observes that founders sort into three categories regarding the CEO question [1]:

1. Those who never want to be CEO -- and that is fine.
2. Those who love another function but realize they need to become CEO to steer the ship.
3. Those who "knew the day you quit your job that you absolutely have to get that authority."

The advice for all three: "Don't delude yourself about whether or not you should be CEO. If you know, you know. Then it's on you to acquire the skills and manifest it" [1].

## References

1. [Should You Be the CEO?](https://ycombinator.com/library/Jq-garry-s-channel-should-you-be-the-ceo) -- [[Garry Tan]] (2021)

title: Startup Ideas
body:
# Startup Ideas

The single most important thing a startup does is choose what to build. [[Paul Graham]] argues across multiple essays spanning 2005 to 2014 that the way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of startup ideas, but to look for problems -- preferably problems you have yourself [1]. The very best startup ideas tend to have three things in common: they are something the founders themselves want, that they themselves can build, and that few others realize are worth doing [1]. Microsoft, Apple, Yahoo, Google, and Facebook all began this way.

## Your Idea Does Not Need to Be Great

[[Michael Seibel]] opens his advice on ideas by dismantling the assumption that a great idea is required to start a company [7]. He cofounded Justin.tv, which later became Twitch and sold to Amazon for almost a billion dollars. The original idea was to create an online reality TV show. "Clearly, it's hard to draw a line between that and creating a live video site where people watch video gamers and chat with their friends, but that's exactly what it became" [7].

The implication: the initial idea is a starting point, not a destination. Founders who wait for the perfect idea before beginning are filtering themselves out of the process.

## Start With a Problem, Not a Solution

Seibel's core advice is to start with a problem rather than an idea [7]. Starting with ideas is tricky because people immediately want to grade the idea. Starting with problems shifts the frame to evaluation criteria that are more useful:

- Do you have any personal relationship with this problem? Is it your problem, your friends' problem, your family's problem, a problem from work, from your community? [7]
- Having a personal connection to the problem helps in two ways: you can judge whether solutions are in the right ballpark, and when you are discouraged and solutions are not working, your connection to the problem sustains you [7].

Seibel recommends keeping a "problem book" rather than an idea book: "Write down notes on the problems that you encounter on a daily basis or that your friends complain about, their family complain about" [7].

## Brainstorm With Friends

The problem-discovery phase is also the perfect time to evaluate potential [[Co-Founders]] [7]. Brainstorming with friends reveals whether two people "trade ideas well" and can build on each other's thinking. "A lot of recruiting cofounders is about having really good brainstorming sessions where you talk about a problem, they elaborate on it, and you kind of realize that you can talk to each other well, you can build ideas together" [7].

## Unique Qualification

Once a problem is identified, Seibel advises asking: "Why are you uniquely qualified to work on this problem?" [7]. This does not mean credentials in the classical sense. It means: is there some angle on the problem that you understand that others do not? Some personal experience, some perspective, some way of attacking the problem that is not generally understood? [7]

For common problems, Seibel recommends studying prior attempts and existing solutions: "Look at some of the products that are trying to solve the problem or have failed to solve the problem, just so you can get a basic understanding of what other people thought their unique insight was and whether it was the same or different than yours" [7].

## Building and Testing the MVP

Seibel's advice on the MVP is direct: "Don't fall in love with it" [7]. The MVP is most likely not going to solve the problem, or will solve it very poorly. It is the first step in learning whether the problem is solvable at all.

"A lot of people fall in love with their product and are not in love with their problem or their customer. I advise the opposite. Be in love with your problem. Be in love with your cause. And treat your product in a way that it can change, it can develop, it can improve" [7].

The MVP should be released quickly -- in days or weeks. It will not be perfect and may not be something the founder is proud of. But it begins the learning process [7].

### Choosing Initial Users

Seibel argues that initial users should be "almost hand-picked" [7]. The goal with the MVP is not to see how many people want to use the product. It is to see whether, for any group of people, the product actually solves the problem [7]. "The best startups actually very heavily filter the people who are able to use the initial product and make sure that they're the right type of initial customer" [7].

See [[Getting First Users]] for the full treatment of finding and learning from early adopters.

## The Organic Method: Notice, Don't Think Up

The verb Graham insists on is "notice," not "think up" [1]. At YC they call ideas that grow naturally out of the founders' own experiences "organic" startup ideas. The most successful startups almost all begin this way. Apple, Yahoo, Google, and Facebook were not even meant to be companies at first -- they were side projects that grew out of things their founders built because there seemed a gap in the world [1] [4].

The formula Graham distills is:

> "Live in the future, then build what's missing." [1]

This phrase, attributed to Paul Buchheit, captures the method: place yourself at the leading edge of a rapidly changing field, and the ideas that will seem uncannily prescient to others will seem obvious to you [1].

Graham's advice to college students boils down to two words: "just learn" [4]. Become good at something that matters, work on problems that interest you, with people you like and respect. The component of entrepreneurship that really matters is domain expertise [4].

## The Well Model: Depth Over Breadth

When evaluating an idea, Graham uses the metaphor of digging a hole [1]. You can either dig a hole that is broad but shallow, or one that is narrow and deep -- like a well. Nearly all good startup ideas are wells.

The critical test: "Who wants this right now? Who wants this so much that they'll use it even when it's a crappy version one made by a two-person startup they've never heard of?" If you cannot answer that, the idea is probably bad [1].

## Dangerous Filters

Two unconscious filters block founders from seeing good ideas [1]:

**The [[Schlep Filter]]**: Most programmers wish they could start a startup by just writing brilliant code. They would prefer not to deal with tedious problems or get involved in messy ways with the real world. Stripe is the canonical example [1].

**The unsexy filter**: Similar to the schlep filter, except it keeps you from working on problems you despise rather than ones you fear [1].

## Idea Quality Score Formula

[[Jared Friedman]], crediting [[Dalton Caldwell]]'s "All About Pivoting" talk, presents a four-criteria scoring formula for evaluating startup ideas [8]:

1. **How big is this idea?** Look for existing large companies doing something similar (banks exist and are big, so a new kind of bank could be big). Or: the market is small today but has a real chance of being huge. Coinbase was started in 2012 when Bitcoin was tiny, but some believed it could replace all existing money [8].
2. **Founder/market fit.** Are the founders experts in the domain? Flexport's founder Ryan worked in international trade for 10 years before starting the company -- "10 out of 10 founder/market fit" [8].
3. **How sure are you that this is a big problem?** Ideally, personal experience with the problem [8].
4. **Do you have a new, important insight?** Airbnb's founders had actually tried hosting strangers and discovered it was fun, when everyone else assumed it was dangerous [8].

## Seven Recipes for Generating Ideas

Friedman lists seven recipes in order from best to worst [8]:

1. **Start with what your team is especially good at.** Generates automatic founder/market fit. Roughly half of YC's most successful companies trace back to this recipe. Examples: SnapDocs (founder worked in mortgage industry 10 years), Lattice (founders noticed bad performance review software at their company), Mixpanel (founder worked at Slide which had built internal analytics tools) [8].
2. **Things you wish someone else would build.** DoorDash: founders could not get Thai food delivered in the suburbs [8].
3. **What would you work on for 10 years even if it didn't succeed?** [[Boom Technology]] (supersonic airplane) came from this. Warning: can lead to passionate but unbusinesslike ideas [8].
4. **Things that recently became possible.** PlanGrid: construction blueprints on tablets, made possible by the iPad. Acquired for $1 billion [8].
5. **Successful companies with new variants.** Standard Cognition: Amazon Go technology for all retail stores. Warning: often produces "Uber for X" SISPs [8].
6. **Crowdsource by asking people for problems.** Works best with startup founders, who already know how to notice ideas [8].
7. **Industries that seem broken.** LendUp: replacement for payday lending. Warning: poor founder/market fit unless you already know the industry [8].

## Common Idea Mistakes

Friedman identifies four common mistakes [8]:

1. **Believing you need an amazing idea.** Google was the 20th search engine. Facebook was the 20th social network. They succeeded through execution [8].
2. **Jumping into the first idea.** If you will spend years on something, spend at least a couple of weeks deciding what to work on [8].
3. **Starting with a solution instead of a problem.** "Uber for plumbers" is a solution. What problem does it solve? This is the SISP pattern [8].
4. **Believing startup ideas are hard to find.** "They're easy to find, because there are many, many real problems left in the world" [8].

## Competition Is Not What Kills You

A crowded market is actually a good sign, because it means there is demand and none of the existing solutions are good enough [1]. Worrying that you are late is one of the signs of a good idea [1]. Friedman reinforces this: "when founders go into spaces with no existing competitors, they usually find out that the reason there are no competitors is because nobody wants the product." Dropbox launched against dozens of file-syncing solutions and beat them all with a better product [8].

# Startup Ideas

## Unique Experience Beats Mimetic Desire (Garry Tan)

[[Garry Tan]] diagnoses the most common idea failure as mimetic desire: "Everyone's making the same photo sharing, podcasting, travel plans, local event apps, probably because desire is mimetic. We want what other people want" [9]. The most famous startups were not famous at the moment they were started -- and as Chris Dixon observed, "the great disruptive startup ideas are hard to spot because when they start, they're toys" [9].

Tan's solution is to embrace what makes you different: "What makes you new, different, and unique in the world will be the exact thing that makes you succeed" [9]. He cites Steve Jobs's insight that innovation requires connecting experiences that other people do not have: "If you're gonna make connections which are innovative, you have to connect two experiences together. You have to not have the same bag of experiences as everyone else does, or else you're going to make the same connections and then you won't be innovative" [9].

### Flexport and Patreon as Examples

Tan provides two concrete examples from his investment portfolio:

- **Flexport**: [[Ryan Petersen]] was one of the first Amazon third-party merchants, imported motorbikes from China, and lived there for two years running supply chain. Tan: "My favorite pitch meetings are with founders who teach you something about the world you didn't know before. Global logistics was one of those things, and he knew it intimately" [9]. Flexport is now worth over $3.2 billion.

- **Patreon**: Jack Conti started a band with his wife and posted YouTube videos. "Because they saw that problem very early -- that monetization methods for creators were totally broken -- they created something amazing that has funded a new generation of creator." Patreon is now worth more than a billion dollars [9].

### Avoiding Infinite Competition

Tan invokes Peter Thiel: "Infinite competition is for losers" [9]. The way to avoid it is not to copy what successful startups did, but to go into your own experience and find what is unique. "Don't do what they did. You have to go into your experience and find things that are unique to you" [9].

He also references [[Paul Graham]]'s 2012 essay on startup ideas, highlighting the passage about Bill Gates and Paul Allen: "If Bill Gates and Paul Allen had constrained themselves to come up with a startup idea in one month, what if they'd chosen a month before the Altair even appeared?" The Altair appeared on the cover of Popular Electronics; they wrote to the company immediately and offered to do a BASIC for it. That became Microsoft [9].

## References (new)

9. [Billion Dollar Startup Ideas](https://ycombinator.com/library/Ji-garry-s-channel-billion-dollar-startup-ideas) -- [[Garry Tan]] (n.d.)

# Startup Ideas

## The Umbrella Tilting Principle (Stewart Butterfield)

Stewart Butterfield, co-founder of Flickr and Slack, offers a parable about noticing problems [9]. Walking down a narrow Vancouver sidewalk in the rain, two-thirds of the umbrella-carrying pedestrians did not tilt their umbrellas to avoid poking Butterfield and his colleague in the eye. Butterfield identifies only two possible explanations: either people walk through the world oblivious to the problems they cause others, or they notice the problem but cannot conceive of a solution -- "despite the fact that it's this tiny, like a hundredth of a calorie worth of effort and a tiny amount of consideration" [9].

The point: "If you're the kind of person who's willing to tilt your umbrella, there's a whole world of opportunity out there" [9]. Most people are oblivious to problems or unable to imagine solutions. Founders who pay attention and act have a structural advantage.

## Recognition Over Intuition (Stewart Butterfield)

Butterfield draws on Daniel Kahneman's "Thinking, Fast and Slow" and psychologist Herbert Simon's study of chess grandmasters [9]. Simon argued that what appears to be miraculous intuition in chess grandmasters is actually recognition -- pattern matching built through years of experience. "An 18-month-old can look at a dog and say 'doggy,'" and within 500 milliseconds of someone speaking, a listener can tell if they are angry [9]. Similarly, after twenty years of building software, Butterfield has developed the ability to recognize whether a feature or product decision is good -- not through explicit analysis but through accumulated pattern recognition.

The implication for founders: ideas are not the scarce resource. "Coming up with new ideas is easy. The recognition of which ones are good is actually much harder" [9]. Evaluation matters more than generation.

## Execution Outweighs Idea (Stewart Butterfield)

Butterfield addresses the spectrum directly: "I would definitely lean more towards execution" [9]. He invokes Steve Jobs's observation that John Sculley hired people at Apple who believed only ideas mattered. "The idea when you start hitting execution has to accommodate so many things" -- physical constraints of materials, market realities, engineering limitations. "Your idea has to stand up to all these things and it'll transmogriphy and alter and may even be unrecognizable at the end" [9]. But a founder who is excellent at execution can preserve the core of an idea through all those transformations.

The balance: "You can execute a lot on a terrible idea and then not get anywhere. So you kind of need both" [9].

## Look at Your Own Experience (Stewart Butterfield)

When asked where to look for ideas today, Butterfield's answer is personal experience as a consumer [9]. "It's really easy to see things that are frustrating. Very easy for me to see things that are frustrating." He cites air travel as an example: "Every time I have to fly somewhere I have at least 10 significant complaints and like 50 minor complaints. And every one of those is an opportunity" [9].

He provides a concrete example of a good idea spotted in the wild: MileIQ, an app that uses location permissions to automatically log car trips and lets users swipe right for reimbursable trips and left for personal ones. "It's a very simple idea. It's actually a really great idea because people have trouble recording those" [9].

## Measurement Can Sharpen Ideas (Adam D'Angelo)

Adam D'Angelo, co-founder and CEO of Quora, argues that back-of-the-envelope calculations can turn a vague idea into a good one and help founders iterate through the idea space faster than building prototypes [9]. He uses Amazon's founding decision as a case study. Jeff Bezos wanted to start an online store and recognized that the internet's advantage over brick-and-mortar was unlimited selection. The question became: which product category benefits most from greater selection? Books had the most individual products (far more than movies, which are expensive to produce). This kind of quantitative filtering is "very good for helping you figure out what products are going to be possible or where you're going to be able to provide a lot of value to a user" [9].

D'Angelo cautions that measurement works less well for estimating market size. Slack's founders estimated a $100 million revenue market and were off by 100x [9]. "I wouldn't spend a lot of time on that. You want to know that whatever you're doing, someday there's some path for it to eventually get really big" [9].

## Slack's Unconscious Design Process (Stewart Butterfield)

Slack's origin illustrates the organic idea method at its most extreme [9]. Butterfield's game company used IRC for internal communication. IRC's deficiencies drove incremental improvements: a bot to log messages (since IRC could not store and forward), then search on those logs, then an HTML5 mobile frontend, then the ability to post from mobile. Each improvement addressed "whatever problem is the most irritating" with "a minimum amount of effort" before going back to the game [9].

The result was "a fully designed product that we were using that was a terrible implementation." Everyone at the company agreed "they would never work without something like this again" [9]. Critically, the design process was unconscious. Normal software development involves ego and speculation ("I think this could be really valuable for someone"). The proto-Slack development had neither: problems were selected by irritation, not speculation, and solutions were minimal [9].

Butterfield did not recognize the market size. He estimated Slack could eventually be "a hundred million a year revenue business." The company passed that in year two and now targets "ten billion in revenue" [9].

## When to Kill an Idea (Stewart Butterfield)

Butterfield describes the moment he decided to shut down the game (Glitch) that preceded Slack [9]. He felt stuck between two narratives: "Good entrepreneurs are resilient. When everyone else thinks it's a bad idea, it's probably really a good idea. You have to dig in" versus the morning when he thought "I can't do this anymore. I'm the CEO. I'm the chairman of the board. I'm sure that this is not going to work" [9].

The decisive signal: "I had exhausted all of the ideas we had to fix the problems." The team had tried every approach to improve new user retention. Ideas that once filled giant whiteboards were reduced to three bad ones. "When you're out of ideas about how to make it work" -- that is when to give up [9].

## The Counterintuitive Nature of Startup Ideas (Paul Graham)

[[Paul Graham]] frames startup ideas within his broader argument that startups are counterintuitive [10]. The way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of them: "If you make a conscious effort to try and think of startup ideas, you will think of ideas that are not only bad, but bad and plausible sounding. Meaning you and everybody else will be fooled by them" [10].

Instead, the method is to "turn your brain into the type that has startup ideas unconsciously" [10]. The process: (1) learn about things that matter, (2) work on problems that interest you, (3) with people you like and respect. The third part is how co-founders and ideas emerge simultaneously [10].

Graham notes that the very best ideas "almost have to start as side projects, because they're always such outliers that your conscious mind would reject them as ideas for companies" [10]. Yahoo, Google, Facebook, and Apple all started this way -- "none of these companies were even supposed to be companies at first" [10].

The optimal preparation is not entrepreneurship classes but genuine intellectual curiosity. "The component of entrepreneurship that really matters is domain expertise. Larry Page is Larry Page because he was an expert on search. And the way he became an expert on search was because he was genuinely interested in it, not because of some ulterior motive" [10].

Graham's ultimate advice: "Just learn" [10].

## References

1. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (November 2012)
2. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
3. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
4. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)
5. [Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2005)
6. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (March 2007)
7. [How to Get and Test Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/7x-how-to-get-and-test-ideas) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)
8. [How to Get Startup Ideas](https://ycombinator.com/library/8g-how-to-get-startup-ideas) -- [[Jared Friedman]] (2020)
9. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
10. [How to Start a Startup: How to Get Ideas and Find What's Working](https://ycombinator.com/library/8B-how-to-start-a-startup-how-to-get-ideas-and-find-what-s-working) -- Stewart Butterfield, Adam D'Angelo (2017)
11. [How to Start a Startup: Before the Startup](https://ycombinator.com/library/JI-how-to-start-a-startup-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (2014)

title: Startup Terminology
body:
# Startup Terminology

[[Dalton Caldwell]] defines the most common terms in the startup world [1]. This article serves as a reference glossary, with links to deeper treatment in other wiki articles where available.

## MVP (Minimum Viable Product)

The keyword is "viable." A product that does not work and is useless to everyone is not viable. An MVP must be useful enough to serve some purpose for the customer. "It doesn't just mean a simple product that you built that doesn't do anything. It's got to be useful to someone for it to be viable" [1]. See [[Startup Ideas]] for guidance on building and testing MVPs.

## Venture Capital (VC)

Venture capitalists invest small amounts of money to buy equity in startup companies, hoping that enough of those companies become very large to return multiples of the original investment [1]. "The majority of a Venture Capital portfolio does not succeed, but the investments in the really big, successful companies pay for everything" [1].

Caldwell shares a historical note: "The original Venture Capital was in the whaling industry. The way it worked is when people would go out to hunt whales, a lot of times the ventures would fail. And so the people that needed money to start a ship needed to raise money from investors" who would invest in multiple ships hoping one successful whale capture would pay for all failed expeditions [1]. See [[Bootstrapping vs. Venture Capital]] for when VC is and is not appropriate.

## Angel Investor

An angel investor invests their own personal money, not from a VC fund. Angels tend to be the earliest-stage investors, writing smaller checks ($20,000-$50,000), and investing as a hobby or side project rather than a full-time job. "There's no qualification to be an angel investor. It's usually just someone that has money" [1].

## Profitability

At its simplest, making more money each month than you spend [1]. Caldwell emphasizes examining how profit margins change at scale. A startup may not be making money at a small scale, but if margins increase as it grows, the business model can still be attractive [1].

Google made no revenue for its first few years, but the profit margins on online advertising are very high. "Once Google turned on their monetization engine, they started to make an enormous amount of money" [1].

## Burn Rate

How much the company's bank account decreases per month [1]. If a company has $1 million and ends the month with $900,000, the burn rate is $100,000. "As a startup founder, it is critical to watch your burn rate... Not paying attention to this is harmful to your startup's health" [1]. See [[Financial Survival]] and [[Runway Management]] for detailed treatment.

## Seed Round

The first amount of money a new startup raises of any consequence [1]. There is no technical definition of size. A $300,000 raise on a SAFE can be called a seed round. A $100 million raise at a billion-dollar valuation is sometimes also called a seed round. In seed rounds, "anything goes. It could be that it's entirely composed of $50k checks and there is no lead investor" [1]. See [[Seed Fundraising]].

## Series A, B, C (and Beyond)

Unlike a seed round, a Series A typically involves a lead investor who gets a significant ownership percentage (traditionally around 20%) and often a board seat [1]. Subsequent rounds increment alphabetically: Series B, C, D, and so on. "I think I've definitely seen" Series G and Series H [1]. Valuations vary widely across rounds; the round letter alone does not indicate the valuation [1]. See [[Series A Fundraising]] and [[Series A Term Sheets]].

## Product-Market Fit (PMF)

When a startup has built something people use and like a lot, and the biggest issue is no longer defining what people want but growing and serving as many people as possible [1]. Before PMF, "startups are just spending all their time trying to test their assumptions, trying to build, trying to design, trying to talk to customers." After PMF, "your job is to stay in that state, to keep product market fit, and to grow and scale to the best of your ability" [1]. See [[Product-Market Fit]] for the comprehensive treatment.

## Bootstrapping

Starting a company without raising venture capital, using personal funds or business revenue [1]. "One of the reasons bootstrapping is a great option is it's entirely within your control." It is a "much, much better option for businesses that you expect to get to $5 to $10 million a year in revenue" [1]. See [[Bootstrapping vs. Venture Capital]].

## Convertible Note

A debt-like financial instrument where an investor gives a startup money with terms that may include interest payments or repayment obligations [1]. Caldwell warns founders to "read the fine print very carefully" [1].

## SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity)

Created by Y Combinator (specifically Carolyn Levy), a SAFE is an alternative to a convertible note [1]. It has fewer terms and fewer investor rights than a convertible note. "If you're raising as a startup founder and you are not doing a priced round and selling preferred equity, a SAFE is a great option to quickly close money in a seed round" [1].

## Equity

Ownership in a startup company, expressed as a percentage that adds up to 100% [1]. Employees may receive stock options rather than direct equity -- "a right to, in the future, execute on the stock options to receive equity at that point." Caldwell advises: "Always read the fine print and understand exactly what you are buying and selling" [1]. See [[Equity Split]].

## TAM (Total Addressable Market)

A thought experiment: if 100% of potential customers purchased the product, how much revenue would the company generate [1]. TAM calculations can underestimate the market. When Tesla started, the TAM for electric cars was tiny; the thesis required believing electric cars would eventually capture the broader car market. Uber dramatically increased the TAM for car services by creating a better experience that brought in people who did not previously use taxis [1]. "Great products tend to grow the TAM" [1].

## Valuation

What valuation the last investor invested at [1]. Unlike public stock prices, startup valuations do not mean there are active buyers and sellers. "Startups with high valuations don't always succeed. And so just think of it as a helpful term but not necessarily always accurate" [1].

## IPO (Initial Public Offering)

When a privately held company sells shares on a public exchange (NASDAQ, NYSE) [1]. IPOs are important to startups because they provide a path for employees, founders, and investors to realize financial returns. An IPO is "a great sign that the company is doing well... financially mature, that it is growing well and has built something of enduring value" [1].

## ARR and MRR (Annual/Monthly Recurring Revenue)

ARR measures yearly revenue from contracts or subscriptions that renew [1]. "If you have ten $100,000 contracts that renew yearly, that would be a million dollars of ARR." MRR is the monthly equivalent, more commonly used when billing is monthly (e.g., a ChatGPT subscription). "Make sure if you're reporting, if you're telling an investor you have ARR, make sure it actually recurs" [1].

## References

1. [Starting A Company? The Key Terms You Should Know](https://ycombinator.com/library/Lk-starting-a-company-the-key-terms-you-should-know) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]] (2024)

title: Early-Stage Mistakes
body:
# Early-Stage Mistakes

In a sense there is just one mistake that kills startups: not making something users want [1]. [[Paul Graham]] argues that if you make something users want, you will probably be fine, whatever else you do or don't do. And if you don't, you are dead, whatever else you do. His 2006 essay catalogues 18 specific mistakes, nearly all of which funnel through that one root cause [1].

## The Root Cause: Not Making Something Users Want

Graham states this is so much harder than it sounds that most startups fail at it. "I don't know why it's so hard to make something people want. It seems like it should be straightforward. But you can tell it must be hard by how few startups do it" [1].

The pattern across his essays is consistent: the biggest mistake is not trying hard enough [1], not understanding users deeply enough [1], or building something nobody actually needs (the "sitcom startup idea" problem described in [[Startup Ideas]]) [3].

## The 18 Mistakes

Graham's complete taxonomy from October 2006 [1]:

### Team Mistakes

**1. Single Founder.** Few successful startups were founded by one person. Having a single founder is "a vote of no confidence" -- it probably means the founder couldn't talk any friends into joining, which is alarming because friends know the founder best [1]. The emotional dimension may matter most: "The low points in a startup are so low that few could bear them alone. When you have multiple founders, esprit de corps binds them together in a way that seems to violate conservation laws. Each thinks 'I can't let my friends down'" [1].

**6. Hiring Bad Programmers.** This is primarily a problem for non-technical founders. "Business guys can't tell which are the good programmers. They don't even get a shot at the best ones, because no one really good wants a job implementing the vision of a business guy" [1]. The result: the startup is in the same position as a big company but without the advantages [1].

**17. Fights Between Founders.** About 20% of YC-funded startups have had a founder leave [1]. Most disputes are not due to the situation but the people, meaning they are inevitable. "Don't include your housemate in your startup because he'd feel left out otherwise. Don't start a company with someone you dislike because they have some skill you need" [1]. YC now advises founders to vest so there will be an orderly way for people to quit [1].

### Idea and Product Mistakes

**3. Marginal Niche.** Choosing a small, obscure niche to avoid competition is the startup equivalent of a child closing his eyes when a fly ball comes his way [1]. "If you make anything good, you're going to have competitors, so you may as well face that" [1]. See [[Startup Ideas]] for more on this.

**4. Derivative Idea.** Imitating existing companies is one source of ideas but not the best. Apple happened because Wozniak wanted a computer. Google because Larry and Sergey couldn't find stuff online. "Instead of starting from companies and working back to the problems they solved, look for problems and imagine the company that might solve them" [1].

**5. Obstinacy.** Startups are more like science than the Olympics: you need to follow the trail wherever it leads, not stick to a rigid plan [1]. Most successful startups end up doing something different than they originally intended, often so different it doesn't even seem like the same company. "But openness to new ideas has to be tuned just right. Switching to a new idea every week will be equally fatal" [1]. The test: ask whether the ideas represent some kind of progression, and ask your users.

**7. Choosing the Wrong Platform.** The scary thing about platforms is that some seem like fine, responsible choices yet will destroy you. Java applets "probably killed just about 100% of the startups who believed that" [1]. PayPal nearly died because the post-merger CEO wanted to switch to Windows, even after Max Levchin showed their software scaled only 1% as well on Windows as Unix. "Fortunately for PayPal they switched CEOs instead" [1].

**10. Having No Specific User in Mind.** "Perhaps you create wealth in proportion to how well you understand the problem you're solving, and the problems you understand best are your own" [1]. If you are building for a group that does not include you, you must shift gears: you are "flying on instruments" and must be empirical, finding real users and measuring their responses [1].

### Launch and Execution Mistakes

**8. Slowness in Launching.** Software is always 85% done. Several distinct problems manifest as delays: working too slowly, not truly understanding the problem, fear of users, fear of being judged, working on too many things, excessive perfectionism [1]. "I've seen a lot of startups die because they were too slow to release stuff, and none because they were too quick" [2].

**9. Launching Too Early.** Less common than launching too slowly by a factor of a hundred, but possible. The minimum: "a core that's both (a) useful on its own and (b) something that can be incrementally expanded into the whole project" [1]. Early adopters are fairly tolerant -- "it just has to do something" [1].

**16. Not Wanting to Get Your Hands Dirty.** Programmers would rather write code than sell. Larry and Sergey initially tried to get another company to buy their search algorithm [1]. In the first YC batch (summer 2005), one founder spent half his time talking to cell phone company executives. "Can you imagine anything more painful for a hacker? But it paid off, because this startup seems the most successful of that group by an order of magnitude" [1].

### Financial Mistakes

**11. Raising Too Little Money.** You must raise enough to get to the next step. Rule of thumb: take 50-100% more than you think you will need [1].

**12. Spending Too Much.** The classic way: hiring a lot of people, which increases costs and slows you down. "Don't do it if you can avoid it" [1]. See [[Financial Survival]] for the full treatment.

**13. Raising Too Much Money.** Changes the company's atmosphere and makes it harder to pivot. "When you raise a lot of money, your company moves to the suburbs and has kids" [1].

**14. Poor Investor Management.** Founders should neither ignore investors nor let them run the company. Apple's board nearly killed the company by firing Steve Jobs [1].

**15. Sacrificing Users to Supposed Profit.** Leave business models for later. Google made search work first, then monetized. "It is irresponsible not to think about business models. It's just ten times more irresponsible not to think about the product" [1].

### Commitment Mistakes

**18. A Half-Hearted Effort.** The most common type of failure is not the spectacular flameout but the startup that never gets going -- a side project that was gradually abandoned. Most founders of failed startups don't quit their day jobs; most founders of successful ones do [1]. See [[Founder Mindset]] for the full treatment of determination and commitment.

**2. Bad Location.** Silicon Valley dominates, then Boston, then Seattle, Austin, Denver, and New York. In towns like Houston and Chicago and Detroit, the number of startups per capita is "too small to measure" [1]. The reasons compound: experts, higher standards, sympathetic people, potential hires who want to live there, supporting industries, and serendipitous encounters [1].

## Playing House

Graham coined the term "playing house" for a pattern he saw repeatedly at YC [4]: young founders who go through all the outward motions of starting a startup -- make up a plausible idea, raise money, rent a cool office, hire people -- while neglecting the one essential thing: making something people want [4].

The reason is training. Students have spent their whole lives learning to game systems. "The next step after rent a cool office and hire a bunch of people is: gradually realize how completely fucked they are" [4]. Startups are where gaming the system stops working: "There is no boss to trick, only users, and all users care about is whether your product does what they want" [4].

## The Class Project Problem

Graham identifies a specific failure mode in young founders: they build things that look like class projects [5]. Two things are missing from class projects:

1. **An iterative definition of a real problem.** In class, problems are predefined. In startups, you discover the real problem through a process of evolution. The very implementation exists to refine the idea -- often the only value of what you build in the first six months is proving your initial idea was mistaken [5].

2. **Market-standard measurement.** Professors judge the distance from starting point to where you are now. Customers judge the distance remaining between where you are and what they need. "The market doesn't give a shit how hard you worked" [5].

## Premature Scaling

Paul Buchheit identifies a related pattern: founders take a small business that isn't really working (bad unit economics, typically) and then scale it up because they want impressive growth numbers [3]. This is similar to overhiring in that it makes the business much harder to fix once it is big, plus they are bleeding cash fast [3]. See [[Default Alive or Default Dead]] for the full framework.

## The Self-Destruction Pattern

Graham argues consistently that competitors are not the primary threat. "Way more startups hose themselves than get crushed by competitors" [2]. The three main ways startups destroy themselves:

1. **Internal disputes** -- fights between founders, usually traceable to choosing the wrong cofounder
2. **Inertia** -- not launching, not iterating, not responding to what users need
3. **Ignoring users** -- the worst of the three. "A couple of founders who have some great idea they know everyone is going to love, and that's what they're going to build, no matter what" [2]

Almost everyone's initial plan is broken. Microsoft would be selling programming languages and Apple would be selling printed circuit boards if they had stuck to theirs. In both cases their customers told them what their business should be -- "and they were smart enough to listen" [2].

## References

1. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
2. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2006)
3. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2015)
4. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2014)
5. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) -- [[Paul Graham]] (October 2006)
6. [Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) -- [[Paul Graham]] (April 2005)

title: Cold Emails
body:
# Cold Emails

[[Aaron Epstein]], YC Group Partner and founder of Creative Market, provides a systematic framework for writing cold emails that convert strangers into customers, partners, or collaborators. The good news: most cold emails are terrible, so the bar for standing out is low [1].

## Start with a Warm Intro

The single best email outreach hack: get a warm introduction. Conversion rates are two to three times higher than cold outreach. Turn over every rock: LinkedIn contacts, friends of friends, current and former coworkers, school alumni networks, employer alumni networks [1].

## Map Your Funnel

Before sending a single email, work backwards from your goal [1]:

Epstein provides a sample B2B funnel with illustrative conversion rates:
- **Goal**: 1 new customer
- **Demo-to-customer conversion**: 10% → need 10 demos
- **Response-to-demo conversion**: 25% → need 40 responses
- **Open-to-response conversion**: 10% → need 400 opens
- **Sent-to-open conversion**: 50% → need 800 emails sent

The math: 800 emails to get one customer. "If you're expecting to just send a handful of emails every day, every week, every month and see results, that's not going to cut it. You're going to need to send dozens of emails per day, possibly even 50 per day" [1].

Start manually and personalized. Do not automate until you have learned what works. The goal of any single email is not to close the sale -- it is to advance to the next step in the funnel [1].

Track conversion rates at each step to identify where to focus improvement. Conversion rates decrease as you scale, so fix poor rates before trying to scale [1].

## Increasing Open Rates

### Targeting Is the Highest-Leverage Activity

Better targeting beats better copywriting. "It's better to send 100 targeted emails that are going to have a much higher open rate rather than a thousand untargeted emails that are mostly going to be deleted" [1].

Epstein shares a cautionary example: he received an email pitching a shipping optimization service. His company, Creative Market, sold digital design assets downloaded from the internet. No amount of brilliant copywriting could have converted him -- the targeting was simply wrong [1].

### The From and Subject Lines

The "from" field should be a personal name, not a company name -- it feels like it comes from a human [1].

Subject lines should be short, relevant, and non-salesy: "Hey quick question," "Can I get your advice," "Help a fellow founder," "Hello from [company name]." It should feel like a friend could have written it [1].

### Channel Selection

Email is usually better than LinkedIn. LinkedIn can feel overwhelming for some recipients and special for others -- adapt to the industry. Text messaging is more personal but only appropriate with explicit permission [1].

### Finding Email Addresses

Ask mutual connections, check LinkedIn profiles and personal blogs/websites, use LinkedIn Sales Navigator, guess based on company email format (firstname.lastname@company.com), or use tools like Apollo.io, Hunter.io, and Clearbit [1].

## Seven Principles of Effective Email Copy

### 1. Have a Focused, Specific Goal

One email, one ask. Multiple asks create a paradox of choice: "If they have too many things they can do, or it's not clear which is important, they're just going to delete your email." Every word should drive toward the single goal. Delete anything that does not [1].

### 2. Be Human

Use emotional language: "I'd love to," "It would mean a lot," "Thanks -- you have no idea how much this helped." Be informal -- "hey" instead of "Hello Mr. Epstein." Typos are acceptable. No capitalization is fine. "Humans talk about emotion, and bots or LLMs are not good at this type of thing" [1].

The test: read the email out loud to your cofounder or yourself. If anything feels awkward to say to a person's face, rewrite it [1].

### 3. Personalize

Everyone can smell spam. The difference between "hi there" (generic) and "hey Aaron" (personal) is substantial. But personalization must be specific. "Hey, love what you're doing at Creative Market" feels like a placeholder variable. "I'm a huge Creative Market fan ever since you launched the photos category -- it's now my go-to resource" shows genuine research [1].

**Uncommon commonalities**: Find things uniquely in common. "We both went to the same college and had classes in the same buildings" -- instantly builds connection. "We're both men" -- weird and useless. Finding uncommon commonalities requires real research: look up the person on LinkedIn, use their product, understand their history [1].

### 4. Keep It Short

A wall of text is an instant delete. The email should be easy to read and respond to on a phone in under 60 seconds. If it requires more cognitive overhead, recipients will archive it and forget [1].

### 5. Establish Credibility

If in YC, mention it. If not, use former schools, notable companies, or impressive achievements. Share well-known customer names as social proof. Publish interesting industry data to position as an expert. Mention shared connections to create baseline trust [1].

### 6. Make It About the Reader, Not About You

Reframe every "I" as "you" and "yours." Tell your story as the quest to solve the reader's problems. This works especially well if you have felt the problem yourself: "I'm dedicating the next ten years of my life to solve this problem that I felt so deeply that I know you feel too" [1].

Use the language your users use to describe you, not your internal terminology. Ask users: "How would you describe my company?" Use their exact phrasing in outreach and on your website [1].

Do not make demands. "You're on their time. You're in their inbox. It's not their responsibility to get back to you" [1].

### 7. Have a Clear Call to Action

End with a concrete next step: "Reply to let me know," "Click here to get started," "Can you intro me to [name]?" Make it a standalone sentence in its own paragraph before the sign-off. Recipients scan to the end to figure out what is being asked -- make it unmistakable [1].

## Follow Up

One email is often not enough. Follow up two, three, four times manually. Give it a few days between emails. Get creative: Epstein once used the subject line "Free donuts," then delivered donuts to the recipient's office. If someone does not respond, do not get angry. "Nobody owes you anything here. Just move on. You can check back in in a couple months" [1].

## Examples of Bad Emails

Epstein critiques real emails he received [1]:

**Bad targeting**: A shipping optimization pitch sent to a digital asset company. "This could have been the most amazingly written, most personalized email, and nothing they could do would make me their customer."

**No personalization, no name, no sender**: "Hey there, hope you are doing great... do you partner with companies to make extra bucks?" No name used, nothing specific, unclear ask, unsigned, and includes a P.S. saying "if you don't want to hear from me anymore, just let me know" -- effectively admitting it is spam.

**All about the sender**: "My name is so-and-so... I'm a freelancer... I have been helping companies since 2014... I can help assist you..." Every sentence starts with "I." Nothing about the recipient.

## Examples of Good Emails

**The uncommon commonality**: Subject line "Go Terps" on LinkedIn. The sender references Van Munching Hall (a specific building at the University of Maryland), establishes credibility (ex-Googlers), makes a clear ask (review YC application), notes they have already had friends review it (not wasting time), and ends with a clear call to action. Epstein responded -- something he rarely does on LinkedIn [1].

**The templated-but-personal**: Epstein's own Creative Market seller recruitment email. Entirely templated except for the name and URL, but uses tricks to feel special: "special link," "super early," "I'd love your feedback," "handpicked sellers only," "welcome to the club." Multiple smiley faces. Written in conversational tone [1].

**The before-and-after**: A smoothie machine company's original email focused on their demo day goal. The rewritten version: "I'd love to throw a free smoothie party for the whole team at your office. It's like happy hour but smoothies. And free." A near no-brainer for the recipient [1].

## Final Advice

Founders should send these emails personally -- recipients take them more seriously from the CEO than from an account executive. Block off hours per day for this work. "You may be small, but that is your advantage because you can give your personalized attention to making these dedicated emails feel special to the recipient" [1].

## References

1. [How To Convert Customers With Cold Emails](https://ycombinator.com/library/LZ-how-to-convert-customers-with-cold-emails) -- [[Aaron Epstein]] (n.d.)

title: Startup Location
body:
# Startup Location

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] stage a deliberate disagreement about San Francisco specifically -- Caldwell prefers the suburbs, Seibel lives on Market Street -- but converge completely on the underlying advice: startups should be in the Bay Area [1]. The disagreement is over city versus suburb within the same ecosystem, not whether to be in the ecosystem at all.

## The Fake Disagreement

Caldwell prefers living in a place that is "warm and bright and sunny" with "trees everywhere," away from dense-city distractions. He has small children and values a yard [1]. Seibel grew up in suburban New Jersey and perceived getting to the city as aspirational. He likes restaurants, bars, and the chip-on-the-shoulder feeling of being surrounded by things he could not yet afford [1].

The resolution: "Would you recommend someone start a startup outside of the Bay Area?" Caldwell: "I genuinely would not" [1]. The Bay Area is large enough to accommodate both lifestyles. Google, Facebook, and Apple headquarters are not in San Francisco -- they are on the Peninsula and in Cupertino. "Clearly in the history of this stuff, all these great companies were built outside of San Francisco" [1]. The claim that every company must be in downtown San Francisco is "factually wrong" [1]. But the claim that startups should be in the Bay Area region is strongly supported.

## Why the Bay Area: Network Effects

The core argument is network effects. Caldwell draws the analogy to the products they fund: network effects make Airbnb, Uber, and Facebook valuable. The Bay Area has network effects for startups [1].

The critical property of network effects: "If you're not benefiting from them, you don't know what you are missing" [1]. Seibel elaborates: "It's all the people that you won't meet, all the chance encounters you won't have, all the employees you won't have, all these surface area for luck" [1].

Seibel observes that people who left the Bay Area during COVID all came back: "All of them said the same thing, which was basically like, 'I missed the community.' But I never really understood that I was consuming the community. I thought I was working all the time, but I actually was consuming a community of people who are really into startups. And when I moved to Seattle or Austin or New York, that community wasn't as strong, and I missed it" [1].

Caldwell adds: "And they also don't say that as publicly" -- the return is quieter than the departure [1]. He also notes a subtlety: "People here sometimes don't understand the network effects they're consuming" -- even people benefiting from the Bay Area's startup network may not recognize it until they leave [1].

## The Industry Analogy

Seibel frames it in terms of other industries: the best finance people end up in New York or London. The best entertainment people end up in LA. "When you want to be top 0.001, you want to be around other people who are trying to be top 0.001" [1]. Neither Caldwell (from Texas) nor Seibel (from New Jersey) grew up in the Bay Area. They both migrated there for the same reason [1].

The analogy extends to acting: "Think about how many people, their story of their big break is that something happened in Los Angeles or New York" [1]. The work they did before that break was putting themselves in position for the luck to happen.

## The Noise That Confuses Founders

Caldwell identifies several sources of misleading discourse [1]:
- **Politicians** trying to attract tech jobs to their cities (Miami, etc.) -- they really want a Google office, not startup culture
- **Social media flamewars** using photos of San Francisco's problems to generate engagement
- **Founders not in the Bay Area** who read Twitter and assume Bay Area = living in the Tenderloin

Caldwell observes that the smartest founders "look past all these stupid debates" and recognize the network effect [1].

## The Odds Argument

Caldwell brings it back to probability: "There's certainly anecdotes of people that were discovered outside the Bay Area, and everything slipped into place for them. But if you actually just look at the numbers of how you increase the odds of success, moving to the big leagues was a key part of it" [1].

He uses a blackjack analogy: you can win without knowing basic strategy, but "it is so easy to get that one hour of instruction, and that increases your odds so much. Getting into the Bay Area is so relatively easy compared to all the other things you have to choose correctly. Why not pick up the easy wins? It's an easy percentage multiplier" [1].

Seibel adds the sports parallel: "The average NBA player was the best basketball player anywhere. Sometimes startup founders miss this point. They're kind of like, 'I want to be in the game,' versus 'I want to win the game'" [1]. You can play basketball for a high school team in any city, but that is not sufficient to make the NBA.

## Anecdotes vs. Numbers

Caldwell anticipates the counterarguments: Shopify in Ottawa, MongoDB in New York. "The anecdotes are true: you can do a startup outside the Bay Area, of course. And maybe there's a really good reason you want to do that -- health reasons or family. Great, of course. But if all you want to do is play the odds and optimize for success, I can't really understand why you wouldn't want to be here" [1].

Seibel emphasizes: "That's not anecdotes, that's numbers" [1].

## References

1. [Does Your Startup Need To Be In San Francisco?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/IN-dalton-michael-does-your-startup-need-to-be-in-san-francisco) -- [[Dalton Caldwell]], [[Michael Seibel]] (2023)

title: Cold Emailing Investors
body:
# Cold Emailing Investors
