[[Michael Seibel]] receives and responds to large volumes of cold emails from founders seeking investment. His advice centers on one principle: the goal of a cold email is not to close an investment. It is to get a reply [1].

## The Do's

### Make It Short

If a cold email takes two to five minutes to read, Seibel will set it aside for later, and "it's not clear when I'll get back to it" [1]. If the email can be read in 60 seconds or less, he can guarantee he will read it. The critical insight: "You need me not to sell you on writing a check and investing in your company. You need me to just read what you're working on and understand what you're working on enough to want to reply back" [1].

The test: "Can I see your email, be interested, and reply all within the same 60 to 120 seconds? That's what you're looking for" [1]. If there is any confusion about length, record yourself reading the email aloud. If it takes too long, cut it down [1].

### Include the Right Information

In that short email, Seibel wants to know [1]:

1. The problem you are trying to solve
2. What your solution is
3. Whether you have launched, and any growth metrics
4. How big the market could be
5. Whether you have co-founders
6. Whether you have the ability to write code
7. Whether you know something controversial about the problem or market that others do not know or would not agree with

### Use Plain Language

Jargon is a common failure mode. There is a difference between a customer pitch and an investor pitch. Customers know the jargon of their industry. Investors may not. "Make sure you're using simple language, language that you can use with any friend that you have, regardless of whether they're in your industry" [1]. See also [[Garry Tan]]'s advice on plain English in [[Applying to YC]].

### Send From a Company Email

Use a company email address with your name in it. Seibel notes that emails from personal addresses or generic "info@" addresses "just kind of set things off the wrong way" [1]. Most investors use tools like Rapportive or Superhuman that surface information about the sender. A personal email address prevents this from working [1].

### Track Opens

Use email tracking to confirm whether investors have actually seen the email. This data informs whether to follow up [1].

### Attach a Deck (Optional)

Attaching a deck is fine but not required. If you do, use the standard Silicon Valley format. Seibel recommends Googling "Airbnb's fundraising deck" as a template. Decks from other industries (finance, marketing) often look completely different and will confuse investors [1]. See [[Seed Fundraising]] for the full deck template.

## The Don'ts

### Don't Make It Long

Seibel repeats this because it is the single most common mistake. Founders try to accomplish too much in the first email -- they want a meeting, a phone call, an investment. "In reality, what you want is a conversation. Once we're in a back-and-forth, that's when I can ask questions" [1].

### Don't Request an In-Person Meeting Immediately

Different investors have different preferences for first engagement. Pushing for an in-person meeting can backfire: "If you are perceived as just pushing for an in-person meeting, it's more often than not that investors are going to think, 'Oh, well, I don't want my arm to be twisted in a meeting where I don't know whether it's going to be a high quality meeting'" [1].

### Don't Send Multiple Follow-Ups Quickly

If you have open tracking and the investor has read the email, they have made a choice about whether to reply now or later. "You don't need to hit them over the head with multiple emails" [1].

### Don't Withhold What Your Company Does

The most important don't is a double negative: "Don't not describe what your company does" [1]. Seibel is shocked by how many emails he receives saying essentially: "Michael, I'm coming to town. I'd love to meet in person for an hour so I can tell you what I'm working on" [1].

This is the wrong order of operations. The correct approach: describe the interesting thing you are working on, get the investor to want to know more, and let the investor come to you. "You want me to be the one seeking more information from you. You want to kind of put something enticing out there and have me come to you. That's what gives you leverage" [1].

## References

1. [How to Cold Email Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/65-how-to-cold-email-investors) -- [[Michael Seibel]] (n.d.)

title: Hard Conversations
body:
# Hard Conversations

[[Dalton Caldwell]] and [[Michael Seibel]] argue that having honest, uncomfortable conversations is one of the most important skills for startup founders, and that avoiding them is one of the most common failure modes [1]. The avoidance operates at every level: between founders and advisors, between co-founders, and between founders and employees. Note: in this transcript, Caldwell is sometimes labeled "Host," but the content is clearly his based on the conversational context.

## What Makes a Conversation Hard

Caldwell defines it by inversion: "If it's a conversation you're looking forward to having, it is not a hard conversation." A hard conversation involves high stakes, vulnerability, saying something the other person does not want to hear, or disagreeing with someone who has power over you. The easy version is "Hey, you're great. Way to go." The hard version is everything else that actually matters [1].

Seibel describes learning this as a YC partner. In his early batches, he measured his effectiveness by how much fun founders had talking to him. "Only after a couple batches did I realize that doesn't really do anything." When he started sharing his actual opinions about products and work ethic, "the job changed" [1].

## The Question Behind the Question

Seibel estimates that 60 to 80 percent of the time at YC office hours, a founder arrives with one question and the conversation ends up being about something entirely different. He and Caldwell call this "the question behind the question" [1].

**Example: The pre-made decision.** Seibel describes a founder who comes to office hours having already made a decision. They want the hour to validate that decision. The easy response: "Seems like you've thought about this really well. I see no errors in your logic." The hard response: "Hey, it seems like you've already made a decision. Cool, why are we talking about this?" Caldwell probes deeper: "Is it that your co-founder disagrees with you and what you're trying to do is bring my opinion into it to prove that you're right?" [1].

**Example: The YC application anxiety.** Seibel describes people who approach with "really important questions about applying." The actual questions are content-free. Caldwell identifies the real question: "I feel insecure, and I want to talk to you so that you give me validation that I should apply" [1].

**Example: The deep-dive request.** A founder asks for a two-hour meeting. Caldwell identifies the real reasons: they think the partners do not understand their business, they want face time for relationship-building, or they are lazy and "didn't come up with the agenda" and want two hours to produce twenty minutes of productivity [1]. Seibel adds a fourth pattern: founders wanting to substitute talking to an advisor for talking to their users. "Folks, so often I'm just like, 'You're having a conversation with me that you should have with your customers. Talk to them. I don't use your product. Talk to the people who do'" [1].

## Hard Conversations with Co-Founders

The failure pattern: two people become co-founders without ever having had a genuine disagreement. The relationship is entirely superficial -- "We're best friends. We worked together. It's all wonderful." Then the first real disagreement arises, and the relationship explodes [1].

Seibel identifies two failure modes [1]:

1. **No pressure release.** Co-founders only have superficial conversations for years. Pressure builds. Eventually someone snaps.
2. **Constant open fighting.** The opposite extreme, where disagreements are never resolved and the relationship is perpetually combative.

Caldwell describes the skill of disagreeing productively -- to "pull your punches and have a disagreement that's a real disagreement and actually release the pressure of the disagreement in a way that doesn't cross the line so that the relationship is irreparably harmed" [1].

Caldwell explains why YC prefers co-founders who have a pre-existing relationship beyond the startup. When there is a friendship worth maintaining even if the startup fails, both people are more careful about what they say. When the only connection is the startup, and the startup is struggling, "there are things you can't unsay" [1].

Caldwell adds a critical nuance about conflict styles, citing former YC batch director Amy: some people lean into conflict and want to talk it through immediately. Others avoid conflict, need time to process, or do not work through disagreements via conversation. "I have to look at those cues. I have to understand who I'm talking to and realize that when I have to kind of take a step back, even though I'm unsatisfied, because I'm creating more of a problem by continuing to push forward" [1].

## Hard Conversations with Employees

Caldwell frames the general problem: if founders only have easy, superficial conversations with their team, "it's cool until it's not." He adds: "You know what they are. You're probably thinking of a few things you would say if you weren't afraid of the consequences" [1].

Seibel offers two specific points for startup employees:

1. **Allow the possibility that you picked the wrong person.** The employee might not be the right fit. They might do ten times better work somewhere else. "Allowing that idea makes their life better just as much as it would make your life better" [1].

2. **Good startup employees want honesty.** They joined a startup because they want to impact the outcome. Seibel recalls sharing bad news -- even "we only have two months of runway" -- and finding it motivating rather than demoralizing. "For great startup employees, they're like, 'All right, how the fuck are we gonna fix this?'" The worst outcome is employees who are clueless about the company's real situation: "Well, I thought everything was going fine. You never told me anything was wrong" [1].

## Hard Conversations with Yourself

Caldwell and Seibel acknowledge that they struggle with this too. "We're not saying we always get this right 100 percent of the time." The only thing they claim to do well is continuously ask themselves: "Are we lying to ourselves? Is there a question behind the question?" [1].

Seibel closes with a metaphor from The West Wing. A character who has overcome addiction sees a colleague struggling but hiding it. Instead of offering superficial advice, he "jumps down to the bottom of the well" with the person. His justification: "I've climbed out of this well before, and I know the way out." Seibel applies this to advising founders: "I've made all these mistakes before, and the only thing that I could say is I know the way out" [1].
